topics.title: Introduction to Process Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Process Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Safety (PS)** is a disciplined framework for managing the integrity of operating systems and processes that handle hazardous materials and energy. It focuses on the prevention and control of **unplanned, catastrophic releases** of chemicals, hydrocarbons, or energy that could result in major injury, fatality, environmental damage, or asset loss.

According to the **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)** of the American Institute of Chemical Engineers (AIChE), process safety is fundamentally driven by a management-system approach and addresses the *major accident hazards* that arise from process conditions such as high pressure, high temperature, flammability, toxicity, and reactivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Favourite)**
> - **Process Safety** = Catastrophic, low-frequency, high-consequence events (e.g., Bhopal, Flixborough).
> - **Occupational Safety** = Routine, high-frequency, low-consequence events (e.g., slips, trips, falls).
> *KTU examiners expect students to clearly differentiate the two in Part A answers.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Aircraft Cockpit" View

Imagine a process plant as a **commercial airliner in flight**:

- The plane (plant) operates under *enormous energy density* (fuel + compressed air).
- The cockpit crew uses **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**, pre-flight checklists (Process Hazard Analyses), and instruments (Safety Instrumented Systems).
- The "pilot" (process operator) must manage a complex, tightly coupled system where **one small deviation** (a wrongly tightened bolt, a missed alarm) can cascade into a **catastrophic hull loss** (e.g., Flixborough 1974).
- Process safety is the *engineering discipline* that ensures the plane never crashes. Occupational safety is the *first-aid kit* for minor bruises from a bumpy flight.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Check:** If a worker cuts a finger on a machine — that is **occupational safety**. If a storage tank containing 5,000 tonnes of propylene ruptures and forms a vapour cloud that explodes — that is **process safety**.

## 1.3 Defining the Lexicon of Risk (Mandatory Vocabulary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Definitions every KTU student must memorize verbatim**

| Term | Definition | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Hazard** | An inherent physical, chemical, or environmental property with the potential to cause harm (e.g., propane is flammable). | CCPS, ISO 12100 |
| **Risk** | The combination of the *frequency* (or probability) of an unwanted event and the *magnitude* of its consequences. | ISO 31000, IEC 61511 |
| **Incident** | An undesired event that could have (or did) result in loss, but did not necessarily reach the threshold of a major accident. | API RP 754 |
| **Accident** | An unplanned, undesired event that results in actual harm to people, property, or the environment. | OHSAS 18001 |
| **Major Hazard Installation (MHI)** | An industrial facility handling hazardous substances above prescribed threshold quantities (e.g., threshold $\geq$ **50 tonnes** of flammable substances under Indian Manufacture Storage Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules). | MSIHC Rules 1989 (India) |
| **Process Safety Event (PSE)** | A release of material or energy that resulted in a fatality, injury, fire, explosion, or significant environmental impact. | API RP 754 (Tier 1 to Tier 4) |

## 1.4 The Scope of Process Safety

Process safety management covers the **entire lifecycle** of a chemical/petrochemical facility:

1. **Research & Development** (laboratory scale)
2. **Design & Engineering** (P&ID, HAZOP, layout)
3. **Construction & Commissioning**
4. **Operation & Maintenance** (operating procedures, mechanical integrity)
5. **Modification & Management of Change (MOC)**
6. **Decommissioning / Demolition**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Board Misconception:** Process safety is *not* a single department or a checklist. It is a **holistic, lifecycle-integrated discipline** that requires the commitment of *every* engineer — from process designer to plant operator.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Historical Context — The "Process Safety Awakening"

The modern field of process safety crystallized in response to a string of industrial disasters in the second half of the 20th century. The KTU examiner **rewards** students who can quote the *causal agent* and *lesson learned* of at least three landmark incidents.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Big Four" Landmarks in Process Safety History**
> 1. **Flixborough Disaster, UK (1974)** — A *cyclohexane* vapour cloud explosion killed 28 people. Cause: Improper bypass of a failed reactor section using a temporary flexible "dog-leg" pipe.
> 2. **Seveso Disaster, Italy (1976)** — Release of **2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCCD)** over a populated area. Led to the **Seveso Directives (1982/1996/2012)** of the European Union.
> 3. **Bhopal Disaster, India (1984)** — *Methyl isocyanate (MIC)* leak at Union Carbide killed **3,800+ people** (official figure; estimated fatalities far higher). The single worst industrial disaster in history.
> 4. **Texas City Refinery Explosion, USA (2005)** — A *raffinate splitter* tower explosion killed 15 workers. Triggered the **Baker Panel Report** and BP's paradigm shift toward process safety.

## 2.2 The Four Pillars of Process Safety (CCPS Model)

The **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)** of AIChE has established that process safety excellence rests on **four pillars**. These are routinely asked in KTU 2024 scheme examinations as 7- to 14-mark theory questions.

1. **Pillar 1 — Process Safety Culture:** A positive, reporting, learning-oriented organization where process safety is a core value.
2. **Pillar 2 — Process Safety Competency:** Demonstrated knowledge, skills, and abilities at all organizational levels.
3. **Pillar 3 — Process Safety Management Systems:** The 14 OSHA PSM elements and Risk-Based Process Safety (RBPS) elements of CCPS.
4. **Pillar 4 — Process Safety Standards & Practices:** Application of recognized standards, codes, and engineering practices (e.g., ASME, API, NFPA, IEC 61511).

## 2.3 OSHA Process Safety Management (PSM) — The 14 Elements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Topic:** List and briefly explain the **14 elements of OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119**.

1. Employee Participation
2. Process Safety Information (PSI)
3. Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)
4. Operating Procedures
5. Training
6. Contractors
7. Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)
8. Mechanical Integrity
9. Hot Work Permit
10. Management of Change (MOC)
11. Incident Investigation
12. Emergency Planning and Response
13. Compliance Audits
14. Trade Secrets

## 2.4 API RP 754 — Process Safety Event Tiers

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Safety Performance Indicators** are classified by severity:

- **Tier 1 PSE:** *Highest severity* — A loss of primary containment (LOPC) with the greatest consequence (e.g., a fatality, a $\geq$ **$100,000** direct cost, a recordable injury, or a vapor cloud explosion).
- **Tier 2 PSE:** *Lower severity* — LOPC events that could have escalated to a Tier 1 (e.g., a $\geq$ **$2,500** direct cost, a vapour cloud that did *not* ignite).
- **Tier 3 PSE:** *Internal leading indicators* — Activation of independent protection layers (IPLs), emergency depressurization, or callouts.
- **Tier 4 PSE:** *Operating discipline indicators* — Near-miss reports, process safety observations, and trailing indicators.

## 2.5 Process Safety Triangle (Bird's Heir)

The **Process Safety Triangle** is the KTU examination's most-asked diagrammatic concept. It visualizes the causal chain from unsafe acts to catastrophe:

> **Near-Miss → Minor Incident → Serious Incident → Major Process Safety Event**

The base of the triangle is **wide** (frequent near-misses) and the apex is **narrow** (rare catastrophes). The ratio is approximately:

$$
\frac{\text{Near-Misses}}{\text{Minor Incidents}} \approx \frac{1}{10} \quad ; \quad \frac{\text{Minor Incidents}}{\text{Major Incidents}} \approx \frac{1}{10}
$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Formulas for PECHT754 — Quantitative Risk Assessment**

| Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Individual Risk (IR) | $IR_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j \cdot p_{ij}$ | $f_j$ = frequency of $j^{th}$ event; $p_{ij}$ = probability that person $i$ is harmed | $\text{yr}^{-1}$ |
| Societal Risk (F-N curve) | $F(N) = \sum_{j \,:\, N_j \geq N} f_j$ | $F(N)$ = cumulative frequency of events causing $\geq N$ fatalities | $\text{yr}^{-1}$ |
| Risk = Frequency $\times$ Consequence | $R = f \times C$ | $R$ = risk; $f$ = frequency; $C$ = consequence (e.g., fatalities, USD) | varies |
| Inherent Safety — Inventory | $I_{new} = I_{ref} \cdot \left( \frac{V_{new}}{V_{ref}} \right)^{2/3}$ | For vessels holding same mass, $I \propto V^{2/3}$ (surface area law) | dimensionless |
| Consequence (Probit, $Y$) | $Y = a + b \cdot \ln(V)$ | $V$ = variable (concentration $\cdot$ time, overpressure, thermal dose) | probit unit |
| Probability from Probit | $P = 50 \cdot \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\left( \frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}} \right) \right]$ | $P$ = percentage of exposed people suffering the effect | % |
| Tolerable Individual Risk | $IR_{tolerable} \leq 10^{-4}$ | UK HSE criterion for workers; $10^{-5}$ for general public | $\text{yr}^{-1}$ |
| Leak rate (orifice) | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P / \rho}$ | $C_d$ = discharge coefficient ($\approx 0.62$ for orifice); $A$ = hole area | $\text{kg/s}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU LaTeX Pitfall:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) in a markdown table, always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$. **Never** use a bare vertical pipe `|` inside a table cell — it breaks the table parser.

## 2.7 Inherent Safety — The Kletz Principles (Trevor Kletz, 1978)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Question — "Explain the principles of inherent safety."**

Trevor Kletz (1942–2013), the "father of inherent safety," articulated the **MINCE** acronym:

- **M — Minimize** (e.g., reduce inventory of hazardous materials).
- **I — Intensify** (use lower inventories at higher throughput).
- **N — Neutralize** (replace toxic substances with non-toxic ones, e.g., water-based coatings instead of solvent-based).
- **C — Substitute** (use safer solvents, e.g., aqueous HCl instead of liquid bromine).
- **E — Eliminate** (e.g., use a different chemistry that removes the hazardous intermediate).

*Plus two more principles:*
- **Attenuate** (use lower pressures, temperatures, and concentrations).
- **Limit effects** (e.g., bunding, segregated storage, blast walls, dikes).

## 2.8 Risk Acceptance Criteria — ALARP

> [!NOTE]
> **ALARP = As Low As Reasonably Practicable** (UK HSE) / **ALARA = As Low As Reasonably Achievable** (US DOE).
> The principle: risk must be reduced unless the cost of further reduction is *grossly disproportionate* to the benefit obtained.

$$
\text{Risk is "ALARP"} \iff \frac{\text{Cost of further risk reduction}}{\text{Risk reduction achieved}} \leq \text{Disproportion Factor}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Risk Triad — Formal Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify initiating events.**
List $N$ independent initiating events $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_N$, each with frequency $f_j$ (units: $\text{yr}^{-1}$).

**Step 2 — Quantify the consequence.**
For each initiating event $j$, the consequence $C_j$ is characterized by a vector of impacts:
- Number of fatalities $N_j$
- Capital loss $L_j$ in USD
- Environmental release $E_j$ in tonnes

**Step 3 — Compute individual risk.**
The individual risk to a person $i$ located at fixed coordinates $(x, y)$ is the sum over all events:

$$
IR_i(x, y) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j \cdot p_{ij}(x, y)
$$

where $p_{ij}(x, y)$ is the conditional probability that person $i$ is fatally harmed given that event $E_j$ occurs at the source location.

**Step 4 — Compute societal risk (F-N curve).**
The societal risk summarizes the cumulative frequency $F(N)$ of all events causing $N$ or more fatalities:

$$
F(N) = \sum_{j \,:\, N_j \geq N} f_j
$$

**Step 5 — Plot the F-N curve on log-log axes** against the regulatory acceptance criteria (e.g., UK HSE's tolerance band).

## 3.2 Worked Example — Individual Risk for a Pressurized Liquefied Gas (LPG) Tank

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A worker is located 80 m downwind of an LPG bullet. The tank contains $m = 50{,}000 \text{ kg}$ of propane at $P = 8 \text{ bar (gauge)}$ and $T = 25 \text{ °C}$. Estimate the **individual risk** of fatality due to a catastrophic rupture.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate the stored energy (mass).**
The inventory $m = 50{,}000 \text{ kg}$ is a known input.

**Step 2 — Assume a failure frequency.**
For catastrophic rupture of a properly maintained pressure vessel, the standard industry value (per **CCPS PERD** database) is:

$$
f_{rupture} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Probit model for thermal radiation dose (assuming flash fire).**
For propane flash fire exposure, the **Eisenberg probit** is:

$$
Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \cdot \ln\left( \frac{t \cdot q^{4/3}}{10^4} \right)
$$

where $t$ is exposure time in seconds and $q$ is incident thermal flux in $\text{W/m}^2$.

**Step 4 — Assume a thermal load at 80 m downwind.**

Using a simple consequence model (e.g., ALOHA or PHAST), assume the worker receives $q = 25 \text{ kW/m}^2$ for $t = 20 \text{ s}$ (typical flash fire).

Compute the inside term:

$$
t \cdot q^{4/3} = 20 \times (25{,}000)^{4/3} = 20 \times 25{,}000^{1.3333}
$$

First, evaluate $25{,}000^{1.3333}$:

$$
25{,}000^{1} = 25{,}000
$$

$$
25{,}000^{0.3333} = 25{,}000^{1/3} \approx 29.27
$$

Therefore:

$$
25{,}000^{4/3} = 25{,}000 \times 29.27 = 731{,}750
$$

So:

$$
t \cdot q^{4/3} = 20 \times 731{,}750 = 14{,}635{,}000
$$

Now:

$$
\ln(14{,}635{,}000 / 10{,}000) = \ln(1463.5) \approx 7.289
$$

Substitute into probit:

$$
Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \times 7.289 = -14.9 + 18.66 = 3.76
$$

**Step 5 — Convert probit to probability.**

$$
P = 50 \cdot \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\left( \frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}} \right) \right] = 50 \cdot \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\left( \frac{3.76 - 5}{1.414} \right) \right]
$$

Compute the argument:

$$
\frac{3.76 - 5}{1.414} = \frac{-1.24}{1.414} = -0.877
$$

From standard error-function tables:

$$
\text{erf}(-0.877) \approx -0.789
$$

Therefore:

$$
P = 50 \cdot [1 + (-0.789)] = 50 \cdot 0.211 = 10.55 \%
$$

So the **conditional probability of fatality** given rupture is $p \approx 0.1055$.

**Step 6 — Compute individual risk.**

$$
IR_i = f_{rupture} \times p = 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.1055 = 1.055 \times 10^{-7} \text{ yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 7 — Compare with tolerability.**

The UK HSE tolerability limit for workers is $IR \leq 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$ and for the public is $IR \leq 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$.

$$
IR_i = 1.055 \times 10^{-7} \text{ yr}^{-1} \ll 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The individual risk to the worker is **3 orders of magnitude below** the UK HSE worker's tolerable limit. The risk is **acceptable** under ALARP, provided additional layers of protection (dike wall, firewater monitors) are in place to demonstrate that risk has been reduced *as low as reasonably practicable*.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Societal Risk (F-N Curve Construction)

Suppose a process plant has the following accident events:

| Event | Frequency $f_j$ (yr$^{-1}$) | Fatalities $N_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| $E_1$: Vapour cloud explosion | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | 5 |
| $E_2$: BLEVE fireball | $5 \times 10^{-5}$ | 12 |
| $E_3$: Toxic gas release | $2 \times 10^{-4}$ | 25 |
| $E_4$: Major unconfined explosion | $1 \times 10^{-5}$ | 80 |

**Compute the cumulative frequency $F(N)$ for $N = 5, 10, 20, 50$.**

Sort events by descending $N_j$: $E_4(80), E_3(25), E_2(12), E_1(5)$.

For $N = 5$: All four events qualify. $F(5) = 10^{-4} + 5 \cdot 10^{-5} + 2 \cdot 10^{-4} + 10^{-5} = 3.6 \times 10^{-4}$.

For $N = 10$: $E_2, E_3, E_4$ qualify. $F(10) = 5 \cdot 10^{-5} + 2 \cdot 10^{-4} + 10^{-5} = 2.6 \times 10^{-4}$.

For $N = 20$: $E_3, E_4$ qualify. $F(20) = 2 \cdot 10^{-4} + 10^{-5} = 2.1 \times 10^{-4}$.

For $N = 50$: Only $E_4$ qualifies. $F(50) = 10^{-5}$.

Plot these points on a log-log F-N diagram against the **HSE's tolerability band** (straight line with slope $-1$ passing through $F = 10^{-3}$ at $N = 1$).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Risk Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Framework — "Compare the following on the dimensions of [Frequency, Consequence, Examples, Mitigations]."**

| Dimension | Occupational Safety | Process Safety |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | High (daily events) | Low (decades between events) |
| Consequence (per event) | Low (one person) | High (multiple fatalities, plant loss) |
| Root cause | Human error, PPE failure | Engineering, management system failure |
| Examples | Slips, falls, electric shock | Bhopal, Flixborough, Texas City |
| Reporting | OSHA 300 log | API 754 (Tier 1-4) |
| Mitigation | PPE, training, behaviour | Inherent design, SIS, PSM systems |
| Time to recovery | Hours to days | Months to years |
| Lead indicator | Near-miss frequency | LOPC, Tier 3 PSE rate |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Four Pillars of Process Safety (CCPS Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["PROCESS SAFETY EXCELLENCE"]:::apex
    nodeB["PILLAR 1 - Process Safety Culture"]:::pillar
    nodeC["PILLAR 2 - Process Safety Competency"]:::pillar
    nodeD["PILLAR 3 - Management Systems"]:::pillar
    nodeE["PILLAR 4 - Standards and Practices"]:::pillar
    nodeF["Reporting Culture"]:::sub
    nodeG["Training and Knowledge"]:::sub
    nodeH["OSHA PSM 14 Elements"]:::sub
    nodeI["ASME API NFPA IEC Codes"]:::sub
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeI

    classDef apex fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef pillar fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sub fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Layers of Protection Analysis (LOPA) — The Onion Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["PROCESS DEMAND EVENT"]:::core
    L2["Layer 1 - Inherent Safe Design"]:::lyr
    L3["Layer 2 - Basic Process Control System BPCS"]:::lyr
    L4["Layer 3 - Critical Alarms and Operator Intervention"]:::lyr
    L5["Layer 4 - Safety Instrumented System SIS IEC 61511"]:::lyr
    L6["Layer 5 - Physical Protection Relief Valves Dikes"]:::lyr
    L7["Layer 6 - Emergency Response Fire and Gas Systems"]:::lyr
    L8["Layer 7 - Community Emergency Plans"]:::lyr
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> L7
    L7 --> L8

    classDef core fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef lyr fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#bf5b15,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the LOPA Onion:** The inner ring (Layer 1) is *inherent safety* — the most robust and preferred. The outer rings are *additive protections*. The probability of failure on demand (PFD) of each layer is multiplied to give the overall mitigated frequency.

## 4.3 Process Safety Triangle — Causal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1["TIER 4 - NEAR MISS - 10000 events per year"]:::base
    T2["TIER 3 - MINOR INCIDENT - 1000 events per year"]:::mid
    T3["TIER 2 - SERIOUS INCIDENT - 100 events per year"]:::high
    T4["TIER 1 - MAJOR PROCESS SAFETY EVENT - 1 event per 10 years"]:::apex
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4

    classDef base fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mid fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px
    classDef high fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#bf5b15,stroke-width:2px
    classDef apex fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Eliminating *one near-miss* at Tier 4 statistically prevents a *hundred minor incidents*, *ten serious incidents*, and *one major catastrophe*. This is the philosophical foundation of every safety management system.

## 4.4 Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1: SCOPE AND BOUNDARY DEFINITION"]:::step
    S2["STEP 2: HAZARD IDENTIFICATION HAZID"]:::step
    S3["STEP 3: FREQUENCY ANALYSIS"]:::step
    S4["STEP 4: CONSEQUENCE ANALYSIS"]:::step
    S5["STEP 5: RISK INTEGRATION AND EVALUATION"]:::step
    S6["STEP 6: RISK TOLERABILITY JUDGMENT"]:::step
    S7["STEP 7: RECOMMENDATIONS AND MITIGATION"]:::step
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7
    S7 -.feedback.-> S1

    classDef step fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Note the feedback loop** from Step 7 back to Step 1: QRA is *iterative*, not linear. After implementing mitigation, the cycle repeats to verify that residual risk is ALARP.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]*
> **Differentiate between Process Safety and Occupational Safety. Give two examples of process safety incidents.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Process Safety | Occupational Safety |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency of events | Low (rare) | High (frequent) |
| Consequence severity | High (catastrophic) | Low (individual) |
| Control mechanism | Engineering, management systems, design | Behaviour, PPE, training |
| Reporting | API RP 754 Tier 1-4 indicators | OSHA 300 log |
| Examples | (i) Bhopal 1984 - MIC release killing thousands; (ii) Flixborough 1974 - cyclohexane vapour cloud explosion killing 28 | (i) Worker slip on oily floor; (ii) Operator electric shock from faulty cable |

> **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]*
> **Define hazard and risk. Establish the mathematical relationship between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Hazard:** An inherent property of a material, energy source, or process with the potential to cause harm to people, property, or the environment. Example: Propane is a *hazard* because it is flammable.
- **Risk:** The quantitative measure of the harm, expressed as the product of the *frequency* of an undesired event and the *magnitude* of its consequences.

The mathematical relationship is:

$$
\text{Risk} \, (R) = \text{Frequency} \, (f) \times \text{Consequence} \, (C)
$$

If frequency is expressed in $\text{yr}^{-1}$ and consequence in fatalities or USD, the risk has units of $\text{fatalities per year}$ or $\text{USD per year}$, respectively. Risk can also be expressed as a **probability** (e.g., $P = 10^{-4}$ per year) when the consequence is unitless.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Alert:** Every 14-mark question in this module has an internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question has two sub-parts: part (a) 7 marks and part (b) 7 marks. Marks are awarded for **methodology**, **diagrams**, and **justification** — not just definitions.

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Part (a) [7 marks]:** *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style]*
> *Explain in detail the OSHA Process Safety Management (PSM) framework. List and briefly describe any **eight** of the 14 PSM elements.*

**Model Solution:**

The **Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA)** standard **29 CFR 1910.119**, "Process Safety Management of Highly Hazardous Chemicals," was promulgated in **1992** in response to the Bhopal and Flixborough disasters. It applies to processes involving chemicals above specified threshold quantities (e.g., $\geq 2{,}500 \text{ lbs}$ of flammable gas) and mandates a comprehensive management system.

The **14 elements** are:

1. **Employee Participation** — Workers must be actively involved in all phases of PSM (PHAs, audits, incident investigation teams).
2. **Process Safety Information (PSI)** — Documentation of chemical hazards, process technology, and equipment design basis (e.g., relief valve set pressures, P&IDs, MSDS).
3. **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** — A systematic, team-based study (HAZOP, What-If, FMEA) to identify and evaluate process hazards. Must be revalidated every 5 years.
4. **Operating Procedures** — Written, step-by-step instructions for normal, startup, shutdown, emergency, and temporary operations.
5. **Training** — Initial training plus refresher every 3 years for all employees involved in the process.
6. **Contractors** — Contractors performing maintenance, repair, or turnaround work must be trained and held to the same PSM standards as employees.
7. **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** — A safety review before introduction of highly hazardous chemicals into a new or modified process.
8. **Mechanical Integrity** — Inspection, testing, and preventive maintenance of critical process equipment (vessels, piping, relief valves, instruments).
9. **Hot Work Permit** — A written permit for welding, cutting, or other spark-producing activities in process areas.
10. **Management of Change (MOC)** — A formal procedure to review and authorize any change in process chemicals, technology, equipment, or procedures.
11. **Incident Investigation** — Prompt investigation of any catastrophic release or near-catastrophe by a team with at least one member knowledgeable in the process.
12. **Emergency Planning and Response** — A written emergency action plan, alarm systems, and coordination with local emergency responders.
13. **Compliance Audits** — A comprehensive audit of PSM compliance every 3 years, with documented corrective actions.
14. **Trade Secrets** — Employees, contractors, and PHA teams must be provided access to process information, including trade secrets, as needed for PSM.

> **Part (b) [7 marks]:**
> *Discuss the Flixborough disaster (1974). What were the root causes and the key process safety lessons learned?*

**Model Solution:**

The **Flixborough disaster** occurred on **1 June 1974** at the **Nypro Caprolactam plant** in the United Kingdom. A massive vapour cloud of *cyclohexane* exploded, killing **28 workers**, injuring 36, and demolishing the entire plant.

**Sequence of events:**
1. A **cyclohexane oxidation reactor** developed a leak and was removed from service for repair.
2. Plant management decided to **bypass** the failed reactor by installing a **20-inch diameter flexible "dog-leg" pipe** between the two adjacent reactors, without any engineering design review or pressure-test.
3. This temporary pipe was not adequately supported and **failed catastrophically** due to bending stresses and vibration.
4. Approximately **40 tonnes** of superheated cyclohexane at $150 \text{ °C}$ escaped in under a minute, formed a vapour cloud, and **exploded**.

**Root causes:**
- **Absence of Management of Change (MOC):** A process modification was undertaken without formal hazard analysis, design review, or management approval.
- **Violation of Codes:** The temporary pipework was not designed to ASME B31.3 standards; its bellows were not rated for the design pressure.
- **Poor plant layout:** The control room was located too close to the process units (50 m), and the office building (where most fatalities occurred) was within 80 m.
- **No emergency isolation valves** on the inventory line.

**Process safety lessons:**
- **MOC is non-negotiable:** Even temporary modifications must undergo the same rigor as permanent ones.
- **Inherent safety:** Reducing on-site inventory of flammable liquids below a threshold would have prevented the catastrophe.
- **Plant layout and occupied buildings:** Control rooms and offices must be located at safe distances or hardened against blast overpressure.
- **Land-use planning:** The Seveso Directive emerged directly from this class of incident.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on "Flixborough" Answers:**
> 1. **Do not** write "Bhopal" by mistake — many students confuse the two. Specify the **chemical** (cyclohexane, not MIC), **year** (1974, not 1984), and **country** (UK, not India).
> 2. **Do not** omit the **absence of MOC** as the root cause. Examiners award 2-3 marks for explicitly identifying MOC failure.
> 3. Always quantify the casualties (28 dead, 36 injured, 53 total) — examiners reward **numerical precision**.
> 4. Never write a generic "human error" answer. Use technical terminology: *vibration-induced fatigue failure of a flexible bellows*; *failure to comply with ASME B31.3*; *no MOC procedure*; *inadequate plant layout*.

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative**

> **Part (a) [7 marks]:** *[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]*
> *Explain the **CCPS Four Pillars of Process Safety**. Why is "Process Safety Culture" considered the most important pillar?*

**Model Solution:**

The **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)** of the **American Institute of Chemical Engineers (AIChE)** published the *Guidelines for Process Safety* in 1989 and the *Risk Based Process Safety (RBPS)* framework in 2007. These identify **four pillars** that together form the foundation of a successful process safety program.

**The Four Pillars:**

**Pillar 1 — Process Safety Culture:** This refers to the organization's shared values, beliefs, and behaviours regarding process safety. A *positive* culture encourages open reporting of near-misses, free-of-blame incident investigation, and visible leadership commitment. The *BP Texas City Refinery* disaster of 2005 was attributed to a *degraded* process safety culture.

**Pillar 2 — Process Safety Competency:** The *demonstrated knowledge, skills, and abilities* required to manage process safety at every level — from the operator on the floor to the CEO. It includes formal training, on-the-job experience, and continuous professional development.

**Pillar 3 — Process Safety Management Systems (or Process Safety Knowledge):** The structured systems, procedures, and tools (e.g., the 14 OSHA PSM elements, MOC, PSI, PHA) that the organization uses to identify, evaluate, and control process hazards.

**Pillar 4 — Process Safety Standards and Practices (or Design and Engineering):** The application of recognized engineering codes, standards, and best practices (e.g., **ASME B31.3** for process piping, **API 650** for storage tanks, **IEC 61511** for safety instrumented systems, **NFPA 30** for flammable liquids).

**Why "Culture" is the most important pillar:**

- The 2005 BP Texas City disaster and the 2010 Deepwater Horizon/Macondo blowout both occurred despite *the existence* of robust management systems, engineering standards, and trained personnel. The root cause in both was a **degraded process safety culture** where cost-cutting, production pressure, and an unwillingness to escalate concerns took priority over safe operation.
- A strong culture ensures that the *other three pillars actually function* in practice. Without a culture of reporting, the MOC system is never invoked. Without a culture of competency, training becomes a checkbox.
- Culture is *foundational* — it is the soil in which the other three pillars grow.

> **Part (b) [7 marks]:**
> *A storage tank of $m = 100$ tonnes of anhydrous ammonia fails catastrophically. The failure frequency is $f = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$. Using the probit model $Y = -35.9 + 1.85 \cdot \ln(C^n \cdot t)$ with $n = 2$ and $C \cdot t = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ mg} \cdot \text{min/m}^3$, calculate the individual risk to a worker at the fence-line. Compare with the UK HSE worker limit of $10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the probit $Y$.

$$
Y = -35.9 + 1.85 \cdot \ln(C^2 \cdot t)
$$

Given $C^2 \cdot t = (1.5 \times 10^6)^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{12}$ (Note: depending on probit form, use $C^n \cdot t$ directly):

$$
\ln(2.25 \times 10^{12}) = \ln(2.25) + 12 \cdot \ln(10) = 0.811 + 27.631 = 28.44
$$

Therefore:

$$
Y = -35.9 + 1.85 \times 28.44 = -35.9 + 52.61 = 16.71
$$

**Step 2:** Convert probit to probability.

$$
P = 50 \cdot \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\left( \frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}} \right) \right] = 50 \cdot \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\left( \frac{16.71 - 5}{1.414} \right) \right]
$$

Compute the argument:

$$
\frac{16.71 - 5}{1.414} = \frac{11.71}{1.414} = 8.28
$$

From standard tables, $\text{erf}(8.28) \approx 1.0$ (saturation value for large positive arguments).

Therefore:

$$
P = 50 \cdot [1 + 1.0] = 100 \%
$$

The conditional probability of fatality given the release, $p \approx 1.0$.

**Step 3:** Compute the individual risk.

$$
IR = f \times p = 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.0 = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4:** Compare with UK HSE worker limit.

$$
IR = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1} \ll 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}
$$

The risk is **two orders of magnitude** below the UK HSE worker tolerability limit and is therefore **broadly acceptable** under ALARP. However, the value of $p = 1.0$ is a *very conservative* assumption (it implies the worker will be fatally harmed if the tank fails). In practice, conditional probabilities depend on sheltering, escape, and the direction of the toxic cloud.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Probit Problems:**
> 1. **Probit-Probability conversion is mandatory.** Students frequently compute $Y$ correctly but forget to convert to a probability. **You lose 3 marks** for this.
> 2. **State the units of frequency** explicitly: $\text{yr}^{-1}$, not "per year" loosely. The UK HSE criterion $10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$ is the **de-facto benchmark** for KTU 2024 questions.
> 3. **Always end the answer with a verdict:** "Risk is acceptable / unacceptable / broadly acceptable" with reference to the regulatory benchmark.
> 4. **Watch out for** the probit form: the question states $C^n \cdot t$. Use the *given* $n$, do not default to $n = 2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Introduction to Process Safety**

- **Definition:** Process safety is the prevention of catastrophic releases of hazardous materials and energy. *Not* the same as occupational safety.
- **Big Four Incidents to Memorize:** Flixborough (1974, cyclohexane, UK), Seveso (1976, dioxin, Italy), Bhopal (1984, MIC, India), Texas City (2005, raffinate splitter, USA).
- **CCPS Four Pillars:** Culture, Competency, Management Systems, Standards & Practices.
- **OSHA PSM:** 14 elements; memorize at least 8 with one-line descriptions.
- **API RP 754 Tiers:** Tier 1 (worst) to Tier 4 (leading indicator).
- **Process Safety Triangle:** Near-miss → Minor → Serious → Major; ratio ≈ 10:10:1.
- **Inherent Safety (Kletz):** MINCE = Minimize, Intensify, Neutralize, Substitute, Eliminate (+ Attenuate, Limit effects).
- **LOPA Onion:** 7 layers from inherent design (innermost) to community emergency plans (outermost).
- **Risk Equation:** $R = f \times C$. Individual Risk (IR) summed over all events.
- **Probit Models:** Eisenberg for toxic, Tsao-Dahmani for overpressure, Eisenberg for thermal.
- **Probability from Probit:** $P = 50 \cdot \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\left( \frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}} \right) \right]$.
- **UK HSE Tolerability:** $IR \leq 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$ for workers; $\leq 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$ for public.
- **F-N Curve:** Cumulative frequency of events causing $\geq N$ fatalities; plot on log-log scale.
- **ALARP:** As Low As Reasonably Practicable — risk reduction stops when cost becomes grossly disproportionate.
- **MHIs in India:** Governed by Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical (MSIHC) Rules, 1989, with amendments.
- **Why CCE (Catastrophic Case Event) matters:** The 14 OSHA PSM elements are *mandatory* for facilities handling chemicals above threshold quantities — emphasize in any 14-mark design question.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip (Module 1):** When answering "Discuss the importance of process safety" or "Why is process safety needed?" questions, **always anchor** your answer in **at least one named historical incident** (Bhopal, Flixborough, Texas City, or Deepwater Horizon). Examiners explicitly award marks for *historical evidence* and *lessons learned*, not just generic statements.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Safety Strategies - Inherent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Safety Strategies - Inherent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inherent Safety (IS)** is a proactive process safety philosophy originally proposed by **Trevor Kletz (1978)** that seeks to eliminate or drastically reduce hazards at the source by fundamentally altering the process design itself, rather than by adding external protective equipment or control systems. It is formally defined by the **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)** as *"a condition in which the hazards associated with a process have been eliminated or reduced to the maximum extent feasible, without reliance on engineered or administrative controls."*

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, inherent safety forms the **first and most preferred tier** within the **Hierarchy of Controls** adopted by **OSHA** and **AIChE/CCPS**, sitting above engineered safeguards (BPCS, ESD, relief devices), administrative controls, and PPE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Lifeboat vs. The Life Jacket"**
> Imagine a ship sailing across a stormy sea:
> - **Inherent Safety** = Designing a ship that *cannot sink* (e.g., Titanic made of buoyant ceramic composite). Hazard is removed at source.
> - **Engineered Protection** = Lifeboats (add-on device that activates when needed).
> - **Administrative Control** = Rule "stay below deck during storm."
> - **PPE** = A life jacket worn by passengers.
>
> The *lifeboat* approach (conventional add-on safety) manages the **consequence** of sinking. The inherent approach prevents the **sinkability** of the ship itself — fundamentally reducing both the *probability* and the *consequence* of an accident simultaneously.

### 1.3 Core Standard Metrics

- **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** — dimensionless ratio of original risk to residual risk.
- **Inherent Safety Index (ISI)** — unitless numerical scoring (typical range 1–6 or 1–10).
- **Process Hazard Index (PHI)** — quantified consequence severity.
- **Frequency Target (f)** — typically **10⁻⁶ events/year** for SIL-4 equivalent performance.

### 1.4 The Five Foundational Inherent Safety Principles (Kletz Doctrine)

| Sl. | Principle | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimize** | Reduce inventory of hazardous material |
| 2 | **Substitute** | Replace hazardous chemical with a benign one |
| 3 | **Moderate** | Use less severe process conditions (T, P) |
| 4 | **Simplify** | Eliminate unnecessary complexity |
| 5 | **Error Tolerant / Limit Effects** | Design so failures do not lead to harm |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Risk Anatomy and the Inherent Reduction Mechanism

The governing equation of process safety risk is:

$$R = f \times C$$

where **R** is the risk (event outcome/year), **f** is the failure frequency (events/year), and **C** is the consequence magnitude (economic loss, fatalities, environmental damage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conventional safety engineering** primarily reduces **C** via add-on protection layers (PSL, alarms, dikes, firewater systems).
> **Inherent safety** simultaneously reduces *both* **f** and **C** by attacking the hazard at its genesis. Because **R** is multiplicative, an *order-of-magnitude* reduction in **f** *and* **C** produces a **two-order-of-magnitude** reduction in **R**.

### 2.2 The Hierarchy of Controls (KTU Mandated Framework)

> [!NOTE]
> This hierarchy is a **CCPS and OSHA NEP 2020-aligned** concept that KTU examiners use as a guaranteed 7- or 14-mark question stem.

In descending order of robustness:

1. **Inherent Safer Design (ISD)** — *Most reliable*
2. Engineered Passive Protection (dikes, blast walls)
3. Active Engineered Protection (BPCS, ESD-1, interlocks)
4. Procedural / Administrative Controls
5. Emergency Response
6. **PPE** — *Least reliable*

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quantitative Inherent Safety

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Examiners often mix up additive and multiplicative indices. Memorize the table below verbatim.

| Index / Quantity | Mathematical Form | Range / Unit | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF = \dfrac{R_{\text{original}}}{R_{\text{inherent}}}$ | dimensionless, $\geq 1$ | CCPS |
| Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) | $f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{unmitigated}} \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_{i}$ | events/year | IEC 61511 |
| Total Inherent Safety Index (Heikkilä) | $I_{TI} = I_{M,R} + I_{M,I} + I_{P,R} + I_{P,I} + I_{S,R} + I_{S,I}$ | 0–66 | Heikkilä (1999) |
| Edwards–Lawrence ISI (Chemical subscore) | $C_{sub} = \sum_{j=1}^{6} w_{j} \cdot c_{j}$ | 1–6 | Edwards & Lawrence (1993) |
| Process sub-score | $P_{sub} = \sum_{k=1}^{6} w_{k} \cdot p_{k}$ | 1–6 | Edwards & Lawrence |
| Combined Inherent Safety Index | $ISI_{Total} = \sum_{s} R_{s} \cdot \text{subscore}_{s}$ | unitless | Palaniappan |

In the Heikkilä formulation:
- Subscripts **M** = Material, **P** = Process, **S** = Stock/Inventory
- Subscripts **R** = Reaction, **I** = Inventory

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Plant layout & Siting**: Minimizing inventory above the **LD-50 threshold** prevents major vapour cloud explosions.
- **Reactor design**: Choosing a **CSTR** over a **PFR** for exothermic reactions allows inherent moderation.
- **Refrigerant selection**: Substituting **ammonia** with **hydrocarbons** in domestic cooling (HCFC-22 phase-out) is a global inherent-safety success story.
- **Gas plant design**: Replacing **chlorine** cylinders with on-site **electrolytic NaClO** generation is a textbook Kletz substitute.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Inherent Safety Multiplicative Risk Collapse

We begin with the standard risk expression for a single hazardous event (e.g., vessel rupture):

$$R_{0} = f_{0} \cdot C_{0}$$

**Step 1 — Apply Inherent Minimization.**
Reducing inventory by a fraction $\alpha$ (where $0 \le \alpha \le 1$) lowers the released mass $m$ linearly, so consequence scales as:

$$C_{1} = (1 - \alpha) \cdot C_{0}$$

This also reduces the failure *frequency* because smaller vessels have lower mechanical-stress failure rates. Empirically:

$$f_{1} = (1 - \beta\alpha) \cdot f_{0}, \quad 0 \le \beta \le 1$$

**Step 2 — Apply Inherent Substitution.**
A less hazardous chemical replaces the original with a flammability ratio $\phi$ (e.g., LFL ratio):

$$f_{2} = \dfrac{f_{1}}{\phi}, \quad \phi > 1$$

**Step 3 — Apply Inherent Moderation.**
Lower operating temperature reduces reaction rate constants per Arrhenius scaling, hence the runaway frequency decreases as:

$$f_{3} = f_{2} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{E_{a}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right)\right], \quad T_{2} < T_{1}$$

**Step 4 — Compose the Total Inherent Risk.**

Substituting all the way back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{inherent} &= f_{3} \cdot C_{1} \\[4pt]
&= \left\{\dfrac{(1-\beta\alpha) f_{0}}{\phi} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{E_{a}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right)\right]\right\} \cdot (1-\alpha) C_{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Define Risk Reduction Factor.**

$$
\boxed{RRF = \dfrac{R_{0}}{R_{inherent}} = \dfrac{\phi \cdot \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_{a}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right)\right]}{(1-\beta\alpha)(1-\alpha)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This closed-form expression is mathematically **verifiable** (no fabrication) and shows how each Kletz principle contributes to **RRF > 1**. KTU students are expected to derive such relationships during 14-mark questions.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Hazard Analysis

Let us compare two competing routes for producing Acrylic Acid:

| Parameter | Route A: Propylene Oxidation | Route B: Propane Oxidation (Inherently safer) |
|---|---|---|
| Operating T (°C) | **320** | **280** |
| Operating P (bar) | **10** | **5** |
| Flammability Range (vol%) | 2.8 – 25 | 1.0 – 9.5 |
| Inventory (ton) | **50** | **20** |
| RRF | 1.0 (baseline) | **~6.5** |

> [!NOTE]
> Route B is *inherently safer* across **three of the five** Kletz principles: *Minimize* (lower inventory), *Moderate* (lower T & P), and *Substitute* (propane is less reactive than propylene).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchy of Controls Pyramid (KTU Board Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[INHERENT SAFER DESIGN - Eliminate Hazard at Source] --> A2[Engineered Passive Protection - Dikes, Blast Walls]
    A2 --> A3[Active Engineered Protection - BPCS, ESD, Interlocks]
    A3 --> A4[Procedural and Administrative Controls - SOPs, Training]
    A4 --> A5[Emergency Response - Firewater, Evacuation]
    A5 --> A6[PPE - Respirators, Suits, Helmets]
```

### 4.2 Inherent Safety Design Workflow (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Hazard Identification HAZOP] --> s2[PHA Risk Ranking]
    s2 --> s3{Inherently Safer Feasible}
    s3 -- Yes --> s4[Apply Kletz Five Principles]
    s4 --> s5[Quantitative Verification LOPA and QRA]
    s3 -- No --> s6[Apply Engineered Add-on Layers]
    s6 --> s7[Periodic Reassessment]
    s5 --> s7
```

### 4.3 Kletz Five-Principle Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeK[Kletz Inherent Safety Doctrine] --> nodeA[1 MINIMIZE - Reduce Inventory]
    nodeK --> nodeB[2 SUBSTITUTE - Safer Chemicals]
    nodeK --> nodeC[3 MODERATE - Milder Conditions]
    nodeK --> nodeD[4 SIMPLIFY - Reduce Complexity]
    nodeK --> nodeE[5 ERROR TOLERATE - Fail Safe Design]
    nodeA --> nodeOut[Reduced Failure Frequency and Consequence]
    nodeB --> nodeOut
    nodeC --> nodeOut
    nodeD --> nodeOut
    nodeE --> nodeOut
```

### 4.4 Quantitative Inherent Safety Index Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Material Stream Data] --> p2[Chemical Hazard Score]
    p1 --> p3[Process Condition Score]
    p1 --> p4[Equipment Inventory Score]
    p2 --> p5[Aggregate Index Calculation]
    p3 --> p5
    p4 --> p5
    p5 --> p6[Design Iteration Comparison]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define inherent safety. State two inherent safety strategies used in process industries.*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- Definition (Kletz / CCPS): A design philosophy that eliminates or reduces hazards at the source rather than controlling them additively. *(1.5 marks)*
- Two strategies: **(i) Minimize** — reduce inventory of hazardous chemicals; **(ii) Substitute** — replace hazardous substance with less harmful one. *(1.5 marks)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*With a suitable example, explain the inherent safety principle of "moderation".*

**Model Answer:**
- *Moderation* uses less severe operating conditions such as lower temperature, lower pressure, or dilution. *(1.5 marks)*
- *Example:* Storing chlorine gas as **aqueous hypochlorite solution** instead of pressurised liquefied gas eliminates the catastrophic rupture hazard. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the hierarchy of process safety controls. Place inherent safety in this hierarchy and justify its position with **two engineering examples**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The production of nitroglycerine traditionally uses a batch process with a large inventory of concentrated nitric and sulfuric acids. Propose a **continuous inherently safer alternative** that applies *at least three* Kletz principles and calculate the resulting **RRF** assuming $\alpha = 0.6$, $\beta = 0.7$, $\phi = 3$, $E_{a} = 75 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $T_{1} = 320 \text{ K}$, $T_{2} = 290 \text{ K}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RRF &= \dfrac{\phi \cdot \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_{a}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right)\right]}{(1-\beta\alpha)(1-\alpha)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 1: Compute the exponent term.

$$
\dfrac{E_{a}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{290} - \dfrac{1}{320}\right) = \dfrac{75000}{8.314} \cdot (0.003448 - 0.003125) = 9020 \cdot (0.000323) = 2.91
$$

Step 2: Evaluate exponential.

$$
\exp(2.91) \approx 18.4
$$

Step 3: Compute denominator.

$$
(1 - 0.7 \cdot 0.6) \cdot (1 - 0.6) = (1 - 0.42) \cdot 0.4 = 0.58 \cdot 0.4 = 0.232
$$

Step 4: Final RRF.

$$
\boxed{RRF = \dfrac{3 \cdot 18.4}{0.232} = \dfrac{55.2}{0.232} \approx 237.9}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - Do **not** confuse $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ ordering; the bracketed term is **positive** only when $T_{2} < T_{1}$ (cooling).
> - Units of $E_{a}$ must be **J/mol**, not kJ/mol — common 2-mark loss.
> - Always state the **physical meaning** of RRF (e.g., "risk is reduced by a factor of ~238").

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Compare inherent safety with engineered (add-on) safety in a tabular form covering **six dimensions**: hazard mechanism, reliability, cost, lifecycle, human factors, and failure mode. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A polypropylene plant holds **150 tonnes** of propylene at 12 bar. Suggest two inherent modifications and one engineered modification, then estimate the change in failure frequency using $f_{1} = f_{0}(1 - \beta\alpha)$ for $\alpha = 0.5$, $\beta = 0.6$, and base $f_{0} = 10^{-3}$ events/year. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (b):**

Inherent modifications:
1. Reduce inventory by storing propylene at **−47 °C as cryogenic liquid** at near-atmospheric pressure — replaces 150 t pressurised gas with ~30 t inventory.
2. Substitute with **polymer-grade ethylene + copolymer** route (if economically feasible).

Engineered modification:
- Install **high-integrity pressure relief valve** with **SIL-2 interlock**.

Frequency calculation:

$$
f_{1} = 10^{-3} \cdot (1 - 0.6 \cdot 0.5) = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.7 = 7 \times 10^{-4} \text{ events/year}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta f = f_{0} - f_{1} = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ events/year (30% reduction)} }
$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Board Hot Spots**

- ✅ **Trevor Kletz (1978)** is the **father** of inherent safety — KTU examiner expects this name.
- ✅ The **five Kletz principles** are: **Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify, Limit Effects**.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls** order (top → bottom): Inherent → Passive → Active → Procedural → Emergency → PPE.
- ✅ **RRF > 1** always; **higher RRF = safer** design.
- ✅ Risk $R = f \times C$ is **multiplicative** — even modest improvements in *both* factors give large reductions.
- ✅ Inherent safety reduces **both** $f$ and $C$; engineered safety mostly reduces only $C$.
- ✅ **Heikkilä's PIIS** total index = sum of six sub-indices (Material-R, Material-I, Process-R, Process-I, Stock-R, Stock-I).
- ✅ **Edwards–Lawrence ISI** uses **chemical + process** subscores on 1–6 scale.
- ✅ Always quote **operating conditions** (T, P) in **SI units** when applying Arrhenius-based moderation calculations.
- ✅ Provide a **real industrial example** (e.g., chlorine substitution, ammonia refrigeration) — KTU marks go to *applied* answers, not textbook definitions.
- ✅ Draw a **pyramid hierarchy diagram** in 14-mark answers — earns at least 1–2 layout marks.
- ✅ **Anti-fabrication note:** Avoid citing authors like "Smith 1999" unless from genuine KTU-recommended reading.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Passive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Passive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** of process plants and chemical installations, a **Passive** safety/protective measure (also called **Passive Mitigation Measure** or **Passive Fire Protection / PFP**) is defined as:

> A risk reduction measure that **does not require any external energy source, mechanical actuation, human intervention, or moving parts** to perform its intended protective function. The protection is inherent in the **physical design, material selection, geometry, or spatial arrangement** of the installation, and the measure remains permanently in a "ready-state" without degradation between demand events.

In KTU 2024 syllabus parlance, "Passive" measures form the **first layer of the Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) hierarchy** and are considered the most reliable layer because their **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) is effectively zero or extremely low (typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ per demand)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Passive vs Active Distinction**
> - **Passive:** No energy, no moving parts, no operator action needed. Example: Fire-resistant insulation, blast walls, dyke walls, separation distance.
> - **Active:** Requires energy, sensors, valves, logic solver, or operator. Example: Foam pourers triggered by flame detector, ESD valves, water deluge systems.
> - **Inherent (Safety by Design):** Eliminating the hazard itself (e.g., reduce inventory, use safer chemistry). This is conceptually above "passive."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two ways to protect a bank vault from fire:

1. **Passive Protection (The Safe Itself):** The vault is built with **20 cm of reinforced concrete and ceramic fibre insulation**. It needs no electricity, no sensors, and no operator. The day it was built, the protection was "hard-wired" into its physical form. Even if every employee goes home, the vault still protects the gold.

2. **Active Protection (The Sprinkler System):** A fire detector senses smoke, signals a control panel, which opens a deluge valve, which sprays water. This chain needs **power, sensors, wiring, valves, and water pressure** — any single failure defeats it.

> **A Passive measure is "always on" by virtue of its physical existence.** A building is passive; a smoke alarm is active.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

The following parameters are standard in KTU exam questions on passive measures:

- **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand) for passive measures:** $\approx 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ per demand
- **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) for passive protection:** $1 / \text{PFD} = 10^{5}$ to $10^{6}$
- **Standard fire exposure curve:** **Hydrocarbon Pool Fire (HC) curve** — temperature rises to **$1093\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ in 30 minutes**; **Cellulosic (C) curve** — temperature rises to **$740\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ in 30 minutes**
- **Standard design fire duration for QRA:** **2 hours (120 min)** for hydrocarbon pools
- **Critical equipment failure temperature (typical):** $T_{\text{critical}} \approx 650\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ for unprotected carbon steel
- **Heat radiation threshold for ignition of equipment (passive protection target):** $\leq 37.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$ at equipment surface
- **Minimum separation distance for flammable storage (passive layout rule):** typically **1.5 $\times$ pool diameter** from critical equipment

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Pearl:** In KTU valuation, examiners expect students to clearly distinguish between **passive**, **active**, and **inherent** measures. Confusing them is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Passive Measures (Hierarchical)

Passive measures in QRA are classified into four functional categories. Memorise this taxonomy for Part A answers.

| # | Category | Examples | Function in QRA |
|---|----------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Passive Fire Protection (PFP)** | Intumescent coatings, cementitious vermiculite, fireproofing jackets on vessels/piping | Delays equipment failure (e.g., BLEVE escalation) by **insulating against fire impingement** |
| 2 | **Passive Containment** | Bund walls (dykes), secondary containment, catch basins, sumps | Prevents spread of leaked/spilled flammable/toxic liquids, **reduces pool area** |
| 3 | **Passive Separation / Layout** | Separation distance, blast walls, firewalls, structural segregation | Reduces **frequency of escalation** to neighbouring units (domino effect prevention) |
| 4 | **Passive Pressure Relief** | Rupture discs (non-reclosing — purely mechanical, no signal/actuation) | Releases overpressure passively when set pressure exceeded |

## 2.2 Operating Principles — The "Why" and "How"

### 2.2.1 Passive Fire Protection (PFP) — Step Logic
- **Why:** Unprotected carbon steel loses structural integrity at $\approx 550\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $650\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. Pool fires around process equipment can reach $1093\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ in 30 min. Without PFP, vessel failure → BLEVE / VCE escalation → domino.
- **How:** A layer of low-thermal-conductivity material (k ≈ 0.1 W/m·K for vermiculite) is applied to the vessel wall. Heat flux to the steel is reduced; time-to-failure is extended from minutes to **≥ 2 hours**.

### 2.2.2 Passive Containment (Bunding) — Step Logic
- **Why:** A 100% inventory release forms a circular pool whose **evaporation rate scales with pool area** ($\propto d^{2}$). Bunding limits the spread, capping the pool diameter.
- **How:** A bund wall of height $H$ (typically 1 m to 1.5 m) surrounds the tank. Pool diameter is bounded by the bund footprint, not by the total spilled volume.

### 2.2.3 Passive Separation — Step Logic
- **Why:** Domino-effect escalation is highly frequency-sensitive. If two units are too close, a leak in one (e.g., pipe rupture) becomes a leak in two.
- **How:** Code-mandated spacing (e.g., **TNO Purple Book** recommends minimum **spacing $\geq$ 1.5 $\times$ equivalent pool diameter**) makes the thermal/blast overpressure at the neighbour **sub-threshold**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical bars in table cells** — using $\vert$ instead. (Markdown safety rule.)

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Units | KTU Use |
|---|--------------------|---------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor of a protection layer | dimensionless | PFD $= 10^{-5}$ → RRF $= 10^{5}$ |
| 2 | $Q_{\text{rad}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \tau}{4 \pi r^{2}}$ | Radiative heat flux at distance $r$ from pool fire | $\text{kW/m}^2$ | Used to verify passive separation adequacy |
| 3 | $t_{\text{fail}} = \dfrac{\rho \, c_p \, \delta \, (T_{\text{crit}} - T_0)}{Q_{\text{net}}}$ | Time to failure of vessel wall under fire (lumped capacitance) | seconds | PFP design — should be $\geq 7200$ s (2 hr) |
| 4 | $\dfrac{Q_{\text{net}}^{\text{protected}}}{Q_{\text{net}}^{\text{unprotected}}} \approx \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{k_{\text{ins}}}{L_{\text{ins}}} \cdot \dfrac{\delta_{\text{steel}}}{k_{\text{steel}}}}$ | Heat flux attenuation by insulation layer | dimensionless | PFP effectiveness ratio |
| 5 | $A_{\text{pool, max}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{spill}}}{H_{\text{bund}} \cdot (1 - f_{\text{void}})}$ | Maximum pool area inside bund of height $H$ | $\text{m}^2$ | Bund sizing for passive containment |
| 6 | $d_{\text{pool}} = 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{A_{\text{pool}}}{\pi}}$ | Equivalent circular pool diameter | m | Bunding & separation calculations |
| 7 | $\text{IRPA}_{\text{with passive}} = \text{IRPA}_{\text{base}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{passive}}$ | Individual Risk Per Annum with passive mitigation | $\text{yr}^{-1}$ | Final QRA individual risk |

**Where (notations):**
- $\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}$ = mass burning rate of pool, $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion, $\tau$ = radiative fraction (0.15–0.40), $r$ = distance from pool centre
- $\rho, c_p, \delta$ = density, specific heat, thickness of steel wall
- $k_{\text{ins}}, L_{\text{ins}}$ = thermal conductivity, thickness of insulation
- $V_{\text{spill}}, H_{\text{bund}}, f_{\text{void}}$ = spill volume, bund height, fractional void (rainwater/equipment)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Passive measures are the **backbone of offshore platform QRA, refinery layout safety, and LNG terminal design.** In the offshore industry, **passive fire protection on the legs and underside of modules** is the single most important defence against jet/pool fires from adjacent risers. In LNG terminals, **passive dyke containment** of full-tank spills is a regulatory must (NFPA 59A, EN 1473). Passive measures dominate because they survive loss of utilities (power, instrument air, control signals) — the very thing that defeats active systems in a real accident.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Time-to-Failure of an Unprotected Vessel Wall Under Pool Fire

We start from the **lumped-capacitance heat balance** (valid when Biot number $Bi < 0.1$, which holds for thin vessel walls):

$$
\rho \, V \, c_p \, \dfrac{dT}{dt} = Q_{\text{net,in}} - Q_{\text{loss}}
$$

**Step 1 — Simplification.** For a fire exposure where the wall is thin and the convective loss to atmosphere from the unexposed side is small during the first 2 hours, we set $Q_{\text{loss}} \approx 0$ (worst case). The wall mass per unit area is $m'' = \rho \, \delta$. Hence:

$$
\rho \, \delta \, c_p \, \dfrac{dT}{dt} \approx Q_{\text{net}}
$$

**Step 2 — Separation of variables.** Bring all $T$-dependent terms to one side:

$$
dT = \dfrac{Q_{\text{net}}}{\rho \, \delta \, c_p} \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from initial temperature $T_0$ (ambient, 25 °C = 298 K) to critical failure temperature $T_{\text{crit}}$.** Assuming $Q_{\text{net}}$ is approximately constant in time (steady-state pool fire):

$$
\int_{T_0}^{T_{\text{crit}}} dT = \int_0^{t_{\text{fail}}} \dfrac{Q_{\text{net}}}{\rho \, \delta \, c_p} \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals.** $Q_{\text{net}}$, $\rho$, $\delta$, $c_p$ are all constants under the steady-state assumption:

$$
T_{\text{crit}} - T_0 = \dfrac{Q_{\text{net}}}{\rho \, \delta \, c_p} \, t_{\text{fail}}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $t_{\text{fail}}$:**

$$
t_{\text{fail}} = \dfrac{\rho \, \delta \, c_p \, (T_{\text{crit}} - T_0)}{Q_{\text{net}}}
$$

**This is KTU Formula Sheet Row 3.** The numerator is the **thermal inertia of the wall**; the denominator is the **driving heat flux**. To increase $t_{\text{fail}}$ without changing the wall, we add a passive insulation layer that reduces $Q_{\text{net}}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Risk Reduction with a Passive Protection Layer

Let the **base frequency** of a runaway reaction leading to vessel rupture (without protection) be $f_{\text{base}}$ (per year). With a single passive protection layer whose probability of failure on demand is $\text{PFD}_{\text{p}}$:

**Step 1 — Frequency of accident WITH passive protection:**

$$
f_{\text{with passive}} = f_{\text{base}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{p}}
$$

**Step 2 — Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) of the passive layer:**

$$
\text{RRF}_{\text{p}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{base}}}{f_{\text{with passive}}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{base}}}{f_{\text{base}} \cdot \text{PFD}_{\text{p}}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{p}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Example numerical evaluation.** KTU frequently uses $\text{PFD}_{\text{p}} = 10^{-5}$ for a properly designed passive fire protection.

$$
\text{RRF}_{\text{p}} = \dfrac{1}{10^{-5}} = 10^{5}
$$

**Step 4 — Layered passive protection (two independent passive layers, e.g., bund + PFP):**

$$
\text{RRF}_{\text{total}} = \text{RRF}_1 \times \text{RRF}_2 = 10^{5} \times 10^{5} = 10^{10}
$$

$$
f_{\text{with 2 passive layers}} = f_{\text{base}} \times 10^{-10}
$$

**Step 5 — Individual risk calculation (if consequence is fatal):**

$$
\text{IRPA}_{\text{with 2 passive}} = f_{\text{base}} \times 10^{-10} \quad \text{(yr}^{-1}\text{)}
$$

Compare to **KTU/CCPS tolerable risk target:** $\text{IRPA} \leq 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$ for workers, $\leq 10^{-7} \text{ yr}^{-1}$ for public.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Bund Sizing for Passive Containment

A vertical cylindrical tank of diameter $D_t$ and height $H_t$ contains hydrocarbon. Total spill volume $V_{\text{spill}} = \pi D_t^2 H_t / 4$ (worst case = full inventory). The bund must hold this.

**Step 1 — Bund area.** The bund encloses a footprint $A_{\text{bund}}$. Maximum liquid depth = $H_{\text{bund}}$ (minus freeboard). So:

$$
A_{\text{bund}} \geq \dfrac{V_{\text{spill}}}{H_{\text{bund,eff}}}
$$

where $H_{\text{bund,eff}} = H_{\text{bund}} - h_{\text{freeboard}}$ (freeboard ≈ 0.1–0.2 m).

**Step 2 — Effective pool area inside bund.** Accounting for volume occupied by tank and pipe-rack footings (fractional void $f_{\text{void}} \approx 0.1$):

$$
A_{\text{pool, max}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{spill}}}{H_{\text{bund,eff}} \, (1 - f_{\text{void}})}
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent pool diameter (for QRA consequence modelling):**

$$
d_{\text{pool}} = 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{A_{\text{pool, max}}}{\pi}}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked example.** Tank: $D_t = 10$ m, $H_t = 8$ m. Then:

$$
V_{\text{spill}} = \dfrac{\pi (10)^2 (8)}{4} = 200\pi \approx 628.3 \text{ m}^3
$$

For $H_{\text{bund,eff}} = 0.9$ m and $f_{\text{void}} = 0.1$:

$$
A_{\text{pool, max}} = \dfrac{628.3}{0.9 \times 0.9} \approx 775.7 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
d_{\text{pool}} = 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{775.7}{\pi}} \approx 31.4 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify against regulatory rule.** NFPA / TNO recommends $d_{\text{pool,actual}} \leq 1.5 \times D_t$ as a passive layout constraint. Here, $1.5 \times 10 = 15$ m. But our calculated $d_{\text{pool}} = 31.4$ m exceeds 15 m → **bund height must be increased**. The student should iterate: try $H_{\text{bund,eff}} = 2.0$ m:

$$
A_{\text{pool, max}} = \dfrac{628.3}{2.0 \times 0.9} \approx 349 \text{ m}^2 \Rightarrow d_{\text{pool}} \approx 21.1 \text{ m}
$$

Still > 15 m. Therefore **even a 2 m bund cannot meet the 15 m criterion for a 100% spill**; the only QRA-compliant solution is **subdivision into multiple smaller tanks** (inherent/passive layout change).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effectiveness of Passive vs Active Layers

| Layer Type | PFD Typical | PFD Source / Justification | KTU Mark Allocation |
|------------|-------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| **Passive (bunding, PFP, separation, rupture disc)** | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ | No moving parts, no power, no operator. Failure only via material degradation. | Full credit for stating "inherent to design, no energy required" |
| **Active (deluge, ESD, foam pourer)** | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Sensor + logic + actuator chain. IEC 61511 SIL 1–2. | 1 mark deducted if student forgets to mention **need for external power/actuation** |
| **Inherent (inventory reduction, safer chemistry)** | Effectively N/A — hazard removed | Hazard eliminated, not just mitigated | 2 marks if student confuses "passive" with "inherent" |
| **Procedural (operator response)** | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{0}$ | Human PFD per CCPS/CCPS PERD | Never a sole layer; KTU deducts if presented as primary |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Passive vs Active Protection Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Hazard Source - Hydrocarbon Inventory"] --> B["Inherent Safety Layer - Reduce Inventory, Safer Chemistry"]
    B --> C["Passive Layer 1 - Bund Dyke Wall"]
    B --> D["Passive Layer 2 - Fireproofing Coating on Vessel"]
    B --> E["Passive Layer 3 - Separation Distance to Neighbour"]
    C --> F["Consequence Reduced - Pool Area Bounded"]
    D --> G["Time to Failure Increased above 2 hr"]
    E --> H["Domino Escalation Prevented"]
    F --> I["Final Event Frequency - Low Value"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Individual Risk - Within Tolerable Limit"]
    K["Active Layer Parallel - Deluge, ESD, Foam"] -.->|"Requires Energy and Actuation"| I
    L["Procedural Layer Parallel - Operator Action"] -.->|"Highest PFD"| I
    style A fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#990000
    style B fill:#E5FFE5,stroke:#006600
    style C fill:#E5F0FF,stroke:#003366
    style D fill:#E5F0FF,stroke:#003366
    style E fill:#E5F0FF,stroke:#003366
    style I fill:#FFF5E5,stroke:#996600
    style J fill:#E5FFE5,stroke:#006600
    style K fill:#F0E5FF,stroke:#330099
    style L fill:#FFFAE5,stroke:#999900
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Selecting a Passive Measure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start - Hazard Identified e.g. Flammable Pool Fire"] --> Q1{"Is the hazard eliminable by design?"}
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| INH["Apply Inherent Measure - reduce inventory, substitute chemical"]
    Q1 -->|"No"| Q2{"Can the consequence be contained passively?"}
    Q2 -->|"Yes - liquid spill"| BUND["Passive Bund Dyke - contain pool"]
    Q2 -->|"Yes - thermal escalation"| PFP["Passive Fire Protection - insulate vessel"]
    Q2 -->|"Yes - escalation to neighbour"| SEP["Passive Separation Distance / Blast Wall"]
    Q2 -->|"No - need active response"| ACT["Move to Active Layer - Deluge, ESD, Foam"]
    BUND --> V["Verify - RRF, PFD, fire resistance duration"]
    PFP --> V
    SEP --> V
    INH --> V
    ACT --> V
    V --> R{"IRPA Acceptable?"}
    R -->|"Yes"| FIN["Document in QRA Report - Module 1 Deliverable"]
    R -->|"No"| LOOP["Add additional layer or revise design"]
    LOOP --> Q1
    style S fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#990000
    style INH fill:#E5FFE5,stroke:#006600
    style BUND fill:#E5F0FF,stroke:#003366
    style PFP fill:#E5F0FF,stroke:#003366
    style SEP fill:#E5F0FF,stroke:#003366
    style ACT fill:#FFFAE5,stroke:#996600
    style FIN fill:#E5FFE5,stroke:#006600
    style LOOP fill:#FFF0E5,stroke:#993300
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Heat Transfer Reduction by Passive Insulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F["Pool Fire Source - Qrad 100 to 150 kW per m2"] --> C["Convective and Radiative Heat Transfer to Insulation Surface"]
    C --> I["Insulation Layer - Vermiculite or Intumescent Coating - k approx 0.1 W per m K"]
    I --> CT["Conduction Through Insulation - Reduced Flux"]
    CT --> S["Steel Vessel Wall - Lumped Capacitance Heating"]
    S --> T["Wall Temperature versus Time Curve"]
    T --> R{"T exceeds T critical 650 C?"}
    R -->|"Yes - Unprotected"| FAIL["Vessel Failure - BLEVE or Rupture within 5 to 15 min"]
    R -->|"No - PFP Active"| SAFE["Time to Failure greater than 2 hr - Domino Prevented"]
    style F fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#990000
    style I fill:#E5F0FF,stroke:#003366
    style FAIL fill:#FFCCCC,stroke:#990000
    style SAFE fill:#E5FFE5,stroke:#006600
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a "passive" safety/protection measure in the context of QRA. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A passive safety/protection measure is one that **does not require any external energy source, mechanical actuation, sensor input, or operator action** to perform its protective function. The protection is **inherent in the physical design, materials, geometry, or spatial arrangement** of the installation. *(1 mark)*
The measure remains in a continuously ready state and has a very low **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**, typically $\approx 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ per demand. *(1 mark)*
**Examples:** (i) Fireproofing / vermiculite insulation on a hydrocarbon storage vessel; (ii) Bund dyke wall around a tank farm; (iii) Separation distance between process units; (iv) Rupture disc on a pressure vessel. *(Any 2 — 1 mark)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between passive and active protection measures. Why are passive measures preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Passive | Active |
|--------|---------|--------|
| Energy needed | No | Yes (electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic) |
| Moving parts | None | Yes (valves, motors, pumps) |
| Operator action | None | Often required |
| PFD | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| Failure mode | Material degradation | Sensor/logic/actuator failure |
| Examples | Bund, PFP, separation | Deluge, ESD, foam pourer |

*(2 marks — tabular comparison)*

**Why preferred:** Passive measures are **always available**, **survive loss of utilities** (power, instrument air, control signals), and have **dramatically lower PFD**, providing much higher **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)**. They form the foundation of the LOPA hierarchy. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks and is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A horizontal cylindrical propane storage bullet (diameter 3 m, length 12 m) is located in a refinery. The vessel is exposed to a credible external pool fire of 30 min duration in the credible leak scenario. Using the lumped-capacitance model, compute the time-to-failure of the vessel:**
- **(a)** Without passive fire protection.
- **(b)** With a 50 mm thick vermiculite passive fire protection coating.

**Data given:**
- Pool fire net heat flux to unprotected surface: $Q_{\text{net,unp}} = 80 \text{ kW/m}^2$
- Steel wall thickness: $\delta = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- Steel density: $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Steel specific heat: $c_p = 500 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
- Initial wall temperature: $T_0 = 25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Critical failure temperature: $T_{\text{crit}} = 650\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Vermiculite thermal conductivity: $k_{\text{ins}} = 0.10 \text{ W/(m·K)}$
- Steel thermal conductivity: $k_{\text{steel}} = 45 \text{ W/(m·K)}$

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Unprotected Vessel (7 marks)

**Step 1: Apply the lumped-capacitance time-to-failure formula.** *(2 marks)*

$$
t_{\text{fail}} = \dfrac{\rho \, \delta \, c_p \, (T_{\text{crit}} - T_0)}{Q_{\text{net,unp}}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values.** *(2 marks)*

$$
t_{\text{fail}} = \dfrac{7850 \times 0.025 \times 500 \times (650 - 25)}{80\,000}
$$

(The factor $80\text{ kW/m}^2 = 80\,000 \text{ W/m}^2$ for unit consistency.)

**Step 3: Evaluate the numerator and denominator.** *(2 marks)*

$$
\text{Numerator} = 7850 \times 0.025 \times 500 \times 625 = 6.133 \times 10^{7} \text{ J/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 80\,000 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Final numerical answer.** *(1 mark)*

$$
t_{\text{fail,unp}} = \dfrac{6.133 \times 10^{7}}{8.0 \times 10^{4}} \approx 766.6 \text{ s} \approx 12.8 \text{ min}
$$

**Conclusion (a):** Without passive protection, the vessel fails in **~12.8 minutes**, well before the standard 30-min hydrocarbon fire exposure → **domino / BLEVE risk is unacceptable.**

---

#### Part (b) — With 50 mm Vermiculite PFP (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute the heat-flux attenuation ratio using the thermal resistance model.** *(2 marks)*

For a composite wall (insulation outside, steel inside) under steady-state conduction, the heat flux reaching the steel is:

$$
Q_{\text{net,prot}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{steel,inner}}}{R_{\text{tot}}}
$$

But in fire impingement, the lumped model gives an effective attenuation factor:

$$
\dfrac{Q_{\text{net,prot}}}{Q_{\text{net,unp}}} \approx \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{k_{\text{steel}} \cdot L_{\text{ins}}}{k_{\text{ins}} \cdot \delta_{\text{steel}}}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.** *(2 marks)*

$$
\text{Resistance ratio} = \dfrac{k_{\text{steel}} \cdot L_{\text{ins}}}{k_{\text{ins}} \cdot \delta_{\text{steel}}} = \dfrac{45 \times 0.050}{0.10 \times 0.025} = \dfrac{2.25}{0.0025} = 900
$$

**Step 3: Compute attenuated heat flux.** *(1 mark)*

$$
Q_{\text{net,prot}} = \dfrac{80\,000}{1 + 900} = \dfrac{80\,000}{901} \approx 88.8 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Recompute time-to-failure with PFP.** *(1 mark)*

$$
t_{\text{fail,prot}} = \dfrac{6.133 \times 10^{7}}{88.8} \approx 690\,700 \text{ s} \approx 192 \text{ hours} \approx 8 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 5: Conclusion.** *(1 mark)*

The 50 mm vermiculite passive fire protection extends the time-to-failure from 12.8 min to **~192 hours**, far exceeding the standard 2-hour QRA design fire duration. The passive measure is **highly effective** and acceptable per CCPS / TNO guidelines.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** A spherical LPG storage tank (diameter 10 m) contains propane at high pressure. A credible leak scenario leads to a 100% inventory release ($V = 525 \text{ m}^3$) of liquid propane. Compute the **bund (dyke) dimensions** required to passively contain the spill such that the equivalent pool diameter does not exceed **15 m**. Assume a square bund footprint and freeboard of 0.2 m. The tank base area and pipe-rack footings occupy 10% of the bund footprint.

**(b)** If the base frequency of vessel rupture (without protection) is $f_{\text{base}} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per year, and the passive bund reduces the escalation frequency to a neighbouring bullet by a factor of $10^{2}$ (i.e., $\text{PFD}_{\text{bund}} = 10^{-2}$), and an additional passive fire protection (PFP) layer on the neighbour has $\text{PFD}_{\text{PFP}} = 10^{-3}$, compute:
- (i) The combined RRF and final accident frequency.
- (ii) The Individual Risk Per Annum (IRPA), assuming the consequence is fatal in 100% of cases.
- (iii) Comment on whether the risk is tolerable as per KTU/CCPS criteria.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Bund Sizing (7 marks)

**Step 1: Maximum allowable pool area for $d_{\text{pool}} = 15$ m.** *(1 mark)*

$$
A_{\text{pool, max}} = \pi \left(\dfrac{d_{\text{pool}}}{2}\right)^2 = \pi \times 7.5^2 = 176.7 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Account for freeboard.** Effective bund height must accommodate the 525 m³ spill within $A_{\text{pool, max}} = 176.7 \text{ m}^2$ gross, less the tank/footprint void. Effective available area for liquid: *(2 marks)*

$$
A_{\text{liq}} = A_{\text{bund,footprint}} \times (1 - f_{\text{void}})
$$

For a square bund of side $L$, $A_{\text{bund,footprint}} = L^2$.

**Step 3: Solve for the required bund area assuming the 15 m circular pool fits inside the square bund.** Equating the liquid volume to liquid area times effective height: *(2 marks)*

$$
V_{\text{spill}} = A_{\text{liq}} \times H_{\text{eff}}
$$

If the pool is constrained to 15 m diameter inside the square, the bund can be $L = 15$ m (a 15 m $\times$ 15 m square). Then:

$$
