$$\boxed{\text{Hazard Identification} \longrightarrow \text{Frequency Analysis} \longrightarrow \text{Consequence Analysis} \longrightarrow \text{Risk Estimation}}$$

#### Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

**Step 1 — Hazard Identification (What is present?)**
- Catalog every source of potential harm within the system boundary.
- Tools: **HAZOP, FMEA, What-If Analysis, Checklist, Brainstorming, Dow Index**.

**Step 2 — Failure Scenario Definition (How can the hazard release?)**
- Define the *initiating event* and the *top event* in fault tree terms.
- Translate hazard into measurable failure scenarios (e.g., 50 mm hole, full-bore rupture).

**Step 3 — Frequency Assignment (How often?)**
- Assign numerical failure frequencies using generic databases (Oreda, IEEE Std 500, T-Book).
- The hazard rate λ is extracted from operating history.

**Step 4 — Consequence Modeling (How bad?)**
- Apply source-term, dispersion, fire, and explosion models.
- The hazard's *intrinsic properties* (e.g., flammability limits, toxicity LC₅₀) determine outcome severity.

**Step 5 — Risk Combination (The Final Number)**
- Multiply frequency by consequence to get risk in expected loss units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core "Why" Behind Each Step:**
> - *Why Step 1?* Because you cannot assess risk for something you have not identified.
> - *Why Step 2?* A hazard is *latent* until an initiating event releases it. The scenario bridges hazard → frequency.
> - *Why Step 3?* Frequency is what converts qualitative danger into a quantifiable rate.
> - *Why Step 4?* Consequence is hazard-property-driven; same frequency with different hazard yields different outcomes.
> - *Why Step 5?* Risk is the *integrated measure* used for decision-making by management and regulators.

---

### 4. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation from Module 1 (Hazard) that KTU examiners can test. Memorize the symbols, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Risk = f \times C$ | Fundamental QRA equation (Frequency × Consequence) | $f$ = events/year, $C$ = consequence units |
| 2 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function (constant hazard rate) | Valid for $t \geq 0$, $\lambda$ = const |
| 3 | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Cumulative probability of failure | $0 \leq F(t) \leq 1$ |
| 4 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Hazard rate (instantaneous failure rate) | Unit: hr⁻¹ or yr⁻¹ |
| 5 | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | Unit: hours or years |
| 6 | $P(\text{event in time } t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of at least one occurrence | For Poisson process |
| 7 | $IR = \sum_{i} f_i \cdot P_{i,\text{exposure}}$ | Individual Risk (per year) | Unit: yr⁻¹; benchmark $\leq 10^{-6}$/yr |
| 8 | $FN\text{-curve} = (f, N)$ | Societal Risk (Frequency vs N fatalities) | Plotted on log-log axes |
| 9 | $ALARP$ test | Risk $\leq$ "As Low As Reasonably Practicable" | Engineering + economic feasibility |
| 10 | $H = \sum p_i \cdot s_i$ | Hazard Score (qualitative matrix) | $p_i$ = probability, $s_i$ = severity |

> **Boundary / Validation Rules:**
> - The reliability function $R(t)$ is **only** valid when the hazard rate λ is *constant* (i.e., during the useful life / "bath-tub flat region").
> - The Poisson assumption (Equation 6) requires **rare, independent** events — true for most industrial hazards.

#### Real-World Utility in Engineering

The hazard-rate formulation is used daily in:
- **Refinery Risk Inspections** (API 581 RBI methodology)
- **Nuclear Plant Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA)**
- **Pipeline Integrity Management** (per ASME B31.8S)
- **Offshore Platform QRA** (mandatory for HSE UK offshore installations)
- **Aerospace Reliability Engineering** (MIL-HDBK-217)

---

### 5. Hazard Identification Methodologies (KTU Frequently Tested)

#### HAZOP — Hazard and Operability Study
- **Origin:** Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI), 1970s.
- **Method:** Apply "Guide Words" (NO/NONE, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN) to each process variable (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level).
- **Output:** Nodes with deviations, causes, consequences, safeguards.

#### FMEA — Failure Mode and Effects Analysis
- **Origin:** US Military (1949, MIL-P-1629).
- **RPN** = $S \times O \times D$ (Severity × Occurrence × Detection).

#### Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)
- **Boolean Logic:** $Top = A \cap B \cap (C \cup D)$
- **Use:** Quantitative frequency computation from basic events upward.

#### What-If Analysis
- Open-ended brainstorming; weaker than HAZOP but faster.

#### Dow's Chemical Exposure Index (CEI)
- Used for chemical plant siting.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 6. Derivation of the Reliability Function from the Hazard Rate

#### Derivation D1: From Failure Density to Reliability

**Given:** A system has a constant hazard rate λ (failures per unit time).

**Step 1:** Define the probability density function of failure time:

$$f(t) = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the **Exponential Distribution** PDF — the canonical distribution for constant-hazard systems.

**Step 2:** The Cumulative Distribution Function (probability of failure by time *t*) is obtained by integrating the PDF from 0 to *t*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(t) &= \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau) \, d\tau \\
&= \int_{0}^{t} \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda \tau} \, d\tau \\
&= \left[ -e^{-\lambda \tau} \right]_{0}^{t} \\
&= 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* We integrate the failure density over the time interval because the cumulative failure probability is the *area under the PDF curve* from time 0 up to the time *t* of interest.

**Step 3:** The Reliability function is the complement of the failure probability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(t) &= 1 - F(t) \\
&= 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t}) \\
&= e^{-\lambda t}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* Subtracting the failure probability from 1 gives us the probability that the system has *survived* — which is precisely the mathematical definition of reliability.

**Step 4:** The instantaneous hazard rate is then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda(t) &= \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} \\
&= \frac{\lambda e^{-\lambda t}}{e^{-\lambda t}} \\
&= \lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* This confirms that for an exponentially distributed time-to-failure, the instantaneous hazard rate is *constant* — a unique property of the exponential distribution. As time passes, the system does **not** become "more likely" to fail in the next instant (memoryless property).

---

#### Derivation D2: Probability of At Least One Event in Time *t* (Poisson Process)

**Given:** Industrial hazards occur as **rare, independent** events with mean rate λ per year.

**Step 1:** The expected number of events in time *t* is:

$$E[N(t)] = \lambda t$$

**Step 2:** For a Poisson process, the probability of *exactly k* events is:

$$P(N = k) = \frac{(\lambda t)^k \cdot e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

**Step 3:** The probability of **zero** events (no failure) in time *t* is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(N = 0) &= \frac{(\lambda t)^0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}}{0!} \\
&= e^{-\lambda t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** The probability of **at least one** event is the complement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(N \geq 1) &= 1 - P(N = 0) \\
&= 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* This is the most commonly used form in QRA frequency calculations. If a pump has λ = 10⁻³ failures/year and the plant runs for 5 years, the probability of *at least one* failure is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 1 - e^{-(10^{-3})(5)} \\
&= 1 - e^{-0.005} \\
&= 1 - 0.99501 \\
&= 0.00498 \approx 4.98 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 7. Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

#### Problem:
A centrifugal pump in an LPG handling facility has a recorded hazard rate of **λ = 2.5 × 10⁻⁴ per hour**. Calculate:
(a) The reliability after **5000 hours** of continuous operation.
(b) The probability of failure in that period.
(c) The Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).
(d) The expected number of failures over **one year** (8760 hours).

---

#### Solution:

**Part (a) — Reliability after t = 5000 hr:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(5000) &= e^{-\lambda t} \\
&= e^{-(2.5 \times 10^{-4})(5000)} \\
&= e^{-1.25} \\
&= 0.2865
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* There is a **28.65%** probability that the pump will survive 5000 hours without failure.

---

**Part (b) — Probability of failure in 5000 hr:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(5000) &= 1 - R(5000) \\
&= 1 - 0.2865 \\
&= 0.7135
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* A **71.35%** probability of at least one failure within 5000 hours.

---

**Part (c) — Mean Time Between Failures:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF &= \frac{1}{\lambda} \\
&= \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= 4000 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* On average, one failure is expected every 4000 operating hours.

---

**Part (d) — Expected number of failures in one year (t = 8760 hr):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[N] &= \lambda t \\
&= (2.5 \times 10^{-4})(8760) \\
&= 2.19 \text{ failures per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* Approximately 2 to 3 failures per year.

---

### 8. Analytical Matrix — Hazard vs. Risk Across Industries

| Industry | Dominant Hazard | Typical λ (per year) | Consequence Range | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Oil & Gas Refining | Flammable liquid release | 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁵ | ₹10⁷ to ₹10⁹ | High |
| Nuclear Power Plant | Core meltdown | 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁷ | Catastrophic | Catastrophic |
| Chemical Storage | Toxic gas cloud | 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ | ₹10⁶ to ₹10⁸ | High |
| Pharmaceutical | Solvent fire | 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁴ | ₹10⁵ to ₹10⁷ | Medium |
| Aviation | Structural failure | 10⁻⁷ per flight | Loss of life | Intolerable per event |
| Construction | Fall from height | 10⁻² to 10⁻³ | Fatalities | Medium-High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 9. Mermaid Diagram 1 — Position of Hazard in the QRA Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Plant Under Study] --> B[Hazard Identification]
    B --> C{What can go wrong?}
    C --> D[Initiating Event Definition]
    D --> E[Frequency Analysis λ per year]
    E --> F[Consequence Analysis]
    F --> G[Source Term and Dispersion]
    G --> H[Fire Explosion Toxicity Models]
    H --> I[Risk Estimation R equals f times C]
    I --> J[Risk Evaluation against Criteria]
    J --> K{ALARP Test}
    K -->|Yes| L[Acceptable Risk]
    K -->|No| M[Risk Reduction Measures]
    M --> B
    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L fill:#81c784,stroke:#000
    style M fill:#ff9800,stroke:#000
```

*Explanation:* The diagram shows the **closed-loop QRA pipeline**. Notice that **Hazard Identification** (highlighted in red) is the **starting node** — everything downstream depends on a correct hazard catalog. If the loop fails the ALARP test, it returns to hazard identification to seek additional safeguards.

---

### 10. Mermaid Diagram 2 — Hazard Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    H[Hazard] --> P[Physical]
    H --> C[Chemical]
    H --> B[Biological]
    H --> E[Ergonomic]
    H --> PS[Psychosocial]
    H --> M[Mechanical]
    H --> EL[Electrical]
    H --> EN[Environmental]
    P --> P1[Noise Vibration Radiation]
    C --> C1[Flammable Toxic Reactive Corrosive]
    B --> B1[Bacteria Viruses Fungi]
    E --> E1[Repetitive Motion Bad Posture]
    PS --> PS1[Stress Fatigue Violence]
    M --> M1[Rotating Parts Sharp Edges Pinch Points]
    EL --> EL1[Shock Arc Flash Static]
    EN --> EN1[Air Water Soil Releases]
    style H fill:#7b1fa2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

*Explanation:* This hierarchy reflects the **KTU 2024 Module 1 taxonomy** of hazards. The Chemical category is highlighted as the most-tested in industrial QRA problems.

---

### 11. Mermaid Diagram 3 — Bath-Tub Curve (Hazard Rate vs. Time)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Infant Mortality Phase λ high decreasing] -->|Burn-in| B[Useful Life Phase λ constant low]
    B -->|Wear-out| C[End of Life Phase λ increasing]
    A --- A1[Early failures: manufacturing defects]
    B --- B1[Random failures: dominant QRA region]
    C --- C1[Wear-out: aging corrosion fatigue]
    style A fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#000
    style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#000
```

*Explanation:* In QRA, we almost always work in the **Useful Life (Phase 2)** region, where the constant hazard rate λ assumption of the exponential distribution is valid. The bath-tub shape is fundamental to KTU's reliability-based QRA questions.

---

### 12. Mermaid Diagram 4 — Fault Tree for a Hazard Scenario

```mermaid
graph TD
    TOP[Top Event: Hazardous Material Release] --> A1[Failure of Containment Boundary]
    TOP --> A2[Human Error During Operation]
    A1 --> B1[Pipe Rupture]
    A1 --> B2[Tank Failure]
    A1 --> B3[Valve Leak]
    B1 --> C1[Internal Corrosion]
    B1 --> C2[External Impact]
    B2 --> C3[Overpressure]
    B2 --> C4[Wall Thinning]
    A2 --> D1[Improper Valve Lineup]
    A2 --> D2[Maintenance Procedure Violation]
    style TOP fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A1 fill:#ef5350,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A2 fill:#ef5350,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

*Explanation:* Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) is the **primary tool for converting a hazard into measurable failure frequencies**. The Top Event branches into OR-gate logic (any branch triggers the top event), and AND-gate logic (all sub-branches needed). Boolean reduction then computes the top-event frequency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 13. Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term "Hazard" in the context of Quantitative Risk Assessment. Differentiate clearly between a Hazard and a Risk, with one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A *Hazard* is an intrinsic source, situation, or condition possessing the potential to cause harm to people, property, or the environment. It is characterized by its inherent dangerous properties — such as flammability, toxicity, or stored energy — independent of how likely it is to cause harm.

A *Risk*, on the other hand, is the combination of the **likelihood** (frequency) of the hazard being realized and the **magnitude** of the resulting consequence. Mathematically:

$$Risk = Likelihood \times Consequence$$

**Example:** A 20,000-liter tank containing liquid chlorine is a **Hazard** (toxic, stored chemical energy). The Risk associated with it is the *annual probability* of a 50-mm leak (say, 10⁻⁴/yr) multiplied by the consequence of chlorine exposure in the nearby residential zone (e.g., 5 fatalities expected). Thus, Risk = 10⁻⁴ × 5 = 5 × 10⁻⁴ fatalities/yr.

**Key Difference:** The hazard can exist without causing harm (latent); risk exists only when both probability and consequence are evaluated.

---

#### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List and briefly explain any FOUR hazard identification techniques commonly used in industrial QRA.**

**Model Answer:**
The four techniques are:

1. **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study):** A systematic technique using *Guide Words* (No, More, Less, Reverse, As Well As, Part Of, Other Than) applied to process parameters (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level) at each node of a P&ID. Identifies deviations and their causes.

2. **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis):** Tabulates each component's possible failure modes, their effects on the system, and computes a Risk Priority Number: $RPN = S \times O \times D$.

3. **What-If Analysis:** A brainstorming technique where a multidisciplinary team asks "What if…?" questions to identify potential hazards. Faster but less rigorous than HAZOP.

4. **Checklist Analysis:** Uses pre-prepared lists of known hazards and failure modes. Good for routine or similar facilities; limited by checklist completeness.

---

### 14. Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

#### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** *Explain in detail the four-stage pipeline of Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA), with specific emphasis on the role of the Hazard in each stage.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A reactor cooling pump operates with a constant hazard rate of λ = 5 × 10⁻⁵ per hour. Calculate the reliability and probability of failure after 8000 hours of operation. Also determine the Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) and the expected number of failures in two years of continuous operation (one year = 8760 hours).* **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question A(a):

The KTU 2024 syllabus presents QRA as a four-stage sequential pipeline:

**Stage 1 — Hazard Identification:**
The QRA process begins by systematically identifying every hazard present in the facility. This involves answering *"What can harm people, the environment, or assets?"* A hazard here is not yet a risk — it is merely catalogued. Common techniques include HAZOP, FMEA, What-If, and Checklist. The output of this stage is a complete **hazard register** listing each hazard, its location, and its properties (e.g., a 5000-liter acetone storage tank at Section B-12 of the plant).

**Stage 2 — Frequency Analysis (Likelihood):**
Each identified hazard is then analyzed to determine *how often* it can be released. This requires defining initiating events (e.g., pump seal failure, gasket rupture) and computing their frequencies using generic databases (Oreda, IEEE Std 500) or plant-specific data. For example, the acetone tank might be assigned a leak frequency of 10⁻⁴ per year for a 25-mm hole, based on historical data.

**Stage 3 — Consequence Analysis:**
The next stage models the *outcome* should the hazard be released. For chemical hazards, this involves source-term modeling, atmospheric dispersion, fire radiation, explosion overpressure, and toxicity calculations. The hazard's intrinsic properties (flammable limits, LC₅₀, vapor pressure) directly drive the consequence magnitude.

**Stage 4 — Risk Estimation and Evaluation:**
Risk is finally computed as $R = f \times C$, then evaluated against acceptance criteria (Individual Risk ≤ 10⁻⁶/yr, FN-curve limits, ALARP). If unacceptable, the loop returns to Stage 1 for additional safeguards.

**The Connecting Thread:** The hazard is the *anchor* of all four stages. It provides the substance (Stage 1), the failure scenario (Stage 2), the consequence driver (Stage 3), and the basis for the risk number (Stage 4). Without rigorous hazard identification, the entire QRA output is unreliable.

---

##### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Given:**
- Hazard rate: λ = 5 × 10⁻⁵ per hour
- Operating time: t = 8000 hours
- One year = 8760 hours
- Two years = 17520 hours

**(i) Reliability after 8000 hours:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(8000) &= e^{-\lambda t} \\
&= e^{-(5 \times 10^{-5})(8000)} \\
&= e^{-0.4} \\
&= 0.6703
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Reliability ≈ **67.03%**.

---

**(ii) Probability of failure in 8000 hours:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(8000) &= 1 - R(8000) \\
&= 1 - 0.6703 \\
&= 0.3297
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Probability of failure ≈ **32.97%**.

---

**(iii) Mean Time Between Failures:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF &= \frac{1}{\lambda} \\
&= \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= 20{,}000 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** MTBF = **20,000 hours**.

---

**(iv) Expected number of failures in 2 years (17520 hours):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[N] &= \lambda t \\
&= (5 \times 10^{-5})(17520) \\
&= 0.876 \text{ failures}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Approximately **0.876 failures** expected over 2 years — i.e., less than one failure in this period.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks - Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** *Discuss the classification of hazards in industrial QRA with suitable examples. Why is the Chemical hazard category the most critical for oil & gas facility QRA?* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A pressure vessel has a recorded hazard rate of λ = 1.5 × 10⁻⁴ per hour. The facility operates continuously for 10,000 hours per year. Using the Poisson model, determine (i) the probability of zero failures in one year, (ii) the probability of at least one failure in one year, (iii) the expected number of failures in five years, and (iv) the Mean Time Between Failures in years.* **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Classification of Hazards in Industrial QRA:**

Industrial QRA classifies hazards into the following major categories, each illustrated with examples:

1. **Physical Hazards:** These arise from environmental factors such as noise (compressor station noise exceeding 85 dBA), vibration (rotating machinery), temperature extremes (cryogenic LNG at −162°C), and radiation (non-ionizing from welding arcs).

2. **Chemical Hazards:** Substances with intrinsic dangerous properties. Sub-categories:
   - *Flammable* (hexane, propane — fire/explosion risk)
   - *Toxic* (H₂S, chlorine, ammonia — inhalation risk)
   - *Reactive* (organic peroxides — runaway reactions)
   - *Corrosive* (H₂SO₄, NaOH — equipment degradation)

3. **Biological Hazards:** Bacteria, mold, bioaerosols. Important in pharmaceutical and wastewater treatment plants.

4. **Mechanical Hazards:** Rotating shafts, unguarded gears, sharp edges. Frequent in manufacturing.

5. **Electrical Hazards:** Shock, arc flash, short-circuit. Present in all switchgear and motor control centers.

6. **Ergonomic Hazards:** Repetitive motion injuries, lifting injuries. Important in warehouses and assembly lines.

7. **Psychosocial Hazards:** Stress, fatigue, workplace violence. Important for human reliability analysis.

**Why Chemical Hazards Dominate Oil & Gas QRA:**

Oil & gas facilities inherently handle **hydrocarbons and toxic gases** in massive quantities — crude oil, naphtha, LPG, LNG, H₂S, benzene. These substances possess three simultaneous intrinsic dangerous properties:

1. **High Flammability:** Low flash points and wide flammability limits (e.g., propane: 2.1%–9.5% in air).
2. **High Toxicity:** Many hydrocarbons and additives are carcinogenic or acutely toxic (e.g., benzene is a Group 1 carcinogen; H₂S is lethal above 700 ppm).
3. **High Stored Energy:** Pressurized vessels and pipelines store enormous mechanical and chemical potential energy.

The combination of these properties with **large inventories** (often thousands of tons) means that any release can escalate to a major accident (fire, explosion, toxic cloud) affecting workers, public, and environment. The Bhopal (1984) and Deepwater Horizon (2010) disasters are textbook examples of chemical hazards dominating QRA outcomes.

---

##### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Given:**
- Hazard rate: λ = 1.5 × 10⁻⁴ per hour
- Operating time per year: t = 10,000 hours
- Analysis period for part (iii): 5 years → 50,000 hours

**(i) Probability of zero failures in one year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(N = 0) &= e^{-\lambda t} \\
&= e^{-(1.5 \times 10^{-4})(10{,}000)} \\
&= e^{-1.5} \\
&= 0.2231
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Approximately **22.31%** probability of zero failures in one year.

---

**(ii) Probability of at least one failure in one year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(N \geq 1) &= 1 - P(N = 0) \\
&= 1 - 0.2231 \\
&= 0.7769
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Approximately **77.69%** probability of at least one failure in a year.

---

**(iii) Expected number of failures in 5 years (50,000 hours):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[N] &= \lambda t \\
&= (1.5 \times 10^{-4})(50{,}000) \\
&= 7.5 \text{ failures}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Approximately **7.5 failures** are expected over 5 years.

---

**(iv) MTBF in years:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF \text{ (hours)} &= \frac{1}{\lambda} \\
&= \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= 6666.67 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to operating hours per year:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF \text{ (years)} &= \frac{6666.67}{10{,}000} \\
&= 0.667 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** MTBF ≈ **0.667 years** (or about 8 months of operating time).

---

### 15. KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in KTU QRA Papers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Hazard with Risk** — Worth up to **3 marks lost** if definitions are interchanged. Always use the word "potential" for hazards and "likelihood × consequence" for risks.
>
> 2. **Skipping Units** — KTU examiners *will* deduct 0.5 to 1 mark for missing units on λ (hr⁻¹ vs yr⁻¹). Always state the time base explicitly.
>
> 3. **Misapplying the Exponential Formula** — Students often write $R(t) = e^{\lambda t}$ instead of $e^{-\lambda t}$. The negative sign is *non-negotiable*.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the Constant Hazard Rate Assumption** — The formula $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is only valid during the "useful life" phase of the bath-tub curve. Stating this assumption earns **bonus marks**.
>
> 5. **Not Converting Time Units** — When λ is given in hr⁻¹ and t is given in years, students forget to convert. ALWAYS convert to a consistent time base before substitution.
>
> 6. **In HAZOP Questions, Forgetting the Guide Words** — Examiners award marks only if the seven standard guide words (No/None, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than) are correctly identified and applied.

---

### 16. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Hazard: Key Takeaways**
>
> - **Hazard** = Source with intrinsic *potential* to cause harm.
> - **Risk** = Likelihood × Consequence; never confuse with Hazard.
> - **Danger** = Immediate exposure scenario, not the same as Hazard.
> - The QRA pipeline is: **Hazard ID → Frequency → Consequence → Risk Estimation**.
> - **Reliability equation:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (valid only for constant λ).
> - **Failure probability:** $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$.
> - **MTBF** = $1/\lambda$.
> - **Poisson probability** of ≥ 1 event in time *t*: $P = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$.
> - **Eight hazard categories:** Physical, Chemical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial, Mechanical, Electrical, Environmental.
> - **Chemical hazards dominate oil & gas QRA** due to flammability + toxicity + stored energy.
> - **Four main hazard ID methods:** HAZOP, FMEA, What-If, Checklist.
> - **HAZOP Guide Words:** NO/NONE, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN.
> - **FMEA Risk Priority Number:** RPN = Severity × Occurrence × Detection.
> - **Individual Risk benchmark:** ≤ 10⁻⁶ per year (Kletz criterion).
> - **ALARP:** "As Low As Reasonably Practicable" — the practical risk-acceptance test.
> - **Bath-tub curve** has three regions; QRA always uses the middle (constant λ) region.
> - **Time unit consistency** is critical: always convert λ and t to the same base.
> - **Always state assumptions** (constant λ, Poisson process, rare independent events) before solving.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Risk # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Risk In the context of **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** and the KTU 2024 Scheme, the term **Risk** carries a precise, mathematically tractable meaning that extends far beyond its everyday colloquial usage. **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard Terminology):** > **Risk** is the quantitative measure of the **probability of occurrence** of a specified hazardous event multiplied by the **magnitude (severity) of the consequences** arising from that event, evaluated over a defined time period and population. Mathematically, the canonical KTU expression is: $$R = P \times C$$ Where: - $R$ = **Risk** (dimensionless or expressed in fatalities/year, financial loss, etc.) - $P$ = **Probability** of the hazardous event occurring (dimensionless, $0 \leq P \leq 1$, or frequency in events/year) - $C$ = **Consequence** or severity (deaths, monetary loss, environmental damage, etc.) A more complete, frequency-based formulation used in industrial safety is: $$R = f \times C$$ Where $f$ is the **frequency** of the initiating event (per year) and $C$ is the consequence. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight — The Tripartite Definition** > Modern risk science (per the KTU syllabus) recognises risk as a **tripartite** construct involving three irreducible components: **(1) Hazard**, **(2) Probability**, and **(3) Consequence**. A *hazard* without probability is a *danger*; a *consequence* without probability is a *certainty*. Only when all three are combined do we obtain a true *risk*. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are crossing a busy highway. Two distinct things influence your decision: 1. **How likely is an accident?** → This is the **Probability** ($P$). Is the traffic dense or sparse? What time of day is it? This answers *“How often could it happen?”* 2. **What happens if you get hit?** → This is the **Consequence** ($C$). A scratch vs. a fatal injury. This answers *“How bad would it be?”* **Risk is the product of these two factors.** Crossing an empty road at 3 AM ($P$ low, $C$ high) and crossing a packed market street at noon ($P$ high, $C$ medium) may yield similar overall *risk values* despite feeling completely different. Your brain intuitively performs this calculation when it makes you nervous — that "gut feeling" is **risk perception**, which is qualitatively different from calculated *risk*. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Insurance Premium** > An insurance company's premium is literally an economic expression of risk: $P \times C$. A young, inexperienced driver has a higher $P$ (more accidents) but a *low* $C$ (smaller claims). A senior executive has lower $P$ but extremely high $C$ (large liability cases). The premium reflects $R = P \times C$ in monetary terms. ## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance | Term | Definition | KTU Context | |------|------------|-------------| | **Hazard** | A source, situation, or act with potential to cause harm | The "what could go wrong" | | **Risk** | Combination of probability and severity of harm | The "how bad and how likely" | | **Threat** | A potential cause of an unwanted incident | Often used interchangeably with hazard | | **Consequence** | The outcome of an event affecting objectives | Measured in deaths, loss, etc. | | **Likelihood** | Chance of something happening | Expressed as probability or frequency | | **Risk Source** | Element which alone or in combination has intrinsic potential to give rise to risk | ISO 31000 terminology | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Hook:** A common KTU exam pitfall is using *hazard* and *risk* interchangeably. They are NOT synonyms. A **hazard** is a potential source of harm; **risk** is the quantified outcome when we account for *how likely* and *how severe* the harm is. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Anatomy of Risk — Decomposition Risk, as treated in KTU PECHT754, is decomposed along **three orthogonal axes**. Each axis is essential to a complete description. ### Axis 1: Probability Dimension (How Often?) - **Probability** ($P$): A dimensionless number between 0 and 1. - **Frequency** ($f$): Events per unit time, e.g., $10^{-4}$/year. - **Return Period** ($T = 1/f$): Average years between occurrences. ### Axis 2: Consequence Dimension (How Bad?) - **Fatalities / Injuries** (human safety) - **Property Damage** (financial loss in USD/INR) - **Environmental Damage** (kg pollutant released, hectares affected) - **Reputational Damage** (qualitative, harder to quantify) ### Axis 3: Context Dimension (To Whom, Where, When?) - **Individual Risk** vs. **Societal Risk** - **Voluntary vs. Involuntary** exposure - **Familiar vs. Unfamiliar** hazards - **Controllable vs. Uncontrollable** events ## 2.2 Core Mathematical Formulations ### Fundamental Risk Equation $$R = P \times C$$ ### Risk for Multiple Scenarios (Additivity) When several independent hazardous scenarios exist, the **total risk** is the sum of individual risks: $$R_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot C_i$$ > This additivity principle underlies **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** and **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)**, which are core KTU Module 1/2 topics. ### Individual Risk (IR) The risk to a **single person** continuously present at a location: $$IR = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot p_i$$ Where: - $f_i$ = frequency of hazardous event $i$ (events/year) - $p_i$ = probability of fatality given event $i$ **Unit:** fatalities per person per year (dimensionless/year). ### Societal Risk (SR) The risk to the **entire exposed population**, typically expressed using **F-N Curves** (Frequency-Number of fatalities): $$F(N) = \sum_{i: C_i \geq N} f_i$$ Where $F(N)$ is the cumulative frequency of accidents causing $N$ or more fatalities. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $R = P \times C$ | Fundamental risk equation | fatalities, USD, etc. | | 2 | $R_{total} = \sum P_i C_i$ | Total risk for independent scenarios | Same as R | | 3 | $IR = \sum f_i p_i$ | Individual Risk | per person-year | | 4 | $F(N) = \sum_{C_i \geq N} f_i$ | Societal risk (F-N Curve) | per year | | 5 | $T = 1/f$ | Return period | years | | 6 | $R_{acceptable} = R_{ALARP}$ | Tolerability threshold (ALARP) | Case-specific | | 7 | $\text{Expected Loss} = P \times L$ | Annual expected monetary loss | INR/year | | 8 | $\text{Risk Ratio} = R_{actual}/R_{tolerable}$ | Compliance metric | Dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Exam Note:** When calculating risk for a sequence of independent events (e.g., failure of two barriers in series), you must **multiply probabilities** and **sum or multiply consequences** depending on whether the events are mutually exclusive or independent. Misapplication of these rules is a top-3 mark-deduction cause in KTU board exams. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Risk Quantification | Industry Domain | Application of $R = P \times C$ | |-----------------|-------------------------------| | **Process Industry (Chemical)** | Design of relief valves, flare stacks, blast walls | | **Nuclear Engineering** | Core Damage Frequency (CDF), Large Early Release Frequency (LERF) | | **Civil/Structural** | Flood risk, seismic hazard, bridge collapse probability | | **Aerospace** | Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) scoring | | **Financial Engineering** | Value-at-Risk (VaR), credit risk modelling | | **Public Health** | Epidemic risk, drug side-effect assessment | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility — Why This Matters in KTU Context** > Quantitative risk assessment is the *lingua franca* of the **Major Accident Hazard (MAH)** regulations under the Indian Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC). Industries in Kerala's petrochemical and rubber sectors (e.g., FACT, BPCL Kochi, Hindustan Petroleum) are required to submit QRA reports to the **Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB)** and **AERB** (for radiation facilities). The $R = P \times C$ framework underpins these submissions. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Hazard to Quantified Risk The transformation of a qualitative **hazard** into a numerical **risk** is not arbitrary — it follows a rigorous 4-step pipeline that is examinable in KTU Module 1. ### Step 1 — Hazard Identification Identify all possible initiating events. For example, in an LPG storage facility: - $E_1$: Catastrophic vessel rupture - $E_2$: Pipeline leak - $E_3$: Pump seal failure ### Step 2 — Frequency Assignment Assign a frequency $f_i$ to each event from historical data, generic failure databases (e.g., OREDA, IEEE Std 500), or expert judgment. ### Step 3 — Consequence Modelling For each event, model the physical outcome (blast overpressure, thermal radiation, toxic dispersion) and translate to consequence metrics $C_i$. ### Step 4 — Risk Aggregation Combine via: $$R_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot C_i$$ ## 3.2 Worked Example — Three-Scenario Risk Calculation **Problem Setup (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style):** A small chemical reactor has three identified failure modes with the following data: | Scenario | Frequency $f_i$ (per year) | Probability of fatality given event $p_i$ | Consequence (fatalities if it occurs) | |----------|---------------------------|-------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------| | $E_1$ | $5 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.4 | 2 | | $E_2$ | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.7 | 5 | | $E_3$ | $2 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.9 | 10 | **Calculate (a) Total Individual Risk, (b) Total Societal Risk (annual expected fatalities), and (c) comment on acceptability if the ALARP target is $10^{-5}$ fatalities/person-year.** ### Step-by-Step Solution #### (a) Individual Risk The Individual Risk is the **frequency-weighted probability of fatality**, summed over all events: $$IR = \sum_{i=1}^{3} f_i \cdot p_i$$ $$IR = (5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4) + (1 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.7) + (2 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.9)$$ $$IR = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} + 7.0 \times 10^{-5} + 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$IR = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} + 0.07 \times 10^{-3} + 0.018 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$IR = 2.088 \times 10^{-3} \text{ fatalities per person-year}$$ #### (b) Societal Risk (Annual Expected Fatalities) $$E[F] = \sum_{i=1}^{3} f_i \cdot p_i \cdot C_i$$ $$E[F] = (5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4 \times 2) + (1 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.7 \times 5) + (2 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.9 \times 10)$$ $$E[F] = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} + 3.5 \times 10^{-4} + 1.8 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$E[F] = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} + 0.35 \times 10^{-3} + 0.18 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$E[F] = 4.53 \times 10^{-3} \text{ fatalities per year}$$ #### (c) Acceptability Analysis The calculated Individual Risk is: $$IR_{calculated} = 2.088 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per year}$$ The ALARP tolerable target is: $$IR_{target} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$ Computing the **Risk Ratio**: $$\text{Risk Ratio} = \frac{IR_{calculated}}{IR_{target}} = \frac{2.088 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 208.8$$ Since the ratio is **208.8 ≫ 1**, the risk is **grossly unacceptable** and the facility must implement additional safety barriers (e.g., relief valves, gas detection, isolation systems) until: $$IR \leq IR_{ALARP}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Converting Frequency to Probability Many KTU problems provide a return period rather than a frequency. The conversion is straightforward but must be shown explicitly: $$P(\text{event in year } T) = 1 - (1 - f)^{T}$$ For small $f$ and moderate $T$ (so that $fT \ll 1$): $$P \approx f \times T$$ This approximation appears frequently in dam safety, flood risk, and seismic risk problems. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Risk Components Cross-Tabulation | Risk Component | Quantitative Metric | Typical Range | KTU Use Case | |----------------|--------------------|--------------|--------------| | Frequency ($f$) | events/year | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-7}$ | Equipment failure | | Probability ($P$) | dimensionless | 0 to 1 | Event tree branches | | Consequence ($C$) | fatalities, INR | 1 to $10^4$ | Hazard modelling | | Individual Risk (IR) | per person-year | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-7}$ | Land-use planning | | Societal Risk (SR) | F-N curve | Variable | Regulatory limits | | Expected Loss (EL) | INR/year | Project-specific | Insurance/finance | ## 3.5 Risk Matrix Construction (Standard 5×5 KTU Format) A **Risk Matrix** is a 2D grid with Likelihood on one axis and Consequence on the other. KTU standard is 5×5: | Likelihood ↓ / Consequence → | Negligible (1) | Minor (2) | Moderate (3) | Major (4) | Catastrophic (5) | |------------------------------|----------------|-----------|--------------|-----------|------------------| | **Almost Certain (5)** | Low (5) | Medium (10) | High (15) | Extreme (20) | Extreme (25) | | **Likely (4)** | Low (4) | Medium (8) | High (12) | Extreme (16) | Extreme (20) | | **Possible (3)** | Low (3) | Medium (6) | High (9) | High (12) | Extreme (15) | | **Unlikely (2)** | Low (2) | Low (4) | Medium (6) | High (8) | High (10) | | **Rare (1)** | Low (1) | Low (2) | Low (3) | Medium (4) | High (5) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerical Risk Score = Likelihood × Consequence** (cell value). The score is dimensionless and used for ranking, not absolute risk quantification. A score of 15+ generally triggers mandatory risk reduction under KTU-aligned industrial safety protocols. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Risk Anatomy — Conceptual Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hazard Source
e.g., LPG Storage Tank] --> B[Hazard Identification] B --> C{Initiating Event
Occurred?} C -- Yes --> D[Consequence Modelling
Blast, Fire, Toxic] C -- No --> E[No Risk Realised] D --> F[Probability of Event
f events per year] D --> G[Severity of Outcome
C fatalities or loss] F --> H[RISK CALCULATION
R = P x C] G --> H H --> I{R vs ALARP
Tolerable?} I -- Yes --> J[Risk Acceptable
Document and Monitor] I -- No --> K[Implement Risk
Reduction Measures] K --> L[Re-evaluate R] L --> I E --> M[Risk = 0] H --> M style A fill:#ffe0e0 style H fill:#fff4cc style I fill:#cce5ff style J fill:#ccffcc style K fill:#ffcc99 ``` ## 4.2 Risk Quantification Pipeline — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Step 1: Hazard
Identification] --> P2[Step 2: Frequency
Assignment f] P2 --> P3[Step 3: Consequence
Analysis C] P3 --> P4[Step 4: Risk
Aggregation R = sum f x C] P4 --> P5[Step 5: Risk
Evaluation] P5 --> P6[Step 6: Risk
Treatment] P6 --> P7[Step 7: Monitoring
and Review] P7 -. feedback .-> P1 style P1 fill:#ffcc99 style P4 fill:#fff4cc style P7 fill:#ccffcc ``` ## 4.3 Risk Taxonomy — Hierarchical Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD R[RISK] --> R1[By Nature] R --> R2[By Scope] R --> R3[By Voluntariness] R --> R4[By Origin] R1 --> R1a[Pure Risk
Loss only] R1 --> R1b[Speculative Risk
Gain or Loss] R2 --> R2a[Individual Risk] R2 --> R2b[Societal Risk] R3 --> R3a[Voluntary
e.g., Smoking] R3 --> R3b[Involuntary
e.g., Pollution] R4 --> R4a[Natural Risk
Earthquake, Flood] R4 --> R4b[Man-made Risk
Industrial, Tech] style R fill:#ff6666 style R1 fill:#ffcc99 style R2 fill:#fff4cc style R3 fill:#cce5ff style R4 fill:#ccffcc ``` ## 4.4 Risk Acceptance Framework — ALARP Triangle ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Unacceptable Region
Risk R too high
MANDATORY reduction] -->|Implement controls| B[ALARP Region
As Low As Reasonably Practicable
Cost-benefit analysis] B -->|Gross disproportion test| C[Acceptable Region
Negligible risk
Monitor only] style A fill:#ff4444 style B fill:#ffcc00 style C fill:#44ff44 ``` ## 4.5 F-N Curve Schematic — Societal Risk Visualisation ```mermaid flowchart LR XAxis["FATALITIES N (log scale)
1 --> 10 --> 100 --> 1000"] --- YAxis["FREQUENCY F per year (log scale)
1 --> 10^-1 --> 10^-3 --> 10^-5 --> 10^-7"] YAxis --- Z["Acceptance Line
F is proportional to N to the power -k"] XAxis --- Z style XAxis fill:#e0e0ff style YAxis fill:#e0e0ff style Z fill:#ffe0e0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation of F-N Curve:** The slope of the acceptance line determines *risk aversion*. The HSE (UK) standard uses a slope of $-1$ for the upper limit and $-2$ for the lower limit. Steeper slopes indicate greater societal aversion to large-scale accidents. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define risk. How is it distinguished from hazard?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Risk is the quantitative combination of the **probability of occurrence** of a hazardous event and the **magnitude of the consequences** of that event, expressed mathematically as $R = P \times C$ (1 mark). A **hazard** is a source, situation, or act with the *potential* to cause harm, whereas **risk** is the *quantified* outcome once we assign probability and severity to that harm (1 mark). For example, a toxic chemical in storage is a **hazard**; the probability of its release multiplied by the number of people exposed yields the **risk** (1 mark). ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Explain the terms Individual Risk and Societal Risk with appropriate expressions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Individual Risk (IR):** The risk to a person located at a specific location, expressed as the frequency of fatality per unit time, given continuous exposure (1 mark). - Expression: $IR = \sum f_i \cdot p_i$ (1 mark) - **Societal Risk (SR):** The risk to the entire exposed population, expressed as the relationship between frequency of occurrence and the number of people harmed (1 mark). - Expression: $F(N) = \sum_{C_i \geq N} f_i$ (F-N Curve) ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Module Internal Choice Pattern) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]** **(a)** A petroleum storage facility has identified three major accident scenarios with the following data: | Scenario | Frequency (per year) | Probability of fatality | Fatalities if it occurs | |----------|---------------------|------------------------|--------------------------| | Pool fire | $1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.30 | 2 | | Vapour cloud explosion | $4.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.55 | 8 | | Toxic release | $1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.20 | 12 | Calculate the **(i)** Individual Risk, **(ii)** Total expected annual fatalities, and **(iii)** comment on the result if the ALARP target is $10^{-5}$ per person-year. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss in detail the classification of risk based on **(i)** nature, **(ii)** voluntariness, and **(iii)** origin. Provide at least one engineering example for each category. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:** **Step 1: Individual Risk calculation (2 marks)** $$IR = \sum_{i=1}^{3} f_i \cdot p_i$$ $$IR = (1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.30) + (4.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.55) + (1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.20)$$ $$IR = 3.6 \times 10^{-4} + 2.2 \times 10^{-4} + 3.0 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$IR = 5.8 \times 10^{-4} + 3.0 \times 10^{-5} = 6.1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per person-year}$$ **Step 2: Expected annual fatalities (2 marks)** $$E[F] = \sum f_i \cdot p_i \cdot C_i$$ $$E[F] = (1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.30 \times 2) + (4.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.55 \times 8) + (1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.20 \times 12)$$ $$E[F] = 7.2 \times 10^{-4} + 1.76 \times 10^{-3} + 3.6 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$E[F] = 7.2 \times 10^{-4} + 17.6 \times 10^{-4} + 3.6 \times 10^{-4} = 2.84 \times 10^{-3} \text{ fatalities/year}$$ **Step 3: Risk Ratio and ALARP Assessment (3 marks)** $$\text{Risk Ratio} = \frac{6.1 \times 10^{-4}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 61$$ The IR exceeds the ALARP target by a factor of **61** (1 mark). The risk is **well above the tolerable limit** (1 mark). Immediate risk reduction measures — such as bund walls, gas detection systems, and emergency shutdown valves — must be implemented to bring IR below $10^{-5}$ per person-year (1 mark). --- **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:** **(i) Risk by Nature (2 marks):** - **Pure Risk:** Risk of loss only, no possibility of gain. *Example:* Fire damage to a chemical plant (1 mark). - **Speculative Risk:** Possibility of both gain and loss. *Example:* Investment in a new production line (1 mark). **(ii) Risk by Voluntariness (2 marks):** - **Voluntary Risk:** Person knowingly accepts the risk. *Example:* Operator choosing to enter a confined space without PPE (1 mark). - **Involuntary Risk:** Risk imposed on a person without consent. *Example:* Residents living near a chemical plant exposed to toxic release (1 mark). **(iii) Risk by Origin (3 marks):** - **Natural Risk:** Originating from natural phenomena. *Example:* Earthquake damage to a refinery in Kerala (1 mark). - **Man-made (Technological) Risk:** Originating from human activity. *Example:* Bhopal-type toxic gas release (1 mark). - **Combined/Socio-natural Risk:** Interaction of natural and man-made. *Example:* Flooding at a fertilizer plant causing toxic release (1 mark). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]** **(a)** What is the ALARP principle? Explain with a diagram the three regions of risk: **Unacceptable**, **Tolerable (ALARP)**, and **Acceptable**. How is the "gross disproportion test" used to decide on risk reduction? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A gas pipeline has a failure rate of $3 \times 10^{-4}$ per km per year. For a 5 km section passing through a residential area with 200 occupants, if the conditional probability of fatality from a rupture is 0.6, determine: **(i)** the Individual Risk for a person residing there 100% of the time, **(ii)** the expected annual fatalities, and **(iii)** the return period of such an accident. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:** **ALARP Definition (2 marks):** ALARP stands for **"As Low As Reasonably Practicable."** It is a risk management principle stating that residual risk must be reduced to a level where the cost of further reduction would be **grossly disproportionate** to the additional safety benefit achieved (2 marks). **Three Regions of Risk (3 marks):** - **Unacceptable Region:** Risk is so high that the activity cannot proceed. Mandatory risk reduction (1 mark). - **ALARP Region (Tolerable):** Risk can be accepted only if reduction is impracticable or cost is grossly disproportionate. Residual risk must be documented (1 mark). - **Acceptable Region (Negligible):** Risk is so low that no further action is required beyond routine monitoring (1 mark). **Gross Disproportion Test (2 marks):** The decision to implement or forego additional risk reduction is governed by: $$\text{If } \frac{\text{Cost of Risk Reduction}}{\text{Risk Reduction Benefit}} \text{ is grossly disproportionate, do not implement.}$$ In practice, courts have used a "factor of 10 to 100" as the threshold of gross disproportion. If the cost is ≤ 10× the benefit, the measure must be implemented; if it exceeds 100× the benefit, the measure may be omitted (2 marks). --- **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:** **Given Data:** - Failure rate per km = $3 \times 10^{-4}$ per year - Length = 5 km - Population = 200 - Conditional probability of fatality = 0.6 **Step 1: Total Failure Frequency (2 marks)** $$f_{total} = (3 \times 10^{-4}) \times 5 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per year}$$ **Step 2: Individual Risk (2 marks)** $$IR = f_{total} \times p_{fatality} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6 = 9.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per person-year}$$ **Step 3: Expected Annual Fatalities (2 marks)** $$E[F] = IR \times N = 9.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 200 = 0.18 \text{ fatalities/year}$$ **Step 4: Return Period (1 mark)** $$T = \frac{1}{f_{total}} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 667 \text{ years}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Risk Questions:** > 1. **Unit confusion:** Mixing up *per year* frequency with *dimensionless* probability. Always check units in the final answer. (1-mark deduction) > 2. **Additivity misuse:** Students often multiply all frequencies and consequences together instead of summing them when scenarios are independent. (2-mark deduction) > 3. **Confusing IR and SR:** Individual Risk is per person; Societal Risk is per population. They are not interchangeable. (1-mark deduction) > 4. **Forgetting the conditional probability $p_i$:** Many KTU answers omit $p_i$ in IR calculations, treating the consequence as certain. (2-mark deduction) > 5. **ALARP without numbers:** Students describe ALARP qualitatively but fail to compute the Risk Ratio numerically. Always calculate $\text{Risk Ratio} = IR_{actual}/IR_{target}$ for full marks. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Risk (Module 1)** - **Risk Definition:** $R = P \times C$ — the *quantitative* combination of probability and consequence. - **Hazard vs Risk:** Hazard is the *potential* source; Risk is the *quantified* outcome. - **Individual Risk (IR):** $\sum f_i p_i$ — measured per person per year. - **Societal Risk (SR):** Expressed via F-N Curves; $F(N) = \sum_{C_i \geq N} f_i$. - **Return Period:** $T = 1/f$ — average years between events. - **Risk Matrix:** Likelihood × Consequence grid (5×5 standard). - **ALARP Principle:** "As Low As Reasonably Practicable" — three regions: Unacceptable / Tolerable / Acceptable. - **Gross Disproportion Test:** Cost of further reduction vs. safety benefit; factor of 10–100 is the typical threshold. - **Pure Risk:** Loss only, no gain. **Speculative Risk:** Loss or gain. - **Voluntary Risk:** Personally accepted. **Involuntary Risk:** Imposed on others. - **F-N Curve Slope:** $-1$ for upper limit, $-2$ for lower limit (HSE-UK standard). - **Common Units:** Frequency (per year), Probability (dimensionless), IR (per person-year), Expected Loss (INR/year or fatalities/year). - **Additivity Rule:** For independent scenarios, $R_{total} = \sum f_i \cdot C_i$ (sum, not product). - **KTU Favourite Numerical Setup:** Always use 3 scenarios with given $f_i$, $p_i$, $C_i$ — compute IR, expected fatalities, and comment on ALARP. - **Kerala Industrial Context:** QRA reports are mandatory for chemical industries (FACT, BPCL Kochi) under MSIHC Rules and KSPCB regulations. > **End of Module 1 — Topic: Risk Notes**

topics.title: Chemical Hazards - Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Chemical Hazards - Fire ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**, a **Chemical Hazard** refers to any chemical substance, condition, or process that has the inherent potential to cause harm to human life, property, or the environment. **Fire** is classified as one of the most catastrophic and frequently occurring chemical hazards in process industries (petrochemical, pharmaceutical, oil & gas, and storage terminals). A **Fire** is defined as a rapid, self-sustaining **oxidation-reduction (redox) chemical reaction** accompanied by the evolution of **heat, light (flame), and smoke**. From a thermodynamic and kinetic perspective, fire is an uncontrolled combustion process in which the rate of heat release exceeds the rate of heat dissipation into the surroundings, leading to a self-propagating exothermic chain reaction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Highlight:** > *Fire is a manifestation of a chemical hazard wherein a combustible substance undergoes rapid oxidation with an oxidizer (typically atmospheric oxygen), releasing thermal energy, luminous radiation, and gaseous products of combustion, and is governed by the four elements of the Fire Tetrahedron.* ### 1.2 The Fire Tetrahedron (Modern Paradigm) The classical **Fire Triangle** (Fuel, Heat, Oxygen) was expanded to the **Fire Tetrahedron** to include the **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction**. For a fire to initiate, propagate, and sustain itself, **all four elements must be present simultaneously**. Removing any single element extinguishes the fire. | Element | Chemical/Physical Role | Removal Mechanism | |---|---|---| | **Fuel** | Electron donor (reducing agent) | Starvation, isolation | | **Oxygen (Oxidizer)** | Electron acceptor | Smothering, inerting | | **Heat (Activation Energy)** | Initiates and sustains reaction | Cooling, heat absorption | | **Chain Reaction** | Propagation of free radicals | Chemical inhibition | ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy - "The Bonfire vs. the Matchstick":** > Imagine trying to light a wet log with a single matchstick in a vacuum chamber — it will not ignite. To get fire, you need: the **log (Fuel)**, the **match flame (Heat)**, the **air (Oxygen)**, and a continuous **chain of energy transfer (Chain Reaction)** through the wood fibers. If you pour water (Heat removal) on the fire, it dies. If you cover it with a blanket (Oxygen removal), it dies. If you cut the log into pieces and scatter them (Fuel separation), it dies. If you spray a fire retardant (Chain inhibitor), it dies. This is the practical reality of the Fire Tetrahedron and forms the foundation of every fire prevention and suppression strategy taught in KTU's QRA framework. ### 1.4 Standard Units and Constants in Fire Hazard Analysis - **Standard Temperature**: $T_0 = 298.15 \text{ K}$ ($25^\circ\text{C}$) - **Standard Pressure**: $P_0 = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ ($1 \text{ atm}$) - **Oxygen Concentration in Air**: $21\%$ by volume (mole fraction $\approx 0.21$) - **Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement**: Determined by fuel chemistry - **Heat of Combustion ($\Delta H_c$)**: Expressed in **kJ/kg** or **kJ/kmol** - **Universal Gas Constant**: $R_u = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Fire Parameters (KTU High-Yield Concepts) To perform a quantitative risk assessment on fire hazards, engineers must first characterize the **fire properties** of the chemical substances. The following parameters are critical and frequently tested in KTU examinations: #### 2.1.1 Flash Point (FP) The **minimum temperature** at which a liquid gives off enough vapor to form an **ignitable mixture** with air near the liquid surface. At the flash point, the vapor can be ignited momentarily, but **sustained combustion does not occur**. #### 2.1.2 Fire Point (FIP) The temperature at which the vapor produced by the liquid burns **continuously for at least 5 seconds** after ignition. Fire Point is **typically 5–10 °C higher** than the Flash Point. #### 2.1.3 Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT) The **minimum temperature** at which a substance ignites **spontaneously** without any external ignition source (no spark or flame). This is purely a function of the material's chemical kinetics and surrounding pressure. #### 2.1.4 Flammability Limits (LEL & UEL) - **LEL (Lower Explosive Limit)**: The minimum concentration of fuel vapor in air below which combustion cannot occur (mixture too lean). - **UEL (Upper Explosive Limit)**: The maximum concentration of fuel vapor in air above which combustion cannot occur (mixture too rich). The range between LEL and UEL is called the **Flammability Range**. #### 2.1.5 Heat of Combustion ($\Delta H_c$) The energy released when **1 mole (or 1 kg)** of a substance undergoes complete combustion with oxygen. It is a direct measure of the **fire load** of a material. #### 2.1.6 Adiabatic Flame Temperature ($T_{ad}$) The maximum theoretical temperature attained when combustion occurs under **adiabatic (no heat loss) conditions**. This is the upper bound for any fire scenario. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio | $A/F = \dfrac{4.773 \times \text{mass O}_2 \text{ required}}{\text{mass of fuel}}$ | kg air / kg fuel | Combustion system design | | 2 | Mass Burning Rate (Pool Fire) | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_{net}^{*}}{H_v}$ | kg/(m²·s) | Pool fire modeling | | 3 | Heat of Combustion | $\Delta H_c = \sum H_{products} - \sum H_{reactants}$ | kJ/mol | Fire load calculation | | 4 | Adiabatic Flame Temperature | $\sum n_i \int_{T_0}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,i}(T) \, dT = -\Delta H_c$ | K | Max fire temperature | | 5 | Fire Load Density | $q = \dfrac{\sum m_i \cdot \Delta H_{c,i}}{A_{floor}}$ | MJ/m² | Building fire safety | | 6 | Radiative Heat Flux (Point Source) | $q'' = \dfrac{\chi_r \cdot \dot{Q}}{4\pi r^2}$ | kW/m² | Thermal radiation dose | | 7 | Thomas Plume Rise | $\Delta h = 5.86 \cdot \dot{Q}^{2/5}$ | m | Smoke layer interface | | 8 | LFL Temperature (Carten's Eqn) | $T_{LFL} = T_{ref} \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{R/T_{ref} \cdot \ln(P_{LFL}/P_0)}{\Delta H_{vap}/R}\right]^{-1}$ | K | Flash point estimation | | 9 | Heskestad Flame Height | $L = 0.235 \cdot \dot{Q}^{2/5} - 1.02 \cdot D$ | m | Jet diffusion flames | | 10 | Crowl-Louvar LFL Estimation | $LFL \approx \dfrac{4.773}{M \cdot (N_C + N_S)} \cdot 100$ | mol % | For hydrocarbons | Where: $\chi_r$ = radiative fraction (typically $0.15$ to $0.40$), $\dot{Q}$ = total heat release rate (kW), $r$ = distance from fire (m), $C_p$ = specific heat at constant pressure, $R$ = universal gas constant, $P$ = vapor pressure, $D$ = pool diameter, $H_v$ = heat of vaporization. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** In numerical problems involving fire risk, the **radiative heat flux** ($q''$) and the **heat release rate** ($\dot{Q}$) are the two most commonly tested parameters. Always carry forward units explicitly during derivations to secure full marks. ### 2.3 Classification of Fires (KTU & Indian Standards - IS 15683) | Class | Fuel Type | Examples | Suppressant | |---|---|---|---| | **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber | Water, foam | | **Class B** | Flammable liquids & gases | Petrol, LPG, acetone, methane | Foam, dry chemical, CO₂ | | **Class C** | Flammable gases under pressure | Hydrogen, propane, acetylene | Dry chemical, inert gas | | **Class D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium, sodium, potassium | Special dry powder | | **Class E (K)** | Cooking oils & fats | Vegetable oils, animal fats | Wet chemical | ### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Fire Parameter - **Why Flash Point matters in QRA**: Determines the safe storage and handling temperature of flammable liquids. Storing a liquid at temperatures **below its FP** eliminates the fire risk. - **Why AIT matters**: Materials can self-heat (e.g., in silos, oily rags) and reach AIT without any spark — a leading cause of industrial fires. - **Why LEL/UEL matter**: For ventilation design and gas detection system calibration. Detectors are typically set to alarm at **25% of LEL**. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Combustion of Methane (CH₄) This is the most frequently tested derivation in KTU examinations. We derive the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio and the products of combustion for methane. **Step 1: Write the unbalanced chemical equation.** $$\text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 2: Balance the carbon atoms.** Carbon appears once on each side — already balanced (1 C atom on each side). **Step 3: Balance the hydrogen atoms.** Four H atoms on the left → need 2 H₂O on the right. $$\text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 4: Balance the oxygen atoms.** RHS has 2 (in CO₂) + 2 (in 2H₂O) = 4 O atoms → need 2 O₂ on the LHS. $$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 5: Convert oxygen requirement to air requirement.** Air contains 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume (mole fraction). So per mole of O₂, we have 3.76 moles of N₂. **Step 6: Calculate moles of air required per mole of CH₄.** $$\text{Moles of air} = 2 \times (1 + 3.76) = 2 \times 4.76 = 9.52 \text{ mol air / mol CH}_4$$ **Step 7: Convert to mass basis.** $$\text{Mass of air} = 9.52 \times 29 \text{ g/mol} = 276.08 \text{ g}$$ $$\text{Mass of CH}_4 = 1 \times 16 \text{ g/mol} = 16 \text{ g}$$ **Step 8: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F).** $$A/F = \dfrac{276.08}{16} = 17.255 \text{ kg air / kg fuel}$$ **Final Stoichiometric Equation:** $$\text{CH}_4 + 9.52 \text{ Air} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52 \text{ N}_2$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** This means **17.255 kg of air** is required to completely combust **1 kg of methane**. Any deviation (excess or deficiency) of air leads to incomplete combustion, soot formation, and reduced heat release. ### 3.2 Derivation: Heat Release Rate from a Pool Fire The **heat release rate (HRR or $\dot{Q}$)** is the single most important variable in fire risk assessment. We derive it for a circular pool fire. **Step 1: Define the mass burning rate per unit area.** $$\dot{m}'' = \dfrac{\text{mass of fuel burned per unit time}}{\text{area of pool}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{A_p}$$ **Step 2: Use the Hottel's correlation for mass burning rate of a pool fire.** $$\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right)$$ where: - $\dot{m}''_{\infty}$ = asymptotic mass burning rate (kg/m²/s) — depends on fuel - $k\beta$ = optical extinction coefficient × mean beam length correction - $D$ = pool diameter (m) **Step 3: For large pools ($D \to \infty$), the term $e^{-k\beta D} \to 0$, so:** $$\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty}$$ **Step 4: Total mass burning rate.** $$\dot{m} = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \cdot A_p = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$$ **Step 5: Heat release rate from fuel combustion.** $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi_c$$ where: - $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion (kJ/kg) - $\chi_c$ = combustion efficiency (typically 0.8 to 0.95) **Step 6: Final consolidated equation for a large pool fire.** $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi_c$$ **Step 7: Substituting values for a typical hexane pool fire.** Given: $D = 5 \text{ m}$, $\dot{m}''_{\infty} = 0.078 \text{ kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}$, $\Delta H_c = 44{,}566 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $\chi_c = 0.85$: $$\dot{Q} = 0.078 \times \dfrac{\pi \times 25}{4} \times 44566 \times 0.85$$ $$\dot{Q} = 0.078 \times 19.635 \times 44566 \times 0.85$$ $$\dot{Q} \approx 57{,}950 \text{ kW} \approx 58 \text{ MW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to multiply by the combustion efficiency $\chi_c$. A 15% error here can lead to severe underestimation of thermal radiation hazards. ### 3.3 Derivation: Adiabatic Flame Temperature The adiabatic flame temperature is derived from the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a steady-flow, adiabatic, constant-pressure combustion process. **Step 1: Energy balance equation.** $$H_{reactants}(T_0) = H_{products}(T_{ad})$$ **Step 2: Express the enthalpy of reactants and products.** $$\sum_j n_{r,j} \cdot \int_{T_0}^{T_{ref}} C_{p,r,j}(T) \, dT + \sum_j n_{r,j} \cdot h^{\circ}_{f,r,j} = \sum_k n_{p,k} \cdot h^{\circ}_{f,p,k} + \sum_k n_{p,k} \cdot \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,p,k}(T) \, dT$$ **Step 3: For constant $C_p$ (simplified assumption), integrate.** $$\sum_j n_{r,j} C_{p,r,j}(T_0 - T_{ref}) + \sum_j n_{r,j} h^{\circ}_{f,r,j} = \sum_k n_{p,k} h^{\circ}_{f,p,k} + \sum_k n_{p,k} C_{p,p,k}(T_{ad} - T_{ref})$$ **Step 4: Rearrange to solve for $T_{ad}$.** $$T_{ad} = T_{ref} + \dfrac{\sum_j n_{r,j} h^{\circ}_{f,r,j} - \sum_k n_{p,k} h^{\circ}_{f,p,k} + \sum_j n_{r,j} C_{p,r,j}(T_0 - T_{ref})}{\sum_k n_{p,k} C_{p,p,k}}$$ **Step 5: The numerator is essentially $-\Delta H_c$ (with sensible heat correction).** $$T_{ad} = T_{ref} + \dfrac{-\Delta H_c + \sum_j n_{r,j} C_{p,r,j}(T_0 - T_{ref})}{\sum_k n_{p,k} C_{p,p,k}}$$ **Step 6: Numerical example for methane (CH₄).** Given data: $\Delta H_c(\text{CH}_4) = -802{,}300 \text{ kJ/kmol}$, $T_0 = 298 \text{ K}$, $T_{ref} = 298 \text{ K}$. Products: 1 CO₂ + 2 H₂O + 7.52 N₂ (per kmol CH₄). Mean $C_p$ values (kJ/kmol·K): $C_{p,\text{CO}_2} = 44$, $C_{p,\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 36$, $C_{p,\text{N}_2} = 29$. $$\sum_k n_{p,k} C_{p,p,k} = 1(44) + 2(36) + 7.52(29) = 44 + 72 + 218.08 = 334.08 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}$$ $$T_{ad} = 298 + \dfrac{802{,}300 - 0}{334.08} = 298 + 2401.5 \approx 2699.5 \text{ K}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The theoretical adiabatic flame temperature of methane is ~2700 K. In real fires, actual temperatures are much lower (800–1200 K in compartment fires) due to **radiative and convective heat losses**. ### 3.4 Derivation: Radiative Heat Flux at a Distance (Point Source Model) For a fire of total heat release rate $\dot{Q}$ and radiative fraction $\chi_r$, the radiative heat flux at distance $r$ is: $$q'' = \dfrac{\chi_r \cdot \dot{Q}}{4\pi r^2}$$ **Derivation logic:** A point source radiates uniformly in all directions over a spherical surface of area $4\pi r^2$. The fraction of total power radiated as thermal radiation is $\chi_r$. So the radiative flux at any distance $r$ is total radiative power divided by the sphere's surface area. **For a solid flame (surface emitter model):** $$q'' = \chi_r \cdot \dot{Q} \cdot \text{View Factor} / A_{receiver}$$ **Worked Example:** A pool fire releases $\dot{Q} = 10{,}000 \text{ kW}$ with $\chi_r = 0.3$. Find the radiative heat flux at $r = 25 \text{ m}$. $$q'' = \dfrac{0.3 \times 10000}{4\pi \times 25^2} = \dfrac{3000}{7853.98} \approx 0.382 \text{ kW/m}^2$$ This is sufficient to cause **second-degree burns** on exposed skin within 30 seconds, and **ignite wooden materials**. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Fire Tetrahedron (Mermaid Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A1[Fuel
Reducing Agent]:::fuel A2[Oxygen
Oxidizer 21 percent]:::oxygen A3[Heat
Activation Energy]:::heat A4[Chain Reaction
Free Radicals]:::chain B1[Solid Wood]:::example B2[Liquid Petrol]:::example B3[Gas Methane]:::example C1[Air Atmosphere]:::example C2[Pure O2 Tank]:::example C3[Chemical Oxidizer]:::example D1[Spark Source]:::example D2[Hot Surface]:::example D3[Self Heating]:::example E1[H radical]:::example E2[OH radical]:::example E3[O radical]:::example F1[FIRE EVENT
SUSTAINED COMBUSTION]:::fire G1[Cooling
Water Spray]:::extinguish G2[Smothering
CO2 Blanket]:::extinguish G3[Starvation
Fuel Removal]:::extinguish G4[Inhibition
Dry Chemical]:::extinguish A1 --> F1 A2 --> F1 A3 --> F1 A4 --> F1 B1 --> A1 B2 --> A1 B3 --> A1 C1 --> A2 C2 --> A2 C3 --> A2 D1 --> A3 D2 --> A3 D3 --> A3 E1 --> A4 E2 --> A4 E3 --> A4 F1 --> G1 F1 --> G2 F1 --> G3 F1 --> G4 classDef fuel fill:#FFB347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef oxygen fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef heat fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef chain fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef example fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef fire fill:#FF0000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF classDef extinguish fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Stages of Fire Development in a Compartment ```mermaid graph LR S1[Stage 1
Incipient
Smoldering]:::s1 S2[Stage 2
Growth
Flame Spread]:::s2 S3[Stage 3
Flashover
Fully Developed]:::s3 S4[Stage 4
Steady Burning
Decay]:::s4 S5[Stage 5
Extinction
Fuel Depletion]:::s5 P1[Detection Systems
Smoke Sensors]:::protect P2[Suppression Stage 1
Portable Extinguisher]:::protect P3[Suppression Stage 2
Sprinkler Activation]:::protect P4[Suppression Stage 3
Fire Brigade]:::protect S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S1 -.Detected.-> P1 S2 -.Intervention.-> P2 S3 -.Critical.-> P3 S4 -.Major Response.-> P4 classDef s1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef s2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef s3 fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef s4 fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef s5 fill:#696969,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef protect fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fire Risk Assessment ```mermaid graph TD B1[Hazard Identification
Inventory of Flammables]:::input B2[Fire Property Data
FP AIT LEL UEL]:::input B3[Scenario Selection
Pool Jet Pool Flash]:::input B4[Source Model
Mass Burning Rate HRR]:::process B5[Fire Plume Model
Heskestad Thomas]:::process B6[Radiative Flux Model
Point Source Solid Flame]:::process B7[Consequence Analysis
Thermal Dose Damage]:::process B8[Frequency Analysis
Ignition Probability]:::process B9[Risk Estimation
Individual Societal]:::output B10[Safety Measures
Inerting Detection]:::output B1 --> B4 B2 --> B4 B3 --> B4 B4 --> B5 B5 --> B6 B6 --> B7 B1 --> B8 B2 --> B8 B3 --> B8 B7 --> B9 B8 --> B9 B9 --> B10 classDef input fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef process fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.4 Fire Propagation Mechanism Flow ```mermaid graph TD Q1[Heat from Flame to Fuel]:::heat Q2[Fuel Pyrolysis and Vaporization]:::process Q3[Mixing of Vapor with Air]:::process Q4[Ignition in Flammability Range]:::process Q5[Flame Established]:::fire Q6[Radiative and Convective Feedback]:::heat Q1 --> Q2 Q2 --> Q3 Q3 --> Q4 Q4 --> Q5 Q5 --> Q6 Q6 --> Q1 classDef heat fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef process fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef fire fill:#FF0000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define Fire with reference to the Fire Tetrahedron. List the four elements and explain how removing any one element extinguishes the fire.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Fire is a rapid, self-sustaining oxidation-reduction chemical reaction that releases heat, light, and gaseous products of combustion. **Four Elements of the Fire Tetrahedron (1 mark):** 1. **Fuel** – The combustible material (solid, liquid, or gas). 2. **Oxygen (Oxidizer)** – Supports the oxidation reaction (typically 21% in air). 3. **Heat (Activation Energy)** – Energy required to initiate and sustain combustion. 4. **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction** – Free radical propagation (H, OH, O) that sustains the fire. **Extinguishment Mechanism (1 mark):** Removal of any single element breaks the tetrahedron. Examples: water cools (removes heat), CO₂ smothers (removes oxygen), foam starves (removes fuel), dry chemical powder interrupts chain reaction (free radical inhibition). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Flash Point, Fire Point, and Auto-Ignition Temperature. Why is this distinction critical in chemical plant design?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Definition (1 mark) | Plant Design Relevance (0.5 marks each) | |---|---|---| | **Flash Point (FP)** | Minimum temperature at which liquid gives off enough vapor to form an ignitable mixture with air, but the vapor burns only momentarily. | Storage tanks are designed to operate at **T < FP − 5 °C** (safety margin). | | **Fire Point (FIP)** | Temperature at which vapor burns **continuously for ≥ 5 seconds** after ignition (FIP > FP by 5–10 °C). | Defines the minimum temperature for sustained pool fire scenarios in QRA. | | **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** | Minimum temperature at which a substance ignites **spontaneously without any external ignition source**. | Determines the safe operating temperature for hot surfaces, heat tracing, and process equipment (T < 0.8 × AIT). | **Critical Plant Design Relevance (1 mark):** This distinction is critical because the **storage and processing temperature regimes** for each parameter are different, and failure to design for the wrong parameter leads to catastrophic fires. E.g., storing hexane at 30 °C (FP = −22 °C) is safe from ignition, but if it leaks onto a hot surface at 225 °C (AIT of hexane = 225 °C), it auto-ignites. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- **PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q.A) (a) A process unit contains 50 kg of acetone stored in an open circular pool of diameter 3 m. Acetone spills and forms a pool fire. Given: Heat of combustion of acetone = 29,080 kJ/kg, Mass burning rate = 0.039 kg/(m²·s), Combustion efficiency = 0.85, Radiative fraction = 0.25. Compute:** - **(i) The total heat release rate (HRR) of the fire. (4 marks)** - **(ii) The radiative heat flux received by a worker located 20 m from the fire center. (3 marks)** **(b) Describe the FIVE stages of fire development in a typical compartment fire with a neat labeled sketch. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Q.A (a)(i): Total Heat Release Rate (4 marks)** **Step 1:** Calculate the pool area. $$A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 3^2}{4} = \dfrac{9\pi}{4} = 7.069 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2:** Calculate the total mass burning rate. $$\dot{m} = \dot{m}'' \times A_p = 0.039 \times 7.069 = 0.2757 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate the heat release rate. $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \times \Delta H_c \times \chi_c$$ $$\dot{Q} = 0.2757 \times 29080 \times 0.85$$ $$\dot{Q} = 681.3 \text{ kW (approx)}$$ **Final Answer (1 mark): $\dot{Q} \approx 681.3 \text{ kW}$** **Q.A (a)(ii): Radiative Heat Flux at 20 m (3 marks)** **Step 1:** Apply the point source model. $$q'' = \dfrac{\chi_r \cdot \dot{Q}}{4\pi r^2}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute values. $$q'' = \dfrac{0.25 \times 681.3}{4\pi \times 20^2} = \dfrac{170.325}{5026.55}$$ $$q'' = 0.0339 \text{ kW/m}^2 = 33.9 \text{ W/m}^2$$ **Final Answer (1 mark): $q'' \approx 33.9 \text{ W/m}^2$** **Interpretation:** This flux is well below the threshold for skin burns (1 kW/m²) and ignition of wood (12.5 kW/m²), so the worker at 20 m is at **low immediate risk**, but PPE and procedural controls are still required. --- **Q.A (b): Five Stages of Compartment Fire (7 marks)** **Stage 1 — Incipient/Smoldering (1 mark):** Initial slow combustion of the fuel with low heat release. Smoldering may transition to flaming if heat accumulates. Detection systems (smoke sensors) are designed to act here. **Stage 2 — Growth (1.5 marks):** The flame spreads to adjacent combustibles, HRR increases (often following a $t^2$ growth curve: $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$). Radiative feedback to the fuel accelerates pyrolysis. Fire may still be controlled with a portable extinguisher. **Stage 3 — Flashover (2 marks):** A critical transition where all exposed combustible surfaces in the compartment ignite almost simultaneously as the upper gas layer temperature reaches ~600 °C. HRR surges to MW levels. Survival without PPE is impossible. Sprinklers must act before or during this stage. **Stage 4 — Fully Developed/Steady Burning (1.5 marks):** All combustibles are involved. HRR is maximum and may be limited by ventilation (oxygen supply) or fuel availability. External fire spread to adjacent structures is the major concern. **Stage 5 — Decay/Extinction (1 mark):** Fuel is consumed, HRR decreases monotonically. The fire may transition back to smoldering before complete extinction. During decay, risks include backdraft (sudden re-ignition when oxygen is reintroduced) and structural collapse due to prolonged heat exposure. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When listing fire stages, students frequently miss the distinction between **backdraft** (oxygen-starved fire that explodes when air is admitted) and **flashover** (thermal radiation ignition of all combustibles). These are different phenomena tested in viva and Part B. Do NOT confuse them. --- **PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q.B) (a) With the help of a stoichiometric analysis, determine the Air-Fuel ratio for the complete combustion of Propane (C₃H₈). The fuel is supplied with 25% excess air. Calculate the actual A/F ratio and the mole fraction of CO₂ in the products. (7 marks)** **(b) Explain the terms Lower Explosive Limit (LEL), Upper Explosive Limit (UEL), and Flammable Range. Describe how LEL data is used in the design of gas detection systems in chemical industries.
