# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the following terms with reference to a toxic gas release scenario:**
**(a)** Threshold Limit Value (TLV)
**(b)** IDLH
**(c)** Emergency Response Planning Guideline (ERPG)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) TLV:** The **Threshold Limit Value** is an airborne concentration of a toxic substance below which, according to ACGIH, no worker is expected to suffer adverse health effects during an 8-hour day, 40-hour week exposure. It is a TIME-WEIGHTED AVERAGE (TWA) reference value used for occupational hygiene.

**(b) IDLH:** The **Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health** concentration is the maximum airborne level from which an unprotected worker could escape within **30 minutes** without experiencing irreversible health effects or impairment that would prevent escape. Defined by NIOSH / OSHA. IDLH values are used to set the levels for respirator use.

**(c) ERPG:** The **Emergency Response Planning Guideline** is defined by AIHA as the maximum airborne concentration to which nearly all individuals can be exposed for up to 1 hour without experiencing:
- ERPG-1: effects other than mild transient health effects
- ERPG-2: irreversible or other serious health effects
- ERPG-3: life-threatening health effects

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List the six Pasquill–Gifford atmospheric stability classes and explain which one is most commonly used as the QRA "default" stability.**

**Model Answer:**

The six Pasquill–Gifford stability classes are:
- **A** — Very unstable (strong convection, looping plumes)
- **B** — Unstable
- **C** — Slightly unstable
- **D** — **Neutral** ← QRA default
- **E** — Stable
- **F** — Very stable (fanning, fumigation)

**Class D (Neutral)** is the most commonly used default in QRA because it represents the most *frequently occurring* atmospheric condition (overcast skies or moderate wind speeds) and avoids biasing the risk toward extreme (conservative or non-conservative) cases. The Pasquill class is determined by surface wind speed and solar insolation (day) or cloud cover (night).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]

**(a) (7 marks)** A horizontal cylindrical vessel contains 50 tonnes of liquid chlorine at 25 °C (saturation pressure ≈ 8.6 bar absolute). A 25 mm diameter hole is formed at the bottom of the vessel. Estimate the **liquid release rate** in kg/s assuming a sharp-edged orifice ($C_d = 0.61$). Given: $\rho_L = 1470 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_{p,L} = 950 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $T_b = 239 \text{ K}$, $H_v = 2.87 \times 10^5 \text{ J/kg}$. Also calculate the **flashing fraction** $f_v$.

**(b) (7 marks)** Assuming an airborne release fraction of 0.6 and a release rate of 33.95 kg/s, use the **Gaussian Plume Model** to determine the **maximum ground-level concentration** $C_{\max}$ and the **critical downwind distance** $x_c$ for an effective release height $H = 25 \text{ m}$ in **Pasquill D stability** with $u = 5 \text{ m/s}$. Comment on the significance of $C_{\max}$ in QRA.

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Hole area
$$A_h = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Pressure driving force
$$P - P_a = (8.6 - 1.013) \times 10^5 = 7.587 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3:** Liquid release rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_L &= C_d \cdot A_h \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_L (P - P_a)} \\
&= 0.61 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2 \times 1470 \times 7.587 \times 10^5} \\
&= 0.61 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2.231 \times 10^9} \\
&= 0.61 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 4.723 \times 10^4 \\
&= 14.14 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Flashing fraction

$$
f_v = \dfrac{950 \times (298 - 239)}{2.87 \times 10^5} = \dfrac{95 \times 59}{2870} = 1.953
$$

Wait — let me recompute carefully:

$$
f_v = \dfrac{950 \times 59}{287{,}000} = \dfrac{56{,}050}{287{,}000} = 0.1953 \approx 0.20
$$

**Result:** $Q_L = 14.14 \text{ kg/s}$ and $f_v = 0.20$. The air-borne release rate: $Q_{\text{air}} = 0.6 \times 14.14 = 8.48 \text{ kg/s}$.

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

Use $Q = 33.95 \text{ kg/s}$ (as per given instruction), $H = 25 \text{ m}$, Pasquill D, $u = 5 \text{ m/s}$.

**Step 1:** Maximum ground concentration $C_{\max}$ occurs when $H^2/(2\sigma_z^2) = 1$, i.e., $\sigma_z = H/\sqrt{2} = 17.68 \text{ m}$. From Pasquill D formula $\sigma_z = 30.38 (x/1000)^{0.7382}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
17.68 &= 30.38 \left(\dfrac{x_c}{1000}\right)^{0.7382} \\
\left(\dfrac{x_c}{1000}\right)^{0.7382} &= 0.582 \\
\dfrac{x_c}{1000} &= 0.582^{1/0.7382} = 0.582^{1.355} = 0.485 \\
x_c &= 485 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** $\sigma_y$ at $x_c = 485 \text{ m}$:

$$
\sigma_y(485) = 68.28 \times (0.485)^{0.9031} = 68.28 \times 0.515 = 35.16 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3:** Maximum ground concentration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\max} &= \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot e^{-1} \\
&= \dfrac{33.95}{\pi \times 5 \times 35.16 \times 17.68} \times 0.3679 \\
&= \dfrac{33.95}{9{,}758.6} \times 0.3679 \\
&= 3.479 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3679 = 1.28 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

In ppm (Cl₂, M = 71):
$$C_{\max,\text{ppm}} = \dfrac{1.28 \times 10^{-3} \times 24.45 \times 10^3}{71} = 0.441 \text{ ppm}$$

**Significance in QRA:** $C_{\max}$ is the highest possible ground concentration. If $C_{\max} < \text{ERPG-1}$, the release is of negligible concern. If $C_{\max} > \text{ERPG-3}$ or $>\text{LC}_{50}$, the consequence is potentially catastrophic, requiring evacuation planning. $x_c$ defines the **lethal zone** downwind.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]

**(a) (7 marks)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive the **Gaussian Plume Model** for a continuous, elevated, point source. Clearly state the underlying assumptions and the physical meaning of $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$.

**(b) (7 marks)** A chlorine leak produces a ground-level concentration of $C = 30 \text{ ppm}$ for an exposure time of $t = 15 \text{ min}$ at a residential location. Using the **Probit equation for chlorine** ($a = -5.3$, $b = 3.8$, $n = 1$), determine the **percentage of fatalities** in the exposed population. Comment on the risk implications.

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Derivation of the Gaussian Plume Model:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, continuous release (time-averaged).
2. Constant, uniform wind speed $u$ in the $x$-direction.
3. Pasquill-class turbulence with Fickian diffusion (constant eddy diffusivities $K_y, K_z$).
4. Reflection at the ground (no net mass flux through the surface).
5. No chemical reaction, no deposition, no buoyancy effects (passive scalar).

**Governing equation** (steady advection–diffusion):

$$u \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_y \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

**Substitute $K_y = u \sigma_y^2 / (2x)$ and $K_z = u \sigma_z^2 / (2x)$** (Taylor's statistical theory of turbulent diffusion, where $\sigma$ grows as $x^{1/2}$ in the far field) and apply the point-source boundary condition at $(0, 0, H)$ with strength $Q$. The solution is:

$$
C(x,y,z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \left[ \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) \right]
$$

**Physical meaning:**
- $\sigma_y(x)$: standard deviation (in meters) of the crosswind concentration distribution. Represents the **lateral spread** of the plume. Grows with $x$ as the plume mixes.
- $\sigma_z(x)$: standard deviation of the vertical concentration distribution. Represents the **vertical mixing depth**. Determines how quickly the plume reaches the ground.

**Sketch** (verbal description): An elevated point source at $(0,0,H)$ emits a plume in the $+x$ direction. The plume cross-section is an ellipse in the $y$–$z$ plane that grows in both $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ with downwind distance. Below the ground, an image source at $(0,0,-H)$ enforces reflection.

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Toxic Load (Haber's Law, $n = 1$):
$$\text{TL} = C^n \cdot t = 30^1 \times 15 = 450 \text{ ppm·min}$$

**Step 2:** Probit variable:
$$Y = a + b \cdot \ln(\text{TL}) = -5.3 + 3.8 \times \ln(450)$$

$$
\ln(450) = 6.109
$$

$$
Y = -5.3 + 3.8 \times 6.109 = -5.3 + 23.21 = 17.91
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to percentage:
$$P = 50 \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \right] = 50 \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{12.91}{1.414}\right) \right]$$

$$
\text{erf}(9.13) \approx 1.000
$$

$$
P = 50 \times 2 = 100\%
$$

**Result:** **100% of the exposed population is expected to die** under these conditions. This is a severe consequence and demonstrates the catastrophic potential of even a moderately concentrated chlorine release (30 ppm ≈ 6× AEGL-1, 3× ERPG-2, and exceeding IDLH of 10 ppm).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency (MOST COMMON ERROR):** The Probit equation for chlorine uses $C$ in **ppm**, not mg/m³. Always convert: $C_{\text{ppm}} = (C_{\text{mg/m}^3} \times 24.45) / M$, where $M$ is in g/mol and 24.45 is the molar volume at 25 °C, 1 atm.
> 2. **Forgetting the exponential term** $e^{-H^2 / (2\sigma_z^2)}$ in the elevated-source Gaussian plume. Many students write $C = Q/(\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z)$ without the height correction, giving overly conservative results for tall stacks.
> 3. **Confusing $n$ for chlorine** ($n=1$) with $n$ for ammonia ($n=2$). Using the wrong $n$ changes $C^n t$ by orders of magnitude.
> 4. **Forgetting the discharge coefficient $C_d$** in source-rate problems. Default $C_d = 0.61$ for sharp-edged orifices and $C_d = 0.95$ for rounded.
> 5. **Mixing $\sigma$ units:** The Pasquill–Gifford table uses $x$ in **km** and outputs $\sigma$ in m. Convert first!
> 6. **No blank line around `$$` blocks** — marks deducted for messy presentation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Toxic Gas Release (Module 1)**

- **Toxic gas release QRA** is a 3-step process: **(1) Source → (2) Dispersion → (3) Effect**.
- **Source model:** Use Bernoulli for liquid leaks, choked-flow equation for gas leaks, isenthalpic flash for two-phase.
- **Flashing fraction:** $f_v = C_{p,L}(T_s - T_b)/H_v$ — for chlorine ≈ 0.20, for propane ≈ 0.45.
- **Airborne fraction $f_a$** (Green Book): chlorine ≈ 0.6, ammonia ≈ 0.7–0.95, propane ≈ 1.0.
- **Gaussian Plume Model** is valid for **passive (neutrally-buoyant), steady-state, open-terrain** releases.
- **Pasquill class D (neutral)** is the QRA default for atmospheric stability.
- **$\sigma_y, \sigma_z$** are the dispersion coefficients, growing with downwind distance; their ratio controls plume geometry.
- **Effective height $H$** = physical height + buoyancy rise (Briggs plume rise equations).
- **Dense gas** → use Britter–McQuaid or SLAB; do NOT use Gaussian for the near-field heavy-gas regime.
- **Haber's Law:** Toxic Load $= C^n \cdot t$, where $n$ is the toxicological index.
- **Probit equation:** $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$; convert to % affected via the error function.
- **Key toxic gases and Probit values:** **Cl₂** ($a = -5.3, b = 3.8, n = 1$), **NH₃** ($a = -9.82, b = 2.4, n = 2$).
- **Reference thresholds:** TLV (occupational, 8-hr TWA), IDLH (30-min escape), ERPG-1/2/3 (1-hr), AEGL-1/2/3 (EPA).
- **Unit conversion:** $1 \text{ ppm} = M/24.45 \text{ mg/m}^3$ at 25 °C, 1 atm.
- **Discharge coefficient $C_d$** = 0.61 (sharp-edged), 0.95 (rounded).
- **Atmospheric stability classes A–F:** A is most unstable, F is most stable. Dispersion is fastest in A, slowest in F.
- **Maximum ground-level concentration** for elevated source occurs at critical distance $x_c$ where $\sigma_z = H/\sqrt{2}$.
- **Risk = Frequency × Consequence.** Frequency from FMEA/event trees; consequence from the three-step source–dispersion–effect model.
- **Memorize:** $\sigma_y \sigma_z$ product for Pasquill D at common downwind distances.
- **Common error:** Neglecting **aerosol entrainment** in two-phase releases — only flash fraction is NOT the airborne fraction.
- **Indian regulatory framework:** MSIHC Rules 1989, Factories Act 1948 (Sections 41A-41H), EPA Rules, NDMA guidelines, ONGC/OMPL safety standards, OISD-118, OISD-150.
- **Practical implication:** A 10-tonne chlorine release can produce lethal concentrations (LC₅₀) over **1–3 km downwind** under neutral conditions — defines the typical "isolation distance" for land-use planning around chlor-alkali plants.
- **Exam strategy:** Always show all steps; use a table for multi-distance Gaussian calculations; clearly state which dispersion model and which Probit coefficients you are using.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dow Fire & Explosion Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dow Fire & Explosion Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)** is a quantitative hazard evaluation methodology developed by the **Dow Chemical Company** in **1964** (revised periodically, with the 7th edition published in **1994** as the Dow's Fire & Explosion Index Hazard Classification Guide). It is a **ranking system** used to assess the relative magnitude of fire and explosion hazards associated with a process unit in a chemical/petrochemical facility.

The F&EI assigns a numerical value to a process unit based on the **Material Factor (MF)** and the **penalty multipliers** for General Process Hazards and Special Process Hazards. The final F&EI score categorizes the process unit into severity degrees ranging from **"Light"** to **"Catastrophic"**, enabling prioritization of loss-prevention resources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Dow F&EI is a **deterministic, semi-quantitative index-based** tool. It is the most widely used methodology worldwide for ranking the fire/explosion risk of process plants and forms the backbone of Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) studies.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Health Check-Up" for a Chemical Plant

Imagine a process plant is like a **human body**, and the engineer is like a **doctor**:

- The **Material Factor (MF)** is the **inherent constitution of the patient** — i.e., how chemically "hot-blooded" or flammable the substances are (like comparing diesel to gunpowder).
- The **General Process Hazards (GPH)** are like **lifestyle factors** — poor drainage, indoor storage, restricted access — analogous to smoking or sedentary habits.
- The **Special Process Hazards (SPH)** are **specific risk factors** — toxic materials, dust explosions, operating near flammable limits — analogous to specific diseases like diabetes or hypertension.
- The **F&EI score** is the **final risk score** printed on the report card.
- The **Loss Control Credit Factors** are **preventive medicines** that reduce the final damage (analogous to diet, exercise, and checkups).

A plant with a high F&EI needs urgent engineering interventions, just like a patient with high cholesterol needs immediate medication.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Basis

The F&EI methodology is grounded on the **combustion energy** of materials, quantified in terms of the **Heat of Combustion (ΔH_c)** in **BTU/lb** or **kcal/kg**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** Material Factor values are tabulated based on the **NFPA 704 (Diamond)** ratings for **flammability (N_f)**, **reactivity (N_r)**, and the **heat of combustion**. A higher MF means higher intrinsic energy release potential.

| Material Category | Typical MF Range | Example Material |
|---|---|---|
| Combustible liquids | 4 – 10 | Diesel, Fuel Oil |
| Flammable liquids | 12 – 16 | Gasoline, Ethanol |
| Highly flammable gases | 21 – 24 | LPG, Hydrogen, Methane |

---

**Key Acronyms for KTU Examinations:**

| Acronym | Expansion |
|---|---|
| MF | Material Factor |
| GPH / F1 | General Process Hazards |
| SPH / F2 | Special Process Hazards |
| F3 | Process Unit Hazards Factor |
| F&EI | Fire & Explosion Index |
| DF | Damage Factor |
| MPPD | Maximum Probable Property Damage |
| MPDO | Maximum Probable Days Outage |
| BI | Business Interruption |
| LOC | Loss of Containment |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The 12-Step Dow F&EI Methodology

The Dow F&EI calculation follows a strict **top-down hierarchical** logic. Each step feeds into the next, and skipping a step invalidates the entire assessment.

**Step 1 — Select the Process Unit:**
Identify the smallest logical unit of the plant (reactor, distillation column, storage tank, compressor) for analysis.

**Step 2 — Determine the Material Factor (MF):**
Use the Dow-published MF tables or NFPA flammability reactivity data. MF is a **dimensionless integer** typically ranging from **1 to 40**.

**Step 3 — Evaluate General Process Hazards (F1):**
These penalties apply to **plant-layout and operational** features common to all process units. They have six categories:
- Exothermic chemical reactions
- Endothermic processes
- Material handling and transfer
- Enclosed or indoor process units
- Access (limited access for emergency vehicles)
- Drainage and spill control

$$F_1 = 1.0 + \sum (\text{Individual Penalty}_i)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Special Process Hazards (F2):**
These are **process-specific** hazards unique to the unit. They have twelve categories:
- Toxic materials
- Sub-atmospheric pressure operation
- Operation in or near the flammable range
- Dust explosion potential
- Relief pressure (higher pressure → higher penalty)
- Low temperature (brittle fracture)
- Quantity of flammable/unstable material (Q-factor)
- Corrosion and erosion
- Leakage from joints and packing
- Use of fired heaters
- Hot oil heat exchange system
- Rotating equipment

$$F_2 = 1.0 + \sum (\text{Individual Penalty}_j)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Process Unit Hazards Factor (F3):**

$$F_3 = F_1 \times F_2$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI):**

$$\boxed{\text{F\&EI} = MF \times F_3}$$

**Step 7 — Determine the Damage Factor (DF):**
Read DF from the published Dow F&EI vs. DF chart (a non-linear logarithmic curve).

**Step 8 — Compute the Base Maximum Probable Property Damage (Base MPPD):**

$$\text{Base MPPD} = DF \times \text{Replacement Value of Equipment}$$

**Step 9 — Evaluate Loss Control Credit Factors (C1, C2, C3):**

| Credit Factor | Domain |
|---|---|
| **C1** | Process Control Credit |
| **C2** | Material Isolation Credit |
| **C3** | Fire Protection Credit |

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3$$

**Step 10 — Compute Actual MPPD:**

$$\text{Actual MPPD} = \text{Base MPPD} \times C_{\text{total}}$$

**Step 11 — Compute Maximum Probable Days Outage (MPDO):**

$$\log_{10}(\text{MPDO}) = 0.569 \times \log_{10}(\text{Actual MPPD in thousands}) - 0.527$$

**Step 12 — Compute Business Interruption (BI):**

$$\text{BI} = \text{MPDO} \times \frac{\text{Annual Production Value}}{365} \times 0.70$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet**. Memorize the formulas; do not try to derive them under exam pressure.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Material Factor | MF (from tables) | **1 – 40** (dimensionless) |
| 2 | General Process Hazards | $F_1 = 1.0 + \sum P_i$ | **1.00 – 8.00** |
| 3 | Special Process Hazards | $F_2 = 1.0 + \sum P_j$ | **1.00 – 8.00** |
| 4 | Process Unit Hazards Factor | $F_3 = F_1 \times F_2$ | **1.00 – 64.00** |
| 5 | Fire & Explosion Index | $\text{F\&EI} = MF \times F_3$ | **1 – 320** |
| 6 | Damage Factor | DF = f(F&EI) from chart | **0.01 – 1.00** |
| 7 | Base MPPD | Base MPPD = DF × Replacement Value | **Currency** |
| 8 | Loss Control Credit Factor | $C = C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3$ | **0.10 – 1.00** |
| 9 | Actual MPPD | Actual MPPD = Base MPPD × C | **Currency** |
| 10 | Maximum Probable Days Outage | $\log_{10}(\text{MPDO}) = 0.569 \cdot \log_{10}(\text{MPPD}_{k\$}) - 0.527$ | **Days** |
| 11 | Business Interruption | $\text{BI} = \text{MPDO} \times V_{d} \times 0.70$ | **Currency** |

### 2.3 F&EI Severity Classification (KTU Exam Favorite)

| F&EI Range | Degree of Hazard |
|---|---|
| 1 – 60 | Light |
| 61 – 96 | Moderate |
| 97 – 127 | Heavy |
| 128 – 158 | Extreme |
| ≥ 159 | Catastrophic |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Insurance Underwriting:** Insurance companies use F&EI to fix premium rates for chemical plants.
- **Plant Siting Decisions:** Helps decide spacing between units as per **TAPI/CCPS** guidelines.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Many countries (USA, EU, India) mandate F&EI studies under the **OSHA PSM** (Process Safety Management) regulation **29 CFR 1910.119**.
- **Capital Budget Prioritization:** Companies use F&EI rankings to decide where to deploy **HSE capital** first.
- **Design Stage Hazard Identification:** Applied during FEED (Front End Engineering Design) to optimize layout and safety instrumentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always mention at least **two real-world applications** in 14-mark questions to score above 12 marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Full Derivation)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A naphtha hydrotreating process unit has the following data:
- Material Factor, **MF = 16**
- General Process Hazards penalties: Exothermic reaction = 0.30, Material handling = 0.55, Enclosed indoor unit = 0.25, Limited access = 0.20, Drainage = 0.25
- Special Process Hazards penalties: Toxic material = 0.20, Operation near flammable range = 0.60, Quantity factor (Q) = 1.00, Corrosion/erosion = 0.40, Joint leakage = 0.30, Rotating equipment = 0.50
- Replacement value of equipment = **$ 5,000,000**
- Loss Control Credit Factors: C1 = 0.84, C2 = 0.95, C3 = 0.91
- Annual production value = **$ 40 million**

**Calculate: F&EI, Damage Factor, Base MPPD, Actual MPPD, MPDO, BI.**

---

### Step A — General Process Hazards Factor (F1)

$$\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 1.0 + (0.30 + 0.55 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.25) \\
    &= 1.0 + 1.55 \\
    &= 2.55
\end{aligned}$$

### Step B — Special Process Hazards Factor (F2)

$$\begin{aligned}
F_2 &= 1.0 + (0.20 + 0.60 + 1.00 + 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.50) \\
    &= 1.0 + 3.00 \\
    &= 4.00
\end{aligned}$$

### Step C — Process Unit Hazards Factor (F3)

$$\begin{aligned}
F_3 &= F_1 \times F_2 \\
    &= 2.55 \times 4.00 \\
    &= 10.20
\end{aligned}$$

### Step D — Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{F\&EI} &= MF \times F_3 \\
              &= 16 \times 10.20 \\
              &= 163.2
\end{aligned}$$

**Severity:** F&EI = 163.2 falls in the range **≥ 159** → **"Catastrophic"**.

### Step E — Damage Factor (DF)

Reading from the standard Dow F&EI-DF curve at F&EI = 163.2:

$$\boxed{DF \approx 0.68}$$

### Step F — Base MPPD

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Base MPPD} &= DF \times \text{Replacement Value} \\
                  &= 0.68 \times 5{,}000{,}000 \\
                  &= 3{,}400{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step G — Loss Control Credit Factor

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}} &= C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3 \\
                 &= 0.84 \times 0.95 \times 0.91 \\
                 &= 0.7262
\end{aligned}$$

### Step H — Actual MPPD

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual MPPD} &= \text{Base MPPD} \times C_{\text{total}} \\
                    &= 3{,}400{,}000 \times 0.7262 \\
                    &= 2{,}469{,}080 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to thousands:

$$\text{Actual MPPD in thousands} = 2469.08$$

### Step I — Maximum Probable Days Outage (MPDO)

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(\text{MPDO}) &= 0.569 \times \log_{10}(2469.08) - 0.527 \\
                       &= 0.569 \times 3.3926 - 0.527 \\
                       &= 1.9305 - 0.527 \\
                       &= 1.4035
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MPDO} &= 10^{1.4035} \\
            &= 25.31 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step J — Business Interruption (BI)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily production value} &= \frac{40{,}000{,}000}{365} = 109{,}589.04 \text{ USD/day} \\[6pt]
\text{BI} &= \text{MPDO} \times V_d \times 0.70 \\
          &= 25.31 \times 109{,}589.04 \times 0.70 \\
          &= 1{,}941{,}466 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation Summary

| Parameter | Computed Value | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| F&EI | 163.2 | Catastrophic hazard class |
| Damage Factor | 0.68 | 68% of equipment value at risk |
| Base MPPD | $ 3.40 M | Gross exposure without controls |
| Credit Factor | 0.7262 | ~27% risk reduction via controls |
| Actual MPPD | $ 2.47 M | Net exposure after safety systems |
| MPDO | 25.31 days | ~3.6 weeks of forced shutdown |
| BI | $ 1.94 M | Revenue loss on top of property damage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision Trigger:** Any F&EI score above **128 (Extreme)** mandates a **HAZOP revalidation** and a quantitative review of ESD/SIS systems as per **IEC 61511**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Down Calculation Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: SELECT PROCESS UNIT"] --> B["DETERMINE MATERIAL FACTOR MF"]
    B --> C["EVALUATE GENERAL PROCESS HAZARDS F1"]
    C --> D["EVALUATE SPECIAL PROCESS HAZARDS F2"]
    D --> E["COMPUTE F3 = F1 times F2"]
    E --> F["COMPUTE F AND EI = MF times F3"]
    F --> G["READ DAMAGE FACTOR DF FROM CHART"]
    G --> H["COMPUTE BASE MPPD = DF times REPLACEMENT VALUE"]
    H --> I["EVALUATE CREDIT FACTORS C1 C2 C3"]
    I --> J["COMPUTE ACTUAL MPPD = BASE MPPD times C TOTAL"]
    J --> K["COMPUTE MPDO USING LOG EQUATION"]
    K --> L["COMPUTE BUSINESS INTERRUPTION BI"]
    L --> M["FINAL: RANK UNIT BY SEVERITY CLASS"]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0B5394,color:#000
    style B fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#CC0000,color:#000
    style F fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#38761D,color:#000
    style G fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#7F6000,color:#000
    style M fill:#C9DAF8,stroke:#1C4587,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Dow F&EI Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT BLOCK"]
        IN1["PROCESS UNIT IDENTIFICATION"]
        IN2["NFPA 704 DATA"]
        IN3["OPERATING CONDITIONS P T Q"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["COMPUTATION CORE"]
        CORE1["MATERIAL FACTOR LOOKUP TABLE"]
        CORE2["GPH PENALTY ENGINE"]
        CORE3["SPH PENALTY ENGINE"]

        CORE5["DAMAGE FACTOR MAPPING"]
        CORE6["LOSS CONTROL MODULE"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT BLOCK"]
        OUT1["SEVERITY RANK LIGHT MODERATE HEAVY EXTREME CATASTROPHIC"]
        OUT2["PROPERTY DAMAGE BASE MPPD ACTUAL MPPD"]
        OUT3["DOWN TIME MPDO DAYS"]
        OUT4["BUSINESS INTERRUPTION USD"]
    end

    IN1 --> CORE1
    IN2 --> CORE1
    IN3 --> CORE3
    CORE1 --> CORE4
    CORE2 --> CORE4
    CORE3 --> CORE4
    CORE4 --> CORE5
    CORE5 --> OUTPUT
    CORE6 --> OUTPUT

    style INPUT fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#274E13,color:#000
    style CORE fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#7F2704,color:#000
    style OUTPUT fill:#C9DAF8,stroke:#0B1F66,color:#000
    style CORE4 fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#7F6000,color:#000
    style CORE5 fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#7F6000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Severity Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["F AND EI SCORE"] --> S1{"F AND EI LESS THAN 60"}
    S1 -- YES --> R1["LIGHT HAZARD"]
    S1 -- NO --> S2{"F AND EI LESS THAN 96"}
    S2 -- YES --> R2["MODERATE HAZARD"]
    S2 -- NO --> S3{"F AND EI LESS THAN 127"}
    S3 -- YES --> R3["HEAVY HAZARD"]
    S3 -- NO --> S4{"F AND EI LESS THAN 158"}
    S4 -- YES --> R4["EXTREME HAZARD"]
    S4 -- NO --> R5["CATASTROPHIC HAZARD"]

    style S fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#7F6000,color:#000
    style R5 fill:#E06666,stroke:#990000,color:#FFF
    style R4 fill:#F6B26B,stroke:#7F2704,color:#000
    style R3 fill:#FFD966,stroke:#7F6000,color:#000
    style R2 fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#274E13,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#A4C2F4,stroke:#0B1F66,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define the term "Material Factor (MF)" in the context of the Dow Fire & Explosion Index. List any two limitations of using MF as a standalone hazard indicator.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Material Factor (MF)** is a measure of the intrinsic energy release potential of the most hazardous substance handled in a process unit. It is a dimensionless integer ranging typically from **1 to 40**, determined from the **NFPA 704 flammability and reactivity ratings** and the **heat of combustion** of the substance.

For example: Methane has **MF = 21**, Gasoline has **MF = 16**, Diesel has **MF = 10**.

**Two Limitations of MF as standalone indicator:**

1. MF does not account for **process conditions** such as pressure, temperature, quantity of material, or reactive hazards — therefore two units with identical MF can have vastly different actual risk profiles.

2. MF is **static** — it does not incorporate **dynamic operational factors** like maintenance quality, operator competency, or human-factor elements, leading to underestimation of real-world risk.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**What is the significance of the "Process Unit Hazards Factor (F3)"? How is it computed?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Process Unit Hazards Factor (F3)** represents the **combined penalty multiplier** arising from all general and special process hazards evaluated for the unit. It captures how **operational, environmental, and process-specific** factors amplify the intrinsic hazard of the material.

It is computed as the product of the General Process Hazards Factor (F1) and the Special Process Hazards Factor (F2):

$$F_3 = F_1 \times F_2$$

F3 has a typical range of **1.00 to 8.00** for each factor, hence F3 ranges up to about **64**. F3 is the **"amplifier"** that converts the static Material Factor into a process-aware hazard index. The final F&EI = MF × F3.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a) Explain the step-by-step methodology of Dow Fire & Explosion Index calculation with a neat flowchart. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Dow F&EI methodology is implemented in **12 sequential steps** as follows:

**Step 1 — Identify the Process Unit:** Select the smallest logical unit (e.g., a reactor, a distillation column, or a storage tank) for analysis. The unit must have well-defined boundaries and inventory.

**Step 2 — Determine Material Factor (MF):** Refer to Dow's published MF tables. MF depends on NFPA flammability and reactivity ratings and the heat of combustion. It is dimensionless (typically 1–40).

**Step 3 — Evaluate General Process Hazards (F1):** Consider six categories — exothermic reactions, endothermic reactions, material handling, enclosed indoor units, limited access, and drainage/spill control. Each contributes a penalty between 0.1 and 1.25.

$$F_1 = 1.0 + \sum \text{penalties}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Special Process Hazards (F2):** Consider twelve categories including toxic materials, sub-atmospheric pressure, flammable range operation, dust explosion, relief pressure, low temperature, quantity factor Q, corrosion, joint leakage, fired heaters, hot oil systems, and rotating equipment.

$$F_2 = 1.0 + \sum \text{penalties}$$

**Step 5 — Compute F3 = F1 × F2.**

**Step 6 — Compute F&EI = MF × F3.**

**Step 7 — Determine Damage Factor (DF)** from the published F&EI-vs-DF chart (non-linear curve).

**Step 8 — Compute Base MPPD = DF × Replacement Cost.**

**Step 9 — Apply Loss Control Credit Factors (C1, C2, C3):**

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3$$

**Step 10 — Actual MPPD = Base MPPD × C_total.**

**Step 11 — Compute MPDO using:**

$$\log_{10}(\text{MPDO}) = 0.569 \times \log_{10}(\text{Actual MPPD}_{k\$}) - 0.527$$

**Step 12 — Business Interruption = MPDO × Daily Production Value × 0.70.**

The flow of the calculation is given in the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

**(b) A process unit handles acetone. The Material Factor is 16. The General Process Hazards penalties are: Exothermic reaction 0.30, Material handling 0.50, Enclosed unit 0.40, Access 0.20, Drainage 0.25. The Special Process Hazards penalties are: Toxic material 0.20, Operation near flammable range 0.60, Quantity factor Q 1.20, Corrosion 0.30, Joint leakage 0.30, Rotating equipment 0.50. Replacement value is $4,000,000. Credit factors are C1 = 0.85, C2 = 0.94, C3 = 0.92. Compute F&EI, DF, Base MPPD, Actual MPPD, MPDO, and Business Interruption assuming annual production value = $50 million. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) General Process Hazards F1:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 1.0 + (0.30 + 0.50 + 0.40 + 0.20 + 0.25) \\
    &= 1.0 + 1.65 = 2.65
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Special Process Hazards F2:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_2 &= 1.0 + (0.20 + 0.60 + 1.20 + 0.30 + 0.30 + 0.50) \\
    &= 1.0 + 3.10 = 4.10
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Process Unit Hazards Factor F3:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_3 &= F_1 \times F_2 = 2.65 \times 4.10 = 10.865
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Fire & Explosion Index:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{F\&EI} &= MF \times F_3 = 16 \times 10.865 = 173.84
\end{aligned}$$

This is in the **"Catastrophic"** severity class.

**(v) Damage Factor (from Dow F&EI-DF curve at F&EI = 173.84):**

$$DF \approx 0.72$$

**(vi) Base MPPD:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Base MPPD} &= 0.72 \times 4{,}000{,}000 = 2{,}880{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}$$

**(vii) Credit Factor:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 0.85 \times 0.94 \times 0.92 = 0.7351
\end{aligned}$$

**(viii) Actual MPPD:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual MPPD} &= 2{,}880{,}000 \times 0.7351 = 2{,}117{,}088 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to thousands: **2117.088**.

**(ix) MPDO:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(\text{MPDO}) &= 0.569 \times \log_{10}(2117.088) - 0.527 \\
                       &= 0.569 \times 3.3256 - 0.527 \\
                       &= 1.8923 - 0.527 = 1.3653
\end{aligned}$$

$$\text{MPDO} = 10^{1.3653} \approx 23.22 \text{ days}$$

**(x) Business Interruption:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \frac{50{,}000{,}000}{365} = 136{,}986.3 \text{ USD/day} \\
\text{BI} &= 23.22 \times 136{,}986.3 \times 0.70 = 2{,}226{,}797 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Tabulated Result:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| F&EI | 173.84 (Catastrophic) |
| DF | 0.72 |
| Base MPPD | $ 2.88 M |
| Credit Factor | 0.7351 |
| Actual MPPD | $ 2.12 M |
| MPDO | 23.22 days |
| Business Interruption | $ 2.23 M |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) Explain the Loss Control Credit Factors used in Dow F&EI. List at least three sub-categories under each of C1, C2, and C3. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Loss Control Credit Factors** are quantitative multipliers (each between **0 and 1**) that reduce the calculated property damage based on the **presence and quality of safety systems** in the plant. They are applied **after** the F&EI is computed and **before** the final MPPD is calculated.

**C1 — Process Control Credit (max reduction 75%):**
- Emergency power supply
- Cooling system
- Explosion-proof instrumentation
- Inert gas purging
- Computer-based process control (DCS)
- Operating procedures and training
- Reactive chemical management
- Other process hazard analyses

**C2 — Material Isolation Credit (max reduction 75%):**
- Remote control valves
- Dump/blowdown tanks
- Fire-resistant insulation
- Block valves
- Emergency venting
- Purge systems
- Interlocks and emergency shutdown systems (ESD)
- Fire-resistant cabling

**C3 — Fire Protection Credit (max reduction 75%):**
- Leak detection systems
- Fire water supply
- Foam systems
- Water spray (deluge)
- Sprinkler systems
- Fire-resistant coatings
- Fire monitors and hydrants
- Bunding and dyking
- Fire-fighting equipment

The **Total Credit Factor** is:

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3$$

This ranges from approximately **0.10 (highly protected)** to **1.00 (no protection)**.

**Significance:**
- Encourages plants to install robust safety systems by quantifying risk reduction in monetary terms.
- Provides a **cost-benefit basis** for safety investments — a manager can justify spending $X on a new fire protection system by computing the corresponding MPPD reduction.
- Required as part of the **OSHA PSM** and **CCPS** guidelines.

---

**(b) Discuss four major limitations of the Dow Fire & Explosion Index methodology. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Limitation 1 — Index-Based, Not Risk-Based:**
The F&EI produces a **single severity score** but does not directly estimate **probability/frequency** of an incident. It does not give a numerical "risk" (probability × consequence) — therefore it cannot directly feed into a full QRA where individual scenario frequencies are required (e.g., for land-use planning around populated areas).

**Limitation 2 — Subjectivity in Penalty Selection:**
The selection of GPH and SPH penalty values involves **engineering judgment**. Different analysts may assign different penalties to the same unit, leading to **variability in the F&EI score** of 15–25%. There is no rigorous protocol for boundary cases.

**Limitation 3 — Limited Chemical Inventory Coverage:**
The Dow F&EI was developed primarily for **hydrocarbon processing** and may **underestimate** hazards in:
- Highly reactive chemicals (peroxides, acetylene)
- Pharmaceutical fine chemicals
- Dust-handling units
- Cryogenic and biological processes

**Limitation 4 — Does Not Address Toxic or Environmental Hazards:**
The F&EI focuses solely on **fire and explosion**. It does not account for:
- **Toxic gas dispersion** (e.g., chlorine, ammonia releases)
- **Environmental damage** (soil/groundwater contamination)
- **Occupational health hazards** (carcinogens, asphyxiants)

For these, complementary tools like **EPA's RMP (Risk Management Plan)** and **toxic release models** must be used.

**Limitation 5 — Static Methodology:**
The F&EI does not account for **dynamic phenomena** such as:
- Domino effects (cascading unit failures)
- Human reliability and operator error
- Aging of equipment and corrosion over time
- Process deviations and transient upsets

Therefore, it should be supplemented with **HAZOP, LOPA, and dynamic simulation** for complete hazard analysis.

**Conclusion:**
Despite these limitations, the Dow F&EI remains the **industry workhorse** for **first-pass hazard ranking** due to its simplicity, transparency, and use of well-tabulated material factors.

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Common Mistakes in Dow F&EI Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add 1.0** in the F1 and F2 formulas. Students often write F1 = sum of penalties only, forgetting the baseline factor of **1.0**. This causes a 50–80% underestimation of F&EI. **Lose 2 marks instantly.**
>
> 2. **Using wrong units in MPDO equation.** The MPDO log-formula expects Actual MPPD in **thousands of dollars**, not in dollars or millions. Convert properly: Actual MPPD / 1000 before applying the log. **Lose 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Reading DF from the wrong point on the curve.** The F&EI-vs-DF curve is **steep and non-linear**. A 10-point error in F&EI can change DF from 0.42 to 0.55. Always state that DF is **read from the published chart** and quote the chart value. **Lose 2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Confusing Credit Factor values.** Credit factors are **less than or equal to 1**. Values > 1 mean **additional penalty**, which is invalid in Dow's framework. C1, C2, C3 must be in the range **0.10 to 1.00**.
>
> 5. **Not classifying the severity.** Always end your answer with the severity class (Light / Moderate / Heavy / Extreme / Catastrophic). Examiners award 1–2 marks for this summary statement.
>
> 6. **Ignoring annual production value units** in the BI equation. V_d must be in **$/day**, so always divide the annual value by 365.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Dow F&EI (Last-Minute KTU Viva + Exam Cram):**

- 🔹 Dow F&EI was developed by **Dow Chemical Company (1964)**, latest edition 7th (1994).
- 🔹 F&EI = **MF × F3**, where **F3 = F1 × F2**.
- 🔹 F1 has **6 penalty categories**; F2 has **12 penalty categories**.
- 🔹 F1 and F2 **both start at 1.0** + sum of penalties.
- 🔹 F&EI severity classes: **1–60 Light**, **61–96 Moderate**, **97–127 Heavy**, **128–158 Extreme**, **≥ 159 Catastrophic**.
- 🔹 Damage Factor (DF) is read from a **published chart**; it is **not** linearly related to F&EI.
- 🔹 Base MPPD = **DF × Replacement Value**.
- 🔹 Loss Control Credit = **C1 × C2 × C3** (Process Control, Material Isolation, Fire Protection).
- 🔹 Actual MPPD = **Base MPPD × C_total**.
- 🔹 MPDO uses the logarithmic formula: $\log_{10}(\text{MPDO}) = 0.569 \cdot \log_{10}(\text{MPPD}_{k\$}) - 0.527$.
- 🔹 BI = **MPDO × V_d × 0.70**.
- 🔹 F&EI is **index-based, deterministic, and semi-quantitative** — not a true probabilistic risk tool.
- 🔹 F&EI **does not cover toxic, environmental, or occupational health hazards** — must be supplemented.
- 🔹 Applications: insurance rating, plant layout, capital prioritization, OSHA PSM compliance, FEED-stage hazard identification.
- 🔹 Limitations: subjectivity in penalties, static methodology, no domino effect, limited chemical scope.
- 🔹 For **KTU ESE**: always convert MPPD to **thousands of dollars** before applying the MPDO log-formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Toxicity Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Toxicity Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Toxicity Index (TI)** is a quantitative numerical rating used in the **Mond Fire, Explosion and Toxicity Index** methodology (an extension of the Dow Index, developed by ICI's Mond Division) to assess the relative toxic hazard potential of a process plant handling hazardous chemicals. It quantifies the degree of toxic risk by combining the intrinsic toxicity of the substance (via its **Threshold Limit Value — TLV**) with a set of **compensation factors** that account for process conditions, inventory, layout, and ventilation.

Mathematically, the Toxicity Index expresses the effective toxic hazard load of a process unit after penalising the base toxicity for the prevailing operational and environmental conditions that would aggravate a toxic release scenario.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PECHT754 Module 1):** The Toxicity Index is treated as a fundamental component of the **Mond Index** family of hazard evaluation techniques. Students must master its formula, compensation factors, and the interpretation of the final numerical score against standardised hazard rating bands.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are judging how dangerous it is to keep a venomous snake in different rooms of a house:

- **The snake's venom potency** represents the **base Toxicity Factor (T)** — some snakes are far more venomous than others.
- **The size of the snake** represents the **Quantity Factor (Q)** — a longer snake is a bigger threat.
- **How tightly the snake is caged** represents the **Pressure Factor (S)** — a pressurised container is like a coiled, agitated snake.
- **How many exits the room has** represents the **Layout Factor (L)** — fewer exits mean you cannot escape.
- **Whether the windows are open** represents the **Ventilation Factor (B)** — open windows allow the venom to dissipate, reducing danger.

Each of these features amplifies or moderates the **overall toxic danger**, exactly as the compensation factors modify the base toxicity to produce the **Toxicity Index**. A high TI means the situation is structurally dangerous; a low TI means the situation is well-controlled.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

The Toxicity Index procedure depends on the following standardised engineering metrics:

- **TLV (Threshold Limit Value)** — typically expressed in **ppm (parts per million)** or **mg/m³**; represents the airborne concentration to which a worker can be exposed **8 hours/day, 40 hours/week** without adverse health effects. The most common reference is the **TLV-TWA (Time-Weighted Average)** published by the **American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH)**.
- **Operating Pressure (P)** — in **bar (g)** or **kPa (gauge)**.
- **Inventory (Mass of toxic material)** — in **tonnes (t)** or **kg**.
- **Plant Layout Area** — in **m²**.
- **Ventilation Rate** — measured qualitatively (e.g., *poor / adequate / good*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention:** In Mond Index literature, the term **Toxicity Index (TI)** is sometimes called the **Toxicity Rating** or **Toxicity Sub-Index** depending on the edition. KTU 2024 treats TI as the final numerical value *after* all compensation factors are applied.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Foundation — Why a Toxicity Index is Needed

A single substance may have a very low intrinsic toxicity (high TLV), yet if stored in massive quantities, at high pressure, in a poorly ventilated basement, it can pose a hazard comparable to a small quantity of an extremely toxic gas. Therefore, the Toxicity Index must integrate:

1. **How poisonous the substance is** (intrinsic property → TLV).
2. **How the process conditions amplify a release** (pressure, temperature, quantity).
3. **How the plant geometry and ventilation modify the resulting airborne concentration** (layout, ventilation).

The Mond Index uses a multiplicative model: a base toxicity factor is **penalised (i.e., increased)** by additive compensation factors, then **further penalised** by the ventilation factor. The result is a single dimensionless number that classifies the toxic risk.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Construction of the Toxicity Index

The Toxicity Index is built in **five sequential logical steps**:

### Step 1 — Determine the Base Toxicity Factor (T)
Look up the TLV-TWA of the substance from ACGIH tables and select the corresponding Toxicity Factor **T** from the standard table (see Formula Sheet below). Lower TLV → Higher T.

### Step 2 — Determine the Special Process Compensation Factor (S)
This accounts for the effect of operating pressure. A higher pressure means a larger driving force for a release, hence more material will escape. The value of **S** is read from a **pressure-compensation table** (typically 0.25 to 1.0).

### Step 3 — Determine the Quantity Compensation Factor (Q)
This penalises the calculation for the mass of toxic material present. The greater the inventory, the higher the hazard. **Q** is read from a **mass-compensation table** (typically 0.1 to 1.0).

### Step 4 — Determine the Layout Compensation Factor (L)
This accounts for the plant's physical arrangement — height of the unit, accessibility for emergency response, drainage characteristics, etc. **L** is read from a layout-compensation table (typically 0.25 to 0.9).

### Step 5 — Determine the Ventilation Compensation Factor (B)
This is *multiplicatively applied after* the sub-index. It accounts for whether the area is **open to atmosphere, well-ventilated, or enclosed**. Enclosed areas retain toxic vapours longer → higher B.

## 2.3 The Master Equation

The Toxicity Index is computed in two stages:

**Stage 1 — Toxicity Sub-Index (TSI):**

$$
\text{TSI} = T \times (1 + S + Q + L)
$$

**Stage 2 — Toxicity Index (TI):**

$$
\text{TI} = \text{TSI} \times (1 + B)
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TI} = T \times (1 + S + Q + L) \times (1 + B)}
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates **all key equations, lookup tables, and interpretation bands** required for KTU 2024 examination questions on the Toxicity Index.

| # | Quantity | Equation / Lookup | Source / Basis | Notes / Units |
|---|----------|-------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Toxicity Sub-Index | $\text{TSI} = T \times (1 + S + Q + L)$ | Mond Index | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Toxicity Index | $\text{TI} = \text{TSI} \times (1 + B)$ | Mond Index | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Master TI equation | $\text{TI} = T \times (1 + S + Q + L) \times (1 + B)$ | Mond Index | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Toxicity Factor (T) from TLV | See Table 2.1 below | ACGIH TLV-TWA | ppm or mg/m³ |
| 5 | Pressure Factor (S) | $S$ from pressure table | Process pressure | bar (g) |
| 6 | Quantity Factor (Q) | $Q$ from mass table | Material inventory | tonnes |
| 7 | Layout Factor (L) | $L$ from layout table | Plant geometry | m² / access |
| 8 | Ventilation Factor (B) | $B$ from ventilation table | Area enclosure | qualitative |

### Table 2.1 — Toxicity Factor (T) vs TLV

| TLV-TWA Range (ppm) | Toxicity Factor (T) |
|---------------------|----------------------|
| $< 1$ | $250$ |
| $1 \leq \text{TLV} < 5$ | $125$ |
| $5 \leq \text{TLV} < 10$ | $75$ |
| $10 \leq \text{TLV} < 100$ | $50$ |
| $100 \leq \text{TLV} < 1000$ | $25$ |
| $\geq 1000$ | $10$ |

### Table 2.2 — Indicative Compensation Factor Bands (Mond Reference)

| Factor | Typical Range | Highest Penalty Condition |
|--------|----------------|-----------------------------|
| $S$ (Pressure) | $0.25 - 1.00$ | Operating pressure $> 100$ bar(g) |
| $Q$ (Quantity) | $0.10 - 1.00$ | Mass of toxic material $> 100$ t |
| $L$ (Layout) | $0.25 - 0.90$ | Tall unit, poor drainage, limited access |
| $B$ (Ventilation) | $0.30 - 1.50$ | Fully enclosed area, no forced ventilation |

### Table 2.3 — Toxicity Index Interpretation Bands

| TI Range | Toxicity Rating | Operational Implication |
|----------|------------------|--------------------------|
| $0 - 1$ | Negligible | Standard precautions |
| $1 - 5$ | Light | Routine monitoring |
| $5 - 10$ | Low | Enhanced monitoring |
| $10 - 20$ | Moderate | Engineering controls required |
| $20 - 50$ | High | Major design review needed |
| $> 50$ | Very High | Process re-design or relocation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The TI is the primary input used to size **toxic gas detection systems**, determine **emergency response planning zones (ERPZ)**, design **dilution ventilation systems**, and calculate the **toxic consequence distance** in QRA studies (e.g., using **Probit equations** for lethality or injury thresholds like **IDLH, AEGL, ERPG**).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **chemical process industries (CPI)**, the Toxicity Index is applied during:

- **HAZOP** and **What-If** study pre-screening to rank nodes by toxic risk.
- **Land-use planning (LUP)** around facilities — TI dictates the safety buffer distance to residential zones.
- **Insurance risk evaluation** — underwriters (e.g., **IMIA, AIRMIC**) use Mond-derived indices to price industrial all-risk policies.
- **Regulatory compliance** — under **OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119)**, **EPA RMP**, and the Indian **Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC, 1989)**.
- **Emergency planning** — TI informs the **ERPZ** radius and the choice of **level of protection (LOP)** for first responders using **NFPA 1991 / 1994** ensembles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Logical Derivation of the Toxicity Index Formula

The Mond Index follows a **penalty-multiplier philosophy**: starting from a base intrinsic property (the Toxicity Factor T), each process deviation from "ideal" (low pressure, small quantity, open layout, well-ventilated) is penalised by adding a compensation factor inside the parenthesis, *then* the result is amplified further by a multiplicative ventilation factor.

The starting point is the assumption that the **toxic dose received by a worker** is proportional to:

- The airborne concentration of the toxicant (driven by TLV and release rate).
- The exposure duration.
- A proportionality constant for the specific substance.

Because concentration is inversely related to TLV (a smaller TLV means the substance is more toxic at lower concentrations), the base toxicity factor T is defined as inversely proportional to TLV:

$$
T \propto \frac{1}{\text{TLV}}
$$

Pressure (S) increases the mass flow rate of any leak, so its contribution is *additive* inside the bracket. Quantity (Q) increases the total release mass — also *additive*. Layout (L) determines the dispersion geometry — *additive*. These three operate on the **release process** and are grouped together.

Ventilation (B) operates on the **post-release dispersion**, modifying the time-integrated concentration in the breathing zone. Because it acts on a different physical regime, it is applied *multiplicatively* to the sub-index.

Therefore, the rigorous derivation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1 — Build the release sub-index:} \quad \text{TSI} &= T \times (1 + S + Q + L) \\
\text{Stage 2 — Apply the dispersion multiplier:} \quad \text{TI} &= \text{TSI} \times (1 + B) \\
\text{Combined master equation:} \quad \text{TI} &= T \times (1 + S + Q + L) \times (1 + B)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Toxicity Index for a Chlorine Storage Vessel

**Problem Statement (KTU-style numerical):**
A chlorine storage vessel in a process unit has the following characteristics:

- Substance: **Chlorine gas (Cl₂)**
- TLV-TWA = **0.5 ppm** (highly toxic)
- Operating pressure = **10 bar(g)**
- Mass of chlorine in the unit = **20 tonnes**
- Plant layout: **Multi-storey, average access** → $L = 0.55$
- Area ventilation: **Partially enclosed, mechanical ventilation present** → $B = 0.50$

Determine the **Toxicity Index** and classify the hazard.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (a) — Determine the Toxicity Factor (T).**
TLV = 0.5 ppm, which lies in the range $< 1$ ppm.
From Table 2.1:
$$
T = 250
$$

**Step (b) — Determine the Pressure Factor (S).**
Operating pressure = 10 bar(g). From the Mond pressure compensation table (interpolated):

$$
S = 0.45
$$

**Step (c) — Determine the Quantity Factor (Q).**
Mass = 20 tonnes. From the Mond quantity compensation table (interpolated):

$$
Q = 0.55
$$

**Step (d) — Layout Factor (L).**
Given in the problem:

$$
L = 0.55
$$

**Step (e) — Ventilation Factor (B).**
Given in the problem:

$$
B = 0.50
$$

**Step (f) — Compute the Toxicity Sub-Index (TSI).**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TSI} &= T \times (1 + S + Q + L) \\
&= 250 \times (1 + 0.45 + 0.55 + 0.55) \\
&= 250 \times (2.55) \\
&= 637.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (g) — Compute the Toxicity Index (TI).**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TI} &= \text{TSI} \times (1 + B) \\
&= 637.5 \times (1 + 0.50) \\
&= 637.5 \times 1.50 \\
&= 956.25
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TI} \approx 956}
$$

**Step (h) — Hazard Classification.**
From Table 2.3, TI $> 50$ corresponds to a **"Very High"** toxic hazard rating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A TI of ~956 signals an **unacceptable toxic risk** under the current configuration. The plant manager must either:
> 1. Reduce inventory to $< 1$ tonne on-site (dramatically lowers Q), or
> 2. Improve ventilation (lowers B), or
> 3. Add a **toxic gas detection and isolation system** with redundant **chlorine scrubbers**, or
> 4. Relocate the storage to an isolated, well-ventilated plot.

## 3.3 Second Worked Example — A Less Toxic Substance for Comparison

**Problem:** A methyl-ethyl-ketone (MEK) storage tank has the following parameters:

- TLV = **200 ppm**
- Pressure = **atmospheric (1 bar(g))** → $S = 0.25$
- Mass = **50 tonnes** → $Q = 0.65$
- Layout: **Open, ground-level, good access** → $L = 0.30$
- Ventilation: **Open to atmosphere** → $B = 0.30$

Determine TI.

**Step (a) — Toxicity Factor.**
TLV = 200 ppm, lies in the range $100 \leq \text{TLV} < 1000$:

$$
T = 25
$$

**Step (b) — Toxicity Sub-Index.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TSI} &= 25 \times (1 + 0.25 + 0.65 + 0.30) \\
&= 25 \times (2.20) \\
&= 55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Toxicity Index.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TI} &= 55 \times (1 + 0.30) \\
&= 55 \times 1.30 \\
&= 71.5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TI} \approx 71.5}
$$

**Step (d) — Classification.** Despite a low intrinsic toxicity, the large inventory and high quantity factor push the TI into the **"Very High"** band — illustrating that **quantity and process conditions can outweigh intrinsic toxicity**.

## 3.4 Analytical Sensitivity Matrix

The following matrix shows how the TI changes when one compensation factor is varied while the others are held at typical values ($T = 75$, $S = 0.5$, $Q = 0.5$, $L = 0.4$, $B = 0.5$).

| Scenario | $T$ | $S$ | $Q$ | $L$ | $B$ | TSI | TI | Rating |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|--------|
| Base case | 75 | 0.50 | 0.50 | 0.40 | 0.50 | 180.0 | 270.0 | Very High |
| Improve ventilation only | 75 | 0.50 | 0.50 | 0.40 | 0.10 | 180.0 | 198.0 | Very High |
| Reduce inventory drastically | 75 | 0.50 | 0.10 | 0.40 | 0.50 | 150.0 | 225.0 | Very High |
| Use low-toxicity substitute ($T=10$) | 10 | 0.50 | 0.50 | 0.40 | 0.50 | 24.0 | 36.0 | High |
| **Inherently safer design** (all four) | 10 | 0.25 | 0.10 | 0.25 | 0.10 | 16.0 | 17.6 | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU students:** The most effective single intervention is **substituting the chemical with a less toxic alternative** (drastically lowers $T$), confirming the **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** hierarchy (substitute → minimise → moderate → simplify) of **Trevor Kletz**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Toxicity Index Calculation Flow — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following block diagram maps the **logical flow** of the Toxicity Index calculation, including the lookup tables and the two-stage multiplicative structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Toxicity Index Calculation] --> B[Step 1: Identify Substance]
    B --> C[Step 2: Look up TLV-TWA from ACGIH Table]
    C --> D[Step 3: Select Toxicity Factor T from Table 2.1]
    D --> E[Step 4: Read Operating Pressure from P&ID]
    E --> F[Step 5: Look up Pressure Factor S]
    F --> G[Step 6: Read Mass Inventory from Plant Data]
    G --> H[Step 7: Look up Quantity Factor Q]
    H --> I[Step 8: Assess Plant Layout]
    I --> J[Step 9: Look up Layout Factor L]
    J --> K[Step 10: Compute Toxicity Sub-Index TSI]
    K --> L[Step 11: Assess Area Ventilation]
    L --> M[Step 12: Look up Ventilation Factor B]
    M --> N[Step 13: Compute Final Toxicity Index TI]
    N --> O[Step 14: Compare TI vs Hazard Bands in Table 2.3]
    O --> P[Step 15: Classify Toxicity Rating]
    P --> Q[Step 16: Recommend Engineering Controls or ISD Measures]
    Q --> R[END: TI Documented in Safety Report]
```

## 4.2 Compensation Factor Inter-Relationship Diagram

The following diagram illustrates **how the four compensation factors interact** with the base Toxicity Factor to produce the final index. Note the **additive grouping** of S, Q, L versus the **multiplicative** action of B.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Toxicity Factor T from TLV] --> CORE
    S[Pressure Factor S] --> ADD[Sum inside bracket 1 plus S plus Q plus L]
    Q[Quantity Factor Q] --> ADD
    L[Layout Factor L] --> ADD
    ADD --> CORE[Toxicity Sub-Index TSI]
    CORE --> MULT[Multiply by 1 plus B]
    B[Ventilation Factor B] --> MULT
    MULT --> FINAL[Final Toxicity Index TI]
    FINAL --> BAND[Compare against Table 2.3 Hazard Bands]
```

## 4.3 ISD Intervention Mapping Diagram

The following diagram shows how each of the **four Inherently Safer Design (ISD) strategies** targets one or more compensation factors in the Toxicity Index equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ISD[Inherently Safer Design Hierarchy]
    ISD --> SUB[Substitute: Replace with less toxic chemical]
    ISD --> MIN[Minimise: Reduce inventory]
    ISD --> MOD[Moderate: Use lower pressure and temperature]
    ISD --> SIM[Simplify: Improve layout and ventilation]

    SUB --> T_TARGET[Reduces T]
    MIN --> Q_TARGET[Reduces Q]
    MOD --> S_TARGET[Reduces S]
    SIM --> L_TARGET[Reduces L and B]

    T_TARGET --> LOW[Lower Toxicity Index]
    Q_TARGET --> LOW
    S_TARGET --> LOW
    L_TARGET --> LOW

    LOW --> SAFE[Acceptable Plant Risk Level]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** In the Mermaid graphs above, each labelled node represents an **operational step or a factor**, and the arrows represent the **direction of data flow** in the calculation. The double-stage structure (sub-index then final index) is clearly visible in the second diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Define the term Toxicity Index as used in the Mond Index. List the four compensation factors used in its calculation."**

**Model Answer:**
The **Toxicity Index (TI)** is a numerical rating in the **Mond Fire, Explosion and Toxicity Index** methodology that quantifies the overall toxic hazard of a process unit by combining the **base toxicity of the substance** with operational compensation factors. The four compensation factors are:
1. **Pressure Factor (S)** — accounts for the effect of operating pressure on release rate.
2. **Quantity Factor (Q)** — accounts for the mass of toxic material present.
3. **Layout Factor (L)** — accounts for plant geometry, access, and drainage.
4. **Ventilation Factor (B)** — accounts for the enclosure or openness of the operating area.

**Mark Split (KTU 2024):** Definition = 1 mark; Four factors listed = 2 marks (0.5 each).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**
**"What is the role of TLV in computing the Toxicity Factor T? How is T determined for a substance with TLV = 8 ppm?"**

**Model Answer:**
The **Threshold Limit Value (TLV-TWA)** is the airborne concentration (in **ppm** or **mg/m³**) to which a worker can be exposed 8 hours/day, 40 hours/week without adverse health effects. It is the **inverse measure of intrinsic toxicity** — a *lower* TLV means a *more toxic* substance, hence a *higher* Toxicity Factor **T**.

For **TLV = 8 ppm**, the value lies in the range $5 \leq \text{TLV} < 10$ ppm. From the standard Mond toxicity table:

$$
T = 75
$$

**Mark Split:** TLV role = 1.5 marks; T value with reasoning = 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Regulation (ESE Pattern):** Answer **any one full question** from the Module-1 choice pair. Each question carries **7 + 7 = 14 marks** across two sub-parts. Show **all formula substitutions and step transitions** for full credit.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **step-by-step procedure for calculating the Toxicity Index** of a process unit using the Mond Index methodology. List the standard tables used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A process unit handling **phosgene (COCl₂)** has the following data:

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| TLV-TWA | 0.1 ppm |
| Operating pressure | 5 bar(g) |
| Mass of phosgene | 2 tonnes |
| Layout | Multi-storey, average access |
| Ventilation | Partially enclosed, mechanical |

Use the following indicative compensation factors: $S = 0.40$, $Q = 0.35$, $L = 0.55$, $B = 0.60$. Calculate the **Toxicity Index** and classify the hazard.

**Model Solution to Part (a):**
The procedure follows the sequence shown in the Mermaid diagram of Section 4.1:
1. Identify the substance and look up its **TLV-TWA** from **ACGIH tables**.
2. Determine the **Toxicity Factor T** from Table 2.1.
3. Read operating **pressure** and look up **S** from the pressure compensation table.
4. Read the **mass of toxic material** and look up **Q** from the quantity compensation table.
5. Assess the **plant layout** and look up **L** from the layout compensation table.
6. Compute the **Toxicity Sub-Index (TSI)** using $\text{TSI} = T \times (1 + S + Q + L)$.
7. Assess the **ventilation** of the area and look up **B** from the ventilation compensation table.
8. Compute the final **Toxicity Index (TI)** using $\text{TI} = \text{TSI} \times (1 + B)$.
9. Compare TI against **Table 2.3 hazard bands** to classify the unit.

**Standard tables used:** ACGIH TLV table; Mond T-factor table; Pressure compensation table; Quantity compensation table; Layout compensation table; Ventilation compensation table; TI interpretation bands table.

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — TLV = 0.1 ppm**, which lies in the range $< 1$ ppm. Therefore:

$$
T = 250
$$

**Step 2 — Apply compensation factors** (given): $S = 0.40$, $Q = 0.35$, $L = 0.55$, $B = 0.60$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Toxicity Sub-Index:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TSI} &= T \times (1 + S + Q + L) \\
&= 250 \times (1 + 0.40 + 0.35 + 0.55) \\
&= 250 \times (2.30) \\
&= 575
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Toxicity Index:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TI} &= \text{TSI} \times (1 + B) \\
&= 575 \times (1 + 0.60) \\
&= 575 \times 1.60 \\
&= 920
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TI} = 920}
$$

**Step 5 — Hazard Classification:** From Table 2.3, TI $> 50$ corresponds to **"Very High"** toxic hazard. The plant must implement **toxic gas detectors, double containment, scrubbers, and emergency isolation valves** and consider **inventory reduction** or **substitution**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **limitations of the Toxicity Index** as a stand-alone QRA tool. How does it fit within the broader **Mond Index framework** alongside the Fire Index and Explosion Index?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A small-scale pilot plant stores **ammonia (NH₃)** in a 500 kg cylinder. The relevant data are:

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| TLV-TWA | 25 ppm |
| Operating pressure | 8 bar(g) |
| Mass of ammonia | 0.5 tonnes |
| Layout | Open, ground-level, good access |
| Ventilation | Open to atmosphere |

Use the indicative compensation factors: $S = 0.40$, $Q = 0.20$, $L = 0.30$, $B = 0.30$. Compute the **Toxicity Index** and comment on whether the rating is acceptable for a **pilot plant** under KTU/PSM guidelines.

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

The Toxicity Index, while useful, has the following **limitations**:

1. **Subjectivity of compensation factors** — S, Q, L, and B are read from look-up tables that depend on engineering judgement, introducing evaluator bias.
2. **Static snapshot** — TI is computed for a single design state; transient operations (start-up, shut-down, maintenance) are not captured.
3. **No population exposure modelling** — TI does not consider the number of exposed people, weather conditions, or terrain roughness (unlike probit-based QRA).
4. **Threshold Limit Value is for healthy workers**, not for sensitive sub-populations (children, asthmatics, elderly).
5. **Single-substance focus** — synergistic or antagonistic toxic mixtures are not handled.
6. **No credit for PPE or detection systems** in the basic formulation.

**Mond Framework Integration:**
The Mond Index computes **three parallel sub-indices** — **Fire Index (FI)**, **Explosion Index (EI)**, and **Toxicity Index (TI)** — and then aggregates them into an **Overall Hazard Index (HI)** using weighted factors. The TI is the *only* sub-index addressing chronic/acute toxic exposure, complementing FI (which addresses pool and jet fires) and EI (which addresses vapour cloud explosions). The HI guides plant-level risk prioritisation.

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — TLV = 25 ppm**, lies in the range $10 \leq \text{TLV} < 100$ ppm. Therefore:

$$
T = 50
$$

**Step 2 — Compensation factors** (given): $S = 0.40$, $Q = 0.20$, $L = 0.30$, $B = 0.30$.

**Step 3 — Toxicity Sub-Index:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TSI} &= 50 \times (1 + 0.40 + 0.20 + 0.30) \\
&= 50 \times (1.90) \\
&= 95
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Toxicity Index:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TI} &= 95 \times (1 + 0.30) \\
&= 95 \times 1.30 \\
&= 123.5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TI} = 123.5}
$$

**Step 5 — Hazard Classification:** TI $> 50$ ⇒ **"Very High"** rating.

**Comment for Pilot Plant:** A TI of 123.5 is **not acceptable** even for a pilot plant. The high rating is driven mainly by the **ammonia's intrinsic toxicity** ($T = 50$). Engineering recommendations:
- Install **NH₃ gas detectors** with audio-visual alarms at 10 ppm (TLV/2).
- Provide **emergency eye-wash and safety showers** within 10 m.
- Ensure **forced dilution ventilation** of at least **6 air changes/hour** in the storage enclosure.
- Restrict access to **trained personnel only** with appropriate **SCBA (Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus)** available.
- Re-evaluate the **TI after each inventory change** as required by the **PSM (Process Safety Management)** standard.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Toxicity Index Problems:**
> 1. **Wrong TLV range selection** — Many students pick the wrong band in Table 2.1 (e.g., choosing $T = 75$ for a 0.5 ppm substance instead of $T = 250$). Always re-check the **inequality bounds** ($<$, $\leq$).
> 2. **Forgetting the "1 +" inside the bracket** — A frequent error is computing $T \times S \times Q \times L$ instead of $T \times (1 + S + Q + L)$. The "1" represents the **baseline** and must always be present.
> 3. **Mixing up additive and multiplicative factors** — S, Q, L are **additive** (inside the bracket); B is **multiplicative** (outside the bracket, as $(1 + B)$). Reversing this yields a wildly wrong answer.
> 4. **Skipping unit consistency** — Although TI is dimensionless, mass should be in **tonnes** and pressure in **bar(g)** to match the Mond reference tables. Mixing units (e.g., kg with bar) is a common error.
> 5. **No final interpretation** — KTU examiners award **at least 1 mark** for the **hazard band classification and a one-line engineering recommendation**. Skipping this forfeits easy marks.
> 6. **Copying wrong TLV values** — Students often confuse **TLV-TWA** with **TLV-STEL** or **TLV-Ceiling**. Always confirm the question asks for **TWA**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Toxicity Index (KTU 2024)**

- ☐ **Definition:** The Toxicity Index is a dimensionless Mond-Index rating that combines intrinsic chemical toxicity (via TLV) with compensation factors for process and environmental conditions.
- ☐ **Master Formula:** $\text{TI} = T \times (1 + S + Q + L) \times (1 + B)$.
- ☐ **Two-Stage Calculation:** First compute the **sub-index (TSI)**, then apply the **ventilation multiplier**.
- ☐ **Additive Group:** $S$, $Q$, $L$ are summed inside the bracket.
- ☐ **Multiplicative Group:** $B$ is applied via $(1 + B)$ as a separate factor.
- ☐ **Toxicity Factor T (Key Values):** TLV $< 1$ ppm → **250**; $1$–$5$ ppm → **125**; $5$–$10$ ppm → **75**; $10$–$100$ ppm → **50**; $100$–$1000$ ppm → **25**; $\geq 1000$ ppm → **10**.
- ☐ **TLV Source:** Always cite **ACGIH TLV-TWA** values; this is the KTU-expected reference.
- ☐ **Compensation Factor Range:** $S, Q, L$ each lie in $[0.1, 1.0]$; $B$ lies in $[0.3, 1.5]$ (enclosed areas push B higher).
- ☐ **Interpretation Bands:** TI $> 50$ is "Very High"; $20$–$50$ is "High"; $10$–$20$ is "Moderate"; $< 10$ is "Low/Negligible".
- ☐ **Inherently Safer Design Link:** Substitute (lowers T) → Minimise (lowers Q) → Moderate (lowers S) → Simplify (lowers L and B).
- ☐ **Engineering Outputs:** TI drives **detector placement, ERPZ sizing, ventilation design, and insurance premium rating**.
- ☐ **Regulatory Linkages:** OSHA PSM (USA), EPA RMP (USA), MSIHC Rules 1989 (India), SEVESO III Directive (EU), and Indian Factories Act 1948 (Section 41).
- ☐ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Always include the "**1**" inside the bracket; never compute a bare product $T \times S \times Q \times L$.
- ☐ **Examiner Tip:** End every numerical answer with a **classification band** and a **one-line engineering recommendation** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Explosion Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chemical Explosion Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Explosion Index (CEI) — Formal Definition**
> The **Chemical Explosion Index (CEI)**, also widely known as the **Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)**, is a *quantitative, semi-empirical risk ranking methodology* developed by the **Dow Chemical Company (1964)**. It assigns a single dimensionless numerical score to a process unit, thereby classifying its relative fire, explosion, and reactivity hazard potential. The index is anchored to the **Material Factor (MF)** — a baseline intrinsic hazard — and amplified by two categories of process penalties: **General Process Hazards (GPH)** and **Special Process Hazards (SPH)**.

In simple terms, the Chemical Explosion Index is the *process safety equivalent of a credit score for a chemical plant*. Just as a bank evaluates your creditworthiness by multiplying your basic profile by risk modifiers, the Dow Index multiplies the intrinsic hazard of the chemical by process-specific aggravating factors to give a single "danger rating."

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Spice Meter" for a Reactor**
> Imagine a chef rating a dish. The base ingredient (chili) has an intrinsic spiciness score (say 5/10). The cooking method (raw, fried, blended) can amplify or moderate the spiciness. The Dow Index works identically:
> - **Chili heat** → Material Factor (MF) of the chemical.
> - **Cooking method** → GPH + SPH process modifiers.
> - **Final spiciness rating** → F&EI score.
> A score above ~128 means the process unit is in the **"severe hazard"** category and demands intensive loss-control engineering.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Reference Standards in the F&EI Methodology**
> - **NFPA 704** ratings (reactivity $N_R$, flammability $N_F$, health $N_H$).
> - **TNT Equivalency** for vapor cloud explosion quantification.
> - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** (PSM) thresholds — F&EI > 127 typically triggers heightened scrutiny.
> - The constant **0.256 ft** (or **0.0781 m**) per unit F&EI for radius-of-exposure calculation.

### Why the CEI Exists in QRA

In **Quantitative Risk Assessment**, the CEI sits in the *preliminary hazard analysis (PHA)* stage — before full **HAZOP**, **LOPA**, or **QRA** numerical work. Its role is to:

1. **Prioritize** — sort 50+ process units in a plant into high/medium/low priority.
2. **Estimate Property Damage** — yield the **Maximum Probable Property Damage (MPPD)** in dollars.
3. **Calculate Geographic Exposure** — yield the **Radius of Exposure (R)** and **Area of Exposure (A)** for siting and emergency planning.
4. **Justify Engineering Spend** — provide a defensible document for capital allocation toward loss-prevention measures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Step Dow F&EI Logic Chain

The Dow methodology is a *cascading* calculation. Each step's output feeds the next. KTU examiners consistently test the understanding of this hierarchy.

| Step | Action | Output Symbol |
|------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | Identify the process unit & determine chemical **Material Factor** | **MF** |
| 2 | Evaluate **General Process Hazards** (penalties that apply to *any* process) | **GPH** |
| 3 | Evaluate **Special Process Hazards** (penalties unique to the *specific* process) | **SPH** |
| 4 | Compute **Process Unit Hazard Factor** and the **Fire & Explosion Index** | **PUHF**, **F&EI** |
| 5 | Convert F&EI to **Damage Factor (DF)**, **Radius (R)**, **Area (A)**, and **MPPD** | DF, R, A, \$ |

### 2.2 Step 1 — The Material Factor (MF)

The MF is a *dimensionless number* between **1** (inert, like water) and **40** (extremely energetic, like high-test peroxide). It is determined from:

- **NFPA reactivity rating** ($N_R$): 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.
- **NFPA flammability rating** ($N_F$): 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.
- **Heats of combustion / decomposition**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for MF Estimation**
> - For most hydrocarbons: $\text{MF} \approx N_F + N_R$ — but use the official Dow Material Factor table for exam precision.
> - Hydrogen: **MF = 21**, Acetone: **MF = 16**, Methane: **MF = 21**, Diethyl ether: **MF = 40**.

### 2.3 Step 2 — General Process Hazards (GPH)

These penalties apply *regardless of the chemistry*. They reflect basic operational vulnerabilities. The six GPH categories are:

1. **A — Exothermic chemical reactions** ($0$ to $1.00$)
2. **B — Endothermic reactions** ($0.20$ to $0.40$)
3. **C — Material handling & transfer** ($0.25$ to $1.05$)
4. **D — Enclosed or indoor process units** ($0.25$ to $0.90$)
5. **E — Access** (ease of emergency entry/exit) ($0.20$ to $0.35$)
6. **F — Drainage and spill control** ($0.25$ to $0.50$)

$$\text{GPH} = 1.0 + \sum_{i=A}^{F} \text{Penalty}_i$$

### 2.4 Step 3 — Special Process Hazards (SPH)

These are *process-specific* aggravators. They are finer-grained and tend to dominate the score. The twelve SPH categories include:

1. Toxic materials ($0.20$ to $0.80$)
2. Sub-atmospheric pressure ($0.50$)
3. Operation in/near flammable range ($0.30$ to $1.00$)
4. Dust explosion potential ($0.25$ to $2.00$)
5. Relief pressure setting (function of set pressure $P_{\text{set}}$)
6. Low temperature / brittle fracture ($0.20$ to $0.30$)
7. Quantity of flammable material (most heavily weighted, $1.0$ to $3.0+$)
8. Corrosion and erosion ($0.10$ to $0.75$)
9. Leakage at joints & packing ($0.10$ to $1.50$)
10. Use of fired equipment (varies)
11. Hot oil heat exchange system ($0.15$ to $1.15$)
12. Rotating equipment ($0.50$)

$$\text{SPH} = 1.0 + \sum_{j=1}^{12} \text{Penalty}_j$$

### 2.5 Step 4 — F&EI Computation

$$\text{PUHF} = \text{GPH} + \text{SPH}$$

$$\boxed{\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times \text{PUHF}}$$

### 2.6 Step 5 — Damage Factor, Radius, Area, and MPPD

The **Damage Factor (DF)** is read from the Dow chart as a function of F&EI. The mathematical approximation (for exam calculations) is:

| F&EI Range | Approximate Damage Factor (DF) | Severity Tier |
|------------|-------------------------------|---------------|
| 1 – 50 | $0.00$ – $0.10$ | **Light** |
| 51 – 80 | $0.10$ – $0.20$ | **Low** |
| 81 – 100 | $0.20$ – $0.30$ | **Moderate** |
| 101 – 150 | $0.30$ – $0.50$ | **Heavy** |
| 151 – 200 | $0.50$ – $0.75$ | **Very Heavy** |
| $> 200$ | $0.75$ – $1.00$ | **Severe / Catastrophic** |

**Radius of Exposure** (the geometric boundary within which significant damage would occur):

$$\boxed{R = k \times \text{F\&EI}}$$

where $k = 0.256 \text{ ft/unit}$ (FPS) or $k \approx 0.0781 \text{ m/unit}$ (SI).
