**Area of Exposure** (the circular zone of damage):

$$A = \pi \cdot R^2$$

**Maximum Probable Property Damage (MPPD)**:

$$\text{Base MPPD} = \text{Replacement Cost} \times \text{DF}$$

$$\text{Actual MPPD} = \text{Base MPPD} \times \text{Location Factor}$$

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| Equation | Variables | Units | Boundary / Range |
|----------|-----------|-------|------------------|
| $\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times (\text{GPH} + \text{SPH})$ | MF dimensionless, GPH/SPH dimensionless | **— (score)** | F&EI typically 0 to $\sim 250$ |
| $R = 0.256 \times \text{F\&EI}$ | R in feet | **ft** | valid for F&EI $\geq 1$ |
| $R = 0.0781 \times \text{F\&EI}$ | R in meters | **m** | SI equivalent |
| $A = \pi R^2$ | area | **ft² or m²** | assumes circular exposure zone |
| $\text{Base MPPD} = C_{\text{rep}} \times \text{DF}$ | replacement cost in USD | **USD** | DF between 0 and 1 |
| $\text{Actual MPPD} = \text{Base MPPD} \times f_{\text{loc}}$ | location factor $f_{\text{loc}}$ typically 0.6 to 1.2 | **USD** | $f_{\text{loc}} = 1.0$ for exposed plant |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The F&EI is used by:
- **Petrochemical majors** (Shell, Dow, BASF) for plant siting and insurance underwriting.
- **Regulators** under OSHA PSM for prioritization of audits.
- **Insurers** (e.g., Industrial Risk Insurers — IRI) for premium calculation.
- **Emergency planners** for determining blast-zone evacuation radii.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Numerical Problems & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Sample Penalty Selection

For clarity, below is how a KTU-style problem statement maps to penalty values. Consider a typical **storage tank containing 50 tonnes of acetone** at ambient conditions, outdoors, with adequate drainage:

| Category | Penalty Selection | Justification |
|----------|------------------|---------------|
| MF | **16** | Acetone has $N_F = 3$, $N_R = 0$; Dow table value |
| GPH-A (Exothermic) | 0 | Storage, no reaction |
| GPH-B (Endothermic) | 0 | No reaction |
| GPH-C (Handling) | 0.50 | Loading/unloading operations |
| GPH-D (Enclosed) | 0 | Outdoor tank |
| GPH-E (Access) | 0.20 | Adequate road access |
| GPH-F (Drainage) | 0.25 | Bund provided |
| SPH-Toxicity | 0.20 | Mild (acetone is an irritant) |
| SPH-Near flammable range | 0.50 | Stored as flammable liquid |
| SPH-Quantity | 1.50 | 50 t is significant inventory |
| SPH-Corrosion | 0.10 | Mild, acetone is non-corrosive |
| (others) | 0 | Not applicable |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Calculation

Compute $\text{GPH}$:

$$\text{GPH} = 1.0 + 0.50 + 0.20 + 0.25 = 1.95$$

Compute $\text{SPH}$:

$$\text{SPH} = 1.0 + 0.20 + 0.50 + 1.50 + 0.10 = 3.30$$

Compute $\text{PUHF}$:

$$\text{PUHF} = \text{GPH} + \text{SPH} = 1.95 + 3.30 = 5.25$$

Compute $\text{F\&EI}$:

$$\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times \text{PUHF} = 16 \times 5.25 = 84$$

Read the Damage Factor from the Dow chart for F&EI = 84:

$$\text{DF} \approx 0.22 \quad (\text{Moderate severity tier})$$

Compute Radius (SI):

$$R = 0.0781 \times 84 = 6.56 \text{ m}$$

Compute Area:

$$A = \pi \times (6.56)^2 = 3.1416 \times 43.04 = 135.2 \text{ m}^2$$

If replacement cost of the tank farm section = **INR 5 Crore (₹ 50,000,000)** and location factor = 1.0:

$$\text{Base MPPD} = 50{,}000{,}000 \times 0.22 = \text{₹ } 11{,}000{,}000$$

$$\text{Actual MPPD} = 11{,}000{,}000 \times 1.0 = \text{₹ } 11{,}000{,}000$$

### 3.3 Alternative Worked Example — Hydrogen Compressor

**Problem Statement:** A hydrogen compressor handling 100 kg of H₂ at 200 bar in an outdoor, well-ventilated plant. Material Factor for H₂ = **21**. Penalties:
- GPH-C (handling compressed gas) = 0.85
- GPH-E (limited access near running equipment) = 0.35
- SPH-Near flammable range = 1.00 (always in flammable range)
- SPH-Relief pressure (set 250 psig) = 0.34
- SPH-Quantity = 1.20
- SPH-Leakage (high-pressure fittings) = 0.80

**Solution:**

$$\text{GPH} = 1.0 + 0.85 + 0.35 = 2.20$$

$$\text{SPH} = 1.0 + 1.00 + 0.34 + 1.20 + 0.80 = 4.34$$

$$\text{PUHF} = 2.20 + 4.34 = 6.54$$

$$\text{F\&EI} = 21 \times 6.54 = 137.34$$

Read DF from the Dow chart for F&EI ≈ 137:

$$\text{DF} \approx 0.45 \quad (\text{Heavy tier})$$

$$R = 0.256 \times 137.34 = 35.16 \text{ ft} \approx 10.72 \text{ m}$$

$$A = \pi \times (10.72)^2 = 361 \text{ m}^2$$

The hydrogen compressor is in the **"Heavy" severity tier** — significantly worse than the acetone storage. This is why the CEI is so valuable for **plant prioritization**.

### 3.4 Sensitivity / "What-If" Analytical Derivation

The CEI is *most sensitive* to MF and to the quantity penalty. The partial derivative gives us:

$$\frac{\partial (\text{F\&EI})}{\partial \text{MF}} = \text{PUHF}$$

$$\frac{\partial (\text{F\&EI})}{\partial \text{SPH}_{\text{quantity}}} = \text{MF}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If a question asks *"which factor most influences F&EI?"*, the answer is: **MF × SPH-quantity**, since both are typically the largest terms in the product.

### 3.5 Loss Control Credit Factor (LCCF) Extension

In the *Dow's Chemical Exposure Index Guide* (companion to F&EI), the **Loss Control Credit Factor** reduces the *actual* MPPD:

$$\text{F\&EI}_{\text{after controls}} = \text{F\&EI} \times C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3$$

where $C_1$ (process control) ∈ $[0.97, 0.99]$, $C_2$ (material isolation) ∈ $[0.97, 0.99]$, $C_3$ (fire protection) ∈ $[0.95, 0.99]$.

$$\text{BF}_2 = \text{BF}_1 \times C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3 \quad \text{(Block Factor adjustment)}$$

This converts the index into an *after-control* projection that supports engineering trade-off studies.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — F&EI Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Unit Identification] --> B[Determine Material Factor MF]
    B --> C{Is the chemical in the Dow MF Table?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Lookup MF]
    C -- No --> E[Compute MF via NFPA ratings and combustion data]
    D --> F[Evaluate General Process Hazards GPH]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Evaluate Special Process Hazards SPH]
    G --> H[Compute PUHF = GPH + SPH]
    H --> I[Compute F and EI = MF x PUHF]
    I --> J[Read Damage Factor DF from Dow Chart]
    J --> K[Compute Radius R = 0.256 x F and EI]
    K --> L[Compute Area A = pi R squared]
    L --> M[Compute Base MPPD = Replacement x DF]
    M --> N[Compute Actual MPPD = Base MPPD x Location Factor]
    N --> O[Apply Loss Control Credits C1 C2 C3]
    O --> P[Final Risk Rank Output]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Severity Tier Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Input F and EI value] --> tier1{F and EI between 1 and 50}
    tier1 -- yes --> light[Tier: Light - DF approx 0 to 0.10]
    tier1 -- no --> tier2{F and EI between 51 and 80}
    tier2 -- yes --> low[Tier: Low - DF approx 0.10 to 0.20]
    tier2 -- no --> tier3{F and EI between 81 and 100}
    tier3 -- yes --> mod[Tier: Moderate - DF approx 0.20 to 0.30]
    tier3 -- no --> tier4{F and EI between 101 and 150}
    tier4 -- yes --> heavy[Tier: Heavy - DF approx 0.30 to 0.50]
    tier4 -- no --> tier5{F and EI between 151 and 200}
    tier5 -- yes --> vheavy[Tier: Very Heavy - DF approx 0.50 to 0.75]
    tier5 -- no --> severe[Tier: Severe - DF approx 0.75 to 1.00]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Hazard Penalty Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MFBlock[Material Factor MF] --> Core
    GPHBlock[GPH Module - Six Universal Penalties] --> Core
    SPHBlock[SPH Module - Twelve Specific Penalties] --> Core
    Core[Combine MF x GPH plus SPH] --> FandEI[F and EI Output]
    FandEI --> DFLookup
    DFLookup[Damage Factor Lookup Table] --> RadiusMath
    RadiusMath[Radius = k x F and EI] --> AreaMath
    AreaMath[Area = pi R squared] --> MPPDCalc
    MPPDCalc[Base MPPD = Cost x DF] --> FinalRank
    FinalRank[Final Rank Light to Severe]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Convention Used:**
> - Rectangular blocks = computational steps.
> - Diamond nodes = conditional branch points.
> - Each downstream block is *strictly dependent* on the upstream calculation completing without error.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]**
*Define the term "Chemical Explosion Index" and state any two of its engineering applications in process plant safety. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The Chemical Explosion Index (CEI), commonly known as the Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI), is a quantitative hazard ranking methodology developed by Dow Chemical Company. It calculates a dimensionless score as:

$$\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times (\text{GPH} + \text{SPH})$$

Two engineering applications:

1. **Prioritization of process units** for detailed HAZOP / LOPA studies within a large chemical complex.
2. **Estimation of Maximum Probable Property Damage (MPPD)** and the geographic **radius of exposure (R)** for emergency planning, insurance underwriting, and plant siting decisions.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Paper]**
*Differentiate between General Process Hazards (GPH) and Special Process Hazards (SPH) with one example each. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | GPH | SPH |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Scope | Applies to *any* process unit universally | Specific to the *particular* chemistry and equipment |
| Categories | Six categories (A–F) | Twelve categories |
| Nature | Generic operational vulnerabilities | Process-specific aggravators |
| Example | **Enclosed/indoor unit** (penalty 0.25–0.90) | **Quantity of flammable material** (penalty 1.0–3.0) |

$$\text{GPH} = 1.0 + \sum_{i=A}^{F} \Delta_i \quad ; \quad \text{SPH} = 1.0 + \sum_{j=1}^{12} \Delta_j$$

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### ✅ QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Q.A (a)** Define the Material Factor (MF). How is it determined for a new chemical not listed in the Dow MF table? Discuss the role of NFPA 704 ratings. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Material Factor (MF):**
The Material Factor is a *baseline intrinsic hazard score* of a chemical substance, derived from its NFPA flammability rating ($N_F$) and reactivity rating ($N_R$). It ranges from **1** (e.g., water) to **40** (e.g., diethyl ether, dibenzoyl peroxide). The MF reflects the energy release potential of the substance independent of process conditions.

**Determination for a new chemical:**
1. Obtain $N_F$ (0–4) from the chemical's flash point, boiling point, and fire behavior.
2. Obtain $N_R$ (0–4) from detonation shock sensitivity, rapid decomposition, or self-reactivity.
3. Cross-reference in Dow's published MF table; if absent, use the correlation:

$$\text{MF}_{\text{estimated}} = N_F + N_R + \text{(dust/thermal correction)}$$

4. If the chemical decomposes exothermically with heat of decomposition > heats of combustion, additional reactivity penalties apply (consult Dow's *Fire and Explosion Index Hazard Classification Guide*, Appendix A).

**Role of NFPA 704:**
- $N_F$ indicates how readily the material ignites.
- $N_R$ indicates how violently it reacts/decomposes.
- Together they form the **foundation** of MF; without NFPA ratings, MF assignment cannot be rigorous.
- *Limitation:* NFPA does not capture all toxic/energetic nuances — thus MF is paired with **GPH + SPH** to capture full risk.

---

**Q.A (b)** A storage tank contains **200 tonnes of toluene** at atmospheric conditions in an outdoor installation. Given: MF for toluene = 16. The relevant penalties are:

| GPH Penalty | Value |
|---|---|
| C — Material handling | 0.50 |
| E — Access | 0.20 |
| F — Drainage | 0.25 |

| SPH Penalty | Value |
|---|---|
| Toxic materials | 0.20 |
| Near flammable range (storage) | 0.50 |
| Quantity | 1.80 |
| Corrosion | 0.10 |

Compute the **F&EI**, **Damage Factor** (use: $\text{DF} \approx 0.05 + 0.0025 \times \text{F\&EI}$), **Radius of exposure** (in m, use $k = 0.0781$), **Area of exposure**, and **Base MPPD** if replacement cost = ₹ 10 Crore. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute GPH:**

$$\text{GPH} = 1.0 + 0.50 + 0.20 + 0.25 = 1.95$$

**Step 2 — Compute SPH:**

$$\text{SPH} = 1.0 + 0.20 + 0.50 + 1.80 + 0.10 = 3.60$$

**Step 3 — Process Unit Hazard Factor:**

$$\text{PUHF} = \text{GPH} + \text{SPH} = 1.95 + 3.60 = 5.55$$

**Step 4 — Fire & Explosion Index:**

$$\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times \text{PUHF} = 16 \times 5.55 = 88.8$$

**Step 5 — Damage Factor (using given formula):**

$$\text{DF} = 0.05 + 0.0025 \times 88.8 = 0.05 + 0.222 = 0.272$$

This places the unit in the **"Moderate"** severity tier (DF between 0.20 and 0.30).

**Step 6 — Radius of Exposure (SI):**

$$R = 0.0781 \times 88.8 = 6.935 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Area of Exposure:**

$$A = \pi \times R^2 = 3.1416 \times (6.935)^2 = 3.1416 \times 48.10 = 151.1 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 8 — Base MPPD:**

$$\text{Base MPPD} = \text{₹ } 10{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.272 = \text{₹ } 2{,}72{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{F\&EI} = 88.8, \quad \text{DF} = 0.272, \quad R = 6.94 \text{ m}, \quad A = 151.1 \text{ m}^2, \quad \text{Base MPPD} = \text{₹ } 2.72 \text{ Crore}}$$

---

### ✅ QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q.B (a)** Explain the significance of the **Radius of Exposure** and **Area of Exposure** in the Dow F&EI methodology. How is the radius calculated in FPS and SI units? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Significance of Radius of Exposure (R):**
The Radius of Exposure is the *horizontal distance from the epicenter of a hypothetical fire/explosion event within which significant property damage would be expected*. It is critical for:

1. **Plant Siting** — determines separation distances between process units per API RP 752 / IS 15499.
2. **Emergency Planning** — sets evacuation perimeter and shelter-in-place radius.
3. **Insurance Mapping** — defines the geographical spread of insurable damage.

**Significance of Area of Exposure (A):**
The Area of Exposure represents the *circular zone of damage* ($A = \pi R^2$). It is used to:
- Estimate **inventory at risk** within the zone.
- Plan **firewater coverage** and foam tanker staging.
- Justify **land-use buffer zones** around the plant boundary.

**Radius Calculation:**

In FPS units:

$$R_{\text{ft}} = 0.256 \times \text{F\&EI}$$

In SI units:

$$R_{\text{m}} = 0.256 \times 0.3048 \times \text{F\&EI} = 0.0781 \times \text{F\&EI}$$

The constant **0.256 ft per unit F&EI** originates from Dow's empirical calibration against historical incident data — it represents the average blast overpressure / thermal radiation decay profile from hydrocarbon fires and vapor cloud explosions.

---

**Q.B (b)** A reactor handling **diethyl ether (MF = 40)** operates at slight positive pressure. Penalties evaluated are:

| GPH | Value |
|---|---|
| A — Exothermic | 0.50 |
| C — Handling | 1.05 |
| D — Enclosed (indoor) | 0.90 |
| E — Access | 0.35 |

| SPH | Value |
|---|---|
| Toxic materials | 0.20 |
| Near flammable range | 1.00 |
| Quantity | 2.00 |
| Leakage (pump seals) | 0.80 |

Compute **F&EI**, **DF** (use $\text{DF} \approx 0.05 + 0.0035 \times \text{F\&EI}$ capped at 1.0), and **R in meters**. Comment on the severity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — GPH:**

$$\text{GPH} = 1.0 + 0.50 + 1.05 + 0.90 + 0.35 = 3.80$$

**Step 2 — SPH:**

$$\text{SPH} = 1.0 + 0.20 + 1.00 + 2.00 + 0.80 = 5.00$$

**Step 3 — PUHF:**

$$\text{PUHF} = \text{GPH} + \text{SPH} = 3.80 + 5.00 = 8.80$$

**Step 4 — F&EI:**

$$\text{F\&EI} = 40 \times 8.80 = 352$$

**Step 5 — Damage Factor:**

$$\text{DF} = \min\{0.05 + 0.0035 \times 352, \, 1.0\} = \min\{1.282, \, 1.0\} = 1.0$$

> This indicates **complete destruction** of the unit — DF saturates at 1.0 because the formula predicts > 100% damage which is physically bounded at 100%.

**Step 6 — Radius of Exposure (SI):**

$$R = 0.0781 \times 352 = 27.49 \text{ m}$$

**Severity Comment:**
With F&EI = **352**, this diethyl ether reactor falls into the **"Severe / Catastrophic"** tier. Key contributors:
- High MF (40) — diethyl ether is among the most hazardous common solvents.
- Indoor placement amplifies GPH significantly.
- Near-flammable-range operation + large inventory + pump leakage all compound the SPH.

**Recommendation:** Substantial loss-control credits (vapor recovery, inerting, explosive-proof electricals) are mandatory. Plant layout must accommodate a ~28 m blast-radius buffer.

$$\boxed{\text{F\&EI} = 352, \quad \text{DF} = 1.0, \quad R \approx 27.5 \text{ m} \quad (\text{Severe Tier})}$$

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in CEI Problems**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1.0" baseline** in GPH and SPH — examiners deduct **2 full marks** if you write $\text{GPH} = \sum$ instead of $\text{GPH} = 1.0 + \sum$.
> 2. **Unit mix-up in radius** — using $k = 0.256$ without converting to SI when the answer requires meters. Always verify the unit system requested.
> 3. **Ignoring DF capping** — when F&EI > 280, the linear DF formula can exceed 1.0. Always bound DF at 1.0.
> 4. **Misapplying LCCF** — Loss Control Credits are *post-index*, applied to MPPD, **not** to F&EI itself.
> 5. **Confusing CEI with CEI Guide** — Dow's **Chemical Exposure Index (CEI Guide)** is for *acute toxic exposure* (in m, ppm-min), distinct from F&EI. Examiners occasionally test this distinction.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 10 Things You Must Memorize for the CEI Topic**
> - [x] **Master Formula:** $\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times (\text{GPH} + \text{SPH})$
> - [x] **GPH has 6 categories, SPH has 12 categories** — count them in the exam.
> - [x] **Baseline constant:** Always start GPH and SPH with $+1.0$.
> - [x] **Radius (FPS):** $R = 0.256 \times \text{F\&EI}$ (ft).
> - [x] **Radius (SI):** $R = 0.0781 \times \text{F\&EI}$ (m).
> - [x] **Area:** $A = \pi R^2$ — circular damage zone.
> - [x] **Damage Factor** is read from the Dow chart; capped at 1.0.
> - [x] **MPPD:** $\text{Base MPPD} = \text{Replacement Cost} \times \text{DF}$.
> - [x] **Severity Tiers:** Light → Low → Moderate → Heavy → Very Heavy → Severe.
> - [x] **F&EI > 128** typically triggers OSHA PSM-level scrutiny.
> - [x] **LCCF** are *post-calculation* credits, not part of F&EI itself.
> - [x] **Most sensitive parameters:** MF and SPH-quantity penalty.
> - [x] **Distinguish:** Dow F&EI (fire/explosion) vs. Dow CEI Guide (acute toxicity).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventory Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inventory Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1: INVENTORY ANALYSIS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inventory Analysis** is the **second phase** of the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) framework, as defined under **ISO 14040 and ISO 14044** standards. It is the systematic, quantitative procedure of **compiling, categorizing, and quantifying** all relevant inputs (raw materials, energy, ancillary inputs) and outputs (products, co-products, emissions to air, water, and soil, plus waste) of a product system throughout its entire life cycle, relative to a declared **Functional Unit (FU)**.

In the context of **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**, Inventory Analysis extends beyond environmental LCA. It encompasses the **quantification of hazardous inventories** (chemicals, fuels, radioactive sources, explosives, biological agents) present at a facility or within a process. The objective is to compute the **total quantity, type, location, and physical state** of dangerous substances to:

1. Determine the **source term** for consequence analysis.
2. Calculate **probit-based damage distances** for toxicity, thermal radiation, and overpressure.
3. Enable **domino effect assessment** by mapping co-located inventories.

### 🌍 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine your kitchen refrigerator as a "facility" and everything inside it as an "inventory".** Before you can plan meals (assess risk), you must first **list every item** — how much milk (litres), how many eggs (count), what vegetables (type/weight), and where each sits (top shelf, door, freezer). You also record what *leaves* the fridge (consumed food, spoiled waste) and what *enters* (new groceries, energy for cooling).
>
> **Inventory Analysis is exactly this** — but for an industrial plant, a chemical process, or a product's life cycle. You tabulate what comes **in** (feedstocks, energy, water), what stays **temporarily** (stockpiled chemicals), and what goes **out** (desired products, accidental releases, waste streams, atmospheric emissions). Without this rigorous "stock-taking," any risk number you calculate later is **guesswork**.

### 🎯 Standardized Metrics & Constants

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional Unit** | FU | Declared, system-specific (e.g., 1 tonne of product, 1 kWh of energy) |
| **Reference Flow** | RF | Mass or energy required to deliver 1 FU |
| **Allocation Factor** | α | Dimensionless, $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ |
| **Characterization Factor** | CF | kg-eq / kg-emission (LCIA) |
| **Avogadro's Number** | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹** |
| **Universal Gas Constant** | R | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** |
| **Standard Pressure** | $P^{\circ}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| **Standard Temperature** | $T^{\circ}$ | **298.15 K** |

### 🏷️ Classification of Inventory Items

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of Inventory Classification in QRA:**
> 1. **Inputs (Raw Materials)** – Virgin feedstock, recycled content, water intake.
> 2. **Inputs (Energy)** – Electricity (kWh), thermal energy (MJ), fuel (kg).
> 3. **Outputs (Products)** – Desired primary product, co-products, by-products.
> 4. **Outputs (Releases)** – Air emissions (kg CO₂-eq), water effluents, solid waste, accidental releases.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

Inventory Analysis sits within the **four-phase LCA methodology** under ISO 14040:

> **Phase 1 → Goal & Scope Definition** (sets the FU and system boundary)
> **Phase 2 → Life Cycle Inventory Analysis (LCI)** ← *THIS TOPIC*
> **Phase 3 → Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)**
> **Phase 4 → Interpretation**

### 🔬 The Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Bulleted Breakdown)

1. **Define the Functional Unit (FU):** Establish the quantified performance of the studied system. The FU normalizes all inventory data so that *apples-to-apples* comparisons become possible.
2. **Draw the System Boundary:** A flowchart that physically traces every unit operation — from raw material extraction ($\text{Cradle}$) → manufacturing → use → final disposal ($\text{Grave}$). Boundary choices: *Cradle-to-Gate*, *Gate-to-Gate*, *Cradle-to-Grave*.
3. **Collect Data for Each Unit Process:**
   - **Foreground data:** Site-specific, measured, primary.
   - **Background data:** Generic datasets (e.g., Ecoinvent, GaBi, USLCI).
4. **Validate Mass & Energy Balance:** Apply conservation laws to every unit operation to verify data consistency.
5. **Allocate Flows:** When a single process yields multiple products, partition inputs/outputs using ISO-compliant rules.
6. **Aggregate & Relate to FU:** Convert raw data into normalized values per FU.
7. **Document Limitations:** Uncertainty, cutoff criteria, and data quality indicators (DQI) must be transparently reported.

### ⚖️ The Conservation Principle (Why & How)

> **Why** it matters: In QRA, an unaccounted mass or energy term translates directly into a **miscalculated source term**, leading to under-estimated blast overpressure, toxic dispersion, or fire radiation. Mass balance is therefore **non-negotiable**.

**Mass Balance Statement** for any steady-state unit process:

$$
\text{Input}_{mass} + \text{Generated}_{in-situ} \;=\; \text{Output}_{mass} + \text{Accumulated} + \text{Consumed}_{reaction}
$$

For a steady-state, non-reactive system: **Input = Output**.

## 2.2 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m_{in} = m_{out} + m_{acc}$ | **Mass Balance** (general) | kg |
| 2 | $\sum m_{in,i} = \sum m_{out,j}$ | **Steady-state** mass balance | kg |
| 3 | $\sum E_{in} = \sum E_{out} + E_{loss}$ | **Energy Balance** (1st Law) | J or kWh |
| 4 | $I_{FU} = \dfrac{I_{raw}}{\text{Reference Flow}}$ | **Normalization to FU** | kg/FU |
| 5 | $\alpha_i = \dfrac{m_i}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} m_k}$ | **Allocation Factor** (mass basis) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{Economic Value}_i}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \text{EV}_k}$ | **Economic Allocation Factor** | dimensionless |
| 7 | $I_{agg} = \sum_{j} I_j \cdot CF_j$ | **LCIA Aggregated Inventory** | kg-eq / FU |
| 8 | $PV = \dfrac{FC}{(1+r)^t}$ | **Present Value of Inventory Holding Cost** | Currency |
| 9 | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | **Economic Order Quantity** (Wilson Model) | units |
| 10 | $SS = z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_{LTD}$ | **Safety Stock** (Statistical) | units |
| 11 | $N = \dfrac{m \cdot N_A}{M}$ | **Moles to Number of Entities** | molecules or atoms |
| 12 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | **Density (Inventory Storage)** | kg/m³ |
| 13 | $P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$ | **Ideal Gas Law (Vapour Inventory)** | Pa·m³ |
| 14 | $C_{release} = C_{sat} \cdot \dfrac{P_{partial}}{P^{\circ}}$ | **Vapour Concentration at Equilibrium** | ppm or kg/m³ |
| 15 | $LFL = \text{Lower Flammable Limit}$ | **Flammability Inventory Threshold** | vol % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall to Avoid:** When using `|` for absolute value in formulas inside markdown tables, always use the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` operator (e.g., $\vert x - \mu \vert$) to avoid breaking table rendering.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Inventory Analysis |
|---|---|
| **Petrochemical** | Quantify hydrocarbon inventories for BLEVE/VCE consequence modelling |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Track Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient (API) flows for EMA/FDA compliance |
| **Nuclear** | Account for fissile material inventories (kg U-235) for criticality safety |
| **Manufacturing** | Bill-of-Materials (BOM) accounting and Just-In-Time (JIT) stock control |
| **Environmental Eng.** | Carbon footprinting, water footprint, and eutrophication potential |
| **Civil / Structural** | Cement, steel, and aggregate inventory per m² of built-up area |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass Balance Normalization to Functional Unit

**Statement:** Given a raw inventory input of $I_{raw}$ kg and a Reference Flow $RF$ (mass of system product per FU), derive the normalized inventory per FU.

**Step 1 — Define the raw inventory measured for the system:**

$$
I_{raw} = 12{,}500 \;\text{kg of CO}_2
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Reference Flow** (e.g., the system produced 2,500 units of finished product during the data collection window):

$$
RF = 2{,}500 \;\text{units}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inventory per unit of product (i.e., per Functional Unit if 1 unit ≡ 1 FU):**

$$
I_{FU} = \frac{I_{raw}}{RF}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
I_{FU} = \frac{12{,}500}{2{,}500}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the quotient:**

$$
I_{FU} = 5.0 \;\text{kg CO}_2 / \text{FU}
$$

**Conversion Logic Explanation:** We divide because the raw data was collected for a *batch* of 2,500 products, but we want the impact associated with a *single* FU (one product). Division distributes the total inventory burden equitably.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Allocation by Mass (Multi-Output Process)

**Scenario:** A refinery distillation column yields three products: Naphtha (4,000 kg/h), Kerosene (3,000 kg/h), and Diesel (3,000 kg/h). The total crude feed (with 200 kg/h of process water) is 10,200 kg/h. Compute the **mass-based allocation factor** for each product.

**Step 1 — Sum all product outputs to obtain the denominator:**

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{3} m_k = 4{,}000 + 3{,}000 + 3{,}000 = 10{,}000 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the mass-allocation formula** $\alpha_i = m_i / \sum m_k$ for each product:

For **Naphtha**:

$$
\alpha_{Naphtha} = \frac{4{,}000}{10{,}000} = 0.40
$$

For **Kerosene**:

$$
\alpha_{Kerosene} = \frac{3{,}000}{10{,}000} = 0.30
$$

For **Diesel**:

$$
\alpha_{Diesel} = \frac{3{,}000}{10{,}000} = 0.30
$$

**Step 3 — Sanity check the sum of allocation factors:**

$$
\sum \alpha_i = 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.30 = 1.00 \;\checkmark
$$

**Conversion Logic Explanation:** Each $\alpha_i$ represents the *fraction* of the input burden (e.g., $CO_2$, energy, water) that is *fairly attributable* to product $i$. If the input inventory of $CO_2$ is 800 kg/h, then:

$$
CO_{2, Naphtha} = 800 \times 0.40 = 320 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Safety Stock for Hazardous Chemical Inventory

**Scenario:** A plant consumes nitric acid ($HNO_3$). Monthly demand follows a normal distribution with mean $\mu = 1{,}200$ L and standard deviation $\sigma = 90$ L. Lead time $L = 0.5$ months. Target service level = 95 % (z-score $z_{0.95} = 1.645$).

**Step 1 — Standard deviation of demand during lead time:**

$$
\sigma_{LTD} = \sigma \cdot \sqrt{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\sigma = 90$ L, $L = 0.5$ months:**

$$
\sigma_{LTD} = 90 \cdot \sqrt{0.5}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\sqrt{0.5}$:**

$$
\sigma_{LTD} = 90 \times 0.7071 = 63.64 \;\text{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Safety Stock (SS):**

$$
SS = z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_{LTD}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $z_{0.95} = 1.645$:**

$$
SS = 1.645 \times 63.64 = 104.69 \;\text{L}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Safety Stock is the *buffer* inventory held against stochastic demand surges during supplier lead time. In QRA, this buffer represents the **maximum credible inventory at any instant** — a critical input to consequence-modelling source terms.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Number of Molecules in a Liquid Inventory

**Scenario:** Inventory contains $m = 500$ g of benzene ($C_6H_6$, $M = 78$ g/mol). Compute the number of molecules.

**Step 1 — Compute moles:**

$$
n = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{500}{78} = 6.410 \;\text{mol}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert moles to number of entities using Avogadro's Number** ($N_A$):

$$
N = n \cdot N_A
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
N = 6.410 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$
N = 3.860 \times 10^{24} \;\text{molecules}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Useful for **toxicological dose-response modelling** and probabilistic risk assessment where exposure is expressed in molecule-counts (e.g., olfactory threshold studies).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Vapour Cloud Inventory from a Pressurised Liquid

**Scenario:** A 1 m³ vessel contains propane ($C_3H_8$) at $T = 298$ K and $P = 9 \times 10^5$ Pa. Compute the molar (and mass) inventory of propane vapour.

**Step 1 — Apply Ideal Gas Law:**

$$
n = \frac{P \cdot V}{R \cdot T}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
n = \frac{(9 \times 10^5)(1)}{(8.314)(298)}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator and denominator:**

$$
n = \frac{9 \times 10^5}{2{,}477.6} = 363.2 \;\text{mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert moles to mass** using $M_{C_3H_8} = 44.097$ g/mol:

$$
m = n \cdot M = 363.2 \times 44.097 = 16{,}016 \;\text{g} = 16.02 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This computed $m$ is the **direct input** to a Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE) TNT-equivalent model.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inventory Analysis Workflow (ISO 14040 Phase 2)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Goal and Scope from Phase 1] --> B[Define Functional Unit FU]
    B --> C[Draw Process Flow Diagram and System Boundary]
    C --> D[Identify All Unit Processes]
    D --> E[Collect Foreground Data Site Specific]
    D --> F[Collect Background Data Generic LCI DBs]
    E --> G[Compile Raw Inputs and Outputs]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Mass and Energy Balance Validation]
    H --> I{Is Balance Satisfied?}
    I -- No --> J[Refine Data Quality]
    J --> G
    I -- Yes --> K[Apply Allocation Rules]
    K --> L[Normalize Flows to Functional Unit]
    L --> M[Aggregate to LCI Results Table]
    M --> N[Document Uncertainty and Limitations]
    N --> O[End: Output to LCIA Phase 3]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hazardous Inventory Mapping for QRA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Inventory Source Identification] --> A2[Physical and Chemical State Recording]
    A2 --> A3[Quantity and Location Mapping GIS]
    A3 --> A4[Classification Flammable Toxic Reactive]
    A4 --> A5[Aggregation by Zone]
    A5 --> A6[Mass and Energy Balance Verification]
    A6 --> A7[Source Term Calculation]
    A7 --> A8[Consequence Modelling Input]
    A8 --> A9[Risk Matrix and Frequency Analysis]
    A9 --> A10[Decision Support to Plant Safety]
```

## 4.3 Inventory Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Plant Inventory Master] --> B1[Raw Materials]
    A0 --> B2[Intermediate Products]
    A0 --> B3[Finished Products]
    A0 --> B4[Hazardous Chemicals]
    B4 --> C1[Flammable Liquids]
    B4 --> C2[Toxic Gases]
    B4 --> C3[Oxidisers]
    B4 --> C4[Cryogenics]
    B4 --> C5[Radioactive Isotopes]
    C1 --> D1[Tank Farm Inventory L]
    C2 --> D2[Pressurised Cylinder Inventory kg]
    C3 --> D3[Bulk Storage Inventory tonnes]
```

## 4.4 Data Validation Loop (Steady-State Mass Balance)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input Stream m_in] --> V1[Validator Sum m_in]
    P2[Output Stream m_out] --> V2[Validator Sum m_out]
    V1 --> CHK{Difference less than 1 percent}
    V2 --> CHK
    CHK -- Yes --> ACC[Balance Achieved: Proceed]
    CHK -- No --> REJ[Flag Data: Re-measure or Re-estimate]
    REJ --> P1
    REJ --> P2
```
