# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define Inventory Analysis as per the ISO 14040 framework. List its four main data categories.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Inventory Analysis is the **second phase** of Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) under **ISO 14040/14044**. It is the systematic **quantification of all inputs and outputs** of a product system throughout its life cycle, expressed per **Functional Unit (FU)**.

The four main data categories are:
1. **Energy inputs** — electricity, fuels, thermal energy (MJ).
2. **Raw material inputs** — virgin and recycled feedstocks (kg).
3. **Products and co-products** — desired outputs (kg).
4. **Emissions and waste** — releases to air, water, and soil (kg or m³).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark for the ISO reference, 1 mark for the FU emphasis, 1 mark for correctly enumerating all four categories. Forgetting "per Functional Unit" costs 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between *Cradle-to-Gate*, *Gate-to-Gate*, and *Cradle-to-Grave* system boundaries with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Boundary Type | Scope | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Gate-to-Gate** | One facility only; from incoming raw to outgoing product | Measuring emissions of a single compressor station |
| **Cradle-to-Gate** | Raw extraction → factory exit (excludes use and disposal) | Cement production from limestone mining to plant gate |
| **Cradle-to-Grave** | Full life cycle: extraction → use → disposal | Electric vehicle LCA including battery end-of-life recycling |

The **system boundary choice directly governs which inventory items must be quantified** — a wider boundary requires more extensive data collection.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 🎯 Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for conducting a Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) Analysis**, highlighting the role of mass and energy balance validation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A distillation column produces three co-products. Total crude feed = **15,000 kg/day** (including 500 kg/day water). The three products are: Product X = 6,000 kg/day; Product Y = 4,500 kg/day; Product Z = 4,000 kg/day. The total $CO_2$ emission from the process is **2,400 kg/day**. Calculate the mass-based allocation factor for each product and the $CO_2$ allocated to each. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

The systematic procedure for LCI analysis is:

1. **Define the Functional Unit (FU):** The quantified performance that all inventory flows are normalised to.
2. **Establish the System Boundary:** Choose between *Cradle-to-Grave*, *Cradle-to-Gate*, or *Gate-to-Gate*.
3. **Construct the Process Flow Diagram:** Every unit process from raw extraction to disposal.
4. **Collect Foreground and Background Data:** Site-specific measurements + generic LCI databases.
5. **Validate Mass and Energy Balances:** Apply **conservation of mass** ($\sum m_{in} = \sum m_{out}$) and the **First Law of Thermodynamics** ($\sum E_{in} = \sum E_{out} + E_{loss}$) to every unit operation.
6. **Apply Allocation Rules:** ISO hierarchy — (i) avoid allocation by subdivision, (ii) use physical/causal relationship (mass, energy), (iii) use other relationships (economic).
7. **Aggregate and Normalize** all flows per FU.
8. **Document Data Quality, Uncertainty, and Cut-off Criteria.**

The **mass and energy balance validation** is the keystone of LCI quality. Any process where $\lvert m_{in} - m_{out}\rvert / m_{in} > 5\%$ is flagged for re-measurement, because unaccounted mass propagates into incorrect emission inventories and downstream LCIA impacts.

#### ✅ Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify organic product output (water is auxiliary, exclude from mass allocation basis):**

$$
\sum m_{organic} = 6{,}000 + 4{,}500 + 4{,}000 = 14{,}500 \;\text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute mass-allocation factor** for each product:

$$
\alpha_X = \frac{6{,}000}{14{,}500} = 0.4138
$$

$$
\alpha_Y = \frac{4{,}500}{14{,}500} = 0.3103
$$

$$
\alpha_Z = \frac{4{,}000}{14{,}500} = 0.2759
$$

**Step 3 — Verify sum of allocation factors equals unity:**

$$
0.4138 + 0.3103 + 0.2759 = 1.0000 \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Allocate total $CO_2$ of 2,400 kg/day using** $CO_{2,i} = \alpha_i \times CO_{2,total}$:

$$
CO_{2,X} = 0.4138 \times 2{,}400 = 993.1 \;\text{kg/day}
$$

$$
CO_{2,Y} = 0.3103 \times 2{,}400 = 744.8 \;\text{kg/day}
$$

$$
CO_{2,Z} = 0.2759 \times 2{,}400 = 662.1 \;\text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check:**

$$
993.1 + 744.8 + 662.1 = 2{,}400.0 \;\text{kg/day} \;\checkmark
$$

---

### 🎯 Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the **significance of inventory analysis in Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**. Explain with suitable examples how chemical inventory data feeds into source-term and consequence modelling. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A storage facility holds **8,000 kg of propane** at **$T = 300$ K** and **$P = 1.2 \times 10^6$ Pa** in a 10 m³ bullet tank. Compute the **mass of propane vapour** present at equilibrium inside the tank. ($M_{C_3H_8} = 44.097$ g/mol, R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹) **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

Inventory analysis is the **foundational quantitative layer** of QRA. Its significance is threefold:

1. **Source Term Determination:** The credible release rate (kg/s) for a Loss of Containment (LOC) event is computed from the *maximum credible inventory* at the failed equipment. Without accurate inventory data, all subsequent dispersion, fire, and explosion calculations are unreliable.
2. **Consequence Severity Modelling:** Toxic inventories feed the **probit equation** $Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$ for fatality estimation. Thermal radiation inventories (LNG, LPG) drive **pool-fire and jet-fire models** using the source term $Q = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$.
3. **Domino Effect Mapping:** By inventorying *all* hazardous substances and their physical separation distances, QRA analysts identify **escalation vectors** (e.g., a 50-tonne chlorine vessel located 30 m downstream of a 200-tonne LPG bullet triggers a domino scenario under specific wind/weather conditions).

**Examples:**
- **Bhopal (1984):** MIC inventory of 41 tonnes was the source term — a QRA inventory analysis would have flagged over-storage as catastrophic.
- **Fukushima (2011):** Zirconium-clad fuel inventory determined hydrogen generation rate inside the containment.

#### ✅ Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Calculate the number of moles of propane vapour using the Ideal Gas Law:**

$$
n = \frac{P \cdot V}{R \cdot T}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values** ($P = 1.2 \times 10^6$ Pa, $V = 10$ m³, $T = 300$ K, R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹):

$$
n = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^6)(10)}{(8.314)(300)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 1.2 \times 10^7
$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$
\text{Denominator} = 8.314 \times 300 = 2{,}494.2
$$

**Step 5 — Divide:**

$$
n = \frac{1.2 \times 10^7}{2{,}494.2} = 4{,}811.0 \;\text{mol}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert moles to mass** using $m = n \cdot M$:

$$
m = 4{,}811.0 \times 44.097 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate:**

$$
m = 212.15 \;\text{kg of propane vapour}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Frequent Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** of molar mass from g/mol to kg/mol → answer off by **1000×**.
> 2. Using °C instead of **K** for $T$ → entire calculation invalidated (loss of 2–3 marks).
> 3. Misapplying Ideal Gas Law to liquids → **propane is in vapour phase inside the tank**, the liquid mass is given separately and is *not* what the Ideal Gas Law computes.
> 4. Omitting the mass-balance check in Part (a) numerical problems → 1 mark deducted.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Inventory Analysis**

- ✅ **ISO 14040/14044** is the governing standard for LCA, with Inventory Analysis as **Phase 2**.
- ✅ **Functional Unit (FU)** is non-negotiable — every flow is normalized to it.
- ✅ **Mass balance:** $\sum m_{in} = \sum m_{out}$ (steady state) is the *single most important* validation step.
- ✅ **Energy balance:** $\sum E_{in} = \sum E_{out} + E_{loss}$ (1st Law of Thermodynamics).
- ✅ **Allocation rules hierarchy** (ISO 14044 §4.3.4): (1) subdivision, (2) physical causality (mass/energy), (3) economic/other.
- ✅ **Allocation factor formula:** $\alpha_i = m_i / \sum m_k$; must sum to **1.00**.
- ✅ **Safety Stock:** $SS = z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{L}$.
- ✅ **EOQ (Wilson Model):** $EOQ = \sqrt{2DS/H}$.
- ✅ **Number of entities:** $N = (m/M) \cdot N_A$ with $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹.
- ✅ **Ideal Gas Law for vapour inventory:** $PV = nRT$ with $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.
- ✅ **System boundaries** types: *Cradle-to-Gate*, *Gate-to-Gate*, *Cradle-to-Grave*.
- ✅ **QRA link:** Inventory Analysis → Source Term → Consequence Modelling → Probit-based Damage.
- ✅ **Data quality indicators (DQI):** precision, completeness, representativeness, consistency, reproducibility.
- ✅ **Always convert** $M$ (g/mol) to kg/mol before multiplying by moles to get kg.
- ✅ **Always convert** °C to K by adding 273.15 before using $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.
- ✅ **Use `\vert` or `\mid`** in LaTeX instead of `|` to prevent markdown table breaks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Major Industrial Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Major Industrial Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Major Industrial Hazard (MIH):** A major industrial hazard is an abnormal and uncontrolled occurrence, resulting from an uncontrolled development of energy or from a sudden release of hazardous substances (toxic, flammable, explosive, or asphyxiating), which causes—or has the potential to cause—loss of life, injury to persons, damage to property, or environmental degradation, on a significant scale.

In the KTU 2024 framework, this concept is formally anchored to:
- The **Factories Act, 1948 (India)** — definition of "hazardous process"
- The **Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC Rules), 1989** (amended 2000)
- The **Chemical Accidents (Emergency Planning, Preparedness and Response) Rules, 1996**
- **CIMAH Regulations (Control of Industrial Major Accident Hazards)**, UK — the original benchmark legislation that emerged after Flixborough (1974) and Seveso (1976)

A **Major Hazard Installation (MHI)** is therefore an industrial unit that stores, manufactures, processes, or handles hazardous chemicals in quantities equal to or exceeding the **threshold quantities** prescribed under Schedule 1 & 2 of the MSIHC Rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Quantity (TQ):** The minimum mass of a hazardous substance above which a unit becomes classified as a Major Hazard Installation. For example, **Ammonia (anhydrous): 50 tonnes**, **Chlorine: 10 tonnes**, **LPG: 50 tonnes**, **Acrylonitrile: 20 tonnes** — values prescribed under MSIHC Rules.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your kitchen at home. A small gas cylinder leakage is a **minor hazard** — unpleasant, but you can open the window, ventilate, and call the LPG agency. Now imagine an industrial complex storing **5,000 tonnes of LPG in pressurized bullets**. If a weld fails on one bullet, the released gas instantly forms a flammable vapor cloud the size of a football stadium. One spark → a **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)** that flattens everything within a **300 m radius** and shatters windows up to **2 km away**. That uncontrolled scaling-up of "the kitchen gas problem" is the essence of a **Major Industrial Hazard**.

**Key Idea:** It is not the *toxicity* or *flammability* of a substance alone that creates a major hazard — it is the **combination of intrinsic danger + inventory + confinement + proximity to population**. Remove any one of these factors and the catastrophic potential collapses.

### 1.3 Why the Topic is High-Yield for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This topic is consistently a **Part A (3-mark)** short-answer or a **Part B (14-mark)** essay-type question in KTU university examinations because it tests:
> (a) factual recall of landmark accidents (Bhopal, Flixborough, Seveso, Texas City, Toulouse),
> (b) classification ability (fire vs. explosion vs. toxic release),
> (c) regulatory awareness (MSIHC Rules, OSHA PSM, Seveso Directives), and
> (d) the ability to link **what happened** with **what should have been done** — i.e., the philosophy of QRA itself.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Energy/Inventory Damage Model

The severity of a major industrial hazard is governed by the **TNT-equivalent or energy-release** principle. The damage potential scales with the quantity of stored energy or mass of hazardous substance released:

$$E_{released} \propto m_{substance} \times \Delta H_{reaction}$$

Where:
- $E_{released}$ = energy released (kJ or MJ)
- $m_{substance}$ = mass of substance involved (kg or tonnes)
- $\Delta H_{reaction}$ = heat of reaction or combustion (kJ/kg)

> [!NOTE]
> This proportionality is the **foundation of all consequence modeling** that we will explore in Module 2 onward. For fire and explosion calculations, the mass of fuel released and its energy content dictate overpressure, thermal radiation, and toxic dose contours.

### 2.2 The Six Pillars of a Major Industrial Hazard (KTU Classification)

A major industrial hazard is conventionally classified along **six physical release phenomena**. Memorize this list — it is a guaranteed 3-marker in KTU:

1. **Fire / Pool Fire** — liquid pool burning on ground/water surface
2. **Jet Fire** — turbulent diffusion flame from a pressurized leak
3. **Flash Fire** — non-damaging flame passage over unburned vapor cloud
4. **Fireball** — buoyant, spherical, short-duration combustion of a released cloud (often post-BLEVE)
5. **Explosion (VCE / BLEVE / Confined)** — rapid energy release producing overpressure
6. **Toxic Release** — dispersion of harmful chemicals causing dose-driven injury/death

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "classify major industrial hazards," students often omit **Flash Fire** and **Fireball**, treating them as the same as Pool Fire. Examiners specifically look for these distinctions. Loss of **1 mark** is routine for this omission.

### 2.3 Landmark Industrial Disasters — Cause → Consequence → Lesson

| Year | Disaster | Location | Substance | Immediate Cause | Fatalities | KTU Lesson |
|------|----------|----------|-----------|-----------------|------------|------------|
| 1974 | **Flixborough** | UK | Cyclohexane (VCE) | Bypass pipe failure, 50 tonnes released | 28 | Importance of **modification management** and plant layout (domino effect between reactors) |
| 1976 | **Seveso** | Italy | TCDD (dioxin) | Runaway reaction, safety valve rupture | 0 immediate (long-term effects) | Need for **land-use planning** around chemical sites |
| 1984 | **Bhopal** | India | Methyl Isocyanate (MIC) | Water ingress into MIC tank | 3,800+ | Necessity of **inventory minimization** and community right-to-know |
| 1988 | **Piper Alpha** | UK (North Sea) | Crude oil / gas condensate | Condensate pump fire → escalation | 167 | **Spatial separation**, isolation valves, and **temporary refuge** |
| 1992 | **Toulouse** | France | Ammonium Nitrate | Improper storage of contaminated AN | 31 | Regulation of **incompatible materials** in storage |
| 2005 | **Texas City Refinery** | USA | Hydrocarbon (Raffinate) | Overfilling of distillation column | 15 | **Alarm rationalization** and operator workload |
| 2005 | **Buncefield** | UK | Petrol (gasoline) | Tank overfill, vapor cloud ignited | 43 injured | Bund wall design, **ATEX zoning**, secondary containment |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing "Bhopal occurred because MIC is a toxic gas." This is **factually incomplete**. Examiners expect the full chain: *Water ingress → runaway exothermic reaction → pressure rise → safety valve failure → MIC release → atmospheric inversion trapped the cloud*. A student who writes only the second half of this chain loses **2 marks** in Part B questions.

### 2.4 Regulatory Framework — A Comparative Snapshot

| Regulation | Jurisdiction | Key Concept Introduced |
|------------|--------------|------------------------|
| **Factories Act, 1948** (Sections 41A–41H) | India | Site appraisal, on-site emergency plan, safety audit |
| **MSIHC Rules, 1989** (amended 2000) | India | Notification of MHI, threshold quantities, safety reports, Safety Audit |
| **CIMAH Regulations, 1984** | UK (pre-Seveso) | First codified major-hazard control (post-Flixborough) |
| **Seveso Directive I (1982)** → **II (1996)** → **III (2012)** | EU | Domino effects, land-use planning, public information |
| **OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119)** | USA | Process Safety Management — 14 elements |
| **COMAH Regulations, 1999** | UK | Consolidation of Seveso II in UK law |
| **EPA Risk Management Plan (RMP)** | USA | Off-site consequence analysis required |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU, the **MSIHC Rules (India)** are the primary regulation to know in depth. Know the four Schedules: Schedule 1 (list of hazardous chemicals), Schedule 2 (isolated storage thresholds), Schedule 3 (list of toxic chemicals), and Schedule 4 (list of flammable chemicals).

### 2.5 The Quantitative Risk Triangle — Foundational Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk = f(Frequency, Consequence)** — Every major industrial hazard analysis rests on this two-dimensional foundation. Frequency is determined by fault-tree/event-tree analysis (Module 3), and consequence is determined by source→dispersion→effect modeling (Module 2).

$$R = f_c \times C$$

Where:
- $R$ = Risk (expected loss per year, e.g., fatalities/year)
- $f_c$ = Frequency of the hazardous event (events/year)
- $C$ = Consequence magnitude (e.g., expected fatalities per event)

This is the **core equation of Quantitative Risk Assessment** and reappears in every later module.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Released in a BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion)

A **BLEVE** is a major-hazard event that occurs when a pressurized liquefied gas container is exposed to external fire, weakening the metal shell until it ruptures catastrophically, releasing a fireball.

The mechanical energy released upon catastrophic vessel failure is given by:

$$E_{BLEVE} = \Delta U = m \cdot [u_2 - u_1]$$

Where:
- $E_{BLEVE}$ = mechanical energy released (kJ)
- $m$ = mass of fluid in vessel (kg)
- $u_1$ = specific internal energy **before** rupture (kJ/kg)
- $u_2$ = specific internal energy **after** rupture to atmospheric pressure (kJ/kg)

**Step-by-step expansion:**

For an ideal-gas approximation of the vapor space, the rapid expansion work against atmospheric pressure is:

$$E_{BLEVE} = m \cdot (u_2 - u_1) + P_{atm}(V_2 - V_1)$$

Since rupture is essentially instantaneous and adiabatic:

$$E_{BLEVE} \approx \frac{(P_1 - P_{atm}) \cdot V_1}{\gamma - 1}$$

Where:
- $P_1$ = internal pressure before rupture (Pa)
- $P_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure (Pa), typically **101,325 Pa**
- $V_1$ = initial gas/vapor volume in vessel (m³)
- $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats, $C_p / C_v$ (dimensionless)

For most hydrocarbons, $\gamma \approx 1.1$ to $1.4$.

**Numerical Demonstration (typical LPG bullet):**

Given:
- $P_1 = 1.6 \times 10^6$ Pa (≈ 16 bar gauge)
- $P_{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $V_1 = 50$ m³ (vapor space of a 100 m³ bullet ~50% full)
- $\gamma = 1.2$

Substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{BLEVE} &= \frac{(1.6 \times 10^6 - 1.013 \times 10^5) \cdot 50}{1.2 - 1} \\
&= \frac{(1.4987 \times 10^6) \cdot 50}{0.2} \\
&= \frac{7.4935 \times 10^7}{0.2} \\
&= 3.747 \times 10^8 \text{ J} \\
&\approx 375 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Convert to TNT equivalent** (1 tonne TNT = 4.184 × 10⁹ J):

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{TNT} &= \frac{3.747 \times 10^8}{4.184 \times 10^9} \\
&= 0.0895 \text{ tonnes TNT} \\
&\approx 89.5 \text{ kg TNT equivalent}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A single 100 m³ LPG bullet BLEVE releases energy equivalent to **~90 kg of TNT**. Now imagine a tank farm with **20 such bullets** — the cumulative TNT equivalent becomes **~1.8 tonnes**, which is in the range of a large military bomb. This is why storage spacing (fire walls, separation distances) is a non-negotiable QRA requirement.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Frequency vs. Consequence Classification

| Event Type | Typical Frequency (per year) | Typical Consequence (Fatalities) | Risk Profile |
|------------|-----------------------------|---------------------------------|--------------|
| Pipe leak (small) | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | 0 | Low consequence, moderate frequency |
| Major leak (10 mm) | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 1–10 | Moderate |
| Catastrophic rupture (full bore) | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ | 100–1,000+ | High consequence, low frequency — **classic major hazard** |
| VCE of large cloud | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ | 1,000+ (off-site) | Major off-site hazard |
| Toxic release (Bhopal-scale) | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ | 1,000–10,000 | **Maximum consequence** event |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Question Pattern:** *"Why are major industrial hazards rare but catastrophic, and how does QRA address this paradox?"* — The answer hinges on this matrix: rare events are statistically hard to predict from incident records alone, so **QRA uses fault-tree and event-tree analysis** (Module 3) to compute frequencies that are otherwise unobservable.

### 3.3 Domino Effect — Mathematical Treatment

A **domino effect** is the propagation of an initial accident to adjacent installations, escalating the overall consequence. The frequency of the *escalated* event is:

$$f_{domino} = f_{primary} \times P_{escalation}$$

Where $P_{escalation}$ is the probability that the primary event damages the secondary installation (depends on overpressure, thermal radiation, and fragment throw distance at the secondary installation's location).

The consequence is now additive:

$$C_{total} = C_{primary} + C_{secondary} + C_{tertiary} + \ldots$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Piper Alpha (1988)** is the textbook domino failure. The initial condensate pump fire escalated to the platform's main oil processing train because isolation valves were inaccessible under fire. Always cite this example when discussing domino effects in KTU answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Major Industrial Hazard Causation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["HAZARDOUS SUBSTANCE STORED"] --> B["INVENTORY ABOVE THRESHOLD QUANTITY"]
    B --> C["INITIATING EVENT OCCURS"]
    C --> D["RELEASE / LOSS OF CONTAINMENT"]
    D --> E["PHYSICAL PHENOMENON TRIGGERED"]
    E --> F1["FIRE PATH"]
    E --> F2["EXPLOSION PATH"]
    E --> F3["TOXIC DISPERSION PATH"]
    F1 --> G1["THERMAL RADIATION"]
    F1 --> G2["FLAME ENVELOPE"]
    F2 --> G3["OVERPRESSURE"]
    F2 --> G4["PROJECTILE FRAGMENTS"]
    F3 --> G5["TOXIC DOSE"]
    G1 --> H["HUMAN / ASSET / ENVIRONMENT IMPACT"]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    H --> I["RISK MANAGEMENT DECISIONS"]
    I --> J1["PREVENTION"]
    I --> J2["MITIGATION"]
    I --> J3["EMERGENCY RESPONSE"]
    I --> J4["LAND USE PLANNING"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The chain moves from left (inventory) to right (consequence). A QRA engineer must insert **barriers** at every stage — early detection, isolation valves, relief devices, evacuation systems — to break the chain before it reaches the impact node.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Six Physical Release Phenomena (Classification)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["MAJOR INDUSTRIAL HAZARD"] --> B["FIRE CATEGORY"]
    A --> C["EXPLOSION CATEGORY"]
    A --> D["TOXIC RELEASE CATEGORY"]
    B --> B1["POOL FIRE"]
    B --> B2["JET FIRE"]
    B --> B3["FLASH FIRE"]
    B --> B4["FIREBALL POST BLEVE"]
    C --> C1["VAPOR CLOUD EXPLOSION VCE"]
    C --> C2["BLEVE MECHANICAL"]
    C --> C3["CONFINED EXPLOSION"]
    C --> C4["UNCONFINED VAPOR CLOUD"]
    D --> D1["NEUTRAL GAS PLUME"]
    D --> D2["HEAVY GAS DISPERSION"]
    D --> D3["INSTANTANEOUS PUFF RELEASE"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Regulatory Pyramid for MHI in India

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["FACTORIES ACT 1948"] --> P2["MSIHC RULES 1989 AMENDED 2000"]
    P2 --> P3["SITE APPRAISAL"]
    P2 --> P4["SAFETY REPORT"]
    P2 --> P5["ON SITE EMERGENCY PLAN"]
    P2 --> P6["OFF SITE EMERGENCY PLAN"]
    P2 --> P7["SAFETY AUDIT"]
    P3 --> Q["MAJOR HAZARD INSTALLATION CONTROLLED"]
    P4 --> Q
    P5 --> Q
    P6 --> Q
    P7 --> Q
    Q --> R["PROTECTION OF WORKERS PUBLIC ENVIRONMENT"]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: QRA Workflow Anchored to Major Hazards

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1 HAZARD IDENTIFICATION"] --> S2["STEP 2 FREQUENCY ANALYSIS"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3 CONSEQUENCE ANALYSIS"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4 RISK ESTIMATION"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5 RISK EVALUATION"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6 RISK TREATMENT"]
    S1 -.->|uses| T1["HAZOP WHAT IF CHECKLIST"]
    S2 -.->|uses| T2["FAULT TREE EVENT TREE"]
    S3 -.->|uses| T3["SOURCE DISPERSION FIRE EXPLOSION MODELS"]
    S4 -.->|uses| T4["RISK = FREQUENCY X CONSEQUENCE"]
    S5 -.->|uses| T5["ALARP COMPARED TO CRITERIA"]
    S6 -.->|uses| T6["PREVENTION MITIGATION EMERGENCY PLANNING"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define the term "Major Hazard Installation" (MHI) as per the Indian MSIHC Rules. Mention the threshold quantities of any two hazardous chemicals that trigger MHI classification.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Major Hazard Installation (MHI)** is an industrial unit, including storage, processing, or transport facility, in which one or more hazardous chemicals are stored, handled, or processed in quantities equal to or exceeding the **threshold quantity** specified in **Schedule 1, 2, 3, or 4** of the MSIHC Rules, 1989 (amended 2000).
Examples of threshold quantities:
- **Ammonia (anhydrous): 50 tonnes**
- **Chlorine: 10 tonnes**
- **Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG): 50 tonnes**
- **Methyl Isocyanate: 150 kg** (extremely low — illustrates its acute toxicity)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Differentiate between a "Pool Fire" and a "Jet Fire" as types of major industrial hazards.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Pool Fire | Jet Fire |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| **Source** | Liquid pool on ground or water surface | Pressurized leak from ruptured pipe/fitting |
| **Flame geometry** | Horizontal, buoyant column above pool | Directional, along leak axis (turbulent jet) |
| **Release pressure** | Atmospheric / low pressure | High pressure (typically > 1 bar gauge) |
| **Typical heat flux** | 25–35 kW/m² near flame | 100–350 kW/m² in flame envelope |
| **Hazard range** | Pool radius + flame reach | Distance along jet axis until dilution |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 marks)**

**(a)** *[7 marks]* With the help of a detailed causation chain, explain how the **Bhopal disaster of 1984** qualifies as a major industrial hazard. Discuss the role of **inventory**, **containment failure**, and **atmospheric conditions** in the magnitude of the tragedy.

**(b)** *[7 marks]* Classify the major industrial hazards into **fire, explosion, and toxic release** categories. For each category, give **two examples** with a brief description of the physical mechanism.

---

##### **Model Solution — Q A (a)**

The Bhopal disaster (December 2–3, 1984, Union Carbide India Ltd.) is the world's worst industrial catastrophe.

**Step 1 — Inventory (1 mark):** Union Carbide stored approximately **41 tonnes of Methyl Isocyanate (MIC)** in three underground steel tanks. MIC is a highly toxic, volatile, and reactive intermediate used in the manufacture of the pesticide *Sevin* (carbaryl).

**Step 2 — Containment Failure (2 marks):** The disaster was triggered by water ingress into Tank 610 containing MIC. Water initiated a runaway exothermic reaction:

$$\text{MIC} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Methylamine} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{Heat}$$

The reaction pressure rose rapidly, exceeding the capacity of the **vent gas scrubber** (which had been turned off for maintenance) and the **flare tower** (which was also non-operational). The pressure-relief valve opened, releasing ~27 tonnes of MIC into the atmosphere over a 2-hour period.

**Step 3 — Atmospheric Conditions (1 mark):** Bhopal on that night had a **temperature inversion** (cold, dense air near ground trapping pollutants), a **low wind speed** (~3 km/h), and **high humidity**. The cloud did not disperse; instead, it hugged the densely populated shantytown **"Null Bazaar"** adjacent to the plant boundary.

**Step 4 — Consequences (2 marks):**
- **Immediate fatalities:** ~3,800 (official), estimates up to 16,000+
- **Long-term health effects:** ~500,000 exposed, chronic respiratory and ocular damage
- **Environmental:** Soil and groundwater contamination persisting for decades

**Step 5 — Why it qualifies as MIH (1 mark):**
- Toxic substance (MIC) stored in quantity **far exceeding threshold**
- Multiple **safety layers failed simultaneously** (scrubber, flare, refrigeration)
- **Off-site population** at close proximity (~100 m) bore the brunt
- Demonstrates that *proximity + inventory + failure of controls = MIH*

---

##### **Model Solution — Q A (b)**

**A. FIRE CATEGORY (2 marks):**
1. **Pool Fire:** A liquid fuel, upon release, pools on the ground or water surface and burns. Flame height is governed by pool diameter; thermal radiation falls off with distance squared.
   - *Example:* Bund fire in a refinery tank farm after a dike breach.
2. **Jet Fire:** A pressurized gas/liquid release through an orifice forms a turbulent diffusion flame aligned with the leak direction.
   - *Example:* Natural gas line rupture at a compressor station.

**B. EXPLOSION CATEGORY (2.5 marks):**
1. **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE):** A large flammable vapor cloud disperses, finds a delayed ignition source, and produces overpressure. Highly damaging due to flame front acceleration in congested regions.
   - *Example:* Flixborough 1974 — 50 tonnes of cyclohexane vapor exploded.
2. **BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion):** A pressurized liquefied-gas vessel fails under external fire; superheated liquid flash-evaporates explosively.
   - *Example:* LPG rail-car BLEVE at San Juan Ixhuatepec (Mexico, 1984).

**C. TOXIC RELEASE CATEGORY (2.5 marks):**
1. **Continuous Toxic Release:** A pressurized toxic gas leak forms a plume (neutral or heavy gas), causing dose-driven casualties at downwind distances.
   - *Example:* Chlorine release from a storage bullet in a water-treatment plant.
2. **Instantaneous Puff:** A pressurized toxic liquefied gas catastrophically ruptures, forming an instantaneous cloud that disperses.
   - *Example:* Anhydrous ammonia refrigerated storage failure.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *[7 marks]* Describe the **"Flixborough disaster (1974)"** with reference to the failure mechanism, immediate cause, regulatory aftermath, and lessons learned for the control of major industrial hazards.

**(b)** *[7 marks]* Explain the concept of **"Threshold Quantity"** under the Indian MSIHC Rules. Why is the concept critical to the classification of an industrial unit as a **Major Hazard Installation**? Illustrate with two examples.

---

##### **Model Solution — Q B (a)**

**Background (1 mark):** The Nypro Caprolactam plant at Flixborough, UK, produced caprolactam, a nylon-6 precursor. The process used **cyclohexane** in a series of six reactors in series.

**Failure Mechanism (2 marks):** A leaking agitation shaft on Reactor 4 (containing cyclohexane at ~155 °C and ~7.9 bar) forced its removal. To maintain production, a temporary **20-inch diameter bypass pipe** was installed to connect Reactor 3 directly to Reactor 5, supported by a metal cradle scaffold. The pipe sagged during operation due to inadequate mechanical design (no flexibility analysis, no pressure testing of the temporary modification).

**Immediate Cause (1 mark):** On 1 June 1974, the bypass pipe failed, releasing approximately **50 tonnes of cyclohexane** in ~50 seconds. The vapor cloud drifted ~30 m to the boiler house, ignited, and produced a **VCE** of TNT equivalent ~15 tonnes.

**Consequences (1 mark):**
- 28 on-site fatalities
- 36 injuries
- Complete destruction of the plant
- 53 off-site injuries

**Regulatory Aftermath (1 mark):** The Flixborough disaster directly motivated the UK Health and Safety Executive to introduce the **CIMAH Regulations, 1984** (Control of Industrial Major Accident Hazards). It also led to mandatory **safety case** and **modification management** requirements worldwide, and to the **Seveso Directive (1982)** in the EU.

**Lessons Learned (1 mark):**
- **Modification Management:** No process change without proper engineering review, MOC (Management of Change) protocol.
- **Plant Layout (Spatial Separation):** Reactor 4 was close to Reactor 5; the domino principle propagated.
- **Vapor Cloud Mitigation:** Need for steam curtains, vapor barriers, and ignition-source control.
- **Role of QRA:** Quantification of off-site risk became legally mandated.

---

##### **Model Solution — Q B (b)**

**Definition (2 marks):** The **Threshold Quantity (TQ)** is the minimum quantity of a hazardous chemical, as specified in Schedules 1–4 of the MSIHC Rules, 1989 (amended 2000), above which an industrial activity is classified as a **Major Hazard Installation (MHI)**. It is the regulatory cut-off that triggers notification, safety reports, and emergency planning obligations.

**Why it is critical (3 marks):**
1. **Proportional Regulation:** A small LPG cylinder refill station and a massive petrochemical complex are not regulated identically. TQ differentiates them on a *scientific, quantity-based* criterion rather than arbitrary judgment.
2. **Risk-Based Trigger:** Higher inventory = greater potential release energy. The TQ is calibrated so that any facility above it has the potential for an off-site consequence.
3. **Legal Obligations Kick-in:** Above TQ, the occupier must:
   - Notify the **Factory Inspectorate** and the **District Authority**
   - Prepare a **Safety Report**
   - Prepare an **On-site Emergency Plan**
   - Cooperate with the **District Collector** on **Off-site Emergency Plans**
   - Conduct periodic **Safety Audits**
   - Maintain a **Major Accident Hazard Policy**

**Examples (2 marks):**
- **Chlorine:** Threshold quantity = **10 tonnes**. A water-treatment plant storing 12 tonnes of chlorine is an MHI; one storing 5 tonnes is not.
- **LPG:** Threshold quantity = **50 tonnes** (in a single storage installation). A small bottling plant with 30 tonnes total is non-MHI; a large bullet farm with 200 tonnes is.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write "Bhopal killed thousands" and stop. Examiners reward the **causation chain** (water ingress → exothermic reaction → pressure rise → safety systems failed → release → atmospheric inversion → exposure). Skipping intermediate steps costs **2–3 marks** per sub-part.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse *Pool Fire* with *Jet Fire* — they have **different thermal radiation profiles** and require different consequence models. The KTU answer key explicitly tests this distinction.
> 3. **Do NOT** write a generic "Industrial safety is important" essay. The question demands **MSIHC Rules, threshold quantities, regulatory references, and accident chronology**. Markers deduct 1 mark per missing factual anchor.
> 4. **Do NOT** present formulas without defining variables. Even a 3-mark Part A answer is penalized if a formula is left "dangling."
> 5. **Do NOT** write about Flixborough and Bhopal as "old incidents" — connect them to the **modern MSIHC Rules** to demonstrate integrated understanding.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Major Industrial Hazard = abnormal uncontrolled release of hazardous substance causing significant loss → MIH if **threshold quantity** exceeded (MSIHC Rules).
- **Six Categories:** Pool Fire, Jet Fire, Flash Fire, Fireball, VCE/BLEVE/Confined Explosion, Toxic Release.
- **Five Landmark Disasters (must know):** Flixborough 1974, Seveso 1976, Bhopal 1984, Piper Alpha 1988, Buncefield 2005.
- **Three Cause-Failure Pillars:** Inventory + Containment Failure + Proximity to Population.
- **Regulatory Core (India):** Factories Act 1948 + MSIHC Rules 1989 (amended 2000) + Chemical Accidents Rules 1996.
- **Threshold Quantity Examples:** Ammonia 50 t, Chlorine 10 t, LPG 50 t, MIC 150 kg, Acrylonitrile 20 t.
- **Core Equation:** Risk = Frequency × Consequence ($R = f_c \times C$).
- **BLEVE Energy Formula:** $E \approx (P_1 - P_{atm})V_1 / (\gamma - 1)$.
- **TNT Equivalent:** 1 tonne TNT = $4.184 \times 10^9$ J.
- **Domino Effect:** $f_{domino} = f_{primary} \times P_{escalation}$.
- **Causation Chain to memorize:** Initiating Event → Loss of Containment → Physical Phenomenon → Dispersion/Engulfment → Impact → Risk Management.
- **QRA Workflow:** Hazard ID → Frequency Analysis → Consequence Analysis → Risk Estimation → Risk Evaluation → Risk Treatment.
- **Three Regulatory Phrases to recall:** *"Threshold Quantity," "Safety Report," "On-site Emergency Plan"* — these are guaranteed marks in any Part A question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reasons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reasons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** as prescribed under the KTU 2024 scheme for Process Engineering / Chemical Technology streams, the term **"Reasons"** refers to the systematic catalogue of **justifications, motivations, and driving forces** that necessitate the formal evaluation of risk in process industries. It encompasses the *why* behind performing QRA — covering **historical, regulatory, economic, technical, ethical, and societal** rationales that have shaped the modern practice of risk-based decision-making in chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas, and allied hazardous process facilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1 - Reasons):**
> *"Reasons for performing Quantitative Risk Assessment — understanding the historical, regulatory, economic, and safety-driven motivations that compel process industries to adopt systematic, quantified risk evaluation methodologies as an integral part of engineering design, plant operation, and corporate governance."*

### 1.2 Key Categories of "Reasons" in QRA

The reasons for conducting QRA can be broadly classified into **six (6) major categories**:

| # | Category | Core Driver |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | Historical Reasons | Major industrial disasters (Bhopal, Flixborough, Seveso, Texas City) |
| 2 | Regulatory Reasons | Statutory compliance (OSHA, EPA, OISD, PESO, MoEFCC) |
| 3 | Economic Reasons | Loss prevention, insurance, business continuity |
| 4 | Safety Reasons | Protection of personnel (HSE), public, environment |
| 5 | Engineering Reasons | Design optimization, siting decisions, layout planning |
| 6 | Ethical / Social Reasons | Duty of care, corporate social responsibility (CSR) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Why Do We Buy Health Insurance?"**
>
> Imagine you are planning a long road trip across Kerala in your car. You *could* drive without insurance — and most days, nothing happens. But you still buy comprehensive insurance. **Why?**
>
> Not because accidents are *guaranteed*, but because:
> - The **consequence** of an accident (a major crash) is so severe that even a *small probability* × *huge consequence* = **unacceptable risk**.
> - **Historical data** shows accidents DO happen to other drivers.
> - **Regulations** (Motor Vehicles Act) make third-party insurance mandatory.
> - **Economic logic** says: paying a small premium is far cheaper than bearing a massive hospital/liability cost.
> - **Engineering logic** says: knowing the risk lets you buy better brakes, airbags, and choose safer routes.
>
> **QRA is the "insurance policy + engineering inspection" of the process industry.** The "Reasons" module explains *why* the industry was forced to adopt this disciplined approach — primarily because catastrophic losses from past disasters proved that *reactive* safety (fixing problems after they occur) is **not** acceptable when human lives, the environment, and capital assets are at stake.

### 1.4 Standard KTU Terminology Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terms to Memorize (KTU Module 1):**
> - **Hazard**: Source of potential harm (intrinsic property).
> - **Risk**: Combination of probability of occurrence and severity of consequence. Mathematically: $R = f(P, C)$ where $P$ = probability, $C$ = consequence.
> - **QRA**: A formal, structured, numerical methodology to estimate risk levels.
> - **ALARP**: As Low As Reasonably Practicable — the risk-reduction principle.
> - **Individual Risk (IR)**: Probability that an average person at a location will be harmed per year.
> - **Societal Risk (SR)**: Relationship between frequency of events and the number of people affected.
- **Threshold Frequency:** **1 × 10⁻⁶ per year** (standard benchmark for individual risk in process industries).
- **Tolerable Frequency (F-N Curve Boundary):** Defined by UK HSE / Dutch regulations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of "Reasons" — Structured Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Historical Reasons (The Most Important KTU Trigger)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Historical disasters are the **most frequently asked topic** in KTU Module 1. Memorize at least **3 landmark incidents** with year, location, cause, fatalities, and lesson learned.

**Landmark Disasters that Forced QRA into Existence:**

| Year | Disaster | Location | Fatalities | Key Lesson |
|------|----------|----------|------------|------------|
| **1974** | **Flixborough Disaster** | UK (Nypro Caprolactam Plant) | **28** | Lesson: Importance of **layout planning**, **vessel spacing**, and management of change (MOC). |
| **1974** | **Bhopal Gas Tragedy** | India (Union Carbide) | **3,800+ immediate; 15,000+ total** | Lesson: **Worst industrial disaster in history** — proved that *uncontrolled* hazardous releases can cause mass casualties, especially in populated areas. Emphasized **land-use planning (LUP)** and **off-site risk**. |
| **1976** | **Seveso Disaster** | Italy (Icmesa Chemical) | **0 immediate (long-term effects)** | Lesson: Led to the **Seveso Directive** in EU. Highlighted **toxic gas dispersion** modelling needs. |
| **1984** | **Mexico City PEMEX** | Mexico | **500+** | Lesson: Importance of **siting of control rooms**, **occupied buildings**, and **fireball/explosion modelling**. |
| **1988** | **Piper Alpha** | North Sea (UK) | **167** | Lesson: **Worst offshore disaster** — importance of **safety instrumented systems (SIS)**, **fire & explosion risk**, **emergency shutdown (ESD)**. |
| **2005** | **Texas City Refinery** | USA (BP) | **15** | Lesson: Importance of **human factors**, **trailer siting** (occupied temporary buildings near process units). |
| **2010** | **Deepwater Horizon** | Gulf of Mexico (BP) | **11** | Lesson: **Worst offshore oil spill** — highlighted **blowout preventer failure** and **process safety management (PSM)** gaps. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Students often write only the *year* and *name* of disasters. Examiners award marks for: **(i) Cause**, **(ii) Consequence**, **(iii) Specific QRA lesson learned**. Always include all three.

#### 2.1.2 Regulatory / Statutory Reasons

QRA is mandated (or strongly recommended) by multiple regulatory frameworks:

- **India:** 
  - **OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate)** — Standard 116, 117, 118 on QRA, Fire & Explosion, and Layout.
  - **PESO (Petroleum & Explosives Safety Organisation)** — Governs storage and handling.
  - **MoEFCC (Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change)** — EIA Notification 2006 mandates risk assessment for EIA.
  - **Factories Act 1948** (Section 7A, 14) — Safety of workers.
- **International:**
  - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** (USA) — Process Safety Management (PSM).
  - **EPA Risk Management Plan (RMP)** (USA).
  - **Seveso III Directive (2012/18/EU)** (EU).
  - **COMAH Regulations** (UK).
  - **IEC 61511** (Functional Safety — SIS).

#### 2.1.3 Economic Reasons

The **economic cost of accidents** has been studied and quantified. Industry data shows:

- For every **1 USD** saved on safety, the long-term loss can be **10 to 100 times higher** when accidents occur.
- **Total Loss Control** approach: QRA is cheaper than reactive disaster recovery.
- **Insurance Premiums**: Insurers (e.g., Lloyds of London) demand QRA reports for underwriting high-hazard facilities.
- **Business Continuity**: A single major accident can shut down a plant for years, leading to massive revenue loss.

#### 2.1.4 Safety Reasons (Personnel, Public, Environment)

- Protection of **workers (occupational safety)**.
- Protection of **neighbouring population (off-site risk)**.
- Protection of **environment** (air, water, soil contamination).
- **Quantitative** risk targets: $IR \leq 10^{-6} \text{ per year}$ for workers; $IR \leq 10^{-7} \text{ per year}$ for public.

#### 2.1.5 Engineering / Design Reasons

QRA inputs directly influence:
- **Plant layout and spacing** (vessel-to-vessel distances).
- **Siting of control rooms, occupied buildings, fire stations**.
- **Selection of safety instrumented systems (SIS) and SIL levels**.
- **Design of fire protection systems** (deluge, foam, gas detection).
- **Emergency response planning**.

#### 2.1.6 Ethical & Social Reasons

- **Duty of care** of employer towards employees.
- **Precautionary Principle**: When hazard exists, quantitative evaluation is ethically mandatory.
- **CSR (Corporate Social Responsibility)** — modern corporations are judged on ESG metrics.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Reference (Module 1 - Reasons):**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Basic Risk Equation** | $R = P \times C$ | $P$ = Probability (per year), $C$ = Consequence (fatalities, INR, etc.) |
| 2 | **Individual Risk (IR)** | $IR_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j \times p_{ij}$ | Per year; sum over all accident scenarios $j$ |
| 3 | **Societal Risk (Potential Loss of Life, PLL)** | $PLL = \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j \times N_j$ | Fatalities per year; $N_j$ = number of fatalities in scenario $j$ |
| 4 | **Tolerable Individual Risk (Worker)** | $IR \leq 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per year}$ | KTU/Kletz benchmark |
| 5 | **Tolerable Individual Risk (Public)** | $IR \leq 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per year}$ | Industry standard |
| 6 | **F-N Curve (Societal Risk)** | $F \cdot N^k \leq C_{risk}$ | $F$ = frequency, $N$ = number of fatalities, $k \approx 1 \text{ to } 2$ |
| 7 | **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** | $RRF = \dfrac{R_{initial}}{R_{reduced}}$ | Used in ALARP demonstration |
| 8 | **Cost of Risk Reduction** | $\text{Gross Cost of Averting a Fatality (GCAF)} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{Cost}}{\Delta \text{PLL}}$ | INR per statistical fatality avoided |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip on Formulas:** For Part A (3-mark) questions, you may be asked to *define and write* the basic risk equation. For Part B (14-mark) questions, expect derivations of IR or PLL with numerical substitution.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The "Reasons" framework is **not theoretical** — it is the **business case** for QRA. In practice:
- An engineering consultant (e.g., DNV, Lloyd's Register, Quest Consultants) writes a QRA report for an oil refinery in Kerala (e.g., BPCL Kochi). The "Reasons" section of the report justifies **why** the assessment is being done — citing Kerala State PCB regulations, OISD-116 compliance, and the plant's proximity to Kochi city.
- This justification is what **binds** the engineering work to the regulatory and ethical foundations of process safety.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From "Consequence + Probability" to Individual Risk (IR)

**Starting Premise:** QRA's fundamental premise is that risk is the *product* (or *function*) of probability and consequence. We will derive the Individual Risk (IR) expression step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Define Consequence Severity Vector:**

For a given process unit, consider $n$ distinct accident scenarios (e.g., BLEVE, VCE, Toxic Release, Jet Fire, Pool Fire). Each scenario $j$ has a consequence severity measured in terms of a *harm footprint* $H_j(x, y)$, which is a function of spatial location $(x, y)$ on the plant.

**Step 2 — Define Frequency of Each Scenario:**

Let $f_j$ be the frequency (per year) at which scenario $j$ occurs. Typical values:

$$f_j \in \left[10^{-7}, \, 10^{-2}\right] \text{ per year}$$

**Step 3 — Define Conditional Probability of Fatality:**

Let $p_{ij}$ be the conditional probability that a person located at point $i$ is fatally injured *given* the occurrence of scenario $j$.

**Step 4 — Formulate Individual Risk at Point $i$:**

The Individual Risk at point $i$ is the **sum** over all scenarios of (frequency of scenario) × (probability of fatality at that point given the scenario):

$$IR_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j \cdot p_{ij}$$

This is the foundational IR equation. It is *additive* in the frequency domain because each scenario is treated as an independent event in the fault tree / event tree analysis.

**Step 5 — Converting to Annual Probability:**

For KTU problems, $IR_i$ is reported as a probability **per year**, e.g., $IR_i = 4.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$.

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

Consider a process unit with 3 accident scenarios:
- Scenario 1 (Toxic Release): $f_1 = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$, $p_{1} = 0.1$ at point $P$.
- Scenario 2 (Jet Fire): $f_2 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$, $p_{2} = 0.05$ at point $P$.
- Scenario 3 (VCE): $f_3 = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$, $p_{3} = 0.5$ at point $P$.

Calculate $IR_P$:

$$IR_P = f_1 p_1 + f_2 p_2 + f_3 p_3$$

$$IR_P = (1 \times 10^{-4})(0.1) + (5 \times 10^{-5})(0.05) + (1 \times 10^{-6})(0.5)$$

$$IR_P = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} + 2.5 \times 10^{-6} + 5.0 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$IR_P = 1.25 \times 10^{-5} + 5.0 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$IR_P = 1.30 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$

**Conclusion:** The Individual Risk at point $P$ is $1.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$, which is **below** the typical worker tolerable limit of $10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$ but **above** the public tolerable limit of $10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$. Hence, point $P$ would be acceptable for *trained workers* but not for *general public*.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Potential Loss of Life (PLL) from Frequency-Consequence Data

**Step 1 — Define PLL Concept:**

PLL is the *expected* number of fatalities per year across the entire plant / facility. It is a scalar metric used in **ALARP** and **cost-benefit** analyses.

**Step 2 — Express PLL Mathematically:**

For each scenario $j$, multiply the frequency $f_j$ (per year) by the number of fatalities $N_j$ expected from that scenario:

$$PLL_j = f_j \times N_j$$

**Step 3 — Sum Over All Scenarios:**

$$PLL_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j \cdot N_j$$

**Step 4 — Sanity Check via the F-N Curve:**

The F-N curve plots *cumulative frequency* $F$ (y-axis) vs *number of fatalities* $N$ (x-axis). For a process unit:

$$F(N \geq N_j) = \sum_{k \,:\, N_k \geq N_j} f_k$$

The point $(N_j, F_j)$ must lie **below** the regulatory F-N tolerance line for the risk to be acceptable.

**Numerical Example:**

| Scenario | $f_j$ (yr⁻¹) | $N_j$ (fatalities) | $f_j \cdot N_j$ (PLL) |
|----------|---------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Toxic Release | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | 2 | $2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Pool Fire | $5 \times 10^{-5}$ | 1 | $5.0 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| VCE | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ | 25 | $2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| BLEVE | $3 \times 10^{-7}$ | 50 | $1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| **Total** | — | — | **$\mathbf{3.15 \times 10^{-4}}$** |

$$PLL_{total} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.0 \times 10^{-5} + 2.5 \times 10^{-5} + 1.5 \times 10^{-5} = 3.15 \times 10^{-4} \text{ fatalities/year}$$

**Interpretation:** Expected ~3 fatalities per 10,000 years. Whether this is *tolerable* depends on the F-N curve comparison with regulatory limits.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Connecting "Reasons" to QRA Methodology

| Reason Category | Specific Motivation | QRA Methodology Triggered | KTU Module Mapping |
|-----------------|--------------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| **Historical** | Prevent recurrence of Bhopal/Piper Alpha | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), Event Tree Analysis (ETA) | Module 2 |
| **Regulatory** | OISD-116 / Seveso compliance | Full quantitative study, F-N curve | Modules 2–5 |
| **Economic** | Insurance premium reduction | Cost-benefit analysis, ALARP demonstration | Module 5 |
| **Safety** | Worker/public protection | Individual Risk (IR) calculation, Contour mapping | Module 3 |
| **Engineering** | Plant layout & siting | Consequence analysis (Pool fire, BLEVE, VCE) | Module 2 |
| **Ethical / Social** | Duty of care, CSR | Societal Risk (F-N), Cumulative risk | Module 4 |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**Q: Define Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) and list any three reasons for performing QRA in process industries.**
*Model Answer:*
QRA is a formal, structured, numerical methodology used to estimate the level of risk to personnel, public, and the environment arising from the operation of hazardous process facilities. Risk is quantified as a function of accident frequency and consequence severity ($R = f(P, C)$).
Three reasons for performing QRA are:
1. **Historical Reasons** — past disasters like Bhopal (1984) and Flixborough (1974) have shown the catastrophic potential of unassessed hazards.
2. **Regulatory Reasons** — compliance with OISD-116, Seveso Directive, OSHA PSM.
3. **Safety Reasons** — to demonstrate that individual and societal risks are within tolerable limits (ALARP).

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Mark KTU Pattern:** Definition (1 mark) + 3 reasons (1.5 marks) + Example (0.5 mark).

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**Q: Differentiate between Individual Risk (IR) and Societal Risk (SR). Why is it important to evaluate both in QRA?**
*Model Answer:*

| Parameter | Individual Risk (IR) | Societal Risk (SR) |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Definition | Probability that an average person at a location is fatally harmed per year | Relationship between frequency of accidents and the number of people harmed in each |
| Metric | $IR = f \times p$ (per year) | F-N curve (cumulative frequency vs. fatalities) |
| Focus | Single person | Population/group |
| Tolerable Limit (Worker) | $10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$ | Depends on N (sloped F-N line) |

**Importance of Evaluating Both:**
- IR protects *individuals* regardless of population density.
- SR protects *groups* (high-consequence low-frequency events like Bhopal can cause mass casualties even if IR is low).
- Regulatory frameworks (e.g., UK HSE) require both to be reported for off-site risk.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (Module 1):** Part B is typically a 14-mark question with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Marks are usually split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the historical reasons that led to the development of Quantitative Risk Assessment as a formal engineering discipline. Describe **any three landmark process industry disasters** with their year, location, cause, and the specific QRA lesson learned. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A refinery has three major accident scenarios with the following data:
- Scenario 1: $f_1 = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$, $p_1 = 0.20$
- Scenario 2: $f_2 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$, $p_2 = 0.10$
- Scenario 3: $f_3 = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$, $p_3 = 0.80$

Calculate the **Individual Risk** at the control room location. Comment on whether the risk is **tolerable** for a worker (limit = $10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$) and for public (limit = $10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$). **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Historical Reasons and Landmark Disasters (7 Marks):**

The formal discipline of QRA emerged largely in response to catastrophic industrial accidents during the 1970s–1980s that exposed the inadequacy of purely qualitative, reactive safety practices. Three landmark disasters are:

**1. Flixborough Disaster (1974) — UK:**
- **Location:** Nypro Caprolactam Plant, Flixborough, UK.
- **Cause:** A temporary bypass pipe connecting two cyclohexane reactors failed due to lack of mechanical support, leading to a massive release of hot cyclohexane which formed a flammable vapor cloud.
- **Consequence:** Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE) killed **28 workers**, destroyed the entire plant, and injured 53 people in the surrounding community.
- **QRA Lesson:** Highlighted the importance of **(i) plant layout and spacing between vessels**, **(ii) management of change (MOC) procedures**, and **(iii) domino effect analysis**. QRA now mandates evaluation of vapor cloud explosion (VCE) scenarios for hydrocarbon facilities.

**2. Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984) — India:**
- **Location:** Union Carbide India Limited (UCIL) pesticide plant, Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh.
- **Cause:** Water ingress into a methyl isocyanate (MIC) storage tank caused an exothermic runaway reaction, releasing ~40 tonnes of MIC gas into the densely populated city.
- **Consequence:** **3,800+ immediate deaths**, 15,000+ total estimated fatalities, 500,000+ exposed — the **worst industrial disaster in history**.
- **QRA Lesson:** Established the absolute necessity of **(i) land-use planning (LUP) and off-site risk assessment**, **(ii) toxic gas dispersion modelling**, **(iii) inventory minimization for highly toxic chemicals**, and **(iv) community emergency planning**. The Indian regulatory framework (OISD, MoEFCC EIA) was strengthened post-Bhopal.

**3. Piper Alpha Disaster (1988) — North Sea, UK:**
- **Location:** Piper Alpha oil platform, North Sea.
- **Cause:** A condensate pump's pressure relief valve was removed for maintenance and a blind flange was not reinstated. During start-up, gas leaked, ignited, and caused a series of explosions.
- **Consequence:** **167 fatalities** (all 165 platform crew + 2 rescue workers), the **worst offshore oil & gas disaster**.
- **QRA Lesson:** Reinforced the critical role of **(i) safety instrumented systems (SIS) and emergency shutdown (ESD)**, **(ii) fire & explosion risk modelling**, **(iii) permit-to-work systems**, and **(iv) human factors analysis**. Post-Piper Alpha, the UK HSE mandated formal **Safety Case** submissions — a QRA-based document — for all offshore installations (post-fixed installations), as per the Cullen Report (1990).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only the *year* and *name* of the disaster. Marks are awarded for **all four** parameters: Year, Location, Cause, Consequence, AND the **QRA-specific lesson learned**. A student who omits the lesson loses ~1.5 marks per disaster.

---

**(b) Individual Risk Calculation (7 Marks):**

Given:
- Scenario 1: $f_1 = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$, $p_1 = 0.20$
- Scenario 2: $f_2 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$, $p_2 = 0.10$
- Scenario 3: $f_3 = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$, $p_3 = 0.80$

**Step 1 — Apply the IR Formula:**

$$IR = \sum_{j=1}^{3} f_j \cdot p_j$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**

$$IR = (1 \times 10^{-4})(0.20) + (5 \times 10^{-5})(0.10) + (1 \times 10^{-6})(0.80)$$

**Step 3 — Compute Each Term:**

$$IR = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} + 5.0 \times 10^{-6} + 8.0 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 4 — Sum (Common Order of Magnitude: $10^{-5}$):**

$$IR = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} + 0.5 \times 10^{-5} + 0.08 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$IR = 2.58 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$

**Step 5 — Tolerance Check:**

| Risk Category | Tolerable Limit | Computed IR | Verdict |
|---------------|------------------|-------------|---------|
| Worker | $10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$ | $2.58 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$ | **Acceptable** ($IR < 10^{-4}$) |
| Public | $10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$ | $2.58 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$ | **Not Acceptable** ($IR > 10^{-6}$) |

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**

The Individual Risk at the control room is $IR = 2.58 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$.
- This is **acceptable for a trained worker** (below $10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$).
- It is **NOT acceptable for the public** (above $10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$).
- Therefore, the control room **must not be located in a public-accessible area**, OR additional risk reduction measures (gas detection, ESD, blast walls) must be implemented to reduce $IR$ below $10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$ for any public-facing location.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **convert all terms to the same order of magnitude** before summing, leading to arithmetic errors. Always normalize: $5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.5 \times 10^{-5}$. Also, the **tolerance comparison** is mandatory — without it, the answer is incomplete (loss of ~1 mark).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **regulatory** and **economic** reasons for performing QRA in the process industry. List at least **two Indian regulations** and **two international regulations** that mandate or strongly recommend QRA. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A process plant has the following accident scenario data:

| Scenario | $f_j$ (yr⁻¹) | $N_j$ (Fatalities) |
|----------|--------------|---------------------|
| S1 | $1 \times 10^{-3}$ | 1 |
| S2 | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | 5 |
| S3 | $1 \times 10^{-5}$ | 25 |
| S4 | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ | 100 |

Calculate the **Potential Loss of Life (PLL)** per year. Plot the **F-N curve points** and comment on the societal risk profile. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Regulatory and Economic Reasons for QRA (7 Marks):**

**Regulatory Reasons:**

QRA is mandated or strongly recommended by numerous national and international regulations because it provides a *defensible, auditable, and quantified* basis for safety decisions.

**Indian Regulations:**
1. **OISD-116** (Oil Industry Safety Directorate, Standard 116): "Guidelines on Quantitative Risk Assessment" — mandatory for all petroleum refineries, petrochemical plants, and oil & gas installations in India. Requires IR contours, F-N curves, and ALARP demonstration.
2. **OISD-117** (Hazardous Classification and Layout Planning) — uses QRA outputs to determine safe distances between process units, control rooms, and boundary walls.
3. **MoEFCC EIA Notification 2006** (as amended) — mandates risk assessment as part of Environmental Impact Assessment for Category A and B projects involving hazardous chemicals.
4. **PESO (Petroleum & Explosives Safety Organisation)** — regulates storage, handling, and transportation of petroleum, explosives, and compressed gases.
5. **Factories Act 1948, Section 7A and Section 14** — empowers state governments to enforce safety standards, including risk assessment.

**International Regulations:**
1. **Seveso III Directive (EU Directive 2012/18/EU)** — requires major hazard installations to maintain a Safety Report based on QRA principles.
2. **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM)** — USA, mandates Process Safety Management including hazard analysis, consequence analysis, and risk evaluation for facilities handling threshold quantities of hazardous chemicals.
3. **EPA Risk Management Plan (RMP) Rule (40 CFR Part 68)** — USA, requires worst-case and alternative release scenario analysis.
4. **COMAH Regulations 2015 (UK)** — Control of Major Accident Hazards, mandates Safety Reports with quantified risk levels.
5. **IEC 61511** — International standard for functional safety of safety instrumented systems (SIS), with risk-based SIL determination.

**Economic Reasons:**

1. **Direct Loss Avoidance:** Major accidents lead to enormous direct costs — plant destruction, business interruption, legal liabilities, environmental remediation, and medical expenses. QRA identifies high-risk scenarios *before* they occur, enabling pre-emptive mitigation at a fraction of the post-accident cost. Industry studies suggest every **$1** saved on safety can result in **$10 to $100** of long-term losses.

2. **Insurance Premium Reduction:** Insurance companies (e.g., Lloyds of London) require QRA reports for underwriting high-hazard facilities. A lower quantified risk leads to significantly reduced premiums.

3. **Business Continuity:** A single major accident can shut down a plant for months or years. QRA-driven design and operational discipline minimizes this risk, ensuring continuous revenue generation.

4. **Optimization of Capital Expenditure:** QRA allows engineering teams to **prioritize** safety investments based on cost-benefit (GCAF) — investing in safeguards with the highest risk-reduction-per-rupee rather than uniformly adding equipment.

5. **Regulatory Fines & Litigation:** Non-compliance with safety regulations (e.g., OISD, Seveso) attracts heavy fines and civil/criminal litigation. QRA demonstrates *due diligence* and can be a legal defence.

---

**(b) PLL and F-N Curve Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate PLL per Scenario:**

$$PLL_j = f_j \times N_j$$

| Scenario | $f_j$ (yr⁻¹) | $N_j$ | $PLL_j$ (fatalities/yr) |
|----------|--------------|-------|-------------------------|
| S1 | $1 \times 10^{-3}$ | 1 | $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| S2 | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | 5 | $5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| S3 | $1 \times 10^{-5}$ | 25 | $2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| S4 | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ | 100 | $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ |

**Step 2 — Calculate Total PLL:**

$$PLL_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{4} f_j \cdot N_j = (1.0 + 0.5 + 0.25 + 0.1) \times 10^{-3}$$

$$PLL_{total} = 1.85 \times 10^{-3} \text{ fatalities/year}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cumulative Frequency for F-N Curve:**

For F-N curve, we sort scenarios by decreasing $N$ and sum frequencies of all scenarios with $N_k \geq N_j$:

| Scenario | $N_j$ | $f_j$ (yr⁻¹) | $F(N \geq N_j)$ (yr⁻¹) |
|----------|-------|--------------|--------------------------|
| S4 | 100 | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| S3 | 25 | $1 \times 10^{-5}$ | $1.1 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| S2 | 5 | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | $1.11 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| S1 | 1 | $1 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1.111 \times 10^{-3}$ |

**Step 4 — Plot F-N Curve (log-log):**

The F-N curve points (on a log-log plot) form a downward-sloping line. Typical regulatory F-N boundaries (UK HSE) are:

$$F \leq \dfrac{C}{N^k}$$

with $k = 1$ (or 2 for stricter standards) and $C$ = constant (e.g., $C = 10^{-3}$ for upper bound, $C = 10^{-5}$ for lower bound).

**Step 5 — Comment on Societal Risk:**

The computed F-N curve has slope approximately:
$$\text{slope} = \dfrac{\log(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) - \log(1.0 \times 10^{-6})}{\log(1) - \log(100)} = \dfrac{3}{2} = -1.5$$

This indicates a **steeper-than-typical slope**, which means the risk profile is dominated by *low-N high-frequency* events. The plant is **NOT** in the "intolerable" region (top-left of F-N plane) — it lies in the **ALARP** region (middle zone) where risk reduction must be implemented only if *reasonably practicable*.

**Conclusion:** The plant's societal risk is dominated by small-but-frequent accidents (S1) rather than catastrophic-low-frequency events (S4). Safety investment should prioritize **reducing the frequency of single-fatality events** (e.g., slip/trip/fall controls, routine maintenance procedures) and **maintaining safeguards** against the 100-fatality BLEVE-like scenario to keep the F-N point in the ALARP region.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **rank** scenarios by $N$ *before* computing cumulative $F$. This leads to an incorrect F-N curve. Also, the **slope interpretation** is mandatory — examiners specifically check whether the student understands *steeper slope = lower societal risk* (less catastrophic-consequence bias).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 — Reasons: Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Six categories of reasons:** Historical, Regulatory, Economic, Safety, Engineering, Ethical/Social.
- ✅ **Landmark disasters to memorize:** Flixborough (1974, VCE, 28 dead, layout lesson), Bhopal (1984, MIC, 3800+ dead, LUP lesson), Seveso (1976, dioxin, Seveso Directive), Piper Alpha (1988, offshore, 167 dead, ESD/Safety Case lesson), Texas City (2005, BP, 15 dead, human factors).
- ✅ **Indian regulations:** OISD-116, OISD-117, MoEFCC EIA 2006, PESO, Factories Act 1948.
- ✅ **International regulations:** Seveso III Directive, OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119, EPA RMP, COMAH, IEC 61511.
- ✅ **Basic Risk Equation:** $R = P \times C$ (or $R = f \times C$ in QRA notation).
- ✅ **Individual Risk (IR):** $IR = \sum f_j \cdot p_{ij}$ per year.
- ✅ **Potential Loss of Life (PLL):** $PLL = \sum f_j \cdot N_j$ fatalities/year.
- ✅ **F-N Curve:** Cumulative frequency vs. number of fatalities; must lie in ALARP region.
- ✅ **Tolerable IR (Worker):** $10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$; **(Public):** $10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$.
- ✅ **ALARP Principle:** Risk must be As Low As Reasonably Practicable — economic reasonableness evaluated via GCAF.
- ✅ **QRA is justified** because: (i) past disasters proved reactive safety is insufficient, (ii) regulations demand it, (iii) economics favour prevention, (iv) ethics mandate duty of care.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When asked *"Reasons for QRA"* in a 3-mark or 7-mark question, **always start with a historical disaster** — it sets the context and signals to the examiner that you understand the *evolution* of the discipline. Then proceed to regulatory/economic/safety reasons in that order.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for Hazard Identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Hazard Identification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hazard Identification (HAZID)** is the systematic, structured, and documented process of recognizing, listing, and characterizing all potential hazards present in a system, process, facility, or operation that have the inherent potential to cause harm to people, property, environment, or business continuity. It is the **first and most critical step** in the overall Risk Assessment (RA) and Risk Management (RM) framework, because un-identified hazards cannot be analyzed, evaluated, or controlled.

In the language of **IEC 31010:2019 (Risk management — Risk assessment techniques)**, hazard identification is the process of finding, recognizing, and describing risks. It answers three fundamental questions:

1. **What can go wrong?** (Hazard source)
2. **How can it happen?** (Initiating event / Failure mode)
3. **What/Who can be affected?** (Target / Receptor)

KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes that hazard identification must be **proactive, iterative, and multidisciplinary** — not a one-time paperwork exercise.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Medical Diagnostic Check-up:**
> Imagine you go to a doctor for a full-body health check-up. Before the doctor prescribes medicine, performs surgery, or suggests lifestyle changes, the very first thing they do is **identify what is wrong** — they run blood tests, an ECG, an MRI, and ask you questions. The list of all possible ailments (high BP, diabetes, cardiac risk, vitamin deficiency) is the **hazard identification** step. Only after this list is ready can the doctor **assess** the severity of each problem, **prioritize** treatment, and **control** the high-risk ones. 
> 
> In a chemical plant, the engineer plays the role of the doctor. **Hazard Identification is the diagnostic phase.** Without it, the plant operates "blind" — accidents appear to come "out of nowhere," but in reality, the hazards were always there, simply un-identified. 

## 1.3 Why Hazard Identification is Indispensable in Process Safety

- **Bhopal (1984, India)** — MIC release killed thousands. The hazard (MIC + water ingress) was not systematically identified at design stage.
- **Flixborough (1974, UK)** — Cyclohexane release, 28 deaths. Hazard of running a temporary bypass pipe was not analyzed.
- **Texas City (2005, USA)** — Refinery explosion, 15 deaths. Hazard of overfilling a distillation column was not explicitly listed in operating procedures.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pearl:** Examiners often award full marks when a student explicitly links a technique to a real industrial accident in the introduction. Mentioning Bhopal, Flixborough, or BP Texas City shows **application-level thinking (Bloom's Level 3)**.

## 1.4 Classification of Hazard Identification Techniques

For KTU Module 2, the techniques are broadly classified as:

| Category | Examples | Typical Use Stage |
|---|---|---|
| **Comparative / Experience-Based** | Checklist Analysis, Safety Audit, Review of Past Incidents | Operating plants |
| **Fundamental / Inductive (Bottom-Up)** | HAZOP, FMEA, What-If Analysis | Design & Detailed Engineering |
| **Fundamental / Deductive (Top-Down)** | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), Event Tree Analysis (ETA) | Quantitative Risk Assessment |
| **Hybrid / Specialized** | HAZAN, Dow Fire & Explosion Index, F-N Curves, QRA | Major Hazard Installations |
| **Creative / Group-Based** | Brainstorming, Delphi Technique, SWIFT | Early conceptual / novel designs |

Standard metrics used throughout this module: **L (Likelihood, in events/year)**, **C (Consequence, in fatalities or ₹ loss)**, **R (Risk = L × C)**, **SIL (Safety Integrity Level, 1 to 4)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HAZOP — Hazard and Operability Study

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** is a **structured, team-based, inductive brainstorming technique** developed by ICI (UK) in the 1960s. It is the **gold standard** for process industries and the most frequently asked topic in KTU examinations.

### 2.1.1 Core Philosophy of HAZOP

HAZOP assumes that **the design intent is correct, but deviations from intent are dangerous**. The team systematically applies a fixed set of **Guide Words** to every node of a process line to discover deviations.

### 2.1.2 The Seven (or Eight) Standard HAZOP Guide Words

| Guide Word | Meaning | Process Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| **NO / NOT** | Complete negation of the design intent | Flow, Reaction |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase | Pressure, Temperature, Flow |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | Level, Concentration |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite | Flow direction |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional material/activity present | Contaminant, Phase |
| **PART OF** | Only a part of the design intent achieved | Composition, Specification |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution | Material, Phase |
| **SOONER / LATER** | Timing deviation (sometimes) | Sequence, Step |

### 2.1.3 HAZOP Procedure (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Define Scope and Objectives** — clearly state the boundary of the study (e.g., P&ID of reactor R-101 section).
2. **Form the HAZOP Team** — multidisciplinary (process, mechanical, instrumentation, safety, operations). Minimum 4–6 members; **one facilitator**.
3. **Obtain Design Documentation** — P&IDs, PFDs, operating procedures, material safety data.
4. **Divide the Plant into Nodes (Study Sections)** — each node = one process intent (e.g., "transfer of toluene from TK-01 to R-101").
5. **Apply Guide Words to Each Node** and document deviations.
6. **Identify Causes** for each deviation (instrument failure, human error, external event).
7. **Identify Consequences** (fire, explosion, toxic release, environmental damage).
8. **Recommend Safeguards / Actions** (additional alarm, interlock, relief valve).
9. **Record, Review, Follow-up, and Close-Out.**

## 2.2 FMEA — Failure Mode and Effects Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **FMEA** is a **bottom-up inductive method** that examines every component of a system, identifies its failure modes, and traces the effect of that failure on the system and the mission.

### 2.2.1 FMEA Procedure

1. List every component, sub-system, and function.
2. For each component, list all possible **failure modes** (e.g., valve — fails open, fails closed, leaks externally, plugs).
3. Determine the **effect** of each failure on the system.
4. Identify the **cause** and existing **controls**.
5. Compute **RPN (Risk Priority Number)**.

### 2.2.2 The RPN Formula (Very High Yield for KTU)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN} &= S \times O \times D
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $S$ = **Severity** rating (1 to 10)
- $O$ = **Occurrence** rating (1 to 10)
- $D$ = **Detection** rating (1 to 10, higher = harder to detect)

**Decision rule:** Items with $\text{RPN} \geq 100$ (or top 20% in ranking) require corrective action.

## 2.3 FTA — Fault Tree Analysis (Top-Down Deductive)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FTA**, formalized by **H.A. Watson of Bell Labs in 1962** for the Minuteman missile launch system, uses **Boolean logic (AND / OR gates)** to model the pathways by which a top undesired event (the **TOP event**) can occur.

### 2.3.1 Boolean Algebra of FTA

For an **OR gate** (any input causes the output):
$$
P(\text{Top Event}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - P(E_i)\right)
$$

For an **AND gate** (all inputs must occur):
$$
P(\text{Top Event}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(E_i)
$$

For small probabilities $P(E_i) \ll 1$, the OR-gate expression simplifies to:
$$
P(\text{Top Event}) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(E_i)
$$

### 2.3.2 Minimal Cut Set (MCS)

A **Minimal Cut Set** is the smallest combination of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the top event. It is the **quantitative heart** of FTA.

## 2.4 ETA — Event Tree Analysis (Bottom-Up Deductive-Inductive Hybrid)
