ETA starts from an **initiating event** and propagates forward through the success/failure of safety functions.

If $p_i$ is the probability of failure of safety function $i$, and $q_i = 1 - p_i$ is the probability of success, the probability of an outcome branch is the product of branch probabilities.

## 2.5 What-If Analysis

A free-form, brainstorming-style review. Team asks open-ended "What if …?" questions (e.g., "What if cooling water fails?", "What if power is lost during start-up?"). Faster than HAZOP, less rigorous — used in early design stages.

## 2.6 Checklist Analysis

A pre-prepared list of known hazards, regulatory items, and good-practice points (e.g., "Is the area classified for flammable atmosphere?", "Are relief valves sized for fire case?"). The plant is checked against the list.

## 2.7 Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)

Performed at the **earliest conceptual design stage**, before P&IDs exist. Uses broad hazard categories (mechanical, thermal, chemical, electrical, radiation) and assigns qualitative severity/likelihood ranks.

## 2.8 Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)

A **specialized proprietary technique** by the American Institute of Chemical Engineers (AIChE) for quantifying fire and explosion damage potential in chemical processes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dow F\&EI} &= \text{Material Factor (MF)} \times \text{General Process Hazards (GPH)} \times \text{Special Process Hazards (SPH)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.9 Delphi Technique

A **structured anonymous expert elicitation method** used when empirical data is missing (e.g., emerging technologies). Multiple rounds of anonymous questionnaires are consolidated, fed back, and refined until consensus emerges.

## 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Technique | Type | Core Output Metric | Key Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| HAZOP | Inductive / Team | Node-by-node deviation register | Qualitative matrix | Detailed design (P&ID stage) |
| FMEA | Inductive | RPN | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Component-level design |
| FTA | Deductive | $P(\text{Top Event})$, MCS | $P_{\text{OR}} \approx \sum P_i$, $P_{\text{AND}} = \prod P_i$ | Complex systems, software safety |
| ETA | Inductive | Branch probabilities | $P_{\text{branch}} = \prod p_i$ | Post-initiating-event consequence |
| What-If | Inductive | Question / answer log | None | Early / conceptual design |
| Checklist | Comparative | Yes / No / NA | None | Routine / operating plants |
| PHA | Inductive | Hazard ranking | Qualitative | Conceptual stage |
| Dow F&EI | Specialized | F&EI number | $\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \cdot \text{GPH} \cdot \text{SPH}$ | Flammable / explosive inventory |
| Delphi | Creative | Consensus expert rating | Median, IQR | Novel / rare hazards |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** In a KTU exam question on selecting a technique, the **rule of thumb** is: use **HAZOP** for chemical processes, **FTA** for software/aviation/nuclear, **FMEA** for mechanical/automotive, **ETA** for consequence modeling, **What-If** for early screening, and **Delphi** when data is absent.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed HAZOP Worked Example (Exam-Oriented)

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A **toluene storage tank TK-01** is fed by a centrifugal pump P-01 from a tanker via line L-01. The line has a manual isolation valve MV-01, a check valve CV-01, a flow transmitter FT-01, and a flow control valve FCV-01. Design intent: **Transfer toluene from the road tanker to TK-01 at a flow rate of 30 m³/h at 4 bar(g).**

Apply HAZOP on the section from the **tanker discharge to the tank inlet** and document findings for at least three guide words.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Define the Node**
**Node 1:** "Transfer of toluene from road tanker to TK-01 through P-01 and FCV-01."

**Step 2 — Apply the Guide Words**

#### Deviation 1: **NO FLOW**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deviation} &= \text{NO} + \text{Flow} \\
\text{Cause} &= \begin{cases} 1. \text{Pump P-01 fails (mechanical seal, motor trip)} \\ 2. \text{Tanker empty / valve closed at source} \\ 3. \text{Line blockage} \end{cases} \\
\text{Consequence} &= \text{No toluene to TK-01 → production loss. Pump dead-heading → overheating → seal failure → fire} \\
\text{Safeguards} &= \text{MV-01, FT-01 low-low trip, pump discharge pressure switch} \\
\text{Action} &= \text{Add a low-low flow interlock + auto-start of standby pump}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Deviation 2: **MORE FLOW**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deviation} &= \text{MORE} + \text{Flow} \\
\text{Cause} &= \text{FCV-01 fails open, or operator sets wrong setpoint} \\
\text{Consequence} &= \text{High inflow → TK-01 overfill → spill of flammable toluene → vapour cloud → fire/explosion} \\
\text{Safeguards} &= \text{TK-01 level switch LSH-01, independent high-level alarm LAH-01} \\
\text{Action} &= \text{Install a separate hardwired independent high-high level switch (SIL 2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Deviation 3: **LESS FLOW**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deviation} &= \text{LESS} + \text{Flow} \\
\text{Cause} &= \text{Partial blockage in strainer, partial pump cavitation, FCV-01 partially closed} \\
\text{Consequence} &= \text{Prolonged transfer → operator may leave line unattended → inventory tracking errors} \\
\text{Safeguards} &= \text{FT-01 trend alarm} \\
\text{Action} &= \text{Add pump NPSH check, strainer DP alarm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> In a 14-mark KTU question, examiners expect **at least four guide words** with full cause–consequence–safeguard–action columns. Always use the **node table format** in your answer.

## 3.2 Detailed FMEA Worked Example

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

Carry out FMEA on the **centrifugal pump P-01** used to transfer toluene. The Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D) ratings for the three critical failure modes are:

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Pump does not start on demand | 8 | 4 | 5 |
| 2. Pump seal leaks | 7 | 6 | 4 |
| 3. Pump dead-heads (flow blocked, pump running) | 9 | 3 | 6 |

Identify which failure mode is the most critical based on RPN.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step RPN Calculation

**Step 1 — Apply the RPN formula for each failure mode:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{1} &= S_1 \times O_1 \times D_1 = 8 \times 4 \times 5 = 160 \\
\text{RPN}_{2} &= S_2 \times O_2 \times D_2 = 7 \times 6 \times 4 = 168 \\
\text{RPN}_{3} &= S_3 \times O_3 \times D_3 = 9 \times 3 \times 6 = 162
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rank the failure modes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rank: } & 168 \;(\text{Pump seal leaks}) > 162 \;(\text{Dead-heads}) > 160 \;(\text{No start})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Recommended Action (highest RPN):**
Replace single mechanical seal with **tandem seal with buffer fluid** and add **seal-pot DP low alarm** to improve detection rating from $D=4$ to $D=2$.

**Step 4 — Re-compute improved RPN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{2,\text{new}} &= 7 \times 6 \times 2 = 84 \\
\text{Net reduction} &= 168 - 84 = 50\% \text{ improvement}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-mark question pattern:** Examiners expect you to (a) show the RPN calculation table, (b) rank, (c) recommend **at least two corrective actions**, and (d) show the **revised RPN** with discussion.

## 3.3 Detailed FTA Worked Example

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

A reactor overpressure accident occurs when either (i) the pressure safety valve (PSV) fails to open, **OR** (ii) the high-pressure interlock (HPI) fails to actuate, **OR** (iii) both the rupture disc and PSV fail. Construct the fault tree, derive the Boolean expression, and compute the top-event probability given:

$$
P(\text{PSV fail}) = 0.01, \quad P(\text{HPI fail}) = 0.02, \quad P(\text{RD fail}) = 0.005
$$

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the Top Event:**
$T$ = "Reactor overpressure leading to rupture"

**Step 2 — Identify Intermediate Events:**
- $A$ = "Pressure relief system fails" (PSV fail OR Rupture Disc fail)
- $B$ = "Instrumented protective system fails" (HPI fail)

**Step 3 — Construct the Fault Tree (Boolean Form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= A \text{ OR } B \\
A &= \text{PSV}_{\text{fail}} \text{ OR } \text{RD}_{\text{fail}} \\
B &= \text{HPI}_{\text{fail}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Flatten into a single Boolean expression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= (\text{PSV}_{\text{fail}} + \text{RD}_{\text{fail}} + \text{HPI}_{\text{fail}})
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a single OR gate with three basic events.

**Step 5 — Apply the OR-gate formula (rare-event approximation, $P \ll 1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &\approx P(\text{PSV}_{\text{fail}}) + P(\text{RD}_{\text{fail}}) + P(\text{HPI}_{\text{fail}}) \\
     &= 0.01 + 0.005 + 0.02 \\
     &= 0.035 \;\text{per reactor-year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Identify the Minimal Cut Sets:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MCS}_1 &= \{\text{PSV}_{\text{fail}}\} \\
\text{MCS}_2 &= \{\text{RD}_{\text{fail}}\} \\
\text{MCS}_3 &= \{\text{HPI}_{\text{fail}}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

All three cut sets are of **order 1 (single-point failure)** — this is a serious design weakness. The recommended improvement is to add **redundancy** (a second PSV in parallel), which converts the cut set to order 2.

**Step 7 — Improved system (two PSVs in parallel, same failure probability each):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{both PSVs fail}) &= P(\text{PSV}_1\text{ fail}) \times P(\text{PSV}_2\text{ fail}) = 0.01 \times 0.01 = 0.0001 \\
P(T)_{\text{new}} &\approx 0.0001 + 0.005 + 0.02 = 0.0251 \;\text{per reactor-year} \\
\text{Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)} &= \frac{0.035}{0.0251} \approx 1.39
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Detailed ETA Worked Example

### 3.4.1 Problem Statement

A **loss-of-cooling-water** event occurs in a toluene storage facility. The event tree considers:
- **B1:** Bund wall holds the spill (success probability $q_1 = 0.95$).
- **B2:** Ignition source present (success = no ignition, probability $q_2 = 0.90$).
- **B3:** Foam fire-fighting system works (success probability $q_3 = 0.85$).

Compute the probabilities of all end-states.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Lay out the Event Tree:**

| Branch | B1 (Bund) | B2 (Ignition) | B3 (Foam) | End-State | Probability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Success | Success | Success | No spill | $0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.85 = 0.7268$ |
| 2 | Success | Success | Failure | Spill but extinguished | $0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.15 = 0.1283$ |
| 3 | Success | Failure | — | Pool fire (small) | $0.95 \times 0.10 = 0.0950$ |
| 4 | Failure | Success | — | Large unignited spill | $0.05 \times 0.90 = 0.0450$ |
| 5 | Failure | Failure | — | Major pool fire | $0.05 \times 0.10 = 0.0050$ |

**Step 2 — Verification (total probability must = 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum P_i &= 0.7268 + 0.1283 + 0.0950 + 0.0450 + 0.0050 \\
&= 1.0001 \;\text{(rounding error } \approx 0.0001) \approx 1.00 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate Consequence Categories:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{No or minor impact}) &= 0.7268 + 0.1283 = 0.8551 \\
P(\text{Significant event, fire or large spill}) &= 0.0950 + 0.0450 + 0.0050 = 0.1450
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The expected value of consequence (EV) for risk ranking is computed as:
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{EV} &= \sum_i P_i \times C_i
> \end{aligned}
> $$
> 
> where $C_i$ is the cost (₹ or fatalities) of end-state $i$.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique

| Criterion | HAZOP | FMEA | FTA | ETA | What-If | Checklist |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direction of analysis** | Inductive (bottom-up) | Inductive | Deductive (top-down) | Inductive (forward) | Inductive | Comparative |
| **Required team size** | 4–8 | 2–4 | 1–3 | 1–3 | 3–6 | 1–2 |
| **Time required** | High (weeks) | Medium | High (days) | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Cost** | High | Medium | Medium | Medium | Low | Very Low |
| **Output** | Deviation register | RPN list | Boolean tree, MCS | Branch probabilities | Question log | Pass/Fail list |
| **Quantitative?** | No (qualitative) | Semi (RPN) | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| **Best stage of project** | Detailed design | Equipment design | Any (post-design) | Post-initiating event | Conceptual | Operating plant |
| **Limitations** | Needs P&IDs, team | Component-centric only | Complex for large systems | Needs initiating event | Inconsistent | Misses novel hazards |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Safeguards

All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed (e.g., `nodeA`, `hazopStep1`). No reserved keywords used as standalone node names. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric only.

## 4.2 HAZOP Study Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START HAZOP STUDY]) --> step1A[DEFINE SCOPE AND OBJECTIVES]
    step1A --> step2A[FORM MULTIDISCIPLINARY TEAM 4 TO 8 MEMBERS]
    step2A --> step3A[GATHER P AND IDS PFDs OPERATING PROCEDURES]
    step3A --> step4A[DIVIDE PLANT INTO NODES]
    step4A --> step5A[APPLY GUIDE WORDS PER NODE]
    step5A --> step6A[IDENTIFY DEVIATIONS]
    step6A --> step7A[RECORD CAUSES]
    step7A --> step8A[RECORD CONSEQUENCES]
    step8A --> step9A[LIST EXISTING SAFEGUARDS]
    step9A --> step10A[RECOMMEND NEW ACTIONS]
    step10A --> step11A{ACTION CLOSED OUT}
    step11A -- NO --> step12A[ASSIGN RESPONSIBILITY AND DUE DATE]
    step12A --> step11A
    step11A -- YES --> step13A[UPDATE HAZOP REGISTER]
    step13A --> step14A[ISSUE FINAL HAZOP REPORT]
    step14A --> endA([END OF STUDY])
```

## 4.3 FMEA Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([START FMEA]) --> step1B[LIST ALL COMPONENTS]
    step1B --> step2B[FOR EACH COMPONENT LIST FUNCTIONS]
    step2B --> step3B[IDENTIFY FAILURE MODES]
    step3B --> step4B[ASSIGN SEVERITY S 1 TO 10]
    step4B --> step5B[ASSIGN OCCURRENCE O 1 TO 10]
    step5B --> step6B[ASSIGN DETECTION D 1 TO 10]
    step6B --> step7B[CALCULATE RPN S TIMES O TIMES D]
    step7B --> step8B{RPN ABOVE THRESHOLD 100}
    step8B -- YES --> step9B[RECOMMEND CORRECTIVE ACTION]
    step9B --> step10B[RECALCULATE REVISED RPN]
    step10B --> step8B
    step8B -- NO --> step11B[CLOSE OUT FMEA ITEM]
    step11B --> step12B[ISSUE FMEA REPORT]
    step12B --> endB([END OF FMEA])
```

## 4.4 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    topEventA([TOP EVENT T REACTOR OVERPRESSURE]) --> gateA{OR GATE}
    gateA --> branchA[INTERMEDIATE EVENT A RELIEF SYSTEM FAILS]
    gateA --> branchB[INTERMEDIATE EVENT B INSTRUMENT PROTECTION FAILS]
    branchA --> gateB{OR GATE}
    gateB --> leafA1[BASIC EVENT PSV FAILS TO OPEN]
    gateB --> leafA2[BASIC EVENT RUPTURE DISC FAILS]
    branchB --> gateC{OR GATE SINGLE EVENT}
    gateC --> leafB1[BASIC EVENT HIGH PRESSURE INTERLOCK FAILS]
    leafA1 --> probA[P equals 0 point 01]
    leafA2 --> probB[P equals 0 point 005]
    leafB1 --> probC[P equals 0 point 02]
```

## 4.5 Event Tree Analysis (ETA) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initEvt([INITIATING EVENT LOSS OF COOLING WATER]) --> b1Node{B1 BUND WALL}
    b1Node -- SUCCESS 0 point 95 --> b2aNode{B2 IGNITION}
    b1Node -- FAILURE 0 point 05 --> b2bNode{B2 IGNITION}
    b2aNode -- SUCCESS 0 point 90 --> b3Node{B3 FOAM SYSTEM}
    b2aNode -- FAILURE 0 point 10 --> endA1([POOL FIRE SMALL])
    b3Node -- SUCCESS 0 point 85 --> endA2([NO SPILL])
    b3Node -- FAILURE 0 point 15 --> endA3([SPILL EXTINGUISHED])
    b2bNode -- SUCCESS 0 point 90 --> endB1([LARGE UNIGNITED SPILL])
    b2bNode -- FAILURE 0 point 10 --> endB2([MAJOR POOL FIRE])
```

## 4.6 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Hazard Identification Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startC([HAZARD IDENTIFICATION REQUIRED]) --> q1{WHAT IS PROJECT STAGE}
    q1 -- CONCEPTUAL --> q2{HAS HAZARD DATA BEEN COLLECTED}
    q2 -- NO --> techA[DELPHI TECHNIQUE]
    q2 -- YES --> techB[PHA OR WHAT IF]
    q1 -- DETAILED DESIGN --> q3{IS PROCESS OR EQUIPMENT FOCUS}
    q3 -- PROCESS FLOW --> techC[HAZOP]
    q3 -- EQUIPMENT --> techD[FMEA]
    q1 -- QUANTITATIVE RISK --> q4{WHAT IS THE QUESTION}
    q4 -- HOW CAN TOP EVENT OCCUR --> techE[FTA]
    q4 -- WHAT HAPPENS AFTER EVENT --> techF[ETA]
    q1 -- OPERATING PLANT --> techG[CHECKLIST OR SAFETY AUDIT]
    techA --> endC([TECHNIQUE SELECTED])
    techB --> endC
    techC --> endC
    techD --> endC
    techE --> endC
    techF --> endC
    techG --> endC
```

> [!TIP]
> In KTU viva or written exams, drawing a **single block diagram** earns easier marks than writing three paragraphs. The above Mermaid diagrams are safe and examiner-friendly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define HAZOP. List any four standard HAZOP guide words with their meaning.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** is a structured, team-based, inductive hazard identification technique developed by ICI in the 1960s. It systematically applies a fixed set of **guide words** to process nodes of a plant to discover deviations from the design intent and their causes, consequences, and safeguards.

**Four standard guide words:**

1. **NO / NOT** — Complete negation of design intent. (Example: NO FLOW.)
2. **MORE** — Quantitative increase of a process parameter. (Example: MORE PRESSURE.)
3. **LESS** — Quantitative decrease. (Example: LESS TEMPERATURE.)
4. **REVERSE** — Logical opposite, especially of flow direction. (Example: REVERSE FLOW — backflow of product.)

Other acceptable guide words: **AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN.**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between inductive and deductive hazard identification techniques. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Inductive (Bottom-Up):** Starts from an initiating event / failure mode and works upward to identify the consequence. Best for detailed design and component-level study. **Example: HAZOP, FMEA, What-If.**
> 
> **Deductive (Top-Down):** Starts from a defined top event (an undesired outcome) and works backward to identify the combinations of basic failures that could cause it. **Example: Fault Tree Analysis (FTA).**
> 
> **Key difference:** Inductive = "What can go wrong starting from causes?" Deductive = "How can the bad event happen starting from the consequence?"

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)** technique. List the standard guide words and explain the procedure step-by-step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply HAZOP on the following process node and document the findings for **at least four guide words**:

> A **heat exchanger E-01** cools hot process oil from 150 °C to 60 °C using cooling water. Cooling water flow is controlled by a temperature transmitter (TT-01) and a control valve (TCV-01). The hot oil inlet has a pressure safety valve (PSV-01) set at 8 bar(g). 

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — HAZOP Technique**

HAZOP is a structured, multidisciplinary team-based technique (Refer to Section 2.1 for the seven guide words table). Key points to write:

1. **Origin** — ICI (UK), 1960s; standardized in **IEC 61882:2016**.
2. **Objective** — Identify deviations from design intent; recommend safeguards.
3. **Team composition** — Facilitator (HAZOP leader), process engineer, instrumentation, operations, safety, maintenance.
4. **Documentation** — P&IDs, PFDs, operating procedures, MSDS, cause-and-effect matrix.
5. **Standard Guide Words** — NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN (and SOONER/LATER for batch).
6. **Procedure** — Define scope → form team → divide into nodes → apply guide words → document cause, consequence, safeguards → recommend actions → follow-up and close out.

**(b) [7 Marks] — HAZOP Application on E-01**

**Node:** Cooling of hot oil from 150 °C to 60 °C using cooling water in E-01.

#### Deviation 1: **NO COOLING WATER FLOW**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cause} &= \text{Cooling water pump trip, TCV stuck closed, supply failure} \\
\text{Consequence} &= \text{Hot oil outlet temperature rises; downstream unit upset; possible thermal degradation} \\
\text{Safeguards} &= \text{TT-01 high alarm, TCV fail-safe open} \\
\text{Action} &= \text{Add redundant cooling water pump and TT-01 high-high interlock}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Deviation 2: **MORE COOLING WATER FLOW**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cause} &= \text{TCV-01 stuck open, operator error} \\
\text{Consequence} &= \text{Overcooling → oil viscosity rises → pumping problems downstream} \\
\text{Safeguards} &= \text{TT-01 low alarm} \\
\text{Action} &= \text{Add low-temperature alarm at outlet}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Deviation 3: **MORE PRESSURE** (on hot oil side)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cause} &= \text{Blockage downstream, PSV-01 fails shut} \\
\text{Consequence} &= \text{Exchanger shell overpressure → rupture → hot oil fire} \\
\text{Safeguards} &= \text{PSV-01 set at 8 bar} \\
\text{Action} &= \text{Add a second PSV in parallel for redundancy (SIL 2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Deviation 4: **AS WELL AS (Contaminant in CW)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cause} &= \text{Sea-water ingress, particulate matter in CW} \\
\text{Consequence} &= \text{Fouling, corrosion of tubes, leakage of oil into CW} \\
\text{Safeguards} &= \text{Inlet strainer, periodic water analysis} \\
\text{Action} &= \text{Install online corrosion coupon and DP alarm across strainer}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Examiner award: 1 mark for stating the design intent, 1 mark for the table format, 1 mark per deviation (max 4 deviations), 1 mark for safeguards, 1 mark for actions, 1 mark for overall conclusion. **Total: 14 marks** when (a) and (b) are combined correctly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** methodology. Define **Top Event, Basic Event, AND gate, OR gate, and Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** with an example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A reactor protection system is designed so that a **high-pressure trip** occurs if **either (i) both pressure transmitters PT-01A and PT-01B fail to detect high pressure OR (ii) the shutdown valve SDV-01 fails to close**. The failure probabilities are:

$$
P(\text{PT-01A fail}) = 0.02, \quad P(\text{PT-01B fail}) = 0.03, \quad P(\text{SDV-01 fail}) = 0.01
$$

Construct the **fault tree**, derive the **Boolean expression**, and compute the **top-event probability**.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — FTA Methodology**

1. **Definition** — FTA is a top-down deductive failure analysis technique developed by **H.A. Watson (Bell Labs, 1962)** for the Minuteman missile program.
2. **Top Event** — The undesired event to be analyzed (e.g., "Reactor explodes").
3. **Basic Event** — A fundamental component failure that does not require further development (e.g., "Pump fails to start").
4. **Gates**:
   - **AND gate** — Output occurs only if **all** input events occur.
   - **OR gate** — Output occurs if **any one or more** input events occur.
5. **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** — Smallest combination of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the top event.
6. **Procedure** — Define top event → construct tree → identify basic events → derive Boolean expression → solve for probability → identify MCS → rank by order.

**(b) [7 Marks] — Reactor Protection Fault Tree**

**Step 1 — Define Top Event:**
$T$ = "High-pressure trip fails to actuate when required."

**Step 2 — Identify Intermediate Events:**
$A$ = "Pressure detection system fails" (PT-01A fail **AND** PT-01B fail)
$B$ = "Shutdown action fails" (SDV-01 fail)

**Step 3 — Boolean Expression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= A \;\text{OR}\; B \\
T &= (X_1 \cdot X_2) + X_3
\end{aligned}
$$

where $X_1$ = PT-01A fail, $X_2$ = PT-01B fail, $X_3$ = SDV-01 fail.

**Step 4 — Compute the AND-gate sub-probability (A):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A) &= P(X_1) \times P(X_2) = 0.02 \times 0.03 = 0.0006
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the OR-gate top probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= P(A) + P(X_3) - P(A) \times P(X_3) \\
     &= 0.0006 + 0.01 - (0.0006)(0.01) \\
     &= 0.0106 - 0.000006 \\
     &= 0.010594 \;\text{per demand}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Identify Minimal Cut Sets:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MCS}_1 &= \{X_1, X_2\} \quad (\text{order 2}) \\
\text{MCS}_2 &= \{X_3\} \quad (\text{order 1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:**

- The lowest-order cut set is of **order 1** (single point of failure — SDV-01). 
- **Recommendation:** Add a second shutdown valve in series. This converts the cut set to order 2 (both SDVs must fail) and reduces the contribution of $X_3$ from $0.01$ to $(0.01)^2 = 0.0001$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — FTA Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the AND-gate multiplication rule.** A common error is adding probabilities of an AND-gate input instead of multiplying. Always multiply for AND, add for OR.
> 2. **Missing the overlap term in OR-gate.** For non-rare events, the exact OR formula is $1 - \prod(1 - P_i)$, **not** simply $\sum P_i$. The simplified sum is valid only when $P_i \ll 1$.
> 3. **Not identifying Minimal Cut Sets.** Examiners allot **at least 2 marks** exclusively for MCS listing.
> 4. **Forgetting units.** The top-event probability is in **"per reactor-year"** or **"per demand"** — always state the basis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Hazard Identification (HAZID)** is the **first and most critical** step of risk assessment — un-identified hazards cannot be controlled.
- [x] **HAZOP** is **inductive, team-based, qualitative**; uses **7–8 guide words** on process nodes.
- [x] **FMEA** computes **RPN = S × O × D**; threshold for action typically **RPN ≥ 100** or top 20%.
- [x] **FTA** is **deductive, Boolean**; uses **AND / OR gates**; **OR-gate ≈ sum** and **AND-gate = product** (for rare events).
- [x] **ETA** is forward-branching; **branch probability = product of success/failure probabilities along that branch**.
- [x] **What-If** is a fast, free-form brainstorming technique for early design stages.
- [x] **Checklist Analysis** is comparative, suitable for routine/operating plants with a known design basis.
- [x] **PHA (Preliminary Hazard Analysis)** is performed at the **conceptual stage** before any P&IDs exist.
- [x] **Dow F&EI** = MF × GPH × SPH — used for **fire & explosion damage potential**.
- [x] **Delphi Technique** = structured, anonymous, iterative expert elicitation for **data-poor** situations.
- [x] Always pair a hazard identification technique with a **real industrial accident** (Bhopal, Flixborough, BP Texas City) for application-level marks.
- [x] In a 14-mark question, **always tabulate** your HAZOP/FMEA/FTA/ETA work — neat tables score more than paragraphs.
- [x] **Minimal Cut Set order** = number of basic events that must all fail; **lower order = higher criticality**.
- [x] **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** = $P_{\text{old}} / P_{\text{new}}$ is a common exam metric for showing improvement.
- [x] KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **CO mapping**: this topic maps to **CO2 — "Apply hazard identification techniques to process facilities"** (Bloom Levels 3, 4, 5).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Audit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Safety Audit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Audit (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **Safety Audit** is a *systematic, independent, and documented evaluation* of an organization's safety management system (SMS), plant equipment, operational procedures, and statutory compliance status, performed against pre-defined standards, codes, and regulatory benchmarks, with the objective of identifying non-conformities, assessing the effectiveness of safety controls, and recommending corrective actions to reduce the **Quantitative Risk** to As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP).

In the context of **Quantitative Risk Assessment (PECHT754)**, a safety audit is not merely a qualitative walk-through — it is a *data-gathering instrument* that feeds numerical risk parameters (failure rates, probability of failure on demand, exposure frequencies, consequence severities) into the broader QRA framework.

### 1.1.1 Key Terminology Anchors

| Term | Meaning in QRA Context |
|---|---|
| **Audit Criteria** | The reference standards (IS, OISD, NFPA, OSHA, ISO 45001) against which performance is measured. |
| **Audit Evidence** | Verifiable records, observations, measurements, and interviews collected during the audit. |
| **Non-Conformity (NC)** | A deviation from audit criteria; classified as *Major NC* or *Minor NC*. |
| **Audit Findings** | Conclusions drawn from the evidence, indicating gaps and their risk significance. |
| **Compliance** | Degree of adherence to statutory and internal safety requirements. |
| **ALARP** | As Low As Reasonably Practicable — the target residual risk threshold. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus treats the safety audit as the *practical data-collection arm* of QRA. Expect questions linking audit findings → hazard identification → frequency/probability estimation → risk quantification.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Hospital Inspection" Analogy

Imagine a **hospital** that treats patients. The hospital has doctors, equipment, protocols, and a regulator (the medical board). Now imagine a **health auditor** arrives once a year.

- The auditor does **not treat patients** — the auditor *examines the system*.
- The auditor checks: "Are the doctors qualified? Is the MRI machine calibrated? Is the hand-wash protocol followed? Is the emergency exit clear?"
- After the audit, a report says: *"Non-conformity: MRI calibration is 2 years overdue → Patient safety risk is HIGH."*

A **Safety Audit** in a chemical plant, oil refinery, or process industry works *exactly the same way*:

- The **plant** is the hospital.
- The **safety management system (SMS)** is the medical protocol.
- The **auditor** is the independent examiner.
- The **NC report** becomes the input for QRA — quantifying *how dangerous* each gap actually is.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory hook:** *"An audit finds the holes; a QRA scores the holes; a risk matrix prioritizes the holes; an action plan plugs the holes."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of a Safety Audit

A safety audit, as covered under **Module 2 of PECHT754**, is engineered to fulfil the following objectives in a sequential, traceable manner:

1. **Verification of Statutory Compliance** — Adherence to the Factories Act 1948 (India), OSH Code 2020, and applicable BIS/IS standards.
2. **Evaluation of SMS Effectiveness** — Checking the maturity of the Occupational Health & Safety Management System (often benchmarked to **ISO 45001:2018**).
3. **Identification of Hazards** — Systematic hazard recognition using techniques that feed QRA (HAZOP, FMEA, Checklist Analysis, What-If).
4. **Quantification of Risk Indicators** — Deriving numerical inputs such as:
   - Loss-time injury frequency rate
   - Audit non-conformity density
   - Equipment failure probability estimates
5. **Review of Emergency Preparedness** — Validating Emergency Response Plans (ERP), On-Site Emergency Plans, and Off-Site Plans.
6. **Recommendation of Corrective Actions** — Time-bound, measurable, and traceable.

## 2.2 Types of Safety Audits (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Audit Type | Scope | Frequency | QRA Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compliance Audit** | Statutory/legal adherence | Annual | Confirms legal floor of risk control |
| **Process Safety Audit** | Process equipment, instrumentation, interlocks | 1–3 years | Direct input to QRA frequencies & PoD |
| **Management System Audit** | SMS, training, documentation | Annual | Validates organizational reliability factor |
| **Product Safety Audit** | Raw materials, finished goods | Per batch | Feeds inventory/consequence data |
| **Contractor Safety Audit** | Third-party vendors, construction sites | Pre-job + periodic | Quantifies off-site/external risk |
| **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** | New/modified installations | Pre-commissioning | Validates risk control before exposure |
| **Internal vs. External Audit** | Internal: own team; External: third-party | Continuous or scheduled | External is statistically more objective |

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory Backdrop (India):** The **Factories Act 1948 (Section 7A, 41, 91)** and the **OSH Code 2020** mandate periodic safety audits for hazardous processes handling threshold quantities of chemicals.

## 2.3 Audit Team Composition

A balanced audit team is critical to credibility. Typical structure for a process industry:

- **Lead Auditor** — Independent, certified (e.g., NEBOSH/IOSH/ISO 45001 Lead Auditor), chairs the team.
- **Process/Technical Expert** — Subject-matter expert on plant operations, reactors, instrumentation.
- **Safety Officer** — Internal or external EHS professional.
- **Maintenance/Reliability Engineer** — Reviews equipment integrity data.
- **Medical/Occupational Health Specialist** — Reviews health surveillance and exposure data.
- **Statutory/ Legal Expert** — Ensures regulatory coverage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Independence Rule (KTU emphasis):** *The auditor must not audit their own work.* This is a fundamental ISO 19011 principle and a frequent KTU short-note question.

## 2.4 Phases of a Safety Audit (ISO 19011 Aligned)

The audit follows a 6-phase lifecycle:

1. **Initiation** — Defining audit scope, criteria, objectives.
2. **Preparation** — Document review, checklist preparation, audit plan.
3. **On-Site Audit Activities** — Opening meeting, evidence collection, site walk-through, interviews.
4. **Audit Report Preparation** — Findings, NC classification, risk scoring.
5. **Audit Report Distribution & Management Review** — Submission to top management.
6. **Follow-up & Corrective Action Verification (CAR Closure)** — Re-verification of NC closure.

## 2.5 The Safety Audit Checklist

A checklist is the operational backbone of the audit. It must be **risk-based, not boiler-plate**. Typical columns of a professional checklist:

| Sl. No. | Audit Element | Reference Standard | Observation | Compliance (Y/N) | NC Category | Risk Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure vessel hydrostatic test current | IS 2825 / ASME Sec VIII | Hydro-test cert dated 2022 | N | Major | High |
| 2 | PSV set pressure verified | OISD-STD-150 | Set at 7.2 kg/cm² (design 7.0) | Y | — | Low |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Always link the checklist to the **Hazard Register** and **Risk Matrix**. This converts a qualitative checklist into a *quantitative risk input*.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas are essential for solving audit-related numerical problems in PECHT754.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Rule:** In all formulas below, $\vert x \vert$ denotes the *absolute value of x* (do not use vertical pipes in tables).

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Audit Effectiveness Index (AEI)** | $AEI = \dfrac{N_c}{N_t} \times 100$ | % | % of checklist items complied |
| 2 | **Non-Conformity Density (NCD)** | $NCD = \dfrac{N_{NC}}{N_{audits}}$ | NCs/audit | Audit-team productivity gauge |
| 3 | **Loss Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR)** | $LTIFR = \dfrac{L \times 10^6}{H}$ | per million man-hours | Industry safety KPI |
| 4 | **Severity Rate (SR)** | $SR = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{H}$ | per million man-hours | Days-lost metric |
| 5 | **Frequency Rate (FR)** | $FR = \dfrac{N \times 10^6}{H}$ | per million man-hours | Total recordable injuries |
| 6 | **Incident Rate (IR)** | $IR = \dfrac{N \times 200{,}000}{H}$ | per 100 FTE | OSHA-equivalent metric |
| 7 | **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | dimensionless | FMEA-derived risk score |
| 8 | **Audit Risk Rating (ARR)** | $ARR = \sum_i (w_i \times s_i)$ | weighted score | Composite audit score |
| 9 | **Residual Risk Reduction Factor (RRRF)** | $RRRF = \dfrac{R_{in}}{R_{out}}$ | dimensionless | Pre/post-audit risk comparison |
| 10 | **ALARP Cost-Benefit Ratio** | $\text{Gross Imbalance} = \dfrac{\Delta R}{\Delta C}$ | dimensionless | Decide if more control is "practicable" |

**Where:**
- $N_c$ = Number of compliant items, $N_t$ = Total items
- $L$ = Lost-time injuries, $D$ = Days lost, $N$ = Number of injuries
- $H$ = Man-hours worked
- $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection (FMEA scale 1–10)
- $w_i$ = Weight of audit element, $s_i$ = Score (1–5)
- $R_{in}$ = Risk before audit, $R_{out}$ = Risk after corrective action
- $\Delta R$ = Risk reduction, $\Delta C$ = Cost of additional control

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Safety audits form the **data backbone** for:

- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** in onshore/offshore oil & gas (per OISD, IRGC, TNO Purple Book guidelines).
- **Hazard and Operability Studies (HAZOP)** validation.
- **Layers of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** — confirming Independent Protection Layers (IPLs) are *independent, valid, and audited*.
- **Process Safety Management (PSM)** compliance — OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, EU Seveso-III Directive.
- **Insurance risk engineering** — premium calculation and loss-control surveys.
- **ESG and Sustainability Reporting** — GRI 403, SASB metrics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Audit Effectiveness Index (AEI)

The AEI quantifies the *compliance density* of a safety audit. It is derived as follows:

**Step 1 — Define the compliance count.**
Let $N_c$ be the number of checklist items found in compliance with the audit criteria.

**Step 2 — Define the total audited items.**
Let $N_t$ be the total number of checklist items examined during the audit, where $N_t \geq N_c$.

**Step 3 — Express the fraction of compliance.**
The fraction of compliant items is given by:

$$
f_c = \frac{N_c}{N_t}
$$

This is a dimensionless ratio bounded as $0 \leq f_c \leq 1$.

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage.**
Multiplying by 100 yields the Audit Effectiveness Index:

$$
AEI = f_c \times 100 = \frac{N_c}{N_t} \times 100 \quad [\%]
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation band (KTU-recommended).**

| AEI (%) | Safety System Status |
|---|---|
| 90 – 100 | Robust / Industry-leading |
| 75 – 89 | Acceptable with improvement plan |
| 60 – 74 | Marginal — management review required |
| Below 60 | Non-acceptable — major re-audit mandated |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Loss Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR)

This is a *de-facto industry KPI* and a direct numerical input into QRA occupational risk modelling.

**Step 1 — Define the count variable.**
Let $L$ = number of lost-time injuries recorded over a defined reporting period.

**Step 2 — Define the exposure variable.**
Let $H$ = total man-hours worked by all personnel (employees + contractors) over the same period.

**Step 3 — Express the raw incident intensity.**
The probability of an injury per man-hour is:

$$
P_{raw} = \frac{L}{H}
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize to industry standard.**
For statistical comparability, normalize to *1 million man-hours*:

$$
LTIFR = \frac{L \times 10^6}{H} \quad \left[ \text{per million man-hours} \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example.**

Given: A refinery employs 1,200 workers, each working 240 days/year, 8 hours/day. In one year, 4 lost-time injuries occur. Calculate LTIFR.

Man-hours worked:
$$
H = 1{,}200 \times 240 \times 8 = 2{,}304{,}000 \; \text{man-hours}
$$

LTIFR calculation:
$$
LTIFR = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{2{,}304{,}000} = 1.736 \; \text{per million man-hours}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare to industry benchmark.**
The **OISD** benchmark for upstream oil & gas is typically $LTIFR \leq 1.0$. A value of $1.736$ indicates the plant is **1.736 times the target** — a clear audit finding to escalate.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Risk Priority Number (RPN) from Audit Findings

Used during audit when transitioning qualitative findings into a quantitative QRA input (FMEA linkage).

**Step 1 — Identify three parameters per finding.**
- $S$ = Severity (scale 1–10)
- $O$ = Occurrence probability (scale 1–10)
- $D$ = Detection difficulty (scale 1–10)

**Step 2 — Define RPN as the multiplicative product.**

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

**Step 3 — Bounded range.**
Since each parameter is in $\vert 1, 10 \vert$, the theoretical bounds are:

$$
RPN_{min} = 1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1
$$
$$
RPN_{max} = 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example: Pump Seal Failure Finding.**

- Severity (potential hydrocarbon release + fire) → $S = 9$
- Occurrence (seal wear observed on 3 pumps) → $O = 5$
- Detection (no online condition monitoring) → $D = 7$

$$
RPN = 9 \times 5 \times 7 = 315
$$

**Step 5 — Classification band.**

| RPN Range | Action Level |
|---|---|
| 1 – 50 | Low — monitor |
| 51 – 150 | Moderate — schedule improvement |
| 151 – 300 | High — corrective action in 3 months |
| 301 – 1000 | Critical — immediate action, re-audit |

An RPN of **315** falls in the *Critical* band → immediate intervention.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: ALARP "Gross Imbalance" Test

A common KTU numerical question concerns *whether further risk reduction is "reasonably practicable"*.

**Step 1 — Quantify risk before control.**
Let $R_{in}$ = individual or societal risk before additional control.

**Step 2 — Quantify risk after control.**
Let $R_{out}$ = risk after proposed additional control.

**Step 3 — Compute risk reduction.**

$$
\Delta R = R_{in} - R_{out}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the cost of the additional control.**
Let $\Delta C$ = annualized cost of the additional control (₹/year).

**Step 5 — Compute the Gross Imbalance Ratio.**

$$
GI = \frac{\Delta R}{\Delta C}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision rule (UK HSE / IRGC framework):**
- If $GI$ is **large** (i.e., risk reduction >> cost) → *control is reasonably practicable* → must implement.
- If $GI$ is **small** (cost ≈ risk reduction) → *gross disproportion* argument applies → may be ALARP.

**Step 7 — Worked Example.**
A flare-tip replacement costs ₹50,00,000/year and reduces individual fatality risk from $5 \times 10^{-4}$ to $1 \times 10^{-4}$ per year.

$$
\Delta R = 5 \times 10^{-4} - 1 \times 10^{-4} = 4 \times 10^{-4}
$$
$$
GI = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{6}} = 8 \times 10^{-11} \; \text{per ₹}
$$

This is a very large risk reduction per rupee — control is **reasonably practicable** and must be implemented for ALARP compliance.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Linking Audit Findings to QRA

The following table maps a *typical* audit finding to the *quantitative QRA parameter* it influences.

| Audit Finding (Qualitative) | Hazard Identified | QRA Parameter Affected | Numerical Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure vessel overdue for hydrotest | Loss of containment | Probability of failure $\lambda$ | $\lambda$ increases (per OISD-150) |
| PSV set pressure outside ±5% tolerance | Overpressure | Failure of IPL (PSV) | PoD changes (from $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$) |
| No gas detection in confined area | Toxic/flammable exposure | Exposure frequency $f$ | $f$ increases 10× |
| Operator competency records missing | Human error | Human Error Probability (HEP) | HEP shifts from $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| Bund wall damaged | Loss of secondary containment | Consequence severity $C$ | Pool area increases 30% |
| Fire water pump auto-start failure | Loss of fire protection | PoD of firewater system | PoD: $10^{-1} \rightarrow 10^{0}$ |
| ESD valve passing leak test | Bypass of safety function | PoD of IPL | PoD: $10^{-2} \rightarrow 1$ (failure) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Audit *findings* are translated to *QRA parameters* via published databases — **OISD, CCPS PERD, TNO Purple Book, EGIG, HSE UK R2P2**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Safety Audit Lifecycle (ISO 19011 Flow)

This block diagram shows the sequential architecture of a professional safety audit, from initiation to corrective-action closure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Audit Mandate] --> B[Phase 1: Initiation - Define Scope, Criteria, Objectives]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Preparation - Document Review, Checklist, Plan]
    C --> D[Phase 3: On-Site Activities - Opening Meeting, Evidence Collection]
    D --> E[Phase 4: Audit Report - Findings, NC Classification, Risk Scoring]
    E --> F[Phase 5: Management Review - Top Management Review]
    F --> G[Phase 6: Follow-up - Corrective Action Verification CAR Closure]
    G --> H{All NCs Closed?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Audit Closed - Archive Records]
    H -->|No| J[Re-audit or Escalate]
    J --> C
    I --> K[End: Feed Findings to QRA Database]
```

## 4.2 Audit Finding → QRA Parameter Conversion Pipeline

This flowchart shows how a *qualitative audit observation* becomes a *quantitative risk input*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    obs1[Field Observation] --> cat[Classify NC: Major or Minor]
    cat --> haz[Hazard Identification: HAZOP or FMEA Linkage]
    haz --> para[Map to QRA Parameter: Lambda, PoD, HEP, f, C]
    para --> db[Reference Database Lookup: OISD, CCPS, TNO]
    db --> qnum[Quantitative Risk Score: RPN, LTIFR, GI]
    qnum --> matrix[Risk Matrix Placement: Low, Med, High, Critical]
    matrix --> action[Corrective Action: Time-bound, Owner-assigned]
    action --> verify[Re-Audit Verification]
    verify --> close[CAR Closure: Updated QRA Input]
```

## 4.3 Audit Team Structure & Information Flow

This diagram shows how a multi-disciplinary audit team interacts with the plant and the data systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    lead[Lead Auditor ISO 45001 Certified] --> tech[Process Technical Expert]
    lead --> safety[Safety Officer EHS]
    lead --> maint[Maintenance Reliability Engineer]
    lead --> med[Occupational Health Specialist]
    lead --> legal[Statutory Compliance Expert]
    tech --> plant[Plant Operations Interface]
    safety --> plant
    maint --> plant
    plant --> dms[Document Management System]
    dms --> qra[QRA Database]
    med --> health[Health Surveillance Records]
    legal --> statute[Factories Act OSH Code ISO 45001]
    dms --> report[Consolidated Audit Report]
    qra --> report
    health --> report
    statute --> report
    report --> mgmt[Top Management Review]
```

## 4.4 Risk Matrix Used During Audit Classification

A 5×5 risk matrix is the most common classification tool used during a safety audit to assign a numerical risk band to each finding.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sev1[Severity Scale] --> matrix5x5[5 by 5 Risk Matrix]
    prob1[Probability Scale] --> matrix5x5
    matrix5x5 --> low[Low: Acceptable]
    matrix5x5 --> med[Medium: Monitor]
    matrix5x5 --> high[High: Action Required]
    matrix5x5 --> crit[Critical: Immediate Stop]
    matrix5x5 --> sev5[Severe: Catastrophic]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Diagram Limitations:** Mermaid cannot render a true grid-matrix table faithfully. The above block diagram conveys the *functional architecture* (inputs → matrix → outputs) of the classification logic. In examination answers, draw the actual 5×5 grid (Probability × Severity) manually.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Safety Audit. List any four objectives of a safety audit. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Safety Audit:** A *systematic, independent, and documented* evaluation of an organization's safety management system, plant, equipment, and statutory compliance, conducted against pre-defined standards and criteria, to identify non-conformities and recommend corrective actions.

**Four Objectives:**
1. To verify *statutory compliance* under the Factories Act 1948 and OSH Code 2020.
2. To assess the *effectiveness* of the Safety Management System (SMS) and its alignment with ISO 45001.
3. To *identify hazards* and provide quantitative data inputs for QRA (failure rates, HEP, PoD).
4. To recommend *time-bound corrective actions* and verify their implementation through follow-up audits.

---

### Q2. Distinguish between a *Major* and a *Minor* non-conformity with one example each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Major Non-Conformity | Minor Non-Conformity |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A *systemic failure* or absence of a required control with direct impact on safety. | An *isolated* lapse or minor deviation, with limited immediate risk. |
| Risk Impact | Could lead directly to a hazardous event. | Indirect or contributory impact only. |
| Example | Pressure vessel *never* hydrostatically tested. | One safety signboard faded but legible. |
| Closure | Immediate corrective action, management notification. | Time-bound action, routine follow-up. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Module 2 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> - Do **not** confuse "Safety Audit" with "Safety Inspection" — the former is *systemic and documented*, the latter is *operational and routine*.
> - When computing LTIFR, students often forget to **multiply by $10^6$** — instant loss of 2 marks.
> - For ALARP questions, always state the **decision rule** explicitly (gross disproportion test) — not just the calculation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Safety Audit Methodology + QRA Linkage

**(a)** Explain the *six phases* of a safety audit as per ISO 19011 guidelines. Discuss the role of the *lead auditor* and *independence principle*. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A process plant with 800 employees works 300 days/year, 8 hours/day. In the audit year, 5 lost-time injuries and 220 lost workdays are recorded. Calculate the **LTIFR**, **Severity Rate (SR)**, and **Frequency Rate (FR)**. Comment on the safety performance if the OISD benchmark for LTIFR is 1.0. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**The Six Phases:**

1. **Initiation** — Audit scope, criteria, and objectives are defined; feasibility and resource requirements are established.
2. **Preparation** — Document review (SMS manuals, P&IDs, regulatory licenses), checklist creation, audit plan distribution, team briefing.
3. **On-Site Audit Activities** — Opening meeting; evidence collection through observation, interviews, document sampling; site walk-through.
4. **Audit Report Preparation** — Findings, NC classification (Major/Minor), risk scoring, drafting of the report.
5. **Report Distribution and Management Review** — Submission to top management; corrective action plans (CAPA) are agreed with timelines.
6. **Follow-up and CAR Closure** — Verification of corrective actions, re-audit if required, closure of audit cycle.

**Role of Lead Auditor:**
- *Plans, leads, and reports* the audit.
- Ensures *objectivity* of the team.
- Holds the *final authority* on findings and NC classification.
- Is *certified* (e.g., ISO 45001 Lead Auditor, NEBOSH).

**Independence Principle:**
> *"Auditors must be free from bias and the influence of those being audited."* — ISO 19011:2018, Clause 5.2.4.

This is critical to audit *credibility*. Auditors must not audit their own work, must declare conflicts of interest, and must be organizationally independent (especially for external audits). Independence is the cornerstone that distinguishes an *audit* from a *self-review* or *inspection*.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Given:**
- Employees $E = 800$
- Working days/year $= 300$
- Hours/day $= 8$
- Lost-time injuries $L = 5$
- Lost workdays $D = 220$

**Step 1 — Total man-hours.**

$$
H = 800 \times 300 \times 8 = 1{,}920{,}000 \; \text{man-hours}
$$

**Step 2 — LTIFR.**

$$
LTIFR = \frac{L \times 10^6}{H} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{1{,}920{,}000} = 2.604 \; \text{per million man-hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Severity Rate (SR).**

$$
SR = \frac{D \times 10^6}{H} = \frac{220 \times 10^6}{1{,}920{,}000} = 114.58 \; \text{per million man-hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Frequency Rate (FR).**

$$
FR = \frac{L \times 10^6}{H} = 2.604 \; \text{per million man-hours}
$$

**Step 5 — Benchmark Comparison.**
The plant's LTIFR of **2.604** is **2.6× the OISD benchmark of 1.0**. This indicates **substandard safety performance**, requires an *immediate management review*, and necessitates a *targeted intervention plan* (engineering controls, training audits, behavioural-based safety).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Audit Findings + RPN + ALARP

**(a)** A safety audit identifies the following finding: *"The flame arrestor on a naphtha storage tank vent line was last inspected 4 years ago, against the 2-year statutory requirement."* Apply the **FMEA-based RPN method** to this finding using $S = 8$, $O = 4$, $D = 6$. State the action priority. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An additional flame-detection system proposed for the tank farm costs ₹30,00,000/year to install and maintain. It reduces the *individual fatality risk* on the tank farm from $3 \times 10^{-4}$ per year to $5 \times 10^{-5}$ per year. Using the **ALARP Gross Imbalance Test**, determine if the additional control is reasonably practicable. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

- Severity $S = 8$ (potential tank fire → multi-fatality)
- Occurrence $O = 4$ (overdue inspection, corrosion likely)
- Detection $D = 6$ (no continuous monitoring)

**Step 2 — Compute RPN.**

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D = 8 \times 4 \times 6 = 192
$$

**Step 3 — Action priority.**
Per the classification band: $151 - 300$ → **High — Corrective action within 3 months**. 

Mandatory actions: immediate inspection, re-certification, and entry into the *asset integrity management* system with an SMS-based reminder.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Compute risk reduction.**

$$
\Delta R = R_{in} - R_{out} = 3 \times 10^{-4} - 5 \times 10^{-5} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{per year}
$$

**Step 2 — Cost of additional control.**

$$
\Delta C = ₹3 \times 10^{6} \; \text{per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Gross Imbalance Ratio.**

$$
GI = \frac{\Delta R}{\Delta C} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{3 \times 10^{6}} = 8.33 \times 10^{-11} \; \text{per ₹}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**
The risk reduction per rupee is **very high** ($\sim 8 \times 10^{-11}$ fatalities saved per rupee). The control is **grossly disproportionate to the cost in favour of implementation**. Therefore, by the UK HSE / IRGC ALARP test, the flame-detection system is **reasonably practicable** and must be implemented. The plant cannot claim ALARP without it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read before the exam):**

- ✅ **Safety Audit** = *systematic, independent, documented* evaluation against pre-defined standards.
- ✅ Audit follows **ISO 19011:2018** — 6-phase lifecycle.
- ✅ **Lead Auditor** must be *independent and certified*; never audits own work.
- ✅ **Major NC** = systemic, immediate risk; **Minor NC** = isolated, low-risk lapse.
- ✅ Always link audit findings to **Hazard Register + QRA parameters** ($\lambda$, PoD, HEP, $f$, $C$).
- ✅ **LTIFR** formula = $L \times 10^6 / H$ — never forget the $10^6$ multiplier.
- ✅ **RPN** = $S \times O \times D$ — range $1$ to $1{,}000$.
- ✅ **ALARP Gross Imbalance Test** = compare $\Delta R$ to $\Delta C$; large ratio → must implement.
- ✅ **Statutory basis (India)**: Factories Act 1948, OSH Code 2020, OISD standards, IS codes.
- ✅ **International standards**: ISO 45001 (SMS), ISO 31000 (Risk), OSHA PSM, EU Seveso-III.
- ✅ Audit outputs feed **PSSR, LOPA, HAZOP** validation — the audit is a *data source*, not an end product.
- ✅ **RPN ≥ 300** = Critical → immediate stop / re-audit.
- ✅ **AEI ≥ 90%** = Robust safety system.
- ✅ A *Checklist* must be *risk-based*, not boilerplate, and tied to standards with measurable criteria.
- ✅ Audit report must specify **owner, deadline, and verification method** for every NC.

> **End of Notes — Module 2: Safety Audit (PECHT754 — QRA, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Safety Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Inspection** is a systematic, scheduled, and documented technical examination of process equipment, piping, structures, and instrumentation systems in an industrial facility, conducted to verify their mechanical integrity, identify deterioration, and ensure continued safe operation within the design intent.

In the context of **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**, safety inspection is the *engineering control* layer that feeds empirical failure-rate data into the risk equation:

$$
R = f \times C
$$

where $R$ = Risk, $f$ = Frequency of failure, and $C$ = Consequence severity. Inspection **reduces $f$** by detecting damage before it escalates to loss of containment.

The standard governing framework is **API 570** (piping), **API 510** (pressure vessels), and **ASME Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code**, while the modern intelligent approach is **API 580 / API 581 — Risk-Based Inspection (RBI)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Think of safety inspection as a **routine medical health check-up** for an industrial plant.
> - Your **car** goes for a periodic service (oil, brakes, tyres) — that is *preventive maintenance inspection*.
> - If the garage uses an **ultrasonic probe** on a critical part, that is **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**.
> - A **doctor** prioritising your heart over a stubbed toe is *risk-based prioritisation*.
> - The **service history file** in your glove box is the *inspection record and documentation*.

Just as a missed ECG check-up can lead to a sudden heart attack, a missed thickness measurement on a corroded pipe can lead to a catastrophic rupture, fire, or toxic release.

## 1.3 Core Objectives of Safety Inspection

| # | Objective | Engineering Significance |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Deterioration Detection** | Find corrosion, erosion, cracking, creep before failure |
| 2 | **Code Compliance** | Meet statutory requirements of Factories Act, OSHA, PESO |
| 3 | **Integrity Assurance** | Confirm fitness-for-service (FFS) of equipment |
| 4 | **Risk Reduction** | Lower the *P* (probability) term in QRA risk matrix |
| 5 | **Data Generation** | Feed real failure-rate data into RBI re-assessment |
| 6 | **Regulatory Evidence** | Produce auditable records for statutory inspectors |

## 1.4 Classification of Safety Inspections

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite — Always remember the 4-tier hierarchy.** A 14-mark question can be built around this single table.

| Type | Trigger | Scope | Frequency |
|------|---------|-------|-----------|
| **Pre-commissioning** | New/Modified equipment | Full dimensional, NDT, hydrotest | Once before start-up |
| **Routine / Periodic** | Calendar time | Visual + selected NDT | 1–5 years (per code) |
| **Risk-Based (RBI)** | Computed PoF × CoF | Focused on high-risk circuits | Variable (1–10 years) |
| **Special / Triggered** | Process upset, near-miss | Forensic NDT, root-cause | Event-driven |
| **Continuous (On-stream)** | 24×7 monitoring | Real-time corrosion coupons, acoustic emission | Continuous |

---

## 1.5 Why Safety Inspection is Indispensable in QRA

The QRA pipeline: **Hazard Identification → Frequency Estimation → Consequence Modelling → Risk Characterisation → Mitigation → Inspection Feedback**.

Inspection sits in the **mitigation verification loop**. Without inspection data, the frequency term $f$ in the risk equation remains a *generic* estimate rather than a *plant-specific* measurement.

$$
\boxed{f_{\text{plant-specific}} = f_{\text{generic}} \times F_{\text{management}} \times F_{\text{inspection}} \times F_{\text{environment}}}
$$

The **Inspection Effectiveness Factor** ($F_{\text{inspection}}$) is a multiplier between **0.1 (excellent) and 10 (none)**, taken from API 581. This is a **guaranteed 14-mark topic** in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Inspection Workflow — Structured Logic

The complete safety-inspection lifecycle, as adopted in plant practice, follows seven sequential stages. Each step has a defined deliverable that an examiner loves to question.

1. **Plan & Schedule**
   - Identify the *Inspection Population* (all in-scope pressure vessels, piping, storage tanks, relief valves).
   - Use the **Piping and Instrument Diagram (P&ID)** and the **Plot Plan** to map circuits.
   - Allocate inspection windows into the plant turnaround schedule.

2. **Select Inspection Points (PoI)**
   - Use RBI screening (Section 2.3) to rank equipment.
   - Mark CMLs (**Circuits of Maintenance/Inspection**) and PoIs on isometric drawings.

3. **Choose Inspection Technique**
   - Match NDT method to the expected damage mechanism (Table 2.2 below).

4. **Execute Field Inspection**
   - Permit-to-work, isolation, scaffolding, access.
   - NDT, thickness gauging (UT), visual examination.

5. **Evaluate Results**
   - Compare measured thickness $t_m$ with required thickness $t_{\text{min}}$ from API 510/570.
   - Calculate **Remaining Life** (Section 3).

6. **Decide Fitness-for-Service**
   - **Continue** (no action), **Monitor** (re-inspect sooner), **Repair**, **Replace**, or **Derate** (lower operating pressure/temperature).

7. **Document & Close-Out**
   - Inspection reports, NDT reports, photographic evidence, RBI database update.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Marks are awarded for the **closed-loop nature** of inspection. The deliverable of one inspection becomes the *input data* for the next RBI cycle — this iterative loop is the heart of any RBI question.

## 2.2 Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) Methods — Master Comparison Table

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Map each NDT method to (a) damage type, (b) advantages, (c) limitations. A common 7-mark question is *"Compare any four NDT methods used in petrochemical plant inspection."*

| NDT Method | Physical Principle | Detects | Cannot Detect | Best Application |
|------------|---------------------|---------|---------------|------------------|
| **Visual Testing (VT)** | Direct eye / borescope | Surface defects, corrosion, leaks | Subsurface flaws | First-pass screening |
| **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** | High-frequency sound waves | Wall thinning, laminations, cracks | Surface-only flaws in thin sections | Thickness gauging (most used) |
| **Radiographic Testing (RT)** | X-ray / Gamma penetration | Volumetric flaws (voids, slag) | Planar defects parallel to beam | Weld inspection |
| **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** | Magnetic flux leakage | Surface & near-surface cracks | Subsurface / non-ferrous | Ferritic welds |
| **Liquid Penetrant Testing (PT)** | Capillary dye bleed-out | Surface-breaking cracks | Subsurface, porous materials | Non-ferrous welds |
| **Eddy Current Testing (ET)** | Electromagnetic induction | Surface cracks, conductivity changes | Thick sections | Heat-exchanger tubes |
| **Acoustic Emission (AE)** | Transient elastic waves from active defects | Growing cracks, leaks in real time | Static flaws | Pressure-test monitoring |
| **Thermography** | Infrared emission | Refractory loss, insulation voids, hot spots | Subsurface corrosion | Furnace tubes, electrical |

## 2.3 Risk-Based Inspection (RBI) — The Modern Approach

RBI replaces rigid time-based inspection with **risk-prioritised** inspection. Risk is the product of two orthogonal axes:

$$
\boxed{\text{Risk} = P_oF \times C_oF}
$$

where:
- **$P_oF$** = Probability of Failure (likelihood per year)
- **$C_oF$** = Consequence of Failure (financial, safety, environmental impact in monetary units)

### 2.3.1 The 5×5 RBI Matrix (KTU Favourite)

| $P_oF$ \ $C_oF$ | A (Negligible) | B (Minor) | C (Moderate) | D (Major) | E (Catastrophic) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **5 (Very High)** | M | H | H | VH | VH |
| **4 (High)** | M | M | H | H | VH |
| **3 (Medium)** | L | M | M | H | H |
| **2 (Low)** | L | L | M | M | H |
| **1 (Very Low)** | L | L | L | M | M |

- **VH** = inspect within 1 year, full NDT
- **H** = inspect within 2–3 years
- **M** = inspect within 5 years
- **L** = inspect on opportunity / "run-to-failure" permitted

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> In the exam hall, *write the formula first*, *then substitute values*, *then state the unit*. The 1-mark "unit" step is the most common mark students lose in the KTU valuation key.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|------|
| 1 | Corrosion rate | $C_R = \dfrac{t_{\text{initial}} - t_{\text{measured}}}{\Delta t}$ | $t$ = thickness; $\Delta t$ = interval | mm/yr |
| 2 | Remaining life | $RL = \dfrac{t_{\text{measured}} - t_{\text{min}}}{C_R}$ | $t_{\text{min}}$ = minimum required | years |
| 3 | Next inspection date | $t_{\text{next}} = t_{\text{last}} + \min(RL, \text{code interval})$ | — | date |
| 4 | Risk score | $R = P_oF \times C_oF$ | Both dimensionless | — |
| 5 | Likelihood (PoF) | $P_oF = f_{\text{base}} \times F_E \times F_I$ | $F_E$ = environment; $F_I$ = inspection | 1/yr |
| 6 | Consequence | $C_oF = \sum_i (A_i \times C_i)$ | Area × Cost | USD |
| 7 | Inspection effectiveness | $F_I = f(\text{NDT, coverage, frequency})$ | API 581 table | — |
| 8 | Failure rate modified | $\lambda_{\text{inspected}} = \lambda_{\text{base}} \times (1 - P_d)$ | $P_d$ = probability of detection | 1/yr |
| 9 | Joint PoF (independent) | $P_{\text{joint}} = 1 - \prod_i (1-P_i)$ | Series weak links | — |
| 10 | Confidence (PoD) | $P_c = 1 - \beta(d)$ | $\beta$ = log-odds fit | — |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Safety Inspection

| Industry Sector | Critical Inspection Application |
|-----------------|----------------------------------|
| **Refineries** | Crude overhead corrosion loop, hydrocracker reactors |
| **Petrochemicals** | Ethylene cracker furnace tubes, ethylene oxide reactors |
| **LNG** | Cryogenic LNG storage tanks (-162 °C) |
| **Power** | Boiler tubes, turbine blades, steam headers |
| **Pharma** | Steriliser vessels, cleanroom HVAC duct integrity |
| **Offshore** | Subsea risers, splash zone corrosion, FPSO hull |

In every sector, inspection is the **empirical heart** of the Bow-Tie risk model — it is the *recovery* side that prevents a *threat* (corrosion, fatigue) from becoming a *consequence* (fire, explosion, toxic release).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Corrosion Rate and Remaining Life

This is the **most frequently asked numerical** in KTU Module 2. You must derive it *and* compute it for a sample value.

### 3.1.1 Starting Physical Principle

The thickness of a pipe/vessel wall decreases *linearly* with time under uniform corrosion (a standard assumption for external atmospheric and general internal corrosion). Let:

- $t_0$ = initial (as-built) wall thickness, mm
- $t_m$ = current measured thickness during inspection, mm
- $t_{\min}$ = minimum thickness allowed by code (e.g., ASME B31.3 excludes corrosion allowance)
- $C_R$ = average corrosion rate, mm/year
- $\Delta t$ = time elapsed between as-built date and current inspection, years

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define thickness loss as a linear function of time.

$$
\Delta t = t_0 - t_m
$$

**Step 2:** Corrosion rate is thickness loss per unit time.

$$
C_R = \frac{\Delta t}{\Delta t} = \frac{t_0 - t_m}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 3:** Remaining Life is the time for the wall to reach the code-minimum.

$$
t_m - t_{\min} = C_R \times RL
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $RL$ by substituting $C_R$:

$$
RL = \frac{t_m - t_{\min}}{C_R}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $C_R$ from Step 2:

$$
\boxed{RL = \frac{(t_m - t_{\min}) \cdot \Delta t}{t_0 - t_m}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical — KTU Exam Style

> **Problem (KTU 2023 style):** A carbon-steel overhead condenser tube has $t_0 = 6.0$ mm, was last inspected 4 years ago when $t_{\text{last}} = 5.2$ mm, and code requires $t_{\min} = 3.0$ mm. The new inspection gives $t_m = 4.6$ mm. Calculate the corrosion rate and the remaining safe life.

**Step 1 — Corrosion rate over the latest 4-year interval:**

$$
C_R = \frac{t_{\text{last}} - t_m}{\Delta t_{\text{new}}} = \frac{5.2 - 4.6}{4} = \frac{0.6}{4} = 0.15 \, \text{mm/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Remaining life from today:**

$$
RL = \frac{t_m - t_{\min}}{C_R} = \frac{4.6 - 3.0}{0.15} = \frac{1.6}{0.15} \approx 10.67 \, \text{years}
$$

**Step 3 — Sanity check against code maximum interval (assume API 570 = 10 years):**

$$
t_{\text{next}} = \min(RL, 10) = \min(10.67, 10) = 10 \, \text{years}
$$

**Verdict:** Re-inspect in **10 years**, but flag for a follow-up UT scan in 5 years because of the high naphthenic-acid service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Key Insight:** If $RL$ is *greater* than the code interval, the code interval governs. If $RL$ is *less*, the $RL$ governs. Always state *which one controls*.

## 3.2 Derivation — Probability of Failure (PoF) in RBI

The PoF for a corroding circuit in API 581 Part 2 is:

$$
P_oF = g(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
$$

where $\lambda$ is the failure rate. For the *thin-wall, uniform-corrosion* damage model:

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{t_{\min}/C_R} = \frac{C_R}{t_{\min}}
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1:** Time to reach $t_{\min}$ = $t_{\min}/C_R$ years.

**Step 2:** Treat this as the *characteristic life* of an exponential distribution:

$$
\theta = \frac{t_{\min}}{C_R}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the exponential CDF:

$$
\boxed{P_oF(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{C_R \cdot t}{t_{\min}}\right)}
$$

**Step 4 — Effect of inspection:** If an inspection is performed at interval $\tau$ and has probability of detection $P_d$, the modified failure rate is:

$$
\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\lambda}{1 + P_d \cdot (\lambda \tau - 1)}
$$

For the *post-inspection* PoF at the *next* interval:

$$
\boxed{P_oF^{\text{inspected}} = 1 - \exp(-\lambda_{\text{eff}} \cdot \tau)}
$$

### 3.2.1 Worked Numerical — RBI Calculation

> **Problem:** A vessel has $C_R = 0.2$ mm/yr, $t_{\min} = 8$ mm. Inspections are scheduled every $\tau = 5$ years, with $P_d = 0.8$. Calculate the base PoF over 5 years and the *inspected* PoF.

**Step 1 — Base failure rate:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{C_R}{t_{\min}} = \frac{0.2}{8} = 0.025 \, \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Base PoF over 5 years:**

$$
P_oF^{\text{base}} = 1 - e^{-0.025 \times 5} = 1 - e^{-0.125} = 1 - 0.8825 = 0.1175 \approx 11.75\%
$$

**Step 3 — Inspected effective rate:**

$$
\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \frac{0.025}{1 + 0.8 \times (0.025 \times 5 - 1)} = \frac{0.025}{1 + 0.8 \times (-0.875)} = \frac{0.025}{0.30} = 0.0833 \, \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Inspected PoF over next 5 years:**

$$
P_oF^{\text{inspected}} = 1 - e^{-0.0833 \times 5} = 1 - e^{-0.4167} = 1 - 0.659 = 0.341 = 34.1\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical note:** Here the inspection effectiveness appears *worse* because the damage is faster than the inspection interval — a *classic RBI insight* that KTU examiners love. Effective inspection requires $\tau < RL/2$.

## 3.3 Derivation — Consequence of Failure (CoF)

The financial CoF for a release in API 581 is:

$$
C_oF = C_{\text{equipment}} + C_{\text{injury}} + C_{\text{environment}} + C_{\text{business}} + C_{\text{incident}}
$$

For a *pool fire* scenario on a pump:

$$
C_{\text{pool}} = m_{\text{burn}} \times V_{\text{pool}} \times H_c \times \eta \times C_{\text{fuel}}
$$

For a *vapour cloud explosion* (TNT-equivalent):

$$
\text{TNT mass} = \frac{\eta \times m \times H_c}{H_{TNT}}
$$

$$
\text{Blast overpressure distance } r = K \times M^{1/3}
$$

where $K$ is the scaled-distance constant (0.05–0.1 for damage threshold).

The **final RBI risk number** (in USD/year) is:

$$
\boxed{R_{\$} = P_oF \times C_oF}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The examiner awards 1 mark for *identifying* the four CoF components, 2 marks for *explaining* each, and 4 marks for the *composite equation*. Memorise the equation in the boxed form above.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Inspection Decision Tree

| Measured $t_m$ vs $t_{\min}$ | Corrosion Trend | Action | Document As |
|---|---|---|---|
| $t_m \gg t_{\min}$, low $C_R$ | Stable / decelerating | **Continue** to next scheduled | Routine report |
| $t_m \gg t_{\min}$, high $C_R$ | Accelerating | **Monitor** at halved interval | Engineering memo |
| $t_m \approx t_{\min}$ | At limit | **Repair or Replace** | Fitness-for-Service report |
| $t_m < t_{\min}$ | Critical | **Immediate shutdown** | Non-conformance report |
| $t_m$ shows localised loss | Pitting / erosion | **Detailed NDT** (RT, ET) | Special inspection |

## 3.5 Inspection Interval Optimisation — Derivation

The economic optimum inspection interval $\tau^*$ minimises total expected cost:

$$
C_{\text{total}}(\tau) = \frac{C_{\text{insp}}}{\tau} + P_oF(\tau) \times C_{\text{failure}}
$$

**Step 1:** Set $dC_{\text{total}}/d\tau = 0$.

**Step 2:** Using the exponential PoF:

$$
-\frac{C_{\text{insp}}}{\tau^{2}} + \frac{\partial P_oF}{\partial \tau} \times C_{\text{failure}} = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $\partial P_oF/\partial \tau = \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau}$:

$$
\boxed{\tau^{*} = \frac{C_{\text{insp}}}{\lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda \tau^{*}} \cdot C_{\text{failure}}}}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve iteratively. The result typically lands in the range $0.3 \, RL$ to $0.7 \, RL$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not over-derive in the exam.** For a 7-mark sub-question, write steps 1–3 only, then quote the boxed $\tau^{*}$ result and the rule-of-thumb "$0.5 \times RL$".
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Safety Inspection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Inventory and P&ID Review] --> B[RBI Screening and PoF x CoF Ranking]
    B --> C[Identify Inspection Points PoI and Circuits]
    C --> D[Select NDT Method per Damage Mechanism]
    D --> E[Field Execution: Permit, Access, NDT]
    E --> F[Data Capture: Thickness, Crack Depth, Photos]
    F --> G[Compare tm with tmin from Code]
    G --> H{Is tm greater than tmin?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Calculate Corrosion Rate and Remaining Life]
    I --> J{Fitness for Service?}
    J -- Acceptable --> K[Schedule Next Inspection within Code Limit]
    J -- Marginal --> L[Reduce Interval or Apply Monitoring]
    J -- Unacceptable --> M[Recommend Repair or Replacement]
    H -- No --> N[Immediate Shutdown and Forensic Analysis]
    K --> O[Update RBI Database and Inspection Records]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Closed-Loop Feedback into Next RBI Cycle]
    P --> B
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — RBI Risk Matrix Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Equipment Inventory] --> A2[Damage Mechanism Screening]
    A2 --> A3[Calculate PoF]
    A3 --> A4[Calculate CoF]
    A4 --> A5[Multiply PoF x CoF]
    A5 --> A6{Compare to Risk Threshold}
    A6 -- Above --> A7[High Risk: Inspect within 1 to 2 years]
    A6 -- Equal --> A8[Medium Risk: Inspect within 5 years]
    A6 -- Below --> A9[Low Risk: Inspect on opportunity or run to failure]
    A7 --> A10[Update Inspection Database]
    A8 --> A10
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11[Next RBI Reassessment Cycle in 5 years]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — NDT Method Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Identify Expected Damage Type] --> B2{Surface or Subsurface?}
    B2 -- Surface --> B3[Choose VT, PT, or MT]
    B2 -- Subsurface --> B4{Volumetric or Planar?}
    B4 -- Volumetric --> B5[Choose RT]
    B4 -- Planar --> B6[Choose UT or AE]
    B3 --> B7[Confirm Material Compatibility]
    B5 --> B7
    B6 --> B7
    B7 --> B8[Execute NDT and Record Results]
    B8 --> B9[Compare Against Acceptance Criteria]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Inspection Effectiveness Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Failure Rate Base] --> C2[Multiply by Environment Factor FE]
    C2 --> C3[Multiply by Inspection Factor FI from API 581]
    C3 --> C4[Modified PoF for the Plant]
    C4 --> C5[Re-enter the Risk Equation PoF x CoF]
    C5 --> C6[Prioritise Future Inspection Resources]
    C6 --> C1
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use these diagrams in the KTU exam:** A well-drawn, *labelled* block diagram of the RBI loop is worth 2–3 bonus marks in a 14-mark question. Examiners explicitly reward "schematic clarity" in the marking scheme.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2, Q1(a)]**

**Q1. Define safety inspection. List any four objectives of safety inspection in a process plant.**

> **Model Answer:**
> **Safety Inspection** is a systematic, planned, and documented technical examination of process equipment, piping, and structures to verify mechanical integrity, detect deterioration, and ensure continued safe operation within design intent.
>
> *Four objectives:*
> 1. **Detection of deterioration** (corrosion, cracking, erosion) before failure.
> 2. **Code compliance** with statutory standards (ASME, API, Factories Act).
> 3. **Fitness-for-service verification** of pressure vessels and piping.
> 4. **Empirical data generation** to feed Risk-Based Inspection (RBI) reassessment.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Q1(b)]**

**Q2. Differentiate between time-based inspection and risk-based inspection (RBI).**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | Time-Based Inspection | Risk-Based Inspection (RBI) |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Driver** | Calendar / clock hours | Computed risk = $P_oF \times C_oF$ |
> | **Interval** | Fixed per code (e.g., 5 yrs) | Variable, 1–10 years per risk rank |
> | **Coverage** | 100% of population | Focused on high-risk circuits |
> | **Resource use** | Uniform, sometimes wasteful | Optimised, cost-effective |
> | **Output** | Compliance certificate | Risk-prioritised inspection plan |
> | **Standard** | API 510 / API 570 | API 580 / API 581 |

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q7(b)]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the complete Risk-Based Inspection (RBI) methodology as per API 580/581. List the steps and explain how PoF and CoF are calculated. (7 Marks)**

**(b) The original thickness of a pipe is 10 mm. After 6 years the measured thickness is 8.4 mm. The code minimum thickness is 6.0 mm. Calculate the corrosion rate, the remaining life, and recommend the next inspection interval if the code interval is 8 years. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution to (a):**
> **RBI Methodology (API 580 / API 581):**
> 1. **Define scope and gather data** — equipment list, P&IDs, operating conditions, fluid service.
> 2. **Identify damage mechanisms** — corrosion (general, pitting), cracking (SCC, HIC), creep, fatigue, erosion.
