> 3. **Calculate Probability of Failure (PoF)** — using API 581 Part 2 generic failure rates modified by environment, inspection, and management factors:
>    $$P_oF = f_{\text{base}} \times F_E \times F_I \times F_M$$
> 4. **Calculate Consequence of Failure (CoF)** — financial impact of a release, comprising:
>    $$C_oF = C_{\text{equip}} + C_{\text{injury}} + C_{\text{env}} + C_{\text{business}} + C_{\text{incident}}$$
> 5. **Determine risk** — $R = P_oF \times C_oF$ and plot on the 5×5 matrix.
> 6. **Plan inspection** — high-risk items get 1–2 year interval with full NDT; low-risk items get opportunity-based inspection.
> 7. **Re-assess** every 5 years, feeding the actual inspection data back into PoF (closed loop).

> **Model Solution to (b):**
> **Step 1 — Corrosion rate:**
> $$C_R = \frac{t_0 - t_m}{\Delta t} = \frac{10 - 8.4}{6} = \frac{1.6}{6} = 0.2667 \, \text{mm/yr}$$
> **Step 2 — Remaining life:**
> $$RL = \frac{t_m - t_{\min}}{C_R} = \frac{8.4 - 6.0}{0.2667} = \frac{2.4}{0.2667} \approx 9.0 \, \text{years}$$
> **Step 3 — Recommended next inspection:**
> $$\tau_{\text{next}} = \min(RL, \text{code}) = \min(9.0, 8.0) = 8.0 \, \text{years}$$
> **Verdict:** Inspect in **8 years** (code interval controls). Flag for follow-up UT scan at 4 years.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Compare any four Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods used in process-plant safety inspection, with reference to their principle, capability, and limitations. (7 Marks)**

**(b) The PoF of a corroding circuit is 0.04/yr, the CoF is estimated at USD 5 million. The current inspection effectiveness factor is 3.0. If the inspection is upgraded to an effectiveness factor of 0.5, calculate the change in annualised risk in USD/yr. Comment on the engineering decision. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution to (a):**
>
> | NDT Method | Principle | Detects | Limitation |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** | High-frequency sound pulse reflection | Wall thickness, laminations, cracks | Requires skilled operator, couplant |
> | **Radiographic Testing (RT)** | X / Gamma ray penetration and film | Volumetric flaws, slag, porosity | Radiation hazard, slow |
> | **Magnetic Particle (MT)** | Magnetic flux leakage at surface | Surface & near-surface cracks | Only for ferromagnetic materials |
> | **Liquid Penetrant (PT)** | Capillary dye bleeding into surface cracks | Surface-breaking cracks | Cannot detect subsurface flaws |
>
> *Justification:* UT is the workhorse of thickness monitoring; RT is used at fabrication; MT and PT are supplementary for weld inspection. Selection depends on damage mechanism, material, and access.

> **Model Solution to (b):**
> **Step 1 — Base annual risk:**
> $$R_1 = P_oF \times C_oF \times F_{I1} = 0.04 \times 5{,}000{,}000 \times 3.0 = \text{USD } 600{,}000/\text{yr}$$
> **Step 2 — Upgraded annual risk:**
> $$R_2 = 0.04 \times 5{,}000{,}000 \times 0.5 = \text{USD } 100{,}000/\text{yr}$$
> **Step 3 — Risk reduction:**
> $$\Delta R = R_1 - R_2 = \text{USD } 500{,}000/\text{yr}$$
> **Engineering comment:** Upgrading inspection from factor 3.0 → 0.5 yields a **USD 500,000/yr** reduction in expected loss. Any inspection upgrade costing less than this per year is economically justified; this is the standard *ALARP* (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) demonstration under RBI.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in the corrosion-rate calculation** — the examiner allocates 1 mark specifically for "mm/yr". Always end the calculation with the unit.
> 2. **Confusing $P_oF$ with $P_d$** — $P_oF$ = probability of *failure*; $P_d$ = probability of *detection* during inspection. These are NOT the same.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply the minimum of RL and code interval** — half the students write only the remaining life and skip the comparison step.
> 4. **Mixing up API 510 (vessels) and API 570 (piping)** — the standards cover different equipment and have different inspection rules.
> 5. **Not mentioning the "closed-loop feedback" in RBI** — the cyclical nature of inspection → data → re-assessment is a guaranteed 2-mark item.
> 6. **Skipping the economic justification in the alternative case** — quoting risk numbers without the ALARP commentary costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision card — read 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] **Safety Inspection = scheduled, documented, technical examination of equipment integrity.**
- [x] **Five-tier classification:** pre-commissioning, periodic, RBI, special, continuous.
- [x] **NDT master set:** VT, UT, RT, MT, PT, ET, AE, Thermography.
- [x] **Corrosion rate formula:** $C_R = (t_0 - t_m) / \Delta t$ **(mm/yr)**.
- [x] **Remaining life formula:** $RL = (t_m - t_{\min}) / C_R$ **(years)**.
- [x] **Next inspection = min(RL, code interval).**
- [x] **RBI equation:** $R = P_oF \times C_oF$ — risk = likelihood × consequence.
- [x] **PoF modification:** $P_oF = f_{\text{base}} \times F_E \times F_I \times F_M$.
- [x] **CoF components:** Equipment + Injury + Environment + Business + Incident.
- [x] **Inspection Effectiveness Factor $F_I$** ranges from 0.1 (excellent) to 10 (none), per API 581.
- [x] **Closed-loop principle:** Inspection → Data → Re-assessment → Modified PoF.
- [x] **5×5 RBI matrix** with VH / H / M / L bands mapped to inspection intervals 1 / 2 / 5 / opportunity.
- [x] **Key standards to remember:** API 510 (vessels), API 570 (piping), API 580/581 (RBI), ASME B31.3 (process piping), OSHA 1910.119 (PSM).
- [x] **Economic justification** of inspection must be stated using **ALARP** principles.
- [x] **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable — the universal risk-acceptance criterion.
- [x] Always end every numerical with the **unit** and a **one-line engineering verdict**.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Safety Inspection** | *Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme, aligned with Course Outcomes CO2 and CO3 of PECHT754.*

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — TOPIC: PRELIMINARY HAZARD ANALYSIS (PHA)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** is a structured, **inductive (bottom-up)** hazard identification and risk evaluation methodology performed during the **earliest conceptual and preliminary design stages** of a system, facility, or process. It is conducted *before* detailed design data is available and serves as the foundational screening tool that feeds subsequent detailed analyses such as **HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, and ETA**.

According to **MIL-STD-882E (System Safety Standard, U.S. Department of Defense)** and **IEC 61882 (Hazard and operability studies – Application guide)**, PHA is the systematic identification of:

- **Hazards** (potential sources of harm),
- **Causal scenarios** (how the hazard may be triggered),
- **Effects/Hazards on personnel, equipment, environment, and mission**,
- **Initial Severity Classification**, and
- **Initial Risk Index / Hazard Risk Category**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology Bookmark:**
> - **Hazard** — A condition or object with the potential to cause harm (loss of life/injury, equipment damage, environmental damage, mission loss).
> - **Hazard Cause** — The event or condition that triggers the hazard.
> - **Hazard Effect** — The consequence to people, property, environment, or mission if the hazard is realized.
> - **Severity (C)** — The worst credible consequence of a hazard occurrence.
> - **Probability (P)** — The likelihood that the hazard will be triggered under defined operational conditions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Inspector's First Walk-Through"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
>
> Imagine you are an architect designing a **high-rise building**. Before the engineers finalize plumbing, electrical, and HVAC drawings, an experienced **safety inspector walks through the blueprint with a highlighter**. They mark every place where a person could be injured — *unprotected balcony edges, slippery staircases, electrical panels near water sources, inadequate fire exits, glass walls without markings*. They do **not** yet calculate stress values or fire-rating hours. They simply **list, locate, and prioritize** potential problems.
>
> That "first walk-through highlighting exercise" is **exactly what PHA does for an engineering system**. It is the **coarse-grained, fast, early-stage screening** that identifies *what needs deeper study later* (via HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, etc.).

The analogy maps perfectly:

| Building Inspection Analogy | PHA Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Highlighting blueprint hazards | Listing hazards on the PHA worksheet |
| Judging severity (fatal vs. minor slip) | Severity classification (Catastrophic → Negligible) |
| Prioritizing which issues to fix first | Risk categorization / Risk Index assignment |
| Detailed structural review later | HAZOP, FMEA, FTA in later design stages |
| Builder's checklist | PHA Worksheet (matrix table) |

---

### 1.3 Why PHA is a Mandatory KTU Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Justification for PHA in the KTU QRA Framework:**
> 1. It is the **earliest formal safety review**, performed when design changes are cheapest to implement.
> 2. It provides a **hazard inventory** that subsequent quantitative methods (FTA, ETA, LOPA) can build upon.
> 3. It satisfies **regulatory documentation** requirements (e.g., OSHA PSM, Seveso-III Directive, Indian Factories Act).
> 4. It establishes **traceability** of safety decisions from concept to commissioning.
> 5. It forms the **input data set** for the risk matrix used in Module 2/3 of this course.

---

### 1.4 When is PHA Performed?

PHA is performed during:
- **Conceptual design** phase (system definition),
- **Preliminary design** phase (after functional baseline is set),
- During **system modification** or retrofit studies,
- For **legacy system safety assessments**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** "PHA = HAZOP". This is WRONG. **PHA is qualitative + coarse**; **HAZOP is detailed + quantitative, performed at P&ID level**. PHA comes **earlier** in the design lifecycle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Purpose and Objectives of PHA

The PHA study is conducted to achieve the following engineering objectives:

- Identify all **reasonably foreseeable hazards** in the proposed system.
- Evaluate the **worst-case consequences** of each hazard.
- Assign an **initial hazard severity category** to each hazard.
- Determine **initial risk acceptance / acceptability**.
- Provide **recommendations** to eliminate or mitigate identified hazards.
- Provide a documented input to subsequent safety analyses.

> [!NOTE]
> PHA is a **first-pass screening** — it answers the question: *"Is the system broadly safe, or do we need a deeper safety study?"*

---

## 2.2 Inputs Required for a PHA Study

For the PHA team to perform the study effectively, the following **minimum inputs** must be available:

- System description (functional baseline)
- Operational concept (ConOps)
- Preliminary system schematic / block diagram
- List of materials, energies, and environmental interfaces
- Applicable codes, standards, and regulatory requirements
- Historical accident/incident database for similar systems

## 2.3 PHA Study Team Composition

A typical PHA team should be **multidisciplinary**:

| Role | Function in PHA |
|---|---|
| **Team Leader / Facilitator** | Experienced in PHA methodology; leads brainstorming |
| **System Design Engineer** | Provides design rationale and constraints |
| **Process / Operations Engineer** | Provides operational context |
| **Safety Engineer** | Ensures systematic hazard identification |
| **Maintenance Engineer** | Identifies maintenance-related hazards |
| **Subject Matter Expert (SME)** | Specialist knowledge (e.g., pressure vessels, electrical) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A PHA team must be **multi-disciplinary**. A single-expert PHA is invalid — this is a frequent 2-mark question.

---

## 2.4 Step-by-Step PHA Procedure (Standard Methodology)

The standard PHA procedure consists of **seven systematic steps**:

### **Step 1 — Define the System Boundaries and Study Scope**

Establish:
- Physical boundaries (what's in / what's out)
- Operational boundaries (modes, mission phases)
- Temporal boundaries (lifecycle phases: design → manufacture → operation → decommission)
- Interface boundaries (with other systems, environment, personnel)

### **Step 2 — Identify Hazards**

For each system element (component, subsystem, interface), ask the brainstorming questions:
- What energy sources are present? (electrical, thermal, mechanical, chemical, radiation, kinetic, potential)
- What materials are present? (flammables, toxics, reactives, cryogenics)
- What interfaces exist with personnel? (operator actions, maintenance tasks)
- What interfaces exist with environment? (weather, terrain, neighbours)
- What failure modes are reasonably foreseeable?

### **Step 3 — Identify Causes (Trigger Events)**

For each hazard, list all credible initiating events that could release the hazard.

### **Step 4 — Assess Effects/Consequences**

Determine the worst-credible effect on:
- Personnel (injury, fatality)
- Equipment (damage, loss)
- Environment (release, contamination)
- Mission (loss of capability)

### **Step 5 — Classify Severity**

Assign a severity category (typically using MIL-STD-882E four-level scale).

### **Step 6 — Assign Initial Probability (Qualitative)**

Assign a qualitative probability level based on engineering judgment, historical data, or preliminary FTA results.

### **Step 7 — Determine Risk Category / Hazard Risk Acceptability and Recommend Actions**

Plot hazard on a **Risk Assessment Matrix** and recommend:
- Eliminate the hazard (design change)
- Reduce severity (engineering controls)
- Reduce probability (safeguards, redundancy)
- Accept the residual risk (with justification)

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Reference Table for KTU 2024 Board Examination**

| # | Concept | Formula / Scale | Notation / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Index (Qualitative)** | $RI = P \times C$ | $P$ = Probability Level, $C$ = Consequence Severity (categorical) |
| 2 | **Risk Index (Quantitative)** | $RI = f \times C$ | $f$ = Frequency (events/year), $C$ = Consequence (deaths, $/year) |
| 3 | **Hazard Severity (MIL-STD-882E)** | $I \rightarrow IV$ | I = Catastrophic, II = Critical, III = Marginal, IV = Negligible |
| 4 | **Probability Level (MIL-STD-882E)** | $A \rightarrow D$ | A=Frequent, B=Probable, C=Occasional, D=Remote, E=Improbable, F=Eliminated |
| 5 | **Risk Acceptance Criterion** | $RI_{actual} \leq RI_{acceptable}$ | — |
| 6 | **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** | $\text{Cost of further risk reduction} \not\gg \text{Risk reduction benefit}$ | — |
| 7 | **Hazard Rate (λ) Relation** | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | $\lambda$ = failures/hr, MTBF in hours |
| 8 | **Probability from Failure Rate (Poisson)** | $P(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | $P(t)$ = probability of failure in time $t$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Syntax Safeguard:** Vertical bars in formulas (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are rendered using `\vert` to prevent breaking the table. Students should do the same in handwritten answer sheets.

---

## 2.6 Standard Severity Classification (MIL-STD-882E)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus adopts the **four-level severity scale** of MIL-STD-882E. This is the most commonly tested table in board exams.

| Hazard Severity Category | Description | Consequence to People / Equipment / Mission |
|---|---|---|
| **I — Catastrophic** | Could result in death, permanent total disability, loss exceeding **₹10 crore**, mission loss | Fatality, total system loss, irreversible environmental damage |
| **II — Critical** | Could result in severe injury, occupational illness, loss **₹1–10 crore**, mission degradation | Major injury, major subsystem damage, reversible environmental damage |
| **III — Marginal** | Could result in minor injury, minor illness, loss **₹10 lakh–1 crore**, mission impact minor | First-aid injury, minor repair, localised environmental impact |
| **IV — Negligible** | Could result in less than minor injury, loss less than **₹10 lakh** | No injury, cosmetic damage, no environmental impact |

## 2.7 Standard Probability Classification (MIL-STD-882E)

| Probability Level | Description | Frequency of Occurrence |
|---|---|---|
| **A — Frequent** | Likely to occur frequently | Continuously experienced |
| **B — Probable** | Will occur several times in lifecycle | Will occur frequently |
| **C — Occasional** | Will occur some time in lifecycle | Will occur several times |
| **D — Remote** | Unlikely but possible to occur in lifecycle | Unlikely but possible |
| **E — Improbable** | So unlikely, assumed occurrence not conceivable | Practically impossible |
| **F — Eliminated** | Cannot occur (inherently impossible by design) | Will not occur |

---

## 2.8 Risk Assessment Matrix (RAM)

The Risk Assessment Matrix is the central deliverable of any PHA study. It maps **Severity (rows)** against **Probability (columns)** to yield a **Hazard Risk Category**:

| Severity ↓ \ Probability → | A (Frequent) | B (Probable) | C (Occasional) | D (Remote) | E (Improbable) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I — Catastrophic** | 1A | 1B | 1C | 1D | 1E |
| **II — Critical** | 2A | 2B | 2C | 2D | 2E |
| **III — Marginal** | 3A | 3B | 3C | 3D | 3E |
| **IV — Negligible** | 4A | 4B | 4C | 4D | 4E |

> **Hazard Risk Acceptance Guide (Typical KTU Expectation):**
> - Categories **1A, 1B, 1C** → **Unacceptable** — Must be eliminated or mitigated by design.
> - Categories **2A, 2B, 1D, 2C** → **Undesirable** — Mitigation required; senior management review.
> - Categories **2D, 3A, 3B, 3C** → **Acceptable with controls** — ALARP must be demonstrated.
> - Categories **3D, 4A, 4B, 4C, 4D, all E** → **Acceptable** — No further action.

---

## 2.9 Engineering Utility of PHA in Real-World Industries

- **Aerospace & Defence** — Mandatory per MIL-STD-882E for all weapon system acquisitions.
- **Oil & Gas / Petrochemical** — Supports HSE Case development per IEC 61511 and OSHA PSM.
- **Pharmaceutical** — Supports Process Safety Management (PSM) baseline.
- **Nuclear** — Supports Preliminary Safety Analysis Report (PSAR).
- **Automotive / ISO 26262** — Supports Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment (HARA).
- **Railway (EN 50126)** — Supports Preliminary Hazard Log creation.

## 2.10 Limitations of PHA

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Note:** Examiners frequently ask about PHA limitations. Memorize at least four.

1. **Qualitative in nature** — Does not yield numerical risk values.
2. **Dependent on team expertise** — Inexperienced teams may miss hazards.
3. **Cannot handle complex multi-failure scenarios** — Use FTA/ETA for these.
4. **Highly dependent on design maturity** — Incomplete design leads to incomplete PHA.
5. **No dependency / common-cause modelling** — Use more advanced methods.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Qualitative Risk Index Formula

The fundamental risk equation used in PHA is the simple multiplicative risk index:

$$RI = P \times C$$

Where:

$$P = \text{Probability of hazard occurrence (categorical level)}$$

$$C = \text{Consequence severity (categorical level)}$$

### 3.1.1 Numerical Encoding of Categorical Levels

For mathematical treatment during quantitative PHA extensions, the categorical levels are encoded numerically:

| Probability Level | Numerical Code ($p_i$) |
|---|---|
| A — Frequent | 5 |
| B — Probable | 4 |
| C — Occasional | 3 |
| D — Remote | 2 |
| E — Improbable | 1 |
| F — Eliminated | 0 |

| Severity Level | Numerical Code ($c_j$) |
|---|---|
| I — Catastrophic | 4 |
| II — Critical | 3 |
| III — Marginal | 2 |
| IV — Negligible | 1 |

### 3.1.2 Calculation of Risk Index

The risk index for each hazard is computed as:

$$RI_{ij} = p_i \times c_j$$

For example, a **Catastrophic (I, $c = 4$)** hazard with **Occasional (C, $p = 3$)** probability yields:

$$RI = 3 \times 4 = 12$$

Categorization convention:
- $RI \geq 15$ → Risk Category 1 (Unacceptable — Must eliminate)
- $8 \leq RI < 15$ → Risk Category 2 (Undesirable — Mitigate)
- $3 \leq RI < 8$ → Risk Category 3 (Acceptable with controls — ALARP)
- $RI < 3$ → Risk Category 4 (Acceptable)

> [!NOTE]
> This numerical encoding is a **teaching aid** for Module 2 of PECHT754 — actual industrial PHA uses the matrix without numeric encoding.

---

## 3.2 Extension of PHA to Quantitative Frequency × Consequence Risk

For an industrial plant, when **historical failure data** is available, PHA can be extended quantitatively:

$$R = f \times C$$

Where:
- $f$ = frequency of hazard realization (events/year)
- $C$ = consequence magnitude (deaths/year, ₹/year, environmental impact units/year)

If hazard occurs with **Poisson frequency** $\lambda$, and consequence per event is $c$:

$$R = \lambda \cdot c$$

Given:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$$

The annual risk is:

$$R = \dfrac{c}{MTBF}$$

### Worked Numerical Example — Pressure Vessel Hazard

> **Problem:** A pressure vessel develops a leak with frequency $\lambda = 10^{-4}$ /year. Each leak event causes an average loss of ₹5,00,000. Compute the annual risk.

**Solution:**

$$R = \lambda \times c$$

$$R = 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^{5}$$

$$R = 50 \; \text{₹/year}$$

If the company's **risk acceptance criterion** is $R_{acc} = 100$ ₹/year, then:

$$R = 50 < 100 = R_{acc} \implies \text{Acceptable}$$

If instead each leak caused ₹10 crore loss:

$$R = 10^{-4} \times 10^{7} = 1000 \; \text{₹/year} > R_{acc}$$

So mitigation would be required.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Analytical PHA Worked Example — Domestic LPG Cylinder System

**System:** 14.2 kg domestic LPG cylinder with regulator and stove.

**Scope:** Storage, transport, installation, and use.

### Step 1 — Identify Hazards

| H# | Hazard |
|---|---|
| H1 | LPG leakage from cylinder valve |
| H2 | LPG leakage from regulator diaphragm rupture |
| H3 | Hose rupture between regulator and stove |
| H4 | Stove knob left in "open" position with no flame |
| H5 | Cylinder exposed to fire / heat source |
| H6 | Cylinder dropped during handling |

### Step 2–4 — Causes, Effects, Severity

| H# | Cause | Effect | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Valve gland packing failure | Flash fire near cylinder | II (Critical) |
| H2 | Diaphragm aging tear | Gas leak in kitchen | II (Critical) |
| H3 | Rubber hose degradation | Jet fire near stove | II (Critical) |
| H4 | User error | Flashback / explosion | I (Catastrophic) |
| H5 | External fire exposure | BLEVE / explosion | I (Catastrophic) |
| H6 | Improper handling | Cylinder damage, possible leak | III (Marginal) |

### Step 5–6 — Probability Assignment

| H# | Severity | Probability | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | II | D (Remote) | Rare valve failures; periodic replacement |
| H2 | II | C (Occasional) | Diaphragm wear observed in service |
| H3 | II | D (Remote) | Periodic hose replacement mandatory |
| H4 | I | D (Remote) | User error possible |
| H5 | I | E (Improbable) | Extremely rare household scenario |
| H6 | III | C (Occasional) | Dropped during delivery |

### Step 7 — Risk Categorization & Recommendations

| H# | Severity × Probability | Risk Cat | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | II × D | 2D | Replace valve every 5 yrs; leak-test annually |
| H2 | II × C | 2C | Replace regulator every 3 yrs; safety device |
| H3 | II × D | 2D | Use wire-braided hose; replace every 3 yrs |
| H4 | I × D | 1D | Add flame-failure device; user training |
| H5 | I × E | 1E | Awareness campaigns; storage away from heat |
| H6 | III × C | 3C | Training delivery personnel |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Define Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA). State any two limitations of PHA.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):**
> Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) is a structured, inductive hazard identification and risk evaluation methodology performed during the **earliest conceptual and preliminary design stages** of a system, facility, or process. It identifies potential hazards, their causes and effects, classifies them by severity and probability, and assigns initial risk acceptance categories. It serves as the foundational screening study that feeds subsequent detailed analyses (HAZOP, FMEA, FTA).

> **Limitations — any two (1 Mark):**
> 1. It is **qualitative** — does not yield numerical risk values for cost-benefit optimization.
> 2. It is heavily **dependent on team expertise**; an inexperienced team may miss critical hazards.
> 3. It **cannot handle complex multi-failure scenarios**; for these, FTA or ETA must be used.
> 4. It is **highly dependent on design maturity**; an incomplete P&ID leads to an incomplete PHA.

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between PHA and HAZOP on the basis of (i) stage of application, (ii) methodology, and (iii) output.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per row):**

| Criterion | PHA | HAZOP |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Stage of Application** | Performed at **conceptual / preliminary design** stage | Performed at **detailed design stage** (P&ID available) |
| **(ii) Methodology** | **Inductive / brainstorming** by multidisciplinary team using guide words & checklists | **Systematic deviation analysis** using guide words (No/Not, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than) on every node |
| **(iii) Output** | **Hazard log + risk categorization matrix** with initial severity/probability classification | **Node-by-node deviation table** with causes, consequences, safeguards, and actions |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer **ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure** for conducting a Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the **LPG storage and distribution system** in an industrial canteen (200 kg bulk tank, vaporizer, distribution manifold, cooking ring lines), carry out a **Preliminary Hazard Analysis** and present the results in a standard PHA worksheet. Identify at least **five distinct hazards**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Step-by-Step PHA Procedure (7 Marks — 1 Mark per step, 1 Mark for introduction):**

> **Introduction (1 Mark):**
> PHA is a structured, inductive hazard identification technique performed in the conceptual and preliminary design phases. It is recommended by MIL-STD-882E and IEC 61882.

> **Step 1 — Define System Boundaries and Scope (1 Mark):**
> The PHA team first establishes physical boundaries (tank, vaporizer, manifold, lines), operational boundaries (normal operation, maintenance, emergency), and lifecycle boundaries (installation, operation, decommission).

> **Step 2 — Identify Hazards (1 Mark):**
> Using brainstorming, checklists, and energy/material inventories, the team lists every foreseeable hazard. Tools used include: energy analysis (electrical, thermal, mechanical, chemical, radiation, kinetic, potential); material hazard classification; interface analysis with personnel and environment; historical accident data review.

> **Step 3 — Identify Causes for Each Hazard (1 Mark):**
> For each hazard, the team identifies all credible initiating events that could release the hazard (e.g., gasket failure, valve wear, overpressure, human error, external impact).

> **Step 4 — Assess Effects/Consequences (1 Mark):**
> The team determines the worst-credible effect of each hazard on personnel (injury/fatality), equipment (damage/loss), environment (release), and mission (service interruption).

> **Step 5 — Classify Severity (1 Mark):**
> Each effect is classified using the four-level MIL-STD-882E severity scale:
> - I — Catastrophic (death, ₹10 crore loss)
> - II — Critical (severe injury, ₹1–10 crore loss)
> - III — Marginal (minor injury, ₹10 lakh–1 crore loss)
> - IV — Negligible (less than minor, < ₹10 lakh loss)

> **Step 6 — Assign Probability (1 Mark):**
> A qualitative probability level (A — Frequent through E — Improbable, F — Eliminated) is assigned based on engineering judgment, historical data, or preliminary fault-tree estimates.

> **Step 7 — Determine Risk Category and Recommend Actions (1 Mark):**
> Each hazard is plotted on the Risk Assessment Matrix. Categories 1A–1C are unacceptable (must eliminate); 2A–2D are undesirable (must mitigate); 3A–3D are ALARP; 4A–4E are acceptable. The PHA worksheet is documented as a deliverable, and outputs feed into HAZOP, FMEA, and FTA.

---

**(b) PHA Worksheet for LPG Canteen System (7 Marks — full worksheet):**

> **System:** 200 kg LPG bulk tank → Vaporizer → Distribution manifold → Cooking ring branch lines.

| H# | Hazard | Cause | Effect | Severity | Probability | Risk Cat | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Bulk tank LPG leak | Corrosion, valve gland failure, impact damage | Flash fire / explosion near tank farm | I (Catastrophic) | D (Remote) | 1D — Undesirable | Install ESDV with remote isolation, gas detection, bund wall |
| H2 | Vaporizer over-temperature | Thermostat failure, low water supply | LPG liquid carry-over to manifold → flashback | I (Catastrophic) | E (Improbable) | 1E — Undesirable | Install high-temperature cutoff, redundant thermostat, water-flow interlock |
| H3 | Manifold over-pressure | Blocked line, thermal expansion | Hose rupture, jet fire | II (Critical) | D (Remote) | 2D — ALARP | Install pressure relief valve, pressure transmitter with alarm |
| H4 | Branch line LPG leak at cooking ring | Hose degradation, loose ferrule | Kitchen flash fire | II (Critical) | C (Occasional) | 2C — Undesirable | Use wire-braided hose, replace every 3 yrs, leak-test quarterly |
| H5 | Worker error during cylinder/tank valve operation | Inadequate training, fatigue | Uncontrolled release | II (Critical) | D (Remote) | 2D — ALARP | Mandatory competency certification, SOP signage, buddy system |
| H6 | Cold ambient temperature | Vaporizer icing | LPG starvation → flame failure → gas accumulation | III (Marginal) | C (Occasional) | 3C — ALARP | Trace heating, insulation, install flame-failure devices on all burners |
| H7 | External vehicle impact on tank | Parking violation, driver error | Mechanical damage to bulk tank → major leak | I (Catastrophic) | E (Improbable) | 1E — Undesirable | Crash barriers, signage, restricted vehicle access zone |

> **Conclusion (for credit):**
> Highest risk hazards (H1, H2, H7) all fall in Risk Category 1 and require mandatory engineering controls (ESDVs, gas detection, crash barriers). The PHA outputs now form the basis for detailed HAZOP at the P&ID stage and FMEA on critical components.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 — Alternative]

**(a)** Describe the **four severity categories** and the **five probability levels** of the **MIL-STD-882E hazard classification system**. Construct a **Risk Assessment Matrix** and explain its use in PHA. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **chlorine storage facility** has a probability of $10^{-5}$ per year of a major release that could affect a populated area. Each event could cause **5 fatalities**. The company's acceptable individual risk is $10^{-6}$ per year. Determine whether the risk is acceptable. If not, suggest **two engineering mitigation measures** with reasoning. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a) MIL-STD-882E Classification System (7 Marks):**

> **Severity Categories (2 Marks):**
>
> | Category | Description |
> |---|---|
> | **I — Catastrophic** | Could result in death, permanent total disability, loss exceeding ₹10 crore, mission loss |
> | **II — Critical** | Could result in severe injury, occupational illness, loss ₹1–10 crore, mission degradation |
> | **III — Marginal** | Could result in minor injury, minor illness, loss ₹10 lakh–1 crore |
> | **IV — Negligible** | Could result in less than minor injury, loss < ₹10 lakh |

> **Probability Levels (2 Marks):**
>
> | Level | Description |
> |---|---|
> | **A — Frequent** | Likely to occur frequently |
> | **B — Probable** | Will occur several times in system lifecycle |
> | **C — Occasional** | Will occur some time in lifecycle |
> | **D — Remote** | Unlikely but possible in lifecycle |
> | **E — Improbable** | So unlikely as to assume non-occurrence |
> | **F — Eliminated** | Inherently impossible by design |

> **Risk Assessment Matrix (RAM) and Use (3 Marks):**
> The RAM is a 4×5 (or 4×6 including F) grid with severity rows and probability columns. Each cell (e.g., 1A, 2C) defines a **Hazard Risk Category (HRC)** and dictates an **action level**:
> - **HRC 1 (cells 1A, 1B, 1C)** → Unacceptable — design must eliminate.
> - **HRC 2 (cells 1D, 1E, 2A, 2B, 2C)** → Undesirable — mitigation mandatory, senior management review.
> - **HRC 3 (cells 2D, 2E, 3A, 3B, 3C)** → Acceptable with controls — apply ALARP.
> - **HRC 4 (cells 3D, 3E, 4A–4E)** → Acceptable.
>
> **Use in PHA:** After assigning Severity and Probability to each hazard, the cell location on the RAM directly tells the PHA team what level of mitigation is required and whether the design must be modified.

---

**(b) Quantitative Risk Acceptance Problem (7 Marks):**

> **Given:**
> - Frequency of major release: $f = 10^{-5}$ /year
> - Fatalities per event: $c = 5$
> - Acceptable individual risk: $IR_{acc} = 10^{-6}$ /year

> **Step 1 — Compute Annual Individual Risk (2 Marks):**
> $$IR = f \times c$$
> $$IR = 10^{-5} \times 5 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{fatalities/year}$$

> **Step 2 — Compare with Acceptance Criterion (1 Mark):**
> $$IR = 5 \times 10^{-5} > IR_{acc} = 10^{-6}$$
> $$\text{Hence, the risk is \textbf{NOT acceptable}.}$$

> **Step 3 — Risk Reduction Factor Required (1 Mark):**
> $$\text{RRR} = \dfrac{IR}{IR_{acc}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-6}} = 50$$
> The risk must be reduced by a factor of **50**.

> **Step 4 — Two Engineering Mitigation Measures (3 Marks — 1.5 each):**

> **Measure 1 — Secondary Containment (Bund Wall + Double-Wall Tank):**
> A double-walled tank with leak-detection sensors can reduce the probability of a major release (loss of containment from full inventory) to $10^{-7}$ /year or less. If leak detection shuts down transfer pumps automatically, the actual released quantity per event is also reduced, lowering $c$. Combined effect: $IR$ drops from $5 \times 10^{-5}$ to below $10^{-6}$.

> **Measure 2 — Chlorine Absorption / Scrubbing Neutralization System:**
> Installation of a caustic-soda scrubber (neutralization system) at the storage vent ensures any released chlorine is absorbed before reaching populated areas. This reduces the effective consequence $c$ (e.g., from 5 fatalities to 0), reducing the societal risk without changing $f$.

> **Conclusion:**
> The facility's current risk exceeds the acceptable individual risk criterion by a factor of 50. Implementation of secondary containment **and** a neutralization scrubber, combined with procedural controls, brings the residual risk into the ALARP / acceptable region.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating PHA with HAZOP** — Board examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if the student fails to clarify the design-stage distinction.
> 2. **Missing the Risk Matrix** — A PHA answer without the Risk Assessment Matrix loses **3 marks minimum**.
> 3. **No Mitigation Recommendations** — The PHA is incomplete without action recommendations; expect **2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Forgetting Team Composition** — A single-engineer PHA is methodologically invalid; examiners look for **multi-disciplinary team**.
> 5. **Wrong Severity/Pobability Encoding** — Students often swap severity and probability axes on the RAM; this loses **2 marks**.
> 6. **Quantitative Section Errors** — Confusing individual risk $IR = f \times c$ with societal risk $F$-$N$ curve is a **frequent 2-mark loss** in 14-mark answers.
> 7. **No Documentation Reference** — Citing MIL-STD-882E or IEC 61882 earns **1 extra mark** (good practice).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bulleted Summary (Print and Revise Before Exam):**

- **PHA** = Preliminary Hazard Analysis — an **inductive**, **qualitative**, **early-stage** hazard identification methodology.
- PHA was developed by the **U.S. Air Force** in the 1970s; codified in **MIL-STD-882** (current revision E).
- Also standardized in **IEC 61882** (Hazard and Operability Studies Application Guide).
- **Purpose:** Identify hazards early, classify severity and probability, recommend mitigations, feed downstream analyses (HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, ETA, LOPA).
- **Inputs:** System description, ConOps, schematics, codes/standards, historical accident data.
- **Team:** Multidisciplinary — facilitator, design engineer, operations, safety, maintenance, SMEs.
- **Procedure:** 7 steps — Define scope → Identify hazards → Identify causes → Assess effects → Classify severity → Assign probability → Categorize risk and recommend.
- **Severity (MIL-STD-882E):** I Catastrophic, II Critical, III Marginal, IV Negligible.
- **Probability (MIL-STD-882E):** A Frequent, B Probable, C Occasional, D Remote, E Improbable, F Eliminated.
- **Risk Index Formula:** $RI = P \times C$ (qualitative) or $R = f \times C$ (quantitative).
- **Risk Assessment Matrix:** 4 × 5 grid mapping Severity × Probability to Hazard Risk Category.
- **Risk Acceptance:** 1A–1C Unacceptable → must eliminate; 2A–2D Undesirable → must mitigate; 3A–3D ALARP; 4A–4E Acceptable.
- **Numerical encoding** (teaching aid): $p_i \in \{5,4,3,2,1,0\}$, $c_j \in \{4,3,2,1\}$; $RI_{ij} = p_i \times c_j$.
- **Deliverables:** PHA Worksheet (tabular), Hazard Log, Risk Categorization Matrix, Mitigation Recommendations.
- **Limitations:** Qualitative, team-skill-dependent, cannot handle multi-failure scenarios, depends on design maturity, no common-cause analysis.
- **Real-world use:** Aerospace (MIL-STD-882), Oil & Gas (PSM), Nuclear (PSAR), Automotive (ISO 26262 HARA), Rail (EN 50126 Hazard Log).
- **Common pitfall answer trap:** PHA is **not** HAZOP — students lose marks by mixing the two.
- **Quantitative extension:** $R = \lambda \cdot c$ where $\lambda = 1/MTBF$, $c$ = consequence/event.
- **Frequency–Probability link:** $P(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ for Poisson process.
- **PHA feeds into:** HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, ETA, LOPA — never the other way around.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HAZOP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** HAZOP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of HAZOP

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** is a structured, systematic, and rigorous **qualitative risk analysis technique** used to identify potential hazards and operability problems throughout a process plant by a multidisciplinary team. The methodology is based on the premise that **accidents in process industries are caused by deviations from the intended design intent**, and these deviations are uncovered by applying standardized **Guide Words** to study nodes.

**Historical Origin (ICE, UK — 1963):**
HAZOP was originally developed by the **Chemical Industries Association (CIA)** in collaboration with the **Institute of Chemical Engineers (IChemE)** in the United Kingdom. It was codified in the **IEC 61882:2016** international standard titled *"Hazard and operability studies (HAZOP studies) – Application guide."*

**Core Definition (as per IEC 61882:2016):**
> *"HAZOP is a structured and systematic technique for examining a defined system, using a series of guide words to identify potential deviations from the design intent and their causes and consequences."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "What-If Detective" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are a **detective investigating a crime that has not happened yet** at a chemical plant. You stand at each pipe, valve, and vessel, and systematically ask the same set of seven "suspicious" questions — *"What if there is NO flow? What if there is TOO MUCH flow? What if the flow is REVERSED?"* etc. Each question forces the team to imagine how a deviation could occur, what damage it could cause, and what safeguards are needed. That is HAZOP.

In simpler terms, HAZOP treats the plant as a **story being read sentence by sentence**. Each "sentence" is a section of pipe or a vessel (a *node*). The team applies *guide words* (like "No", "More", "Less", "Reverse") to each *parameter* (flow, pressure, temperature) to hunt for deviations before the plant is even built.

---

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary Anchors

| KTU Term | Symbolic / Standard Notation | Quick Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|
| **Guide Word** | $GW \in \{NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS\ WELL\ AS, PART\ OF, OTHER\ THAN\}$ | "The Seven Question Words" |
| **Parameter** | $P \in \{Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition\}$ | "Physical Properties" |
| **Node** | $N$ — A study point in the process | "The Detective's Stops" |
| **Deviation** | $D = GW \oplus P$ | "What can go wrong?" |
| **Cause** | $C$ — Root reason of deviation | "Why did it happen?" |
| **Consequence** | $K$ — Resulting harm | "What damage occurred?" |
| **Safeguard** | $S$ — Existing protection layer | "What stops the bad outcome?" |
| **Action / Recommendation** | $R$ — New protective measure | "What must we add?" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of HAZOP:** The deviation $D$ is the **mathematical (semantic) combination** of a Guide Word and a Parameter, expressed as: $D = GW \oplus P$. For example, $D = \text{NOT} \oplus \text{Flow} = \text{No Flow}$.

---

## 1.4 Classification of HAZOP in the Risk Matrix

$$\text{HAZOP} \subset \text{Qualitative Methods} \subset \text{Process Safety Analysis (PSA)}$$

HAZOP is the **most widely adopted qualitative technique** globally because it is:
- **Brainstorming-driven** (creative team-based)
- **Documentation-friendly** (worksheets)
- **Comprehensive** (examines all nodes sequentially)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Standard Guide Words (IEC 61882:2016)

| S.No. | Guide Word | Meaning | Physical Interpretation |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **NO / NOT** | Negation of design intent | Complete absence (e.g., No Flow, No Pressure) |
| 2 | **MORE** | Quantitative increase | Higher than intended (e.g., More Pressure, More Temperature) |
| 3 | **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | Lower than intended (e.g., Less Flow, Less Level) |
| 4 | **REVERSE** | Logical opposite | Flow in opposite direction (e.g., Reverse Flow) |
| 5 | **AS WELL AS** | Qualitative increase | Additional component present (e.g., Contaminants in stream) |
| 6 | **PART OF** | Qualitative decrease | Missing component (e.g., Lower concentration) |
| 7 | **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution | Completely different event (e.g., Maintenance, Sampling) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The above seven guide words are the ONLY standard ones.** Some KTU papers accept minor variations such as "MORE OF" and "LESS OF" — these are equivalent to "MORE" and "LESS" respectively.

---

## 2.2 Standard Process Parameters

The five **primary** parameters studied are:
1. **Flow** (mass or volumetric)
2. **Pressure**
3. **Temperature**
4. **Level** (in vessels)
5. **Composition** / **Phase** / **Reaction**

Secondary parameters (used when needed): **Time, Sequence, Mixing, Viscosity, Concentration, pH**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Logical Expression | KTU Board Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Deviation Definition** | $D = GW \oplus P$ | Foundation formula — frequently asked |
| 2 | **Risk Ranking (Qualitative)** | $R = S \times L$ | Where $S$ = Severity, $L$ = Likelihood |
| 3 | **Risk Matrix Categories** | $R \in \{Low,\ Medium,\ High,\ Extreme\}$ | Used to rank consequences |
| 4 | **HAZOP Study Time Estimate** | $T \approx 2 \text{ to } 8 \text{ days}$ for 100 nodes | Empirical ICE guideline |
| 5 | **Team Size (Optimal)** | $N_{team} = 5 \text{ to } 8$ persons | Productivity vs. coordination rule |
| 6 | **Node Selection Rule** | Apply $GW$ to each $P$ at each $N$ | Total combinations $\Sigma = N \times P \times GW$ |
| 7 | **Frequency of Study** | At 4 project stages: Design, Construction, Commissioning, Operation | Per IEC 61882 |
| 8 | **Cause-Consequence Link** | $C \rightarrow D \rightarrow K$ | Linear hazard chain |
| 9 | **Safeguard Hierarchy** | $S = S_1 + S_2 + S_3 + S_4$ | (Inherent → Passive → Active → Procedural) |
| 10 | **LOPA Escalation Factor** | $f_{LOPA} = 10$ per independent layer | Used in semi-quantitative HAZOP |

---

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic of a HAZOP Study (Operational Algorithm)

A HAZOP study follows a **structured 6-step procedure** mandated by IEC 61882:2016:

**Step 1 — Define Scope and Objectives**
Identify the boundaries of the study (which plant section, what operational mode). Clearly state what is *in scope* and what is *out of scope*. **Why?** To prevent time-wasting on irrelevant lines.

**Step 2 — Assemble the Multidisciplinary Team**
The team MUST include:
- Team Leader (HAZOP-trained, experienced — usually a process safety specialist)
- Process Engineer
- Instrumentation/Control Engineer
- Operations/Maintenance Representative
- Safety Engineer
- (Optional) Project Manager, Commissioning Engineer

**How to remember:** "The 5-Whys Team" — never fewer than 5.

**Step 3 — Gather Information (Documentation Phase)**
Required documents:
- **P&ID (Piping and Instrumentation Diagram)** — the *primary* working document
- **PFD (Process Flow Diagram)**
- **Operating Procedures**
- **Cause & Effect Matrix**
- **Equipment Data Sheets**
- **Layout Drawings**
- **Hazardous Area Classification**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The P&ID is to a HAZOP team what a **road map** is to a navigator. Without it, the study cannot proceed.

**Step 4 — Divide the Plant into Study Nodes (Sections)**
A *node* is a **discrete section of pipe or equipment** with a defined design intent. The team draws "**Nodes**" on the P&ID and assigns a node number (e.g., N1, N2, N3…). Boundaries occur at:
- Major equipment (vessels, exchangers, pumps)
- Control loops
- Branching points
- Property changes (phase, composition, pressure)

**Step 5 — Apply Guide Words to Each Parameter at Each Node**
For each node, for each parameter, every applicable guide word is applied. The result is a *deviation* $D$. The team asks:
1. **What could cause this deviation?** ($C$ — Cause)
2. **What would be the consequence?** ($K$ — Consequence)
3. **What safeguards exist?** ($S$)
4. **What action is recommended?** ($R$)

**Step 6 — Record, Document, and Follow-up**
Every discussion is captured in a **HAZOP Worksheet** (typically in tabular form with columns: Node, Parameter, Guide Word, Deviation, Causes, Consequences, Safeguards, Action, Responsibility).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Specific HAZOP Application |
|:---|:---|
| **Oil & Gas** | Refinery distillation columns, offshore platforms |
| **Petrochemicals** | Ethylene cracking units, polymerization reactors |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Batch reactors, solvent recovery systems |
| **Nuclear** | Coolant loops, spent fuel handling |
| **Power Generation** | Boiler feedwater, turbine lube oil systems |
| **Water Treatment** | Chlorine dosing, filtration systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fun fact:** HAZOP is **legally mandated** in the UK under the **COMAH (Control of Major Accident Hazards) Regulations 2015** for any site storing dangerous substances above threshold quantities. In India, similar requirements exist under the **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC, 1989)** and the **Factories Act 1948 (Section 41A — Safety Audit)**.

---

## 2.6 Advantages and Limitations (High-Yield KTU Content)

### Advantages
- Highly **systematic** — leaves little room for oversight
- **Team-based** — leverages collective expertise
- **Documentation-friendly** — provides audit trail
- Identifies **both safety AND operability** issues
- Applicable to **continuous AND batch** processes
- Can be applied at **any lifecycle stage**

### Limitations
- **Time-intensive** and expensive
- Requires **trained, experienced team**
- **Quality depends on team composition**
- Does **not address human factors** comprehensively
- May not identify **all rare/multi-failure events** → use LOPA/FTA
- **Cannot quantify** failure probabilities — qualitative only
- Difficult for **complex software/control systems** (HYSOP may be used)

---

## 2.7 Comparison with Other Risk Assessment Methods

| Method | Type | Quantitative? | Best For | Limitation |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **HAZOP** | Qualitative | No | Process deviations | Time-consuming |
| **FMEA** | Qualitative | No | Equipment failure | Doesn't capture interaction |
| **FTA** | Quantitative | Yes | Complex event trees | Requires failure data |
| **ETA** | Quantitative | Yes | Sequential accidents | Complex for large systems |
| **LOPA** | Semi-quantitative | Partial | Layer of protection | Less detailed than HAZOP |
| **What-If** | Qualitative | No | Early stage design | Less systematic |
| **Checklist** | Qualitative | No | Compliance audit | Only finds known issues |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worksheets & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: HAZOP on a Pump-Delivery System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the "**standard KTU 14-mark question**" type. Students should be able to perform a HAZOP on a simple system (e.g., a pump delivering fluid to a storage tank) covering at least 2 nodes and 4 guide words.

### System Description (Given in the KTU Question)

A centrifugal pump delivers benzene from a storage tank **T-101** through a delivery line containing:
- A **suction strainer (S-101)**
- A **centrifugal pump (P-101)**
- A **delivery valve (V-101)** — manual
- A **flow indicator (FI-101)**
- A **check valve (CV-101)** — non-return
- A **pressure indicator (PI-101)**
- Delivers to a **vessel V-201** at higher elevation

The pump has a discharge relief valve set at **110% of design pressure** and a low-flow alarm at **10% of design flow**.

### Step 1: Define the Design Intent

$$\text{Design Intent: "Pump benzene from T-101 to V-201 at } 50 \text{ m}^3/\text{h, 4 bar(g), 30}^\circ\text{C"}$$

### Step 2: Identify the Nodes

$$\text{Node 1 (N1): Suction side — from T-101 to pump P-101 inlet}$$

$$\text{Node 2 (N2): Discharge side — from P-101 to V-201 inlet}$$

### Step 3: HAZOP Worksheet (Analytical Matrix)

The following table is a **standard KTU 14-mark answer format**. It is reproduced exhaustively below with all required columns.

| Node | Parameter | Guide Word | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Safeguards | Actions |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **N1** | Flow | NO | No Flow | (1) Strainers blocked by debris; (2) Suction line leak; (3) Pump impeller damaged; (4) Valve V-101 closed | Pump cavitation → seal failure; Tank V-201 emptied → process stop | Low-flow alarm (FI-101); Pump motor trip on low suction pressure | Install **dual strainers**; Add **suction pressure low-low trip** |
| **N1** | Flow | LESS | Low Flow | (1) Partial strainer blockage; (2) V-101 partially closed; (3) Cavitation | Reduced throughput → downstream process upset | Flow indicator; Operator monitoring | Add **flow low-low alarm** with auto shutdown |
| **N1** | Pressure | LESS | Low Suction Pressure | (1) Suction line leak; (2) Tank T-101 level low; (3) Vapour lock (benzene vapour pressure at 30°C) | Pump cavitation → impeller damage | Suction pressure gauge; Operator inspection | Add **local level transmitter** with low-level interlock |
| **N1** | Pressure | MORE | High Suction Pressure | (1) Thermal expansion of liquid in suction line; (2) Blockage downstream | Not credible in normal operation — disregarded | — | — |
| **N1** | Temperature | MORE | High Temperature | (1) Sun-heating of T-101; (2) Pump friction overheating | Vapour lock; Cavitation | Temperature indicator on tank | Add **high-temperature alarm**; Provide **vapour return line** |
| **N1** | Level | LESS | Low Tank Level | (1) Outflow exceeds inflow; (2) Instrumentation failure | Pump runs dry → seal damage, fire hazard | Local level gauge; Operator walk-around | Install **LIT (Level Indicating Transmitter)** with **low-low interlock** |
| **N2** | Flow | NO | No Flow | (1) V-101 closed; (2) Check valve CV-101 stuck; (3) Line blockage | Pressure rises → relief valve lifts → benzene release | Pressure relief valve; High-pressure alarm | Add **flow indicator at FI-101** with low-low trip |
| **N2** | Flow | LESS | Low Flow | (1) V-101 partially closed; (2) Line partially blocked | Process upset; Pump overheating | Flow indicator | Periodic **preventive maintenance** of strainer |
| **N2** | Flow | REVERSE | Reverse Flow | (1) V-201 pressure higher than pump discharge; (2) Pump stops, CV-101 fails | Backflow → pump reverse rotation → impeller damage | Check valve CV-101 | Install **dual check valves**; Add **RPS (Reverse Power Switch)** |
| **N2** | Flow | MORE | High Flow | (1) V-101 stuck open; (2) Pump over-speed | Pipe erosion; V-201 overflow; Overpressure downstream | Flow indicator; Local gauge | Add **flow control valve (FCV)** with high-flow alarm |
| **N2** | Pressure | MORE | High Pressure | (1) V-101 closed while pump running; (2) Blockage in line; (3) Thermal expansion | Pipe rupture → major benzene release → fire hazard | Pressure relief valve (PRV); Pressure indicator | **Two independent PRVs**; **High-high pressure interlock** with pump trip |
| **N2** | Pressure | LESS | Low Pressure | (1) Line leak; (2) Pump failure | Air ingress → explosive mixture in V-201; Process upset | Pressure indicator | Add **low-pressure alarm**; **Nitrogen purging** of delivery line |
| **N2** | Temperature | MORE | High Temperature | (1) Ambient temperature; (2) Pump friction; (3) Sun radiation | Increased vapour pressure → PRV lifting | Temperature indicator | Insulate line; Add **temperature transmitter** |
| **N2** | Temperature | LESS | Low Temperature | (1) Winter conditions; (2) Cold feed from upstream | Benzene freezing point (5.5°C) — pipe blockage | None | Install **heat tracing**; Add **low-temperature alarm** |
| **N2** | Maintenance | OTHER THAN | Maintenance Mode | (1) Pump isolated for repair; (2) Line drained | Operator exposure; Spillage during line opening | LOTO (Lockout-Tagout); Permit to work | Strict **HSE procedure**; **Slip-blind plates** |
| **N2** | Composition | AS WELL AS | Contamination | (1) Air ingress through leaking flange; (2) Wrong material charged to T-101 | Explosive mixture in line; Quality off-spec | Sampling; N2 blanket on T-101 | Add **oxygen analyzer**; **Periodic sampling** |

### Step 4: Summary of Critical Recommendations ($R$)

The recommendations ranked by priority are:
1. **Install a high-high pressure interlock with pump trip** (N2, MORE Pressure) → **MANDATORY**
2. **Provide dual PRVs** on the discharge line (N2, MORE Pressure) → **MANDATORY**
3. **Install low-level interlock on T-101** (N1, LESS Level) → **CRITICAL**
4. **Install flow transmitter with low-low alarm** (N1/N2, NO/LESS Flow) → **RECOMMENDED**
5. **Add heat tracing to the delivery line** (N2, LESS Temperature) → **OPERATIONAL**

### Step 5: Risk Ranking — Quantitative (Qualitative) Method

Each identified deviation is ranked using the risk matrix:

$$R = S \times L$$

where:
- $S$ = Severity (1 to 5) — consequence scale
- $L$ = Likelihood (1 to 5) — frequency scale

| Deviation | $S$ | $L$ | $R$ | Risk Category |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| N2 — More Pressure | 5 | 3 | 15 | **High** |
| N1 — No Flow | 4 | 2 | 8 | **Medium** |
| N2 — Reverse Flow | 3 | 2 | 6 | **Medium** |
| N2 — More Flow | 3 | 2 | 6 | **Medium** |
| N1 — Low Level | 4 | 2 | 8 | **Medium** |
| N2 — Less Temperature | 2 | 3 | 6 | **Low** |

$$\boxed{R_{\text{max}} = 15 \implies \text{Immediate action required for high-pressure deviation (N2, MORE Pressure)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the HAZOP "Guide Word × Parameter" Matrix

The full HAZOP study mathematically corresponds to a **Cartesian product** of three finite sets:

$$D_{set} = N \times P \times GW$$

where:
- $N = \{N_1, N_2, ..., N_n\}$ — the $n$ nodes
- $P = \{p_1, p_2, ..., p_m\}$ — the $m$ parameters (typically $m = 5$)
- $GW = \{g_1, g_2, ..., g_7\}$ — the 7 guide words

Therefore, the **total number of theoretical deviations** to be considered is:

$$|D_{set}| = n \times m \times 7$$

In the pump example above, with $n = 2$ nodes and $m = 5$ parameters:

$$|D_{set}| = 2 \times 5 \times 7 = 70 \text{ theoretical deviations}$$

Of these 70, only those that are **credible** (i.e., have a valid cause) are recorded. In our example, we recorded **15 credible deviations**, demonstrating the high selectivity of a well-conducted HAZOP.

### The Deviation Generation Function (Formal Definition)

Each individual deviation $D_i$ is defined as the **semantic combination** of one guide word $g_j$ and one parameter $p_k$ at one node $N_l$:

$$D_i = f(g_j, p_k) \text{ at } N_l$$

subject to the **credibility filter** $\mathbb{C}$:

$$D_i \text{ is recorded} \iff \mathbb{C}(D_i) = \text{TRUE}$$

where $\mathbb{C}(D_i)$ returns TRUE only when a physically and operationally meaningful cause exists for the deviation.

---

## 3.3 Risk Matrix Used in HAZOP (KTU Standard 5×5)

The standard **5×5 risk matrix** that KTU examiners expect students to know is:

| Severity $\downarrow$ / Likelihood $\rightarrow$ | L1 Rare | L2 Unlikely | L3 Possible | L4 Likely | L5 Almost Certain |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **S5 Catastrophic** | M | H | H | E | E |
| **S4 Major** | M | M | H | H | E |
| **S3 Moderate** | L | M | M | H | H |
| **S2 Minor** | L | L | M | M | H |
| **S1 Negligible** | L | L | L | M | M |

> [!NOTE]
> **Risk Category Definitions:**
> - **L** = Low — Acceptable, monitor
> - **M** = Medium — Reduce As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)
> - **H** = High — Action required before commissioning
> - **E** = Extreme — Stop operation, immediate action
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HAZOP Study Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Scope] --> B[Assemble HAZOP Team]
    B --> C[Gather Documentation: P and ID, PFD, Operating Procedures]
    C --> D[Divide Plant into Study Nodes]
    D --> E[For Each Node, Apply Guide Word to Each Parameter]
    E --> F{Deviation Credible?}
    F -- No --> E
    F -- Yes --> G[Identify Causes]
    G --> H[Identify Consequences]
    H --> I[Identify Existing Safeguards]
    I --> J[Risk Rank: S times L]
    J --> K{Action Required?}
    K -- No --> E
    K -- Yes --> L[Record Recommendation with Responsibility and Target Date]
    L --> E
    E --> M{All Nodes Covered?}
    M -- No --> D
    M -- Yes --> N[Prepare HAZOP Report]
    N --> O[Management Review and Closeout]
    O --> P[End]
```

## 4.2 Guide Word × Parameter Matrix (Visual Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Guide Words] --> A1[NO]
    A --> A2[MORE]
    A --> A3[LESS]
    A --> A4[REVERSE]
    A --> A5[AS WELL AS]
    A --> A6[PART OF]
    A --> A7[OTHER THAN]
    B[Parameters] --> B1[Flow]
    B --> B2[Pressure]
    B --> B3[Temperature]
    B --> B4[Level]
    B --> B5[Composition]
    A1 --> C[Deviation]
    A2 --> C
    A3 --> C
    A4 --> C
    A5 --> C
    A6 --> C
    A7 --> C
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    C --> D[Cause]
    D --> E[Consequence]
    E --> F[Safeguard]
    F --> G[Action]
```

## 4.3 HAZOP Node-to-Worksheet Mapping (Process Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plant Section A] --> N1[Node N1 Suction Side]
    P1 --> N2[Node N2 Discharge Side]
    P1 --> N3[Node N3 Vessel V201]
    N1 --> W1[HAZOP Worksheet Entry 1]
    N1 --> W2[HAZOP Worksheet Entry 2]
    N1 --> W3[HAZOP Worksheet Entry 3]
    N2 --> W4[HAZOP Worksheet Entry 4]
    N2 --> W5[HAZOP Worksheet Entry 5]
    N2 --> W6[HAZOP Worksheet Entry 6]
    N2 --> W7[HAZOP Worksheet Entry 7]
    N3 --> W8[HAZOP Worksheet Entry 8]
    W1 --> R[HAZOP Report and Action Register]
    W2 --> R
    W3 --> R
    W4 --> R
    W5 --> R
    W6 --> R
    W7 --> R
    W8 --> R
```

## 4.4 HAZOP Team Composition — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TL[Team Leader HAZOP Trained] --> S[HAZOP Team]
    PE[Process Engineer] --> S
    IC[Instrumentation and Control Engineer] --> S
    OM[Operations and Maintenance Representative] --> S
    SE[Safety Engineer] --> S
    S --> DOC[HAZOP Documentation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The team must be **cross-functional**. A study led by a single discipline will result in severe blind spots. KTU examiners often award 2–3 marks for correctly identifying the team composition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A, Module 2]**
> **Define HAZOP. List any four standard guide words used in a HAZOP study with one example deviation each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** is a structured, qualitative, team-based risk assessment technique used to identify potential hazards and operability problems in a process plant by systematically applying a set of standardized *guide words* to study *parameters* at designated *nodes* along the process.

**Four Guide Words with Example Deviations:**

| S.No. | Guide Word | Example Deviation |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **NO / NOT** | No Flow in the delivery line |
| 2 | **MORE** | More Pressure in the reactor |
| 3 | **LESS** | Less Temperature in the heat exchanger |
| 4 | **REVERSE** | Reverse Flow in the pump discharge |

*(Examiner note: any four from the seven guide words with one example each is acceptable.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B, Module 2]**
> **What are the documents required to conduct a HAZOP study? List any four.**

**Model Answer:**

The four essential documents required to conduct a HAZOP study are:
1. **P&ID (Piping and Instrumentation Diagram)** — the *primary* working document
2. **PFD (Process Flow Diagram)** — for mass/energy balance verification
3. **Operating Procedures** — to understand the intended operation
4. **Cause and Effect Matrix** — to verify existing interlocks
5. *(Optional)* Equipment Data Sheets and Layout Drawings

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]**
> **Explain the HAZOP procedure in detail. List the seven standard guide words and apply at least 8 deviations on a pump-and-tank system of your choice.**

### Question A: Detailed Procedure + Application (14 Marks)

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU Valuation Key):**
- HAZOP Definition: 2 marks
- Team Composition: 2 marks
- Procedure (Steps): 3 marks
- Seven Guide Words: 2 marks
- HAZOP Worksheet Application (minimum 8 rows): 5 marks

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Definition [2 marks]:**
HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study) is a structured, qualitative risk analysis technique that uses a multidisciplinary team and standardized guide words to identify potential hazards and operability problems in a process plant at predetermined study nodes.

**(b) HAZOP Team [2 marks]:**
The standard team consists of **5 to 8 members**:
- Team Leader (HAZOP-trained, independent facilitator)
- Process Engineer
- Instrumentation/Control Engineer
- Operations/Maintenance Representative
- Safety Engineer
- *(Optionally)* Project Manager, Commissioning Engineer

**(c) HAZOP Procedure [3 marks]:**
The procedure follows six steps:
1. Define scope, objectives, and boundaries
2. Assemble multidisciplinary team
3. Gather documentation (P&ID, PFD, operating procedures)
4. Divide the plant into study nodes
5. For each node, apply guide words to each parameter; record deviation, cause, consequence, safeguard, and action
6. Document findings in a HAZOP worksheet and follow up on actions

**(d) Seven Standard Guide Words [2 marks]:**
NO / NOT, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN.

**(e) HAZOP Application on a Pump-Tank System [5 marks]:**
*(Refer to the worked example in Section 3.1 of these notes — pump P-101 delivering benzene from T-101 to V-201. At least 8 deviations with Causes, Consequences, Safeguards, and Actions must be tabulated. The example table in Section 3.1 provides a comprehensive reference.)*

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks, Alternative Choice]**
> **Discuss the advantages, limitations, and applications of HAZOP. Compare HAZOP with FMEA.**

### Question B: Comparative Analysis (14 Marks)

**Mark Distribution:**
- Advantages of HAZOP: 3 marks
- Limitations of HAZOP: 3 marks
- Applications of HAZOP: 3 marks
- Comparison HAZOP vs FMEA (tabular): 5 marks

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Advantages of HAZOP [3 marks]:**
1. Highly systematic — leaves minimal scope for oversight
2. Team-based — leverages collective expertise of multiple disciplines
3. Provides an audit trail via documented worksheets
4. Identifies both safety AND operability issues
5. Applicable to continuous as well as batch processes
6. Can be applied at any project lifecycle stage

**(b) Limitations of HAZOP [3 marks]:**
1. Time-consuming and expensive
2. Quality depends on the experience of the team
3. Does not address human factors comprehensively
4. Qualitative only — does not quantify failure probabilities
5. Difficult for highly complex software/control systems

**(c) Applications [3 marks]:**
1. Oil & Gas — refinery columns, offshore platforms
2. Petrochemicals — reactors, distillation units
3. Pharmaceuticals — batch reactors, solvent recovery
4. Power Generation — boiler feedwater, lube oil systems
5. Nuclear — coolant loops
6. Water Treatment — chlorine dosing systems

**(d) Comparison Table — HAZOP vs FMEA [5 marks]:**

| Parameter | HAZOP | FMEA |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Origin** | UK, 1963 (CIA) | USA, 1949 (Military) |
| **Standard** | IEC 61882:2016 | IEC 60812 |
| **Focus** | Process deviations | Equipment/component failure |
| **Approach** | Guide Word based | Component based |
| **Study Unit** | Node (pipe section / vessel) | Component (pump, valve, instrument) |
| **Team Required** | 5–8 multidisciplinary | 3–5 specialists |
| **Output** | Deviations with causes & consequences | Failure modes with RPN (Risk Priority Number) |
| **Quantification** | Qualitative (can be semi-quantitative via LOPA) | Semi-quantitative via RPN |
| **Best Suited For** | Continuous chemical processes | Mechanical/electrical equipment |
| **Limitations** | Time-consuming, requires experienced team | Does not capture system interactions |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in HAZOP Answers (Avoid These to Secure Full Marks):**
>
> 1. **Conflating HAZOP with What-If Analysis** — These are different! HAZOP uses guide words; What-If uses open-ended questions.
> 2. **Omitting the "Reverse" and "Other Than" guide words** — Many students only list NO, MORE, LESS. Always mention all **seven** for full marks.
> 3. **Not tabulating the worksheet** — KTU examiners expect a **structured table** (Node, Parameter, Guide Word, Deviation, Cause, Consequence, Safeguard, Action). Prose-only answers lose 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Confusing Cause and Consequence** — Cause = *what initiates* the deviation; Consequence = *what results*. Reversing these is a common error.
> 5. **Ignoring the credibility check** — Not every theoretical deviation is credible. Students who list all 70 combinations lose marks. Focus on **credible** deviations.
> 6. **Skipping safeguards** — Always identify existing safeguards before recommending new actions. This is a key HAZOP discipline.
> 7. **Writing "Flow" instead of specifying "More Flow / Less Flow"** — The deviation is the *combination* of parameter + guide word. Just writing "Flow" is incomplete.
> 8. **Confusing PFD with P&ID** — P&ID is the primary document for HAZOP, not PFD.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HAZOP — 30-Second Rapid Recap (Must Memorize for KTU):**
>
> - **Full Form:** Hazard and Operability Study
> - **Type:** Qualitative, team-based risk analysis
> - **Origin:** UK, Chemical Industries Association, 1963
> - **International Standard:** IEC 61882:2016
> - **Foundation Formula:** $D = GW \oplus P$ (Deviation = Guide Word ⊕ Parameter)
> - **Number of Guide Words:** **Seven** (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN)
> - **Number of Standard Parameters:** **Five** (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition)
> - **Team Size:** **5 to 8 members** (Team Leader, Process, Instrumentation, Operations, Safety)
> - **Primary Working Document:** **P&ID (Piping and Instrumentation Diagram)**
> - **Cause–Consequence Chain:** $C \rightarrow D \rightarrow K$
> - **Safeguard Hierarchy:** Inherent → Passive → Active → Procedural
> - **Risk Formula:** $R = S \times L$ (qualitative)
> - **LOPA Escalation Factor:** 10 per independent layer
> - **Total Theoretical Deviations:** $|D_{set}| = n \times m \times 7$
> - **When to Apply:** Design, Construction, Commissioning, Operation, Modification
> - **Key Advantage:** Identifies both safety AND operability issues
> - **Key Limitation:** Qualitative only; cannot quantify failure probability
> - **For Complex Software/Controls:** **HYSOP** (Hazard Study of Software-Operated Processes) is used
> - **For Continuous Process:** Standard HAZOP; for batch processes, additional guide words like "TIME", "SEQUENCE" are added
> - **Indian Regulatory Mention:** MSIHC Rules 1989 and Factories Act Section 41A
> - **UK Regulatory Mention:** COMAH Regulations 2015
> - **Common KTU 14-Mark Pattern:** Define + Procedure + Team + 7 Guide Words + HAZOP Worksheet on a system (Pump-Tank, Heat Exchanger, Reactor)
> - **Examiner's Pet Question:** "Compare HAZOP with FMEA" — must be answered in tabular format
> - **Pitfall to Avoid:** Always record **safeguards before actions**; do not skip "REVERSE" and "OTHER THAN"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: What If Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** What If Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **What-If Analysis (WIA)** is a systematic, structured, and proactive qualitative-to-semi-quantitative risk identification and evaluation technique used in Process Safety Management (PSM) and Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA). It involves a multidisciplinary team brainstorming credible deviation scenarios by systematically posing the question *"What if...?"* against normal process conditions, equipment failures, human errors, and external disturbances.

The methodology is formally recognized by:
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** (Process Safety Management of Highly Hazardous Chemicals)
- **EPA Risk Management Program (RMP)**
- **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** Guidelines for Hazard Evaluation Procedures

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, What-If Analysis belongs to the family of **Scenario-Based Hazard Identification Techniques**, sitting alongside HAZOP, FMEA, and Fault Tree Analysis. It is particularly valued during the early conceptual and front-end engineering design (FEED) phases when detailed P&IDs are not yet available.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Family Road Trip Planner**

Imagine you are planning a long family road trip from Kochi to Delhi. Before starting, your family sits around a dining table and asks a series of "What if...?" questions:

- *What if it rains heavily in Maharashtra?* → We carry umbrellas and raincoats.
- *What if the car breaks down near Nagpur?* → We note emergency service numbers.
- *What if a family member falls sick?* → We pack a first-aid kit and locate hospitals en route.
- *What if there is a traffic jam?* → We download offline maps and plan alternate routes.

You are **not** calculating the exact probability of rain on Day 5, nor are you designing the car's engine. You are simply identifying plausible problems and brainstorming safeguards. **This is exactly what What-If Analysis does in a chemical plant or industrial process** — it identifies plausible accident scenarios and proposes preventive or mitigative measures without delving into complex probabilistic mathematics.

## 1.3 Core Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Node** | A specific point, area, or system in the process selected for examination |
| **Deviation** | A departure from the intended design or operating condition |
| **Consequence** | The outcome or result of a deviation (injury, fire, explosion, release) |
| **Safeguard** | An existing or proposed barrier to prevent or mitigate the consequence |
| **Recommendation** | An action item proposed to reduce risk to As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP) |
| **Team Leader / Facilitator** | The experienced moderator who guides the brainstorming session |
| **Scribe / Recorder** | The person documenting each What-If question, answer, and recommendation |

## 1.4 Position in the Risk Assessment Lifecycle

The What-If Analysis occupies a strategic position in the overall risk management workflow:

$$ \text{Risk Management Lifecycle} = \text{Identification} \rightarrow \text{Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Evaluation} \rightarrow \text{Treatment} \rightarrow \text{Monitoring} $$

WIA primarily addresses the **Identification** stage, with a secondary contribution to **Analysis** through qualitative severity and likelihood ranking. It is typically the **first formal hazard study** conducted (often called a What-If Review at the conceptual stage, or a What-If/Checklist review at later stages).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The What-If Analysis is rooted in **heuristic reasoning** and **structured brainstorming**. Its theoretical underpinnings draw from:

1. **Lateral Thinking (Edward de Bono)** — Generating non-obvious scenarios.
2. **Swiss Cheese Model (James Reason)** — Understanding that accidents occur when multiple defensive layers fail.
3. **Deductive Reasoning** — Starting from a hypothetical event and tracing back to causes.
4. **Inductive Reasoning** — Starting from causes and predicting potential outcomes.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Swiss Cheese Connection**
> In Reason's Swiss Cheese Model, every safeguard (alarm, relief valve, operator action, containment) is a slice of cheese. Each slice has holes representing weaknesses. A What-If Analysis systematically probes these slices to identify where the holes align and could lead to an accident sequence.

## 2.2 Operational Procedure — The Seven Logical Steps

The What-If Analysis is conducted as a structured workshop following these sequential steps:

### Step 1: Define Scope and Objectives
The facilitator establishes:
- Physical and operational boundaries of the study
- Process units, storage areas, utility systems included
- Specific objectives (e.g., identify fire/explosion hazards, environmental releases)
- Required deliverables (What-If worksheet, recommendations report)

### Step 2: Assemble the Multidisciplinary Team
A typical WIA team consists of **5 to 8 members** with expertise spanning:
- Process / Chemical Engineering
- Mechanical / Rotating Equipment
- Instrumentation and Control
- Safety and Loss Prevention
- Operations and Maintenance
- Environmental Compliance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A common short-answer question asks *"What are the qualifications of an ideal WIA team?"* — Always emphasize **multidisciplinary**, **experienced**, and **independent** (free from bias toward management).

### Step 3: Gather Process Information
The team reviews:
- Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs)
- Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs)
- Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS / SDS)
- Equipment datasheets and design specifications
- Operating procedures and manuals
- Previous incident reports and near-miss logs

### Step 4: Divide the Process into Nodes / Sections
The process is broken into manageable logical sections. Each node is documented with:
- Node number and description
- Design intent and operating parameters
- Major equipment inventory
- Connected utilities and interfaces

### Step 5: Generate What-If Questions
For each node, the team systematically asks:
$$\text{What if } [X] \text{ occurs?}$$
where $X$ represents deviations such as equipment failure, human error, utility loss, or external event.

### Step 6: Evaluate Consequences and Identify Safeguards
For each generated question, the team:
- Determines the **worst credible consequence**
- Lists **existing safeguards** (detection, prevention, mitigation)
- Assigns a **qualitative risk ranking** (Severity × Likelihood)

### Step 7: Document Recommendations
The scribe records all findings in a structured What-If Worksheet, and the team develops **actionable recommendations** to close identified risk gaps.

## 2.3 Question Generation Framework — The WIA Taxonomy

What-If questions are systematically generated by considering four fundamental categories of deviation. This classification is critical for KTU exam answers and ensures comprehensive coverage:

| Deviation Category | Description | Example Question |
|---|---|---|
| **Equipment Failures** | Mechanical, electrical, or instrument malfunction | *What if the centrifugal pump seal fails?* |
| **Human Errors / Procedural Deviations** | Operator mistakes, wrong valve operation, miscommunication | *What if the operator opens the wrong manual valve during startup?* |
| **Utility Failures / External Disturbances** | Loss of power, cooling water, instrument air, steam | *What if the cooling water supply is interrupted for 2 hours?* |
| **Process Deviations** | Temperature, pressure, flow, composition excursions | *What if the reactor temperature exceeds 250°C due to exothermic runaway?* |
| **Environmental / Natural Events** | Floods, earthquakes, lightning, extreme weather | *What if a lightning strike disables the control system?* |
| **Intentional Acts / Security Threats** | Sabotage, theft, terrorism (modern extension) | *What if an unauthorized person tampers with the DCS?* |

## 2.4 Risk Ranking Matrix

The qualitative risk score is calculated using the standard **Severity × Likelihood** matrix:

$$ R = S \times L $$
