where $R$ is the risk rank, $S$ is the severity score, and $L$ is the likelihood score.

| Severity Level | Description | Score $S$ |
|---|---|---|
| Catastrophic | Multiple fatalities, permanent environmental damage, > \$1M loss | **5** |
| Critical | Single fatality, serious injury, significant environmental release | **4** |
| Marginal | Lost-time injury, moderate release, regulatory violation | **3** |
| Minor | First-aid injury, minor release contained on-site | **2** |
| Negligible | No injury, no release, no significant impact | **1** |

| Likelihood Level | Description | Score $L$ |
|---|---|---|
| Frequent | Occurs more than once per year | **5** |
| Probable | Occurs once in 1–10 years | **4** |
| Occasional | Occurs once in 10–100 years | **3** |
| Remote | Occurs once in 100–1000 years | **2** |
| Improbable | Less than once in 1000 years | **1** |

| Risk Score $R$ | Risk Category | Action Required |
|---|---|---|
| 15 – 25 | **High** | Immediate action, stop operation if necessary |
| 8 – 14 | **Medium** | Action required within a defined timeframe |
| 1 – 7 | **Low** | Monitor and document; no immediate action |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk Score | $R = S \times L$ | Dimensionless | Qualitative risk ranking |
| ALARP Inequality | $\text{Risk}_{\text{residual}} \leq \epsilon$ | Dimensionless | As Low As Reasonably Practicable criterion |
| F-N Curve | $N \leq C \cdot x^{-a}$ | $N$ = cumulative frequency (per year), $x$ = consequence magnitude | Societal risk quantification |
| Individual Risk | $IR = \sum_{i} f_i \cdot p_i$ | Probability of fatality per year | Location-specific risk |
| Societal Risk | $SR = \sum_{i} f_i \cdot N_i$ | Expected fatalities per year | Population risk |
| Probit Equation | $Y = a + b \ln(V)$ | $Y$ = probit value, $V$ = intensity measure | Consequence-to-harm conversion |
| Probit to Probability | $P = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} e^{-t^2/2} \, dt$ | Probability (0 to 1) | Consequence damage estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Reminder:** The Probit Equation appears frequently in QRA problems. The standard forms for common overpressure and thermal radiation effects are:
> - **Overpressure (lethality):** $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(P)$ where $P$ is peak overpressure in kPa
> - **Thermal radiation:** $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln(I \cdot t^{2/3})$ where $I$ is radiation intensity in W/m² and $t$ is exposure time in seconds
> - **Toxic exposure (e.g., chlorine):** $Y = -10.6 + 0.5 \ln(C^{2.75} \cdot t)$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Probit Value to Probability of Fatality

The Probit equation is a cornerstone of quantitative consequence analysis, frequently used in conjunction with What-If scenarios that involve toxic release, fire, or explosion. Below is the complete mathematical derivation from probit variable to fatality probability.

### Step 1: Define the Probit Variable

The probit variable $Y$ is defined as the standard normal deviate increased by a constant offset of 5 to eliminate negative values:

$$ Y = 5 + \frac{(x - \mu)}{\sigma} $$

where $x$ is the dose or intensity measure, $\mu$ is the mean dose producing 50% lethality, and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of the dose-response distribution.

### Step 2: Express the Standard Normal Variable

Rearranging the above equation to isolate the standard normal variate $Z$:

$$ Z = Y - 5 = \frac{(x - \mu)}{\sigma} $$

### Step 3: Relate Z to Cumulative Probability

The probability of fatality $P$ is the cumulative distribution function of the standard normal distribution evaluated at $Z$:

$$ P = \Phi(Z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Z} e^{-t^{2}/2} \, dt $$

### Step 4: Substitute Z = Y − 5

$$ P = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} e^{-t^{2}/2} \, dt $$

This integral cannot be expressed in closed elementary form; it is evaluated numerically using standard normal tables, computational tools, or the error function:

$$ P = \frac{1}{2} \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\left( \frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}} \right) \right] $$

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example — Explosion Overpressure

**Problem:** A What-If scenario identified that rupture of a propane storage vessel could produce a peak overpressure of $P = 50$ kPa at a control room located 100 m away. Calculate the probability of fatality for an operator in the control room.

**Solution using the overpressure probit equation:**

$$ Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(P) $$

Substituting $P = 50$ kPa:

$$ Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(50) $$

$$ \ln(50) = 3.912 $$

$$ Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \times 3.912 $$

$$ Y = -77.1 + 27.033 $$

$$ Y = -50.067 $$

Since $Y$ is far below 5, this indicates an extremely low probability of fatality from overpressure at 50 kPa. This makes physical sense — 50 kPa overpressure generally causes minor structural damage but rarely direct lethality to personnel in robust structures.

**Recalculation with a closer location, $P = 180$ kPa:**

$$ Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(180) $$

$$ \ln(180) = 5.193 $$

$$ Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \times 5.193 $$

$$ Y = -77.1 + 35.884 $$

$$ Y = -41.216 $$

Still well below 5, indicating that the overpressure probit equation yields very high safety margins for typical accidental explosion scenarios when applied to personnel in enclosed structures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The What-If question *"What if the operator is outdoors during explosion?"* would lead the team to consider the probit equation differently. Outdoor exposure to overpressure has different damage thresholds (eardrum rupture at ~17 kPa, lung damage at ~70 kPa, lethality at ~100+ kPa for direct exposure).

## 3.2 Derivation: Individual Risk Calculation

The Individual Risk (IR) at a specific location is the sum of the product of frequency and fatality probability for all identified accident scenarios at that location:

$$ IR = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_{i} \cdot p_{i} $$

where $f_i$ is the frequency of accident scenario $i$ (per year) and $p_i$ is the probability of fatality given scenario $i$ occurs at the location.

### Step-by-Step Calculation for a Hypothetical LPG Storage Facility

Consider the following What-If scenarios identified for an LPG bullet tank:

| Scenario $i$ | Description | Frequency $f_i$ (per year) | Fatality Probability $p_i$ | Contribution $f_i \cdot p_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Major BLEVE at tank | $1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.85 | $8.5 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 2 | Jet fire from flange leak | $5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.15 | $7.5 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| 3 | Vapour cloud explosion | $2.0 \times 10^{-6}$ | 0.60 | $1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 4 | Uncontrolled release (asphyxiation) | $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.05 | $5.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |

**Summation:**

$$ IR = 8.5 \times 10^{-6} + 7.5 \times 10^{-5} + 1.2 \times 10^{-6} + 5.0 \times 10^{-6} $$

$$ IR = 8.95 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year} $$

This can be expressed as:

$$ IR \approx 9 \times 10^{-5} \text{ fatalities per person per year} $$

Comparing to the typical UK HSE individual risk tolerance of $10^{-4}$ per year for workers and $10^{-5}$ per year for the public, the calculated IR of $9 \times 10^{-5}$ is borderline acceptable for workers but exceeds the public risk criterion, triggering the ALARP demonstration requirement.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of WIA with Other Techniques

For KTU 2024 Scheme exam preparation, the following comparative matrix is essential. Examiners frequently ask students to differentiate between hazard identification techniques.

| Parameter | What-If Analysis | HAZOP | FMEA | Fault Tree |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Brainstorming | Systematic deviation study | Bottom-up component | Top-down deductive |
| **Trigger Word** | "What if...?" | Guide words (No, More, Less, As Well As) | Component failure modes | Top event |
| **Detail Required** | Low-Medium | High (P&ID level) | Medium (component level) | High (system level) |
| **Quantitative Output** | Mostly qualitative | Can be extended to quantitative | Risk Priority Number | Cut sets, probabilities |
| **Best Application Phase** | Conceptual / FEED | Detailed design / operations | Equipment design | Complex systems / SIL |
| **Team Size** | 5–8 | 6–10 | 3–5 (single discipline) | 3–5 (specialized) |
| **Time Required** | 1–3 days | 1–4 weeks | 1–2 weeks | 2–8 weeks |
| **Documentation** | Worksheet | Full node report | FMEA table | Logic tree diagram |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Boundary Case:** WIA is sometimes confused with HAZOP. The key distinction is that **HAZOP uses fixed guide words** applied systematically to every parameter at every node, whereas **WIA uses open-ended brainstorming** that can explore broader scenarios including human factors, external events, and multi-node interactions. WIA is faster but less rigorous; HAZOP is slower but more comprehensive at the P&ID level.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: What-If Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Study Scope] --> B[Assemble Multidisciplinary Team]
    B --> C[Gather Process Information PFD P&ID MSDS]
    C --> D[Divide Process into Logical Nodes]
    D --> E[For Each Node Generate What If Questions]
    E --> F{Qualitative Risk Ranking}
    F --> G[Identify Existing Safeguards]
    G --> H{Risk Acceptable}
    H -->|No| I[Develop Recommendations]
    H -->|Yes| J[Document as Accepted Risk]
    I --> K[Document in What If Worksheet]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Review and Follow Up on Actions]
    L --> M[End: Final WIA Report]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: What-If Question Generation Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[What If Question Generation] --> B[Equipment Failures]
    A --> C[Human Errors]
    A --> D[Utility Failures]
    A --> E[Process Deviations]
    A --> F[External Events]
    B --> B1[Pump seal failure]
    B --> B2[Instrument malfunction]
    B --> B3[Vessel rupture]
    C --> C1[Wrong valve operation]
    C --> C2[Missed reading]
    C --> C3[Communication error]
    D --> D1[Power loss]
    D --> D2[Cooling water failure]
    D --> D3[Instrument air loss]
    E --> E1[High temperature]
    E --> E2[High pressure]
    E --> E3[Wrong flow]
    F --> F1[Flood]
    F --> F2[Lightning]
    F --> F3[Earthquake]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Risk Reduction Loop Using WIA Findings

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identified What If Scenario] --> B[Evaluate Consequence Severity]
    B --> C[Determine Frequency of Occurrence]
    C --> D[Calculate Risk Score R = S times L]
    D --> E{R Greater than Threshold}
    E -->|Yes| F[Design Additional Safeguard]
    E -->|No| G[Document and Monitor]
    F --> H[Re-evaluate Residual Risk]
    H --> I{Residual Risk ALARP}
    I -->|Yes| G
    I -->|No| F
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Mapping: WIA Within the QRA Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Plant or Facility] --> B[Hazard Identification Phase]
    B --> B1[What If Analysis]
    B --> B2[HAZOP Study]
    B --> B3[Checklist Review]
    B1 --> C[Consequence Analysis]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Fire and Explosion Modelling]
    C --> C2[Toxic Dispersion Modelling]
    C1 --> D[Frequency Analysis]
    C2 --> D
    D --> D1[Historical Data Review]
    D --> D2[Generic Failure Rate Databases]
    D1 --> E[Risk Estimation]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Individual Risk and Societal Risk]
    F --> G[Risk Evaluation Against Criteria]
    G --> H{Risk Tolerable}
    H -->|Yes| I[Document and Operate]
    H -->|No| J[Implement Risk Reduction Measures]
    J --> K[Re-evaluate Risk]
    K --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block in the WIA workflow represents a discrete deliverable. The conditional nodes (diamonds) represent decision points where the team evaluates whether the identified risk requires additional engineering or procedural controls. The iterative loop in the QRA framework reflects the continuous improvement principle enshrined in ISO 31000 and the KTU 2024 Scheme risk management curriculum.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define What-If Analysis. List any four categories of What-If questions.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **What-If Analysis** is a qualitative hazard identification technique in which a multidisciplinary team systematically generates hypothetical deviation questions (in the form *"What if...?"*) for a process or system, evaluates the potential consequences, identifies existing safeguards, and recommends additional risk reduction measures.

**Four categories of What-If questions:**

1. **Equipment Failures** — *What if the pump seal leaks?*
2. **Human Errors** — *What if the operator opens the wrong valve?*
3. **Utility Failures** — *What if the cooling water supply is lost?*
4. **External Events** — *What if lightning strikes the storage tank?*

---

### Question 2: State the difference between What-If Analysis and HAZOP.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | What-If Analysis | HAZOP |
|---|---|---|
| Methodology | Open-ended brainstorming using *"What if...?"* questions | Systematic application of guide words (No, More, Less, Reverse, As Well As, Other Than) to process parameters |
| Information Required | General process knowledge, block diagrams | Detailed P&IDs, process data, control philosophy |
| Application Stage | Conceptual and front-end engineering design | Detailed design and operational stages |
| Time and Cost | Relatively quick and inexpensive | Time-consuming and resource-intensive |
| Breadth vs Depth | Broader, more flexible | More thorough at the P&ID node level |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### Question A(a): Explain the step-by-step methodology of conducting a What-If Analysis for a chemical storage facility handling ammonia. Include team composition, question categories, and risk ranking. (7 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

The What-If Analysis for an ammonia storage facility is conducted as follows:

**Step 1 — Define Scope:** The study covers one pressurized ammonia storage bullet (capacity 2000 tonnes), associated pumps, vaporizers, pressure relief system, emergency shutdown valves, and the loading/unloading gantry. Objectives include identifying fire, explosion, toxic release, and environmental hazards.

**Step 2 — Team Composition:** A typical 7-member team would include:
- Team Leader — Senior process safety engineer (facilitator)
- Process Engineer — Ammonia process specialist
- Mechanical Engineer — Static and rotating equipment expertise
- Instrument Engineer — DCS, SIS, and field instrumentation
- Operations Supervisor — Field operational experience
- Safety Officer — PSM and emergency response expertise
- Scribe — Documenter (often a junior engineer)

**Step 3 — Gather Information:** The team reviews P&IDs, ammonia MSDS (toxicity data: IDLH = 300 ppm, LC50 = 2000 ppm/30 min), equipment datasheets, relief valve sizing calculations, and the facility's emergency response plan.

**Step 4 — Node Division:** Nodes are defined as:
- Node 1: Storage vessel and relief system
- Node 2: Transfer pumps and associated piping
- Node 3: Vaporizer section
- Node 4: Loading gantry
- Node 5: Control and shutdown systems

**Step 5 — Question Generation:** The team generates questions in all four categories. Representative examples:

| What-If Question | Consequence | Existing Safeguards | Risk Rank |
|---|---|---|---|
| What if the storage vessel is overfilled during loading? | Ammonia release, toxic exposure | Level transmitter, high-level alarm, ESD at 95% | High |
| What if the pressure relief valve fails open? | Continuous ammonia vapor release | Secondary block valve, vent scrubber | High |
| What if the cooling system fails on the vaporizer? | Overheating, increased vaporization rate | Temperature alarm, redundant chiller | Medium |
| What if instrument air supply is lost? | Control valves fail open or closed (per design) | Air receiver, backup nitrogen supply | Medium |
| What if a heavy monsoon flood inundates the bund? | Vessel floatation, flange failure, mass release | Bund wall height 1.2 m, drainage pumps | Medium |

**Step 6 — Risk Ranking:** Each question is scored using the $R = S \times L$ matrix. For the overfilling scenario: $S = 5$ (potential fatality), $L = 3$ (occasional, depends on operator compliance), giving $R = 15$ (High risk).

**Step 7 — Recommendations:** For the overfilling scenario, the team recommends installing an independent high-high-level SIL-2 shutdown system with a separate level sensor, automatic block valve on the inlet line, and procedural enhancement for overfill checks.

---

### Question A(b): Using the overpressure probit equation, calculate the probability of fatality for a person exposed to a peak overpressure of 120 kPa following a vapour cloud explosion. Comment on the result. (7 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the applicable probit equation for overpressure lethality:**

$$ Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(P) $$

where $P$ is the peak side-on overpressure in kPa and $Y$ is the probit variable.

**Step 2 — Substitute P = 120 kPa:**

$$ \ln(120) = 4.787 $$

$$ Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \times 4.787 $$

$$ Y = -77.1 + 33.079 $$

$$ Y = -44.021 $$

**Step 3 — Convert probit to probability of fatality:**

The probability of fatality is calculated as:

$$ P = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} e^{-t^{2}/2} \, dt $$

Substituting $Y - 5 = -49.021$:

$$ P = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{-49.021} e^{-t^{2}/2} \, dt $$

This integral evaluates to an astronomically small value, effectively $P \approx 0$ (less than $10^{-300}$).

**Step 4 — Interpretation and Engineering Comment:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The result indicates that the overpressure probit equation $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(P)$ yields very conservative lethality predictions. The probit value of $-44$ is far below the 50% lethality threshold ($Y = 5$), suggesting essentially zero probability of direct fatality from overpressure at 120 kPa for the general population model. However, this is misleading because:
> 1. The 120 kPa value represents significant structural damage (heavy damage to buildings).
> 2. Secondary effects (debris, building collapse, missile projection) are not captured in the overpressure-only probit.
> 3. For a What-If Analysis, the team should also apply the probit equations for **debris** and **glass breakage** to obtain a realistic combined fatality estimate.

The more commonly cited threshold for significant overpressure injury is:
- **17 kPa** — Eardrum rupture threshold
- **35 kPa** — Threshold for lung damage
- **70 kPa** — Threshold for significant lethality from direct overpressure

The What-If conclusion would be that at 120 kPa, a person inside a robust building has a low direct fatality probability from overpressure, but a person outdoors or in a weak structure faces a much higher combined risk.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### Question B(a): Discuss the advantages and limitations of What-If Analysis as a hazard identification tool. Compare it with HAZOP in terms of applicability and thoroughness. (7 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages of What-If Analysis:**

1. **Simplicity and Flexibility:** The technique uses natural-language questions that do not require specialized training in guide words or formal logic. Team members from diverse backgrounds can contribute effectively.
2. **Speed and Cost-Effectiveness:** A typical What-If review can be completed in 1–3 days, making it suitable for early project stages when time and budget are constrained.
3. **Broad Scope:** WIA is not limited to process deviations; it can explore human factors, security threats, natural disasters, and multi-node interactions.
4. **Useful in Data-Scarce Conditions:** Unlike quantitative techniques (FTA, ETA), WIA does not require detailed failure rate data. It is effective when only conceptual design information is available.
5. **Encourages Creative Thinking:** The open-ended format stimulates lateral thinking and surfaces scenarios that a checklist-based approach might miss.
6. **Excellent Documentation:** The What-If worksheet provides a clear, traceable record of identified hazards, safeguards, and recommendations.

**Limitations of What-If Analysis:**

1. **Highly Team-Dependent:** The quality of the output is critically dependent on the experience, creativity, and knowledge of the team members. A weak team will miss significant scenarios.
2. **Not Systematically Exhaustive:** Unlike HAZOP, WIA does not apply a fixed set of guide words to every parameter at every node. Important deviations may be overlooked.
3. **Qualitative Output:** WIA does not directly produce numerical risk estimates. Quantitative risk assessment requires follow-up techniques.
4. **Difficulty with Complex Systems:** For highly instrumented processes with sophisticated interlocks, WIA may fail to identify subtle interactions between control loops.
5. **Potential for Groupthink:** In a poorly facilitated session, dominant personalities may suppress alternative viewpoints.

**Comparison with HAZOP:**

| Criterion | What-If Analysis | HAZOP |
|---|---|---|
| Thoroughness | Moderate; relies on team creativity | High; systematic guide-word coverage |
| Data Requirement | Low (conceptual) | High (detailed P&ID) |
| Cost and Duration | Low | High |
| Human Factor Coverage | Strong | Weak (unless extended) |
| Quantitative Output | Primarily qualitative | Primarily qualitative (unless extended) |
| Best Project Phase | Conceptual / FEED | Detailed design / pre-commissioning |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Listing advantages without explaining them** — Always provide a one-line justification for each point.
> 2. **Confusing limitations of WIA with limitations of HAZOP** — The question specifically asks about WIA.
> 3. **Omitting the team-dependency limitation** — Examiners specifically look for this critical weakness.
> 4. **Failing to differentiate the techniques at the methodological level** — Mention guide words vs open-ended brainstorming.

---

### Question B(b): A What-If Analysis team identifies a scenario where a 10,000 m³ refrigerated propane storage tank could fail catastrophically (BLEVE). The estimated failure frequency is $1 \times 10^{-6}$ per year, and the conditional probability of fatality for an operator at 500 m is 0.7. Calculate the individual risk. State whether the risk is tolerable with reference to the UK HSE criteria of $10^{-4}$ per year for workers and $10^{-5}$ per year for the public. (7 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant formula:**

The individual risk (IR) is the product of the scenario frequency and the conditional probability of fatality:

$$ IR = f \times p $$

For multiple scenarios, the total individual risk is the sum:

$$ IR_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \times p_i $$

**Step 2 — For this single BLEVE scenario:**

$$ f_{BLEVE} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per year} $$

$$ p_{BLEVE} = 0.7 $$

$$ IR_{BLEVE} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.7 = 7 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per year} $$

**Step 3 — Compare with the UK HSE criteria:**

- **Worker tolerable risk:** $10^{-4}$ per year
- **Public tolerable risk:** $10^{-5}$ per year

$$ IR_{BLEVE} = 7 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per year} $$

For workers:

$$ 7 \times 10^{-7} \ll 10^{-4} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Risk is well within tolerable limit for workers} $$

For the public:

$$ 7 \times 10^{-7} \ll 10^{-5} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Risk is well within tolerable limit for the public} $$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**

The individual risk from this single scenario is two orders of magnitude below the public risk criterion. However, the What-If team must consider the **cumulative individual risk** from all identified scenarios. If there are 20 similar scenarios each contributing $10^{-6}$ per year, the total individual risk could approach $10^{-5}$ per year.

Additionally, the team should evaluate **societal risk** using the F-N curve, since a BLEVE in a populated area could affect many people simultaneously. The tolerable societal risk criterion is typically an F-N curve with a slope of $-1$ and an anchor point at $F = 10^{-3}$ per year for $N = 1$ fatality.

**Step 5 — Recommendation:** Although the individual BLEVE risk is tolerable, the team should recommend:
- Installation of a water deluge system around the propane tank
- A second foam pourer on the dike
- A 500 m exclusion zone enforcement
- Quarterly inspection of the relief valves

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — High-Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using wrong formula:** Some students incorrectly add frequency and probability. Always emphasize $IR = f \times p$.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Frequency must be in per year; probability is dimensionless.
> 3. **Ignoring the summation:** For full credit, mention that real assessments sum over all scenarios.
> 4. **Not comparing with both criteria:** Workers and public have different tolerable risk levels.
> 5. **Stopping at the calculation:** Always interpret the numerical result in engineering terms.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — What-If Analysis**

- **Definition:** A qualitative hazard identification technique using open-ended *"What if...?"* brainstorming by a multidisciplinary team.
- **Origin:** Rooted in PSM regulations (OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119) and CCPS guidelines.
- **Team Size:** 5–8 multidisciplinary members; team leader is a senior process safety expert.
- **Four Question Categories:** Equipment failure, human error, utility failure, process deviation. (Extended categories include external events and security threats.)
- **Risk Score:** $R = S \times L$ where $S$ is severity (1–5) and $L$ is likelihood (1–5). High risk = 15–25; Medium = 8–14; Low = 1–7.
- **WIA vs HAZOP:** WIA is open-ended and flexible; HAZOP is systematic with guide words. WIA is faster but less exhaustive.
- **Probit Equation for Overpressure:** $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(P)$, with $P$ in kPa.
- **Probit Equation for Thermal Radiation:** $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln(I \cdot t^{2/3})$, with $I$ in W/m² and $t$ in seconds.
- **Probit-to-Probability Conversion:** $P = \frac{1}{2} \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\left( \frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}} \right) \right]$.
- **Individual Risk:** $IR = \sum f_i \cdot p_i$, with units of fatalities per person per year.
- **UK HSE Tolerable Risk:** Workers $10^{-4}$/year; Public $10^{-5}$/year.
- **ALARP Principle:** Risk must be As Low As Reasonably Practicable; demonstrated through cost-benefit analysis.
- **Documentation:** All findings recorded in a What-If Worksheet; recommendations tracked to closure.
- **Limitations:** Highly team-dependent, not systematically exhaustive, primarily qualitative output.
- **Best Application Phase:** Conceptual design and front-end engineering design (FEED) when detailed P&IDs are not available.
- **Follow-up Actions:** Recommendations must be assigned to responsible persons with target closure dates and verified during subsequent safety audits.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fault Tree Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a deductive (top-down) systematic, graphical, and analytical failure analysis methodology used in reliability engineering, safety engineering, and risk assessment to determine the probability of an undesired system-level event (the **Top Event / TOP**) by analyzing the combinations of lower-level failures that can cause it. It was originally developed in 1962 by **H. A. Watson** of Bell Telephone Laboratories for the U.S. Air Force **Minuteman Missile Launch Control System**.

The technique applies **Boolean Algebra** to model the logical combinations of component-level (basic) failures that produce the system-level failure. FTA is classified as a *deductive* analysis because it begins with a known undesirable outcome (Top Event) and works backwards to identify its root causes, in contrast to Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) which is *inductive* (bottom-up).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** FTA is a cornerstone of the *Quantitative Risk Assessment* syllabus. Mastery of Boolean reduction, Minimal Cut Set (MCS) determination, and Top Event Probability (Q) calculation using rare-event approximation and exact inclusion-exclusion formula is **mandatory** for ESE questions.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|:------:|------------|
| **Top Event** | T | The undesired system-level event being analyzed (e.g., "Pump Fails") |
| **Intermediate Event** | — | An event resulting from a combination of lower-level events via a logic gate |
| **Basic Event** | $X_i$ | A leaf-level component failure requiring no further development |
| **Undeveloped Event** | — | An event not developed further due to lack of data or insignificance |
| **External / House Event** | — | A condition assumed to exist (e.g., power available) |
| **Cut Set** | C | A set of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the Top Event |
| **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** | $K_i$ | A cut set where removal of any single event negates the set |
| **Transfer Gate** | △ | Used in replicated sub-trees (avoids drawing redundancy) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Recipe for Disaster"**
>
> Imagine your car **fails to start** on a cold Monday morning. You ask: *"What combination of things could cause this?"*
>
> - **Battery dead** *OR* **Starter motor broken** *OR* **No fuel in tank**
> - "No fuel" itself could mean: the **fuel gauge is wrong** *AND* you **forgot to refuel** *OR* the **fuel pump failed**
>
> This is exactly how an engineer draws an FTA: the "car won't start" is the **Top Event**, the "fuel pump failed" is a **Basic Event** at the bottom, and the connecting words "AND / OR" become **Logic Gates** in the tree. A **Minimal Cut Set** is the *smallest* set of independent failures that, together, sink the car — for instance, "fuel empty AND fuel gauge broken" is one such set.
>
> Once you have the cut sets, you can compute *how likely* the disaster is — that is the **Quantitative** part of Quantitative Risk Assessment.

## 1.4 Symbols (KTU Board Examination Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These symbols are **must-know** for KTU board examination diagrams. Drawing an OR-gate as an AND-gate or vice-versa results in full-mark deductions.

| Symbol | Meaning |
|:------:|---------|
| **Rectangle** | Top Event or Intermediate Event (the event being analyzed) |
| **Circle** | Basic Event (component-level failure) |
| **Diamond** | Undeveloped Event (insufficient data or impact) |
| **House/Pentagon** | External / Normal Event (boundary condition) |
| **AND-Gate (flat bottom)** | Output occurs *only if* **ALL** inputs occur |
| **OR-Gate (curved bottom)** | Output occurs *if any one or more* inputs occur |
| **Triangle (Transfer)** | Connects sub-trees of the same logic |
| **Oval/Pentagon** | Conditioning event or trigger event |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a Fault Tree — Step-by-Step Logic

The construction follows a strict 5-step deductive procedure:

1. **Define the Top Event (T):** State clearly, in operational terms, the undesired system-level failure. Example: *"Loss of Emergency Cooling to Reactor."*
2. **Identify Immediate Causes:** Determine the *next-lower* set of events that, individually or together, can directly produce the Top Event.
3. **Apply Logic Gates:** Connect these immediate causes using **AND** (must all fail) or **OR** (any one fails) gates. Use a **NOT-gate** for negation logic.
4. **Recursive Development:** For each Intermediate Event identified, repeat Step 2–3 until only **Basic Events**, **Undeveloped Events**, or **External Events** remain at the leaves.
5. **Validate:** Verify the tree using *consistency checks* — every gate input/output must be defined, no orphans, no circular logic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** FTA is used in nuclear reactor safety (NRC mandates), aerospace (FAA Part 25), automotive ISO 26262 functional safety, offshore oil & gas, and chemical process industries. It enables computation of system unreliability from component failure data, identification of single-point failures, and ranking of components for reliability improvement.

## 2.2 Boolean Algebra Foundations (Mandatory for KTU)

FTA relies on **Switching Algebra** (B. Boole, 1854) to algebraically reduce the fault tree into its **structure function**.

| Law | Boolean Form | Set-Theory Equivalent |
|-----|--------------|----------------------|
| Identity | $A + 0 = A,\;\; A \cdot 1 = A$ | $\emptyset$ identity |
| Domination | $A + 1 = 1,\;\; A \cdot 0 = 0$ | Universe identity |
| Idempotent | $A + A = A,\;\; A \cdot A = A$ | Set repetition |
| Complement | $A + A' = 1,\;\; A \cdot A' = 0$ | Union with complement |
| Commutative | $A + B = B + A$ | — |
| Associative | $(A+B)+C = A+(B+C)$ | — |
| Distributive | $A + (B \cdot C) = (A+B)(A+C)$ | Expansion law |
| **Absorption** | $A + (A \cdot B) = A$ | Set absorption |
| **De Morgan's** | $(A+B)' = A' \cdot B'$ | Complement of union |
| **De Morgan's** | $(A \cdot B)' = A' + B'$ | Complement of intersection |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam-critical**. Memorize the distinction between **rare-event approximation** and **exact inclusion-exclusion** — most FTA numerical problems require this judgment.

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|:-:|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **AND-gate unreliability** (independent events) | $Q_{AND} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} q_i$ | Used for rare-event approximation |
| 2 | **OR-gate unreliability** (independent events) | $Q_{OR} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - q_i)$ | Exact form for OR combination |
| 3 | **OR-gate rare-event approximation** | $Q_{OR} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i$ | Valid only when $q_i \ll 1$ and independent |
| 4 | **Top Event via Minimal Cut Sets (rare-event)** | $Q_{TOP} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{m} \prod_{X_j \in K_i} q_j$ | $m$ = number of MCS |
| 5 | **Top Event via Inclusion-Exclusion (exact)** | $Q_{TOP} = \sum_i P(K_i) - \sum_{i<j} P(K_i \cap K_j) + \ldots$ | Tractable only for small $m$ |
| 6 | **System reliability** | $R = 1 - Q_{TOP}$ | Fundamental complement |
| 7 | **Birnbaum Importance Measure** | $I_B(i) = \frac{\partial Q_{TOP}}{\partial q_i} = Q(1_i, \vec{q}) - Q(0_i, \vec{q})$ | Sensitivity of Top to component $i$ |
| 8 | **Criticality Importance (Vesely-Fussell)** | $I_V(i) = \frac{q_i \cdot I_B(i)}{Q_{TOP}}$ | Fractional contribution of $i$ to TOP |
| 9 | **Number of 1st-order MCS** | $N_1$ | **Single-point failures** (highest priority) |
| 10 | **Number of 2nd-order MCS** | $N_2$ | Redundancy required (1-out-of-2) |
| 11 | **Upper Bound (rare-event)** | $Q_{TOP} \le \sum_{i=1}^{m} \prod_{j \in K_i} q_j$ | Always valid, conservative |
| 12 | **Lower Bound (rare-event)** | $Q_{TOP} \ge \sum_{i=1}^{m} \prod_{j \in K_i} q_j - \sum_{i<j}\prod_{l \in K_i \cup K_j} q_l$ | Used when events overlap |

## 2.4 Method for Finding Minimal Cut Sets (MCS)

The KTU board typically tests one of three methods. Practice all three:

### Method A: Direct Substitution (Top-Down Substitution)
- Step 1: Replace OR-gates with **Boolean addition** ($+$)
- Step 2: Replace AND-gates with **Boolean multiplication** ($\cdot$)
- Step 3: Apply **Distributive Law** to expand
- Step 4: Apply **Absorption Law** to remove supersets
- Result: A sum-of-products form whose terms are the **Cut Sets**, and surviving minimal ones are the **MCS**

### Method B: Bottom-Up Substitution
Substitute outputs of lower gates into upper gates working from the leaves upward.

### Method C: MOCUS Algorithm (top-down, formal procedure)
A formal algorithm by J. B. Fussell (1973) used in software packages (e.g., SAPHIRE, CAFTA). For KTU, the algebraic Method A is sufficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Use:** Identifying single-point failures (1st-order MCS) is the *primary deliverable* of FTA in safety engineering. If a 1st-order MCS exists, redundancy must be added — it is a **mandatory design change** in IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 SIL determination.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: MCS Determination (Algebraic Method)

**Problem Statement:** A fault tree for "System Fails" is given with the following structure (this is a classic KTU board exemplar):

$$T = A_1 + A_2 \cdot A_3$$

where:

$$A_1 = X_1 + X_2 \cdot X_3$$

$$A_2 = X_3 + X_4$$

$$A_3 = X_1 + X_5 \cdot X_2$$

Find all **Minimal Cut Sets** for the Top Event $T$.

### Step 1 — Substitute $A_1$ into the Top Event

$$T = (X_1 + X_2 \cdot X_3) + A_2 \cdot A_3$$

**Logic:** Replace the intermediate event $A_1$ with its Boolean expression. The Top Event's OR-gate makes the substitution additive.

### Step 2 — Substitute $A_2$ and $A_3$

$$T = (X_1 + X_2 \cdot X_3) + (X_3 + X_4)(X_1 + X_5 \cdot X_2)$$

**Logic:** Each intermediate event is replaced by its Boolean expression while preserving gate structure.

### Step 3 — Apply the Distributive Law

The AND-gate output is the product $(X_3 + X_4)(X_1 + X_5 \cdot X_2)$, which expands to four products by distributing:

$$(X_3 + X_4)(X_1 + X_5 \cdot X_2) = X_3 X_1 + X_3 X_5 X_2 + X_4 X_1 + X_4 X_5 X_2$$

**Logic:** Each term of the first bracket multiplies with the entire second bracket. Distributive law of Boolean Algebra: $A(B+C) = AB + AC$.

### Step 4 — Combine the OR terms

$$T = X_1 + X_2 X_3 + X_1 X_3 + X_2 X_3 X_5 + X_1 X_4 + X_2 X_4 X_5$$

**Logic:** With the OR-gate, the additive structure collects all disjunctive products into a sum-of-products form.

### Step 5 — Apply the Absorption Law to remove supersets

**Absorption law:** $A + AB = A$. This means a smaller cut set absorbs any cut set that contains it.

Examining each term against the existing terms:

- $X_1 X_3$ contains $X_1$ → **absorbed** by $X_1$
- $X_1 X_4$ contains $X_1$ → **absorbed** by $X_1$
- $X_2 X_3 X_5$ contains $X_2 X_3$ → **absorbed** by $X_2 X_3$
- $X_2 X_4 X_5$ contains no smaller term — **kept as MCS**

### Step 6 — Final Minimal Cut Set List

$$\boxed{\;\text{MCS} = \{X_1\},\;\{X_2, X_3\},\;\{X_2, X_4, X_5\}\;}$$

**Result Summary:** The system has 3 minimal cut sets, one of which is 1st-order (single-point failure $X_1$), one 2nd-order ($\{X_2, X_3\}$), and one 3rd-order ($\{X_2, X_4, X_5\}$).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Top Event Probability Calculation

**Problem:** Using the MCS from Example 1, compute the **Top Event unreliability** $Q_{TOP}$ given:

$$q_1 = 0.005,\;\; q_2 = 0.010,\;\; q_3 = 0.010,\;\; q_4 = 0.020,\;\; q_5 = 0.015$$

### Step 1 — Compute individual cut set probabilities

For a cut set $K_i$, its probability under the assumption of independent events is the product of the component unreliabilities:

$$P(K_1) = P(X_1) = 0.005$$

$$P(K_2) = P(X_2 \cap X_3) = (0.010)(0.010) = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P(K_3) = P(X_2 \cap X_4 \cap X_5) = (0.010)(0.020)(0.015) = 3.0 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Logic:** For independent events, the joint probability equals the product of marginal probabilities.

### Step 2 — Apply the Rare-Event Approximation

The rare-event approximation (also called the *upper bound formula*) states:

$$Q_{TOP} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{m} P(K_i)$$

**Assumption:** All $q_i \ll 1$ (typically $q_i < 0.1$), and cut sets are nearly mutually exclusive. This is the standard assumption in KTU board problems.

Substituting the values:

$$Q_{TOP} \approx 0.005 + 0.0001 + 0.000003$$

$$\boxed{\;Q_{TOP} \approx 5.103 \times 10^{-3}\;}$$

**Logic:** The dominant contributor is the 1st-order MCS ($X_1$ alone contributes ~98% of the Top probability). This insight is the **power** of FTA in design — $X_1$ must be hardened.

### Step 3 — Verification using Exact Inclusion-Exclusion

To verify the approximation, we apply the exact formula for **three** cut sets:

$$Q_{TOP} = \sum_i P(K_i) - \sum_{i<j} P(K_i \cap K_j) + P(K_1 \cap K_2 \cap K_3)$$

Computing the pairwise intersections (with $X_1$ dominating, all pairwise intersections of $K_1$ with $K_2$ and $K_3$ collapse to $P(K_1)$):

$$P(K_1 \cap K_2) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_2) \cdot P(X_3) = (0.005)(0.010)(0.010) = 5.0 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$P(K_1 \cap K_3) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_2) \cdot P(X_4) \cdot P(X_5) = (0.005)(0.010)(0.020)(0.015) = 1.5 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$P(K_2 \cap K_3) = P(X_2) \cdot P(X_3) \cdot P(X_4) \cdot P(X_5) = (0.010)(0.010)(0.020)(0.015) = 3.0 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$P(K_1 \cap K_2 \cap K_3) = 3.0 \times 10^{-11} \approx 0$$

$$Q_{TOP, exact} = 0.005103 - (5.0 \times 10^{-7} + 1.5 \times 10^{-8} + 3.0 \times 10^{-8}) \approx 0.0051025$$

The exact and approximate values agree to 4 significant figures, confirming the rare-event approximation is justified.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Birnbaum Importance

**Problem:** For the same system above, compute the **Birnbaum Importance** $I_B(1)$ for component $X_1$.

**Formula:**

$$I_B(i) = Q_{TOP}(q_i = 1) - Q_{TOP}(q_i = 0)$$

### Step 1 — Compute $Q_{TOP}$ with $X_1$ failed with certainty ($q_1 = 1$)

Setting $X_1 = 1$ activates the cut set $\{X_1\}$ immediately, so $Q_{TOP} = 1$.

### Step 2 — Compute $Q_{TOP}$ with $X_1$ perfect ($q_1 = 0$)

With $X_1 = 0$, the cut sets reduce to:
- $\{X_1\}$ → 0 (removed)
- $\{X_2, X_3\}$ → $0.010 \times 0.010 = 10^{-4}$
- $\{X_2, X_4, X_5\}$ → $0.010 \times 0.020 \times 0.015 = 3.0 \times 10^{-6}$

$$Q_{TOP}(q_1 = 0) \approx 1.03 \times 10^{-4}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Birnbaum Importance

$$I_B(1) = 1 - 1.03 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.9999$$

**Interpretation:** $X_1$ is **the most critical component** — improving its reliability yields the maximum reduction in system risk. This validates the engineering decision to invest in component $X_1$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

    bx2(("X2")):::basic
    bx3(("X3")):::basic
    bx4(("X4")):::basic
    bx5(("X5")):::basic

    orG1 --> evA1
    orG1 --> andG1

    andG2 --> bx3

## 4.3 Reading the Tree — A Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagrams during your KTU viva or board exam:**
>
> 1. Start at the **Top Event** (red box) at the top.
> 2. The Top Event is fed by an **OR-gate** (meaning any of the branches below can cause it).
> 3. The OR-gate has two inputs: Intermediate Event $A_1$ and the output of an **AND-gate** $A_2 \cdot A_3$.
> 4. Continue expanding until you reach the **Basic Events** (green circles), which have no further development.
> 5. Trace every path from Top to leaves — each path corresponds to a **cut set**. After Boolean reduction, the **Minimal Cut Sets** are found.
