# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Pattern]**

**"What is a Minimal Cut Set in Fault Tree Analysis? Why is the identification of Minimal Cut Sets considered the most important outcome of FTA?"**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

A **Cut Set** is any combination of basic events whose *simultaneous occurrence* causes the Top Event to occur. A **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** is a cut set from which *no single basic event can be removed* without losing the property that the set causes the Top Event. Formally, a cut set $K$ is minimal if no proper subset of $K$ is itself a cut set.

Identification of MCS is important for **three reasons**:

1. **Reveals single-point failures** — A 1st-order MCS (size = 1) means the system has no redundancy; if that single component fails, the system fails. **(1 mark)**
2. **Quantifies top event probability** — MCS enable direct application of the rare-event formula $Q_{TOP} \approx \sum P(K_i)$ to compute system unreliability. **(1 mark)**
3. **Guides design improvement** — Knowing the smallest (lowest-order) MCS allows engineers to prioritize adding redundancy to the most critical components. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — December 2023, Replicated Pattern]**

**"Distinguish between a deductive analysis method and an inductive analysis method. Identify whether FTA is deductive or inductive and justify your answer."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Aspect | Inductive Analysis | Deductive Analysis |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Direction | Bottom-up | Top-down |
| Question asked | "What can go wrong with this component?" | "What combinations of failures can cause this Top Event?" |
| Method example | FMEA, HAZOP | **FTA (Fault Tree Analysis)** |
| Starting point | Component-level failure mode | System-level undesired event |

**FTA is deductive** because it begins with a known *undesired system-level event* (the **Top Event**) and works **downward** through logic gates to identify the combinations of component-level failures that can produce it. This is the opposite of FMEA, which begins with a single component failure and works upward to assess its effect on the system. **(2 marks for justification)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Symbol Confusion:** Students frequently draw an AND-gate with a curved bottom and an OR-gate with a flat bottom. This single drawing error can cost 2-3 marks in the construction step.
> 2. **Forgetting Absorption:** After applying the distributive law, students often list *all* cut sets without eliminating supersets. The absorption law $A + AB = A$ is **mandatory** before declaring minimal cut sets.
> 3. **Rare-Event vs. Exact:** When computing $Q_{TOP}$ from a fault tree with OR-gates, the **exact** formula is $1 - \prod(1-q_i)$. The **rare-event approximation** is $\sum q_i$. Failing to specify which is being used costs 1-2 marks.
> 4. **Independence Assumption:** All $Q_{TOP}$ formulas assume *statistically independent* component failures. If common-cause failure (CCF) is suspected, the formulas must be modified — this is a 2-mark trap in KTU questions.
> 5. **Units:** Unreliability $Q$ is dimensionless (probability), not a failure rate. Confusing $Q$ with failure rate $\lambda$ is a common mistake.

---

### Question Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Module 2 ESE Pattern, Replicated]**

A chemical process has a Top Event *"Toxic Gas Release"* modeled by the following fault tree structure:

$$T = A + B \cdot C$$

where the intermediate events are:

$$A = X_1 + X_2$$

$$B = X_2 + X_3 \cdot X_4$$

$$C = X_1 + X_5$$

**Part (a)** [7 Marks] — Reduce this fault tree to its **Minimal Cut Sets** using Boolean algebra. Show all algebraic steps, the application of distributive law, and absorption.

**Part (b)** [7 Marks] — Given the component unreliabilities:

$$q_1 = 0.01,\;\; q_2 = 0.02,\;\; q_3 = 0.005,\;\; q_4 = 0.008,\;\; q_5 = 0.003$$

Compute the **Top Event probability** $Q_{TOP}$ using (i) the rare-event approximation, and (ii) the exact inclusion-exclusion formula. Comment on whether the rare-event approximation is justified.

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Substitute $A$, $B$, and $C$ into the Top Event expression:

$$T = (X_1 + X_2) + (X_2 + X_3 X_4)(X_1 + X_5)$$

**(1 mark)** for correct substitution.

**Step 2:** Expand the AND-gate output using the distributive law $A(B+C) = AB + AC$:

$$(X_2 + X_3 X_4)(X_1 + X_5) = X_2 X_1 + X_2 X_5 + X_3 X_4 X_1 + X_3 X_4 X_5$$

**(2 marks)** for correct expansion — each of the two terms in the first bracket multiplies with both terms in the second.

**Step 3:** Combine all terms into the OR-gate (sum-of-products form):

$$T = X_1 + X_2 + X_1 X_2 + X_2 X_5 + X_1 X_3 X_4 + X_3 X_4 X_5$$

**(1 mark)** for collecting the terms.

**Step 4:** Apply the absorption law $A + AB = A$:

- $X_1 + X_1 X_2 = X_1$ → $X_1 X_2$ absorbed by $X_1$
- $X_1 + X_1 X_3 X_4 = X_1$ → $X_1 X_3 X_4$ absorbed by $X_1$
- Remaining: $X_2$, $X_2 X_5$, $X_3 X_4 X_5$

**(2 marks)** for correct identification of all absorption pairs.

**Step 5:** State the final Minimal Cut Sets:

$$\boxed{\;\text{MCS} = \{X_1\},\;\;\{X_2\},\;\;\{X_2, X_5\},\;\;\{X_3, X_4, X_5\}\;}$$

**(1 mark)** for the final answer set.

**Result Summary:** Four minimal cut sets — two 1st-order (single-point failures $X_1$ and $X_2$), one 2nd-order ($\{X_2, X_5\}$), and one 3rd-order ($\{X_3, X_4, X_5\}$).

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Compute individual cut set probabilities (assuming independent components):

$$P(K_1) = P(X_1) = 0.010$$

$$P(K_2) = P(X_2) = 0.020$$

$$P(K_3) = P(X_2 \cap X_5) = (0.020)(0.003) = 6.0 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$P(K_4) = P(X_3 \cap X_4 \cap X_5) = (0.005)(0.008)(0.003) = 1.2 \times 10^{-7}$$

**(2 marks)** for correct products.

**Step 2:** Apply the rare-event approximation:

$$Q_{TOP, approx} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} P(K_i) = 0.010 + 0.020 + 6.0 \times 10^{-5} + 1.2 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$Q_{TOP, approx} = 0.03006 + 0.00000012 \approx 0.03006$$

**(1 mark)** for correct summation.

**Step 3:** Apply the exact inclusion-exclusion formula. For four cut sets, the formula involves 4 single terms, 6 pairwise intersections, 4 triple intersections, and 1 quadruple intersection:

$$Q_{TOP, exact} = \sum_i P(K_i) - \sum_{i<j} P(K_i \cap K_j) + \sum_{i<j<k} P(K_i \cap K_j \cap K_k) - P(K_1 \cap K_2 \cap K_3 \cap K_4)$$

**Observation:** $K_1$ and $K_2$ both contain the single-point failures $X_1$ and $X_2$, and they share the basic event $X_2$. All intersections involving $K_1$ and $K_2$ together with any other set reduce to $K_1 \cap K_2 = X_1 \cap X_2$.

Computing the dominant pairwise intersections:

$$P(K_1 \cap K_2) = (0.010)(0.020) = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P(K_1 \cap K_3) = (0.010)(0.020)(0.003) = 6.0 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$P(K_2 \cap K_3) = (0.020)(0.020)(0.003) = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$$

All other pairwise intersections are even smaller ($\sim 10^{-8}$ or less). **(1 mark)** for partial calculations and identification of dominant terms.

**Step 4:** Substituting (dropping terms of order $10^{-8}$ and smaller):

$$Q_{TOP, exact} \approx 0.03006 - 2.0 \times 10^{-4} - 6.0 \times 10^{-7} - 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$Q_{TOP, exact} \approx 0.03006 - 0.000201 \approx 0.02986$$

**(1 mark)** for the final numeric result.

**Step 5:** Comparison and comment:

$$\text{Relative Error} = \frac{0.03006 - 0.02986}{0.02986} \times 100\% \approx 0.67\%$$

The rare-event approximation **overestimates** $Q_{TOP}$ by less than 1%. Since the difference is small and the approximation is conservative (safe-side for risk assessment), **the rare-event approximation is justified** in this case. **(1 mark)** for the comparison and justified conclusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Valuation:** The examiner will deduct a mark if you do not explicitly mention *independence of basic events* and *conservatism (overestimation) of the rare-event bound*.

---

### Question Choice 2 (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Module 2 ESE Internal Choice Pattern]**

A pressure vessel protection system has the following fault tree:

- **Top Event** T: *"Vessel Overpressure Protection Fails"*
- T is output of an **OR-gate** with inputs $A$ and $B$
- $A$ is output of an **AND-gate** with inputs $X_1$ and $X_2$ (pressure sensor fails AND shutdown valve fails)
- $B$ is output of an **OR-gate** with inputs $X_3$ and $X_4$ (alarm fails OR rupture disk fails)

**Part (a)** [7 Marks] — Draw the complete fault tree with proper KTU-standard symbols, identify all events and gates, and determine the **Minimal Cut Sets** algebraically.

**Part (b)** [7 Marks] — Given $q_1 = 0.002, q_2 = 0.003, q_3 = 0.004, q_4 = 0.005$, compute the **Top Event unreliability** using the rare-event approximation. Then compute the **Birnbaum Importance** $I_B(X_3)$ and comment on which component is most critical.

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Write the structure function in Boolean form:

$$T = A + B = (X_1 \cdot X_2) + (X_3 + X_4)$$

**(1 mark)** for the structure function.

**Step 2:** Distribute and expand:

$$T = X_1 X_2 + X_3 + X_4$$

**Step 3:** Apply absorption law $X_3 + X_1 X_2 X_3 = X_3$ (not applicable here since $X_3$ and $X_4$ do not appear within other terms). All three terms are minimal.

**(2 marks)** for the correct expansion and absorption check.

**Step 4:** Final Minimal Cut Sets:

$$\boxed{\;\text{MCS} = \{X_1, X_2\},\;\; \{X_3\},\;\; \{X_4\}\;}$$

**(1 mark)** for the final answer.

**Step 5:** The fault tree diagram must include: Top Event in a rectangle at the top; OR-gate below it with two inputs $A$ and $B$; AND-gate producing $A$ from $X_1$ and $X_2$; OR-gate producing $B$ from $X_3$ and $X_4$. All four basic events must be in **circles** at the leaves. **(3 marks)** for the diagram — symbol correctness and gate types are critical.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Compute individual cut set probabilities:

$$P(K_1) = P(X_1 \cap X_2) = (0.002)(0.003) = 6.0 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P(K_2) = P(X_3) = 0.004$$

$$P(K_3) = P(X_4) = 0.005$$

**(1 mark)** for correct products.

**Step 2:** Apply the rare-event approximation:

$$Q_{TOP} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{3} P(K_i) = 6.0 \times 10^{-6} + 0.004 + 0.005$$

$$\boxed{\;Q_{TOP} \approx 0.009006\;}$$

**(1 mark)** for the summation.

**Step 3:** Compute Birnbaum Importance $I_B(X_3)$:

**Formula:** $I_B(i) = Q_{TOP}(q_i = 1) - Q_{TOP}(q_i = 0)$

Sub-case 1: Set $q_3 = 1$ (X_3 fails with certainty). This activates the cut set $K_2 = \{X_3\}$, so $Q_{TOP} = 1$ (full system failure because the OR-gate passes the failure up).

Sub-case 2: Set $q_3 = 0$ (X_3 perfect). The cut set $K_2$ is removed, leaving:

$$Q_{TOP}(q_3 = 0) \approx P(K_1) + P(K_3) = 6.0 \times 10^{-6} + 0.005 = 0.005006$$

Therefore:

$$I_B(X_3) = 1 - 0.005006 = 0.994994 \approx 0.995$$

**(2 marks)** for the correct sub-case evaluations.

**Step 4:** Compare importance across all four components. By symmetry of structure, $I_B(X_3) \approx I_B(X_4) \approx 0.995$ (both are 1st-order MCS). For $X_1$ or $X_2$:

$$I_B(X_1) = Q_{TOP}(q_1 = 1) - Q_{TOP}(q_1 = 0)$$

With $q_1 = 1$: $Q_{TOP} = 1$ (since the AND-gate $A$ produces failure).

With $q_1 = 0$: $Q_{TOP} = Q_{TOP}(q_1 = 0) \approx 0.009 + 0 = 0.009$ (the 2nd-order MCS $K_1$ is removed). So $I_B(X_1) \approx 0.991$.

**(1 mark)** for the comparison calculation.

**Step 5:** Conclusion:

The components $X_3$ and $X_4$ have **slightly higher** Birnbaum importance than $X_1$ and $X_2$. However, the **most engineering-critical** decision is to address the single-point failures: $X_3$ and $X_4$ should receive immediate reliability improvement (e.g., add redundancy, schedule preventive maintenance), because they are 1st-order MCS. **(1 mark)** for the engineering conclusion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision Checklist — Fault Tree Analysis**

- [x] **Definition:** FTA is a *deductive, top-down* failure analysis method developed by H. A. Watson (Bell Labs, 1962) for the U.S. Minuteman missile system.
- [x] **Symbols Mastery:** Rectangle = event, Circle = basic event, Diamond = undeveloped, House = external, Flat-bottom gate = AND, Curved-bottom gate = OR.
- [x] **Top Event:** The undesired system-level failure you are analyzing — must be defined precisely.
- [x] **Minimal Cut Set (MCS):** A cut set from which no event can be removed while still causing the Top Event. Determined by Boolean expansion + **Absorption Law** $A + AB = A$.
- [x] **Order of MCS:** A 1st-order MCS (size 1) is a **single-point failure** — highest priority in safety design.
- [x] **AND-gate probability:** $Q_{AND} = \prod q_i$ (exact for independent events).
- [x] **OR-gate probability (exact):** $Q_{OR} = 1 - \prod (1 - q_i)$.
- [x] **OR-gate probability (rare-event approx):** $Q_{OR} \approx \sum q_i$ — valid only when $q_i \ll 1$.
- [x] **Top Event from MCS (rare-event):** $Q_{TOP} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{m} P(K_i)$.
- [x] **Top Event from MCS (exact):** Inclusion-Exclusion principle — tractable only for $m \le 4$ cut sets.
- [x] **Birnbaum Importance:** $I_B(i) = Q(1_i) - Q(0_i)$ — measures sensitivity of Top Event to component $i$.
- [x] **Vesely-Fussell (Criticality) Importance:** $I_V(i) = q_i I_B(i) / Q_{TOP}$ — fractional contribution.
- [x] **Independence Assumption:** All formulas assume *statistically independent* failures. CCF (common-cause failure) requires modification.
- [x] **Three Boolean Reduction Methods:** Top-down substitution, Bottom-up substitution, MOCUS algorithm.
- [x] **Absorption Law is the KEY** to finding MCS — forgetting it leads to non-minimal cut sets and mark loss.
- [x] **FTA vs. FMEA:** FTA = deductive (top-down), FMEA = inductive (bottom-up). Often used together in industry.
- [x] **Engineering Use Cases:** Nuclear (NRC), Aerospace (FAA), Automotive (ISO 26262), Process safety (IEC 61511).
- [x] **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Drawing the fault tree with reversed gate symbols — costs 2-3 marks per gate.
- [x] **Strategic Tip:** When asked to compute $Q_{TOP}$, always state which method (rare-event vs. exact) you are using and justify why.
- [x] **Birnbaum Insight:** A component with high $I_B$ is *not necessarily* the one with high failure rate — the importance is structural, not just numerical.
- [x] **Single-Point Failure Rule:** If a 1st-order MCS exists, redundancy must be designed in. This is the most important actionable finding of FTA.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Event Tree Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** is an *inductive* (forward-looking) probabilistic risk assessment technique that graphically and quantitatively models the possible outcomes (success or failure) of safety functions following the occurrence of a specified **initiating event**. It uses a binary branching tree structure where each branch represents the success (YES / 1) or failure (NO / 0) of a designated safety function, and each terminal node represents a unique **end state** (accident sequence) with an associated probability and consequence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology (Exact Phrasing):**
> ETA is a "forward logic" diagram, in contrast to the "backward logic" of Fault Tree Analysis (FTA). It is also referred to as an **Accident Sequence Diagram** or **Event Sequence Diagram** in PRA literature (NUREG-1855, IAEA Safety Standards).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **fork in a road that keeps splitting**:

A train is moving along a single track (the **initiating event** has occurred — say, a sudden brake failure). Ahead of the train, there is a series of safety checkpoints:
- **Checkpoint 1:** Does the emergency braking system work? → YES (train stops) / NO (train continues).
- **Checkpoint 2:** Does the second-line backup brake work? → YES / NO.
- **Checkpoint 3:** Does the trackside sand barrier stop it? → YES / NO.

Each YES/NO creates a new branch. The final position of the train (i.e., the *consequence*: safe stop, minor collision, major derailment) is the **end state**. ETA simply maps out every possible journey of that train after the initiating event, multiplies the probability of each checkpoint outcome along the way, and tells you the likelihood of every final scenario.

### 1.3 Key Terminology with KTU-Standard Definitions

| Term | Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **Initiating Event (IE)** | The first event in an accident sequence whose occurrence places the system in an abnormal state requiring safety function response. |
| **Safety Function (SF)** | A pre-identified system/component designed to mitigate the consequences of the initiating event. |
| **Top Event** | The undesired event whose occurrence begins the event tree (synonymous with IE in ETA context). |
| **Branch Point / Node** | A decision point in the tree where a safety function either succeeds or fails. |
| **End State / Accident Sequence** | The terminal outcome after all safety functions have responded (or failed). |
| **Accident Sequence** | A unique path from initiating event to end state. |
| **Branch Probability** | The probability of success or failure of the corresponding safety function. |
| **Path Probability** | The product of all branch probabilities along a single accident sequence. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metric to Remember:** The fundamental calculation constant is the **branching probability**, with the boundary condition that for any binary safety function: $P(\text{success}) + P(\text{failure}) = 1$ (often written as $p + q = 1$, where $q$ is unavailability/unreliability).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

**Step 1 — Identify the Initiating Event (IE)**
The analysis begins by selecting an undesired initiating event (e.g., loss of coolant, pipe rupture, hydrocarbon release, aircraft engine failure). This is normally derived from a preliminary hazard analysis (PHA) or HAZOP study.

**Step 2 — Identify the Safety Functions in Chronological Order**
List all the protective systems that are designed to respond to the IE. These are arranged in a *time-sequential* order (functional sequence), not in random order. The order matters because later safety functions only act if earlier ones fail.

**Step 3 — Construct the Binary Branching Structure**
At each safety function, two branches emerge:
- **Upper branch (typically marked "Y" or "S" for SUCCESS):** The safety function performs as designed; consequence is mitigated.
- **Lower branch (typically marked "N" or "F" for FAILURE):** The safety function fails; consequences escalate.

**Step 4 — Define the End States**
After the final safety function, the terminal nodes are the **end states** — the set of all possible final accident outcomes (e.g., Safe Shutdown, Minor Release, Major Fire, Catastrophic Rupture).

**Step 5 — Assign Branch Probabilities**
For each safety function, assign the probability of success ($p_i$) and failure ($q_i = 1 - p_i$). These come from component reliability databases (e.g., OREDA, IEEE Std 500) or component-specific failure rate analysis using $\lambda$ and $\mu$ (failure and repair rates).

**Step 6 — Compute Path Probabilities**
For each accident sequence (path from IE to end state), multiply all the branch probabilities along that path.

**Step 7 — Aggregate and Evaluate**
- Sum the path probabilities leading to each end state.
- Multiply by the **frequency of the initiating event** to obtain the *frequency* of each end state.
- Compare against acceptance criteria (e.g., $\text{Frequency} \leq 10^{-6}$ per year — the ALARP/tolerability threshold).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(\text{path}_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} b_{i,k}$ | Probability of accident sequence $k$ (product of branch probabilities) | Dimensionless (probability) |
| 2 | $b_{i,k} \in \{p_i,\ q_i\}$ where $q_i = 1 - p_i$ | Branch probability for SF $i$ on path $k$ | $p_i + q_i = 1$ |
| 3 | $P(\text{End State}_j) = \sum_{k \in \text{ES}_j} P(\text{path}_k)$ | Sum of path probabilities leading to end state $j$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $f_j = f_{\text{IE}} \times P(\text{End State}_j)$ | Frequency of end state $j$ per unit time | (events)/(year) |
| 5 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability of component (assuming exponential distribution) | $t$ in hours, $\lambda$ in $\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $q = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Unreliability (probability of failure on demand) | For low $\lambda t$: $q \approx \lambda t$ |
| 7 | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | Mean Time To Failure | Hours |
| 8 | $\text{Risk} = \sum_j f_j \times C_j$ | Total risk (frequency × consequence) | Consequence units vary (deaths, $) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule (KTU Board favourite):** When $\lambda t \ll 1$ (typical for safety systems tested periodically), use the approximation $q \approx \lambda T_{\text{proof}}$ where $T_{\text{proof}}$ is the proof-test interval. This avoids the exponential and is the standard industrial shortcut.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear Power Industry:** The landmark **WASH-1400 Reactor Safety Study (Rasmussen Report, 1975)** and **NUREG-1855** use ETA as the primary methodology for Level-1, 2, and 3 Probabilistic Risk Assessments (PRA).
- **Offshore Oil & Gas:** Used to estimate the frequency of hydrocarbon releases, fires, and explosions on platforms (per NORSOK Z-013 and API RP 581).
- **Chemical Process Industry:** ETA complements HAZOP and LOPA for SIL (Safety Integrity Level) determination per **IEC 61511**.
- **Aerospace & Aviation:** Used in system safety assessment per MIL-STD-882.
- **KTU Industry Relevance (Petchem / Process Plants):** ETA is the backbone technique for determining whether a process plant meets the "Tolerable Risk" criteria mandated by the **Major Accident Hazard (MAH) rules** of the Factories Act and the **ONGC/HPCL safety guidelines** in India.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Path Probability of an Accident Sequence

Consider a system with an initiating event **IE** and a chain of $n$ safety functions $\text{SF}_1, \text{SF}_2, \dots, \text{SF}_n$ acting sequentially. The branches of safety function $i$ are mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive at each decision node (binary logic).

**Step 1 — Define the branch events.**
Let $A_i$ denote the event "Safety function $i$ succeeds" and $\overline{A_i}$ denote "Safety function $i$ fails". Then:
$$P(A_i) = p_i, \qquad P(\overline{A_i}) = q_i = 1 - p_i$$

**Step 2 — Express the accident sequence as a joint event.**
An accident sequence is a single path through the tree, e.g., $\text{IE} \rightarrow A_1 \rightarrow \overline{A_2} \rightarrow A_3 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \overline{A_n}$.

The probability of this specific sequence is:
$$P(\text{sequence}) = P(\text{IE}) \times P(A_1 \mid \text{IE}) \times P(\overline{A_2} \mid \text{IE} \cap A_1) \times \cdots$$

**Step 3 — Apply conditional independence (standard ETA assumption).**
ETA assumes the safety functions are **stochastically independent** (no common-cause failure coupling unless explicitly modeled). Hence:
$$P(A_2 \mid \text{IE} \cap A_1) = P(A_2) = p_2$$

**Step 4 — Collapse the joint probability to a product.**
$$P(\text{sequence}_k) = P(\text{IE}) \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} b_{i,k}$$

If we are working with **conditional** path probabilities (i.e., given that the IE has already occurred), we drop $P(\text{IE})$:
$$P(\text{path}_k \mid \text{IE}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} b_{i,k}$$

This is **Equation 1** of the KTU Formula Sheet.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A chemical reactor has an initiating event "loss of cooling" with frequency $f_{\text{IE}} = 10^{-2}$ per year. The following safety functions act in sequence:
- **SF1:** High-temperature alarm works. Failure probability $q_1 = 0.05$.
- **SF2:** Automatic cooling water valve opens. Failure probability $q_2 = 0.02$.
- **SF3:** Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) opens on demand. Failure probability $q_3 = 0.01$.

The end states are:
- **ES-A:** Safe Shutdown (all 3 SFs succeed)
- **ES-B:** Controlled Shutdown (SF1 fails, SF2 + SF3 succeed)
- **ES-C:** Localized Release (SF1 + SF2 fail, SF3 succeeds)
- **ES-D:** Catastrophic Rupture (all 3 SFs fail)

**Find:** Frequency of each end state.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — List the 8 paths and their branch outcomes.**
Since there are 3 binary SFs, the tree has $2^3 = 8$ paths. Grouping them by end state:

| Path | SF1 | SF2 | SF3 | End State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | S ($p_1=0.95$) | S ($p_2=0.98$) | S ($p_3=0.99$) | ES-A |
| 2 | F ($q_1=0.05$) | S | S | ES-B |
| 3 | S | F ($q_2=0.02$) | S | ES-C |
| 4 | S | S | F ($q_3=0.01$) | ES-A (still safe) |
| 5 | F | F | S | ES-D (rupture path begins) |
| 6 | F | S | F | ES-B |
| 7 | S | F | F | ES-C |
| 8 | F | F | F | ES-D |

*(Note: the end-state mapping depends on system logic — re-classified here per the problem's stated end states.)*

**Step 2 — Compute the conditional path probability for ES-D (the worst case).**
$$P(\text{ES-D} \mid \text{IE}) = q_1 \cdot q_2 \cdot q_3 = 0.05 \times 0.02 \times 0.01$$

$$P(\text{ES-D} \mid \text{IE}) = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the absolute frequency of ES-D.**
$$f_D = f_{\text{IE}} \times P(\text{ES-D} \mid \text{IE}) = 10^{-2} \times 10^{-5}$$

$$f_D = 10^{-7} \text{ events/year}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
The catastrophic rupture frequency is $10^{-7}$/year, which is **below** the typical industrial tolerability threshold of $10^{-6}$/year. The system therefore meets the ALARP criterion.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ETA vs. FTA

| Feature | Event Tree Analysis (ETA) | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) |
|---|---|---|
| Logic direction | Inductive (forward) | Deductive (backward) |
| Starting point | Initiating event | Undesired top event |
| Branching | Binary at each safety function | Boolean gates (AND/OR) |
| Output | End-state frequencies | Minimal cut sets |
| Strength | Shows accident sequence progression | Identifies root-cause combinations |
| Weakness | Requires pre-defined IEs and SFs | Can become combinatorially explosive |
| Best use | Consequence-side quantification | Cause-side quantification |
| KTU 2024 context | Used with human reliability analysis | Used with component failure data |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Event Tree (Mermaid)

The diagram below maps the logical architecture of constructing an ETA, from initiating event identification through end-state frequency calculation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Step 1 Define Initiating Event IE from PHA HAZOP"] --> A2["Step 2 List Sequential Safety Functions SF1 SF2 SFn"]
    A2 --> A3["Step 3 Build Binary Branching Tree Success and Failure at each node"]
    A3 --> A4["Step 4 Assign Branch Probabilities from OREDA IEEE500 LambdaT"]
    A4 --> A5["Step 5 Compute Path Probabilities Product of branches per sequence"]
    A5 --> A6["Step 6 Aggregate to End States Sum paths per terminal node"]
    A6 --> A7["Step 7 Multiply by fIE to obtain End State Frequency"]
    A7 --> A8["Step 8 Compare with Tolerability Criteria ALARP Threshold 1E-6 per year"]
    A8 --> A9["Step 9 Document Mitigations and Update SIL Targets"]

    A9 --> B1["Output Set of Frequencies fA fB fC fD"]
    A9 --> B2["Output Risk Curve F N Curve for End States"]
    A9 --> B3["Output Insights for IEC 61511 SIL Assignment"]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of a Sample Event Tree (Mermaid)

The schematic below shows a 3-safety-function event tree for the worked numerical example (Section 3.2). Each end state is annotated with its computed frequency.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IE["INITIATING EVENT Loss of Cooling fIE 1E-2 per year"] --> SF1{"SF1 Alarm Works"}
    SF1 -- Success p1 0.95 --> SF2a{"SF2 Cooling Valve"}
    SF1 -- Failure q1 0.05 --> SF2b{"SF2 Cooling Valve"}
    SF2a -- Success p2 0.98 --> SF3a{"SF3 PRV Opens"}
    SF2a -- Failure q2 0.02 --> SF3b{"SF3 PRV Opens"}
    SF2b -- Success p2 0.98 --> SF3c{"SF3 PRV Opens"}
    SF2b -- Failure q2 0.02 --> SF3d{"SF3 PRV Opens"}
    SF3a -- Success p3 0.99 --> ESA["ES-A Safe Shutdown f 9.22E-3 per year"]
    SF3a -- Failure q3 0.01 --> ESC["ES-C Localized Release f 1.88E-5 per year"]
    SF3b -- Success p3 0.99 --> ESB["ES-B Controlled Shutdown f 9.41E-5 per year"]
    SF3b -- Failure q3 0.01 --> ESD["ES-D Catastrophic Rupture f 1.00E-7 per year"]
    SF3c -- Success p3 0.99 --> ESB
    SF3c -- Failure q3 0.01 --> ESD
    SF3d -- Success p3 0.99 --> ESC
    SF3d -- Failure q3 0.01 --> ESD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** Each terminal node displays the *absolute frequency* (i.e., $f_{\text{IE}} \times$ conditional path probability). Notice that the **Catastrophic Rupture** frequency ($10^{-7}$/year) is two orders of magnitude below the **Localized Release** frequency ($10^{-5}$/year) — this is the typical "bow-tie" risk profile in a well-designed process plant.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"What is an Event Tree Analysis (ETA)? List its four essential components."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Event Tree Analysis (ETA) is a forward-looking, inductive safety analysis technique used to model the possible outcomes of an engineered system after the occurrence of a specified initiating event. It traces the success or failure of each safety function in chronological order and assigns a probability to every resulting end state.

The four essential components are:
1. **Initiating Event (IE):** The first undesired event that starts the analysis.
2. **Safety Functions (SFs):** A sequential set of protective systems that respond to the IE.
3. **Branches:** Binary YES/NO outcomes at each safety function.
4. **End States:** The final outcomes (accident sequences) with associated frequencies.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Differentiate between Event Tree Analysis (ETA) and Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) in terms of logic direction, starting point, and primary output."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | ETA | FTA |
|---|---|---|
| Logic direction | Inductive (forward) | Deductive (backward) |
| Starting point | Initiating event | Undesired top event |
| Primary output | End-state frequencies and accident sequences | Minimal cut sets (MCS) listing root-cause combinations |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** A reactor vessel in a petrochemical plant has an initiating event "**overpressure due to blocked outlet**" with a frequency of $5 \times 10^{-2}$ per year. Three safety functions are designed to act in sequence:
- **SF1:** Blockage detection sensor operates. Unreliability $q_1 = 0.01$.
- **SF2:** Operator intervenes within 30 minutes. Human Error Probability HEP $= 0.05$.
- **SF3:** Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) opens on demand. Unreliability $q_3 = 0.02$.

Define the four end states (Safe, Controlled, Localized, Catastrophic). **Compute the frequency of the catastrophic end state.** *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss, with a labelled sketch, the **construction procedure of an Event Tree**. Explain why the order of safety functions in the tree must be *chronological* and not *arbitrary*. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Solution:**

Step 1 — Define end states based on the problem convention:
- **ES-A — Safe Shutdown:** All 3 SFs succeed.
- **ES-B — Controlled Shutdown:** SF1 fails, but SF2 and SF3 succeed.
- **ES-C — Localized Release:** SF1 and SF2 fail, SF3 succeeds.
- **ES-D — Catastrophic Rupture:** All 3 SFs fail.

Step 2 — Compute the conditional path probability for the catastrophic sequence (F-F-F):
$$P(\text{ES-D} \mid \text{IE}) = q_1 \times \text{HEP} \times q_3$$

$$P(\text{ES-D} \mid \text{IE}) = 0.01 \times 0.05 \times 0.02$$

$$P(\text{ES-D} \mid \text{IE}) = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$$

Step 3 — Compute the absolute frequency:
$$f_D = f_{\text{IE}} \times P(\text{ES-D} \mid \text{IE})$$

$$f_D = 5 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{f_D = 5.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ events/year}}$$

Step 4 — Verification against tolerability: $5 \times 10^{-7}$/year $< 10^{-6}$/year → **ACCEPTABLE per ALARP.**

---

**(b) Solution:**

The construction procedure of an Event Tree is the **7-step procedure** described in Section 2.1. A labelled sketch is provided below:

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | Identify the **initiating event** (leftmost node) |
| 2 | List the **safety functions in chronological order** (top-to-bottom header) |
| 3 | Draw the **binary branches** (upper = success, lower = failure) |
| 4 | Define the **end states** (rightmost column) |
| 5 | Assign **branch probabilities** ($p_i$ and $q_i = 1 - p_i$) |
| 6 | Compute **path probabilities** (product rule) |
| 7 | Multiply by $f_{\text{IE}}$ to obtain **end-state frequencies** |

**Why chronological order is mandatory:**
- The operation of later safety functions is **physically contingent** on the failure of earlier ones. For example, a Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) only has to open if the *alarm* and *operator intervention* have both failed.
- If the order is shuffled, the *path probabilities* become incorrect because the conditional independence assumption is violated.
- Chronological order is also the only way to **represent the actual accident sequence** as it unfolds in time, which is the entire purpose of ETA.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Past Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** An offshore platform has an initiating event "**small hydrocarbon leak on the process deck**" with $f_{\text{IE}} = 10^{-1}$/year. Two safety functions act:
- **SF1:** Gas detection system isolates the leak. $q_1 = 0.10$.
- **SF2:** Emergency Shutdown (ESD) system closes isolation valves. $q_2 = 0.05$.

Draw the complete event tree and **compute the frequency of the "Un-isolated Leak leading to Fire/Explosion" end state.** *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A component has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 10^{-5}$ per hour. It is proof-tested every 2 years (17,520 hours). Using the approximation $q \approx \lambda T_{\text{proof}}$, compute its **probability of failure on demand** and explain its significance in Event Tree Analysis. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Solution:**

Step 1 — Draw the event tree. With 2 binary safety functions, there are $2^2 = 4$ paths.

| Path | SF1 | SF2 | End State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | S ($0.90$) | S ($0.95$) | Safe — leak isolated and shut down |
| 2 | F ($0.10$) | S ($0.95$) | Un-isolated Leak (SF2 acts directly) |
| 3 | S | F ($0.05$) | Un-isolated Leak (only SF1 acts) |
| 4 | F ($0.10$) | F ($0.05$) | Fire / Explosion (catastrophic) |

Step 2 — Compute the conditional probability of the **Fire/Explosion** end state (Path 4 only):
$$P(\text{F/E} \mid \text{IE}) = q_1 \times q_2 = 0.10 \times 0.05$$

$$P(\text{F/E} \mid \text{IE}) = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

Step 3 — Compute absolute frequency:
$$f_{\text{F/E}} = f_{\text{IE}} \times P(\text{F/E} \mid \text{IE})$$

$$f_{\text{F/E}} = 10^{-1} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{f_{\text{F/E}} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ events/year}}$$

This is **above** the $10^{-6}$/year tolerability threshold. The system **does not meet ALARP** — design changes are required (e.g., add a third independent safety function or improve ESD reliability).

---

**(b) Solution:**

Step 1 — Given $\lambda = 10^{-5}$/hour and $T_{\text{proof}} = 17{,}520$ hours.

Step 2 — Apply the standard approximation (valid because $\lambda T \ll 1$):
$$q \approx \lambda T_{\text{proof}}$$

$$q \approx 10^{-5} \times 17{,}520$$

$$\boxed{q \approx 0.1752 \quad \text{(i.e., } 17.52\% \text{ probability of failure on demand)}}$$

Step 3 — Significance in ETA:
- The value $q = 0.1752$ is the **branch failure probability** that would be entered into the event tree for this safety function.
- It is **unacceptably high** for a safety-critical function (industrial target is typically $q \leq 10^{-2}$ for SIL 1, $\leq 10^{-3}$ for SIL 2, etc., per IEC 61511).
- This immediately flags the component for either: (i) more frequent proof-testing, (ii) redundant parallel installation, or (iii) replacement with a more reliable design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $f_{\text{IE}}$.** Many students compute only the *conditional* path probability and report it as the final frequency. **0 marks** for the frequency step if this is omitted.
> 2. **Mixing up success and failure branches.** The convention is upper = success. Inverting this gives a wrong end-state mapping.
> 3. **Summing path probabilities to check = 1.** A frequent board check: if your 4 paths in a 2-SF tree do not sum to 1.0, you have made an arithmetic error.
> 4. **Ignoring conditional independence.** If two safety functions share a common cause (e.g., same power supply, same operator, same sensor), you **must** model the common-cause failure (CCF) parameter $\beta$ — otherwise your risk is under-estimated. This is a common **5-mark penalty** in Part B answers.
> 5. **Not stating the ALARP/tolerability comparison.** A numerical answer without an engineering interpretation loses the "engineering judgment" marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ETA is an **inductive, forward-logic** safety analysis method.
- The **initiating event** starts the tree on the left; **end states** terminate it on the right.
- Each **safety function** introduces a binary branch (Success / Failure).
- **Path probability** = product of all branch probabilities along the path.
- **End-state probability** = sum of all path probabilities leading to that end state.
- **End-state frequency** = $f_{\text{IE}} \times P(\text{end state} \mid \text{IE})$ — this is the KTU Board's favourite final-step calculation.
- Safety functions must be in **chronological (time-sequential) order** — never arbitrary.
- The approximation $q \approx \lambda T_{\text{proof}}$ is the industry-standard shortcut for low-$\lambda T$ components.
- ETA assumes **stochastic independence** of safety functions unless a Common-Cause Failure (CCF, parameter $\beta$) is explicitly modeled.
- ETA is the **consequence-side** complement to FTA; together they form the **"bow-tie"** risk picture.
- Tolerability benchmark: **$10^{-6}$/year** for individual risk; **$10^{-5}$/year** for societal risk F/N curve (Farmsum, Rijnmond, HSE UK standards).
- For KTU 2024 ESE, always: (i) draw the tree, (ii) label all branches, (iii) show the multiplication, (iv) state the end-state frequency, (v) compare with the ALARP threshold.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Mode and Effect Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITUM RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Failure Mode and Effect Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, **bottom-up**, **inductive**, **qualitative (and semi-quantitative)** analytical technique used to identify, evaluate, and prioritize potential failure modes of a system, design, process, or service by determining the **effect** of each failure on mission success, personnel safety, system performance, and operational availability.

The methodology establishes a structured framework to:

1. **Identify** how a component, subsystem, or process can fail (the *Failure Mode*).
2. **Assess** the *consequences* of that failure (the *Effect* on system, mission, personnel, and environment).
3. **Determine** the *root cause* of failure (the *Cause / Mechanism*).
4. **Quantify** the risk using the triplet **(Severity, Occurrence, Detection)**.
5. **Prioritize** corrective actions through the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Governing Standards & References (Verifiable):**
> - **IEC 60812:2015** — *Analysis techniques for system reliability — Procedure for failure mode and effects analysis (FMEA)*.
> - **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (1st Edition, 2019)** — Used globally in automotive and manufacturing sectors.
> - **MIL-STD-1629A (1980)** — *Procedures for Performing a Failure Mode, Effects and Criticality Analysis* (US DoD, military/aerospace standard).
> - **SAE J1739** — *Potential Failure Mode and Effects Analysis in Design and Manufacturing* (Society of Automotive Engineers).

---

## 1.2 Classification of FMEA (Three Principal Variants)

| FMEA Type | Scope of Analysis | Typical Application Stage |
|---|---|---|
| **System FMEA (SFMEA)** | Complete functional system, including interfaces, software, and operational environment. | Conceptual / Preliminary Design |
| **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** | Component- and sub-assembly-level design of a product prior to production release. | Detailed Design Phase |
| **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** | Manufacturing and assembly operations, including machines, tooling, gauges, and human factors. | Process Planning / Pilot Production |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board examinations, when a generic question on "FMEA" appears, the expected answer is the **Design FMEA** form, as it is the most frequently cited variant in PECHT754 case studies.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pre-Monsoon House Inspection" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine your home just before the Kerala monsoon arrives. You walk room by room, **inspection block-by-block**, and ask three simple questions:
>
> 1. *"How could this *break*?"* — The **Failure Mode** (e.g., roof tile crack, window seal failure).
> 2. *"What will happen if it *does* break?"* — The **Effect** (e.g., water seepage → wall short-circuit → fire risk).
> 3. *"Why *might* it break?"* — The **Cause** (e.g., aging sealant, poor workmanship).
>
> For each defect, you rate three quantities:
> - **Severity (S):** How *bad* would the damage be? (1 = cosmetic spot, 10 = full house collapse)
> - **Occurrence (O):** How *likely* is it? (1 = once in 1000 years, 10 = almost every monsoon)
> - **Detectability (D):** How *hard* is it to spot *before* the disaster? (1 = obvious leak, 10 = invisible until ceiling caves in)
>
> You multiply S × O × D to get a **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**, and you fix the highest RPNs first.
>
> **FMEA is exactly this — but applied to engineered systems instead of homes.**

---

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics Introduced in FMEA

- **S — Severity Rating:** A dimensionless ordinal integer in **[1, 10]**.
- **O — Occurrence Rating:** A dimensionless ordinal integer in **[1, 10]**.
- **D — Detection Rating:** A dimensionless ordinal integer in **[1, 10]**.
- **RPN — Risk Priority Number:** A composite ordinal metric defined as:

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

- **SCD — Severity Category Decomposition:** used in the **AIAG-VDA 2019** handbook as an alternative to RPN, replacing the multiplicative model with **Action Priority (AP)** levels: **High**, **Medium**, **Low**.

> **Boundary of RPN:** Theoretical maximum = $10 \times 10 \times 10 = \mathbf{1000}$. Any item with RPN > 100 is conventionally flagged for **mandatory corrective action** (this threshold is application-specific, but is the most cited benchmark in industrial practice).

---

## 1.5 Difference Between FMEA and Criticality Analysis (CA)

> [!NOTE]
> Often confused, but they are **complementary**:
>
> - **FMEA** = *What can fail, and what happens?* (Qualitative + semi-quantitative)
> - **Criticality Analysis (CA)** = *How much does each failure contribute to system unreliability?* (Quantitative, uses failure rate data $\lambda$ in failures per million hours, **FPMH**).
>
> A **FMECA** = FMEA + Criticality Analysis (combined methodology).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Eight Pillars of a Standard FMEA Worksheet

A KTU-compliant FMEA table contains the following eight analytical columns (mapped to the IEC 60812:2015 and AIAG-VDA structures):

1. **Item / Function** — The component or process step being analyzed.
2. **Failure Mode** — The manner in which the item fails to perform its intended function.
3. **Effect** — The *consequence* on the system, mission, personnel, or environment.
4. **Severity (S)** — Numeric rating of consequence severity (scale 1–10).
5. **Cause / Mechanism** — The *root cause* that triggers the failure mode.
6. **Occurrence (O)** — Numeric rating of cause frequency (scale 1–10).
7. **Current Controls** — Detection mechanisms already in place.
8. **Detection (D)** — Numeric rating of *detectability* before failure reaches the customer (scale 1–10).
9. **RPN** — Computed product $S \times O \times D$.
10. **Recommended Action** — Mitigation measure with assigned responsibility and target completion date.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exam answers, **always present FMEA in tabular form** with at least **5 fully populated rows** to secure full marks. Examiners explicitly look for the column sequence: *Item → Mode → Effect → S → Cause → O → D → RPN → Action*.

---

## 2.2 Standard 1–10 Rating Scales (Reproduced From Industry Convention)

### 2.2.1 Severity (S) Scale

| Rating | Effect on System | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | Hazardous — loss of life / safety violation | Brake failure in a car |
| 9 | Major — loss of primary function, no warning | Engine seizure |
| 8 | Major — loss of primary function with warning | Check-engine light on |
| 7 | Significant — degraded primary function | Reduced acceleration |
| 6 | Loss of secondary function | Power window failure |
| 5 | Loss of tertiary function | AC compressor noise |
| 4 | Minor — noticed by most customers | Slight rattle |
| 3 | Minor — noticed by some customers | Cosmetic blemish |
| 2 | Minor — noticed by discerning customers | Slight finish mismatch |
| 1 | None — no effect | Within tolerance |

### 2.2.2 Occurrence (O) Scale (Failures per Item)

| Rating | Failure Rate (per million items) | Equivalent Cpk Description |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | $\geq 100{,}000$ | Very high — almost inevitable |
| 9 | $50{,}000$ | Failure persistent |
| 8 | $10{,}000$ | High — frequent failure |
| 7 | $5{,}000$ | High — occasional failure |
| 6 | $2{,}000$ | Moderate — frequent minor failures |
| 5 | $1{,}000$ | Moderate |
| 4 | $500$ | Moderately low |
| 3 | $100$ | Low |
| 2 | $50$ | Low |
| 1 | $\leq 1$ | Remote — failure unlikely |

### 2.2.3 Detection (D) Scale

> [!NOTE]
> Crucial: **D = 1** means the failure is **highly detectable** (easy to spot before reaching the customer). **D = 10** means the failure is **virtually undetectable** (reaches the customer before discovery).

| Rating | Detection Capability | Probability of Detection |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | Absolute uncertainty | Cannot detect — no controls |
| 9 | Very remote | Very low detection probability |
| 8 | Remote | Low detection probability |
| 7 | Very low | Low-to-moderate |
| 6 | Low | Moderate |
| 5 | Moderate | Moderate |
| 4 | Moderately high | Moderately high |
| 3 | High | High |
| 2 | Very high | Very high |
| 1 | Almost certain | Automated detection — 100% |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> This is the **cheat sheet** you must memorize for the board exam. Each formula is tested directly or in a worked-numerical form.

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units / Range | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{RPN}$ | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Dimensionless, $[1, 1000]$ | Risk prioritization in FMEA |
| $\text{CA}$ (Criticality) | $\text{CA} = S \times O \times \beta$ | Dimensionless, $[1, 1000]$ | Used in MIL-STD-1629A |
| $\beta$ | Mission-failure conditional probability | $[0, 1]$ | Probability that the failure mode causes mission loss |
| $C_m$ (Mode Criticality) | $C_m = \alpha \times \lambda_p \times t$ | Dimensionless | Quantitative criticality from failure rate |
| $\lambda_p$ | Part failure rate | failures / $10^6$ hours | From reliability prediction handbooks |
| $t$ | Mission duration | hours | Operating interval of analysis |
| $\alpha$ | Failure mode ratio | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of $\lambda_p$ that causes the specific mode |
| $\text{AP (Action Priority)}$ | Look-up table from $(S, O, D)$ | High / Medium / Low | AIAG-VDA 2019 replacement of RPN |
| $\text{Pareto Threshold}$ | Items with top 20% RPN | — | 80/20 rule for action prioritization |

---

## 2.4 The FMEA Logic Chain — Step-by-Step Reasoning

> The **Why** and **How** behind each step, written for clarity:

1. **Define the System Boundary** — *Why?* To prevent scope creep. The boundary tells the analyst what is *inside* the analysis and what is an external interface.
2. **Build the Block Diagram** — *How?* A functional block diagram showing interconnections. Every block is a candidate failure item.
3. **Establish Ground Rules / Assumptions** — *Why?* To ensure that severity ratings are consistent across analysts. Use an *FMEA Team Charter*.
4. **Identify Failure Modes per Block** — *How?* Use the four-function taxonomy: *No function / Partial function / Degraded function / Over-function*.
5. **Determine Effects (Local → Next-Level → End-Item → Mission)** — *Why?* A failure can be locally benign but mission-critical. Always trace the chain to the system level.
6. **Assign Severity (S)** — *How?* Use the standardized 1–10 table. Severity is **always independent** of Occurrence and Detection.
7. **Identify Causes / Mechanisms** — *How?* Use techniques like *5-Why analysis*, *Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)*, or *Ishikawa (Fishbone)* diagrams.
8. **Assign Occurrence (O)** — *How?* Reference historical failure data (e.g., warranty databases, FRACAS — Failure Reporting, Analysis, and Corrective Action System).
9. **Identify Current Detection Controls** — *Why?* To determine whether the team *currently* can detect the failure mode before delivery.
10. **Assign Detection (D)** — *How?* Use the standardized 1–10 table. D evaluates the *worst-case* detection chain.
11. **Compute RPN** — *Why?* To rank-order failures for action.
12. **Take Action** — *How?* For high-RPN items, define countermeasures, **re-rate** S, O, D after action, and **re-compute** the new RPN.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Mandatory RPN analysis for safety-critical subsystems (brakes, airbags, steering) per IATF 16949.
- **Aerospace:** Required by AS9100D; failure analysis uses MIL-STD-1629A criticality matrices.
- **Medical Devices:** Mandatory under **ISO 14971:2019** (Risk management for medical devices) — FMEA is the primary tool.
- **Process Industry:** Used in **HAZOP** and **LOPA** studies as a complementary technique.
- **Software Engineering:** **Software FMEA (SFMEA)** and **Software FMEA with Criticality (SWFMECA)** follow the same S–O–D paradigm.

---

## 2.6 Limitations of Traditional RPN Method

> [!WARNING]
> The classical RPN has documented limitations that the AIAG-VDA 2019 Handbook addresses:
>
> 1. **RPN obscures severity** — Two items with RPN = 50 can have S = 5, O = 5, D = 2 versus S = 50, O = 1, D = 1. The first is *more dangerous*; the second is a minor nuisance. RPN treats them identically.
> 2. **No reflection of high-S, low-O, low-D** cases (e.g., S = 10, O = 1, D = 1 → RPN = 10, but the failure is *catastrophic* if it occurs).
> 3. **Threshold ambiguity** — A rigid 100-cut-off ignores application-specific risk tolerance.
> 4. **Subjective ratings** — Inter-rater reliability is notoriously low.
> 5. **The Action Priority (AP) approach** in AIAG-VDA 2019 fixes items 1 and 2 by emphasizing **Severity** as the dominant axis.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Industrial Pump System (KTU Board Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **fully worked FMEA** of a centrifugal pump used in a chemical process plant. The question is typical of KTU Part B 14-mark questions.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

> *"A centrifugal pump in a chemical plant is used to transfer a corrosive solvent at high temperature. Perform a Design FMEA on this pump. Identify at least 5 failure modes, rate them on the S–O–D scale, compute the RPN, and recommend corrective actions for the top-ranked mode. Assume standard 1–10 rating scales."*

### 3.1.2 System Block Diagram (Textual)

The pump consists of:

- **Block P1:** Impeller (rotating element that imparts kinetic energy to fluid)
- **Block P2:** Mechanical Seal (prevents leakage along shaft)
- **Block P3:** Casing (volute, contains pressure)
- **Block P4:** Bearings (support shaft, allow rotation)
- **Block P5:** Coupling (transmits motor torque to pump shaft)

### 3.1.3 FMEA Worksheet — Full Derivation Row by Row

For each block, we derive a single failure mode below. The RPN is computed as the explicit product of three integers.

#### **Row 1 — Impeller (Block P1)**

- **Failure Mode (FM1.1):** Impeller vane erosion.
- **Effect:** Loss of head capacity → process flow rate falls below design point → downstream reactor receives insufficient solvent → production shutdown (Estimated loss: ₹ 2 lakh/hour).
- **Severity (S):** Rating = **7** (significant — loss of primary function, no immediate safety hazard).
- **Cause:** Cavitation due to low NPSH + corrosive fluid attack.
- **Occurrence (O):** Rating = **5** (moderate — occurs roughly 1 in 1000 pump-hours based on plant FRACAS data).
- **Current Controls:** Quarterly visual inspection; vibration monitoring.
- **Detection (D):** Rating = **4** (moderately high — vibration trend catches it within 2 weeks).
- **RPN Calculation:**

$$\text{RPN}_1 = S \times O \times D = 7 \times 5 \times 4 = \mathbf{140}$$

- **Recommended Action:** Upgrade to **duplex stainless-steel impeller (SS-2205)**; install **inline cavitation monitor**.

#### **Row 2 — Mechanical Seal (Block P2)**

- **Failure Mode (FM2.1):** Seal face wear → leakage.
- **Effect:** Solvent leakage creates slip hazard + fire risk (flash point 38°C) → potential plant incident.
- **Severity (S):** Rating = **9** (major — safety hazard with warning).
- **Cause:** Abrasive solids in fluid + inadequate flushing.
- **Occurrence (O):** Rating = **6** (moderate — once in 2000 seal-hours).
- **Current Controls:** Daily leak-check by operator; weekly seal-flush flow verification.
- **Detection (D):** Rating = **5** (moderate — leak usually visible within 24 hours).
- **RPN Calculation:**

$$\text{RPN}_2 = 9 \times 6 \times 5 = \mathbf{270}$$

- **Recommended Action:** Switch to **tungsten-carbide seal faces**; install **continuous seal-flush flow transmitter** with alarm.

#### **Row 3 — Casing (Block P3)**

- **Failure Mode (FM3.1):** Casing corrosion pitting.
- **Effect:** Casing wall thinning → eventual rupture → catastrophic solvent release.
- **Severity (S):** Rating = **10** (hazardous — potential fatality, environmental release).
- **Cause:** Internal corrosion from chloride-bearing solvent + stagnant pockets.
- **Occurrence (O):** Rating = **3** (low — once per 5 years based on similar units).
- **Current Controls:** Annual ultrasonic thickness (UT) survey.
- **Detection (D):** Rating = **7** (very low — UT is a manual, scheduled activity; small pits easily missed).
- **RPN Calculation:**

$$\text{RPN}_3 = 10 \times 3 \times 7 = \mathbf{210}$$

- **Recommended Action:** Apply **PTFE-based internal coating**; install **online corrosion-rate probes**.

#### **Row 4 — Bearings (Block P4)**

- **Failure Mode (FM4.1):** Premature bearing fatigue.
- **Effect:** Shaft seizure → pump stops → production loss + secondary damage to impeller and seal.
- **Severity (S):** Rating = **8** (major — loss of primary function with warning).
- **Cause:** Improper lubrication + radial load misalignment.
- **Occurrence (O):** Rating = **5** (moderate).
- **Current Controls:** Vibration analysis; monthly greasing.
- **Detection (D):** Rating = **3** (high — vibration trend catches it weeks in advance).
- **RPN Calculation:**

$$\text{RPN}_4 = 8 \times 5 \times 3 = \mathbf{120}$$

- **Recommended Action:** Use **automatic grease-matic lubricators**; install **laser alignment tool** at every overhaul.

#### **Row 5 — Coupling (Block P5)**

- **Failure Mode (FM5.1):** Coupling element (rubber spider) failure.
- **Effect:** Torque transmission interrupted → pump stops.
- **Severity (S):** Rating = **6** (loss of secondary function — production impact but no safety issue).
- **Cause:** Rubber aging + chemical exposure.
- **Occurrence (O):** Rating = **7** (high — every 6–8 months).
- **Current Controls:** Visual inspection at every preventive maintenance.
- **Detection (D):** Rating = **3** (high — visual cracks are obvious).
- **RPN Calculation:**

$$\text{RPN}_5 = 6 \times 7 \times 3 = \mathbf{126}$$

- **Recommended Action:** Replace rubber spider with **polyurethane element rated for hydrocarbon service**; reduce PM interval to 3 months.

### 3.1.4 Consolidated FMEA Table

| Row | Item | Failure Mode | Effect | S | Cause | O | Current Control | D | RPN | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Impeller | Vane erosion | Loss of head → reactor feed loss | 7 | Cavitation + corrosion | 5 | Vibration monitor | 4 | 140 | Duplex SS impeller |
| 2 | Mechanical Seal | Face wear | Solvent leak → fire risk | 9 | Abrasive + no flush | 6 | Daily leak check | 5 | **270** | WC faces + flush flow Tx |
| 3 | Casing | Pitting corrosion | Wall rupture → catastrophic release | 10 | Chloride attack | 3 | Annual UT | 7 | 210 | PTFE coating + probes |
| 4 | Bearings | Fatigue | Shaft seizure | 8 | Bad lubrication | 5 | Vibration analysis | 3 | 120 | Auto lubricator + laser align |
| 5 | Coupling | Spider failure | Torque loss → stop | 6 | Aging rubber | 7 | Visual PM | 3 | 126 | Polyurethane element |

### 3.1.5 Ranking and Decision Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Pareto Principle Applied:**
>
> The top-ranked failure mode is the **Mechanical Seal** with RPN = 270.
>
> The **second-ranked** is the **Casing Pitting** with RPN = 210.
>
> Both exceed the conventional action threshold of **RPN > 100**, but the *seal* has a higher action priority due to combined high-S, high-O, and moderate-D — meaning the failure is *severe*, *frequent*, and *only moderately detectable*.

### 3.1.6 Post-Action RPN Re-Rating (Closure of the FMEA Loop)

After implementing the recommended actions, the team re-rated the seal failure:

- New Severity: remains **9** (inherent to leakage in flammable fluid).
- New Occurrence: drops to **2** (low — WC faces + flush eliminates 80% of causes).
- New Detection: drops to **2** (very high — continuous flow transmitter with 100% automatic alarm).

$$\text{RPN}_{\text{new}} = 9 \times 2 \times 2 = \mathbf{36}$$

$$\text{Reduction Factor} = \frac{\text{RPN}_{\text{old}} - \text{RPN}_{\text{new}}}{\text{RPN}_{\text{old}}} = \frac{270 - 36}{270} = 0.8667 \equiv \mathbf{86.7\%}$$

> The 86.7% reduction demonstrates the **closure and effectiveness verification** step of FMEA — a mandatory component of IATF 16949 and AS9100D.

---

## 3.2 Criticality Analysis (CA) Numerical Derivation — Per MIL-STD-1629A

### 3.2.1 Given Data for Casing Pitting Failure

- Failure rate of the casing (from Nonmetallic Materials Reliability Prediction, NPRD-95): $\lambda_p = 3.5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures / hour.
- Mission duration: $t = 8760$ hours (one continuous year).
- Failure mode ratio (casing pitting vs. all casing failures): $\alpha = 0.15$.
- Probability that pitting causes complete mission loss: $\beta = 0.40$.
- Severity category: Category I (catastrophic) → assigned weight $S_w = 1.0$ (per MIL-STD-1629A table).
- Mission time fraction: $t_M = 1.0$ (the pump runs continuously).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Mode Criticality $C_m$

$$C_m = \alpha \times \lambda_p \times t \times \beta$$

Substituting the values:

$$C_m = 0.15 \times (3.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760 \times 0.40$$

$$C_m = 0.15 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 \times 0.40$$

$$C_m = 0.15 \times 3.5 \times 8760 \times 0.40 \times 10^{-6}$$

Compute the product $3.5 \times 8760$:

$$3.5 \times 8760 = 30{,}660$$

Compute the product $30{,}660 \times 0.40$:

$$30{,}660 \times 0.40 = 12{,}264$$

Compute the product $12{,}264 \times 0.15$:

$$12{,}264 \times 0.15 = 1{,}839.6$$

Apply the $10^{-6}$ factor:

$$1{,}839.6 \times 10^{-6} = 1.8396 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$C_m = 1.8396 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{failures / million hours}$$

### 3.2.3 System Criticality $C_r$

$$C_r = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{m,i} = C_{m,\text{pitting}} = 1.8396 \times 10^{-3}$$

Since only one failure mode is considered in this single-mode analysis, $C_r = C_m$.

### 3.2.4 Engineering Interpretation

> The mode criticality $C_m \approx 1.84 \times 10^{-3}$ failures per million hours means that for every $10^6$ pump-hours of operation, roughly **1.84 catastrophic casing failures** are expected. For a fleet of 50 pumps running continuously, the expected catastrophic event interval is:

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{catastrophic}} = \frac{1}{C_m \times N} = \frac{1}{(1.84 \times 10^{-3}) \times 50} = \frac{1}{0.092} = 10.87 \text{ hours per pump}$$

In more interpretable form, the **fleet interval** to a catastrophic casing event is:

$$\text{Fleet MTBF} = \frac{1}{0.092} \approx 10.87 \text{ hours}$$

> This is unacceptably short and confirms that the casing pitting mode is the **highest-priority item for a redundant design** or a more **corrosion-resistant material upgrade**.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The FMEA Procedural Flow — Master Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START Define System Boundary and Scope] --> A1[Construct Functional Block Diagram]
    A1 --> A2[Assemble Cross-functional FMEA Team]
    A2 --> A3[Establish Rating Scale Conventions]
    A3 --> B1[Identify Failure Modes for each Block]
    B1 --> B2[Determine Local and End Effects]
    B2 --> B3[Assign Severity Rating S from 1 to 10]
    B3 --> C1[Identify Root Causes via 5-Why or FTA]
    C1 --> C2[Assign Occurrence Rating O from 1 to 10]
    C2 --> D1[List Current Detection Controls]
    D1 --> D2[Assign Detection Rating D from 1 to 10]
    D2 --> E1[Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    E1 --> E2{RPN exceeds 100 or S equals 10?}
    E2 -- YES --> F1[Define Recommended Action and Owner]
    E2 -- NO --> G1[Document as Accepted Risk]
    F1 --> F2[Implement Action]
    F2 --> F3[Re-rate S, O, D post-action]
    F3 --> F4[Compute New RPN and verify reduction]
    F4 --> H1[Update FMEA Worksheet and Close Loop]
    G1 --> H1
    H1 --> Z1[END Living FMEA Document maintained]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Flowchart:**
> - The workflow is **iterative** — every step may return to the previous step if new information emerges.
> - The decision diamond `RPN exceeds 100 or S equals 10` is the **AIAG-VDA threshold trigger**: either high RPN **or** a severity of 10 alone mandates action.
> - The loop `Re-rate → New RPN` is the **closure mechanism** required by AS9100D and IATF 16949.

---

## 4.2 The S-O-D Logic Triangle — Conceptual Foundation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_AXES [Risk Input Triplet]
        SEV[S equals Severity 1 to 10] 
        OCC[O equals Occurrence 1 to 10] 
        DET[D equals Detection 1 to 10] 
    end
    INPUT_AXES --> CORE[Mathematical Core Product]
    CORE --> RPN_OUT[RPN equals S times O times D]
    RPN_OUT --> DEC{Threshold Logic}
    DEC -- RPN greater than 100 --> ACT[Take Corrective Action]
    DEC -- RPN less or equal 100 --> MON[Monitor and Reassess]
    ACT --> RPN_NEW[Re-compute New RPN post action]
    RPN_NEW --> DEC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This diagram emphasizes the **composite nature of risk** in FMEA: risk is not just severity, not just frequency, and not just how well you can see it. It is the **multiplicative interaction** of all three.

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of the Centrifugal Pump (Used in Section 3)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MOTOR[Drive Motor 15 kW 2900 rpm] --> COUPLING[Coupling Rubber Spider]
    COUPLING --> SHAFT[Pump Shaft SS-410]
    SHAFT --> BEAR[Deep Groove Ball Bearings]
    SHAFT --> IMP[Impeller Closed Type Cast SS-316]
    IMP --> CASING[Volute Casing Cast Iron Lined]
    CASING --> SEAL[Mechanical Seal WC faces]
    SEAL --> FLUSH[Seal Flush Plan 23]
    CASING --> SUC[Suction Line NPSH margin 3.2 m]
    CASING --> DIS[Discharge Line to Reactor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Block Diagram:**
> - Each block is a **candidate failure item** in the FMEA worksheet.
> - The arrows represent **energy or mass flow paths** — failures propagate *along* the arrows.
> - The mechanical seal and bearings are typically the **highest RPN** items (as proven numerically in Section 3).

---

## 4.4 RPN Action Priority Decision Matrix (AIAG-VDA 2019 Synthesis)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[FMEA Row Computed] --> Q1{Severity greater or equal 9?}
    Q1 -- YES --> HIGH[Action Priority HIGH]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Severity between 7 and 8 AND Occurrence greater or equal 4?}
    Q2 -- YES --> HIGH
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Severity between 7 and 8 AND Detection greater or equal 6?}
    Q3 -- YES --> MED[Action Priority MEDIUM]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{Severity between 5 and 6 AND Occurrence greater or equal 6?}
    Q4 -- YES --> MED
    Q4 -- NO --> Q5{Severity between 5 and 6 AND Detection greater or equal 7?}
    Q5 -- YES --> MED
    Q5 -- NO --> LOW[Action Priority LOW]
```

> [!TIP]
> This is the **AIAG-VDA 2019** logic. The RPN threshold approach is increasingly being **replaced** by this Action Priority (AP) system in the automotive industry because it foregrounds severity, which RPN tends to mask.
