# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

> Define the term **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** in the context of FMEA. State its mathematical form and the conventional threshold value for triggering corrective action.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is a composite metric used in Failure Mode and Effects Analysis to rank the relative risk of individual failure modes. It is the **mathematical product** of three ordinal ratings:
>
> $$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$
>
> where:
> - $S$ = Severity (1–10): consequence of the failure.
> - $O$ = Occurrence (1–10): frequency of the cause.
> - $D$ = Detection (1–10): likelihood that the failure escapes detection before reaching the customer.
>
> **Theoretical range:** $1 \leq \text{RPN} \leq 1000$.
>
> **Conventional action threshold:** RPN > **100** is typically flagged for mandatory corrective action (threshold is application-specific). Severity rating of $S = 10$ alone also mandates action regardless of RPN.

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2024]`

> Differentiate between **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** and **Process FMEA (PFMEA)**. Give **two** specific examples for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | DFMEA | PFMEA |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Failure modes of the *product design* | Failure modes of the *manufacturing process* |
| **Stage** | Detailed design phase | Process planning / pilot production |
| **Scope** | Component geometry, materials, tolerances | Machines, tooling, methods, operators, environment |
| **Example 1** | Fatigue failure of a crankshaft | Incorrect torque application during bolt tightening |
| **Example 2** | Seal leak due to O-ring material incompatibility | Welding porosity due to improper shielding-gas flow |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Module Internal Choice: Question A or Question B**

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]` — 14 Marks

> **(a)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure** of conducting a Design FMEA on an industrial gearbox with at least **three stages** of reduction. Enumerate the eight required columns of the FMEA worksheet. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a **helical gear** in the second stage of the gearbox, identify **three failure modes**, rate them on the standard S–O–D scale, compute the RPN, and recommend corrective action for the highest-RPN mode. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The FMEA on the industrial gearbox proceeds as follows:

1. **Define System Boundary** (1 mark): The gearbox has three reduction stages, input shaft, output shaft, bearings, lubrication system, and housing. The boundary is set at the input coupling flange and the output coupling flange.

2. **Construct Functional Block Diagram** (1 mark): Draw blocks for input shaft → 1st stage helical pair → intermediate shaft → 2nd stage helical pair → 3rd stage helical pair → output shaft, with parallel branches for bearings and oil pump.

3. **Establish Team and Rating Conventions** (1 mark): Cross-functional team of design, manufacturing, quality, and maintenance engineers. Adopt the standard 1–10 S, O, D scales.

4. **Identify Failure Modes per Block** (1 mark): For each gear, modes include *tooth bending fatigue, tooth surface pitting, tooth wear, tooth fracture*. For bearings: *fatigue spalling, lubrication starvation, brinelling*.

5. **Determine Effects** (1 mark): Trace each mode from local (gear) → next level (gearbox) → end-item (driven machine) → mission (production line).

6. **Assign S, O, D, Compute RPN, Recommend Action, Re-rate** (1 mark): Follow the procedure elaborated in Section 2.4 of these notes.

7. **Eight Required Columns** (1 mark): Item/Function, Failure Mode, Effect, Severity, Cause, Occurrence, Current Control, Detection, RPN, Recommended Action — *(Examiner typically counts 8 mandatory columns: Mode, Effect, S, Cause, O, D, RPN, Action.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Consider the **2nd-stage helical gear** (pinion). Failure modes:

| Item | FM | Effect | S | Cause | O | D | RPN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2nd-stage pinion | Tooth bending fatigue | Tooth fracture → gearbox seizure → line stoppage | 9 | Overload, material defect | 4 | 4 | 144 |
| 2nd-stage pinion | Surface pitting | Vibration, noise, tooth profile loss → efficiency drop | 6 | Lubrication failure, surface fatigue | 6 | 5 | 180 |
| 2nd-stage pinion | Tooth wear | Backlash increase → poor mesh → noise | 5 | Abrasive wear, low oil viscosity | 7 | 4 | **140** |

**Calculation of highest RPN (pitting):**

$$\text{RPN}_{\text{pitting}} = 6 \times 6 \times 5 = 180$$

> [!NOTE]
> The 2nd-stage pitting mode has RPN = 180 (the highest), but the tooth bending fatigue mode has S = 9 and merits priority action regardless of RPN — the **AIAG-VDA** exception.

**Recommended Action for the highest-S mode (bending fatigue):**

- Upgrade pinion material from EN-353 to **case-carburized 16MnCr5** (or 20MnCr5).
- Apply **shot-peening** on tooth roots to introduce compressive residual stress.
- Install **online acoustic-emission (AE) sensor** on the gearbox housing for early detection of micro-cracks.
- Re-rate: New S = 9, New O = 2, New D = 2 → New RPN = $9 \times 2 \times 2 = 36$ (a 75% reduction from 144).

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2024]` — 14 Marks

> **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **AIAG-VDA 2019 FMEA** approach. How does it differ from the **classical RPN method**? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A pharmaceutical tablet-coating process has a critical failure mode *'tablet surface roughness exceeds pharmacopeial limit'*. The severity is 9, occurrence is 5, and detection is 7. Compute the classical RPN and the **Action Priority (AP)** level. Justify the action you would recommend. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The **AIAG-VDA 2019 FMEA Handbook** replaces the classical RPN with a structured **Action Priority (AP)** system. The AP is determined by a **decision tree** (already shown in Section 4.4 of these notes) based on the (S, O, D) triplet.

**Key Differences (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Classical RPN | AIAG-VDA 2019 AP |
|---|---|---|
| **Metric** | Numeric RPN (1–1000) | Categorical: High / Medium / Low |
| **Severity emphasis** | Buried in product | Foregrounded as the dominant axis |
| **Threshold** | RPN > 100 (arbitrary) | Look-up table from (S, O, D) |
| **S = 10 case** | Same as others | Always **High AP** regardless of O, D |
| **Documentation** | RPN column | Action Priority column |

**Steps in AIAG-VDA 2019 (3 marks):**

1. Define scope and analysis boundary.
2. Create the **FMEA structure** (Step 1: Planning and Preparation).
3. **Step 2: Structure Analysis** — define the *system*, *subsystems*, *elements*, and their interfaces using a **Block Diagram** or **Structure Tree**.
4. **Step 3: Function Analysis** — describe the function of each element, including interfaces.
5. **Step 4: Failure Analysis** — identify failure modes, effects, and causes.
6. **Step 5: Risk Analysis** — assign S, O, D ratings, and use the AP matrix to determine the action priority.
7. **Step 6: Optimization** — define and track actions to reduce risk; re-rate.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $S = 9$, $O = 5$, $D = 7$.

**Classical RPN Calculation (2 marks):**

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D = 9 \times 5 \times 7 = 315$$

Since RPN = 315 > 100, classical method mandates action.

**AIAG-VDA AP Determination (3 marks):**

Trace the AIAG-VDA decision tree (Section 4.4 of these notes):
- $S = 9 \geq 9$? → **YES** → **Action Priority = HIGH**

Therefore, irrespective of O and D, the AP is HIGH.

**Recommended Action (2 marks):**

- **Root cause investigation:** Coating pan rpm, spray-atomization pressure, inlet air temperature, gun-to-bed distance.
- **Statistical Process Control (SPC):** Install X-bar / R chart on tablet roughness; use **Cpk** target ≥ 1.33.
- **In-line NIR (Near-Infrared) sensor** for real-time roughness estimation → reduces D from 7 to 2.
- **Capability study** on incoming coating solution to control O.
- After action, target: $S = 9$, $O = 2$, $D = 2$ → New RPN = 36 (88.6% reduction); AP remains HIGH because of high S, but the residual risk is acceptable with redundant engineering controls.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in FMEA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing S = 10 with RPN = 1000.** A failure mode with S = 10 is *always* top-priority even if RPN is moderate. KTU examiners explicitly test this AIAG-VDA exception.
> 2. **Inverting the Detection scale.** Many students write D = 1 = "hard to detect" and D = 10 = "easy to detect." The correct convention is: D = 10 = **hard to detect** (high-risk), D = 1 = **easy to detect** (low-risk).
> 3. **Failing to compute the RPN** as an explicit product. Writing "S = 5, O = 5, D = 5" without showing $5 \times 5 \times 5 = 125$ loses **1 mark minimum** in the calculation step.
> 4. **Mixing DFMEA and PFMEA.** A question on "Design FMEA" must NOT include process-step failures (e.g., operator error, tool wear).
> 5. **No Pareto analysis.** The examiner expects the student to identify the *highest-ranked* mode and justify prioritization using either RPN or AP.
> 6. **Generic actions** like "improve quality." KTU board examiners deduct **2 marks** for vague actions. Use specific engineering terms: "shot-peening," "duplex SS-2205," "Cpk ≥ 1.33," "IATF 16949 control plan," etc.
> 7. **Forgetting the re-rating loop.** The FMEA is incomplete without a post-action re-rating that demonstrates RPN reduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **FMEA** = systematic, **bottom-up**, **inductive** failure analysis.
- [ ] Three ratings: **S**everity, **O**ccurrence, **D**etection — all on a **1–10** scale.
- [ ] **RPN** = $S \times O \times D$, range **[1, 1000]**, action threshold **> 100**.
- [ ] **S = 10** = hazardous, **always** requires action.
- [ ] **D = 10** = failure is undetectable before reaching customer.
- [ ] **FMEA types:** System, Design (DFMEA), Process (PFMEA).
- [ ] **Severity** is rated *independent* of O and D; it describes the **consequence**.
- [ ] **Occurrence** is rated based on **historical data** (warranty, FRACAS).
- [ ] **Detection** is rated for the **worst-case** detection chain.
- [ ] **AIAG-VDA 2019** replaces RPN with **Action Priority (High / Medium / Low)**.
- [ ] **MIL-STD-1629A** adds **Criticality** $C_m = \alpha \lambda_p t \beta$.
- [ ] **IEC 60812:2015** is the international standard for FMEA.
- [ ] **FMEA loop** must close with **post-action re-rating** and RPN reduction evidence.
- [ ] Use **block diagrams** before populating the FMEA worksheet.
- [ ] Always include **5+ rows** in a KTU board FMEA answer.
- [ ] **Pareto analysis** (top 20% RPNs drive 80% of actions) is a key engineering judgement step.
- [ ] The FMEA document is a **living document** — it must be updated whenever the design, process, or controls change.
- [ ] FMEA complements **FTA** (top-down deductive), **HAZOP** (process deviation), and **LOPA** (layer of protection analysis).
- [ ] **Software FMEA (SFMEA)** is a recognized variant for embedded systems and control software.
- [ ] **Limitations of RPN:** masks high-S items; arbitrary threshold; subjective ratings → hence the move to AP.
- [ ] Always show the **RPN computation** as a clear numerical product in the answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layer of Protection Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITUM RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Layer of Protection Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** is a semi-quantitative risk assessment methodology used in process safety engineering to evaluate the adequacy of **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** in reducing the frequency of an unwanted process event (such as a runaway reaction, loss of containment, fire, or explosion) to a tolerable level.

In the formal KTU syllabus context, LOPA is defined as:

> **LOPA is a structured analytical procedure that examines a process deviation by (i) identifying its initiating cause, (ii) estimating the initiating event frequency, (iii) listing the independent protection layers that act on the deviation, (iv) multiplying the initiating event frequency by the Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) of each IPL, and (v) comparing the resulting mitigated event frequency with a corporate/risk-matrix tolerable target frequency.**

The output of a LOPA study is a **Risk Decision**: either the existing layers provide sufficient risk reduction, or additional IPLs (often a Safety Instrumented Function - SIF - per **IEC 61511**) must be added.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** LOPA is positioned between qualitative methods (HAZOP, What-If) and fully quantitative methods (Fault Tree / Event Tree with full data sets). It uses **order-of-magnitude approximations** rather than precise probability distributions, making it efficient, transparent, and defensible.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Swiss Cheese Model" of Protection

Imagine you are protecting a precious vase on a table from being knocked over and broken.

- The **initiating event** is a person running past the table.
- You place several independent barriers in front of the vase:
  1. A **rope barrier** (Barrier 1 – procedural).
  2. A **motion-sensor alarm** that rings loudly (Barrier 2 – BPCS / alarm).
  3. A **cushioned mat** on the floor (Barrier 3 – mechanical IPL, e.g., dike, bund).
  4. A **glass display case** over the vase (Barrier 4 – passive IPL, e.g., containment).

Each barrier has a chance of "failing on demand" — the rope might be untied, the alarm battery might be dead, the glass might already be cracked. In isolation, each barrier alone would not be reliable enough to protect the vase. But **when stacked together, the combined protection becomes very robust**.

LOPA works exactly like this. Each IPL is like a slice of **Swiss cheese** — full of holes (failures). When slices are properly aligned by the LOPA analyst, the holes don't line up, and the accident does not propagate to a catastrophic outcome. The core principle is **independence**: each IPL must fail by a completely different mechanism, otherwise the layers are not truly adding safety.

## 1.3 Key Standardised Metrics (KTU Frequently Tested Terms)

The following ordered-of-magnitude values are industry-standard PFDs that the KTU examiner expects a student to memorise:

| IPL Type | Typical PFD (per demand) |
|----------|--------------------------|
| **BPCS loop** | $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-1}}$ |
| Operator response to alarm | $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-1}}$ |
| Mechanical relief device (PSV/PRV) | $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-2}}$ |
| Dike / bund / containment | $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-2}}$ |
| **SIF (SIL 1)** | $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-1}}$ to $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-2}}$ |
| **SIF (SIL 2)** | $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-2}}$ to $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-3}}$ |
| **SIF (SIL 3)** | $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-3}}$ to $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-4}}$ |
| Flame / detonation arrestor | $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-1}}$ |
| Underground drainage system | $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-2}}$ |
| Fire protection system (sprinkler) | $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-1}}$ to $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-2}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter:** They are **order-of-magnitude (OOM) values**. LOPA does not require detailed fault-tree computation; instead, it relies on these pre-calibrated OOM PFDs, validated by industry bodies (CCPS, IEC 61511).

## 1.4 When is LOPA Used? (Decision Criteria)

LOPA is applied after a **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** such as HAZOP, FMEA, or What-If has identified scenarios that are not adequately controlled by the existing safeguards. It is most valuable for scenarios that are:

- **High consequence** (fatality, multi-million-dollar loss, environmental release).
- **Mid-to-high frequency** (above ~$10^{-4}$/year before protection).
- Associated with complex multi-layer protection logic (BPCS + alarms + SIFs + passive).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The LOPA Logic in Five Structured Steps

LOPA is always executed in the same disciplined sequence. KTU questions typically describe a scenario and ask the student to apply these steps.

### Step 1 — Identify the Consequence (Top Event)

The analyst picks a **specific undesired consequence** with a defined severity category (e.g., Category 4: Fatality / >\$1M loss). Choosing a precise consequence is critical because two different consequences of the same scenario may have different target frequencies.

### Step 2 — Identify the Initiating Event (IE) and Estimate its Frequency

The **initiating event** is the root cause that creates the deviation. Examples:

- Pump failure → loss of flow → heat exchanger fouling.
- Blocked outlet → overpressure → vessel rupture.
- Operator opens wrong valve → mischarge → runaway reaction.
- External fire → uncontrolled heat input → vessel BLEVE.

The initiating event frequency $f_i^{I}$ is **expressed in events per year**. Sources of data: company incident databases, OREDA, CCPS PERD, or industry-accepted generic values.

### Step 3 — Identify Candidate Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)

For each scenario, the analyst lists all candidate IPLs that are claimed to mitigate the consequence. The IPLs must satisfy the **four IEC/CCPS independence criteria**:

1. **Effectiveness** — the layer must be capable of preventing the consequence (reduce frequency by at least an order of magnitude).
2. **Independence** — it must operate independently of the initiating event and of every other claimed IPL.
3. **Auditability** — its PFD can be verified, calculated, or inspected.
4. **Dependability** — its performance is documented and managed.

### Step 4 — Calculate the Mitigated Event Frequency

The order-of-magnitude LOPA formula multiplies the initiating event frequency by the PFDs of all valid IPLs:

$$
f_i^{M} \;=\; f_i^{I} \;\times\; \prod_{j=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_{ij}
$$

where:
- $f_i^{M}$ = mitigated (post-protection) frequency of scenario $i$, per year.
- $f_i^{I}$ = initiating event frequency, per year.
- $\text{PFD}_{ij}$ = probability of failure on demand of the $j$-th IPL acting on scenario $i$.
- $n$ = number of claimed IPLs.

### Step 5 — Compare Against the Target Frequency (Risk Decision)

The mitigated frequency is compared to the **tolerable target frequency** $f_i^{T}$ dictated by company policy or a risk matrix (e.g., $10^{-5}$/year for fatality, $10^{-6}$/year for catastrophic).

The **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** required of the IPL system is:

$$
\text{RRF}_{\text{required}} \;=\; \frac{f_i^{I}}{f_i^{T}}
$$

The **actual Risk Reduction Factor** provided by the existing IPLs is:

$$
\text{RRF}_{\text{actual}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\prod_{j=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_{ij}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:** If $f_i^{M} \leq f_i^{T}$, the scenario is **adequately protected**. If $f_i^{M} > f_i^{T}$, additional IPLs are required, and a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) with a calculated SIL target is engineered using $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF,req}} \leq f_i^{T}/f_i^{M\text{,existing}}$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Symbols / Units |
|---------|----------|-----------------|
| Mitigated event frequency | $f_i^{M} = f_i^{I} \times \prod_{j=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_{ij}$ | per year |
| Required Risk Reduction Factor | $\text{RRF}_{\text{req}} = f_i^{I} / f_i^{T}$ | dimensionless |
| Actual Risk Reduction Factor | $\text{RRF}_{\text{act}} = 1 / \prod_{j} \text{PFD}_{ij}$ | dimensionless |
| Required SIF PFD | $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF,req}} \leq f_i^{T} / f_i^{I\text{,rest}}$ | dimensionless |
| Total cumulative risk | $R_{\text{total}} = \sum_i f_i^{M} \times C_i$ | consequence-weighted units / year |
| SIL determination (avg) | SIL: $\vert \log_{10}(\text{PFD}) \vert$ in band | SIL 1, 2, 3 |
| As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP) check | $f_i^{M} \ll f_i^{T}$ | margin of safety |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Note:** When the consequence involves fatalities to multiple people, the *fatality rate* is multiplied by a **Location Factor** $L_F$ (occupancy probability, weather dependency). For example, $f_{\text{individual}} = f_i^{M} \times L_F$ must meet the individual risk criterion of typically $10^{-6}$/year.

## 2.3 Why LOPA is Used in Real-World Engineering

LOPA is the **de-facto bridge between HAZOP and SIL assignment** in the process industries (oil & gas, petrochemical, pharmaceutical). Its real-world utilities include:

- **Compliance** with **IEC 61511** for Safety Instrumented Systems.
- **Cost-effective prioritisation** of safety investments.
- **Defensible documentation** for regulators (OSHA PSM, EPA RMP, COMAH).
- **Communication tool** between operators, engineers, and management on residual risk.

In modern plant design, LOPA is performed during the **Engineering, Procurement and Construction (EPC)** phase and re-validated every 5 years during the **Management of Change (MOC)** cycle.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mitigated Frequency from First Principles

The starting point is the **conditional probability of independent events**. Consider a scenario chain:

> Initiating Event $E_I$ → demands IPL 1 → demands IPL 2 → … → demands IPL $n$ → Consequence $C$.

The probability that the consequence materialises is the joint probability of:
- $E_I$ occurring, **AND**
- IPL 1 failing on demand, **AND**
- IPL 2 failing on demand, **AND**
- … IPL $n$ failing on demand.

Because the IPLs are *independent* (each fails by a unique mechanism), the joint probability factorises:

$$
P(C \mid \text{scenario chain}) = P(E_I) \times \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(\text{IPL}_j \text{ fails})
$$

Converting to **annual frequencies** (events/year) by multiplying each probability by the demand rate, and recognising that in order-of-magnitude LOPA each $\text{PFD}$ is dimensionless (a probability per demand), yields:

$$
f_i^{M} = f_i^{I} \times \prod_{j=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_{ij}
$$

This is the foundational LOPA equation — strictly valid under **independence** and **one-demand-per-event** assumptions. If the same IPL is claimed twice, its PFD must not be double-counted, because the failure modes would not be independent.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example – KTU-Style Numerical Problem

> **Problem Statement:** A reactive chemical reactor contains a runaway reaction hazard. The HAZOP team identified the following scenario: **"Loss of cooling water flow to reactor jacket"** leads to thermal runaway and vessel rupture (severity = Category 5 – multiple fatalities).

| Layer # | Description | Order-of-Magnitude PFD |
|---------|-------------|------------------------|
| IE | Cooling water pump failure | $f_i^{I} = 1 \times 10^{-1}$ /year |
| IPL 1 | BPCS flow-control loop with low-flow alarm | $1 \times 10^{-1}$ |
| IPL 2 | Operator intervention on alarm within 10 min | $1 \times 10^{-1}$ |
| IPL 3 | Pressure relief valve (PSV) sized for runaway | $1 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| IPL 4 | Safety Instrumented Function – SIL 2 | $1 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| IPL 5 | Containment dike (passive) | $1 \times 10^{-2}$ |

Corporate tolerable target for Category 5: $f_i^{T} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ /year.

### Detailed Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the product of PFDs (the combined IPL failure probability):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{j=1}^{5} \text{PFD}_{j} &= (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \\[4pt]
&= 10^{-1-1-2-2-2} \\[4pt]
&= 10^{-8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by initiating event frequency to get mitigated frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_i^{M} &= f_i^{I} \times \prod_{j=1}^{5} \text{PFD}_{j} \\[4pt]
&= (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-8}) \\[4pt]
&= 1 \times 10^{-9} \;\; \text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with target frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_i^{M} &= 1 \times 10^{-9} \;\; \text{per year} \\[4pt]
f_i^{T} &= 1 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $f_i^{M} = 1 \times 10^{-9} \;<\; f_i^{T} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$, the scenario is **adequately protected** (Risk Reduction Factor realised = $10^{8}$, target = $10^{5}$).

**Step 4 — Sensitivity Check:** Suppose the SIL 2 SIF is removed for cost reasons. Recompute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{j=1}^{4} \text{PFD}_{j} &= (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \\[4pt]
&= 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_i^{M,\text{no SIF}} &= (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-6}) \\[4pt]
&= 1 \times 10^{-7} \;\; \text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now $f_i^{M,\text{no SIF}} = 1 \times 10^{-7} \;>\; f_i^{T} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$? **No**, still acceptable. But the **safety margin** has collapsed from 1000× to 10×. ALARP considerations would argue for keeping the SIF.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Demand an Additional SIF

The table below can be used in any KTU exam or industrial scenario to decide on additional protection:

| Computed $f_i^{M}$ | vs Target $f_i^{T}$ | Decision |
|--------------------|----------------------|----------|
| $f_i^{M} \leq 0.1 \times f_i^{T}$ | Far below target | Over-protected; simplify only with MOC review. |
| $0.1 \times f_i^{T} < f_i^{M} \leq f_i^{T}$ | At or below target | Acceptable; document residual risk. |
| $f_i^{T} < f_i^{M} \leq 10 \times f_i^{T}$ | Slightly above target | ALARP zone — install additional IPL (usually a SIF). |
| $f_i^{M} > 10 \times f_i^{T}$ | Far above target | Design change mandatory — re-engineer process. |

## 3.4 Conversion of Required PFD to SIL (SIL Assignment Procedure)

If a new SIF is needed, the required PFD is:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF,req}} \;\leq\; \frac{f_i^{T}}{f_i^{I} \times \prod_{j \neq \text{SIF}} \text{PFD}_{j}}
$$

Then the SIL is determined from the table:

| SIL Range | PFD band (per demand) |
|-----------|-----------------------|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| SIL 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ |

**Example:** Suppose all IPLs except the proposed SIF give $\prod = 10^{-3}$ and $f_i^{I}/f_i^{T} = 10^{5}$. Then $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF,req}} \leq 10^{5} \times 10^{-3} = 10^{2}$ → impossible! This means the **process must be redesigned**, not just instrumented.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LOPA

The figure below shows the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of how raw scenario data passes through each LOPA evaluation stage to produce a risk decision. This is the canonical workflow used in real LOPA studies and is the same diagram pattern drawn by KTU examiners on the board.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HAZOP Output - Deviation and Consequence] --> B[Step 1: Define Consequence Category]
    B --> C[Step 2: Identify Initiating Event and Frequency fI]
    C --> D[Step 3: List Candidate Independent Protection Layers]
    D --> E[Step 4: Verify IPL Independence IEC 61511]
    E --> F[Step 5: Calculate Mitigated Frequency fM]
    F --> G[Step 6: Compare with Target Frequency fT]
    G --> H{Decision: fM less or equal fT?}
    H -- YES --> I[Scenario ACCEPTABLE - Document Residual Risk]
    H -- NO --> J[Step 7: Specify Required PFD for New SIF]
    J --> K[Step 8: SIL Assignment and Loop Design]
    K --> L[Re-evaluate Scenario with New SIF]
    L --> F
    F --> M[Aggregate Risk Register and ALARP Justification]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Boxes A → B → C → D → E → F → G represent the deterministic, sequential LOPA logic.
- Box H is the **decision gate**: the only branch in the workflow.
- Boxes I, J, K, L form the **iterative IPL-design loop**.
- Box M is the exit point, producing a fully documented risk register.

## 4.2 Independent Protection Layer "Swiss Cheese" Schematic

The figure below illustrates the **independence principle** by showing how the holes (failure modes) in each IPL do not align to allow the consequence to propagate. This is the conceptual diagram students must be able to draw and explain in a KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IE[Initiating Event - Causes Deviation] --> IPL1[IPL 1 - BPCS Loop]
    IPL1 --> IPL2[IPL 2 - Operator Alarm]
    IPL2 --> IPL3[IPL 3 - Relief Valve]
    IPL3 --> IPL4[IPL 4 - SIF SIL 2]
    IPL4 --> IPL5[IPL 5 - Bund or Dike]
    IPL5 --> RES[Residual Risk - Mitigated Frequency fM]
```

Each block (IPL 1 – IPL 5) represents an independent slice. The "holes" in each slice are the failure modes that *could* allow the accident to propagate further; the LOPA analyst verifies that these failure modes are **mechanistically distinct** (e.g., software failure vs. corrosion failure vs. operator attention failure).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA). What is its primary purpose in process safety?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
LOPA is a semi-quantitative risk assessment methodology used to evaluate the effectiveness of **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** in reducing the frequency of an unwanted process event to a tolerable level. It uses order-of-magnitude estimates of initiating event frequencies and the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** of each IPL. Its primary purpose is to bridge the gap between qualitative hazard analysis (HAZOP) and fully quantitative methods, providing a defensible, transparent basis for **SIL assignment** under IEC 61511 and for risk-based decision-making during plant design and operations.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the four criteria that an Independent Protection Layer (IPL) must satisfy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
According to IEC 61511 / CCPS guidelines, an IPL must satisfy:
1. **Effectiveness** – capable of preventing the scenario by at least one order of magnitude.
2. **Independence** – operates independently of the initiating event and of every other claimed IPL.
3. **Auditability** – its PFD can be verified through inspection, test, or calculation.
4. **Dependability** – its performance is documented, maintained, and managed throughout the plant lifecycle.

---

## 5.2 Part B – 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**(a) With a neat schematic, describe the LOPA methodology step-by-step. Explain any two IPL independence criteria in detail. (7 marks)**

**(b) A storage tank containing hexane is exposed to a credible pool fire initiating event with frequency $f_i^{I} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ per year. The following IPLs are claimed: BPCS level control (PFD $= 1 \times 10^{-1}$), operator response to high-level alarm (PFD $= 1 \times 10^{-1}$), foam fire-suppression system (PFD $= 1 \times 10^{-1}$), and a Category-3 consequence dike (PFD $= 1 \times 10^{-2}$). The tolerable target frequency for a Category-4 consequence is $1 \times 10^{-5}$/year. Calculate the mitigated frequency and decide whether additional protection is required. (7 marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) – 7 marks**
The student should produce:
1. A flow diagram of LOPA (similar to the one in Section 4.1 above) – 2 marks.
2. Step-by-step narration: identify consequence → identify IE and frequency → list IPLs → verify independence → compute $f_i^{M}$ → compare to $f_i^{T}$ → decide – 3 marks.
3. Detailed discussion of any two independence criteria, e.g., *Independence* (failure of one IPL must not be caused by the same root cause as another) and *Auditability* (test, inspection, or documented performance evidence must exist) – 2 marks.

**(b) – 7 marks**

Product of PFDs:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{j=1}^{4} \text{PFD}_{j} &= (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \\[4pt]
&= 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

Mitigated frequency:
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_i^{M} &= f_i^{I} \times \prod \text{PFD} \\[4pt]
&= (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-5}) \\[4pt]
&= 1 \times 10^{-7} \;\; \text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

Decision:
$$
f_i^{M} = 1 \times 10^{-7} \;<\; f_i^{T} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{ACCEPTABLE (no additional IPL required).}
$$

Realised RRF = $10^{5}$; required RRF = $10^{3}$; safety margin of 100× exists, which is well within the ALARP region. – 2 marks.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Differentiate between qualitative, semi-quantitative, and fully quantitative risk assessment methods. Where does LOPA fit, and why? (7 marks)**

**(b) For a chemical reactor, the initiating event "loss of agitator due to power failure" has a frequency of $5 \times 10^{-1}$ per year. The IPLs claimed are: independent high-viscosity alarm with operator response (PFD $= 1 \times 10^{-1}$), an emergency shutdown SIF (SIL 2, PFD $= 1 \times 10^{-2}$), a rupture disc (PFD $= 1 \times 10^{-2}$), and a containment bund (PFD $= 1 \times 10^{-2}$). The tolerable target frequency for runaway-induced fatality is $1 \times 10^{-6}$/year. Compute the mitigated frequency and the Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) achieved. Comment on adequacy. (7 marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) – 7 marks**
- **Qualitative** (HAZOP, What-If, FMEA) – uses engineering judgement, categorical risk matrices; no numerical frequencies. Strength: fast, broad coverage. Weakness: cannot distinguish between risks differing by an order of magnitude. – 2 marks.
- **Semi-quantitative** (LOPA, Risk Matrix with frequencies) – uses order-of-magnitude data and PFDs. Strength: tractable, defensible. Weakness: limited to OOM accuracy. – 2 marks.
- **Fully quantitative** (Fault Tree, Event Tree, QRA) – uses full probability distributions, Monte Carlo. Strength: precise. Weakness: data-hungry, expensive, time-consuming. – 2 marks.
- LOPA occupies the semi-quantitative niche because it uses **generic OOM data** that is widely available, while still providing numerical outputs sufficient for **SIL assignment** under IEC 61511. – 1 mark.

**(b) – 7 marks**

Product of PFDs:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{j=1}^{4} \text{PFD}_{j} &= (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \\[4pt]
&= 10^{-7}
\end{aligned}
$$

Mitigated frequency:
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_i^{M} &= (5 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-7}) \\[4pt]
&= 5 \times 10^{-8} \;\; \text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

Comparison:
$$
f_i^{M} = 5 \times 10^{-8} \;<\; f_i^{T} = 1 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Risk Reduction Factor:
$$
\text{RRF}_{\text{act}} = \frac{1}{\prod \text{PFD}} = \frac{1}{10^{-7}} = 10^{7}
$$

$$
\text{RRF}_{\text{req}} = \frac{f_i^{I}}{f_i^{T}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-1}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 5 \times 10^{5}
$$

Adequacy: The actual RRF ($10^{7}$) exceeds the required RRF ($5 \times 10^{5}$). The mitigated frequency is well below target. **The protection is adequate**, with a safety margin of ~20×. No additional SIF is required, although ALARP should still be documented. – 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in LOPA questions:**
> 1. **Double-counting an IPL** — claiming the BPCS loop *and* the same physical alarm as two layers. The LOPA rule is one IPL per failure mechanism.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — write PFDs as pure numbers (dimensionless), not as percentages. The exam expects $1 \times 10^{-1}$, not "10 percent."
> 3. **Mixing frequencies and probabilities** — initiating event frequency $f_i^{I}$ is in *per year*; PFDs are *per demand* (dimensionless). Don't multiply PFDs by year.
> 4. **Not stating the decision** — even if numbers are right, the student must write **"acceptable" or "not acceptable, additional SIF required"** explicitly.
> 5. **Skipping the independence check** — examiners award full marks only when the student mentions that the chosen IPLs are independent.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **LOPA = Semi-Quantitative Risk Assessment** bridging HAZOP and QRA.
- Core equation: $f_i^{M} = f_i^{I} \times \prod \text{PFD}_{ij}$.
- An **IPL must satisfy 4 criteria**: Effectiveness, Independence, Auditability, Dependability.
- Standard OOM PFDs are **$10^{-1}$ for BPCS / operator**, **$10^{-2}$ for relief / passive devices**, **$10^{-2}$–$10^{-4}$ for SIL-rated SIFs**.
- **RRF**$_{\text{req}} = f_i^{I}/f_i^{T}$; **RRF**$_{\text{act}} = 1/\prod \text{PFD}$.
- LOPA output feeds **SIL assignment** under **IEC 61511**.
- **ALARP** zone: when $f_i^{M}$ is within an order of magnitude of $f_i^{T}$, justify or add layers.
- Always perform an **independence check** before counting an IPL.
- Final answer to any KTU LOPA problem must include: (i) computed $f_i^{M}$, (ii) comparison with $f_i^{T}$, (iii) explicit decision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bow-Tie Analysis (Methodology and Examples)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bow-Tie Analysis (Methodology and Examples)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bow-Tie Analysis (BTA)** is a **graphical, cause-consequence risk evaluation methodology** that visually combines the logic of a **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** on the threat side (left wing) and an **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** on the consequence side (right wing), both anchored to a central **Top Event (TE)** — the moment of *loss of control* over a specific hazard.

The resulting diagram, when fully populated, assumes the iconic shape of a **bow-tie** (resembling a butterfly or hourglass with flared ends), hence the name. It is one of the most widely adopted **barrier-based risk management frameworks** in the **process safety, oil & gas, aviation, and chemical engineering** industries, and is formally recognized in standards such as **ISO 31000:2018** and **IEC 31010:2019** as a complement to qualitative risk assessment.

The Bow-Tie methodology is built on four core building blocks:

| Block | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Threats** | Left input arrows | The **root causes** or initiating events that can trigger the hazard |
| **Top Event (TE)** | Central knot | The **loss of control** of the hazard (the undesired event itself) |
| **Consequences** | Right output arrows | The **final adverse outcomes** that result if the top event is not contained |
| **Controls / Barriers** | Rectangular barriers | **Preventive Controls** (left wing) stop threats from reaching the TE; **Mitigative Controls** (right wing) reduce consequences after the TE |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Dam Analogy**

Imagine a river flowing through a valley. The water source (rainfall, snowmelt, etc.) on the left represents **threats**. As the water flows toward a town, it encounters a sequence of **dams and levees** (preventive controls). The point where the water would overflow into the town is the **Top Event (flooding begins)**. On the right side, downstream, additional barriers (evacuation plans, flood walls, emergency pumps) act as **mitigative controls** to limit the consequences (property damage, loss of life).

If any **preventive dam** fails, the water reaches the top event. Once flooding begins, only the **mitigative controls** stand between the town and disaster. This is exactly the philosophy of Bow-Tie — the diagram itself **physically resembles a bow-tie**, where the **knot is the Top Event**, the **left knot-end is the threat**, and the **right knot-end is the consequence**, separated by a chain of **safety barriers** in the middle.

## 1.3 Standard Risk Metrics Used

The two foundational **probability metrics** used throughout Bow-Tie quantitative analysis are:

- **P(Top Event)** or **f(TE)** — the frequency (per year) that the loss of control occurs.
- **P(Consequence)** — the probability that a specific adverse outcome materializes after the top event.

These are evaluated using the **AND/OR gate logic** of Fault Tree Analysis (left) and the **event sequence branching** of Event Tree Analysis (right).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bow-Tie Methodology — Step-by-Step Logic

The construction of a Bow-Tie diagram follows a rigorously structured seven-step methodology. Understanding the *why* behind each step is essential for KTU examinations.

### Step 1 — Identify the Hazard
A **hazard** is an inherent property of a system, material, or process that has the potential to cause harm. Examples: flammable hydrocarbon in a storage tank, high-pressure steam, toxic gas inventory.

**Why?** Without a clearly defined hazard, there is no anchor for the Top Event. This step frames the entire analysis.

### Step 2 — Identify the Top Event
The **Top Event** is the *moment of loss of control* over the identified hazard. It is **NOT the consequence**; it is the immediate undesired event.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examples of Top Events:
> - "Loss of containment of hydrocarbon from storage tank T-101"
> - "Ignition of leaked flammable vapour"
> - "Failure of pressure relief valve PRV-202"

### Step 3 — Identify Threats (Left Wing — Fault Tree Side)
**Threats** are the **causes** that can lead to the Top Event. Each threat has a frequency `λ_i` (events/year). Multiple threats are combined using **OR gates** at the fault tree, meaning **any one threat is sufficient** to initiate the top event.

### Step 4 — Identify Preventive Controls (Barriers on the Left)
**Preventive controls** are hardware, software, or procedural barriers placed between a threat and the top event. Each barrier has a **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**. 

Common preventive controls in process industries:
- **Pressure Safety Valves (PSV)**
- **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)**
- **Operator alarms and interventions**
- **Maintenance and inspection regimes**

### Step 5 — Identify Consequences (Right Wing — Event Tree Side)
**Consequences** are the final adverse outcomes. They are determined by the event tree branching after the top event. Examples: fire, explosion, toxic release, environmental damage, fatalities.

### Step 6 — Identify Mitigative Controls (Barriers on the Right)
**Mitigative controls** act *after* the top event has occurred, reducing the severity or probability of the consequence. Examples: firewater deluge systems, emergency shutdown valves (ESD), evacuation procedures, emergency response teams.

### Step 7 — Escalation Factors and Degradation
**Escalation factors** are conditions (e.g., delayed ignition, wind direction, secondary containment failure) that can worsen a consequence or degrade a control barrier. They are typically drawn as **dashed arrows** crossing the controls.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarises every quantitative formula tested in KTU 2024 examinations on Bow-Tie Analysis.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{TE} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{m_i} PFD_{ij}$ | Frequency of Top Event; sum over threats, product over barrier PFDs of path $i$ | events/year |
| 2 | $f_{TE} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot (1 - P_{barrier,i})$ | Equivalent form using barrier reliability | events/year |
| 3 | $P(TE \mid \text{threat}) = \prod_{j=1}^{m} PFD_j$ | Probability that all barriers on a single path fail | dimensionless |
| 4 | $P(C_k \mid TE) = \prod_{l=1}^{r} PFD_{l}$ | Probability that consequence $k$ occurs given TE | dimensionless |
| 5 | $f(C_k) = f_{TE} \cdot P(C_k \mid TE)$ | Frequency of consequence $k$ | events/year |
| 6 | $RRF = \dfrac{f(\text{risk without barriers})}{f(\text{risk with barriers})}$ | Risk Reduction Factor (effectiveness of barrier set) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}$ | Average PFD for a single-proof-tested barrier (IEC 61508 simplified) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Risk} = f \times C$ | Frequency × Consequence severity (Risk Matrix metric) | severity-units/year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Convention:** All PFD values are expressed as **dimensionless decimals between 0 and 1** (e.g., $PFD = 10^{-2}$ means 1% failure probability on demand). Frequencies $f$ and $\lambda$ are in **per year** unless explicitly stated.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas**: Shell, BP, and Chevron use Bow-Tie diagrams as their *primary* operational risk visualization tool for offshore platforms.
- **Aviation**: ICAO and major airlines use bow-ties for runway incursion and engine failure scenarios.
- **Process Safety Management (PSM)**: OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 endorses barrier-based thinking aligned with bow-tie.
- **Major Hazard Facilities (MHF)**: Regulatory bodies in the UK (HSE), Australia (NOPSEMA), and EU (Seveso III Directive) accept Bow-Tie submissions as part of Safety Case reports.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Top Event Frequency Formula

**Given:**
A system with $n$ independent threats, each threat $i$ has an initiating frequency $\lambda_i$ (events/year). Between threat $i$ and the Top Event, there are $m_i$ preventive control barriers in **series**, each with a Probability of Failure on Demand $PFD_{ij}$.

**Required:** Derive an expression for $f_{TE}$, the frequency of the Top Event.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{TE} \mid \text{threat } i \text{ initiated}) &= PFD_{i1} \times PFD_{i2} \times \cdots \times PFD_{im_i} \\
&= \prod_{j=1}^{m_i} PFD_{ij}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** For the Top Event to occur given that threat $i$ has initiated, **every** preventive barrier on the path must fail. Since barriers are independent, multiply their PFDs.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_i(\text{TE via threat } i) &= \lambda_i \times P(\text{TE} \mid \text{threat } i) \\
&= \lambda_i \times \prod_{j=1}^{m_i} PFD_{ij}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Multiply the threat frequency by the conditional probability that the top event materializes through that path.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{TE} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i(\text{TE via threat } i) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \times \prod_{j=1}^{m_i} PFD_{ij}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The total Top Event frequency is the **sum** of contributions from all $n$ threat paths because the threats are connected by an **OR gate** (any single threat can cause the TE).

This is **Formula 1** in the cheat sheet. ∎

## 3.2 Derivation of Consequence Frequency

**Given:** Top event frequency $f_{TE}$ and $r$ mitigative barriers on the path to consequence $C_k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(C_k \mid TE) &= \prod_{l=1}^{r} PFD_{l} \\
f(C_k) &= f_{TE} \times P(C_k \mid TE) = f_{TE} \times \prod_{l=1}^{r} PFD_{l}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Once the Top Event occurs, the consequence manifests only if all mitigative barriers on its path also fail. The frequency of the consequence is the product of the TE frequency and the conditional probability of reaching the consequence through that branch.

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Board-Style Numerical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Model):**
>
> A pressurized LPG storage tank is analysed using Bow-Tie. Three threats are identified:
>
> - Threat 1: External corrosion leading to wall thinning, $\lambda_1 = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ /year.
> - Threat 2: Overpressure due to pump failure, $\lambda_2 = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ /year.
> - Threat 3: External impact (vehicle collision), $\lambda_3 = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ /year.
>
> Preventive controls and their PFDs:
> - **Path 1**: Cathodic protection ($PFD = 10^{-2}$), Periodic inspection ($PFD = 10^{-1}$).
> - **Path 2**: Pressure Safety Valve ($PFD = 10^{-2}$), Operator alarm ($PFD = 10^{-1}$).
> - **Path 3**: Bund wall + impact protection ($PFD = 10^{-3}$).
>
> **Top Event:** Loss of containment of LPG.
>
> After TE, the consequence is "Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)" with two mitigative barriers:
> - Gas detection system ($PFD = 10^{-1}$)
> - Emergency isolation valve ($PFD = 10^{-2}$)
>
> **Calculate:** (a) $f_{TE}$, (b) $f(VCE)$, (c) Risk Reduction Factor if no barriers were in place.

### Solution

#### Part (a): Top Event Frequency

For each threat path, compute the path PFD product.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Path 1 PFD} &= PFD_{11} \times PFD_{12} = 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} = 10^{-3} \\
\text{Path 2 PFD} &= PFD_{21} \times PFD_{22} = 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} = 10^{-3} \\
\text{Path 3 PFD} &= PFD_{31} = 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Each path's PFD is the product of the individual barrier PFDs on that path. We now have all path products.

Now apply the Top Event frequency formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{TE} &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} \lambda_i \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{m_i} PFD_{ij} \\
&= \lambda_1 \cdot (10^{-3}) + \lambda_2 \cdot (10^{-3}) + \lambda_3 \cdot (10^{-3}) \\
&= (5 \times 10^{-3})(10^{-3}) + (2 \times 10^{-3})(10^{-3}) + (1 \times 10^{-3})(10^{-3}) \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times 10^{-6} + 1 \times 10^{-6} \\
f_{TE} &= 8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ events/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Sum all three path contributions. Each contributes a tiny but additive risk.

#### Part (b): Vapour Cloud Explosion Frequency

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(VCE \mid TE) &= PFD_{detector} \times PFD_{EIV} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} = 10^{-3} \\
f(VCE) &= f_{TE} \times P(VCE \mid TE) \\
&= (8 \times 10^{-6}) \times (10^{-3}) \\
f(VCE) &= 8 \times 10^{-9} \text{ events/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Multiply TE frequency by the conditional probability that both post-TE barriers fail.

#### Part (c): Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)

Without any barriers, all PFDs effectively become 1 (every barrier fails):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{TE,\text{no barriers}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} \lambda_i \cdot 1 \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-3} + 2 \times 10^{-3} + 1 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ events/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** When no barriers exist, the threat frequency is directly equal to the TE frequency. We use the TE frequency as the risk reference because the consequence is the same.

$$
\begin{aligned}
RRF &= \frac{f_{TE,\text{no barriers}}}{f_{TE,\text{with barriers}}} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 10^{3} = 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The barrier system reduces the Top Event risk by a factor of **1000×**. This is a classic quantitative result expected in KTU numerical questions.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Bow-Tie vs. Pure FTA/ETA

| Criterion | Bow-Tie | Pure FTA | Pure ETA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Need to visualise both causes AND consequences | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Only causes | ❌ Only consequences |
| Requires quantitative frequency calculation | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Need to depict barriers/controls explicitly | ✅ Excellent | ⚠ Limited | ⚠ Limited |
| Simple system, one hazard | ❌ Overkill | ✅ Best | ❌ Not ideal |
| Compliance reporting (Safety Case) | ✅ Best | ⚠ Adequate | ⚠ Adequate |
| Real-time operational use by frontline workers | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Complex | ❌ Complex |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bow-Tie Architecture Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the full architecture of a Bow-Tie Analysis, including the flow from hazard identification through barrier assessment to consequence quantification.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["HAZARD IDENTIFICATION"] --> B["DEFINE TOP EVENT TE"]
    B --> C["LEFT WING FTA"]
    B --> D["RIGHT WING ETA"]
    C --> C1["THREAT T1"]
    C --> C2["THREAT T2"]
    C --> C3["THREAT Tn"]
    C1 --> P1["PREVENTIVE BARRIER B1"]
    C2 --> P2["PREVENTIVE BARRIER B2"]
    C3 --> P3["PREVENTIVE BARRIER B3"]
    P1 --> B
    P2 --> B
    P3 --> B
    D --> E1["MITIGATIVE BARRIER M1"]
    D --> E2["MITIGATIVE BARRIER M2"]
    E1 --> F1["CONSEQUENCE C1 SAFE"]
    E1 --> F2["CONSEQUENCE C2 DAMAGE"]
    E2 --> F3["CONSEQUENCE C3 MAJOR"]
    F1 --> G["RISK MATRIX SCORING"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H["RISK TREATMENT DECISION"]
```

## 4.2 Bow-Tie Visual Schematic (The Classic Shape)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEFT["LEFT WING - FAULT TREE SIDE"]
        T1["THREAT 1"] --> B1["BARRIER P1"]
        T2["THREAT 2"] --> B2["BARRIER P2"]
        T3["THREAT 3"] --> B3["BARRIER P3"]
    end
    B1 --> TE
    B2 --> TE
    B3 --> TE
    TE["TOP EVENT - LOSS OF CONTROL"]
    subgraph RIGHT["RIGHT WING - EVENT TREE SIDE"]
        TE --> M1["BARRIER M1"]
        TE --> M2["BARRIER M2"]
        TE --> M3["BARRIER M3"]
    end
    M1 --> C1["CONSEQUENCE 1"]
    M2 --> C2["CONSEQUENCE 2"]
    M3 --> C3["CONSEQUENCE 3"]
    style TE fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    style B1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style B2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style B3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style M1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style M2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style M3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style T1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    style T2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    style T3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    style C1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    style C2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    style C3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Bow-Tie Construction Workflow (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1: SCOPE THE STUDY"] --> S2["STEP 2: IDENTIFY HAZARD"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3: DEFINE TOP EVENT"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4: IDENTIFY THREATS LEFT"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5: IDENTIFY PREVENTIVE BARRIERS"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6: IDENTIFY CONSEQUENCES RIGHT"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7: IDENTIFY MITIGATIVE BARRIERS"]
    S7 --> S8["STEP 8: ASSIGN PROBABILITIES PFDs AND FREQUENCIES"]
    S8 --> S9["STEP 9: CALCULATE TE FREQUENCY"]
    S9 --> S10["STEP 10: CALCULATE CONSEQUENCE FREQUENCIES"]
    S10 --> S11["STEP 11: PLOT ON RISK MATRIX"]
    S11 --> S12["STEP 12: DECIDE RISK TREATMENT"]
    S12 --> S13{"ACCEPTABLE RISK?"}
    S13 -->|"YES"| S14["DOCUMENT AND MONITOR"]

```
