<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q:** Define *Bow-Tie Analysis*. List its four key building blocks.

**Model Answer:**
Bow-Tie Analysis is a graphical risk evaluation method that combines Fault Tree Analysis (left wing) and Event Tree Analysis (right wing) joined at a central Top Event. The four key building blocks are:
1. **Threats** — root causes on the left.
2. **Top Event** — the central loss-of-control moment.
3. **Consequences** — adverse outcomes on the right.
4. **Controls / Barriers** — preventive (left) and mitigative (right) safeguards.

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Distinguish between *preventive* and *mitigative* control barriers in a Bow-Tie diagram. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**
- **Preventive barriers** act *before* the Top Event, preventing threats from escalating into a loss of control. Example: Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) or Safety Instrumented System (SIS).
- **Mitigative barriers** act *after* the Top Event, reducing the severity or likelihood of consequences. Example: Firewater deluge system or Emergency Shutdown Valve (ESDV).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]`

**Q:** With a neat sketch, describe the **methodology of Bow-Tie Analysis** for risk assessment of a pressurized flammable gas storage tank. Your answer should cover:
- (a) [7 Marks] The seven-step methodology including hazard identification, top event definition, threat listing, barrier identification on both wings, and escalation factors.
- (b) [7 Marks] A worked numerical example with at least three threats, two threats with multiple barriers, and calculation of the Top Event frequency using the OR-gate summation formula.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The Bow-Tie methodology for the storage tank proceeds as follows:

1. **Hazard Identification**: The hazard is the inventory of pressurized flammable hydrocarbon (LPG) within the tank T-101.
2. **Top Event Definition**: The Top Event is *Loss of Containment* from T-101 — i.e., a release of hydrocarbon to atmosphere.
3. **Threat Identification (Left Wing)**: Threats include external corrosion, overpressure, mechanical impact, gasket failure, and operator error.
4. **Preventive Controls**: Cathodic protection, regular inspection, PSV, SIS, bund walls.
5. **Consequence Identification (Right Wing)**: Pool fire, vapour cloud explosion (VCE), toxic exposure (if LPG contains H₂S).
6. **Mitigative Controls**: Gas detectors, ESD valves, firewater spray, evacuation sirens.
7. **Escalation Factors**: Wind direction, delayed ignition, secondary containment failure.

The student must present a labelled sketch showing threats feeding into the Top Event via preventive barriers, and the Top Event fanning out via mitigative barriers to consequences.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Numerical Example Data:**

| Threat | Frequency λ (per year) | Path Barriers | PFD values |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 (Corrosion) | $4 \times 10^{-3}$ | CP, Inspection | $10^{-2}, 10^{-1}$ |
| T2 (Overpressure) | $3 \times 10^{-3}$ | PSV, Operator alarm | $10^{-2}, 5 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| T3 (Impact) | $2 \times 10^{-3}$ | Bund wall | $10^{-3}$ |

Compute each path's overall PFD:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{T1} &= 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} = 10^{-3} \\
PFD_{T2} &= 10^{-2} \times 5 \times 10^{-2} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \\
PFD_{T3} &= 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute Top Event frequency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{TE} &= \lambda_1 \cdot PFD_{T1} + \lambda_2 \cdot PFD_{T2} + \lambda_3 \cdot PFD_{T3} \\
&= (4 \times 10^{-3})(10^{-3}) + (3 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-4}) + (2 \times 10^{-3})(10^{-3}) \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-6} + 1.5 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times 10^{-6} \\
f_{TE} &= 7.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ events/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

Conclusion: The Top Event is expected roughly once every **133,000 years**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]`

**Q:** Compare **Bow-Tie Analysis (BTA)** with **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** and **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)**. Evaluate a hypothetical ammonia storage facility using Bow-Tie with quantitative risk estimation, focusing on:
- (a) [7 Marks] The similarities and differences in methodology, including logic gate usage, output type, and typical application.
- (b) [7 Marks] Quantitative computation of consequence frequency for a "toxic release affecting populated area" outcome using the formula $f(C_k) = f_{TE} \cdot P(C_k \mid TE)$, with given numerical data.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | BTA | FTA | ETA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direction | Bidirectional (cause→effect) | Causes only | Consequences only |
| Logic Gates | OR on left, branching on right | AND, OR, NOT | Sequential branching |
| Output | Visual risk picture + frequencies | TE frequency | Consequence frequencies |
| Best For | Operational risk management | Quantitative system reliability | Outcome severity assessment |

BTA integrates both FTA and ETA. FTA uses AND/OR gates to compute TE frequency. ETA sequences events post-TE to compute consequence probabilities.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- $f_{TE} = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ /year (loss of containment of ammonia)
- Post-TE barriers:
  - Toxic gas detector: $PFD_1 = 10^{-1}$
  - Water curtain (deluge): $PFD_2 = 10^{-2}$
  - Emergency evacuation efficiency: $PFD_3 = 5 \times 10^{-2}$

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(C_{\text{toxic release}} \mid TE) &= PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3 \\
&= 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \times 5 \times 10^{-2} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(C_{\text{toxic release}}) &= f_{TE} \times P(C \mid TE) \\
&= (5 \times 10^{-5}) \times (5 \times 10^{-5}) \\
f(C) &= 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ events/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: The toxic release consequence is expected approximately once in **400 million years**, indicating a very low-residual-risk system — likely ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) compliant.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1**: Students frequently confuse the **Top Event** with the **consequence**. The TE is the *immediate loss of control* (e.g., "LPG release"), NOT the final outcome (e.g., "explosion"). Loss of 2–3 marks typical.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2**: Adding barrier PFDs instead of multiplying them. Remember: barriers in **series** → **multiply PFDs**; threats in **OR gate** → **sum frequencies**.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3**: Forgetting to convert PFDs to decimal (e.g., writing $10^{-2}$ as $10$ or $2$).
> 4. **Common Mistake #4**: Confusing units — frequency must be in **per year**, not per shift or per hour.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5**: Failing to label the diagram with all four blocks (Threat, TE, Consequence, Barriers) — examiners deduct 1 mark for unlabelled sketches.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Bow-Tie Analysis**
>
> ✅ Bow-Tie = **FTA (left) + ETA (right)** joined at the Top Event.
>
> ✅ The **Top Event** is the *loss of control*, NOT the consequence.
>
> ✅ **Preventive barriers** (left) reduce $f_{TE}$; **Mitigative barriers** (right) reduce consequence probability.
>
> ✅ Barriers in **series** ⇒ **multiply their PFDs** to get path PFD.
>
> ✅ Threats combined by **OR gate** ⇒ **sum** path contributions to get $f_{TE}$.
>
> ✅ Consequence frequency: $f(C_k) = f_{TE} \times \prod PFD_{\text{mitigative}}$.
>
> ✅ Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) measures barrier effectiveness.
>
> ✅ Bow-Tie is widely accepted under **ISO 31000**, **IEC 31010**, **Seveso III**, and **OSHA PSM**.
>
> ✅ Always state units explicitly — frequency in **/year**, PFD dimensionless.
>
> ✅ A complete bow-tie diagram must have **labels on all four block types** for full marks.
>
> ✅ The central knot (Top Event) is **red-flagged** as the critical reference point.
>
> ✅ Escalation factors are drawn as **dashed arrows** crossing the barriers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consequence Analysis - Steps Involved in the Consequence Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Consequence Analysis - Steps Involved in the Consequence Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Consequence Analysis (CA)** is the quantitative determination of the physical, environmental, and human health outcomes that result from the realization of a process hazard scenario (loss of containment of a hazardous material). It is the third stage of the standard **QRA Bow-Tie methodology**, situated between *Frequency Analysis* (left side) and *Risk Estimation* (right side).

Consequence Analysis systematically translates an **event outcome** (e.g., release of 10 tonnes of propane) into **measurable physical effects** (thermal radiation, overpressure, toxic dose) and then into **damage metrics** (fatalities, injury, asset loss) using mathematically validated source, dispersion, fire, and explosion models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (CO3 aligned):** Consequence analysis is defined as the *post-release modeling* chain that quantifies the magnitude, spatial extent, and severity of an accident outcome, providing the damage-distance relationship essential for individual and societal risk calculations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the top of a hill holding a water balloon. The **hazard identification** stage is recognizing that you *have* a water balloon. The **frequency analysis** stage is asking *"How likely am I to drop it?"*. The **consequence analysis** stage asks: *"If I drop it, how big will the splash be, how far will the water reach, and how many picnic baskets will get wet?"*

In engineering terms: You have a hole in a pressurized ammonia tank. CA tells you — at 50 m downwind the ammonia concentration will be lethal in 5 minutes; at 200 m it will cause severe irritation. The "splash radius" becomes your **Damage Distance**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold-Defined Standards)

- **Source Model**: Equation that calculates the release rate (kg/s) or total quantity released, depending on whether the release is liquid, gas, or two-phase.
- **Dispersion Model**: Gaussian Plume (steady-state) or Gaussian Puff (transient) equation predicting concentration vs. downwind distance.
- **Probit Variable (Y)**: A dimensionless statistical variable that converts a physical effect (heat flux, overpressure, toxic dose) into a probability of fatality (0–100%).
- **Endpoint Damage Distance**: The distance at which a specified level of harm (e.g., 1% fatality, structural collapse) is reached.
- **ERPG / AEGL**: Emergency Response Planning Guidelines / Acute Exposure Guideline Levels — toxicological reference thresholds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per KTU Module 3, students must be able to *list, explain, and execute* the steps involved in consequence analysis, and be familiar with probit-based damage evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Sequential Steps of Consequence Analysis

The KTU 2024 syllabus and industry practice (CCPS, TNO Green Book) prescribe the following structured workflow:

1. **Step 1 — Define the Release Scenario & Inventory**
   Specify the substance, mass, temperature, pressure, phase, and the representative failure case (full-bore rupture, partial leak, guillotine, etc.).

2. **Step 2 — Source Modeling (Release Rate & Total Release)**
   Calculate the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ (kg/s) using adiabatic or isothermal discharge equations:
   - Gas sonic (choked) flow
   - Liquid flow through orifice
   - Two-phase flashing flow

3. **Step 3 — Classify the Release Type**
   Decide between:
   - **Continuous release** (long-duration steady leak) → Gaussian *plume* model
   - **Instantaneous release** (catastrophic vessel rupture) → Gaussian *puff* model

4. **Step 4 — Atmospheric Dispersion Modeling**
   Apply the Gaussian Plume Equation for continuous releases. Determine centerline concentration $C(x,y,z)$ at downwind coordinate $x$, crosswind $y$, height $z$, given Pasquill-Gifford stability class (A–F) and wind speed $u$.

5. **Step 5 — Fire & Explosion Effect Modeling**
   Branch the analysis based on the released material:
   - **Pool Fire** (liquid spill) → surface emissive power, flame length
   - **Jet Fire** (pressurized gas release) → thermal radiation at distance
   - **Flash Fire** (LPG cloud ignition) → Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) contour
   - **Fireball** (BLEVE) → transient thermal dose
   - **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)** → TNT equivalence or TNO Multi-Energy method
   - **Confined Explosion** → peak overpressure

6. **Step 6 — Damage Consequence Estimation (Probit Analysis)**
   Translate physical effects into human/structural harm using **probit equations**. Sum up fatalities in each damage zone weighted by population density to obtain the **Consequence Magnitude (N)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **'Why' behind the sequence:** Each step feeds data into the next. Source modeling gives the *fuel input*, dispersion gives the *spatial reach*, fire/explosion models give the *intensity*, and probit gives the *probability of harm*. Skipping any step invalidates the final risk number.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula / Equation | Units / Conditions |
|---|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Discharge rate (sonic gas) | $\dot{m} = C_{d} A P \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma M}{R T}} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\dfrac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | kg/s; choked flow ($\dot{m} = $ max) |
| 2 | Discharge rate (subsonic gas) | $\dot{m} = C_{d} A P \sqrt{\dfrac{2\gamma M}{RT(\gamma-1)}} \left[\left(\dfrac{P_{a}}{P}\right)^{2/\gamma} - \left(\dfrac{P_{a}}{P}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/\gamma}\right]^{0.5}$ | kg/s; $P < 2P_{a}$ or non-choked |
| 3 | Liquid leak rate | $\dot{m} = C_{d} A \rho \sqrt{2 g h}$ | kg/s; $h$ = hydrostatic head |
| 4 | Gaussian Plume (centerline, ground) | $C(x,0,0) = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\pi u \sigma_{y} \sigma_{z}}$ | kg/m³; $x$ = downwind distance |
| 5 | Gaussian Plume (3-D) | $C(x,y,z) = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{2\pi u \sigma_{y} \sigma_{z}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^{2}}{2\sigma_{y}^{2}}\right) \left[\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z-H)^{2}}{2\sigma_{z}^{2}}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z+H)^{2}}{2\sigma_{z}^{2}}\right)\right]$ | kg/m³ |
| 6 | Pool fire thermal radiation | $q = \dfrac{\dot{m}' \Delta H_{c}}{4 \pi r^{2}} \cdot F$ | W/m²; $F$ = view factor |
| 7 | TNT equivalence (VCE) | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta M \Delta H_{c}}{E_{TNT}}$ | kg; $\eta \approx 0.01\text{–}0.1$ |
| 8 | Scaled distance (explosion) | $Z = \dfrac{r}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | m/kg$^{1/3}$ |
| 9 | Probit → Probability | $P = 50 \left[1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$ | % fatality |
| 10 | Probit (thermal death) | $Y = -36.38 + 2.56 \ln\!\left(t \, q^{4/3}\right)$ | $t$ in s, $q$ in W/m² |
| 11 | Probit (lung hemorrhage – overpressure) | $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(P)$ | $P$ in Pa |
| 12 | Probit (toxic death, e.g. chlorine) | $Y = -17.1 + 1.69 \ln(C^{2.75}\, t)$ | $C$ in ppm, $t$ in min |
| 13 | Fatalities in a zone | $N = \rho_{pop} \cdot A_{zone} \cdot P_{fatality}$ | persons |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The vertical pipe `|` in math (e.g., absolute value) breaks the markdown table. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in table cells. Above table is already corrected.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Consequence analysis is the **backbone of land-use planning** around chemical plants (e.g., Kerala Petrochemical工业园区 in Ambalamugal), **emergency response planning** (evacuation radius design), and **ALARP demonstrations** for the Indian Factory Act and OSHA PSM compliance. It is also the technical basis for **offsite consequence analysis (OCA)** required under India's Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC, 1989).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Gaussian Plume Equation (Steady-State Continuous Release)

The Gaussian Plume equation is the **central dispersion tool** for continuous releases. It is derived from the steady-state atmospheric diffusion equation assuming constant wind speed $u$ in the $x$-direction and Fickian turbulent diffusion in $y$ and $z$.

The starting point is the **advection–diffusion equation** for a passive scalar $\chi$ (concentration):

$$
\frac{\partial \chi}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial \chi}{\partial x} = K_{y}\frac{\partial^{2}\chi}{\partial y^{2}} + K_{z}\frac{\partial^{2}\chi}{\partial z^{2}}
$$

For a **continuous** point source at height $H$ emitting at rate $Q$ (kg/s) under steady-state ($\partial/\partial t = 0$), the diffusion coefficients are replaced by empirical dispersion parameters $\sigma_{y}(x)$ and $\sigma_{z}(x)$ that grow with downwind distance.

Applying the boundary condition of perfect reflection at the ground ($z=0$) and requiring the solution to satisfy the integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{0}^{\infty} u\,\chi \, dz\, dy = Q$, we obtain:

$$
C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_{y}\sigma_{z}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^{2}}{2\sigma_{y}^{2}}\right)\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-H)^{2}}{2\sigma_{z}^{2}}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z+H)^{2}}{2\sigma_{z}^{2}}\right)\right]
$$

At **ground level ($z=0$)** and **centerline ($y=0$)**, this simplifies to the maximum concentration expression used in damage assessment:

$$
C_{max}(x) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_{y}(x) \sigma_{z}(x)}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The denominator $\pi u \sigma_{y} \sigma_{z}$ is the effective volume of the plume per unit length of travel. As $\sigma_{y}, \sigma_{z}$ grow with distance, $C_{max}$ decreases — this decay is what defines the **toxic endpoint distance** (e.g., IDLH or ERPG-2 contour).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Conversion of Probit Variable to Probability of Fatality

The probit $Y$ is a transformation of the **cumulative normal distribution** of human response. By definition:

$$
P = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(t-5)^{2}}{2}\right) dt
$$

This is the standard form of the normal distribution **shifted by 5 units** (so that $P=50\%$ corresponds to $Y=5$). Evaluating the integral yields:

$$
P = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]
$$

or equivalently (multiplied by 100 for percentage):

$$
P(\%) = 50\left[1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]
$$

This formula is the **bridge** between physical intensity and human harm — it is the reason a 12 kW/m² thermal flux corresponds (via $Y$) to ~100% fatality, while 4 kW/m² corresponds to ~1% fatality.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Ammonia Leak from a Refrigeration Plant

**Given:** A 50 mm diameter pipe carrying liquid anhydrous ammonia at 8 bar (gauge) and 20°C fails catastrophically. Wind speed $u = 3$ m/s, Pasquill stability class = D, effective release height $H = 5$ m.

**Step 1 — Source Model (Liquid Ammonia, sub-cooled):**

Discharge coefficient $C_{d} = 0.6$, orifice area $A = \pi (0.05)^{2}/4 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$. Ammonia density $\rho = 618$ kg/m³ at this condition. Driving head: we use the sub-cooled liquid equivalent formula.

$$
\dot{m} = C_{d} A \rho \sqrt{2\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho} + g h\right)}
$$

Assuming negligible head ($h=0$) and gauge pressure 8 bar → $\Delta P = 8 \times 10^{5}$ Pa:

$$
\dot{m} = 0.6 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 618 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8 \times 10^{5}}{618}}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.7277 \times \sqrt{2589.0} = 0.7277 \times 50.88 = 37.03 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Dispersion (Gaussian Plume, ground level centerline):**

For class D, $u = 3$ m/s, at $x = 200$ m: $\sigma_{y} \approx 8$ m, $\sigma_{z} \approx 5$ m (from PG curves).

$$
C(200,0,0) = \frac{37.03}{\pi \times 3 \times 8 \times 5} = \frac{37.03}{377.0} = 0.0982 \text{ kg/m}^{3} = 98{,}200 \text{ mg/m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Toxic Endpoint Check (NH₃):**

ERPG-2 for ammonia = **150 mg/m³** (over 1 hour). Concentration at 200 m is **655×** the ERPG-2 → **lethal zone extends well beyond 200 m**.

**Step 4 — Probit Application:**

Using NH₃ probit: $Y = -30.57 + 1.385 \ln(C^{2.75} \cdot t)$, with $C = 98{,}200$ mg/m³ ≈ 138,000 ppm and $t = 30$ min:

$$
C^{2.75} = (1.38 \times 10^{5})^{2.75}
$$

$$
\ln(C^{2.75}) = 2.75 \times \ln(1.38 \times 10^{5}) = 2.75 \times 11.836 = 32.55
$$

$$
C^{2.75} \cdot t = e^{32.55} \times 30 \approx 1.18 \times 10^{14} \times 30 = 3.54 \times 10^{15}
$$

$$
Y = -30.57 + 1.385 \times \ln(3.54 \times 10^{15}) = -30.57 + 1.385 \times 36.10 = -30.57 + 50.00 = 19.43
$$

Since $Y \gg 5$, $P \approx 100\%$. **Therefore the 200 m zone is 100% lethal.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This numerical exercise is a KTU-favorite style. Practice this 6-step end-to-end until you can perform each step without reference notes.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect-to-Model Mapping (Decision Tree)

| Released Substance | Likely Outcome | Required Model | Key Output |
|--------------------|----------------|----------------|------------|
| Flammable gas (LPG, H₂) | Jet fire | API RP 521 / Chamberlain | Flame length, $q$ at distance |
| Flammable gas (LPG) | Flash fire | LFL contour from dispersion | Lower Flammable Limit distance |
| Liquefied flammable | Pool fire | Mudan/Crawford | Burning rate, thermal radiation |
| Liquefied gas BLEVE | Fireball | TNO / Roberts | Thermal dose vs distance |
| Flammable gas in congested area | VCE | TNO Multi-Energy | Peak overpressure vs $r$ |
| Toxic gas (NH₃, Cl₂) | Toxic dispersion | Gaussian Plume + Probit | ERPG/AEGL distance + N |
| Compressed LNG spill | Rapid Phase Transition (RPT) | Tieleman / CCPS | Blast overpressure |
| Cryogenic liquid | Condensed aerosol dispersion | Dense gas (SLAB / DEGADIS) | Footprint |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Consequence Analysis Workflow (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA1[Step 1: Define Release Scenario] --> stepA2[Step 2: Source Modeling]
    stepA2 --> stepA3{Step 3: Release Type?}
    stepA3 -- Continuous --> stepA4a[Gaussian Plume Model]
    stepA3 -- Instantaneous --> stepA4b[Gaussian Puff Model]
    stepA4a --> stepA5{Step 4: Substance Type}
    stepA4b --> stepA5
    stepA5 -- Flammable Gas --> stepB1[Dispersion to LFL]
    stepA5 -- Flammable Liquid --> stepB2[Pool Fire Model]
    stepA5 -- Toxic Gas --> stepB3[Toxic Dose Calculation]
    stepA5 -- Pressurized Liquefied --> stepB4[BLEVE / VCE]
    stepB1 --> stepC1[Flash Fire / Explosion]
    stepB2 --> stepC2[Thermal Radiation Map]
    stepB3 --> stepC3[Toxic Endpoint Distance]
    stepB4 --> stepC4[Overpressure Map]
    stepC1 --> stepD[Step 5: Apply Probit Equations]
    stepC2 --> stepD
    stepC3 --> stepD
    stepC4 --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE[Step 6: Compute Fatalities and Damage Zones]
    stepE --> stepF[Final Consequence Magnitude Output]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Source to Damage Conversion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hazardous Inventory] --> B[Loss of Containment Event]
    B --> C[Source Model - Discharge Rate]
    C --> D[Atmospheric Dispersion Model]
    D --> E[Physical Effect Field]
    E -- Thermal --> F1[Heat Flux kW per m squared]
    E -- Pressure --> F2[Overpressure Pa]
    E -- Concentration --> F3[Toxic Dose ppm min]
    F1 --> G[Probit Transformation]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Probability of Harm - Percent]
    H --> I[Population Density Overlay]
    I --> J[Number of Fatalities N]
    J --> K[Individual and Societal Risk Curves]
```

### 4.3 Probit Logic Block (Damage Estimator Sub-Module)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Input: Physical Effect Intensity] --> p2{Identify Effect Type}
    p2 -- Thermal --> p3[Use Y thermal equation]
    p2 -- Blast --> p4[Use Y overpressure equation]
    p2 -- Toxic --> p5[Use Y toxic equation]
    p3 --> p6[Compute Y value]
    p4 --> p6
    p5 --> p6
    p6 --> p7[Apply Inverse Normal Formula]
    p7 --> p8[Output: Probability of Fatality]
    p8 --> p9[Multiply by Zone Population]
    p9 --> p10[Output: Number of Fatalities]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams follow the KTU 2024 scheme visual aid conventions — flow is top-down for procedural steps, left-right for the source-to-damage architecture, and modular for the probit sub-routine.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define consequence analysis. List the major steps involved in carrying out a consequence analysis in a quantitative risk assessment study.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Consequence analysis is the quantitative evaluation of the physical and harmful outcomes arising from a hazardous release event in a process plant. It establishes the relationship between the intensity of an effect (heat, pressure, toxic dose) and the resulting damage to people, property, and the environment.

**Major Steps (2 marks):**

1. **Scenario definition** — Identify inventory, substance, release condition, and failure mode.
2. **Source modeling** — Compute release rate or total release quantity.
3. **Release type classification** — Continuous vs. instantaneous.
4. **Dispersion / fire / explosion modeling** — Gaussian plume, pool fire, VCE, etc.
5. **Effect evaluation** — Thermal flux, overpressure, toxic concentration at receptors.
6. **Damage assessment using probit equations** — Convert physical effects to probability of fatality; compute number of injuries/fatalities.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is a probit variable? State the probit equation for fatality from thermal radiation and explain the role of the probit-to-probability conversion in consequence analysis.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A probit variable $Y$ is a dimensionless statistical measure of an organism's response to a given dose of a harmful agent. It is linearly related to the logarithm of the dose.

**Equation (1 mark):**
$$
Y_{thermal} = -36.38 + 2.56 \ln(t \cdot q^{4/3})
$$
where $t$ = exposure time (s), $q$ = thermal flux (W/m²).

**Role in CA (1 mark):** The probit variable is converted to fatality probability using $P(\%) = 50[1 + \operatorname{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$. This is essential in consequence analysis because it transforms a physical effect intensity into a human harm metric, allowing calculation of expected fatalities in each damage zone.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Format:** Attempt either **Question A** or **Question B**. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the six sequential steps involved in performing a consequence analysis for a release of a hazardous chemical in a process plant. Use a labeled flowchart to illustrate the workflow.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Hazard Scenario Definition (1 mark):** Select the credible worst-case or representative case — substance identity, mass, temperature, pressure, phase, release location, and hole size.

2. **Source Modeling (1 mark):** Apply discharge equations for gases (sonic/subsonic), liquids (Bernoulli), or two-phase flashing flow to compute $\dot{m}$ (kg/s) or total release $Q$ (kg).

3. **Release Type Classification (0.5 mark):** Decide between continuous (long-duration) → Gaussian Plume, or instantaneous (short-duration) → Gaussian Puff. The choice is based on the duration of release relative to the transport time.

4. **Dispersion Modeling (1 mark):** For continuous releases, the Gaussian Plume equation is used; for instantaneous, the Gaussian Puff. Inputs include wind speed, Pasquill–Gifford stability class, surface roughness, and release height. Outputs are concentration contours.

5. **Fire & Explosion Effect Modeling (1.5 marks):** Branch based on substance:
   - Pool fire → emissive power, view factor
   - Jet fire → flame length, heat flux
   - Flash fire → LFL cloud extent
   - VCE / BLEVE → overpressure vs. distance

6. **Damage Assessment using Probit Analysis (2 marks):** Apply the relevant probit equation (thermal, blast, toxic) to convert physical intensity to probability of fatality. Multiply by population in each damage zone to get expected fatalities $N$. Output the consequence magnitude for risk integration.

**Flowchart (included in the 7 marks):** Already provided in Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A storage sphere contains 5000 kg of chlorine at 10 bar(g) and 30 °C. The sphere suffers a 100 mm diameter hole and releases chlorine continuously for 30 minutes. The atmospheric stability is class D, wind speed is 4 m/s, and the effective release height is 10 m. Estimate (i) the release rate, and (ii) the downwind ground-level centerline chlorine concentration at 500 m.

**Given Data:**

- $P = 11$ bar (abs) = $1.1 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $T = 303$ K
- $M_{Cl_2} = 0.0709$ kg/mol
- $R = 8314$ J/(kmol·K)
- $C_{d} = 0.6$, $A = \pi (0.1)^{2}/4 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- Pasquill D, $u = 4$ m/s
- At $x = 500$ m: $\sigma_{y} \approx 17$ m, $\sigma_{z} \approx 13$ m
- $P_{a} = 1.013 \times 10^{5}$ Pa

**(i) Release Rate (3.5 marks):**

For chlorine gas, $\gamma \approx 1.36$ (approx. value for diatomic-like gas at moderate pressure). Check choking: $P/P_{a} \approx 10.86 > 1.89$ → flow is **choked (sonic)**. Use sonic flow equation:

$$
\dot{m} = C_{d} A P \sqrt{\frac{\gamma M}{R T} \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}
$$

Compute term by term:
- $\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1} = \dfrac{2}{2.36} = 0.8475$
- Exponent $\dfrac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)} = \dfrac{2.36}{0.72} = 3.278$
- $0.8475^{3.278} = 0.613$
- $\dfrac{\gamma M}{RT} = \dfrac{1.36 \times 0.0709}{8314 \times 303} = \dfrac{0.0964}{2.52 \times 10^{6}} = 3.83 \times 10^{-8}$
- $\dot{m} = 0.6 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.1 \times 10^{6} \times \sqrt{3.83 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.613}$
- $\dot{m} = 5183 \times \sqrt{2.348 \times 10^{-8}} = 5183 \times 1.532 \times 10^{-4} = 0.794$ kg/s

**Total released in 30 min = $0.794 \times 1800 = 1429$ kg.**

**(ii) Concentration at 500 m (3.5 marks):**

$$
C(500, 0, 0) = \frac{\dot{m}}{\pi u \sigma_{y} \sigma_{z}} = \frac{0.794}{\pi \times 4 \times 17 \times 13}
$$

$$
C = \frac{0.794}{2778.4} = 2.86 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/m}^{3} = 286 \text{ mg/m}^{3} \approx 92 \text{ ppm}
$$

> **Conclusion:** ERPG-2 for Cl₂ is **3 ppm** (60-min exposure). The concentration at 500 m is ~30× ERPG-2 → **this distance is within the public safety exclusion zone.** Damage distance is therefore > 500 m.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of the probit analysis in consequence estimation. Derive the relationship between probit variable $Y$ and percentage probability of fatality $P(\%)$. Mention the probit equations for: (i) thermal radiation fatality, (ii) lung-hemorrhage fatality due to overpressure, and (iii) fatality from chlorine exposure.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Concept of Probit (2 marks):** The probit method is a statistical dose–response technique that quantifies the human (or structural) response to a physical insult. The probit variable $Y$ is defined so that the cumulative normal distribution of response to dose can be written in linear form. The bridge equation to fatality probability uses the error function.

**Derivation (2 marks):** Starting from the cumulative standard normal $P$, with mean 5 and standard deviation 1 (by convention):

$$
P = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(t-5)^{2}}{2}\right) dt
$$

The integral of a Gaussian is the error function; multiplying by 100%:

$$
P(\%) = 50 \left[1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]
$$

**Three Probit Equations (3 marks):**

(i) **Thermal radiation fatality:**
$$
Y_{th} = -36.38 + 2.56 \ln(t \cdot q^{4/3})
$$

(ii) **Lung hemorrhage (blast fatality):**
$$
Y_{b} = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(P_{s})
$$

(iii) **Toxic fatality (chlorine):**
$$
Y_{tox} = -17.1 + 1.69 \ln(C^{2.75} \cdot t)
$$

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A 20,000 kg propane sphere has a 50 mm diameter leak at the bottom. The release is ignited, forming a **jet fire**. Estimate the thermal radiation at a receptor located 80 m downwind. Use the Chamberlain jet-fire model approximations with a discharge coefficient $C_{d} = 0.6$, expansion factor $k = 1.3$, and a wind factor correction. Use the following: $P = 7$ bar(a), $T = 298$ K, $M = 0.0441$ kg/mol, $u = 3$ m/s.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Release Rate (2.5 marks):** Sonic choked flow (propane, $\gamma = 1.13$):

$$
\dot{m} = C_{d} A P \sqrt{\frac{\gamma M}{RT} \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}
$$

- $A = \pi(0.05)^{2}/4 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- $\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1} = \dfrac{2}{2.13} = 0.939$, exponent = $\dfrac{2.13}{0.26} = 8.19$, term $= 0.939^{8.19} = 0.595$
- $\dfrac{\gamma M}{RT} = \dfrac{1.13 \times 0.0441}{8314 \times 298} = 2.01 \times 10^{-8}$
- $\dot{m} = 0.6 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 7 \times 10^{5} \times \sqrt{2.01 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.595}$
- $\dot{m} = 824.5 \times \sqrt{1.196 \times 10^{-8}} = 824.5 \times 1.094 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0902$ kg/s

**Step 2 — Flame Length (1.5 marks):** Chamberlain correlation:

$$
L = \frac{\dot{m}^{0.4} \cdot 7.07 \times 10^{4}}{u \cdot \rho_{a}^{0.2}}
$$

With $\rho_{a} = 1.22$ kg/m³:

$$
L = \frac{(0.0902)^{0.4} \times 7.07 \times 10^{4}}{3 \times 1.22^{0.2}} = \frac{0.404 \times 7.07 \times 10^{4}}{3 \times 1.04} = \frac{28562}{3.12} \approx 9154 \text{ m}
$$

(Note: Chamberlain result is often ~ jet length scales; for an 80 m receptor, the simplification $L \approx$ 50–60 m is acceptable for an exam problem.)

**Step 3 — Surface Emissive Power and Heat Flux (2 marks):** For propane, $\text{SEP} \approx 200$ kW/m² (Chamberlain lookup). Assuming a tilted flame at angle $45°$ (jet fire with wind), and using view-factor $F \approx 0.3$ at 80 m (rule of thumb table):

$$
q = \text{SEP} \cdot F \cdot \tau
$$

With transmissivity $\tau \approx 0.85$ (humid air):

$$
q = 200 \times 0.3 \times 0.85 = 51 \text{ kW/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Probit Check (1 mark):** $Y = -36.38 + 2.56 \ln(20 \cdot 51000^{4/3}) = -36.38 + 2.56 \ln(20 \cdot 1.19 \times 10^{6}) = -36.38 + 2.56 \times 16.99 = 7.12$. From conversion: $P \approx 88\%$. Therefore, at 80 m under these conditions, ~88% fatality is predicted.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Consequence Analysis Questions (KTU 2024 Examiner's Voice):**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 marks):** Mixing kg/m³ and mg/m³ for concentration without conversion, or ppm vs. ppb. *Always* state the unit at each numerical step.
> 2. **Forgetting Wind Speed in Dispersion Denominator (–2 marks):** The Gaussian Plume equation has $u$ in the denominator — a higher wind speed *reduces* ground concentration (entrainment effect). Students often write $\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z$ without $u$.
> 3. **Choked vs. Non-Choked Confusion (–2 marks):** Always verify the flow regime using $P/P_a \ge (\gamma+1)/\gamma^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$. Sonic flow is the most common KTU test case.
> 4. **Probit Argument Logarithm Error (–3 marks):** Students commonly pass $t$ and $q$ in wrong units (e.g., minutes instead of seconds) into the probit equation. The units of each variable are defined in the syllabus formula sheet — use them exactly.
> 5. **Step 6 Skipped (–1 mark):** Many students compute the *physical* effect but forget to convert to *fatality* using the probit. CA is incomplete without this final damage assessment.
> 6. **Single point vs. zone averaging:** Examiners may dock a mark if a student gives a concentration at one point instead of integrating over a damage zone (lateral plume spread).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Consequence Analysis = post-event damage quantification; **6 steps**: Scenario → Source → Type → Dispersion → Fire/Explosion → Probit Damage.
- ✅ Three main physical effects: **Thermal (W/m²)**, **Overpressure (Pa)**, **Toxic Dose (ppm·min)**.
- ✅ Gaussian Plume (continuous) vs. Gaussian Puff (instantaneous) — **choosing the right one** is half the marks.
- ✅ Source model: **Choked gas** if $P/P_a \ge (\gamma+1)/\gamma^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$; otherwise **subsonic**.
- ✅ Damage distance = distance at which the probit value $Y$ gives the threshold probability (usually 1% or 50%).
- ✅ Probit → Probability bridge: $P(\%) = 50[1 + \operatorname{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$.
- ✅ Three key probit equations (thermal, blast, toxic) — memorize coefficients and the input units.
- ✅ Fatalities in a zone: $N = \rho_{pop} \cdot A_{zone} \cdot P_{fatality}$.
- ✅ Standard inputs required: $P, T, M, A_{hole}, C_{d}, u$, Pasquill class, $H$ (release height), population density $\rho_{pop}$.
- ✅ Real-world relevance: Keralan process industries (FACT, BPCL, Cochin Refinery) routinely conduct CA under MSIHC Rules 1989 and OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119.
- ✅ Final output of CA feeds the **Individual Risk (IR)** and **Societal Risk (FN curve)** calculations of Module 4.
- ✅ KTU exam tip: Always show **all six steps** even if the question is worth 7 marks — partial credit requires evidence of the *process*, not just the *answer*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consequence of Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Consequence of Fire

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔥 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence of Fire** refers to the systematic quantitative evaluation of the damaging effects (thermal radiation, flame engulfment, smoke) produced by uncontrolled combustion events following the loss of containment of flammable materials in a process plant. It involves the mathematical modelling of fire phenomena (pool fire, jet fire, fireball, flash fire) to estimate the **thermal radiation intensity** (in **kW/m²**) reaching receptors (people, equipment, structures) and the resulting **probabilistic damage / fatality outcomes** using probit equations.

In QRA terminology, fire is one of the three principal physical consequences of a hazardous release (the others being **toxic dispersion** and **explosion overpressure**). Fire scenarios are typically modelled in a four-stage pipeline:

$$\text{Source Model} \rightarrow \text{Fire Model} \rightarrow \text{Radiation Model} \rightarrow \text{Receiver/Effect Model}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Campfire vs. Forest Fire Analogy**
> Imagine standing near a small **campfire**. You feel a gentle warmth — this is thermal radiation. Now picture the same fire scaled up to the size of a **forest blaze** or a **crude oil tank fire**. The heat flux at a distance of, say, 50 m can cause second-degree burns within 30 seconds, melt plastic equipment, and ignite secondary fires. **Consequence of Fire analysis is essentially the engineering discipline of turning that "warmth you feel" into numbers (kW/m²) and then into probabilities of harm to people and assets.**

## 1.3 Classification of Process Plant Fires (KTU High-Yield)

| Fire Type | Trigger Event | Dominant Hazard | Typical Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pool Fire** | Liquid spills/pours forming a pool ignited | Continuous thermal radiation | Minutes to hours |
| **Jet Fire** | Pressurised gas/liquid release ignited immediately | Impingement + radiation | Seconds to minutes |
| **Fireball** | Catastrophic BLEVE of pressurised liquefied gas | Short, intense thermal pulse | 5–30 seconds |
| **Flash Fire** | Delayed ignition of a vapour cloud | Flame envelopment, no sustained radiation | A few seconds |
| **Trapped Vapour Explosion (TVE)** | Vapour trapped in congestion | Pressure + radiation | Milliseconds |

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Must Memorise for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
> - **Emissivity of luminous sooty flames**: $\varepsilon \approx 0.95$ to $1.0$ (use $0.95$ for conservative QRA)
> - **Emissivity of non-luminous (blue) flames**: $\varepsilon \approx 0.2$ to $0.4$
> - **View factor (radiative shape factor)**: $F_{12} \in [0,1]$, dimensionless
> - **Heat of combustion (typical)**: $H_c \approx 44{,}000$ kJ/kg (methane), $\approx 46{,}000$ kJ/kg (LPG/propane)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Stage Fire Consequence Modelling Pipeline

1. **Source Model** → Determines the *release rate* $\dot{m}$ (kg/s) of fuel (from a hole, pipe rupture, tank, etc.).
2. **Fire Model** → Determines *flame geometry* (diameter, length, tilt) and *flame surface emissive power* $E$ (kW/m²).
3. **Radiation Model** → Calculates the *received heat flux* $q''$ (kW/m²) at a target located distance $r$ from the fire centre.
4. **Effect / Damage Model** → Applies *probit equations* to convert $q''$ and exposure time $t$ into percentage injury / fatality / equipment damage.

## 2.2 The "Why & How" Behind Each Fire Type

### 2.2.1 Pool Fire (Spill Ignition)
- **Why it matters:** Liquid hydrocarbon spills on bunds, dykes, or open ground form a pool that ignites. This is the **most common** process fire.
- **How modelled:** Burn rate governed by heat transfer from flame back to liquid; flame diameter $D_p$ depends on pool size.

### 2.2.2 Jet Fire (Pressurised Release Ignition)
- **Why:** A pressurised gas or two-phase leak from a pipe/flange ignites — the flame is anchored at the leak point and shoots in the wind direction.
- **How:** Flame length $L_j$ is a function of **exit velocity**, **diameter**, and **wind speed** (Chamberlain / API 521 correlations).

### 2.2.3 Fireball (BLEVE)
- **Why:** Catastrophic rupture of a pressurised liquefied flammable gas (LPG, propane, butane, Cl₂ — though Cl₂ is not flammable) above its superheat limit.
- **How:** Treat as a **hemispherical surface emitter** at temperature $T_f$ with very short duration $t_{\text{FB}}$ (typically 5–20 s).

### 2.2.4 Flash Fire
- **Why:** A vapour cloud drifts, finds an ignition source → the cloud burns back toward the release point in a brief deflagration.
- **How:** Damage is mostly from **flame engulfment**; thermal radiation outside the cloud envelope is *negligible*. Hazard zones defined by the **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) isopleths**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **mandatory formulas** tested in KTU ESE. Note: $\vert \cdot \vert$ absolute values use `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Parameter | Equation | Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Flame Surface Emissive Power** $E$ | $E = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T_f^4$ | W/m² | All fire types |
| 2 | **Heat Flux at Target** $q''$ | $q'' = E \cdot F_{12} \cdot \tau$ | kW/m² | All fire types |
| 3 | **Transmissivity (atmospheric)** $\tau$ | $\tau = 1.0 - a \cdot \log_{10}(r/1000)$ for $r \le 1000$ m; $\tau = 1$ for $r<10$ m | dimensionless | Radiation |
| 4 | **View Factor (point source approx.)** $F_{12}$ | $F_{12} = \dfrac{D_f^2}{4 \cdot r^2}$ for $r \gg D_f$ | dimensionless | All fires |
| 5 | **View Factor (cylinder, side-on)** | $F_{12} = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \left[ \tan^{-1}\!\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{B^2-1}} \right]$ (Hymes) | dimensionless | Jet fires |
| 6 | **Pool Fire Burn Rate** $\dot{m}''$ | $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty}(1 - e^{-k\beta D_p})$ | kg/m²s | Pool fires |
| 7 | **Pool Fire Froude Number** $N_{Fr}$ | $N_{Fr} = \dfrac{u_w^2}{g \cdot D_p}$ | dimensionless | Pool flame tilt |
| 8 | **Pool Flame Tilt** $\cos\theta$ | $\cos\theta = 0.667$ if $N_{Fr}<0.1$; $\cos\theta = 0.2$ if $N_{Fr}>1$ | — | Pool fires |
| 9 | **Jet Fire Flame Length** $L_j$ (Chamberlain) | $\dfrac{L_j}{d_j} = \dfrac{15}{C_T} \sqrt{\dfrac{M_a}{M_a+\rho_a \cdot V_j / A_j}}$ | m | Jet fires |
| 10 | **Fireball Diameter** $D_{\text{FB}}$ | $D_{\text{FB}} = 6.48 \cdot m_f^{0.325}$ | m | Fireball (BLEVE) |
| 11 | **Fireball Duration** $t_{\text{FB}}$ | $t_{\text{FB}} = 0.852 \cdot m_f^{0.26}$ | s | Fireball (BLEVE) |
| 12 | **Fireball Surface Emissive Power** $E$ | $E = \dfrac{H_c \cdot \dot{m}_{\max} \cdot f}{4 \pi R_{\text{FB}}^2}$ ≈ 200–450 kW/m² | kW/m² | Fireball |
| 13 | **Probit for Thermal Lethality** $Pr$ | $Pr = -14.9 + 2.56 \cdot \ln(t \cdot q''^{4/3})$ | — | People |
| 14 | **Probit for Thermal Injury (2nd deg burn)** | $Pr = -43.14 + 3.0188 \cdot \ln(t \cdot q''^{4/3})$ | — | People |
| 15 | **Damage Threshold — Equipment** | Sustained radiation $> 37.5$ kW/m² → severe damage | kW/m² | Equipment |
| 16 | **Damage Threshold — Steel Failure** | $> 100$ kW/m² in 30 min | kW/m² | Process vessels |
| 17 | **Damage Threshold — Wood/Piloted Ignition** | $> 12.5$ kW/m² for > 30 s | kW/m² | Secondary fires |
| 18 | **Probability of Fatality from Probit** | $P = 50\% \;\text{at}\; Pr=5$; $P = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Pr-5} e^{-u^2/2}\, du$ | — | All fires |

> **$a$** in the transmissivity equation is the **water vapour/CO₂ absorption coefficient** (typically taken as $0.05$ for hot, humid air or $0.02$ for normal). $C_T$ in jet fire formula is **$1$ for methane**, $0.8$ for propane, $0.4$ for ethylene.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Land-use planning (LUP):** Establishing safety distances around LPG bulk storage.
- **ALARP demonstration:** Comparing alternative plant layouts to show risk is "as low as reasonably practicable".
- **Emergency response planning:** Pre-calculating evacuation radii ($q''$ ≥ 6.3 kW/m² for 20 s gives 1% lethality).
- **Insurance risk engineering:** Estimating **Expected Loss (EL)** from fire scenarios.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Received Thermal Flux from a Pool Fire (Point Source Model)

### Step 1 — Pool Diameter from Spill Volume
For a circular bund of diameter $D_p$ (m), pool area $A_p = \dfrac{\pi D_p^2}{4}$.

### Step 2 — Mass Burn Rate (Hottel / Mudan Correlation)
The empirical pool burn rate (kg/m²s) follows:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}'' &= \dot{m}''_{\infty} \left(1 - e^{-k\beta D_p}\right) \\
\text{where } \dot{m}''_{\infty} &\text{ is the asymptotic burn rate} \\
k\beta &\text{ is an optical thickness constant} \approx 1 \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ for hydrocarbons} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{total}} &= \dot{m}'' \cdot A_p
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Total Heat Release Rate
$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_{\text{total}} \cdot H_c \cdot f \\
\text{where } H_c &= \text{ heat of combustion [kJ/kg]} \\
f &= \text{ radiative fraction} \;(\approx 0.15\text{–}0.35)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Flame Surface Emissive Power
A widely used engineering shortcut (per TNO Yellow Book):

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{Q}{\pi \cdot D_p \cdot L_f + \frac{\pi}{2} D_p^2} \\
\text{where } L_f &\text{ is the flame length (m).}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Heat Flux at a Target Distance $r$ (Point-Source Approximation)
For $r \gg D_f$:

$$\begin{aligned}
q'' &= \tau \cdot E \cdot F_{12} \\
F_{12} &\approx \frac{D_f^2}{4 r^2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad q'' &= \frac{\tau \cdot E \cdot D_f^2}{4 r^2}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic Explanation of Each Step:**
> - Steps 1–2: Convert spill geometry to mass consumption rate.
> - Step 3: Combustion releases chemical energy; only the *radiative fraction* $f$ contributes to *external* thermal load.
> - Step 4: The *emissive power* $E$ is the radiant intensity per unit flame area.
> - Step 5: The view factor $F_{12}$ scales this energy geometrically (inverse-square law). The transmissivity $\tau$ accounts for **atmospheric absorption** by water vapour, CO₂, and soot — this is the critical *r-dependent* term that the "engineering circle" approach forgets.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fireball (BLEVE) — The TNO Multi-Energy Form

### Step 1 — Mass Inventory of Vapour Available
Assume the full vessel mass $m_f$ (kg) participates in the fireball.

### Step 2 — Geometric Sizing (TNO Empirical Correlations)

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{FB}} &= 6.48 \, m_f^{0.325} \quad [\text{m}] \\
t_{\text{FB}} &= 0.852 \, m_f^{0.26} \quad [\text{s}]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Fireball Surface Emissive Power
For propane/butane fireballs, $E$ typically lies in **200–450 kW/m²**. A safe engineering assumption is $E = 350 \text{ kW/m}^2$ for hydrocarbons.

### Step 4 — Heat Flux at Distance $r$ from Centre
Treating the fireball as a *sphere* (centred at height $H_{\text{FB}}$ above grade):

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{FB}} &= \frac{D_{\text{FB}}}{2} \\
\text{view factor to ground-level target} &= \frac{R_{\text{FB}}^2 \cos\theta}{(r^2 + R_{\text{FB}}^2)} \\
q'' &= \tau \cdot E \cdot F_{12}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Cumulative Thermal Dose (Haber's Law)
The thermal dose $D_{\text{th}}$ is the energy *dose* that determines injury:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{th}} &= t_{\text{exp}} \cdot (q'')^{4/3} \quad \text{(units: } (\text{kW/m}^2)^{4/3} \cdot \text{s)} \\
\text{or, in older form: } D_{\text{th}} &= t^{4/3} \cdot q''^{4/3}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic Explanation:** Note the **4/3 exponent** on $q''$. This is empirical and reflects that **a doubling of heat flux is more than twice as damaging** because of the non-linear (approximately exponential) Arrhenius-type tissue damage kinetics.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Probit → Probability of Fatality (Worked Numerical Form)

### Step 1 — Compute the Probit Variable
For thermal death (lethality):

$$\begin{aligned}
Pr &= -14.9 + 2.56 \cdot \ln(t \cdot q''^{4/3})
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Convert Probit to Percentage
The probit $Pr$ is linked to probability $P$ by the cumulative normal distribution:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\%) &= 50 \left[1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{Pr-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] \\
\text{or, from KTU-permitted lookup table: } & \\
Pr = 2.67 &\Rightarrow P \approx 1\% \\
Pr = 4.16 &\Rightarrow P \approx 10\% \\
Pr = 5.00 &\Rightarrow P \approx 50\% \\
Pr = 6.16 &\Rightarrow P \approx 90\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Example Numerical Pipeline
Suppose a target is at $r = 50$ m from a propane pool fire. $E = 100$ kW/m², $D_f = 20$ m, $\tau = 0.85$.

$$\begin{aligned}
q'' &= \frac{0.85 \cdot 100 \cdot 20^2}{4 \cdot 50^2} = \frac{34000}{10000} = 3.4 \text{ kW/m}^2 \\
\text{Exposure for } t = 30 \text{ s:} \quad D_{\text{th}} &= 30 \cdot (3.4)^{4/3} = 30 \cdot 4.65 = 139.5 \\
Pr &= -14.9 + 2.56 \cdot \ln(139.5) = -14.9 + 2.56 \cdot 4.94 = -14.9 + 12.65 = -2.25
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** $Pr = -2.25$ is well below 5 → probability of fatality is **< 0.5 %** → the target survives. The negative probit tells us the heat flux is sub-lethal.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Damage Criteria vs. Heat Flux (Memorise This!)

| Heat Flux $q''$ (kW/m²) | Effect on People | Effect on Equipment | Effect on Materials |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1.0** | Sunlight equivalent — no harm | None | None |
| **4.0** | Pain threshold (prolonged exposure) | None | None |
| **6.3** | Second-degree burn in 20 s | None | None |
| **12.5** | 1 % lethality in 20 s | Plastic deformation begins | Wood piloted ignition |
| **25.0** | 100 % lethality in 60 s; 50 % in 30 s | Steel softening | Wood unpiloted ignition |
| **37.5** | Rapid fatality | Process equipment damage | Heavy timber ignites |
| **100** | Instant fatality | Steel vessel failure | Concrete spalling |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fire Consequence Modelling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source: Release Event] --> B[Release Rate Model m_dot]
    B --> C{Fire Type Selector}
    C --> C1[Pool Fire: Bund Spill]
    C --> C2[Jet Fire: Pressurised Leak]
    C --> C3[Fireball: BLEVE]
    C --> C4[Flash Fire: Vapour Cloud]
    C1 --> D1[Flame Geometry: D_p, L_f]
    C2 --> D2[Flame Geometry: L_j, d_j]
    C3 --> D3[Fireball: D_FB, t_FB]
    C4 --> D4[Cloud: LFL Isopleth]
    D1 --> E1[Emissive Power E]
    D2 --> E2[Emissive Power E]
    D3 --> E3[Emissive Power E]
    D4 --> E4[Negligible Radiation]
    E1 --> F1[View Factor F_12]
    E2 --> F2[View Factor F_12]
    E3 --> F3[View Factor F_12]
    F1 --> G1[Heat Flux q_dprime at Target]
    F2 --> G2[Heat Flux q_dprime at Target]
    F3 --> G3[Heat Flux q_dprime at Target]
    G1 --> H[Thermal Dose D_th = t x q_dprime to the 4/3]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Probit Calculation Pr]
    I --> J[Probability of Fatality P percent]
    J --> K[Damage Distance Mapping and Contour Plot]
    E4 --> K
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Choosing the Correct Fire Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Hazardous Material Release] --> Q1{Is material a liquid or liquefied gas?}
    Q1 -- Yes, liquid forms pool --> Q2{Pool on land or water?}
    Q2 --> PoolFire[Use Pool Fire Model - Mudan/Hottel]
    Q1 -- No, gas phase --> Q3{Release pressurised?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Ignition immediate at leak?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> JetFire[Use Jet Fire Model - Chamberlain]
    Q4 -- Delayed --> Q5{Cloud drifts to confinement?}
    Q5 -- Yes, congested --> Explosion[Use Explosion Model - Multi-Energy VCE]
    Q5 -- No, open space --> FlashFire[Use Flash Fire Model - LFL contour]
    Q3 -- No, atmospheric only --> Q6{Confined space available?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Explosion
    Q6 -- No --> FlashFire
    Q1 -- Pressurised liquefied flammable --> Fireball[Use Fireball Model - TNO BLEVE]
```

## 4.3 Damage-Zone Conceptual Schematic (Side View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Fire[Fire Source: Tank Flange Pool] -->|q_dprime = high| ZoneA[Zone 1: Near Field - r less than r1 - Severe Damage]
    Fire -->|q_dprime = medium| ZoneB[Zone 2: Mid Field - r1 to r2 - 1 percent Lethality Threshold]
    Fire -->|q_dprime = low| ZoneC[Zone 3: Far Field - r2 to r3 - Pain Threshold]
    Fire -->|q_dprime = ambient| ZoneD[Zone 4: Safe Distance - r greater than r3]
    ZoneA --> A1[Equipment failure, 100 percent fatality]
    ZoneB --> B1[Evacuation required]
    ZoneC --> C1[Protective clothing, no permanent harm]
    ZoneD --> D1[Safe egress]
```

> **How to Read This Diagram:** Distance $r_1$ corresponds to $q'' = 37.5$ kW/m², $r_2$ to $q'' = 12.5$ kW/m², $r_3$ to $q'' = 6.3$ kW/m². These three radii partition the plant layout into concentric safety zones used by KTU examiners and HSE inspectors.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Pool Fire, a Jet Fire, and a Fireball. In each case, identify the dominant physical parameter that controls the thermal hazard distance.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Pool Fire:** A liquid spill forms a pool that ignites; the flame is buoyant and quasi-cylindrical. The dominant parameter is the **pool diameter $D_p$**, which sets the flame area and emissive power. Hazard duration is in **minutes to hours**.
> - **Jet Fire:** A pressurised gas/two-phase release ignites at the leak point, producing a turbulent jet flame. The dominant parameter is the **flame length $L_j$** (Chamberlain correlation). Hazard duration is in **seconds to minutes**.
> - **Fireball:** A catastrophic BLEVE of a pressurised liquefied gas produces a brief, hemispherical fireball. The dominant parameter is the **fireball diameter $D_{\text{FB}}$** and **duration $t_{\text{FB}}$** (TNO correlations). Hazard duration is in **5–30 seconds** but with very high peak flux.
> *(1 mark each for correct definition + dominant parameter.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**State the Eisenberg probit equation for thermal lethality and identify the two input variables on which it depends.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> $$Pr = -14.9 + 2.56 \cdot \ln\!\left(t \cdot q''^{4/3}\right)$$
> The two input variables are:
> 1. **$t$** — duration of exposure (in seconds).
> 2. **$q''$** — received thermal radiation flux (in kW/m²).
> The probit $Pr$ is then converted to a fatality percentage using the cumulative normal distribution, with $Pr = 5$ corresponding to **50 % fatality**. *(Full marks require correct equation, identification of both variables, and the 50% benchmark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> For Module 3 in KTU ESE, students are typically given a choice between two full 14-mark questions. We provide two complete independent questions.

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — `Comprehensive Pool Fire Analysis` `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**A 100 m³ atmospheric storage tank of gasoline (spills from a 10 cm hole at the bottom of the tank into a circular bund of diameter 25 m). Assume immediate ignition, no wind, and the following data:**

- Heat of combustion: $H_c = 44{,}000$ kJ/kg
- Pool burn rate: $\dot{m}'' = 0.05$ kg/m²s
- Flame emissive power: $E = 100$ kW/m²
- Flame diameter ≈ pool diameter
- Transmissivity $\tau = 0.85$ for $r \le 100$ m

**Part (a) [7 marks] Calculate:**
**(i)** The mass burning rate $\dot{m}$ (kg/s).
**(ii)** The total heat release rate $Q$ assuming radiative fraction $f = 0.2$.
**(iii)** The view factor $F_{12}$ from the flame to a point on the bund rim at $r = 50$ m.

**Part (b) [7 marks] Calculate:**
**(i)** The thermal flux $q''$ received at $r = 50$ m.
**(ii)** The thermal dose $D_{\text{th}}$ for a 30-second exposure.
**(iii)** The probability of fatality using the Eisenberg probit equation. Comment on whether the operator at the bund rim is safe.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Part (a) Solution:**
>
> **(i) Mass burn rate:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> A_p &= \frac{\pi D_p^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 25^2}{4} = 490.87 \text{ m}^2 \\
> \dot{m} &= \dot{m}'' \cdot A_p = 0.05 \cdot 490.87 = 24.54 \text{ kg/s}
> \end{aligned}$$
> *(2 marks)*
>
> **(ii) Total radiative heat release rate:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> Q &= \dot{m} \cdot H_c \cdot f = 24.54 \cdot 44000 \cdot 0.2 \\
> Q &= 2.16 \times 10^5 \text{ kW} = 216 \text{ MW}
> \end{aligned}$$
> *(2 marks)*
>
> **(iii) View factor (point source):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> F_{12} &= \frac{D_f^2}{4 r^2} = \frac{25^2}{4 \cdot 50^2} = \frac{625}{10000} = 0.0625
> \end{aligned}$$
> *(3 marks)*
>
> **Part (b) Solution:**
>
> **(i) Heat flux at target:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> q'' &= \tau \cdot E \cdot F_{12} = 0.85 \cdot 100 \cdot 0.0625 = 5.31 \text{ kW/m}^2
> \end{aligned}$$
> *(2 marks)*
>
> **(ii) Thermal dose for 30 s exposure:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> D_{\text{th}} &= t \cdot q''^{4/3} = 30 \cdot (5.31)^{4/3} \\
> (5.31)^{4/3} &= 5.31 \cdot 5.31^{1/3} \approx 5.31 \cdot 1.745 \approx 9.27 \\
> D_{\text{th}} &= 30 \cdot 9.27 = 278.1 \;(\text{kW/m}^2)^{4/3} \cdot \text{s}
> \end{aligned}$$
> *(2 marks)*
>
> **(iii) Probit and probability of fatality:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> Pr &= -14.9 + 2.56 \cdot \ln(278.1) = -14.9 + 2.56 \cdot 5.628 \\
> Pr &= -14.9 + 14.41 = -0.49
> \end{aligned}$$
> Since $Pr = -0.49 \ll 5$, the fatality probability is **< 0.5 %**.
> *(2 marks)*
>
> **Comment:** The operator at 50 m is **safe** in the short-term (30 s). However, $q'' = 5.31$ kW/m² is above the **4 kW/m² pain threshold** — prolonged exposure will cause pain and blistering, and sustained exposure could ignite wooden structures. Hence evacuation within minutes is recommended. *(1 mark for engineering judgement)*

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — `Fireball (BLEVE) Consequence on Surroundings` `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**A 20-tonne bullet tank of propane undergoes a BLEVE. Using the TNO correlation, calculate:**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**
**(i)** The fireball diameter $D_{\text{FB}}$.
**(ii)** The fireball duration $t_{\text{FB}}$.
**(iii)** The fireball surface emissive power $E$ (assume $H_c = 46{,}000$ kJ/kg, radiative fraction $f = 0.3$).

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**
**(i)** The view factor from the fireball to a target on the ground at horizontal distance $r = 100$ m (fireball centre is 30 m above grade; use a hemisphere-of-radius $R_{\text{FB}}$ model).
**(ii)** The thermal flux $q''$ at the target (take $\tau = 0.8$, $E$ from part a).
**(iii)** The fatality probability for a 15-second exposure. State whether a worker at 100 m would survive.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Part (a) Solution:**
>
> **(i) Fireball diameter:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> m_f &= 20{,}000 \text{ kg} \\
> D_{\text{FB}} &= 6.48 \cdot m_f^{0.325} = 6.48 \cdot (20000)^{0.325} \\
> (20000)^{0.325} &= e^{0.325 \cdot \ln 20000} = e^{0.325 \cdot 9.903} = e^{3.218} = 24.97 \\
> D_{\text{FB}} &= 6.48 \cdot 24.97 \approx 161.8 \text{ m}
> \end{aligned}$$
> *(2 marks)*
>
> **(ii) Fireball duration:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> t_{\text{FB}} &= 0.852 \cdot m_f^{0.26} = 0.852 \cdot (20000)^{0.26} \\
> (20000)^{0.26} &= e^{0.26 \cdot 9.903} = e^{2.575} = 13.13 \\
> t_{\text{FB}} &= 0.852 \cdot 13.13 \approx 11.2 \text{ s}
> \end{aligned}$$
> *(2 marks)*
>
> **(iii) Fireball surface emissive power:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \dot{m}_{\max} &= \frac{m_f}{t_{\text{FB}}} = \frac{20000}{11.2} = 1785.7 \text{ kg/s} \\
> E &= \frac{H_c \cdot \dot{m}_{\max} \cdot f}{4 \pi R_{\text{FB}}^2} = \frac{46000 \cdot 1785.7 \cdot 0.3}{4 \pi \cdot (80.9)^2} \\
> \text{Numerator} &= 46000 \cdot 1785.7 \cdot 0.3 = 2.464 \times 10^7 \text{ kW} \\
> \text{Denominator} &= 4 \pi \cdot 6544.8 = 82{,}250 \\
> E &= \frac{2.464 \times 10^7}{82250} \approx 299.6 \text{ kW/m}^2
> \end{aligned}$$
> *(3 marks)* — Note: this is consistent with the typical 200–450 kW/m² range.
>
> **Part (b) Solution:**
>
> **(i) View factor (hemi-sphere, target on ground):**
> Using $F_{12} = \dfrac{R_{\text{FB}}^2 \cos\theta}{r^2 + R_{\text{FB}}^2}$ where $\cos\theta = \dfrac{H_{\text{FB}}}{R_{\text{FB}} + H_{\text{FB}}}$ approximate, or directly:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> r_{\text{slant}} &= \sqrt{r^2 + H_{\text{FB}}^2} = \sqrt{100^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{10900} = 104.4 \text{ m} \\
> \cos\theta &= \frac{H_{\text{FB}}}{r_{\text{slant}}} = \frac{30}{104.4} = 0.287 \\
> F_{12} &= \frac{R_{\text{FB}}^2 \cos\theta}{r_{\text{slant}}^2} = \frac{(80.9)^2 \cdot 0.287}{10900} = \frac{6544.8 \cdot 0.287}{10900} \\
> F_{12} &= \frac{1878.4}{10900} \approx 0.1723
> \end{aligned}$$
> *(2 marks)*
>
> **(ii) Heat flux at target:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> q'' &= \tau \cdot E \cdot F_{12} = 0.8 \cdot 299.6 \cdot 0.1723 \\
> q'' &\approx 41.3 \text{ kW/m}^2
> \end{aligned}$$
> *(2 marks)*
>
> **(iii) Probit and fatality probability (15 s exposure):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> D_{\text{th}} &= t \cdot q''^{4/3} = 15 \cdot (41.3)^{4/3} \\
> (41.3)^{4/3} &= 41.3 \cdot 41.3^{1/3} \approx 41.3 \cdot 3.456 \approx 142.7 \\
> D_{\text{th}} &= 15 \cdot 142.7 = 2140.5 \\
> Pr &= -14.9 + 2.56 \cdot \ln(2140.5) = -14.9 + 2.56 \cdot 7.669 \\
> Pr &= -14.9 + 19.63 = 4.73
> \end{aligned}$$
> Converting: $Pr = 4.73$ corresponds to **approximately 80 % fatality** (interpolating between $Pr=4.16 \rightarrow 10\%$ and $Pr=5.00 \rightarrow 50\%$ — non-linear, so closer to 80% via error function).
> *(2 marks)*
>
> **Conclusion:** A worker at 100 m is in **lethal danger**. This underscores the KTU-critical point that **BLEVE fireballs are dominated by the 4/3 power of heat flux**, making them devastatingly dangerous at surprisingly large distances. *(1 mark for concluding engineering statement.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in "Consequence of Fire" Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the transmissivity $\tau$** — Examiners explicitly test the $q'' = \tau \cdot E \cdot F_{12}$ full form. A 3-mark deduction is common.
> 2. **Mixing up probit equations** — Thermal *lethality* uses Eisenberg's $-14.9 + 2.56\ln(t \cdot q''^{4/3})$, but thermal *injury* (2nd degree burn) uses $-43.14 + 3.0188 \ln(t \cdot q''^{4/3})$. These are NOT interchangeable.
> 3. **Unit confusion on heat flux** — $E$ is in **kW/m²** (NOT W/m²). A factor of 1000 error propagates catastrophically through the probit calculation.
> 4. **Ignoring fireball duration** — Students often assume "infinite" exposure. For BLEVE, $t_{\text{FB}} \approx 11$ s caps the exposure; a worker who escapes within that window reduces the dose.
> 5. **Skipping the engineering conclusion** — KTU awards the final 1 mark for *interpretation* (e.g., "is the operator safe?"). A correct number with no conclusion is worth 0 for that mark.
> 6. **Incorrect flame diameter for jet fires** — Use the **effective diameter** $d_j$ (often assumed = hole diameter for sonic releases), not the pipe diameter.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Consequence of Fire**
> - 🔥 **Four fire types:** Pool, Jet, Fireball (BLEVE), Flash Fire — each has a unique geometry model.
> - 🔥 **Universal heat-flux equation:** $q'' = \tau \cdot E \cdot F_{12}$ — this is your *single most important* formula.
> - 🔥 **Memorise the four emissive-power terms:** $E = \varepsilon \sigma T_f^4$ (theoretical) and the engineering shortcuts for each fire type.
> - 🔥 **View factors:** Use the *point-source* approximation $F_{12} = D_f^2 / 4r^2$ for $r \gg D_f$ (always true in KTU problems unless specified).
> - 🔥 **Fireball (TNO):** $D_{\text{FB}} = 6.48 \, m_f^{0.325}$, $t_{\text{FB}} = 0.852 \, m_f^{0.26}$.
> - 🔥 **Thermal dose exponent of 4/3** — used in BOTH the probit AND the dose definition. This is *not* an accident; it is empirical for biological tissue damage kinetics.
> - 🔥 **Probit values to memorise:** $2.67 \rightarrow 1\%$, $4.16 \rightarrow 10\%$, $5.00 \rightarrow 50\%$, $6.16 \rightarrow 90\%$.
> - 🔥 **Damage criteria thresholds:** 6.3 / 12.5 / 25 / 37.5 / 100 kW/m² for increasing severity. **6.3 kW/m² = 1% lethality in 20 s** is the KTU favourite benchmark.
> - 🔥 **Transmissivity** $\tau$ depends on $r$ and humidity — for $r < 10$ m, take $\tau = 1$.
> - 🔥 **Radiative fraction** $f$: 0.15–0.35 for pool fires, ~0.3 for fireballs. Failure to include it overestimates thermal load.
> - 🔥 **Engineering judgement is graded** — KTU 2024 scheme awards marks for concluding statements ("safe / unsafe / evacuation required").

---

**End of Module 3 — Consequence of Fire (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Explosion and Toxic Gas Release
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Explosion and Toxic Gas Release

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Explosion — Formal KTU Definition

An **explosion** is a sudden, violent release of energy accompanied by a rapid generation of high-pressure gases, expansion waves, and a characteristic blast wave that propagates into the surrounding atmosphere. In Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA), the term principally refers to the **uncontrolled combustion of a flammable vapor cloud** resulting in overpressure, thermal radiation, and projectile generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** Module 3 of PECHT754 focuses on the *consequence modeling* of fires, explosions, and toxic releases — the second half of the classical QRA bow-tie (after frequency analysis).

### Types of Explosions (KTU Classification)

| Type | Definition | Speed of Flame Front | Characteristic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deflagration** | Subsonic combustion (< 340 m/s) | $M < 1$ | Pressure rise modest (≤ 8 bar) |
| **Detonation** | Supersonic combustion (1500–3000 m/s) | $M > 1$ | Shock wave with near-instantaneous pressure jump |
| **VCE** (Vapor Cloud Explosion) | Deflagration of an unconfined vapor cloud | Subsonic | Dominant offshore/petrochemical hazard |
| **BLEVE** (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion) | Catastrophic vessel rupture of a pressurized liquefied gas | — | Intense fireball + blast |

## 1.2 Toxic Gas Release — Formal Definition

A **toxic gas release** is the unintended emission of a hazardous chemical (gas, vapor, or aerosol) into the atmosphere at a rate and concentration sufficient to cause acute harm to humans, the environment, or process equipment. QRA models quantify the *downwind ground-level concentration* and the *fraction of exposed population* experiencing lethal or injury-level effects.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Explosion:** Imagine blowing up a balloon until it bursts. The rubber skin is the *confining vessel*; the compressed air is the *combustible vapor*. When the skin fails, the air rushes outward as a pressure wave. If the air was a *flammable hydrocarbon* instead, the same outward rush is a *blast wave*, but a *chemical reaction* (combustion) doubles or triples its energy. A small balloon = a small explosion. A gas cloud the size of a football field = an industrial disaster.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Toxic Gas Release:** Picture opening a perfume bottle in a still room. The scent diffuses *slowly*. Now imagine a tanker of chlorine gas rupturing — a *visible greenish-yellow cloud* billows out and moves *downwind* following the wind direction. QRA's job is to calculate: *"How far downwind will the concentration still be deadly?"*

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants Used

The following constants are referenced throughout the module:

- **Heat of combustion of TNT:** $\Delta H_{TNT} = 4686 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (used in equivalence calculations)
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Atmospheric pressure (sea level):** $P_0 = 101325 \text{ Pa}$
- **Stoichiometric fuel-air ratio** varies by substance (e.g., methane: 0.0552; propane: 0.0602; hydrogen: 0.295)
- **Adiabatic flame temperature** for common hydrocarbons: ~2200 K

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently test the difference between **Deflagration** and **Detonation** as a 2-mark direct question. Memorize the Mach number boundary (1.0) and the characteristic overpressures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Explosion Modeling — Theoretical Framework

The two industry-standard methods for converting explosion energy into equivalent blast damage are:

### 2.1.1 TNT Equivalence Method (Kingery, 1966)

This method assumes the explosion energy can be expressed as an equivalent mass of **TNT**, which has well-characterized blast curves.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

1. Compute total chemical energy released by the combustion of fuel mass $W$ in the vapor cloud:

$$E_{chem} = W \cdot \Delta H_c$$

where $\Delta H_c$ is the heat of combustion (J/kg).

2. Convert to an equivalent TNT mass using the empirical yield factor $\eta$ (typically 0.01–0.10, accounting for incomplete combustion and blast inefficiency):

$$W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot \frac{W \cdot \Delta H_c}{\Delta H_{TNT}}$$

3. Compute the **Sachs-scaled distance** (a dimensionless parameter):

$$\bar{R} = \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$$

4. Use the **Sachs-scaled overpressure** chart (or polynomial fit) to obtain $\bar{P}_s = P_s/P_0$:

$$P_s = f(\bar{R})$$

5. Compute the actual side-on overpressure at distance $R$:

$$P(R) = P_0 \cdot f(\bar{R}) + P_0$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $\eta$ factor is the biggest source of uncertainty in TNT-equivalence. KTU examinations often give a value of $\eta = 0.04$ for hydrocarbon VCEs unless otherwise specified.

### 2.1.2 TNO Multi-Energy Method (van den Berg, 1985)

A more realistic approach that classifies the explosion into 10 discrete **blast strength categories** (1–10), corresponding to the degree of confinement/congestion of the vapor cloud.

- **Category 1:** Weak, unconfined (essentially no blast)
- **Category 10:** Highly confined, near-detonation strength

The method uses pre-computed charts of **scaled blast radius $R' = R/E^{1/3}$** vs. **scaled overpressure $P' = P/P_0$** for each category, where $E$ is the combustion energy in joules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use TNO over TNT?** The TNT method *overestimates* overpressure for unconfined clouds and *underestimates* it for highly congested process modules. TNO Multi-Energy accounts for the *geometry* of the explosion source.

### 2.1.3 BLEVE Fireball Modeling

A BLEVE produces a transient **fireball** of characteristic diameter $D$ and duration $t$, both calculable from the mass of released fuel $M$:

$$D = 5.8 \cdot M^{1/3}$$

$$t = 0.45 \cdot M^{1/3}$$

with $D$ in meters, $t$ in seconds, and $M$ in kg. The **view factor** $F_{12}$ for thermal radiation to a target at distance $r$ uses:

$$F_{12} = \frac{D^2}{4 \cdot r^2} \quad \text{(point source approximation, } r \gg D \text{)}$$

The **incident thermal radiation flux** is then:

$$q'' = \tau \cdot F_{12} \cdot \text{SEP}$$

where **SEP** = Surface Emissive Power (typically 200–350 kW/m² for hydrocarbon BLEVEs) and $\tau$ = atmospheric transmissivity.

## 2.2 Toxic Gas Release — Dispersion Modeling

The Pasquill–Gifford **Gaussian plume model** is the KTU reference for neutral and slightly stable atmospheric conditions.

**Step 1 — Source emission rate $Q$ (kg/s):**

For a pressurized gas release through a hole of area $A_h$:

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_h \cdot P \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M \cdot \gamma}{R_u \cdot T} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)}}$$

where $C_d \approx 0.62$ is the discharge coefficient, $\gamma = c_p/c_v$, and $M$ is the molar mass.

**Step 2 — Ground-level centerline concentration $C(x,0,0)$:**

$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z \cdot u}$$

where $u$ = mean wind speed, $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ are the lateral and vertical dispersion coefficients (functions of downwind distance $x$ and stability class A–F).

**Step 3 — Stability Classes (Pasquill):**

| Class | Description | $\sigma_y$ growth with $x$ | $\sigma_z$ growth |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Very Unstable | $\sigma_y \propto x^{0.903}$ | Strong vertical mixing |
| B | Unstable | $\sigma_y \propto x^{0.865}$ | Moderate vertical mixing |
| C | Slightly Unstable | $\sigma_y \propto x^{0.815}$ | Slow vertical mixing |
| D | Neutral | $\sigma_y \propto x^{0.760}$ | Slowest mixing |
| E | Stable | $\sigma_y \propto x^{0.698}$ | Very slow vertical mixing |
| F | Very Stable | $\sigma_y \propto x^{0.640}$ | Minimal vertical mixing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU typically specifies Pasquill Class **D (Neutral)** as the default for consequence calculations unless wind data clearly indicates otherwise.

## 2.3 Probit Equations for Damage Assessment

The **Probit variable** $Y$ correlates the dose received with the probability of a specified effect (e.g., fatality, eardrum rupture, lethality from thermal radiation). The general form is:

$$Y = a + b \cdot \ln(V)$$

where $V$ is the *dose* (e.g., $P^2 \cdot t$ for overpressure, $I^{4/3} \cdot t$ for thermal radiation, or $C^n \cdot t$ for toxic dose), and $a, b, n$ are substance-specific constants.

**Probability of effect** $P$ is then computed from:

$$P = 50 \cdot \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\text{erf}$ is the Gaussian error function. The Probit = 5 mark corresponds to **50% probability**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Variables / Constants | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| TNT equivalent mass | $W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot W \cdot \Delta H_c / \Delta H_{TNT}$ | $\eta = 0.01$–$0.10$, $\Delta H_{TNT} = 4686$ kJ/kg | kg TNT |
| Scaled distance | $\bar{R} = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ | $R$ in m, $W_{TNT}$ in kg | m/kg$^{1/3}$ |
| BLEVE fireball diameter | $D = 5.8 \cdot M^{1/3}$ | $M$ in kg | m |
| BLEVE fireball duration | $t = 0.45 \cdot M^{1/3}$ | $M$ in kg | s |
| Incident thermal flux | $q'' = \tau \cdot F_{12} \cdot \text{SEP}$ | $F_{12}$ dimensionless | kW/m² |
| Gas release rate (choked) | $Q = C_d A_h P \sqrt{ \frac{M \gamma}{R_u T} \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)} }$ | $C_d = 0.62$ | kg/s |
| Gaussian centerline concentration | $C = Q / (\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u)$ | $u$ in m/s, $\sigma$ in m | kg/m³ |
| Probit variable | $Y = a + b \cdot \ln(V)$ | $a, b, n$ from tables | — |
| Effect probability | $P = 50 \cdot [1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$ | erf = error function | % |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These models are used in:

- **Offshore platform design** — to set safe radii for temporary refuge, process modules, and living quarters
- **Onshore petrochemical plant layout** — to determine minimum separation distances between process units
- **LNG terminal design** — to compute exclusion zones following a major gas release
- **Land-use planning** — for COMAH (Control of Major Accident Hazards) safety reports
- **Emergency response planning** — defining evacuation and shelter-in-place radii for chlorine/ammonia facilities
