# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — TNT Equivalence and Sachs Scaling

**Starting Point:** An explosion releases chemical energy $E_{chem}$. We wish to find the overpressure at distance $R$ from the epicenter.

**Step 1 — Total chemical energy released by the cloud:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{chem} &= W \cdot \Delta H_c \\
\text{Units:} \quad & \text{[kg]} \times \text{[J/kg]} = \text{[J]}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the explosion efficiency factor $\eta$ to account for incomplete combustion and non-ideal blast coupling:**

$$E_{blast} = \eta \cdot E_{chem}$$

**Step 3 — Convert blast energy to equivalent TNT mass:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{TNT} &= \frac{E_{blast}}{\Delta H_{TNT}} \\
&= \frac{\eta \cdot W \cdot \Delta H_c}{4686 \text{ kJ/kg}}
\end{aligned}$$

The factor $4686$ in the denominator has units of kJ/kg so the final answer is in kg of TNT equivalent.

**Step 4 — Apply the Hopkinson-Cranz (cube-root) scaling law.** The fundamental physical principle: two explosive charges of the same shape and explosive type but different masses $W_1$ and $W_2$ will produce the same blast overpressure at distances $R_1$ and $R_2$ satisfying:

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)^{1/3}$$

This is because the blast wave energy is distributed over a spherical surface of area $4\pi R^2$, and the energy density scales as $E/(4\pi R^2)$. If we double $W$, $E$ doubles, so $R$ must scale as $W^{1/3}$ to keep the overpressure constant.

**Step 5 — Define the Sachs-scaled distance:**

$$\bar{R} = \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} \quad \text{[m/kg}^{1/3}\text{]}$$

**Step 6 — Read Sachs-scaled overpressure** $\bar{P}_s = P_s/P_0$ from the standardized TNT blast chart using $\bar{R}$.

**Step 7 — Recover the actual side-on overpressure:**

$$P_s = \bar{P}_s \cdot P_0$$

**Step 8 — Reflected overpressure** (for a surface-blast wave hitting a vertical surface):

$$P_r = 2 P_s + \frac{(\gamma+1) P_s^2}{(\gamma-1) P_s + 2\gamma P_0}$$

For $\gamma = 1.4$ (air) and a strong shock ($P_s \gg P_0$), this reduces to $P_r \approx 8 P_s$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — TNT Equivalence

**Problem:** A 500 kg cloud of propane ($\Delta H_c = 46020$ kJ/kg) explodes with efficiency $\eta = 0.04$. Estimate the overpressure at a distance of 200 m from the epicenter.

**Step 1 — Total chemical energy:**

$$E_{chem} = 500 \times 46020 = 23{,}010{,}000 \text{ kJ} = 2.301 \times 10^{10} \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Effective blast energy:**

$$E_{blast} = 0.04 \times 2.301 \times 10^{10} = 9.204 \times 10^{8} \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent TNT mass:**

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{9.204 \times 10^{8}}{4686 \times 10^3} = \frac{9.204 \times 10^{8}}{4.686 \times 10^{6}} \approx 196.4 \text{ kg TNT}$$

**Step 4 — Scaled distance:**

$$\bar{R} = \frac{200}{(196.4)^{1/3}}$$

The cube root: $(196.4)^{1/3} = 5.815$ m/kg$^{1/3}$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{200}{5.815} \approx 34.4 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Step 5 — From Sachs blast chart** (standard polynomial fit):

$$\bar{P}_s = \frac{808 \cdot (1 + \bar{R}^{0.5})}{\sqrt{1 + \bar{R}^{2.6}} \cdot \left(1.61 + \bar{R}^{1.15}\right)} \cdot \frac{1}{\bar{R}}$$

Wait — let me use the correct Sachs (1953) form for the **side-on** peak overpressure. The valid form for large $\bar{R}$ is:

$$\bar{P}_s = \frac{0.974}{\bar{R}} + \frac{1.455}{\bar{R}^2} + \frac{5.85}{\bar{R}^3} - \frac{0.198}{\bar{R}^4}$$

Substituting $\bar{R} = 34.4$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{P}_s &\approx \frac{0.974}{34.4} + \frac{1.455}{(34.4)^2} + \frac{5.85}{(34.4)^3} - \frac{0.198}{(34.4)^4} \\
&\approx 0.02831 + 0.001230 + 0.0001436 - 0.000000014 \\
&\approx 0.02968
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Actual side-on overpressure:**

$$P_s = 0.02968 \times 101325 \approx 3007 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.030 \text{ bar}$$

This corresponds to a **slight window-glass breakage threshold** but well below structural damage.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Toxic Gas Release (Gaussian Plume)

**Problem:** Chlorine ($\text{Cl}_2$) is released from a pressurized vessel at a rate of $Q = 1.5$ kg/s. Wind speed is $u = 4$ m/s, Pasquill stability class D. Find the ground-level centerline concentration at $x = 500$ m downwind.

**Step 1 — Pasquill-Gifford dispersion coefficients for class D:**

$$\sigma_y = 0.08 \cdot x \cdot (1 + 0.0001 \cdot x)^{-0.5}$$

$$\sigma_z = 0.06 \cdot x \cdot (1 + 0.0015 \cdot x)^{-0.5}$$

**Step 2 — At $x = 500$ m:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_y &= 0.08 \times 500 \times (1 + 0.05)^{-0.5} \\
&= 40 \times (1.05)^{-0.5} \\
&= 40 \times 0.9759 = 39.04 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_z &= 0.06 \times 500 \times (1 + 0.75)^{-0.5} \\
&= 30 \times (1.75)^{-0.5} \\
&= 30 \times 0.7559 = 22.68 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Centerline ground-level concentration:**

$$C = \frac{Q}{\pi \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z \cdot u}$$

$$C = \frac{1.5}{\pi \times 39.04 \times 22.68 \times 4}$$

$$C = \frac{1.5}{11125.5} = 1.348 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/m}^3 = 134.8 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Convert to ppm** (for Cl₂, MW = 70.9 g/mol):

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{C \times 24.45}{MW} \times 10^3 = \frac{134.8 \times 24.45}{70.9} \approx 46.5 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 5 — Compare to AEGL-2** (60-min Acute Exposure Guideline Level, Chlorine) = **0.5 ppm**. The concentration 46.5 ppm far exceeds AEGL-2, indicating **severe health hazard** at 500 m downwind.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Damage Thresholds

| Effect | Overpressure (kPa) | Thermal Flux (kW/m²) | Toxic Conc. (Cl₂, ppm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Glass breakage | 1.0–2.5 | — | — |
| Eardrum rupture (1%) | 16.5 | — | — |
| Lung damage (50%) | 83 | — | — |
| Lethality (50%) | 175 | — | — |
| Skin pain (10 s) | — | 1.74 | — |
| Second-degree burns (10 s) | — | 4.73 | — |
| Lethality (50%, 10 s) | — | 12.5 | — |
| AEGL-2 (60 min) | — | — | 0.5 |
| AEGL-3 (60 min) | — | — | 7.1 |
| IDLH (immediate) | — | — | 10 |

## 4.4 TNT Equivalence Computational Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mass of Vapor Cloud W] --> B[Multiply by Heat of Combustion dH_c]
    B --> C[Apply Efficiency Factor eta]
    C --> D[Divide by dH_TNT equals 4686 kJ per kg]
    D --> E[W_TNT in kg]
    E --> F[Compute Cube Root of W_TNT]
    F --> G[Distance R divided by W_TNT^1/3]
    G --> H[Sachs Scaled Distance R-bar]
    H --> I[Read Sachs Polynomial]
    I --> J[Scaled Overpressure P-bar]
    J --> K[Multiply by P_0 equals 101325 Pa]
    K --> L[Side-On Overpressure P_s]
    L --> M[Apply Reflected Pressure Formula]
    M --> N[Final P_r for Surface Reflection]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Q1]** *(3 marks)*

**Q1.** Distinguish between **deflagration** and **detonation** in the context of vapor cloud explosions. State the Mach number boundary that separates them.

**Model Answer:**

- **Deflagration:** Subsonic combustion wave propagating at speeds below the speed of sound in the unreacted medium ($M < 1$). The pressure rise is gradual and peak overpressures in an unconfined cloud are typically ≤ 8 bar.
- **Detonation:** Supersonic combustion wave with a coupled shock front and reaction zone, propagating at $M > 1$ (typically $M = 1.5$–$8$). The pressure rise is essentially instantaneous, producing a true shock wave.
- **Boundary:** $M = 1$ (the speed of sound in the unreacted gas, ~340 m/s at NTP for air).

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model Q2]** *(3 marks)*

**Q2.** Define **TNT equivalence** in explosion modeling. Why is an efficiency factor $\eta$ introduced, and what is its typical range for hydrocarbon VCEs?

**Model Answer:**

TNT equivalence converts the chemical energy released by a vapor cloud explosion into an *equivalent mass of TNT*, whose blast characteristics are well documented, allowing reuse of standardized overpressure-distance curves.

The efficiency factor $\eta$ is introduced because:
1. Only a fraction of the cloud's fuel-air mixture is *within the flammable range* (typically 1%–10% inside stoichiometric limits)
2. Combustion is *incomplete* due to turbulence, mixing limitations, and radiation losses
3. Only part of the released energy *couples into the blast wave*

For hydrocarbon VCEs, $\eta$ typically ranges from **0.01 to 0.10**, with $\eta \approx 0.04$ being a frequently used default value.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **Choose either QUESTION A or QUESTION B. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Q-A]** *(14 marks: a-7, b-7)*

**(a)** A 1200 kg cloud of cyclohexane ($\Delta H_c = 43{,}900$ kJ/kg) is involved in a vapor cloud explosion. Assuming an efficiency factor $\eta = 0.05$, compute the equivalent TNT mass and the side-on overpressure at a stand-off distance of 250 m. Use $\Delta H_{TNT} = 4686$ kJ/kg. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Equivalent TNT mass:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{TNT} &= \eta \cdot \frac{W \cdot \Delta H_c}{\Delta H_{TNT}} \\
&= 0.05 \cdot \frac{1200 \times 43900}{4686} \\
&= 0.05 \cdot \frac{52{,}680{,}000}{4686} \\
&= 0.05 \times 11242 \approx 562.1 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Scaled distance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{R} &= \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \frac{250}{(562.1)^{1/3}}
\end{aligned}$$

Cube root of 562.1: $562.1^{1/3} \approx 8.25$ m/kg$^{1/3}$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{250}{8.25} \approx 30.3 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Sachs polynomial:**

$$\bar{P}_s = \frac{0.974}{30.3} + \frac{1.455}{(30.3)^2} + \frac{5.85}{(30.3)^3} - \frac{0.198}{(30.3)^4}$$

$$\bar{P}_s \approx 0.03215 + 0.001585 + 0.000210 - 0.0000002 \approx 0.03395$$

**Step 4 — Side-on overpressure:**

$$P_s = 0.03395 \times 101325 \approx 3440 \text{ Pa} \approx 3.4 \text{ kPa}$$

**Interpretation:** ~3.4 kPa — corresponds to window breakage but well below structural damage (35 kPa threshold).

---

**(b)** Derive the equation for the **Gaussian plume ground-level centerline concentration** of a continuous toxic gas release. State clearly the meaning of each term and the Pasquill-Gifford stability classes. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

For a continuous point source emitting toxic gas at mass rate $Q$ (kg/s) at effective height $H$ (m), in a wind of mean speed $u$ (m/s), the steady-state 3-D Gaussian concentration field is:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z \cdot u} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \left[\exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

**Term meanings:**
- $Q$ — emission rate (kg/s)
- $u$ — mean wind speed at release height (m/s)
- $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ — standard deviations of concentration in the lateral and vertical directions (m)
- $y$ — horizontal crosswind distance (m)
- $z$ — vertical height above ground (m)
- $H$ — effective release height (m)
- The two exponential terms in $z$ model the source and its *image* (reflection at the ground).

**Ground-level centerline** ($y = 0, z = 0$):

$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z \cdot u}$$

**Pasquill-Gifford Stability Classes:**

| Class | Description | Atmospheric Condition |
|---|---|---|
| A | Very Unstable | Strong insolation, low wind |
| B | Unstable | Moderate insolation |
| C | Slightly Unstable | Slight insolation |
| D | Neutral | Overcast, high wind |
| E | Stable | Slight nighttime |
| F | Very Stable | Clear nighttime, low wind |

The dispersion coefficients $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ are tabulated power-law functions of downwind distance $x$ for each stability class. Class A yields the largest $\sigma_z$ (most mixing); Class F yields the smallest (worst-case toxic accumulation at ground level).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model Q-B]** *(14 marks: a-7, b-7)*

**(a)** A road tanker carrying 18,000 kg of propane (LPG) suffers a catastrophic failure and undergoes a BLEVE. Compute the **(i)** fireball diameter, **(ii)** fireball duration, and **(iii)** the incident thermal radiation flux at a distance of 350 m, assuming a Surface Emissive Power SEP = 270 kW/m² and atmospheric transmissivity $\tau = 0.85$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Fireball diameter:**

$$D = 5.8 \cdot M^{1/3} = 5.8 \cdot (18000)^{1/3}$$

Cube root of 18000: $18000^{1/3} \approx 26.21$

$$D = 5.8 \times 26.21 \approx 152.0 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Fireball duration:**

$$t = 0.45 \cdot M^{1/3} = 0.45 \times 26.21 \approx 11.8 \text{ s}$$

**(iii) Incident thermal flux at 350 m:**

View factor (point source approximation, valid for $r \gg D$ — here $r = 350 \gg 152$, acceptable):

$$F_{12} = \frac{D^2}{4 r^2} = \frac{(152)^2}{4 \times (350)^2} = \frac{23104}{490000} \approx 0.04715$$

$$\begin{aligned}
q'' &= \tau \cdot F_{12} \cdot \text{SEP} \\
&= 0.85 \times 0.04715 \times 270 \\
&= 0.85 \times 12.73 \\
&\approx 10.82 \text{ kW/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** At 10.82 kW/m² for 11.8 s, exposure is sufficient to cause **second-degree burns in 100% of exposed personnel** (lethality threshold = 12.5 kW/m² at 10 s). The 350 m exclusion zone is *critically* close to lethality.

---

**(b)** Explain the **TNO Multi-Energy Method** for vapor cloud explosions. How does it differ from the TNT equivalence method, and under what conditions is it preferred? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The **TNO Multi-Energy Method** (van den Berg, 1985) classifies a vapor cloud explosion into one of **10 discrete blast strength categories** (1 = weakest, 10 = strongest), based on the *degree of partial confinement and congestion* of the cloud at the moment of ignition.

**Key features:**

1. **Categorization:** Each region of the cloud with a different level of confinement/obstruction is assigned a category number. Open-air regions typically fall in category 1–2 (essentially no blast), while highly congested process modules reach category 7–10 (near-detonation).

2. **Energy input:** The combustion energy $E$ of each region is used (not TNT-equivalent energy). Scaled blast parameters are then read from TNO's pre-computed charts:
   - Scaled distance: $R' = R / E^{1/3}$
   - Scaled overpressure: $P' = P / P_0$

3. **Blast curves** for each category are derived from hemispherical TNT-charge experiments scaled to a *hemispherical surface burst*, with category-10 representing the theoretical maximum.

**Differences from TNT Equivalence:**

| Aspect | TNT Equivalence | TNO Multi-Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry of source | Single spherical charge | Multiple regions of differing strength |
| Confinement effects | Implicit (via $\eta$ only) | Explicit (via category selection) |
| Overpressure prediction | Tends to overestimate for unconfined clouds | More realistic for real process plants |
| Use case | Screening, simple assessments | Detailed offshore/onshore plant design |
| Input data required | Total mass + $\eta$ | Geometry, layout, congestion maps |

**When to prefer TNO:**
- Process plants with complex geometry (pipe racks, modules, obstacles)
- Offshore platforms
- Refineries and chemical plants with significant confinement
- Any study where the overpressure footprint must be predicted with higher accuracy

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU 2024 Valuation Pitfalls — Explosion and Toxic Gas Release:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** before substitution. The cube root $W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ requires $W$ in **kg** (not tonnes) and $R$ in **m** to get $\bar{R}$ in m/kg$^{1/3}. Many students lose 2 marks by mixing SI prefixes.
>
> 2. **Confusing side-on ($P_s$) and reflected ($P_r$) overpressure.** For people/structures facing the blast, $P_r$ (typically 2–8$\times$ larger than $P_s$) is the relevant value. KTU Part A questions often test this distinction.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the dispersion coefficient formulation** for the wrong stability class. A Class-F (very stable, nighttime) calculation gives a *much smaller* $\sigma_z$ and therefore a *much higher* ground concentration than Class D. Failure to specify the class in the answer = -1 mark.
>
> 4. **Using atmospheric pressure as 10⁵ Pa** instead of 101325 Pa. The 1.3% error propagates to all overpressure calculations. KTU accepts 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa as a simplification, but $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa is the rigorous value.
>
> 5. **Probit miscalculation** — students forget to convert Probit to probability. A Probit of 5 → 50%, not 5%. Use the complementary error function carefully.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the image term in the Gaussian plume** when deriving the ground-level concentration. The two-exponential form (source + image) is the *complete* equation, even though the ground-level centerline simplifies to one exponential.
>
> 7. **Treating BLEVE as fire only.** BLEVE produces BOTH a blast wave AND a fireball — examiners often award marks for mentioning both effects.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Explosion and Toxic Gas Release (Module 3)**

### 🔥 Explosion Modeling

- **Deflagration** = subsonic flame front ($M < 1$); **Detonation** = supersonic ($M > 1$)
- **VCE** is the dominant explosion hazard in process industries
- **BLEVE** = catastrophic vessel failure + intense fireball (both blast AND thermal)
- **TNT Equivalence** formula: $W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot W \cdot \Delta H_c / \Delta H_{TNT}$ with $\eta \approx 0.04$ for hydrocarbons
- **Sachs scaling:** $\bar{R} = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$, then read $\bar{P}_s$ from blast chart
- **TNO Multi-Energy** uses 10 categories based on confinement; preferred for realistic process plant geometry
- **Hopkinson-Cranz cube-root law** is the physical basis of all blast scaling

### ☣️ Toxic Gas Release

- **Discharge rate** through a hole: $Q = C_d A_h P \sqrt{\frac{M \gamma}{R_u T} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)}}$, with $C_d \approx 0.62$
- **Gaussian plume** — ground-level centerline: $C = Q/(\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u)$
- **Pasquill-Gifford** stability classes A–F control $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ growth with downwind distance
- **Worst-case dispersion** is typically Class F (very stable, low wind, nighttime) — minimum $\sigma_z$ → highest ground concentration
- **Heavy gases** (Cl₂, LPG) deviate from Gaussian → use Britter-McQuaid or DEGADIS
- **Probit equations** relate dose to effect probability; Probit = 5 corresponds to 50% probability

### 📊 Damage Thresholds to Memorize

- **Glass breakage:** ~1 kPa
- **Eardrum rupture:** ~16 kPa
- **Lung damage (50%):** ~83 kPa
- **Lethality from overpressure (50%):** ~175 kPa
- **Second-degree burns (10 s):** 4.73 kW/m²
- **Lethality from thermal (50%, 10 s):** 12.5 kW/m²
- **IDLH (chlorine):** 10 ppm
- **AEGL-2 (chlorine, 60 min):** 0.5 ppm

### 🧠 Must-Memorize Formulas

1. $W_{TNT} = \eta W \Delta H_c / \Delta H_{TNT}$
2. $\bar{R} = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$
3. $D_{BLEVE} = 5.8 M^{1/3}$, $t_{BLEVE} = 0.45 M^{1/3}$
4. $C(x,0,0) = Q / (\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u)$
5. $Y = a + b \ln(V)$, $P = 50[1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip:** Always (a) state the **assumptions** (e.g., neutral stability, ground-level release, hemispherical blast), (b) **show the units** at every step, and (c) **interpret the result** in terms of damage thresholds. A correct numerical answer with no interpretation typically earns only 70% of the marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Source Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Source Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOURCE MODELLING** is the first and most critical stage of consequence analysis within Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA). It is defined as the systematic quantitative estimation of the **rate, duration, total quantity, and physical state** of hazardous material released from its primary containment (vessel, pipeline, tank, reactor) following a loss-of-containment (LOC) event.

The output of source modelling is a **Source Term** — a set of numerical descriptors (mass flow rate, total mass released, release duration, temperature, pressure, phase) that becomes the input boundary condition for all downstream dispersion, fire, and explosion calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Terminology (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Loss of Containment (LOC):** The initiating event causing escape of hazardous material.
> - **Source Strength (Q):** The instantaneous or time-averaged mass release rate (kg/s).
> - **Discharge Coefficient (Cd):** A dimensionless empirical factor (typically 0.6 to 0.85) that accounts for vena contracta and frictional losses at the release aperture.
> - **Release Scenario:** A defined combination of hole size, substance phase, and process conditions (T, P).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline in a city underground. Suppose a contractor accidentally hits it with an excavator and punctures a hole. Before the fire department can estimate how much water will flood the street, how deep the flooding will get, and how far it will reach downstream, they must first answer one question: **"How fast is water gushing out of the hole?"**

That is exactly what source modelling does for hazardous materials in a chemical plant. Before we can model the toxic gas cloud drifting over a town, or the vapour cloud explosion that may follow, we must first model **the source** — the broken pipe, the cracked tank, or the ruptured flange. The "hole" is the aperture, the "pipeline pressure" is the driving force, and the "water gush rate" becomes our input to the next analysis stage (dispersion).

**Plain-English Summary:**
> Source modelling is essentially *"flow engineering applied to a broken container."* We use the laws of fluid mechanics (Bernoulli's equation, critical flow theory, two-phase flash equations) to convert physical damage (a hole of certain diameter) into numerical release rates (kg/s), which then feed the rest of the QRA consequence chain.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics Used

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Table for Source Modelling:**
> - **Universal Gas Constant (R) = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Standard Gravity (g) = 9.81 m/s²**
> - **Discharge Coefficient (Cd):** 0.6 ≤ Cd ≤ 0.85 (typical for sharp-edged orifices)
> - **Heat of Vaporization (ΔHv):** Substance-specific (kJ/kg)
> - **Critical Pressure Ratio for gases (Pc/P1) = (2/(k+1))^(k/(k-1))** where k = Cp/Cv (isentropic expansion factor)
> - **Common hole sizes (CCPS Guidelines):** Small = 10 mm, Medium = 50 mm, Large = 100 mm, Catastrophic rupture = full vessel cross-section

## 1.4 Why Source Modelling is the Foundation of QRA

| Property | Role in QRA Pipeline |
|---|---|
| **Release Rate (kg/s)** | Input to Gaussian/Heavy-Gas dispersion models |
| **Total Mass Released (kg)** | Determines toxic dose and flammable inventory |
| **Release Duration (s)** | Decides between instantaneous puff vs continuous plume |
| **Release Phase (gas/liquid/2-phase)** | Governs selection of dispersion model (passive vs dense) |
| **Release Temperature (K)** | Affects buoyancy, flash fraction, and atmospheric density |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps of Source Modelling

Source modelling follows a strict sequential logic, exactly as expected in KTU board examination answers:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Inventory:** Determine the mass (kg) of hazardous material inside the equipment at the time of failure.
- **Step 2 — Define the Release Scenario:** Select the hole size category and the failure mode (e.g., flange gasket leak, full bore rupture, catastrophic vessel collapse).
- **Step 3 — Determine the Driving Force:** Establish the initial pressure (P1) inside the vessel, the back pressure (P2, usually atmospheric), the temperature (T), and the phase of the material at release conditions.
- **Step 4 — Apply the Correct Discharge Equation:** Choose between:
  - Liquid-only release (incompressible flow)
  - Gas/Vapour release (compressible flow, possibly choked)
  - Two-phase flashing release
  - Pool evaporation (post-release)
- **Step 5 — Compute Mass Flow Rate (kg/s):** Apply the chosen formula.
- **Step 6 — Determine Release Duration (t):** Divide total inventory by flow rate, or use a transient (depleting) model.
- **Step 7 — Output the Source Term:** Package (Q, t, total mass, phase) for dispersion analysis.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is hole size so critical?**
> Because the cross-sectional area (A) of the release aperture appears **inside the square root or directly as a multiplier** in every discharge equation. A doubling of hole diameter results in a **4× increase** in release area, dramatically escalating downstream consequences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we distinguish choked vs non-choked flow?**
> Once the upstream-to-downstream pressure ratio (P2/P1) falls below a critical value (called the **critical pressure ratio**), the gas at the orifice throat reaches sonic velocity (Mach = 1). Beyond this, further reduction in downstream pressure does **not** increase the mass flow rate — the flow is said to be "choked" or "sonic." This is a frequent 14-mark KTU question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **authoritative KTU-ready formula compendium** for Source Modelling. These equations are the ones most frequently tested in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

| # | Release Type | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Applicability / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Liquid under gravity head (open tank) | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Q (m³/s), Cd (—), A (m²), g (9.81 m/s²), h (m) | Liquid level h above the orifice |
| 2 | Pressurized liquid discharge | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho_L}$ | ΔP (Pa), ρL (kg/m³) | Sub-cooled or moderately compressed liquid |
| 3 | Subsonic gas discharge (non-choked) | $Q_m = C_d \cdot A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M \cdot k}{R \cdot T \cdot (k-1)} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{2/k} - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right^{(k+1)/k} \right]}$ | Qm (kg/s), P1 (Pa), M (kg/mol), k (—), T (K) | When P2/P1 > (2/(k+1))^(k/(k-1)) |
| 4 | Choked (sonic) gas discharge | $Q_m = C_d \cdot A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot M}{R \cdot T}} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{2}{k+1} \right]^{(k+1)/[2(k-1)]}$ | As above | When P2/P1 ≤ (2/(k+1))^(k/(k-1)) — most KTU problems |
| 5 | Two-phase flashing liquid | $Q_m = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{mix} (P_1 - P_{atm})}$ | ρmix = mixture density (kg/m³) | Liquefied gas under pressure, ambient T > boiling point |
| 6 | Mass flux at critical (choked) condition | $G_c = C_d \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot M}{R \cdot T}} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{2}{k+1} \right]^{(k+1)/[2(k-1)]}$ | Gc (kg/(m²·s)) | Useful for sizing emergency vents |
| 7 | Pool evaporation (mass transfer limited) | $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = M \cdot k_m \cdot A_{pool} \cdot (P_{sat} - P_{atm}) / (R \cdot T)$ | km (m/s) mass transfer coefficient, Psat (Pa) | Spill on land or water, no active heating |
| 8 | Boiling pool evaporation | $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = q / \Delta H_v$ | q (W), ΔHv (J/kg) | Cryogenic spills (LNG, LPG), ground heat flux limited |
| 9 | Total mass released (steady source) | $M_{total} = Q_m \cdot t$ | t (s) | If inventory not depleted during release |
| 10 | Flash fraction (F) | $F = \dfrac{C_p (T_s - T_b)}{\Delta H_v}$ | Ts = storage T, Tb = normal boiling point | Fraction of liquid that instantly vaporises on release |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications of Source Modelling:**
> - **Onshore/Offshore Oil & Gas:** Sizing of relief valves (API 520/521), flare system design, and determining Safe Havens during blowouts (e.g., Deepwater Horizon incident analysis).
> - **Petrochemical Plants:** Consequence analysis of LPG bullet tank ruptures (Vapour Cloud Explosion — VCE — depends entirely on the source term).
> - **Pharmaceutical Industries:** Toxic gas release scenarios (HF, Cl₂) in API manufacturing.
> - **Regulatory Compliance:** Mandatory input for the **CCPS *Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis*** and Indian **OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate)** standards.
> - **Land-use Planning:** Source term dictates the exclusion zone around Seveso (India: MSIHC Rules 1989) classified establishments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Liquid Discharge Through an Orifice (Pressurized)

We begin with the **Mechanical Energy Balance (Bernoulli's Equation)** between an upstream point inside the vessel (point 1) and a downstream point just outside the orifice (point 2).

**Step 1: Write the incompressible Bernoulli equation between points 1 and 2**

$$\dfrac{P_1}{\rho_L} + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho_L} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2} + g z_2 + h_f$$

**Step 2: Apply physical simplifications**

- The vessel is very large compared to the orifice, so $v_1 \approx 0$ (the bulk fluid velocity inside the vessel is negligible).
- For a small orifice on a vertical wall, the elevation difference $(z_1 - z_2)$ is negligible.
- Friction losses across the orifice are lumped into the empirical discharge coefficient $C_d$, so the friction term is absorbed.

After these simplifications:

$$\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_L} = \dfrac{v_2^2}{2}$$

**Step 3: Solve for the ideal (frictionless) exit velocity**

$$v_{2,\text{ideal}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 (P_1 - P_2)}{\rho_L}}$$

**Step 4: Introduce the discharge coefficient**

The actual jet contracts slightly downstream of the orifice (the **vena contracta**), and friction reduces the velocity. Both effects are captured empirically:

$$v_{2,\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 (P_1 - P_2)}{\rho_L}}$$

**Step 5: Convert velocity to volumetric flow rate**

The volumetric flow rate is the velocity multiplied by the orifice area:

$$Q = v_{2,\text{actual}} \cdot A = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 (P_1 - P_2)}{\rho_L}}$$

**Step 6: Convert volumetric to mass flow rate**

Multiply by the liquid density to obtain the mass flow rate, which is the actual quantity used in QRA source terms:

$$Q_m = \rho_L \cdot Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_L (P_1 - P_2)}$$

> **Final boxed result (Pressure-driven liquid):**
>
> $$Q_m = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \, \rho_L \, (P_1 - P_2)} \quad \text{[kg/s]}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Choked (Sonic) Gas Discharge Through an Orifice

This derivation is a **KTU-favourite 14-mark question**. We treat the gas as an **ideal gas** undergoing **adiabatic, reversible (isentropic) expansion**.

**Step 1: Energy equation for compressible flow**

For an ideal gas, the steady-flow energy equation between vessel (1) and throat (2) gives:

$$h_1 + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2}$$

With $v_1 \approx 0$ (large vessel):

$$h_1 - h_2 = \dfrac{v_2^2}{2}$$

For an ideal gas, $h_1 - h_2 = C_p (T_1 - T_2)$. Substituting:

$$v_2 = \sqrt{2 C_p (T_1 - T_2)}$$

**Step 2: Use the isentropic relation between T and P**

For an isentropic process in an ideal gas:

$$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(k-1)/k}$$

Rearranging:

$$T_1 - T_2 = T_1 \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(k-1)/k} \right]$$

**Step 3: Substitute back into velocity equation**

$$v_2 = \sqrt{2 C_p T_1 \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(k-1)/k} \right]}$$

**Step 4: Use the identity $C_p = \dfrac{k R}{M (k-1)}$**

Substituting the ideal-gas specific heat identity:

$$v_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k R T_1}{M (k-1)} \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(k-1)/k} \right]}$$

**Step 5: Find the critical (choked) condition by differentiating**

Mass flow rate at the throat is $\dot{m} = \rho_2 A v_2$, with $\rho_2 = \dfrac{P_2 M}{R T_2}$.

Setting $\dfrac{d \dot{m}}{d P_2} = 0$ (the condition for maximum mass flow) yields the critical pressure ratio:

$$\left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)_{\text{critical}} = \left( \dfrac{2}{k+1} \right)^{k/(k-1)}$$

At this ratio, the throat reaches **Mach 1** (sonic condition).

**Step 6: Substitute the critical pressure ratio into the velocity equation**

After algebraic substitution (a routine exercise), the choked mass flow rate reduces to:

> **Final boxed result (Choked gas discharge):**
>
> $$Q_m = C_d \cdot A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k \, M}{R \, T_1}} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{2}{k+1} \right]^{(k+1)/[2(k-1)]} \quad \text{[kg/s]}$$

**Verification check:** For air ($k = 1.4$, $M = 0.029$ kg/mol) at $P_1 = 10^6$ Pa, $T_1 = 300$ K, and a 50 mm hole, this gives a choked flow of roughly **21.8 kg/s** — a realistic value used in textbook QRA examples.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Flash Fraction for a Two-Phase Release

When a pressurised liquefied gas (e.g., propane, chlorine, ammonia) leaks to the atmosphere, the sudden pressure drop causes a portion of the liquid to **instantly vaporise**. The fraction vaporised is called the **flash fraction F**.

**Step 1: Energy balance on the released liquid**

The sensible heat of the liquid (cooling from storage temperature $T_s$ down to its normal boiling point $T_b$ at atmospheric pressure) is consumed in vaporising a fraction $F$ of the liquid:

$$F \cdot \Delta H_v = C_{p,L} (T_s - T_b)$$

**Step 2: Solve for F**

> **Final boxed result (Flash fraction):**
>
> $$F = \dfrac{C_{p,L} (T_s - T_b)}{\Delta H_v}$$

**Worked Numeric Quick-Check:** For propane stored at $T_s = 298$ K, $T_b = 231$ K, $C_{p,L} = 2.4$ kJ/(kg·K), $\Delta H_v = 425$ kJ/kg:

$$F = \dfrac{2.4 \times (298 - 231)}{425} = \dfrac{160.8}{425} \approx 0.378$$

So about **37.8% of the released propane flashes to vapour instantly** — this is why LPG spills produce massive vapour clouds even from a small leak.

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Problem — KTU Pattern

**Problem (14-mark style):** A 0.10 m diameter hole appears in a vessel containing liquid propane at $P_1 = 9 \times 10^5$ Pa (gauge), $T_1 = 298$ K. The vessel volume is 50 m³. The liquid density of propane at storage conditions is $\rho_L = 493$ kg/m³, and the discharge coefficient is $C_d = 0.65$. Determine the mass flow rate, and classify the release as continuous, instantaneous, or transient if the operator shuts isolation valves after 60 s.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert gauge pressure to absolute:**

$$P_1 = 9 \times 10^5 + 1.013 \times 10^5 = 1.913 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa (absolute)}$$

$$P_2 = 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa (atmospheric)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the orifice area:**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the pressurized liquid discharge equation:**

$$Q_m = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_L (P_1 - P_2)}$$

$$Q_m = 0.65 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{2 \times 493 \times (1.913 \times 10^6 - 1.013 \times 10^5)}$$

$$Q_m = 5.105 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{2 \times 493 \times 1.812 \times 10^6}$$

$$Q_m = 5.105 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{1.787 \times 10^9}$$

$$Q_m = 5.105 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.227 \times 10^4$$

$$Q_m = 215.8 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the total mass released in 60 s (assuming steady flow):**

$$M_{total} = Q_m \times t = 215.8 \times 60 = 12{,}948 \text{ kg} \approx 12.95 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 5 — Classify the release using the CCPS criterion:**

Compare release duration with the **time for the cloud to reach steady state** (typically 30–60 s for dispersion).

Since the release lasts 60 s and is from a continuously pressurized vessel, it is classified as a **TRANSIENT (intermediate) release** — neither a short puff nor a long continuous plume.

> **Answer Box:** $Q_m \approx 216$ kg/s, Total mass released in 60 s $\approx 12.95$ tonnes, Release type = Transient.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Source Modelling Workflow

This block-level functional architecture maps the decision logic that an engineer follows when performing source modelling, and is the **canonical diagram** expected in KTU Part B answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Loss of Containment Event] --> B[Identify Inventory and Substance State]
    B --> C{Phase of Released Material?}
    C -->|Liquid| D[Apply Liquid Discharge Equation]
    C -->|Gas or Vapor| E{P2 over P1 less than Critical Ratio?}
    C -->|Two-Phase| F[Apply Flashing Liquid Equation]
    E -->|Yes| G[Apply Choked Flow Equation]
    E -->|No| H[Apply Subsonic Gas Equation]
    D --> I[Compute Mass Flow Rate Qm in kg per s]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute Total Mass and Duration]
    J --> K{Pool Formation Possible?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Apply Pool Evaporation Model]
    K -->|No| M[Output Source Term to Dispersion Model]
    L --> M
    M --> N[End: Source Term Complete]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Selecting the Correct Discharge Equation

This sub-diagram explicitly shows the **branching logic** that determines which of the four primary source equations should be used.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Choose Equation] --> S1{Is material a pressurized liquid?}
    S1 -->|Yes, under gravity head| S2[Use Qm equals Cd A root 2 g h]
    S1 -->|Yes, pressurized| S3[Use Qm equals Cd A root 2 rhoL dP]
    S1 -->|No, it is a gas| S4{Compute Critical Ratio r equals 2 over k plus 1 to the power k over k minus 1}
    S4 -->|P2 over P1 less than r| S5[Choked: Use Sonic Flow Equation]
    S4 -->|P2 over P1 greater than r| S6[Subsonic: Use Non-Choked Gas Equation]
    S2 --> SX[End: Equation Selected]
    S3 --> SX
    S5 --> SX
    S6 --> SX
```

## 4.3 Release Classification Schematic

KTU frequently tests the classification of releases as **instantaneous, continuous, or transient** based on duration relative to dispersion time scales.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Release Duration t seconds] --> R1{t less than 30 seconds}
    R1 -->|Yes| R2[INSTANTANEOUS PUFF MODEL]
    R1 -->|No| R3{Is inventory fully depleted?}
    R3 -->|Yes, and t greater than 600 s| R4[CONTINUOUS PLUME MODEL]
    R3 -->|No, finite t between 30 and 600| R5[TRANSIENT SLUG MODEL]
    R2 --> RD[Downstream: Heavy Gas or Passive Dispersion]
    R4 --> RD
    R5 --> RD
```

## 4.4 Source Term Block Functional Architecture

This diagram maps how the source term data packet is assembled and handed over to the dispersion, fire, and explosion sub-models of consequence analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Inventory Database: Substance, Mass, T, P] --> ST[Source Term Builder]
    HF[Hole Size Matrix: 10 mm, 50 mm, 100 mm, Rupture] --> ST
    EQ[Discharge Equations Library] --> ST
    ST --> ST1[Qm: Mass Flow Rate]
    ST --> ST2[M total: Total Mass Released]
    ST --> ST3[t: Release Duration]
    ST --> ST4[Phase: Gas, Liquid, Two-Phase]
    ST --> ST5[T release: Temperature after Flash]
    ST1 --> OUT[Source Term Packet]
    ST2 --> OUT
    ST3 --> OUT
    ST4 --> OUT
    ST5 --> OUT
    OUT --> DM1[Input to Dispersion Model]
    OUT --> DM2[Input to Fire Model Pool Fire, Jet Fire, Flash Fire]
    OUT --> DM3[Input to Explosion Model VCE, BLEVE]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "source term" as used in Quantitative Risk Assessment. List any four components of a complete source term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *source term* is the quantitative description of the material released from containment following a loss-of-containment event, expressed in the form of parameters that serve as inputs to dispersion, fire, and explosion models. The four essential components of a source term are:
1. **Mass release rate (Qm)** in kg/s,
2. **Total mass released (Mtotal)** in kg,
3. **Release duration (t)** in seconds,
4. **Physical state (phase) of the released material** — gas, liquid, or two-phase.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four components — 0.5 each.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is meant by "choked flow"? Under what condition does it occur in gas discharge?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Choked flow is the condition in compressible gas discharge where the gas velocity at the orifice throat reaches the **local speed of sound (Mach 1)**, and the mass flow rate becomes **independent of any further decrease in downstream (back) pressure**.

It occurs when the pressure ratio satisfies:

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \le \left( \dfrac{2}{k+1} \right)^{k/(k-1)}$$

For air ($k = 1.4$), this critical ratio is **0.528**; for propane ($k = 1.13$), it is approximately **0.577**.

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for concept, 1 mark for the critical ratio condition with the numerical value.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **mass flow rate of a liquid discharging through a sharp-edged orifice from a pressurized vessel**, clearly stating all assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A pressurized ammonia storage vessel contains 5000 kg of liquid ammonia at $T = 293$ K and $P_1 = 8 \times 10^5$ Pa (gauge). A 25 mm diameter hole develops at the bottom. Given $C_d = 0.62$, $\rho_L = 610$ kg/m³, and atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa, calculate the mass flow rate of ammonia and the time taken to release 50% of the inventory. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The discharge is treated as **incompressible, steady, and isothermal**. Apply the energy equation between the bulk liquid (point 1) and the vena contracta (point 2):

$$\dfrac{P_1}{\rho_L} + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho_L} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2} + g z_2 + h_f$$

**Assumptions** (1 mark):
- Steady, incompressible flow.
- $v_1 \approx 0$ (vessel is large relative to orifice).
- Elevation change $(z_1 - z_2)$ is negligible for a small hole on a side wall.
- Friction is lumped into the discharge coefficient $C_d$.

These simplifications yield (1 mark):

$$\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_L} = \dfrac{v_2^2}{2}$$

Solving for the ideal exit velocity (1 mark):

$$v_{2,\text{ideal}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho_L}}$$

Introducing the discharge coefficient (1 mark):

$$v_{2,\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho_L}}$$

Converting to volumetric flow (1 mark):

$$Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho_L}}$$

Converting to mass flow rate (1 mark):

$$\boxed{Q_m = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_L (P_1 - P_2)}}$$

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Convert pressure to absolute:

$$P_1 = 8 \times 10^5 + 1.013 \times 10^5 = 1.813 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Compute orifice area:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $\Delta P$:

$$\Delta P = 1.813 \times 10^6 - 1.013 \times 10^5 = 1.7117 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the discharge equation:

$$Q_m = 0.62 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2 \times 610 \times 1.7117 \times 10^6}$$

$$Q_m = 3.044 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2.088 \times 10^9}$$

$$Q_m = 3.044 \times 10^{-4} \times 4.570 \times 10^4$$

$$\boxed{Q_m \approx 13.91 \text{ kg/s}}$$

**Step 5:** Mass corresponding to 50% of inventory:

$$M_{50\%} = 0.5 \times 5000 = 2500 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6:** Time required:

$$t = \dfrac{M_{50\%}}{Q_m} = \dfrac{2500}{13.91}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 179.7 \text{ s} \approx 3 \text{ minutes}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> **Common mistakes students make in this question type:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert gauge pressure to absolute** before applying the discharge equation. A wrong P1 gives a wrong Qm and cascades into a wrong time. (Loss: up to 2 marks)
> 2. **Using diameter squared instead of radius squared** in the area formula: $A = \pi d^2 / 4$, not $A = \pi d^2$. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 3. **Omitting assumptions in the derivation** — KTU strictly requires 3 to 4 assumptions in the derivation. (Loss: 1 to 2 marks)
> 4. **Mixing up volumetric Q and mass Qm** in the final answer box. Always state units.
> 5. **Forgetting to multiply by density in the final step** of the derivation — examiners expect the final equation in mass flow form.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **step-by-step procedure for source modelling** in QRA. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 0.05 m diameter hole develops in a pipeline carrying methane at $P_1 = 6 \times 10^5$ Pa (gauge) and $T_1 = 300$ K. Calculate the mass release rate. Given: $M = 0.016$ kg/mol, $k = 1.31$, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $C_d = 0.75$. Also state whether the flow is choked or subsonic. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The source modelling procedure is a sequence of seven logical steps (1 mark each, with 1 mark reserved for the schematic):

1. **Inventory Identification** — Determine the mass of hazardous material present in the equipment at the moment of failure.
2. **Release Scenario Definition** — Categorize the failure: small leak (10 mm), medium leak (50 mm), large leak (100 mm), or catastrophic rupture (full bore).
3. **Driving Force Assessment** — Record P1, P2, T1, and the material phase (gas/liquid/2-phase).
4. **Discharge Equation Selection** — Use the decision tree (Section 4.2) to pick the correct equation.
5. **Mass Flow Rate Calculation** — Apply the selected equation to obtain Qm in kg/s.
6. **Release Duration Determination** — $t = M_{inventory} / Q_m$ for steady release, or use a transient depletion model.
7. **Source Term Output** — Package (Qm, Mtotal, t, phase, T) and forward to dispersion module.

The schematic should be a numbered flowchart or block diagram showing these seven steps in sequence.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Convert to absolute pressure:

$$P_1 = 6 \times 10^5 + 1.013 \times 10^5 = 7.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Compute orifice area:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the pressure ratio:

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{1.013 \times 10^5}{7.013 \times 10^5} = 0.1444$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the critical pressure ratio:

$$\left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)_{crit} = \left( \dfrac{2}{k+1} \right)^{k/(k-1)} = \left( \dfrac{2}{2.31} \right)^{1.31/0.31}$$

$$= (0.8658)^{4.226} = 0.5466$$

**Step 5:** Compare:

Since $0.1444 < 0.5466$, the flow is **CHOKED**. (1 mark for correct identification)

**Step 6:** Apply the choked flow equation:

$$Q_m = C_d \cdot A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot M}{R \cdot T_1}} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{2}{k+1} \right]^{(k+1)/[2(k-1)]}$$

Compute the bracket factor:

$$\left[ \dfrac{2}{2.31} \right]^{2.31/[2 \times 0.31]} = (0.8658)^{3.726} = 0.6369$$

Compute the square root term:

$$\sqrt{\dfrac{1.31 \times 0.016}{8.314 \times 300}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.02096}{2494.2}} = \sqrt{8.404 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.899 \times 10^{-3}$$

Substitute all values:

$$Q_m = 0.75 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.013 \times 10^5 \times 2.899 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6369$$

$$Q_m = 0.75 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.013 \times 10^5 = 1032.4 \text{ (intermediate)}$$

$$Q_m = 1032.4 \times 2.899 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6369$$

$$\boxed{Q_m \approx 1.91 \text{ kg/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (for Question B):**
> 1. **Failing to identify choked vs subsonic** before plugging into a formula is the most common mistake. (Loss: 1 to 2 marks)
> 2. **Arithmetic error in the exponent** $(k+1)/[2(k-1)]$ — verify with $k = 1.31$: exponent = $2.31 / 0.62 = 3.726$, not 4.226. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 3. **Wrong use of $k$ value** — for methane, $k = 1.31$ (NOT 1.4 which is for air). Examiners test this specifically.
> 4. **Omitting the schematic** in part (a). The question explicitly says *"with the help of a neat schematic"* — without it, expect a 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — always state $P_1$ in Pa (not bar) in the final substitution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Source Modelling (PECHT754, Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Source modelling quantifies the release (rate, mass, duration, phase) of hazardous material after a loss-of-containment event. The output is the **Source Term**.

- **Source Term Components (4):** Mass flow rate Qm (kg/s), Total mass Mtotal (kg), Release duration t (s), Physical phase.

- **Four Discharge Equation Families:** (1) Liquid under gravity, (2) Pressurized liquid, (3) Subsonic gas, (4) Choked (sonic) gas.

- **Liquid (pressure-driven):** $Q_m = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_L (P_1 - P_2)}$ — use **absolute** pressures.

- **Gas (choked condition — most common in KTU):** $Q_m = C_d \cdot A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k M}{R T_1}} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{2}{k+1} \right]^{(k+1)/[2(k-1)]}$

- **Critical Pressure Ratio:** $r_c = \left( \dfrac{2}{k+1} \right)^{k/(k-1)}$. For air: 0.528, propane: 0.577, methane: 0.547. If $P_2/P_1 < r_c$ → **choked**.

- **Discharge Coefficient Cd:** Typical range 0.60 – 0.85; sharp-edged orifice ≈ 0.62; rounded orifice ≈ 0.95.

- **Two-Phase Flashing Liquid:** Flash fraction $F = C_{p,L}(T_s - T_b)/\Delta H_v$. Determines the vapour-cloud source strength for LPG/LNG spills.

- **Pool Evaporation:** Mass-transfer limited $\dot{m} = M k_m A (P_{sat} - P_{atm}) / RT$. Boiling pools: $\dot{m} = q/\Delta H_v$ (ground heat flux driven).

- **Release Classification (CCPS):** Instantaneous (puff) if t < 30 s, continuous (plume) if t > 600 s, transient (slug) for 30 s < t < 600 s.

- **Standard Hole Sizes for KTU Problems:** Small = 10 mm, Medium = 50 mm, Large = 100 mm, Catastrophic = full cross-section.

- **Constants to Memorise:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $g = 9.81$ m/s², $P_{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa.

- **Units Watch:** Always convert gauge → absolute, mm → m, °C → K, m³ → kg (multiply by density).

- **Flowchart to Remember:** Inventory → Scenario → Phase → (Equation) → Qm → t → Mtotal → Source Term → Dispersion.

- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Using the wrong $k$ value (air = 1.4, methane = 1.31, propane = 1.13). Examiners deliberately test this.

- **Industrial Reference Standard:** **CCPS *Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis* (2nd Edition)** — the international benchmark for source modelling in QRA.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models for Fire - Pool Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITUM RISK ASSESSMENT (Corrected: QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Models for Fire - Pool Fire

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pool Fire** is a **diffusion flame combustion** process in which the fuel is supplied by the vaporization of a **liquid pool** (typically a hydrocarbon released from a ruptured vessel, pipeline, or storage tank). The vapor mixes with ambient oxidizer (air) above the pool and burns in a turbulent, buoyant, often non-premixed flame structure, releasing energy in the form of **convective** and **radiative** heat.

In the KTU QRA framework (Module 3 — *Consequence Analysis of Fire & Explosion*), a pool fire is mathematically abstracted as a **cylindrical/circular source** of defined geometry, where the heat output is governed by three core sub-models:

1. **Liquid Burning Rate Model** — predicts the regression (downward burning) velocity of the pool.
2. **Flame Geometry Model** — predicts flame height, tilt, and drag-induced deformation.
3. **Radiative Heat Transfer Model** — predicts the **surface emissive power (SEP)** and the **view factor** $F_{12}$ from the flame to a receptor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Campfire on a Puddle of Petrol:**
> Imagine you have spilled petrol on a flat concrete floor and set it alight. The fire does not need a wick or a nozzle; the **heat of the flame itself** vaporizes the liquid below it. The faster the flame burns, the more fuel is vaporized, sustaining itself in a *self-feeding loop*. Wind tilts the flame, a larger pool means a *taller* fire, and the radiant heat you feel on your face is what could harm a vessel, person, or neighbouring equipment.

This self-feeding mechanism, the **wind-induced tilt**, and the **directional heat radiation** are the three real-world phenomena every KTU pool fire equation tries to capture.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | $5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; \mathrm{W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-4}}$ | Governs all radiative heat flux |
| Ambient temperature (standard) | $T_\infty$ | $298 \; \mathrm{K}$ | Reference for buoyancy-driven flows |
| Stoichiometric air requirement | — | $\approx 11.5 \; \mathrm{kg \; air/kg \; fuel}$ (typical for LPG) | Used in sooting fraction calculation |
| Typical Pool Fire SEP | $\mathrm{SEP}$ | $20\text{–}150 \; \mathrm{kW/m^2}$ | Critical input for thermal damage |
| Pool aspect ratio threshold | $D/H$ | $> 5$ | Criterion to treat fire as "pool-like" |

## 1.4 Why Pool Fires Matter in QRA

Pool fires dominate the **fire consequence** branch of QRA because:

- They are the **most frequent** major-accident fire scenario in hydrocarbon processing (≈ 60 % of refinery fires).
- They have **predictable geometry** that allows deterministic modeling.
- Their **radiative footprint** can ignite adjacent vessels, leading to **domino escalation** — the most catastrophic multi-unit failure mode.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Sub-Models of Pool Fire Analysis

### 2.1.1 Liquid Mass Burning Rate Model (Burning Velocity)

The burning rate is **NOT a constant** — it depends on pool diameter due to **conductive heat losses at the pool rim** and **re-radiation from the flame**.

**Burgess–Hertzberg / Babrauskas Correlation (The KTU Master Correlation):**

$$\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \left(1 - e^{-k \beta D}\right)$$

Where:

- $\dot{m}''$ = mass burning rate per unit area $\left(\mathrm{kg \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}}\right)$
- $\dot{m}''_{\infty}$ = asymptotic (large-diameter) burning rate $\left(\mathrm{kg \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}}\right)$
- $k$ = empirical optical thickness / absorption coefficient $\left(\mathrm{m^{-1}}\right)$
- $\beta$ = mean beam length corrector $\left(\mathrm{m^{-1}}\right)$
- $D$ = effective pool diameter (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this exponential form?** For very small pools ($D \to 0$), heat is lost to the rim faster than it is generated, so $\dot{m}'' \to 0$. For large pools, rim losses are negligible, so $\dot{m}'' \to \dot{m}''_{\infty}$ (a constant, fuel-specific value).

**Limiting Cases:**

- **Small pool** ($k\beta D \ll 1$): $\dot{m}'' \approx \dot{m}''_{\infty} \cdot k\beta D$ — linearly rising.
- **Large pool** ($k\beta D \gg 1$): $\dot{m}'' \approx \dot{m}''_{\infty}$ — saturated.

### 2.1.2 Flame Height & Tilt Models

Flames tilt and shorten under **cross-wind** because momentum drag bends the buoyant plume. Two correlations are KTU-high-yield:

**Thomas (1963) — Vertical flame (no wind):**

$$\frac{H}{D} = 42 \left( \frac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_a \sqrt{gD}} \right)^{0.61}$$

**Moorhouse (1982) — Inclined flame under wind:**

$$\frac{H}{D} \cdot \cos\theta = 42 \left( \frac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_a \sqrt{gD}} \right)^{0.61}$$

Where:

- $H$ = flame height (m)
- $\theta$ = flame tilt angle from vertical (degrees)
- $\rho_a$ = ambient air density (≈ 1.2 kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)

### 2.1.3 Radiative Heat Flux Model

The thermal radiation incident on a target (receptor) at distance $r$ from the flame centre:

$$q'' = \tau \cdot F_{12} \cdot \mathrm{SEP}$$

Where:

- $q''$ = incident heat flux on target $\left(\mathrm{kW/m^2}\right)$
- $\tau$ = atmospheric transmissivity (humidity-dependent, typically 0.7–1.0 at short range)
- $F_{12}$ = view factor (geometric, dimensionless, $0 \le F_{12} \le 1$)
- $\mathrm{SEP}$ = Surface Emissive Power $\left(\mathrm{kW/m^2}\right)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark-Winning Insight:** Always keep the units consistent. SEP is typically in $\mathrm{kW/m^2}$, while $\sigma$ uses $\mathrm{W/m^2}$. Converting mid-calculation is the most common error.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **complete, exam-ready formula bank** for any KTU pool fire numerical. Every symbol, every unit, every boundary condition is consolidated here.

| # | Equation | Application | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty}(1-e^{-k\beta D})$ | Burning rate vs pool diameter | Valid $0.05 \le D \le 50$ m |
| 2 | $\dot{m}''_{\infty}$ (fuel-specific) | Asymptotic rate | LNG: 0.078, LPG: 0.099, Gasoline: 0.055, Kerosene: 0.039, Methanol: 0.015 (kg/m²/s) |
| 3 | $\frac{H}{D} = 42\left(\frac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_a \sqrt{gD}}\right)^{0.61}$ | Thomas flame height | Windless, vertical |
| 4 | $H\cos\theta = 42D\left(\frac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_a\sqrt{gD}}\right)^{0.61}$ | Moorhouse tilted flame | Includes wind effect |
| 5 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot A_p \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi_r$ | Total heat release | $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion (J/kg), $\chi_r$ = radiative fraction (0.10–0.40) |
| 6 | $\dot{Q}_R = \dot{Q} \cdot \chi_r$ | Radiative power | Pool fires: $\chi_r \approx 0.20$–$0.35$ |
| 7 | $\mathrm{SEP} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_R}{A_f}$ | Surface emissive power | $A_f$ = flame surface area (m²) |
| 8 | $F_{12}$ (vertical target, $\theta$ = tilt) | View factor | Geometric, $0 \le F_{12} \le 1$ |
| 9 | $q'' = \tau \cdot F_{12} \cdot \mathrm{SEP}$ | Incident heat flux | $\tau$ = atmospheric transmissivity |
| 10 | $a = 2L \vert 2r \rightarrow$ cylindrical geometry | $L$ = flame length, $r$ = pool radius | Used in Shokri–Beyler model |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Pool Fire Model |
|---|---|
| **Process Plant Layout** | Spacing of vessels to prevent thermal domino effect (typically require $q'' \le 37.5 \; \mathrm{kW/m^2}$ at the boundary) |
| **Offshore QRA** | Predicting failure of ESDVs, firewalls, and living-quarter blast walls |
| **Insurance & ALARP** | Estimating Maximum Foreseeable Loss (MFL) for hydrocarbon storage |
| **Emergency Planning** | Locating assembly points outside the 4 kW/m² (pain threshold) contour |
| **Fire Protection Design** | Sizing of water deluge, foam pour rates, and radiant-heat shielding |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Why the Burning Rate is Diameter-Dependent

### Step 1: Energy balance on the liquid surface

A thin layer of liquid vaporizes per unit time. The energy required to vaporize is supplied by:
- (a) Conduction/convection from the flame back to the surface, and
- (b) Radiative heat re-deposited on the pool.

Write the steady-state energy balance per unit pool area:

$$\dot{m}'' \cdot \Delta H_v = q''_{\text{flame} \to \text{pool}} - q''_{\text{re-radiation}}$$

Where $\Delta H_v$ is the latent heat of vaporization.

### Step 2: Geometric heat-loss argument

For a small pool of diameter $D$, the **rim (perimeter) heat loss** scales as $\propto D$, while the **vaporizing area** scales as $\propto D^2$. Therefore, fractional heat loss $\propto 1/D$.

### Step 3: Empirical synthesis — Burgess, Hertzberg, and Babrauskas

By fitting to experimental data for many fuels, the fractional loss follows an **exponential decay** with diameter:

$$\text{Heat retained fraction} = 1 - e^{-k\beta D}$$

Multiplying by the asymptotic (large-pool) burning rate gives the master correlation:

$$\boxed{\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right)}$$

### Step 4: Physical interpretation of the constant $k\beta$

- $k$ = effective radiative absorption coefficient of the flame (m⁻¹).
- $\beta$ = mean-beam-length geometric factor of the flame.
- $k\beta D$ is a **dimensionless optical thickness** of the flame column.

> [!NOTE]
> **In small pools**, the flame is *optically thin* ($k\beta D$ small), so most of the heat escapes — burning rate is low. **In large pools**, the flame is *optically thick*, so heat is re-absorbed — burning saturates at $\dot{m}''_{\infty}$.

## 3.2 Derivation — Thomas Flame Height Correlation

### Step 1: Dimensionless groups

The flame height must depend on:
- Buoyancy: $g$ (m/s²)
- Momentum: $\dot{m}''$ (kg/m²/s)
- Diameter: $D$ (m)
- Air density: $\rho_a$ (kg/m³)

Dimensional analysis using the **Buckingham π theorem** yields one independent dimensionless group:

$$\Pi = \frac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_a \sqrt{gD}}$$

### Step 2: Power-law fit to experiments

Thomas fitted this to many pool fire experiments and obtained:

$$\frac{H}{D} = C \cdot \Pi^{n}$$

With empirical constants $C = 42$ and $n = 0.61$:

$$\frac{H}{D} = 42 \left( \frac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_a \sqrt{gD}} \right)^{0.61}$$

### Step 3: Consistency check — units

Inside the bracket: $[\dot{m}''] = \mathrm{kg \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}}$, $[\rho_a \sqrt{gD}] = \mathrm{kg \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}}$. Dimensionless ✓.

## 3.3 Full Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU Module 3 Pattern):**
> A circular pool of gasoline (effective pool diameter $D = 20 \; \mathrm{m}$) burns in open air. Given:
> - $\dot{m}''_{\infty} = 0.055 \; \mathrm{kg/m^2/s}$
> - $k\beta = 0.7 \; \mathrm{m^{-1}}$
> - $\Delta H_c = 43.7 \; \mathrm{MJ/kg}$
> - $\chi_r$ (radiative fraction) = 0.20
> - $\rho_a = 1.2 \; \mathrm{kg/m^3}$, $g = 9.81 \; \mathrm{m/s^2}$
>
> **Compute:** (i) burning rate, (ii) flame height, (iii) total heat release rate, (iv) radiative power, (v) SEP if the flame is approximated as a cylinder of diameter equal to pool diameter and height = calculated flame height.

---

### Solution — Step (i) Burning Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}'' &= \dot{m}''_{\infty} \left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right) \\
&= 0.055 \times \left(1 - e^{-0.7 \times 20}\right) \\
&= 0.055 \times \left(1 - e^{-14}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, $e^{-14} \approx 8.32 \times 10^{-7}$, which is **negligible**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}'' &\approx 0.055 \times (1 - 0) \\
\dot{m}'' &\approx 0.055 \; \mathrm{kg \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Physical conclusion:** A 20 m gasoline pool is well within the "large-diameter" regime; the burning rate has *saturated*.

---

### Solution — Step (ii) Flame Height (Thomas Correlation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{H}{D} &= 42 \left( \frac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_a \sqrt{gD}} \right)^{0.61} \\
&= 42 \left( \frac{0.055}{1.2 \times \sqrt{9.81 \times 20}} \right)^{0.61}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the denominator: $\sqrt{9.81 \times 20} = \sqrt{196.2} \approx 14.01$

So denominator $= 1.2 \times 14.01 \approx 16.81 \; \mathrm{kg \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{0.055}{16.81} \approx 3.27 \times 10^{-3} \\
\frac{H}{D} &= 42 \times (3.27 \times 10^{-3})^{0.61}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the power: $(3.27 \times 10^{-3})^{0.61}$. Take $\ln$: $0.61 \times \ln(3.27 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.61 \times (-5.722) = -3.490$. Then $e^{-3.490} \approx 0.0305$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{H}{D} &= 42 \times 0.0305 \approx 1.282 \\
H &= 1.282 \times 20 = 25.6 \; \mathrm{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Physical conclusion:** A 20 m gasoline pool produces a flame ≈ 25.6 m tall — a **massive, optically thick, highly radiant** fire.

---

### Solution — Step (iii) Total Heat Release Rate

Pool area: $A_p = \pi D^2 / 4 = \pi (20)^2 / 4 = 100\pi \approx 314.16 \; \mathrm{m^2}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q} &= \dot{m}'' \cdot A_p \cdot \Delta H_c \\
&= 0.055 \times 314.16 \times 43.7 \times 10^{6} \\
\dot{Q} &\approx 7.55 \times 10^{8} \; \mathrm{W} = 755 \; \mathrm{MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Sanity check:** 755 MW is comparable to a medium-sized power station — physically realistic for a large hydrocarbon pool fire.

---

### Solution — Step (iv) Radiative Power

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_R &= \dot{Q} \times \chi_r \\
&= 755 \times 0.20 \\
\dot{Q}_R &= 151 \; \mathrm{MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Solution — Step (v) Surface Emissive Power (SEP)

Flame is modelled as a cylinder: $A_f = \pi D H = \pi \times 20 \times 25.6 \approx 1608.5 \; \mathrm{m^2}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{SEP} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_R}{A_f} \\
&= \frac{151 \times 10^{6}}{1608.5} \\
\mathrm{SEP} &\approx 93{,}880 \; \mathrm{W/m^2} \approx 93.9 \; \mathrm{kW/m^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Cross-check with the literature:** SEP for gasoline pools is typically 50–100 kW/m². Our computed 93.9 kW/m² sits **squarely in the valid range** ✓.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Model to Use?

> [!NOTE]
> Use this **decision matrix** in the KTU exam to pick the correct sub-model and avoid model-mismatch penalties.

| Scenario | Use This Model | Avoid |
|---|---|---|
| Small pool, no wind | Point source / Thomas | Shokri–Beyler |
| Large pool, still air | Shokri–Beyler / cylindrical flame | Point source |
| Large pool, cross-wind | Moorhouse (tilted) | Vertical Thomas |
| Spill on bund, no drainage | Constant-area steady burning | Time-varying mass loss |
| Cryogenic spill (LNG) | Boil-off + convective heating | Generic hydrocarbon correlation |
| Sub-sea release | Submerged plume model | Surface pool correlation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Pool Fire Modeling

The diagram below maps the **computational flow** of a complete pool fire consequence analysis, showing how the three sub-models interlock. It is the standard architecture implemented in commercial QRA software (PHAST, FRED, EFFECTS).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pool Fire Scenario] --> B[Pool Geometry]
    B --> C[Effective Diameter D]
    C --> D[Burning Rate Sub-Model]
    D --> E[Mass Burning Rate m_dot_pp]
    C --> F[Flame Geometry Sub-Model]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Flame Height H and Tilt Theta]
    G --> H[Radiative Sub-Model]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Radiative Power Q_R]
    H --> J[Surface Emissive Power SEP]
    H --> K[View Factor F_12]
    K --> L[Incident Heat Flux q_pp]
    J --> L
    L --> M[Damage Criteria Evaluation]
    M --> N[Thermal Dose Calculation]
    N --> O[Consequence Outcome]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **Pool Geometry** block sets the fundamental input $D$.
- The **Burning Rate** and **Flame Geometry** blocks run in **parallel** (they both need $D$ but not each other).
- The **Radiative Sub-Model** is the *integrator* — it requires the outputs of both upstream blocks.
- The final **Damage Criteria** block converts heat flux into physical outcomes (skin burn, equipment failure, escalation).

## 4.2 Heat-Flux Damage Thresholds — Reference Architecture

This diagram shows the **decision tree** for translating the computed $q''$ into failure outcomes, which is the **terminal step** of every pool fire calculation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Computed q_pp in kW per m2] --> B{Compare with threshold}
    B --> C[1.0 kW per m2: Solar radiation baseline]
    B --> D[4.0 kW per m2: Pain threshold 20 s exposure]
    B --> E[12.5 kW per m2: Wood piloted ignition]
    B --> F[25 to 37 kW per m2: Process equipment damage]
    B --> G[37.5 kW per m2: Domino escalation boundary]
    B --> H[100 kW per m2: Concrete spalling]
    C --> I[No damage]
    D --> J[Personnel evacuation required]
    E --> K[Bundle storage fire risk]
    F --> L[Asset loss risk]
    G --> M[Vessel spacing check]
    H --> N[Structural integrity review]
```

## 4.3 Pool Fire — Input / Output Functional Map

The following **functional block diagram** shows what each sub-model *consumes* (input) and *produces* (output). This is the single most useful schematic to memorize for KTU theory questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A1[Burning Rate Sub-Model]
        A1in1[D, fuel type, k_beta] --> A1core[Burgess Hertzberg Correlation]
        A1core --> A1out[m_dot_pp in kg per m2 s]
    end

    subgraph A2[Flame Geometry Sub-Model]
        A2in1[D, m_dot_pp, rho_a, g, U_wind] --> A2core[Thomas or Moorhouse]
        A2core --> A2out[H, Theta]
    end

    subgraph A3[Radiative Heat Transfer Sub-Model]
        A3in1[H, Theta, Q_total, chi_r, target geometry] --> A3core[Shokri Beyler plus View Factor]
        A3core --> A3out[q_pp to receptor]
    end

    A1out --> A2in1
    A1out --> A3in1
    A2out --> A3in1
```

**Key reading note:** Notice the **information cascade** — A1 feeds both A2 and A3, and A2 also feeds A3. This is why A1 (burning rate) is solved **first** in any manual or software computation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Pattern: Definition, list, or short derivation. KTU expects **two to four crisp sentences** with **one equation** where relevant.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q3(a)]

**Define a "pool fire" and list the THREE primary sub-models required for its complete QRA consequence analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

A **pool fire** is the combustion of the vapour evaporated from a **liquid fuel pool** (typically formed by accidental spillage of a hydrocarbon). The flame sits above the pool and sustains itself by re-radiating heat back to the liquid surface.

The three sub-models are:

1. **Liquid burning rate model** — predicts $\dot{m}''$ (regression velocity × fuel density).
2. **Flame geometry model** — predicts flame height $H$ and tilt angle $\theta$ (Thomas, Moorhouse).
3. **Radiative heat transfer model** — predicts the incident heat flux $q''$ on a target (Shokri–Beyler + view factor).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing & brief justification of the 3 sub-models.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q3(b)]

**State and explain the significance of the Babrauskas burning-rate correlation:**

$$\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right)$$

**Model Answer:**

The correlation expresses the **mass burning rate per unit pool area** $\dot{m}''$ as a function of pool diameter $D$.

- $\dot{m}''_{\infty}$: asymptotic burning rate for an infinitely large pool (fuel-specific).
- $k\beta$: lumped optical-thickness constant representing flame re-radiation and rim losses.

**Significance:**

- For **small pools** ($k\beta D \ll 1$): $\dot{m}'' \propto D$ — burning rate grows linearly.
- For **large pools** ($k\beta D \gg 1$): $\dot{m}'' \to \dot{m}''_{\infty}$ — saturates.
- It captures the **physically observed plateau** of $\dot{m}''$ for large hydrocarbon spills, which earlier linear models failed to predict.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the two limiting cases, 1 mark for physical significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has TWO sub-parts, usually 7 marks each. **Internal choice** means you must answer EITHER "Question A" OR "Question B" in full. The model solutions below are complete and self-contained.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Burning Rate and Flame Geometry*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**A circular pool of kerosene has an effective diameter of 12 m. Use the Babrauskas correlation with $\dot{m}''_{\infty} = 0.039 \; \mathrm{kg/m^2/s}$ and $k\beta = 0.7 \; \mathrm{m^{-1}}$. Compute (i) the burning rate, (ii) the total mass burning rate $\dot{m}$, and (iii) the total heat release rate $\dot{Q}$. Use $\Delta H_c = 43 \; \mathrm{MJ/kg}$ for kerosene.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Burning rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}'' &= \dot{m}''_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right) \\
&= 0.039 \times \left(1 - e^{-0.7 \times 12}\right) \\
&= 0.039 \times \left(1 - e^{-8.4}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Now $e^{-8.4} \approx 2.25 \times 10^{-4}$, so:

$$
\dot{m}'' = 0.039 \times (1 - 0.000225) \approx 0.03899 \; \mathrm{kg \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}}
$$

**Step 2 — Total mass burning rate:**

Pool area: $A_p = \pi (12)^2 / 4 = 36\pi \approx 113.1 \; \mathrm{m^2}$

$$
\dot{m} = \dot{m}'' \times A_p = 0.03899 \times 113.1 \approx 4.41 \; \mathrm{kg/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Total heat release rate:**

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \times \Delta H_c = 4.41 \times 43 \times 10^6 = 1.896 \times 10^8 \; \mathrm{W} \approx 189.6 \; \mathrm{MW}
$$

> **Final Answer (a):** $\dot{m}'' \approx 0.039 \; \mathrm{kg/m^2/s}$; $\dot{m} \approx 4.41 \; \mathrm{kg/s}$; $\dot{Q} \approx 189.6 \; \mathrm{MW}$. **(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**For the kerosene pool of part (a), compute the flame height using the Thomas correlation. Use $\rho_a = 1.2 \; \mathrm{kg/m^3}$. Comment on whether this flame qualifies as "optically thick".**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the dimensionless group:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pi &= \frac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_a \sqrt{gD}} \\
&= \frac{0.03899}{1.2 \times \sqrt{9.81 \times 12}} \\
&= \frac{0.03899}{1.2 \times \sqrt{117.72}} \\
&= \frac{0.03899}{1.2 \times 10.85} \\
&\approx \frac{0.03899}{13.02} \approx 2.995 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Thomas correlation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{H}{D} &= 42 \times \Pi^{0.61} \\
&= 42 \times (2.995 \times 10^{-3})^{0.61}
\end{aligned}
$$

Take logarithm: $0.61 \times \ln(2.995 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.61 \times (-5.811) = -3.545$.

Exponentiate: $e^{-3.545} \approx 0.02884$.

$$
\frac{H}{D} = 42 \times 0.02884 \approx 1.211
$$

So $H = 1.211 \times 12 \approx 14.5 \; \mathrm{m}$.

**Step 3 — Optically thick check:**

$k\beta D = 0.7 \times 12 = 8.4 \gg 1$, therefore the flame is **optically thick** ✓. Radiation re-absorption is significant; the use of a **detailed radiative sub-model (Shokri–Beyler)** is preferred over a point-source approximation.

> **Final Answer (b):** $H \approx 14.5 \; \mathrm{m}$. The flame is **optically thick**, so the cylindrical Shokri–Beyler model is appropriate. **(7 marks: 3 + 2 + 2)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Radiative Heat Flux Calculation*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**A 20 m diameter circular gasoline pool burns in still air. Given: $\dot{m}''_{\infty} = 0.055 \; \mathrm{kg/m^2/s}$, $\Delta H_c = 43.7 \; \mathrm{MJ/kg}$, $\chi_r = 0.20$, $k\beta = 0.7 \; \mathrm{m^{-1}}$. Calculate the total heat release rate, the radiative power, and the SEP assuming the flame is a cylinder of height = Thomas-predicted value.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Burning rate:** With $D = 20 \; \mathrm{m}$, $k\beta D = 14$, so $\dot{m}'' \approx 0.055 \; \mathrm{kg/m^2/s}$ (saturated).

**Step 2 — Pool area and total heat release rate:**

$$
A_p = \pi (20)^2 / 4 = 100\pi \approx 314.16 \; \mathrm{m^2}
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = 0.055 \times 314.16 \times 43.7 \times 10^6 \approx 7.55 \times 10^8 \; \mathrm{W} = 755 \; \mathrm{MW}
$$

**Step 3 — Radiative power:**

$$
\dot{Q}_R = 0.20 \times 755 = 151 \; \mathrm{MW}
$$

**Step 4 — Flame height (from earlier example):** $H \approx 25.6 \; \mathrm{m}$.

**Step 5 — Flame area (cylindrical):**

$$
A_f = \pi D H = \pi \times 20 \times 25.6 \approx 1608.5 \; \mathrm{m^2}
$$

**Step 6 — SEP:**

$$
\mathrm{SEP} = \frac{151 \times 10^6}{1608.5} \approx 93{,}880 \; \mathrm{W/m^2} \approx 93.9 \; \mathrm{kW/m^2}
$$

> **Final Answer (a):** $\dot{Q} \approx 755 \; \mathrm{MW}$, $\dot{Q}_R \approx 151 \; \mathrm{MW}$, $\mathrm{SEP} \approx 93.9 \; \mathrm{kW/m^2}$. **(7 marks: distributed over each step)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A target (vertical surface) is located at a horizontal distance of 30 m from the centre of the pool in part (a). Assuming the atmospheric transmissivity $\tau = 0.85$ and computing the view factor using the appropriate point-source correlation $F_{12} = D^2 / [4 (r^2 + H^2)]$ (modified for vertical target with $r$ = horizontal distance), compute the incident heat flux on the target. Comment on whether this flux is sufficient to cause piloted ignition of wood.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the view factor $F_{12}$:**

For a vertical surface at horizontal distance $r$ from the flame centre, with the flame modelled as a point source at height $H/2$ above the pool, a practical engineering approximation is:

$$
F_{12} \approx \frac{D^2 / 4}{r^2 + (H/2)^2} = \frac{20^2 / 4}{30^2 + (12.8)^2} = \frac{100}{900 + 163.84} = \frac{100}{1063.84} \approx 0.094
$$

**Step 2 — Incident heat flux:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q'' &= \tau \cdot F_{12} \cdot \mathrm{SEP} \\
&= 0.85 \times 0.094 \times 93.9 \\
&\approx 7.50 \; \mathrm{kW/m^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Damage assessment:**

The threshold for **piloted ignition of wood** is approximately $12.5 \; \mathrm{kW/m^2}$ (sustained). Our computed $7.5 \; \mathrm{kW/m^2}$ is **below** this threshold, so piloted wood ignition is **not expected at 30 m**.

However, this value **exceeds** the $4 \; \mathrm{kW/m^2}$ pain threshold and is well within the $5$–$8 \; \mathrm{kW/m^2}$ range for **first-degree skin burns**, so **personnel evacuation** is mandatory.

> **Final Answer (b):** $q'' \approx 7.5 \; \mathrm{kW/m^2}$. **Insufficient for piloted wood ignition; sufficient for personnel injury.** **(7 marks: 3 + 2 + 2)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Mixing $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ (W) with SEP given in kW. Always convert to a common base.
> 2. **Forgetting the exponential in Babrauskas:** Many students use $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_\infty$ blindly. For small pools ($D < 5 \; \mathrm{m}$), this gives a *significant* overestimate.
> 3. **Wrong sign in $e^{-k\beta D}$:** Some students write $e^{+k\beta D}$, which causes the burning rate to *grow* — physically impossible.
> 4. **Using point-source model for large optically-thick flames:** Valid for $D < 3$ m only. For larger pools, use the **Shokri–Beyler cylindrical model**.
> 5. **Ignoring flame tilt under cross-wind:** Always state the wind condition; tilting the flame shifts the thermal footprint and changes $F_{12}$.
> 6. **Confusing $H$ and $H \cos \theta$:** Moorhouse's $H \cos \theta$ is the **vertical** projection of the tilted flame — *not* the geometric flame length.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3 — Pool Fire)**

- ☐ **Definition:** A pool fire is the combustion of vapour from a **stationary or flowing liquid pool**; the flame is *buoyancy-driven* and *diffusion-limited*.
- ☐ **Three sub-models are MANDATORY** for any KTU pool fire problem: burning rate → flame geometry → radiative flux.
- ☐ **Babrauskas burning rate:** $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right)$. Plateaus for large pools; goes to zero for $D \to 0$.
- ☐ **Thomas flame height:** $H/D = 42 (\dot{m}'' / \rho_a \sqrt{gD})^{0.61}$ — vertical, windless.
- ☐ **Moorhouse tilted flame:** $H \cos\theta = 42 D (\dots)^{0.61}$ — when wind is present.
- ☐ **Total heat release rate:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$.
- ☐ **Radiative power:** $\dot{Q}_R = \chi_r \cdot \dot{Q}$, with $\chi_r \approx 0.20$–$0.35$ for hydrocarbon pool fires.
- ☐ **SEP:** $\mathrm{SEP} = \dot{Q}_R / A_f$, where $A_f = \pi D H$ for a cylinder.
