- ☐ **Incident heat flux:** $q'' = \tau \cdot F_{12} \cdot \mathrm{SEP}$.
- ☐ **Damage thresholds (memorize!):** $4 \; \mathrm{kW/m^2}$ (pain), $12.5 \; \mathrm{kW/m^2}$ (wood ignition), $25\text{–}37.5 \; \mathrm{kW/m^2}$ (equipment damage, domino).
- ☐ **Burning rate is NOT constant** — it depends on $D$. Don't fall for "linear $\dot{m}''$" trap.
- ☐ **Asymptotic $\dot{m}''_\infty$ values to remember (kg/m²/s):** LNG 0.078, LPG 0.099, gasoline 0.055, kerosene 0.039, methanol 0.015, crude oil 0.045.
- ☐ **Radiative fraction $\chi_r$ increases with pool diameter** — sooting, thick flames radiate more.
- ☐ **Atmospheric transmissivity $\tau$** reduces with distance and humidity — important beyond 50 m.
- ☐ **Atmospheric stability (Pasquill classes)** affects plume rise and radiative footprint for very large fires.
- ☐ **Buncefield, Jaipur, Skikda** are real-world case studies that show pool fire domino effects — expect a one-line Q on these in KTU.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models for Explosion - TNT Equivalent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Models for Explosion - TNT Equivalent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **TNT Equivalent Method** is a widely used empirical-probabilistic modelling approach in Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) that expresses the destructive energy released by a chemical or physical explosion as an equivalent mass of Trinitrotoluene (TNT). The method was originally developed by military ordnance engineers during the mid-twentieth century (notably from the work of **Wilfred E. Baker, C. D. Westine, F. T. Dodge, and others**) and was later adopted by the process industries (CCPS, TNO) for consequence analysis of vapour-cloud explosions (VCE), Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosions (BLEVE), and runaway reactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The TNT Equivalent model is a *single-energy-point-source* explosion model in which the released combustion energy of a fuel-air cloud is replaced by the equivalent mass of TNT such that the resulting blast-wave parameters (peak overpressure, impulse, and positive-phase duration) are reproduced at the same scaled distance from the centre of the cloud.

The fundamental energy equivalence relation is written as:

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_{c}}{E_{TNT}}$$

where $W_{TNT}$ is the equivalent TNT mass (kg), $\eta$ is the **explosion efficiency factor** (dimensionless), $m_{fuel}$ is the mass of fuel participating in combustion (kg), $\Delta H_c$ is the **heat of combustion** of the fuel (kJ/kg), and $E_{TNT}$ is the **specific energy of TNT = 4520 kJ/kg**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine comparing the destructive power of two crackers — one made of gunpowder and one made of a special chemical. To decide which cracker is more dangerous for safety zoning, we cannot simply weigh them. Instead, we ask: *"How much TNT would release the same amount of energy?"* If 1 kg of gunpowder releases the same energy as 0.6 kg of TNT, then for safety calculations, we **treat the gunpowder explosion as if it were a 0.6 kg TNT bomb**.

In a refinery, when 10,000 kg of propane leaks and forms a vapour cloud that ignites, safety engineers do not directly compute the blast from propane chemistry. They convert that propane release into an *equivalent TNT mass* (typically only a few per cent efficiency) and then use well-established military blast tables to predict overpressure, fragmentation distance, and lethality. This conversion is the **TNT Equivalent Method**.

### 1.3 Energy Reference Constant

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Energy of TNT (Per KTU Convention):**
> **$E_{TNT} = 4520 \text{ kJ/kg}$** (some references adopt **$4680 \text{ kJ/kg}$** for high-explosive detonation energy).
> For KTU board examinations, the value **$E_{TNT} = 4520 \text{ kJ/kg}$** is the recommended benchmark.

### 1.4 Classification of Explosions Modelled by TNT Method

| Explosion Class | Typical $\eta$ | Physical State | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Detonation of condensed explosive** | $\eta \approx 1.0$ | Solid/liquid high explosive | TNT, RDX, PETN |
| **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE), unconfined** | $\eta = 0.01 - 0.10$ | Gaseous cloud | Propane, LPG, methane release |
| **Vapour Cloud Explosion, partially confined** | $\eta = 0.10 - 0.40$ | Gaseous cloud in congested geometry | Offshore module, pipe-rack |
| **BLEVE** (fireball) | Thermal — not directly TNT | Pressurised liquefied gas | LPG tank rupture |
| **Dust Explosion** | $\eta = 0.05 - 0.20$ | Suspended particulate | Grain silo, coal dust |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physical Rationale

The TNT method assumes a **point-source, hemispherical, instantaneous release of energy** at ground level. The four physical assumptions are:

1. The fuel-air mixture combusts instantaneously (constant-volume / constant-energy model).
2. All released energy is converted into the blast wave (no thermal radiation, no cratering).
3. The blast wave propagates as a self-similar Friedlander-type shock.
4. The peak side-on overpressure $P_s$ depends only on the **scaled distance** $\bar{R}$.

The self-similarity (Hopkinson-Cranz scaling) is the cornerstone of the method and is expressed as:

$$P_s = f\left(\frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}\right)$$

This means a **1 kg charge at 10 m produces the same overpressure as a 1000 kg charge at 100 m**.

### 2.2 Logic Flow of the TNT Equivalent Computation

The complete KTU-standard sequence is:

- **Step 1:** Identify the inventory of flammable material released to the atmosphere (kg or m³ of vapour).
- **Step 2:** Determine the **mass participating in the explosion** — for a VCE this is the mass within the flammable region of the cloud.
- **Step 3:** Multiply by the **heat of combustion** $\Delta H_c$ (kJ/kg) to obtain the total chemical energy available.
- **Step 4:** Apply the **explosion efficiency factor** $\eta$ (based on congestion, confinement, reactivity) to obtain the energy actually converted to blast.
- **Step 5:** Divide by $E_{TNT}$ to obtain the **equivalent TNT mass** $W_{TNT}$.
- **Step 6:** Compute the **scaled distance** $\bar{R} = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ at the location of interest.
- **Step 7:** Read the **scaled overpressure** $\bar{P} = P_s / P_0$ from a TNT blast curve (Baker, Sachs, or Kingery tables).
- **Step 8:** Convert $\bar{P}$ back to absolute overpressure and apply probit equations for human/infrastructure damage.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are KTU-board-tested. Pipe notation uses $\vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | Validity / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_{c}}{E_{TNT}}$ | TNT equivalent mass | kg | $\eta$ between 0.01 – 1.0 |
| 2 | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | Scaled (Hopkinson) distance | m / kg$^{1/3}$ | All distances |
| 3 | $\bar{P} = \dfrac{P_s - P_0}{P_0}$ | Scaled side-on overpressure | dimensionless | $0.01 \le \bar{R} \le 100$ |
| 4 | $P_s = \bar{P} \cdot P_0 + P_0$ | Absolute side-on overpressure | Pa (kPa) | $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa |
| 5 | $\dfrac{i}{\sqrt[3]{W_{TNT}}} = \phi\left(\bar{R}\right)$ | Scaled impulse relation | Pa·s/kg$^{1/3}$ | Use impulse charts |
| 6 | $Z_e = \dfrac{R}{\left(E_d\right)^{1/3}}$ | TNO energy-scaled distance | m / J$^{1/3}$ | Multi-Energy variant |
| 7 | $E_d = m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \eta$ | Total mechanical explosion energy | J | Used in TNO method |
| 8 | $Y = a + b \ln(V)$ | Probit equation form | — | For fatality / structural damage |
| 9 | $P_r = 5 - 5 \cdot e^{-0.04 \, P_s}$ | Decay probit for lung haemorrhage (Baker) | — | $P_s$ in psi |
| 10 | $V = \dfrac{P_s^2 \cdot t_e}{m}$ | Effective overpressure-impulse load | Pa²·s/kg | Damage criterion |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hazardous-area classification and plant layout:** Establishing safe separation distances between process units and occupied buildings.
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA):** Generating individual-risk and societal-risk contours.
- **Offshore platform design:** Sizing blast walls, fire-rated cabins, and escape-route integrity.
- **Onshore chemical parks:** Land-use-planning consultations under Indian Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation (PESO) rules.
- **Emergency response planning:** Defining shelter-in-place and evacuation radii.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the TNT Equivalent Mass Formula

Starting from the principle of **energy equivalence**, the energy released by a fuel-air cloud during combustion is:

$$E_{fuel} = m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c$$

However, only a fraction of this energy is converted into the blast wave; the remainder is dissipated as heat, radiation, and acoustic loss. Introducing the **mechanical (explosive) efficiency** $\eta$:

$$E_{blast} = \eta \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c$$

For an equivalent mass $W_{TNT}$ of TNT, the released explosive energy is:

$$E_{TNT,total} = W_{TNT} \cdot E_{TNT}$$

Setting the two energies equal (energy-equivalence postulate):

$$\eta \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c = W_{TNT} \cdot E_{TNT}$$

Solving for $W_{TNT}$:

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$$

This is the master equation of the TNT-equivalent method.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Scaled Distance and Self-Similarity

Consider two hemispherical TNT charges of mass $W_1$ and $W_2$ detonated at ground level. Their blast waves are characterised by a non-dimensional radial coordinate. For a point-source isentropic expansion in the strong-shock limit, dimensional analysis of the governing Euler equations (continuity, momentum, energy) shows that the only dimensionless group combining length $R$ and energy $E$ is:

$$\bar{R} = \frac{R}{E^{1/3}}$$

Since $E \propto W_{TNT}$ (linearly proportional), the **Hopkinson-Cranz scaled distance** is:

$$\bar{R} = \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$$

The corresponding scaled peak overpressure is:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{P_s - P_0}{P_0}$$

The functional relation $\bar{P} = f(\bar{R})$ is the universal TNT blast curve, independent of charge size.

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Board-Style Numerical)

**Problem Statement:** A vapour cloud of 5,000 kg of propane is released from a ruptured sphere. The cloud is ignited in a partially congested region. Assume explosion efficiency $\eta = 0.05$, $\Delta H_c = 46,000$ kJ/kg for propane, $E_{TNT} = 4520$ kJ/kg. Determine the peak side-on overpressure at a receptor located **200 m downwind**.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute equivalent TNT mass**

Substituting directly into the master equation:

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$$

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{0.05 \cdot 5000 \cdot 46{,}000}{4520}$$

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{11{,}500{,}000}{4520}$$

$$W_{TNT} = 2544.25 \text{ kg} \approx 2.54 \times 10^3 \text{ kg TNT}$$

**Solution — Step 2: Compute scaled distance**

$$\bar{R} = \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \frac{200}{(2544.25)^{1/3}}$$

Cubing the numerator: $2544.25^{1/3}$. Using iterative cube-root:

$$13^3 = 2197, \quad 14^3 = 2744$$

Interpolating linearly:

$$(2544.25 - 2197) / (2744 - 2197) = 347.25 / 547 \approx 0.635$$

$$W_{TNT}^{1/3} \approx 13.635$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{R} = \frac{200}{13.635} = 14.67 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Solution — Step 3: Read scaled overpressure from TNT chart**

For $\bar{R} = 14.67$, using the standard Baker-Strehlow TNT chart for a hemispherical surface burst:

$$\bar{P} \approx 0.045$$

(The student should consult the KTU-recommended blast chart during the exam.)

**Solution — Step 4: Convert back to absolute side-on overpressure**

$$P_s = \bar{P} \cdot P_0 + P_0 = 0.045 \cdot 101.325 + 101.325$$

$$P_s = 4.560 + 101.325 = 105.885 \text{ kPa}$$

**Solution — Step 5: Compute overpressure above ambient**

$$\Delta P_s = P_s - P_0 = 4.560 \text{ kPa} \approx 4.6 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Damage implication:** A side-on overpressure of $\approx 4.6$ kPa corresponds to **partial demolition of houses** and **broken window glass at significant distance**, with probit-equation fatality potential of roughly **10–15 %** for unsheltered occupants.

### 3.4 Alternative Quick Reference: Empirical Correlation for $\bar{P}$

For students without access to a blast chart, a frequently cited polynomial approximation (valid for $\bar{R} > 1$) is the **Baker correlation**:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{8.06}{\bar{R}} + \frac{143}{\bar{R}^2} - \frac{585}{\bar{R}^3} + \frac{626}{\bar{R}^4} \quad \text{(hemispherical TNT)}$$

Substituting $\bar{R} = 14.67$:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{8.06}{14.67} + \frac{143}{215.2} - \frac{585}{3157.6} + \frac{626}{46{,}267}$$

$$\bar{P} = 0.549 + 0.664 - 0.185 + 0.014$$

$$\bar{P} \approx 1.042$$

This value is in the **strong-shock regime** and is higher than the chart value because the polynomial is calibrated for the **incident / reflected** regime at smaller $\bar{R}$. For hemispherical surface-burst, the chart value ($\approx 0.045$ at $\bar{R}=14.67$) is the correct reference for the side-on blast.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of $\eta$ on Damage Radius

| $\eta$ | $W_{TNT}$ (kg) | $W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ | Damage radius for $\Delta P = 10$ kPa (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.01 | 508.8 | 7.97 | ≈ 119 m |
| 0.05 | 2544.3 | 13.63 | ≈ 205 m |
| 0.10 | 5088.5 | 17.19 | ≈ 258 m |
| 0.40 | 20354.0 | 27.30 | ≈ 410 m |

The cubic-root dependence demonstrates that even small increases in confinement dramatically extend the blast damage radius.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
    F --> G[Blast Energy]
    G --> H[Divide by E_TNT 4520 kJ per kg]

```
