<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
Define the **TNT Equivalent Method** for explosion modelling. List two limitations.

**Model Answer:**

The TNT Equivalent Method expresses the destructive energy released by an explosion (e.g., a vapour-cloud explosion) as the mass of trinitrotoluene (TNT) that would release the same energy. The equivalent mass is computed as $W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c / E_{TNT}$, where $E_{TNT} = 4520$ kJ/kg.

**Limitations:**

1. The method assumes instantaneous point-source release and ignores directional blast focusing in congested geometries.
2. It cannot distinguish between confined and unconfined vapour-cloud explosions or model jet-firing and partial confinement accurately; for these, the TNO Multi-Energy method is preferred.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Series A]**
What is the significance of the **Hopkinson-Cranz scaled distance** in explosion analysis?

**Model Answer:**

The scaled distance is defined as $\bar{R} = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$. It expresses blast-wave self-similarity: identical overpressures are produced at geometrically similar positions relative to the energy source. This allows the use of a single universal blast curve ($\bar{P}$ versus $\bar{R}$) regardless of charge size, simplifying consequence calculations.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to (a) use **$E_{TNT} = 4520$ kJ/kg** consistently, (b) report the **cube-root** computation correctly, and (c) specify whether the blast is **hemispherical surface burst or free-air burst**. Each omission costs **2 marks**. Always state the boundary condition and the unit of $W_{TNT}$ explicitly.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Vapour Cloud Explosion via TNT Method

**(a)** A 10,000 kg leak of butane ($\Delta H_c = 45{,}750$ kJ/kg) forms an unconfined vapour cloud that ignites with an explosion efficiency $\eta = 0.04$. Estimate the equivalent TNT mass. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the TNT equivalent obtained, compute the peak side-on overpressure at a receptor 250 m away using the empirical correlation
$$\bar{P} = \frac{8.06}{\bar{R}} + \frac{143}{\bar{R}^2} - \frac{585}{\bar{R}^3} + \frac{626}{\bar{R}^4}$$
and interpret the damage implications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

Applying the master formula:

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$$

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{0.04 \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 45{,}750}{4520}$$

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{18{,}300{,}000}{4520} = 4048.67 \text{ kg} \approx 4.05 \times 10^3 \text{ kg TNT}$$

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The cube-root of $W_{TNT}$:

$$W_{TNT}^{1/3} = (4048.67)^{1/3}$$

$$15^3 = 3375, \quad 16^3 = 4096, \quad \text{interpolation: } (4048.67 - 3375)/(4096 - 3375) = 673.67 / 721 \approx 0.934$$

$$W_{TNT}^{1/3} \approx 15.934 \approx 15.93$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{R} = \frac{250}{15.93} = 15.69 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

Substituting into the empirical polynomial:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{8.06}{15.69} + \frac{143}{(15.69)^2} - \frac{585}{(15.69)^3} + \frac{626}{(15.69)^4}$$

Computing each term:

$$\frac{8.06}{15.69} = 0.5136$$

$$15.69^2 = 246.18, \quad \frac{143}{246.18} = 0.5809$$

$$15.69^3 = 3862.4, \quad \frac{585}{3862.4} = 0.1515$$

$$15.69^4 = 60{,}602, \quad \frac{626}{60{,}602} = 0.01033$$

Summing:

$$\bar{P} = 0.5136 + 0.5809 - 0.1515 + 0.01033 \approx 0.953$$

Converting to overpressure:

$$P_s = \bar{P} \cdot P_0 + P_0 = 0.953 \cdot 101.325 + 101.325 \approx 197.9 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\Delta P_s \approx 96.6 \text{ kPa}$$

**Damage Interpretation:** A side-on overpressure of $\approx 97$ kPa is in the **heavy-damage range** — most residential structures collapse, reinforced-concrete buildings sustain severe damage, and fatality probability for exposed personnel exceeds 50 %.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Comparative Analysis of Explosion Models

**(a)** With neat sketches, differentiate between the **TNT Equivalent Method** and the **TNO Multi-Energy Method** for vapour-cloud explosion modelling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 30 m × 30 m congested region contains a stoichiometric propane-air cloud with mass 2,500 kg. Estimate the explosion energy using both methods (assume $\eta_{TNO} = 1.0$ for the strong blast class). Compare the results and discuss why the TNO method is generally preferred for partially confined clouds. $\Delta H_c$ (propane) = 46,000 kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Feature | TNT Equivalent Method | TNO Multi-Energy Method |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Equivalent mass of TNT | Fuel-air cloud as energy source |
| Energy conversion | Single global efficiency factor $\eta = 0.01 – 0.10$ | Strong-blast class $\eta \approx 1.0$ |
| Blast curve | TNT hemispherical curve | Ten blast-strength classes (1–10) |
| Confinement treatment | Ignored (averaged) | Explicitly accounted for |
| Output | Conservative **under-estimate** in unconfined cases; **over-estimate** in confined cases | More realistic spatial damage |
| Source | Baker / Wilfred 1950s | van den Berg, 1980s (TNO) |

A **neat sketch** should show: (i) hemispherical surface burst for TNT; (ii) disc-shaped strong-blast source for TNO with radial blast wave from the congested plane.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**TNT Equivalent Method (assuming $\eta = 0.05$ for partially congested):**

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{0.05 \cdot 2500 \cdot 46{,}000}{4520} = \frac{5{,}750{,}000}{4520} \approx 1272.1 \text{ kg TNT}$$

**TNO Multi-Energy Method:**

$$E_d = m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \eta_{TNO} = 2500 \cdot 46{,}000 \cdot 1.0 = 1.15 \times 10^9 \text{ kJ}$$

Converting to TNT mass:

$$W_{TNT}^{TNO} = \frac{E_d}{E_{TNT}} = \frac{1.15 \times 10^9}{4520} = 2.54 \times 10^5 \text{ kg} = 254{,}424 \text{ kg TNT}$$

**Comparison:** The TNO method predicts an equivalent TNT mass roughly **200 times** larger than the simple TNT method. This reflects the recognition that, in a congested region, the fuel-air cloud can behave almost as efficiently as a high explosive — a phenomenon the simple TNT method completely neglects by assuming a low uniform $\eta$.

**Why TNO is preferred for partially confined clouds:**

1. The TNO method explicitly accounts for **congestion and confinement**, recognising that partial confinement can produce blast intensities comparable to high explosives.
2. It uses a **spectrum of blast strengths** (classes 1–10), allowing realistic modelling of both weak and strong regions within the same cloud.
3. It avoids the systematic under-estimation inherent in the single $\eta$ of the TNT method.
4. It is recommended by **CCPS Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis (CPQRA)** for vapour-cloud explosion modelling.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert kJ to J (factor of 1000) when applying the TNO energy-scaled distance formula $Z_e = R/(E_d)^{1/3}$.
> 2. Confusing $E_{TNT} = 4520$ kJ/kg with $4680$ kJ/kg without stating the reference.
> 3. Assuming $\eta = 1.0$ in the TNT method for unconfined VCE — this is a **fatal error** for KTU exams.
> 4. Omitting the unit of scaled distance (m/kg$^{1/3}$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Formula:** $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$ with $E_{TNT} = 4520$ kJ/kg.
- **Hopkinson-Cranz Scaling:** $P_s$ depends only on $\bar{R} = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$.
- **Typical efficiency ranges:** Unconfined VCE $\eta = 0.01 - 0.10$; Confined VCE $\eta = 0.10 - 0.40$; condensed explosives $\eta \approx 1.0$.
- **Two key assumptions:** Point-source energy release; hemispherical surface burst at ground level.
- **Use TNO Multi-Energy Method** for congested / partially confined vapour clouds where the simple TNT method would significantly under-predict overpressure.
- **Always convert** $\Delta P$ to absolute $P_s$ before applying probit equations.
- **Self-similarity** means a 1 kg charge at 10 m and a 1000 kg charge at 100 m produce identical overpressures.
- **For board exams**, write down units at every step and explicitly state the assumed $\eta$ value and source of $E_{TNT}$.
- **Damage interpretation benchmark:** 10 kPa — glass breakage; 35 kPa — house demolition; 70 kPa — heavy structural damage; 200 kPa — reinforced concrete failure.
- **Probit equation for fatalities (lung haemorrhage):** $Y = 5 - 5 \exp(-0.04 P_s)$ with $P_s$ in psi.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models for Dispersion - Gaussian Gas Dispersion Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITUM RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Models for Dispersion - Gaussian Gas Dispersion Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Gaussian Plume Dispersion Model** is a steady-state, straight-line trajectory atmospheric transport model that describes the concentration of a pollutant (or hazardous gas) released continuously from an elevated or ground-level source, assuming that the dispersion in the **crosswind (y)** and **vertical (z)** directions follows a normal (Gaussian) probability distribution. It is the cornerstone passive dispersion model endorsed by regulatory agencies such as the **U.S. EPA** and forms the mathematical backbone of consequence analysis within **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** studies under frameworks like **CCPS / TNO Purple Book**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The model assumes the following boundary conditions: (i) steady-state emission, (ii) constant wind speed and direction, (iii) conservative (non-reactive, non-depositing) pollutant, (iv) turbulent diffusion dominates over molecular diffusion, and (v) reflection of the plume at the ground surface.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine lighting a single stick of incense in a windless corridor and walking away while watching the smoke. Initially, the smoke travels in a tight ribbon, but as it moves downwind, it spreads *side-to-side* and also *up-and-down* forming an ever-widening cone. Now, picture thousands of smoke particles released every second — the resulting cloud's centerline travels with the wind, but the *probability* of finding a particle at any off-axis point tapers off following the famous bell-curve shape.

That bell-curve is the **Gaussian distribution**. The mathematical model essentially asks: *"At a given distance downwind, what is the probability density of finding the gas molecule at a particular crosswind offset and height?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Crowd Leaving a Stadium**
> When a stadium releases fans through a single narrow gate onto a wide street, the crowd forms a wedge. The densest concentration is along the gate's centerline (downwind axis), thinning toward the sidewalks. The further from the gate, the wider and shorter the wedge becomes due to people wandering. This is *exactly* how a Gaussian plume behaves in 3D space, where the gate is the release point and the sidewalk boundary is the ground reflection.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Atmospheric stability parameter ($\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$)** — measured in meters (m), defines plume spread.
- **Standard wind speed reference height ($z_{ref}$)** — typically **10 m** above ground.
- **Gas emission rate ($Q$)** — measured in **g/s** or **kg/s**.
- **Effective stack/release height ($H$)** — measured in meters (m).

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exam answers, always explicitly state the **assumptions of the Gaussian model** at the start of your solution — this is a guaranteed 2-mark bonus trigger for the examiner.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The Gaussian model is built upon the **advection–diffusion equation**, where the steady-state concentration distribution $C(x,y,z)$ at a downwind distance $x$, crosswind offset $y$, and height $z$ is governed by:

1. **Advection** — bulk transport by mean wind $u$ along the $x$-axis.
2. **Turbulent Diffusion** — modeled as Fickian with coefficients $K_y$ and $K_z$.
3. **Mass Conservation** — emission rate $Q$ must equal the integrated concentration over all $y$ and $z$.

Solving this equation under the listed boundary conditions yields the **canonical Gaussian plume equation**.

### 2.2 Atmospheric Stability Classes — Pasquill–Gifford Scheme

The vertical and lateral spread parameters $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ depend strongly on **atmospheric turbulence intensity**, classified into six categories:

| Class | Stability Description | Surface Heat Flux | Wind Condition (typical) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A** | Very Unstable | Strong solar heating | Low wind, clear skies |
| **B** | Moderately Unstable | Moderate solar heating | Low–moderate wind |
| **C** | Slightly Unstable | Weak solar heating | Moderate wind |
| **D** | **Neutral** (default) | Overcast / day-to-night | Moderate wind ($\geq 3$ m/s) |
| **E** | Slightly Stable | Weak radiative cooling | Low wind, clear night |
| **F** | Moderately Stable | Strong radiative cooling | Very low wind, clear night |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Class D (Neutral)** is the default scenario in most KTU numerical problems unless otherwise specified.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Gaussian Plume Equation** | $C(x,y,z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\left[\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$ | $C$ in g/m³, $Q$ in g/s, $u$ in m/s |
| 2 | **Ground-level Concentration** ($z=0$) | $C(x,y,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | Direct receptor on ground |
| 3 | **Centerline Ground Concentration** ($y=0$, $z=0$) | $C(x,0,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | Maximum at any downwind distance |
| 4 | **Wind Power Law (Height Correction)** | $u(z) = u_{ref}\!\left(\dfrac{z}{z_{ref}}\right)^{p}$ | $p$ depends on stability class |
| 5 | **Briggs Formula for $\sigma_y$** | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{a\, x^{b}}{1000}$ | $a$, $b$ are stability-class constants |
| 6 | **Briggs Formula for $\sigma_z$** | $\sigma_z = c\, x^{d}$ for $x \leq x_{lim}$, else piecewise | $c$, $d$ depend on class |
| 7 | **Effective Stack Height** | $H = h_s + \Delta h$ | $h_s$ = physical stack height, $\Delta h$ = plume rise |
| 8 | **Briggs Plume Rise (Buoyant)** | $\Delta h = 1.6\, F^{1/3}\, x_f^{2/3}\, / u$ | $F$ = buoyancy flux (m⁴/s³) |
| 9 | **Max Ground Concentration Distance** | $x_{max} = \dfrac{H}{\sigma_z(x)\, x^{-1}}\vert_{\text{critical}}$ | Found by differentiating $C(x,0,0)$ wrt $x$ |
| 10 | **Critical $\sigma_z$ for Max Concentration** | $\sigma_{z,crit} = \dfrac{H}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Plug-and-choke simplification |

> [!NOTE]
> The dispersion parameters $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ are **NOT universal constants** — they grow with downwind distance $x$. The Briggs (1973) correlations and Pasquill–Gifford curves provide tabulated coefficients for each stability class.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Petrochemical Refineries | Toxic gas (NH₃, Cl₂, H₂S) consequence analysis |
| LNG Terminals | Flammable vapor cloud dispersion post-spill |
| Nuclear Power Plants | Routine radioactive effluent concentration maps |
| Urban Air Quality | PM₂.₅ and NO₂ regulatory compliance modeling |
| Fire & Safety Engineering | Occupant evacuation distance calculations |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Advection–Diffusion to Gaussian Plume

**Starting Equation (steady-state 3D advection–diffusion):**

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

**Step 1 — Introduce new variables** to reduce the equation to a diffusion form. Let $C$ be a function of $x$, $y$, $z$ where $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ grow with $x$.

**Step 2 — Apply Gaussian ansatz.** Assume the solution takes the form of a product of two Gaussians in $y$ and $z$, with spreading standard deviations $\sigma_y(x)$ and $\sigma_z(x)$:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary condition at the ground ($z=0$).** To ensure no net flux across the ground (reflection), use the **method of images** by adding an *image source* at $z = -H$:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Verify mass conservation.** Integrate $C$ over all $y$ and $z$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} C\, u\, dy\, dz = Q$$

This confirms the formula is dimensionally and physically consistent.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Location of Maximum Ground-Level Concentration

**Step 1 — Start with centerline ground-level concentration** (setting $y=0$ and $z=0$ in the general formula):

$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Assume $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ follow power-law growth.** For neutral (Class D) conditions, Briggs gives:
- $\sigma_y \approx 0.08\, x / \sqrt{1 + 0.0001 x}$ ≈ constant-ratio regime
- $\sigma_z \approx 0.02\, x$ (for short range)

For the **idealized** case where $\sigma_z = c\, x^{d}$:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left[\ln C\right] = \frac{d}{dx}\left[-\ln(\sigma_y \sigma_z) - \frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right] = 0$$

**Step 3 — Set derivative to zero** and solve for critical $\sigma_z$:

$$\sigma_{z,crit} = \frac{H}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back** into the power law $\sigma_z = c\, x^{d}$:

$$x_{max} = \left(\frac{H}{c\sqrt{2}}\right)^{1/d}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $x_{max}$ into the concentration formula** to get $C_{max}$:

$$C_{max} = \frac{Q}{\pi u\, \sigma_y(x_{max})\, \sigma_{z,crit}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)$$

The exponential factor $e^{-1/2} \approx 0.6065$ is a **constant multiplier** appearing in all $C_{max}$ derivations.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** A refinery stack releases SO₂ at a rate of $Q = 80$ g/s. Wind speed at 10 m is $u_{10} = 5$ m/s. Effective stack height is $H = 60$ m. Stability class is **D (neutral)**. Calculate the ground-level centerline concentration at $x = 500$ m downwind.

**Given Data:**
- $Q = 80$ g/s
- $u = 5$ m/s  
- $H = 60$ m
- $x = 500$ m
- Class D Briggs coefficients: $\sigma_y \approx 0.08\, x (1+0.0001 x)^{-1/2}$, $\sigma_z \approx 0.06\, x$

**Step 1 — Calculate $\sigma_y$:**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{0.08 \times 500}{\sqrt{1 + 0.0001 \times 500}} = \frac{40}{\sqrt{1.05}} = \frac{40}{1.0247} \approx 39.04 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\sigma_z$:**

$$\sigma_z = 0.06 \times 500 = 30 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply ground-level centerline concentration formula:**

$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$C = \frac{80}{\pi \times 5 \times 39.04 \times 30}\exp\!\left(-\frac{60^2}{2 \times 30^2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the prefactor:**

$$\frac{80}{\pi \times 5 \times 39.04 \times 30} = \frac{80}{18,398.4} \approx 4.348 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the exponential:**

$$-\frac{3600}{1800} = -2.0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$$

**Step 7 — Combine:**

$$C \approx 4.348 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1353 \approx 5.88 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g/m}^3$$

**Final Answer:** $C(x,0,0) \approx \mathbf{5.88 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g/m}^3}$ = **0.588 mg/m³**

> [!NOTE]
> For comparison, the WHO 24-hour guideline for SO₂ is 40 µg/m³ (0.04 mg/m³). Hence this release would exceed safe levels within 500 m — a critical QRA finding.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Plume vs. Puff Models

| Feature | Gaussian Plume | Gaussian Puff |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Time dependence** | Steady-state (continuous release) | Transient (instantaneous release) |
| **Geometry** | Cylindrical spreading along wind | Spherical 3D cloud |
| **Application** | Tall stack emissions, routine leaks | Accidental bursts, BLEVEs, valve ruptures |
| **Mathematical form** | Exponential along $x$, Gaussian in $y$, $z$ | 3D Gaussian in all three directions |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Gaussian Plume Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source: Emission Rate Q g per s] --> B[Effective Height H = hs + Plume Rise]
    B --> C[Atmospheric Stability Class Selection A to F]
    C --> D[Wind Profile Power Law u_z correction]
    D --> E[Compute Sigma_y and Sigma_z via Briggs Correlations]
    E --> F[Apply Gaussian Plume Equation]
    F --> G{Is receptor at ground level z=0?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Ground Reflection Term Added]
    G -->|No| I[Standard Plume Formula]
    H --> J[Compute Concentration at x, y, 0]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compare with Toxic Exposure Limits ERPG, AEGL, IDLH]
    K --> L[Decision: Safe or Evacuation Required]

    subgraph INPUT_DATA
        A
        B
    end

    subgraph DISPERSION_ENGINE
        C
        D
        E
        F
    end

    subgraph POST_PROCESSING
        G
        H
        I
        J
    end

    subgraph RISK_DECISION
        K
        L
    end
```

### 4.2 Coordinate System Schematic — Plume Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DOWNWIND_AXIS
        X[Wind Direction +x axis]
    end

    subgraph VERTICAL_AXIS
        Z[Height +z axis upward]
    end

    subgraph CROSSWIND_AXIS
        Y[Crosswind +y axis]
    end

    SRC[Source at height H] --> PLUME[Plume Travels along +x]
    PLUME --> SPREAD[Lateral Spread Sigma_y]
    PLUME --> VSPREAD[Vertical Spread Sigma_z]
    SPREAD --> RECEPTOR[Receptor Point x, y, z]
    VSPREAD --> RECEPTOR
```

### 4.3 Stability Class Decision Logic — Pasquill Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Wind Speed u at 10m]) --> DAY{Night or Day?}
    DAY -->|Day| D1{Cloud Cover?}
    DAY -->|Night| N1{Clear Sky?}

    D1 -->|Strong Insolation| A[Class A Very Unstable]
    D1 -->|Moderate Insolation| B[Class B Mod Unstable]
    D1 -->|Slight Insolation| C[Class C Slight Unstable]
    D1 -->|Overcast| D[Class D Neutral]

    N1 -->|Clear + Low Wind| F[Class F Very Stable]
    N1 -->|Cloudy or Windy| D2[Class D Neutral]
    N1 -->|Mild Conditions| E[Class E Slight Stable]

    A --> OUT[Use Briggs Table for Class A]
    B --> OUT
    C --> OUT
    D --> OUT
    D2 --> OUT
    E --> OUT
    F --> OUT
```

> [!TIP]
> For KTU derivations involving $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$, always cite **Briggs (1973)** as the source of correlation constants. This shows the examiner you know the engineering provenance.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PECHT754]`
**Define the Pasquill–Gifford stability classes and explain their significance in atmospheric dispersion modeling.**

**Model Answer:**
The Pasquill–Gifford scheme classifies atmospheric turbulence into six classes (**A, B, C, D, E, F**) based on surface heat flux (solar radiation) and wind speed. Classes A–C represent unstable conditions (rapid vertical mixing), **Class D** is neutral (default reference), and **E–F** are stable (suppressed vertical mixing, plumes travel flat and far). The chosen class directly determines the dispersion parameters $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$, which control predicted ground-level concentrations. Hence, misclassification can cause errors of one to two orders of magnitude in predicted concentration.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]`
**What is meant by "effective stack height" in the Gaussian plume model? Mention its components.**

**Model Answer:**
The **effective stack height $H$** is the actual height at which the pollutant effectively enters the atmosphere for dispersion purposes. It is given by $H = h_s + \Delta h$, where:
- $h_s$ = physical stack height (m)
- $\Delta h$ = **plume rise** (m), the additional vertical lift due to buoyancy and momentum of the exiting gas

For buoyant releases, Briggs' formula gives $\Delta h = 1.6\, F^{1/3}\, x_f^{2/3}\, / u$, where $F$ is the buoyancy flux and $x_f$ is the distance to final rise.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — With Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper PECHT754, Module 3]`**

> A fertilizer plant releases anhydrous ammonia (NH₃) at a rate of **$Q = 150$ g/s** from a stack with effective height $H = 75$ m. Wind speed at 10 m is $u_{10} = 4$ m/s. Atmospheric stability is **Class D (neutral)**. Using Briggs' correlations: $\sigma_y = 0.08\, x\, (1+0.0001 x)^{-1/2}$ and $\sigma_z = 0.06\, x$ (for $x \leq 1000$ m).
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for ground-level centerline concentration and calculate the value at **$x = 800$ m**.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Determine the **distance $x_{max}$** at which maximum ground-level concentration occurs and compute $C_{max}$.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Recalling the centerline ground-level concentration formula:**

$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\sigma_y$ at $x = 800$ m:**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{0.08 \times 800}{\sqrt{1 + 0.0001 \times 800}} = \frac{64}{\sqrt{1.08}} = \frac{64}{1.0392} \approx 61.59 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $\sigma_z$ at $x = 800$ m:**

$$\sigma_z = 0.06 \times 800 = 48 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute exponential term:**

$$\exp\!\left(-\frac{75^2}{2 \times 48^2}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{5625}{4608}\right) = \exp(-1.2207) \approx 0.2949$$

**Step 5 — Compute prefactor:**

$$\frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} = \frac{150}{\pi \times 4 \times 61.59 \times 48} = \frac{150}{37,142.4} \approx 4.038 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Final multiplication:**

$$C(800, 0, 0) \approx 4.038 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2949 \approx 1.191 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{C(x,0,0) \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/m}^3 = 1.19 \text{ mg/m}^3}$$

**Interpretation:** The **ERPG-2 (Emergency Response Planning Guideline)** for NH₃ is 200 mg/m³ (i.e., 0.2 g/m³ = 200,000 µg/m³), so this predicted ground-level concentration is far below lethal thresholds — but it exceeds odor detection (~5 ppm ≈ 3.5 mg/m³), causing public nuisance.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Condition for maximum ground-level centerline concentration:**

Differentiate $C(x,0,0)$ with respect to $x$ and set to zero. Since $Q$ and $u$ are constant, we maximize:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma_y(x)\, \sigma_z(x)}\exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2(x)}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Taking natural log and differentiating:**

$$\frac{d}{dx}\!\left[\ln f\right] = -\frac{1}{\sigma_y}\frac{d\sigma_y}{dx} - \frac{1}{\sigma_z}\frac{d\sigma_z}{dx} + \frac{H^2}{\sigma_z^3}\frac{d\sigma_z}{dx} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply the critical condition** $\sigma_{z,crit} = \dfrac{H}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$\sigma_{z,crit} = \frac{75}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 53.03 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x_{max}$ using $\sigma_z = 0.06 x$:**

$$x_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{z,crit}}{0.06} = \frac{53.03}{0.06} \approx 883.9 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $\sigma_y$ at $x_{max}$:**

$$\sigma_y(x_{max}) = \frac{0.08 \times 883.9}{\sqrt{1 + 0.0001 \times 883.9}} = \frac{70.71}{\sqrt{1.0884}} \approx 67.78 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Apply $C_{max}$ formula:**

$$C_{max} = \frac{Q\, e^{-1/2}}{\pi u\, \sigma_y(x_{max})\, \sigma_{z,crit}} = \frac{150 \times 0.6065}{\pi \times 4 \times 67.78 \times 53.03}$$

$$C_{max} = \frac{90.97}{45,168.6} \approx 2.014 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{C_{max} \approx 2.01 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/m}^3 = 2.01 \text{ mg/m}^3 \quad \text{at} \quad x_{max} \approx 884 \text{ m}}$$

---

#### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Supplementary Exam, Module 3]`**

> A chlorine manufacturing facility vents Cl₂ at **$Q = 60$ g/s** from an elevated release with effective height **$H = 40$ m**. The wind blows at **$u = 3$ m/s** under **Class C (slightly unstable)** conditions. Briggs' parameters for Class C: $\sigma_y = 0.22\, x\, (1+0.0001 x)^{-1/2}$ and $\sigma_z = 0.20\, x$ (valid up to 1000 m).
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Calculate the **ground-level concentration at $x = 400$ m on the centerline** ($y=0$, $z=0$).
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Using the Pasquill stability classification table, discuss how the result would change if the conditions were **Class F (moderately stable)** instead, with $\sigma_z = 0.04\, x$. Provide numerical evidence.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Evaluate $\sigma_y$ at $x = 400$ m:**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{0.22 \times 400}{\sqrt{1 + 0.0001 \times 400}} = \frac{88}{\sqrt{1.04}} = \frac{88}{1.0198} \approx 86.29 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $\sigma_z$ at $x = 400$ m:**

$$\sigma_z = 0.20 \times 400 = 80 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute exponential term:**

$$\exp\!\left(-\frac{40^2}{2 \times 80^2}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1600}{12{,}800}\right) = \exp(-0.125) \approx 0.8825$$

**Step 4 — Compute prefactor:**

$$\frac{60}{\pi \times 3 \times 86.29 \times 80} = \frac{60}{65{,}067.6} \approx 9.22 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$C(400,0,0) \approx 9.22 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.8825 \approx 8.14 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 0.814 \text{ mg/m}^3}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

For **Class F (stable)**, vertical mixing is severely suppressed: $\sigma_z = 0.04\, x$ (5× smaller than Class C). At $x = 400$ m:

$$\sigma_z^{(F)} = 0.04 \times 400 = 16 \text{ m}$$

**Step 1 — Recompute exponential term:**

$$\exp\!\left(-\frac{40^2}{2 \times 16^2}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1600}{512}\right) = \exp(-3.125) \approx 0.0439$$

**Step 2 — New prefactor (assuming $\sigma_y$ also smaller, say $\sigma_y \approx 30$ m for Class F):**

$$\frac{60}{\pi \times 3 \times 30 \times 16} = \frac{60}{4523.9} \approx 1.326 \times 10^{-2} \text{ g/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$C^{(F)}(400,0,0) \approx 1.326 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.0439 \approx 5.82 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{C^{(F)} \approx 0.582 \text{ mg/m}^3}$$

**Discussion:** Despite a smaller prefactor (due to less lateral spread), the *much sharper* vertical concentration profile in stable conditions produces a slightly different ground-level concentration. However, the critical concern with stable conditions is **long-range plume travel at higher elevations** with low ground concentration — yet **dangerous fumigation events** can occur during morning transitions. For toxic gases like Cl₂ (IDLH = 10 ppm ≈ 29 mg/m³), both scenarios require monitoring.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Student Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the reflection term** — losing 2–3 marks when $z=0$.
> 2. **Using $u$ at stack height instead of reference height** without applying the wind profile power law.
> 3. **Mixing up Briggs constants** between classes — always cite the table.
> 4. **Not stating units** in the final answer — automatic 0.5 mark deduction per occurrence.
> 5. **Skipping the critical-condition derivation** in $x_{max}$ problems — must show $\frac{dC}{dx}=0$ work.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Equation:** Gaussian Plume $C(x,y,z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\left[\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$
- **Ground-level simplification:** Set $z=0$ → prefactor doubles, single exponential in $H$.
- **Centerline simplification:** Set $y=0$ → Gaussian in $y$ disappears.
- **Stability Classes A–F** dictate dispersion parameter growth rates.
- **Briggs Formulas** are the de facto industry standard for $\sigma_y(x)$ and $\sigma_z(x)$.
- **Effective height** $H = h_s + \Delta h$ — never use physical stack height alone for buoyant releases.
- **Maximum ground concentration** occurs where $\sigma_z = \dfrac{H}{\sqrt{2}}$.
- **Reflection term** (image source) is mandatory when the receptor is at ground level.
- **Wind profile power law** $u(z) = u_{ref}\left(\dfrac{z}{z_{ref}}\right)^{p}$ corrects measured $u_{10}$ to actual stack height.
- **Always compare** predicted $C$ values against **ERPG / AEGL / IDLH** for toxicological interpretation.
- **Assumptions to state upfront:** steady-state, constant $u$, conservative pollutant, no deposition, no chemical reaction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probit Equation for Fire, Explosion and Toxic Gas Release
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITUM RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Probit Equation for Fire, Explosion and Toxic Gas Release

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Probit Equation** (Probability Unit Equation) is a statistically derived dose-response relationship used in Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) to convert the physical intensity of a hazardous event (thermal radiation, blast overpressure, or toxic gas concentration) into the **probability of a specified harmful outcome** (usually fatality or a defined injury level) for exposed personnel.

The general canonical form is:

$$
Y = a + b \cdot \ln(V)
$$

Where:
- $Y$ = Probit value (a transformed probability unit, dimensionless).
- $V$ = Dose or intensity variable (e.g., $t \cdot I^{4/3}$, $\Delta P$, $C^{n} \cdot t$).
- $a, b$ = Empirically fitted constants specific to the hazard type.
- $\ln$ = Natural logarithm (base $e \approx 2.718$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** The Probit equation is the bridge between **physical consequence modelling** (heat flux, overpressure, concentration fields) and **human vulnerability assessment** (probability of lethality or injury). It is the cornerstone of QRA in chemical process industries.

The percentage probability of fatality $P$ is then obtained from $Y$ via the cumulative normal distribution:

$$
P(\%) = 50 \times \left[ 1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left( \frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}} \right) \right]
$$

where $\operatorname{erf}$ is the **error function**, and $P = 50\%$ corresponds to $Y = 5$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a stadium full of 1,000 people standing at different distances from a roaring bonfire. Some feel scorching heat; others barely feel warmth. The Probit equation is like a **calibrated "danger translator"** — it takes the *recipe* of hazard (intensity × duration) and tells you: *"Out of every 100 exposed persons under these exact conditions, X are expected to be fatally injured."* It is the universal language through which engineers convert engineering units (W/m², kPa, ppm) into the language of *risk percentage*.

The **Probit scale** is essentially a stretched version of the percentage scale:
- A Probit value of **2.67 ≈ 1 % fatality**.
- A Probit value of **5.00 = 50 % fatality** (the statistical "midpoint").
- A Probit value of **6.28 ≈ 90 % fatality**.

This transformation linearizes the S-shaped (sigmoidal) dose-response curve into a straight line, which is much easier to handle mathematically and graphically.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics in Probit Calculations:**
> - **Thermal intensity** $I$: in $\mathrm{W/m^2}$ (watts per square metre).
> - **Exposure time** $t$: in seconds (s).
> - **Peak side-on overpressure** $\Delta P$: in Pa (SI) or psi (imperial).
> - **Toxic concentration** $C$: in ppm (parts per million by volume).
> - **Empirical constants** $a$ and $b$: dimensionless, fitted from accident/laboratory data.
> - **Universal**: a Probit value of $Y = 5$ always corresponds to **50 % probability**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why and How the Probit Equation Works

### The Logic Behind the Transformation

**Step 1 — Dose Definition:**
A single engineering quantity cannot describe harm. Burn severity, for example, depends on **both** intensity and time. Hence a *dose variable* $V$ is constructed that combines them (e.g., $V = t \cdot I^{4/3}$ for thermal radiation).

**Step 2 — Empirical Fitting:**
Laboratory and accident data (animal experiments, Hiroshima–Nagasaki survivors, Piper Alpha, Flixborough) are used to fit constants $a$ and $b$ in $Y = a + b \ln(V)$ such that $Y = 5$ corresponds to 50 % response at known toxic doses or blast loads.

**Step 3 — Probability Conversion:**
Since biological response follows a roughly normal distribution when plotted against $\ln(V)$, the Probit transformation converts the cumulative normal curve into a linear expression.

**Step 4 — Real-World Utility:**
Once $P(\%)$ is known for a given location, it is multiplied by the **population density** at that location to estimate **individual risk** and combined with event frequency to compute **societal risk** (F-N curves).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet for KTU Examinations**

| **Hazard Type** | **Dose Variable $V$** | **Probit Equation $Y = a + b \ln(V)$** | **Outcome** | **Units of $V$** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fire – Thermal radiation** | $V = t \cdot I^{4/3}$ | $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln(V)$ | 1st-degree burn fatality (TNO) | $(W/m^2)^{4/3} \cdot s$ |
| **Fire – Thermal radiation (alt.)** | $V = t \cdot I^{4/3}$ | $Y = -39.83 + 3.0188 \ln(V)$ | Lethality (Eisenberg) | $(W/m^2)^{4/3} \cdot s$ |
| **Explosion – Lung haemorrhage** | $V = \Delta P$ | $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(V)$ | Lung haemorrhage death (Eisenberg) | Pa |
| **Explosion – Eardrum rupture** | $V = \Delta P$ | $Y = -15.6 + 1.93 \ln(V)$ | Eardrum rupture (Eisenberg) | Pa |
| **Explosion – Body translation** | $V = \Delta P$ | $Y = 5.13 + 1.47 \ln(V)$ | Whole-body translation fatality (HSE) | psi |
| **Toxic – Chlorine $\mathrm{Cl_2}$** | $V = C^{2} \cdot t$ | $Y = -19.27 + 3.686 \ln(V)$ | Lethality (TNO) | $\mathrm{ppm^2 \cdot s}$ |
| **Toxic – Ammonia $\mathrm{NH_3}$** | $V = C^{2} \cdot t$ | $Y = -30.0 + 1.04 \ln(V)$ | Lethality (TNO) | $\mathrm{ppm^2 \cdot s}$ |
| **Toxic – Hydrogen Sulphide $\mathrm{H_2S}$** | $V = C^{1.4} \cdot t$ | $Y = -31.42 + 3.008 \ln(V)$ | Lethality (TNO) | $\mathrm{ppm^{1.4} \cdot s}$ |
| **Toxic – Carbon Monoxide CO** | $V = C^{1} \cdot t$ | $Y = -37.0 + 3.0 \ln(V)$ | Lethality (TNO) | $\mathrm{ppm \cdot s}$ |
| **Probability Conversion** | — | $P(\%) = 50 \times [1 + \operatorname{erf}(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}})]$ | — | — |

**Standard Probit-to-Probability Reference Table (Most Used in KTU):**

| **Probit $Y$** | **% Probability $P$** | **Probit $Y$** | **% Probability $P$** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 2.67 | 1.0 | 5.00 | 50.0 |
| 3.72 | 10.0 | 5.52 | 70.0 |
| 4.16 | 15.0 | 6.00 | 84.1 |
| 4.48 | 31.7 | 6.28 | 90.0 |
| 4.83 | 42.0 | 7.33 | 99.0 |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Plant Layout:** Used to determine safe separation distances between units handling LPG, chlorine, ammonia.
- **Offshore Platforms:** Establishes Temporary Refuge Impairment (TRI) criteria using overpressure probits.
- **Land-Use Planning:** Defines consultation zones around facilities storing toxic gases under the Seveso Directive / Indian Manufacture Storage Import of Hazardous Chemical (MSIHC) Rules.
- **Emergency Response Planning:** Predicts lethality footprints for evacuating populations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of the Probit-to-Probability Conversion

The Probit variable $Y$ is defined so that $Y - 5$ follows a standard normal distribution with zero mean and unit standard deviation. Therefore:

$$
P(\text{response} \mid \ln V) = \Phi(Y - 5) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} \exp\!\left( -\frac{u^{2}}{2} \right) du
$$

Using the definition of the error function $\operatorname{erf}(z) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_{0}^{z} e^{-u^{2}} du$, and the identity $\Phi(x) = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[ 1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left( \frac{x}{\sqrt{2}} \right) \right]$, we obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\%) &= 100 \times \Phi(Y - 5) \\
      &= 100 \times \frac{1}{2}\!\left[ 1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left( \frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}} \right) \right] \\
      &= 50 \times \left[ 1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left( \frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}} \right) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical probability conversion used in every KTU problem.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Thermal Radiation Fatality (Fire)

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A pool fire produces a thermal radiation intensity of $I = 12.5 \,\mathrm{kW/m^2}$ at a location. An unprotected worker is exposed for $t = 20 \,\mathrm{s}$. Estimate the probability of fatality using the TNO thermal probit equation.

**Step 1 — Convert intensity to SI units**

$$
I = 12.5 \,\mathrm{kW/m^2} = 12{,}500 \,\mathrm{W/m^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the dose variable $V$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I^{4/3} &= (12{,}500)^{4/3} \\
        &= \left[ (12{,}500)^{1/3} \right]^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

The cube root of 12,500 is $(12{,}500)^{1/3} \approx 23.21$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I^{4/3} &\approx (23.21)^{4} \\
        &\approx 23.21^{2} \times 23.21^{2} \\
        &\approx 538.7 \times 538.7 \\
        &\approx 290{,}200 \,\mathrm{(W/m^2)^{4/3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now multiply by exposure time:

$$
V = t \cdot I^{4/3} = 20 \times 290{,}200 = 5.804 \times 10^{6} \,\mathrm{(W/m^2)^{4/3} \cdot s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Probit value**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= -14.9 + 2.56 \times \ln(V) \\
  &= -14.9 + 2.56 \times \ln(5.804 \times 10^{6}) \\
  &= -14.9 + 2.56 \times 15.574 \\
  &= -14.9 + 39.87 \\
  &= 24.97
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert Probit to probability**

Since $Y \gg 6.28$, $P \approx 100\%$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The worker is in extreme danger. Any unshielded exposure at $12.5 \,\mathrm{kW/m^2}$ for 20 s yields near-certain fatality. Mitigation must rely on shielding, evacuation, or PPE.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Explosion Overpressure Fatality

> **Problem:** A vapour cloud explosion produces a peak side-on overpressure of $\Delta P = 70 \,\mathrm{kPa}$. Estimate the probability of fatality due to lung haemorrhage using the Eisenberg probit.

**Step 1 — Convert to SI units (Pa)**

$$
\Delta P = 70 \,\mathrm{kPa} = 70{,}000 \,\mathrm{Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Eisenberg lung haemorrhage probit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= -77.1 + 6.91 \times \ln(\Delta P) \\
  &= -77.1 + 6.91 \times \ln(70{,}000) \\
  &= -77.1 + 6.91 \times 11.156 \\
  &= -77.1 + 77.09 \\
  &\approx -0.01
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert Probit to probability**

Since $Y \approx 0$ corresponds to a probability far below 1 %, by the standard Probit table, $P \approx 0\%$ (essentially zero fatality from lung haemorrhage).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even at 70 kPa (about 10 psi), lung haemorrhage is unlikely, although eardrum rupture is already a certainty (the eardrum probit $Y = -15.6 + 1.93 \ln(70{,}000) \approx +5.94$, i.e. $\approx 73\%$ rupture). This illustrates the importance of choosing the **correct endpoint probit** — examiners frequently mark off when students confuse endpoints.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Toxic Gas Release (Chlorine)

> **Problem:** During a chlorine leak, an operator is exposed to a concentration $C = 50 \,\mathrm{ppm}$ for $t = 10 \,\mathrm{min}$. Estimate the fatality probability using the TNO chlorine probit.

**Step 1 — Convert exposure time to seconds**

$$
t = 10 \,\mathrm{min} \times 60 = 600 \,\mathrm{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the dose variable**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= C^{2} \cdot t = (50)^{2} \times 600 \\
  &= 2{,}500 \times 600 = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \,\mathrm{ppm^{2} \cdot s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the chlorine probit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= -19.27 + 3.686 \times \ln(V) \\
  &= -19.27 + 3.686 \times \ln(1.5 \times 10^{6}) \\
  &= -19.27 + 3.686 \times 14.22 \\
  &= -19.27 + 52.41 \\
  &= 33.14
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert Probit to probability**

Since $Y \gg 7.33$, the probability of fatality is essentially **100 %**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A modest chlorine concentration (50 ppm, just above the IDLH threshold of 10 ppm) maintained for 10 minutes yields near-certain lethality. This is why chlorine plants demand extremely rapid isolation valves and gas-detection systems.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Step** | **Input Required** | **Computation** | **Output** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Hazard type (fire / explosion / toxic) | Identify correct $V$ definition | Dose formula |
| 2 | Source strength + dispersion / radiation model | Calculate field value ( $I$, $\Delta P$, $C$ ) at receptor | Field intensity |
| 3 | Exposure duration $t$ | $V = f(\text{intensity}, t)$ | Dose variable |
| 4 | Hazard-specific constants $a, b$ | $Y = a + b \ln(V)$ | Probit value |
| 5 | Cumulative normal / standard table | $P = 50[1 + \operatorname{erf}(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}})]$ | Probability % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Probit Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the end-to-end engineering workflow for applying the Probit equation inside a QRA study — from physical hazard modelling to societal risk curves.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Identification Fuel Inventory Process Conditions] --> B[Source Term Modelling Release Rate Discharge Coefficient]
    B --> C[Dispersion / Radiation Modelling Gaussian Plume Pool Fire BLEVE VCE]
    C --> D[Field Variable Computation Thermal Intensity I Overpressure dP Toxic Concentration C at Receptor]
    D --> E[Dose Variable Construction V equals t times I to the 4/3 OR V equals dP OR V equals C to the n times t]
    E --> F[Probit Equation Selection a plus b times ln V constants from TNO Eisenberg HSE TNO Toxic Tables]
    F --> G[Probit Value Y Output Dimensionless Number typically 0 to 8]
    G --> H[Probability Conversion P equals 50 times 1 plus erf of Y minus 5 over sqrt 2 OR Standard Table]
    H --> I[Individual Risk P multiplied by Event Frequency Per Year]
    H --> J[Societal Risk Aggregation Over Exposed Population F-N Curve]
    I --> K[Risk Informed Decision ALARP Demonstration Layout Design]
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Mapping of the Three Hazard Families

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Probit Equation Framework] --> B[Fire Hazards]
    A --> C[Explosion Hazards]
    A --> D[Toxic Release Hazards]
    B --> B1[Thermal Dose V equals t times I to the 4/3]
    B --> B2[Y equals minus 14.9 plus 2.56 ln V]
    C --> C1[Overpressure Dose V equals dP in Pa]
    C --> C2[Y equals minus 77.1 plus 6.91 ln V]
    D --> D1[Toxic Dose V equals C to the n times t]
    D --> D2[Y equals a plus b ln V varies per chemical]
    B1 --> B2
    C1 --> C2
    D1 --> D2
    B2 --> E[Probability of Fatality P percent]
    C2 --> E
    D2 --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first diagram is the *operational workflow* — what an engineer performs step by step inside a QRA software such as **SAFETI**, **PHAST**, or **EFFECTS**. The second diagram is a *taxonomic map* — showing how the same Probit framework branches into the three KTU syllabus hazard families, each with its own dose variable and empirical constants.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Define the Probit equation. Why is it used in Quantitative Risk Assessment?**

**Model Answer:**
The Probit equation is an empirically derived dose-response relationship of the form $Y = a + b \ln(V)$, where $Y$ is the probit value, $V$ is a dose variable combining hazard intensity and exposure duration, and $a, b$ are fitted constants. It is used in QRA because it:
1. Converts engineering units (heat flux, overpressure, concentration) into a **probability of fatality or injury** at a receptor location.
2. Provides a linear link between the **S-shaped dose-response curve** of biological systems and the **logarithmic dose variable**, simplifying risk calculations.
3. Forms the basis for **individual risk contours** and **societal risk (F-N) curves** required for land-use planning under the MSIHC Rules and Seveso-III Directive.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**State the probit equation for thermal radiation fatality and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer:**
The TNO thermal probit equation for fatality is $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln(V)$, where:
- $Y$ = Probit value (probability unit).
- $V = t \cdot I^{4/3}$ = thermal dose variable.
- $t$ = exposure duration in seconds.
- $I$ = thermal radiation intensity in $\mathrm{W/m^2}$.
- The exponent $4/3$ reflects the experimentally observed dependence of skin burn injury on heat flux and time.
- $-14.9$ and $2.56$ are empirical constants fitted from accident data; together they position the line so that $Y = 5$ (50 % fatality) occurs at the observed lethal thermal dose.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks for **(a)** using the wrong probit constants (e.g., Eisenberg vs TNO) without citing the reference, **(b)** mixing up units (psi vs Pa vs kPa), and **(c)** skipping the explicit Probit-to-probability conversion step. Always **show** the formula, plug values step by step, and quote the final probability.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the Probit value $Y$ and the percentage probability of fatality $P$. Use the standard normal distribution. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A worker in a refinery control room is exposed to a thermal radiation intensity of $I = 25 \,\mathrm{kW/m^2}$ for a duration of $t = 30 \,\mathrm{s}$. Using the TNO thermal probit equation $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln(t \cdot I^{4/3})$, calculate the probability of fatality. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

By definition, the probit variable $Y - 5$ follows a standard normal distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 1. The cumulative probability of response is therefore:

$$
P = \Phi(Y - 5) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} e^{-u^{2}/2} \, du
$$

Express this in terms of the error function using the identity $\operatorname{erf}(x) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_{0}^{x} e^{-u^{2}} du$, and noting $\int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{-u^{2}/2} du = \sqrt{\pi/2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(Y - 5) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{-u^{2}/2} du + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{0}^{Y-5} e^{-u^{2}/2} du \\
            &= \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \operatorname{erf}\!\left( \frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying by 100 to express as a percentage:

$$
\boxed{P(\%) = 50 \times \left[ 1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left( \frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}} \right) \right]}
$$

This derivation earns full marks provided the student explicitly writes the substitution step $\int_{-\infty}^{0} = \frac{1}{2}$ and shows the connection to the Gaussian integral.

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

Step 1 — Convert intensity to SI:

$$
I = 25{,}000 \,\mathrm{W/m^2}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the thermal dose $V$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I^{4/3} &= (25{,}000)^{4/3} \\
        &= \left[ (25{,}000)^{1/3} \right]^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

$(25{,}000)^{1/3} \approx 29.24$; squaring gives $(29.24)^{2} \approx 855.0$; squaring again gives $855.0^{2} \approx 731{,}000$.

$$
V = t \cdot I^{4/3} = 30 \times 731{,}000 = 2.193 \times 10^{7} \,\mathrm{(W/m^2)^{4/3} \cdot s}
$$

Step 3 — Compute the Probit value:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= -14.9 + 2.56 \ln(2.193 \times 10^{7}) \\
  &= -14.9 + 2.56 \times 16.905 \\
  &= -14.9 + 43.28 \\
  &= 28.38
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Convert to probability:

Since $Y \gg 7.33$, by the standard probit table, $\operatorname{erf}((28.38-5)/\sqrt{2}) \to 1$ and therefore:

$$
P(\%) = 50[1 + 1] = 100\%
$$

**Result:** The probability of fatality is essentially **100 %** — the worker would be fatally injured within seconds at this heat flux.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled sketch, explain the dose-response curve and how the Probit transformation linearises it. State at least three standard probit equations used for thermal, overpressure, and toxic hazards. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A chlorine storage vessel ruptures, releasing toxic $\mathrm{Cl_2}$ that disperses such that a residential colony 500 m downwind experiences a sustained concentration of $C = 30 \,\mathrm{ppm}$ for $t = 20 \,\mathrm{min}$. Using the TNO chlorine probit $Y = -19.27 + 3.686 \ln(C^{2} \cdot t)$, calculate the fatality probability. Comment on whether the colony should be evacuated. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theoretical (7 Marks):**

The **dose-response curve** plots the percentage probability of a biological response (e.g., fatality) against the logarithm of the dose variable $V$. It is intrinsically **sigmoidal** because:
- At low doses, almost no response occurs (lower plateau).
- At intermediate doses, response rises steeply (linear middle region).
- At high doses, response saturates near 100 % (upper plateau).

The Probit transformation substitutes the y-axis (probability %) with $Y = 5 + \Phi^{-1}(P/100)$, where $\Phi^{-1}$ is the inverse of the cumulative standard normal. This **straightens the S-curve into a line** whose slope is $b$ and intercept is $a$ in $Y = a + b \ln(V)$. Students should sketch both the sigmoid and its linearised form for full credit.

Three standard probit equations:

1. **Thermal radiation:** $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln(t \cdot I^{4/3})$ — TNO, $I$ in $\mathrm{W/m^2}$, $t$ in s.
2. **Explosion overpressure (lung haemorrhage):** $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(\Delta P)$ — Eisenberg, $\Delta P$ in Pa.
3. **Toxic release (chlorine):** $Y = -19.27 + 3.686 \ln(C^{2} \cdot t)$ — TNO, $C$ in ppm, $t$ in s.

**(b) Numerical (7 Marks):**

Step 1 — Convert exposure time:

$$
t = 20 \times 60 = 1200 \,\mathrm{s}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the chlorine dose:

$$
V = C^{2} \cdot t = (30)^{2} \times 1200 = 900 \times 1200 = 1.08 \times 10^{6} \,\mathrm{ppm^{2} \cdot s}
$$

Step 3 — Compute the Probit value:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= -19.27 + 3.686 \ln(1.08 \times 10^{6}) \\
  &= -19.27 + 3.686 \times 13.892 \\
  &= -19.27 + 51.21 \\
  &= 31.94
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Convert to probability:

$$
P(\%) = 50[1 + \operatorname{erf}((31.94 - 5)/\sqrt{2})] \approx 100\%
$$

**Comment:** The colony should be **immediately evacuated** because the fatality probability is essentially 100 %. Emergency response plans for chlorine installations must include **Early Warning Sirens**, **gas-detection networks**, and **pre-defined exclusion zones** of at least 1 km for sustained releases.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3**

- The **Probit equation** is always of the form $Y = a + b \ln(V)$ with constants fitted from empirical data.
- $Y = 5$ **always** corresponds to **50 % probability** of the specified endpoint.
- The dose variable $V$ is constructed from the physics of the hazard:
  - Thermal: $V = t \cdot I^{4/3}$.
  - Overpressure: $V = \Delta P$.
  - Toxic: $V = C^{n} \cdot t$ ($n$ varies per chemical).
- **Reference sources for constants:** TNO Green Book (NL), Eisenberg (US), HSE (UK), CCPS (US). Always cite the source in your answer.
- **Unit traps to avoid:**
  - Eisenberg overpressure → **Pa**, not kPa or psi.
  - HSE overpressure → **psi**.
  - Toxic concentrations → **ppm**.
  - Thermal intensity → **W/m²**.
- **Probability conversion** uses the cumulative normal distribution; in KTU exams you may either compute via $\operatorname{erf}$ or read from the standard probit table.
- A Probit value **above 7.33 ≈ 99 % fatality** — practically certain death.
- A Probit value **below 2.67 ≈ 1 % fatality** — practically safe.
- The Probit framework produces **individual risk** ($P \times f$) and **societal risk** ($F$-$N$ curve), the two deliverables of any QRA study.
- **Common mistakes:** (i) using $a, b$ from the wrong reference, (ii) forgetting the $(Y-5)/\sqrt{2}$ factor in the erf, (iii) confusing fatality with injury endpoints, (iv) not converting minutes to seconds.
- **Examiner favourite topics:** (i) Derivation of the Probit–probability relation, (ii) Worked numerical on chlorine/ammonia/CO, (iii) Comparison of fire vs explosion vs toxic probits, (iv) Role of probit in $F$-$N$ curve construction.
- **Memorise three probit constants:** Thermal (-14.9, 2.56); Chlorine (-19.27, 3.686); Lung haemorrhage (-77.1, 6.91).
- Always express the **final answer in % probability** with one decimal place; round $Y$ to two decimal places.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quantitative Risk Assessment - Procedure for QRA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quantitative Risk Assessment - Procedure for QRA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of QRA and its Procedure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** is a formal, systematic, and structured engineering methodology used to numerically estimate the **probability (frequency)** of potential accidents and the **magnitude of their consequences** (in terms of fatalities, injuries, property damage, or environmental impact) for an industrial facility, process, or operation.

The **QRA Procedure** refers to the standardized, step-by-step framework that guides an analyst from the initial definition of the problem to the final risk evaluation and risk-reduction recommendations. It is a **risk-informed decision-making tool** widely endorsed by safety regulatory bodies such as the **Health and Safety Executive (HSE), UK**, the **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)**, and **API (American Petroleum Institute)**.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT754)**, the QRA procedure is the central pillar of the course. It transforms qualitative hazard identification into measurable risk values such as **Individual Risk (IR)** and **Societal Risk (F-N curves)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** QRA answers two fundamental questions for every hazard:
> 1. **How often can it happen?** → Frequency / Probability
> 2. **How bad can it be if it happens?** → Consequence Severity
> Risk (R) = Frequency (f) × Consequence (C)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are planning a **long road trip from Kerala to Delhi** in your car. Before starting, you instinctively (and informally) think like a risk analyst:

| Real-World Analogy (Road Trip Planning) | Equivalent QRA Concept |
|---|---|
| Listing possible dangers (potholes, rain, tyre burst) | **Hazard Identification** |
| Asking: "How likely is a tyre burst on a national highway?" | **Frequency Analysis** |
| Asking: "If a tyre bursts at 100 km/h, what is the worst outcome?" | **Consequence Analysis** |
| Combining likelihood × severity to decide if the trip is safe | **Risk Estimation** |
| Deciding to install a spare tyre, check brakes, etc. | **Risk Evaluation & Mitigation** |

A **QRA procedure** simply formalizes this thinking into a rigorous mathematical engineering exercise for complex industrial systems like **LNG terminals, refineries, offshore platforms, and chemical plants**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for QRA Procedure — "SHIRE"**:
> **S** – Scope Definition
> **H** – Hazard Identification
> **I** – Initiating Event / Frequency Estimation
> **R** – Risk Calculation (Frequency × Consequence)
> **E** – Evaluation against Acceptance Criteria

---

### 1.3 Why is the QRA Procedure Essential?

- **Compliance:** Mandatory under **OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119)** and Indian **Factories Act / Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC, 1989)** for chemical process industries.
- **Land-use Planning:** Provides **Consultative Distance** for settlements around hazardous installations.
- **Cost-Benefit Analysis:** Justifies **ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** expenditure for safety measures.
- **Emergency Planning:** Informs **On-Site** and **Off-Site Emergency Plans** (OSE/OSE).
- **Insurance & Liability:** Quantifies risk exposure for insurance underwriting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Reference Values (Industry Benchmarks):**
> - **Acceptable Individual Risk (IR):** **≤ 10⁻⁶ per year** (HSE, UK — upper tolerable limit for workers)
> - **Tolerable Societal Risk:** **F-N curve slope = −1** (CPR-12E, Netherlands)
> - **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR):** < **1.0** (HSE benchmark)
> - **Acceptable Frequency of Major Hazard Event:** < **10⁻⁴ per year**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 11-Step Standard QRA Procedure (KTU 2024 Aligned)

The QRA procedure is a **closed-loop, iterative engineering process**. Each step's output becomes the next step's input. Below is the KTU-aligned detailed breakdown:

#### **Step 1: Define the Scope, Objective, and Boundaries of the Study**
- Identify the **physical boundary** of the system (e.g., a hydrocarbon storage tank farm, a hydrocracker unit, an offshore platform).
- Decide the **type of risk** to be quantified: **risk to people (fatalities/injuries)**, **environmental risk (tonnes of oil spilled)**, **property/asset risk ($)**, or **business interruption risk**.
- Decide the **level of detail**: **Screening-level QRA (Level 1)**, **Intermediate QRA (Level 2)**, or **Detailed/Site-Specific QRA (Level 3)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common mistake is treating the entire plant as one unit. Always define **plant node, equipment item, and pipeline segment** boundaries explicitly.

#### **Step 2: Hazard Identification (HAZID)**
Identify what can go wrong using one or more of:
- **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** — for process deviations.
- **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** — for equipment failures.
- **What-If Analysis / Checklists** — for preliminary screening.
- **Historical accident data review** (e.g., **HSE Major Hazard Incident Database, eMARS, TNO FACTS**).

Output: A list of **Top Events / Major Hazard Scenarios** such as:
- **VCE (Vapour Cloud Explosion)** from hydrocarbon release
- **BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion)** of LPG tank
- **Pool Fire / Jet Fire / Flash Fire**
- **Toxic gas dispersion** (e.g., Cl₂, NH₃, H₂S)
- **Runaway reaction / Unconfined Vapour Cloud Explosion**

#### **Step 3: Select Initiating Events and Build Event Sequences**
- For each hazard, identify the **initiating event (IE)** and construct the chain of events that could escalate.
- Use **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** to model the propagation from initiating event → intermediate events → final outcomes.
- Use **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** for the inverse (top-down Boolean logic) to compute system failure frequency.

#### **Step 4: Estimate Frequencies / Probabilities of Initiating Events**
Sources of failure data:
- **Generic Industry Databases:** **Oreda (Offshore and Onshore Reliability Data), TNO Purple Book, CCPS PERD, IEEE Std 500, NUREG, Hydrocarbon Release Database (HCRD)**.
- **Plant-Specific Data:** Maintenance records, trip logs, near-miss reports.
- **API RP 581** for RBI-based component failure frequencies.

Common initiating event frequencies (order of magnitude):

| Initiating Event | Typical Frequency (per year) |
|---|---|
| Major pipe rupture (full bore) | 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁶ |
| Large flange gasket leak | 10⁻² to 10⁻³ |
| Pump failure (mechanical seal) | 10⁻¹ to 10⁰ |
| Cooling water failure | 10⁻¹ to 1 |
| Operator error | 10⁻¹ to 10⁻² per task |
| Lightning strike | 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁴ |
| Major tanker collision (offshore) | 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ |

#### **Step 5: Consequence Modelling — Source, Dispersion, Fire, Explosion, Toxic**
Use software tools such as **PHAST, FRED, SAFETI, ALOHA, EFFECTS, KFX** to estimate:
- **Release rate** (kg/s) of hazardous material.
- **Pool spread and evaporation.**
- **Jet dispersion (passive, dense, neutral).**
- **Thermal radiation from fires** (kW/m²).
- **Overpressure from explosions** (kPa or bar).
- **Toxic concentration footprint** (ppm, IDLH, ERPG levels).

#### **Step 6: Estimate Conditional Probabilities of Branches in Event Tree**
For each branch (e.g., **immediate ignition, delayed ignition, no ignition**), assign a probability. Example (for hydrocarbon release):

| Branch Event | Probability |
|---|---|
| Immediate Ignition | 0.10 to 0.30 |
| Delayed Ignition | 0.20 to 0.50 |
| No Ignition (vapour cloud disperses) | 0.40 to 0.70 |

#### **Step 7: Calculate Risk Metrics (Individual Risk & Societal Risk)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Individual Risk (IR):** The frequency at which an individual at a specific location is expected to experience a specified level of harm (usually fatality) from the realization of the hazards under study. Units: **per year (yr⁻¹)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Societal Risk:** The relationship between the frequency of an accident and the number of people affected in a given population. Represented as **F-N curves** on a log-log plot.

#### **Step 8: Summation of Risks — Aggregated Risk**
Sum individual scenario risks over **all initiating events × all branches × all outcomes** to obtain the total plant risk.

#### **Step 9: Comparison with Risk Acceptance Criteria**
Compare computed IR and F-N curve against:
- **HSE R2P2 framework** (UK)
- **CPR-12E** (Netherlands)
- **AIChE/CCPS Guidelines**
- **National/local tolerability criteria.**

#### **Step 10: Risk Reduction / Mitigation Recommendations**
If risk is **intolerable**, propose additional **Barriers (B)**: 
- **Passive:** Bunds, blast walls, dykes, fireproofing.
- **Active:** ESD (Emergency Shutdown), PSV (Pressure Safety Valve), foam pourers, gas detection, deluge systems.
- **Procedural:** Permit-to-work, training, maintenance discipline.
- **Inherent Safety:** Minimization, substitution, moderation, simplification (**Kletz's principles**).

#### **Step 11: Documentation, Sensitivity Analysis, and Review**
- **Sensitivity analysis:** Identify dominant contributors (often 80% of risk comes from 20% of scenarios — **Pareto principle**).
- **Uncertainty analysis** (Monte Carlo simulation if needed).
- **Peer review** and **management of change (MOC)** documentation.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` in all tables to avoid markdown parser conflicts with absolute value notation.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Units | Application in QRA Procedure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = f \times C$ | R = Risk, f = Frequency, C = Consequence | yr⁻¹ × consequences | **Fundamental risk equation** — backbone of every QRA |
| 2 | $IR_{x,y} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \cdot P_i \cdot p(x,y \vert E_i)$ | Individual Risk at location (x,y) | yr⁻¹ | **Step 7** — Risk contour (IR-isopleth) plotting |
| 3 | $P(E_i) = \prod_{j} q_j$ | Event probability in ETA (product of branch probs) | dimensionless | **Step 6** — Event tree quantification |
| 4 | $Q_{system} = Q_1 + Q_2 - Q_1 \cdot Q_2$ | OR-gate unreliability | dimensionless | **Step 3 (FTA)** — Top event frequency |
| 5 | $Q_{AND} = Q_A \cdot Q_B$ | AND-gate unreliability | dimensionless | **Step 3 (FTA)** — Common cause failures |
| 6 | $\text{FAR} = \frac{N_f \times 10^8}{T_h}$ | FAR = Fatal Accident Rate, $N_f$ = fatalities, $T_h$ = person-hours worked | fatalities per 10⁸ person-hours | **Step 9** — Occupational risk benchmark |
| 7 | $\text{LTIR} = \frac{L \times 10^5}{T_h}$ | LTIR = Lost Time Injury Rate | injuries per 10⁵ person-hours | Step 9 — Injury risk metric |
| 8 | $N_{L_k} = \sum_i f_i \cdot N_{i,k}$ | Expected annual loss $L_k$ category | fatalities/yr or $/yr | **Step 7** — Aggregated loss expectancy |
| 9 | $T = \frac{1}{f}$ | Return period of an event | years | **Step 7** — Risk communication metric |
| 10 | $R = \sum_i f_i \cdot C_i$ (scalar) | Total risk summation | mixed | **Step 8** — Aggregated QRA |
| 11 | $\frac{dF}{dN} = k \cdot N^{-m}$ | F-N curve power law slope | slope $m$ | **Step 7, 9** — Societal risk criterion |
| 12 | $f_{ij} = f_i \cdot P_{i \to j}$ | Frequency of consequence outcome j from event i | yr⁻¹ | **Step 7** — Frequency-consequence pairs |
| 13 | $\text{PPE} = 1 - e^{- \lambda t}$ | PPE (Probability of Equipment failure) | dimensionless | **Step 4** — Equipment failure probability over time |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of the QRA Procedure in Engineering

- **Refineries & Petrochemicals:** Used by **Reliance, IOCL, BPCL** to demonstrate ALARP for **MoEF&CC (Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change)** statutory clearances.
- **Offshore Oil & Gas:** Mandatory under **API RP 14C** and **UK HSE SAD/HAS** for offshore installations in the **Bombay High, KG Basin, or North Sea**.
- **LNG Terminals:** Critical for siting **FSRU (Floating Storage Regasification Unit)** and designing exclusion zones.
- **Pipeline Crossings:** Justifies **depth of cover, wall thickness, hydrostatic test pressure** per **ASME B31.4 / B31.8**.
- **Nuclear Safety:** Forms the basis of **Level-1, Level-2, and Level-3 Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA)** in **BARC/AERB** practice.
- **Transportation of Hazardous Materials (Hazmat):** Used to define **routes, night-driving restrictions, and Emergency Response Centres**.

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU 2024 ESE, examiners often award extra marks for **citing specific industry codes (e.g., API 581, Purple Book, OREDA)**. Always mention at least one code in your answer.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Event Tree to Frequency of Consequence Outcome

**Problem Statement:**
A 100 m³ pressurized **LPG bullet** can develop a **20 mm hole leak** with a frequency of $f_1 = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ /yr. The released LPG may either (a) ignite immediately as a **jet fire**, (b) be delayed ignited causing a **VCE**, or (c) disperse without ignition. Conditional ignition probabilities are:

- **P(Immediate Ignition)** = 0.20
- **P(Delayed Ignition)** = 0.30
- **P(No Ignition)** = 0.50

The estimated consequences (in **fatalities at the boundary, N**) are:
- **Jet fire:** $N_{JF} = 2$ fatalities
- **VCE:** $N_{VCE} = 15$ fatalities
- **No ignition (toxic / asphyxiation):** $N_{NI} = 1$ fatality

Derive the **frequency of each consequence outcome** and the **expected annual fatalities (N̄)**.

---

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step A — Frequency of Immediate Ignition Outcome ($f_{JF}$):**
The frequency of the consequence outcome = frequency of initiating event × conditional probability of that branch.

$$
f_{JF} = f_1 \times P(\text{Immediate Ignition})
$$

$$
f_{JF} = (1 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{yr}^{-1}) \times 0.20 = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step B — Frequency of Delayed Ignition / VCE Outcome ($f_{VCE}$):**

$$
f_{VCE} = f_1 \times P(\text{Delayed Ignition})
$$

$$
f_{VCE} = (1 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{yr}^{-1}) \times 0.30 = 3.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step C — Frequency of No Ignition Outcome ($f_{NI}$):**

$$
f_{NI} = f_1 \times P(\text{No Ignition})
$$

$$
f_{NI} = (1 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{yr}^{-1}) \times 0.50 = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step D — Verification (Probabilities must sum to unity):**

$$
P_{JF} + P_{VCE} + P_{NI} = 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.50 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step E — Compute Expected Annual Fatalities $\bar{N}$:**

$$
\bar{N} = f_{JF} \cdot N_{JF} + f_{VCE} \cdot N_{VCE} + f_{NI} \cdot N_{NI}
$$

$$
\bar{N} = (2.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 2) + (3.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 15) + (5.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 1)
$$

$$
\bar{N} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} + 45.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.0 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\bar{N} = 54.0 \times 10^{-4} = 5.4 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{fatalities/yr}
$$

**Step F — Interpretation:**

$$
\bar{N} = 5.4 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{fatalities/yr} \iff T = \frac{1}{\bar{N}} \approx 185\ \text{years between statistical fatalities}
$$

This value can be **plotted on an F-N curve** for societal risk evaluation and **compared to the acceptance criteria** (e.g., the Dutch CPR-12E line of slope −1).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fault Tree Quantification Using Boolean Algebra

**Problem Statement:**
A **gas detection + shutdown (SDS) system** is designed to isolate a flammable release. The system fails (top event T) if either:
- **Path A:** Both gas detectors (D1 AND D2) fail to detect, AND the logic solver (LS) fails.
- **Path B:** The shutdown valve (SDV) fails to close, OR the manual isolation (MI) fails (operator fails to act).

Given data:

$$
Q_{D1} = Q_{D2} = Q_{LS} = 0.01,\quad Q_{SDV} = 0.005,\quad Q_{MI} = 0.02
$$

Find the **unreliability (probability of failure on demand) of the top event T**, assuming independence.

---

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step A — Build the Fault Tree Boolean Equation:**

$$
T = (A_{path}) + (B_{path})
$$

$$
A_{path} = D1 \cap D2 \cap LS \qquad B_{path} = SDV \cup MI
$$

$$
T = (D1 \cdot D2 \cdot LS) + SDV + MI - (D1 \cdot D2 \cdot LS \cdot SDV) - \ldots
$$

For rare events (small probabilities), the cross-product terms are negligible. Use the **rare-event approximation**:

$$
Q_T \approx Q_A + Q_B
$$

**Step B — Compute $Q_A$ (Path A: All three components must fail):**

$$
Q_A = Q_{D1} \cdot Q_{D2} \cdot Q_{LS}
$$

$$
Q_A = 0.01 \times 0.01 \times 0.01 = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step C — Compute $Q_B$ (Path B: OR-gate):**

$$
Q_B = Q_{SDV} + Q_{MI} - Q_{SDV} \cdot Q_{MI}
$$

$$
Q_B = 0.005 + 0.02 - (0.005 \times 0.02)
$$

$$
Q_B = 0.025 - 0.0001 = 0.0249
$$

**Step D — Combine via OR-gate (Top Event):**

$$
Q_T \approx Q_A + Q_B = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} + 0.0249
$$

$$
Q_T \approx 0.024901 \approx 2.49 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step E — Interpretation:**
The dominant contributor is **Path B (OR-gate)**. The probability of failure is dominated by the **shutdown valve** and the **manual isolation failure**, contributing ≈ 99.99% of total top-event unreliability. The double detector + logic solver path is **negligible**. Engineering intervention should therefore focus on **improving SDV reliability** and **operator training** rather than detector redundancy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight (KTU High-Yield):** The dominant contributor in a QRA is rarely the most complex safeguard — it is usually the **simplest, most-overlooked component**. This is why **Pareto analysis (80/20 rule)** is mandatory in QRA.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — QRA Procedure at a Glance

| Step | QRA Procedure Stage | Primary Input | Method / Tool | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Define Scope | Plant P&ID, layout | Document review | Study boundary document |
| 2 | Hazard Identification | Process info, chemicals | HAZOP, FMEA, Checklist | List of Top Events |
| 3 | Select Initiating Events & Build Logic | Top Events, safeguards | ETA, FTA | Event trees / fault trees |
| 4 | Estimate Frequencies | OREDA, plant data, API 581 | Statistical / Bayesian | $f_i$ in yr⁻¹ |
| 5 | Consequence Modelling | Release scenarios | PHAST, FRED, ALOHA, KFX | Hazard zones (m, kW/m², ppm) |
| 6 | Branch Probabilities | Event trees | Expert judgment + TNO Purple Book | $P_j$ values |
| 7 | Calculate Risk | $f_i$, $P_j$, $C_{ij}$ | $R = f \times C$ | IR maps, F-N points |
| 8 | Aggregate Risk | All scenarios | Summation | Total risk profile |
| 9 | Compare to Criteria | Risk profile | HSE R2P2, CPR-12E | Tolerable / ALARP zone |
| 10 | Recommend Mitigation | Risk gap | Inherent safety + BPCS/SIS | New risk profile |
| 11 | Sensitivity, Documentation, Review | All outputs | Pareto, Monte Carlo | Final QRA report |
