## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA). List any four steps involved in the QRA procedure.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Definition (2 Marks):** Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) is a systematic engineering methodology that uses mathematical and statistical techniques to numerically estimate the **frequency (probability)** of hazardous events and the **magnitude of their consequences** (e.g., fatalities, injuries, financial loss, environmental damage) for an industrial facility, in order to support **risk-informed decision-making**.

**Any four steps (1 Mark — 0.25 each):**
1. Define the scope, objective, and boundaries of the study.
2. Identify hazards using HAZOP, FMEA, or checklists.
3. Select initiating events and develop event trees / fault trees.
4. Estimate frequencies using databases such as OREDA, TNO Purple Book.
5. Model consequences (dispersion, fire, explosion, toxic release).
6. Calculate risk as **R = f × C**; plot IR contours and F-N curves.
7. Compare with acceptance criteria and recommend mitigation.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **What is the difference between Individual Risk (IR) and Societal Risk? State one acceptance criterion for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Aspect | Individual Risk (IR) | Societal Risk (SR) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition (1 Mark) | Frequency at which an individual at a specific location experiences a specified level of harm (usually fatality) | Relationship between frequency of accidents and the number of people affected in the population |
| Representation (1 Mark) | IR isopleths / contours on a site map (yr⁻¹) | F-N curve (log-log plot of cumulative frequency vs. number of fatalities) |
| Acceptance Criterion (1 Mark) | HSE: ≤ **10⁻⁶ /yr** for public; ≤ **10⁻⁴ /yr** for workers (upper tolerable) | CPR-12E (Dutch): F-N slope of **−1** for the limit line, **−2** for the ALARP region |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 Choice]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **standard 11-step procedure for conducting a Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** for a hydrocarbon processing facility. State the relevant data sources for frequency estimation and consequence modelling. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A **25 mm leak** in a benzene storage line has an initiating frequency of **$5 \times 10^{-3}$ /yr**. Event-tree branch probabilities are: immediate ignition (jet fire) = 0.15, delayed ignition (VCE) = 0.25, no ignition (toxic vapour cloud) = 0.60. Fatalities estimated: jet fire = 1; VCE = 8; toxic cloud = 3. Calculate the **frequency of each outcome** and the **expected annual fatalities (N̄)**. Comment on the **return period**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — 11-Step QRA Procedure (7 Marks):**

A structured 11-step procedure as covered in **Section 2.1** of these notes is to be presented. The model answer should be summarized as:

1. **Define scope, objective, boundary, and depth of study.** (0.5 Mark)
2. **Hazard identification (HAZID)** using HAZOP, FMEA, What-If, accident history. (0.5 Mark)
3. **Initiating event selection + Event Tree Analysis (ETA) + Fault Tree Analysis (FTA).** (0.5 Mark)
4. **Frequency estimation** using **OREDA, TNO Purple Book, CCPS PERD, API RP 581, plant-specific maintenance data, IEEE Std 500.** (1 Mark)
5. **Consequence modelling** using **PHAST, FRED, ALOHA, EFFECTS, KFX, SAFETI** for dispersion, pool fire, jet fire, VCE, BLEVE, toxic release. (1 Mark)
6. **Branch / conditional probability assignment** (immediate, delayed, no ignition; weather classes; etc.). (0.5 Mark)
7. **Risk calculation** — $R = f \times C$; compute **Individual Risk isopleths** and **F-N curves**. (0.5 Mark)
8. **Aggregation of risk** across all scenarios. (0.5 Mark)
9. **Comparison with acceptance criteria** — **HSE R2P2, CPR-12E, AIChE/CCPS, ALARP.** (0.5 Mark)
10. **Risk mitigation recommendation** — inherent safety, BPCS, SIS, procedural, passive protection. (0.5 Mark)
11. **Sensitivity analysis, uncertainty analysis, documentation, peer review, MOC update.** (0.5 Mark)

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute frequency of each outcome (3 Marks):**

$$
f_{JF} = f \cdot P_{JF} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.15 = 7.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

$$
f_{VCE} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.25 = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

$$
f_{TOX} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.60 = 3.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Verification:** $0.15 + 0.25 + 0.60 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 2: Calculate expected annual fatalities $\bar{N}$ (3 Marks):**

$$
\bar{N} = (7.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1) + (1.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 8) + (3.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 3)
$$

$$
\bar{N} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-2} + 9.0 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\bar{N} = 0.00075 + 0.01000 + 0.00900 = 0.01975
$$

$$
\bar{N} \approx 1.975 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{fatalities per year}
$$

**Step 3: Return period (1 Mark):**

$$
T = \frac{1}{\bar{N}} = \frac{1}{0.01975} \approx 50.6\ \text{years}
$$

**Interpretation:** On average, one statistical fatality is expected approximately **once every 50 years**. This value should be plotted on the F-N curve (N=1, F=0.01975/yr) and checked against the **ALARP criterion** (typically slope −1 to −2 on the F-N plot per CPR-12E).

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – KTU University Exam, Dec 2024]**
> **(a)** With the help of a **suitable flowchart**, describe the **complete QRA procedure**. Explain the role of **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** and **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** in step 3 of the QRA procedure. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A fire protection system has three independent barriers: **B1, B2, B3** with unreliabilities $Q_{B1} = 0.02$, $Q_{B2} = 0.03$, $Q_{B3} = 0.05$. The barriers are configured in **series**. The initiating event frequency is **$1 \times 10^{-2}$ /yr**. Calculate: (i) the **system unreliability**, (ii) the **frequency of system failure**, and (iii) the **expected annual loss** if each system failure causes **2 fatalities**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Flowchart + ETA/FTA explanation (7 Marks):**

**Flowchart (3 Marks):** Draw a Mermaid-style or block flowchart of the 11-step procedure (refer to **Section 4.1**).

**ETA role (2 Marks):** Event Tree Analysis is a **forward, inductive** logic model that begins with an **initiating event** (e.g., a 50 mm pipeline leak) and progresses **left to right** through a series of **safety function successes or failures** (ignition/no ignition, isolation success/failure, ignition time, weather class). Each branch ends in a **consequence outcome** with an associated frequency $f \cdot P_{branch}$. ETA quantifies the full spectrum of possible outcomes from a single initiating event.

**FTA role (2 Marks):** Fault Tree Analysis is a **backward, deductive** logic model that starts with a **top event** (e.g., "loss of containment" or "fire protection failure") and identifies the **minimal cut sets** — combinations of basic events that cause the top event. FTA uses **AND/OR gates** and **Boolean algebra** to compute the top event frequency. It is especially useful for **complex systems** with multiple redundant safeguards, and for identifying the **dominant contributors** (Pareto analysis of cut sets).

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Three barriers in **series**: B1, B2, B3
- $Q_{B1} = 0.02,\ Q_{B2} = 0.03,\ Q_{B3} = 0.05$
- Initiating event frequency $f_0 = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ /yr
- $N = 2$ fatalities per failure

**Step (i): System Unreliability (2 Marks):**

For **series** configuration, **all** barriers must fail:

$$
Q_{sys} = Q_{B1} \times Q_{B2} \times Q_{B3}
$$

$$
Q_{sys} = 0.02 \times 0.03 \times 0.05
$$

$$
Q_{sys} = 3.0 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step (ii): Frequency of System Failure (2 Marks):**

$$
f_{sys} = f_0 \times Q_{sys}
$$

$$
f_{sys} = (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (3.0 \times 10^{-5})
$$

$$
f_{sys} = 3.0 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step (iii): Expected Annual Loss (3 Marks):**

$$
\bar{N} = f_{sys} \times N
$$

$$
\bar{N} = 3.0 \times 10^{-7} \times 2 = 6.0 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{fatalities/yr}
$$

**Return period:**

$$
T = \frac{1}{\bar{N}} = \frac{1}{6.0 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.67 \times 10^{6}\ \text{years}
$$

**Interpretation:** The risk is in the **broadly acceptable** region (< 10⁻⁶/yr for public) and meets HSE acceptance. The **dominant barrier** is B3 (largest $Q$); focusing reliability-improvement efforts on B3 yields the largest risk reduction.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in QRA Procedure Questions — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the order of steps:** Many students list "consequence modelling" before "hazard identification." The correct logical order is **HAZID → Initiating Event → Frequency → Consequence → Risk**.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Always write **yr⁻¹** for frequency and **fatalities/yr** for expected loss. Marks are deducted if units are missing.
> 3. **Not verifying branch probabilities sum to 1:** In event-tree problems, examiners expect you to verify $0.15 + 0.25 + 0.60 = 1.00$. Skipping this check costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing ETA vs FTA:** ETA is **forward** (initiating event → outcomes); FTA is **backward** (top event → causes). Writing "fault tree goes from left to right" is a guaranteed 0 for that line.
> 5. **Not citing industry codes:** Always mention **OREDA, TNO Purple Book, API 581, HSE R2P2, CPR-12E** to demonstrate industry-level awareness. 1–2 marks are reserved for this in 14-mark questions.
> 6. **Skipping the ALARP discussion:** Even in numerical problems, a 1-line comment on whether the result falls in the **tolerable / ALARP / unacceptable** zone earns 1 bonus mark.
> 7. **Decimal and exponent errors:** Watch for $(1 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.20 = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ — many students write $2 \times 10^{-3}$ due to careless exponent handling.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — QRA Procedure (Module 4)**

- [x] **QRA Definition** = Frequency × Consequence, systematically quantified.
- [x] **Core Equation:** $R = f \times C$ — the heartbeat of every QRA.
- [x] **11 Steps:** Scope → HAZID → Initiating Event/ETA/FTA → Frequency → Consequence → Branch Probabilities → Risk Calculation → Aggregation → Comparison → Mitigation → Sensitivity/Documentation.
- [x] **Hazard Identification tools:** HAZOP, FMEA, What-If, Checklists, Historical data.
- [x] **ETA** = **Forward**, **inductive** (left-to-right) — used to map outcomes from one initiating event.
- [x] **FTA** = **Backward**, **deductive** (top-down) — used to compute top event frequency from cut sets; uses **AND / OR gates** + **Boolean algebra**.
- [x] **Frequency data sources:** **OREDA, TNO Purple Book, CCPS PERD, API RP 581, IEEE 500, plant data**.
- [x] **Consequence software:** **PHAST, FRED, ALOHA, EFFECTS, KFX, SAFETI**.
- [x] **Individual Risk (IR)** = frequency of fatality of a person at a location; plotted as **isopleths**; units **yr⁻¹**.
- [x] **Societal Risk (SR)** = frequency vs. N fatalities; plotted as **F-N curve** on log-log scale; CPR-12E slope **−1** is the limit line.
- [x] **HSE Acceptance:** IR ≤ **10⁻⁶/yr (public)**, ≤ **10⁻⁴/yr (workers)**.
- [x] **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable — cost-benefit justified risk reduction.
- [x] **Mitigation hierarchy:** **Inherent Safety → Passive → Active (BPCS/SIS) → Procedural**.
- [x] **Series barriers:** $Q_{sys} = \prod Q_i$ (all must fail). **Parallel barriers:** $Q_{sys} = \prod (1 - Q_i)$.
- [x] **Pareto principle in QRA:** 80% of risk typically comes from 20% of scenarios — always perform sensitivity analysis.
- [x] **Return period:** $T = 1/f$ — key communication metric.
- [x] **Codes & Standards to mention in exams:** API 581, OREDA, Purple Book, CCPS Guidelines, HSE R2P2, CPR-12E, OSHA PSM, MSIHC Rules (India).
- [x] **Always verify** $R = f \times C$ units and **branch probabilities sum to 1**.

---

**End of Notes — Module 4: Quantitative Risk Assessment — Procedure for QRA**
**Total Marks Available in ESE for this Topic (typical): 14 Marks (one full Part B question with internal choice)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITUM RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Risk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**, the term **Risk** is formally defined as the mathematical expectation of an undesirable consequence, expressed as a combination of the **probability of occurrence of a hazardous event** and the **magnitude of its consequence (severity)** on human life, property, environment, or business continuity.

Mathematically, the foundational expression as per KTU/CCPS/ISO 31000 framework is:

$$R = f(P, C)$$

Where:
- **R** = Risk (a measure of harm)
- **P** = Probability (or frequency) of the hazardous event occurring per unit time
- **C** = Consequence (severity of harm — death, injury, financial loss, environmental damage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Standard Definition:** "Risk is the product of the probability that an event will occur and the severity (consequence) of the harm that would result from that event." This is the exact phrasing examiners look for in 3-mark and 7-mark answer scripts.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-world Analogy — Crossing a Busy Highway:**
Imagine you want to cross a six-lane national highway on foot.

- **P (Probability)** → How busy is the highway? How fast are the vehicles? Is there a divider? *(chance of something going wrong)*
- **C (Consequence)** → If a vehicle hits you, the outcome is almost certainly fatal or severely injurious. *(how bad it is if it goes wrong)*

If you cross a deserted village road, **P is low and C is moderate → Low Risk.**
If you cross a Mumbai-Pune expressway at peak hour, **P is moderate and C is very high → High Risk.**

> Risk, therefore, is never about just one factor — it is the **marriage of "how likely" with "how bad."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> - **Hazard** = Source of potential harm (the speeding truck itself).
> - **Risk** = The combination of likelihood and severity (chance the truck hits you × fatality).
> Many students lose marks by interchanging these two terms.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in QRA

The following internationally accepted units are used throughout KTU assessments:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Acceptable Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Individual Risk | IR | probability per year (yr⁻¹) | 10⁻² to 10⁻⁷ /yr |
| Societal Risk | SR | fatalities per year (F-N curve) | 10⁻² to 10⁻⁷ /yr |
| Frequency | f | events/year | — |
| Probability | P | dimensionless (0 to 1) | 0 ≤ P ≤ 1 |
| Consequence | C | fatalities / monetary loss (₹) | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this hierarchy of risk values** — it is the most repeated numerical concept in KTU QRA papers. The internationally accepted **tolerable individual risk** for workers is **10⁻³ per year**, and for the general public is **10⁻⁴ per year**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Risk — The Three Pillars

Every QRA study decomposes risk into three structural pillars. Examiners commonly award 7 marks for a clean explanation of these three components:

1. **Threat (Hazard Source):** The inherent property of a system, substance, or activity to cause harm. *Example:* Storage of 50 tonnes of LPG, operation of a high-pressure boiler.
2. **Vulnerability:** The susceptibility of the receptor (people, assets, environment) to suffer harm if exposed to the threat. *Example:* A residential colony located 100 m downwind of an LPG bullet tank.
3. **Consequence:** The quantified measure of harm if the undesired event fully materializes. Measured in fatalities (F), injury severity, ₹ loss, or ecosystem damage units.

### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of Risk

The complete mathematical structure of risk used in KTU 2024 QRA syllabus is presented below.

**Step 1: Simple Risk Expression**

$$R = P \times C$$

This is the **zero-dimensional risk** — useful for a single, isolated event (e.g., failure of one pressure vessel).

**Step 2: Frequency-Based Risk (Time-Independent)**

When the event can occur multiple times over a system's lifetime:

$$R = f \times C$$

Where *f* = frequency of the initiating event (events per year).

**Step 3: Multi-Pathway / Multi-Scenario Risk (Realistic QRA)**

$$\boxed{R_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \times C_i}$$

Where:
- *i* = index for each accident scenario (pool fire, BLEVE, toxic release, VCE, jet fire, etc.)
- *f_i* = frequency of scenario *i*
- *C_i* = consequence magnitude of scenario *i*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The summation form is what real QRA software (SAFETI, PHAST, ALOHA) computes internally. In your KTU 14-mark answers, always write the summation — never use the simple P × C alone for industry-scale problems.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> **Critical Table Instruction:** All absolute values and conditionals below use `\vert` and `\mid` to avoid markdown table syntax breakage.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple Risk | $R = P \times C$ | Single event, single pathway |
| 2 | Frequency Risk | $R = f \times C$ | Repeatable event over a year |
| 3 | Total Plant Risk | $R_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \times C_i$ | Multi-scenario industrial QRA |
| 4 | Individual Risk (Point) | $IR(x,y) = \sum_{j} f_j \times P_{j}(x,y)$ | Risk at a geographic location |
| 5 | Individual Risk (Annual) | $IR = \sum_{j} f_j$ | Frequency of fatality at a point |
| 6 | Societal Risk (F-N Curve) | $F - N \text{ plot of } F(N) \text{ vs. } N$ | Cumulative risk vs. consequence |
| 7 | Expected Fatalities | $EF = \sum_{i} f_i \times N_i$ | Average fatalities per year |
| 8 | ALARP Boundary | $R \leq R_{tolerable}$ | Risk must be As Low As Reasonably Practicable |
| 9 | Conditional Probability | $P(A\vert B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ | Event trees / fault trees |
| 10 | Risk Ratio | $RR = \dfrac{R_{actual}}{R_{tolerable}}$ | Decision-making for acceptability |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Industry (Oil & Gas):** QRA is mandatory under **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** and **Indian Factories Act (Amended 1987)** for any facility storing > 50 tonnes of flammable material.
- **Nuclear Industry:** Risk = Core Damage Frequency (CDF) × Release Fraction, used by AERB (India) and IAEA.
- **Pharmaceutical & Chemical:** Used to decide whether to implement a ₹10 Crore safety upgrade.
- **Civil Engineering:** Used in dam break analysis, building fire safety, and structural collapse risk.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Single-Event Risk to Societal Risk

The KTU 2024 syllabus demands that students demonstrate the **analytical bridge** between individual event risk and societal risk (F-N curve). Below is the complete derivation.

**Step 1: Define a single accident scenario**

Let there be a single accident scenario *i* with frequency *f_i* (events per year) causing *N_i* fatalities.

**Step 2: Expected fatalities per year from scenario *i***

The expected number of fatalities per year (also called *Expected Loss of Life*, ELL) is:

$$ELL_i = f_i \times N_i$$

**Reasoning:** If scenario *i* happens *f_i* times a year, and each occurrence kills *N_i* people on average, the average annual death toll is the product.

**Step 3: Sum across all accident scenarios**

For *n* mutually independent scenarios, the total expected fatalities per year is:

$$ELL_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \times N_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \times C_i$$

**Reasoning:** This is the linear superposition principle applied to low-probability events (valid when probabilities are very small, which is the QRA assumption).

**Step 4: Rank scenarios by consequence magnitude**

Sort all accident scenarios in descending order of *N_i*:

$$N_1 \geq N_2 \geq N_3 \geq \dots \geq N_n$$

**Step 5: Construct the cumulative frequency function**

Define *F(N)* = cumulative frequency of all accidents causing **N or more fatalities**:

$$F(N) = \sum_{i : N_i \geq N} f_i$$

**Reasoning:** This is mathematically the *survival function* (or *complementary cumulative distribution function*) of fatality consequence.

**Step 6: Plot the F-N Curve**

The **F-N curve** is a log-log plot of *F(N)* (y-axis, in events/year) versus *N* (x-axis, fatalities per event). It is the most important societal risk diagram in KTU's QRA module.

**Step 7: Derive the Individual Risk (IR)**

For a person located at geographic point *(x, y)*, the individual risk is the sum of fatality probabilities from all accident sources *j*:

$$IR(x,y) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} f_j \times P_{fatality}^{j}(x,y)$$

Where $P_{fatality}^{j}(x,y)$ is the probability that scenario *j* causes the fatality of a person standing at *(x, y)*, derived using probit equations or dose-response models.

**Step 8: Apply the Probit Equation for Consequence**

The probability of fatality from a toxic release or thermal radiation is computed via the probit equation:

$$P_{fatality} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y - 5} e^{-u^2/2} \, du$$

Where *Y* is the probit variable defined for thermal radiation as:

$$Y = a + b \ln(Q^{4/3} \cdot t)$$

Where *Q* = thermal radiation intensity (kW/m²), *t* = exposure time (seconds), *a* and *b* are probit constants (for thermal fatality: a = -14.9, b = 2.56).

**Step 9: Final Risk Equation (Full Form)**

The complete risk expression used in KTU board solutions is:

$$\boxed{R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} f_i \cdot P_{path}^{i \to j} \cdot C_j}$$

Where:
- $f_i$ = frequency of initiating event *i*
- $P_{path}^{i \to j}$ = probability that the consequence path leads to outcome *j*
- $C_j$ = severity of outcome *j* (in fatalities)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** In the 14-mark "Calculate the risk" question, the examiner expects you to (a) identify at least 3–4 scenarios, (b) tabulate *f_i* and *C_i*, (c) multiply and sum, (d) compare with tolerable risk criteria, (e) comment on ALARP. Skipping any one step costs you 2–3 marks.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

**Problem:** A chlorine storage facility has three credible accident scenarios:

| Scenario | Frequency f_i (per year) | Fatalities N_i |
|---|---|---|
| Small release | 0.01 | 1 |
| Medium release | 0.001 | 10 |
| Major release | 0.0001 | 100 |

**Calculate:** (i) Expected Loss of Life (ELL), (ii) Total Risk.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Calculate ELL per scenario:

$$ELL_1 = 0.01 \times 1 = 0.01 \text{ fatalities/year}$$

$$ELL_2 = 0.001 \times 10 = 0.01 \text{ fatalities/year}$$

$$ELL_3 = 0.0001 \times 100 = 0.01 \text{ fatalities/year}$$

Step 2: Total expected fatalities per year:

$$ELL_{total} = 0.01 + 0.01 + 0.01 = 0.03 \text{ fatalities/year}$$

Step 3: Total risk (R) — using summation form:

$$R = \sum_{i=1}^{3} f_i \times N_i = 0.03 \text{ fatalities/year}$$

**Interpretation:** This is equivalent to saying that, on average, 1 person dies every ~33 years due to this facility. Compared to the UK HSE's tolerable individual risk of 10⁻⁴/year for the public, the **societal risk** is significant and mitigation is required under ALARP.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Risk Assessment Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture of a complete Quantitative Risk Assessment** workflow, exactly as required by KTU Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SystemDefinition] --> B[HazardIdentificationHAZID]
    B --> C[ScenarioDevelopment]
    C --> D[FrequencyAnalysisf_i]
    D --> E[ConsequenceAnalysisC_i]
    E --> F[RiskCalculationR_eq]
    F --> G[IndividualRiskMap]
    F --> H[SocietalRiskFN_Curve]
    G --> I{RiskAcceptableCheck}
    H --> I
    I -->|Yes| J[DocumentationAndReview]
    I -->|No| K[MitigationMeasures]
    K --> L[RecalculateRisk]
    L --> I
    J --> M[FinalQRA_Report]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

1. **System Definition** — Establish system boundaries, inventory, operating conditions.
2. **HAZID** — Hazard Identification using HAZOP, FMEA, What-If, or Checklist methods.
3. **Scenario Development** — Enumerate all credible accident scenarios (jet fire, VCE, BLEVE, toxic dispersion).
4. **Frequency Analysis** — Calculate *f_i* using fault trees, event trees, or historical failure databases (OIRDA, PERD, IEEE 500).
5. **Consequence Analysis** — Calculate *C_i* (thermal radiation, overpressure, toxic dose) using source models, dispersion models, fire models, and explosion models.
6. **Risk Calculation** — Combine *f_i* and *C_i* using the summation equation.
7. **Individual Risk Map** — Plot iso-risk contours around the facility.
8. **Societal Risk (F-N Curve)** — Plot the F-N curve to assess cumulative fatality potential.
9. **Risk Acceptance Check** — Compare with tolerable risk criteria (HSE, AERB, OISD).
10. **Mitigation Loop** — If unacceptable, propose engineered, procedural, or administrative controls and recalculate.

### 4.2 Risk Triad Diagram (The Three Pillars)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Threat] --> R[Risk]
    V[Vulnerability] --> R
    C[Consequence] --> R
    R -->|Output| IR[IndividualRisk]
    R -->|Output| SR[SocietalRisk]
    R -->|Decision| A[ALARPAssessment]
```

### 4.3 Risk Acceptance Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: RiskComputed] --> D1{IR_gt_10_neg3}
    D1 -->|Yes| UN[UnacceptableRegion]
    D1 -->|No| D2{IR_between_10_neg6_and_10_neg3}
    D2 -->|Yes| ALARP[ALARPRegion]
    D2 -->|No| AC[AcceptableRegion]
    UN -->|Action| AB1[StopOperationOrMajorEngineeringChange]
    ALARP -->|Action| AB2[CostBenefitAnalysisAndReduceToALARP]
    AC -->|Action| AB3[DocumentAndMonitor]
```

**Interpretation of the decision tree:**

- **IR > 10⁻³ /yr** → Unacceptable. Operation must not continue.
- **10⁻⁶ < IR ≤ 10⁻³ /yr** → ALARP region. Risk must be reduced unless the cost is grossly disproportionate to the safety benefit gained.
- **IR ≤ 10⁻⁶ /yr** → Broadly acceptable. Document and monitor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Is this risk acceptable?", you **must** show the numerical IR, identify which region it falls into, and apply the cost-benefit test for the ALARP region. Just saying "yes" or "no" will cost you 2–3 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define the term "Risk" as used in Quantitative Risk Assessment. How is it different from "Hazard"?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Risk is the mathematical combination of the probability of occurrence of a hazardous event and the severity of its consequence on people, property, or the environment. It is expressed as *R = P × C*.
A **Hazard**, on the other hand, is only the source or situation that has the inherent potential to cause harm (e.g., a pressurised LPG tank). Risk quantifies *how likely* and *how bad* the harm would be; hazard only describes the *what could go wrong*. Thus, all hazards carry risk, but the magnitude of risk depends on both probability and consequence. *(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
List any three components of risk and state the standard unit of individual risk.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three components of risk are:
1. **Threat** (the source of potential harm),
2. **Vulnerability** (the susceptibility of the receptor to harm), and
3. **Consequence** (the magnitude of harm if the event occurs).

The standard unit of **Individual Risk (IR)** is **probability per year (yr⁻¹)**. *(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Choose EITHER This OR Question B

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Supple Series]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the difference between Individual Risk (IR) and Societal Risk (SR). How is each represented graphically? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A petroleum refinery storage area stores 5,000 tonnes of crude oil. The QRA team has identified four credible accident scenarios. Calculate the total risk (in expected fatalities per year) and plot the F-N curve points. Comment on the ALARP status, given that the UK HSE tolerable societal risk line is approximated by:

$$F(N) = \frac{10^{-2}}{N^2} \quad \text{(events/year)}$$

| Scenario | Frequency f_i (per year) | Fatalities N_i |
|---|---|---|
| S1 — Bund fire | 0.005 | 2 |
| S2 — Pool fire | 0.001 | 5 |
| S3 — Vapour Cloud Explosion | 0.0005 | 20 |
| S4 — Major BLEVE | 0.00005 | 100 |

*(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (Q-A):**

**(a) Individual Risk vs Societal Risk (7 marks):**

**Definition:**
- **Individual Risk (IR):** The frequency at which an individual at a specific location is expected to sustain a specified level of harm (usually fatality) from exposure to hazardous events. It is *location-specific* and *person-independent*.
- **Societal Risk (SR):** The relationship between the frequency of accidents and the number of people suffering from a specified level of harm in a given population. It accounts for the *number of people exposed* and is *cumulative*.

**Graphical Representation:**

- IR is represented as an **iso-risk contour map** — closed curves drawn around the hazard source, with each contour labelled with a constant IR value (e.g., 10⁻⁴/yr, 10⁻⁵/yr, 10⁻⁶/yr).
- SR is represented as an **F-N curve** — a log-log plot of cumulative frequency *F(N)* (y-axis) versus number of fatalities *N* (x-axis). Tolerability lines (upper and lower) are superimposed.

**Key Differences Table:**

| Parameter | Individual Risk | Societal Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Dependence on population | No | Yes |
| Considers societal aversion | No | Yes |
| Graphical form | Iso-risk contours | F-N curve |
| Common unit | yr⁻¹ | events/year |
| Criteria source | HSE IRPA | HSE FN-curve |

*(7 marks distributed: 2 marks definitions + 2 marks graphical forms + 3 marks comparison table)*

---

**(b) Calculation of Total Risk and F-N Curve (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Calculate cumulative frequency F(N) for each scenario:**

For each N value, F(N) is the sum of frequencies of all scenarios with fatalities ≥ N.

- *F(2)* = f(S1) + f(S2) + f(S3) + f(S4) = 0.005 + 0.001 + 0.0005 + 0.00005 = **0.00655 /yr**
- *F(5)* = f(S2) + f(S3) + f(S4) = 0.001 + 0.0005 + 0.00005 = **0.00155 /yr**
- *F(20)* = f(S3) + f(S4) = 0.0005 + 0.00005 = **0.00055 /yr**
- *F(100)* = f(S4) = **0.00005 /yr**

**Step 2: Total Risk (Expected Loss of Life):**

$$R_{total} = \sum f_i N_i$$

$$R = (0.005)(2) + (0.001)(5) + (0.0005)(20) + (0.00005)(100)$$

$$R = 0.010 + 0.005 + 0.010 + 0.005 = \mathbf{0.030 \text{ fatalities/year}}$$

**Step 3: ALARP Comparison:**

Compute F(N) from the tolerability line for each N:

- F_HSE(2) = 10⁻² / 2² = 2.5 × 10⁻³ /yr → Our F(2) = 6.55 × 10⁻³ /yr → **ABOVE the line (unacceptable side)**
- F_HSE(5) = 10⁻² / 5² = 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ /yr → Our F(5) = 1.55 × 10⁻³ /yr → **ABOVE the line**
- F_HSE(20) = 10⁻² / 20² = 2.5 × 10⁻⁵ /yr → Our F(20) = 5.5 × 10⁻⁴ /yr → **ABOVE the line**
- F_HSE(100) = 10⁻² / 100² = 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ /yr → Our F(100) = 5.0 × 10⁻⁵ /yr → **ABOVE the line**

**Conclusion:** The facility's F-N curve lies entirely in the **unacceptable region** above the HSE tolerability line. **Risk reduction is mandatory** under ALARP. Suggested measures: install foam pourers, increase bund capacity, install gas detection with automatic shutdown, and relocate the closest residential area beyond 500 m.

*(7 marks distributed: 2 marks F(N) calculation + 2 marks total risk + 2 marks ALARP comparison + 1 mark conclusion)*

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Regular Series]**

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the complete methodology of Quantitative Risk Assessment. List the four major steps in sequence. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A chemical plant stores 200 tonnes of ammonia. Two release scenarios are identified. Calculate the Individual Risk at a downwind distance of 500 m, given the following data:

| Scenario | Frequency f (per year) | Probability of fatality at 500 m |
|---|---|---|
| Small release (5 tonnes) | 0.001 | 0.05 |
| Major release (50 tonnes) | 0.0001 | 0.40 |

State whether the IR is acceptable as per UK HSE criteria (tolerable IR for public = 10⁻⁴/yr). *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (Q-B):**

**(a) QRA Methodology (7 marks):**

The four major steps of QRA, in sequence, are:

1. **Hazard Identification (HAZID):** Identify all potential hazards using HAZOP, FMEA, What-If, or Checklist. Output: List of credible accident scenarios.
2. **Frequency Analysis:** Estimate the frequency of each scenario using fault trees (top-down), event trees (forward), or historical failure rate databases (OIRDA, IEEE Std 500).
3. **Consequence Analysis:** Estimate the physical effects (thermal radiation, overpressure, toxic dose) using source models, dispersion models, fire models, and explosion models.
4. **Risk Calculation & Evaluation:** Combine frequency and consequence to compute individual and societal risk, plot F-N curve, compare with tolerability criteria, and recommend mitigation.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per step explanation + 3 marks for block diagram description)*

---

**(b) Individual Risk Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Formula**

$$IR = \sum_{j=1}^{2} f_j \times P_{fatality}^{j}$$

**Step 2: Contribution from Scenario 1 (Small release):**

$$IR_1 = 0.001 \times 0.05 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /yr}$$

**Step 3: Contribution from Scenario 2 (Major release):**

$$IR_2 = 0.0001 \times 0.40 = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /yr}$$

**Step 4: Total Individual Risk at 500 m:**

$$IR_{total} = IR_1 + IR_2 = 5 \times 10^{-5} + 4 \times 10^{-5} = 9 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /yr}$$

**Step 5: Comparison with HSE Tolerable Limit:**

- HSE tolerable IR for general public = 10⁻⁴ /yr.
- Our computed IR = 9 × 10⁻⁵ /yr = 0.9 × 10⁻⁴ /yr.
- 9 × 10⁻⁵ < 1 × 10⁻⁴ → **IR is JUST WITHIN the tolerable limit.**

**Step 6: ALARP Assessment:**

Since IR is in the ALARP region (10⁻⁶ to 10⁻³ /yr), the "As Low As Reasonably Practicable" principle applies. A cost-benefit analysis must be performed to determine if further risk reduction (e.g., water curtains, gas detection, evacuation drills) is justified. **Recommendation:** Implement additional safety measures to bring IR closer to 10⁻⁵ /yr.

*(7 marks distributed: 1 mark formula + 2 marks individual contribution + 1 mark total + 2 marks comparison + 1 mark ALARP recommendation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Hazard and Risk:** Many students define "Risk" as the hazard itself. This costs 1 full mark in 3-mark questions and up to 2 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Using only R = P × C:** For industrial-scale QRA problems, the **summation form** $R = \sum f_i \times C_i$ is mandatory. Using the simple product form is considered an incomplete answer.
> 3. **Forgetting Units:** Always write the unit (yr⁻¹) when reporting Individual Risk. Markers specifically deduct ½ to 1 mark for missing units.
> 4. **Not Comparing with Tolerability Criteria:** A risk value without comparison to HSE / AERB / OISD criteria is incomplete. Always end with a comparison and a recommendation.
> 5. **Wrong F-N Curve Plotting:** The F-N curve is **log-log**. Plotting on linear axes loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Skipping ALARP:** The "ALARP" discussion is compulsory in any 14-mark QRA problem involving risk evaluation. Skipping it costs 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 60-Second Glance:**

- ✅ **Risk = Probability × Consequence** (*R = P × C*); for industry: $R = \sum f_i C_i$.
- ✅ **Hazard ≠ Risk.** Hazard is the source; Risk is the quantified measure.
- ✅ **Three components of risk:** Threat, Vulnerability, Consequence.
- ✅ **Individual Risk (IR):** Frequency of fatality at a specific point, unit = yr⁻¹.
- ✅ **Societal Risk (SR):** F-N curve — frequency of N or more fatalities.
- ✅ **Tolerable IR:** Worker = 10⁻³/yr, Public = 10⁻⁴/yr, Negligible = 10⁻⁶/yr.
- ✅ **F-N curve is log-log plot.** The tolerability line is typically of slope −1 or −2.
- ✅ **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable — apply cost-benefit analysis.
- ✅ **F-N Curve calculation steps:** Compute *f_i N_i* for each scenario, sort by N, compute cumulative F(N), plot, compare with HSE line.
- ✅ **Probit equations** convert toxic dose / thermal dose into probability of fatality.
- ✅ **QRA Methodology in 4 steps:** HAZID → Frequency → Consequence → Risk Calculation.
- ✅ **Real software used:** SAFETI, PHAST, ALOHA, FRED, Shell FRED.
- ✅ **Standards & Acts:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, Indian Factories Act 1987, ISO 31000, OISD-118, AERB guidelines.
- ✅ **Common summation formula** (always write the summation, not the product alone, for 7+ mark answers).

---

**END OF KTU-PREMIUM NOTES — MODULE 4: RISK**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — PECHT754 Quantitative Risk Assessment*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Risk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** as prescribed under the KTU 2024 scheme, **risk** is formally defined as the mathematical product of the probability of an adverse event and the magnitude of its consequence (or damage). Mathematically, it is represented as:

$$R = f(P, C)$$

where **P** is the probability (or frequency) of occurrence and **C** is the consequence (or severity).

A **Type of Risk** refers to the classification of risk events based on their **origin, nature, predictability, financial implication, controllability, and scope of impact**. Classification is the foundational step in any risk assessment because the analytical tools (e.g., FMEA, HAZOP, Monte Carlo simulation) used to quantify one type of risk may differ fundamentally from another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to identify, classify, and distinguish between **pure vs. speculative**, **static vs. dynamic**, **systematic vs. unsystematic**, **financial vs. non-financial**, and **operational vs. strategic** risk categories. Each type must be linked to its real-world industrial example.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. Before prescribing medicine, the doctor classifies the illness — is it viral or bacterial? Acute or chronic? Communicable or genetic? Likewise, a **risk engineer** must classify a risk before quantifying it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Toolbox":** Think of risk types as different tools in an engineer's toolbox. A hammer (pure risk) is used for nails; a wrench (speculative risk) is used for bolts. You cannot hammer a bolt or wrench a nail. Similarly, **insurable pure risk** requires statistical loss-distribution analysis, whereas **speculative risk** requires portfolio variance and expected-value models.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

Some universally accepted metrics used across all risk types in QRA:

- **Loss Exposure (LE)** — measured in monetary units (USD, INR, EUR)
- **Probability of Occurrence (P)** — dimensionless, range $[0, 1]$
- **Expected Loss (EL)** — $EL = P \times C$
- **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)** — measures volatility of risk outcomes
- **Value at Risk (VaR)** — maximum expected loss at a given confidence level (e.g., **95%** or **99%**)
- **Risk Tolerance Threshold** — organization-specific acceptable loss limit

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Risk Types

Risk types are broadly categorized along **six dimensions**:

### Dimension 1: Nature of Outcome

#### A. Pure Risk
- A risk that results in **either a loss or no loss** — never a gain.
- Examples: fire, theft, natural calamities, equipment failure, occupational accidents.
- **Insurable** in most jurisdictions.
- Predictable using **actuarial tables** and historical loss data.

#### B. Speculative Risk
- A risk that results in **either a loss, no loss, or a gain**.
- Examples: stock market investments, R&D projects, new product launches.
- **Not generally insurable** by traditional insurance products.
- Quantified using **expected return**, **variance**, and **Sharpe ratio**.

### Dimension 2: Scope of Impact

#### A. Systematic Risk (Market Risk)
- Affects the **entire system, market, or economy**.
- Cannot be eliminated through diversification.
- Examples: interest rate changes, inflation, recession, war, pandemic.
- Measured via **Beta ($\beta$)** coefficient in finance: $\beta = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$.

#### B. Unsystematic (Idiosyncratic) Risk
- Affects a **specific company, asset, or project**.
- Can be reduced or eliminated through diversification.
- Examples: management failure, product recall, plant shutdown.
- Measured via **firm-specific standard deviation**.

### Dimension 3: Time Behavior

#### A. Static Risk
- Risks arising from **imbalances or discontinuities** in the environment (e.g., natural disasters).
- Loss occurs at a **specific point in time**; not profit-driven.

#### B. Dynamic Risk
- Risks arising from **changes in the economic, technological, or political environment**.
- Associated with **innovation and progress**.

### Dimension 4: Financial vs. Non-Financial

| Category | Sub-Type | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Financial Risk** | Market Risk | Loss due to price movements |
| | Credit Risk | Counterparty default |
| | Liquidity Risk | Inability to meet short-term obligations |
| | Operational Risk | Internal process failures |
| **Non-Financial Risk** | Strategic Risk | Wrong business decisions |
| | Legal Risk | Litigation, regulatory non-compliance |
| | Reputational Risk | Brand damage |
| | Environmental Risk | Pollution, climate events |

### Dimension 5: Origin / Source

- **Business Risk** — variability in operating income due to changes in sales, costs, or competition.
- **Financial Risk** — variability in earnings due to the presence of **debt financing** (leverage).
- **Country/ Sovereign Risk** — political and economic instability of a nation.
- **Currency/ Exchange Risk** — adverse movements in foreign exchange rates.

### Dimension 6: Project-Specific (Engineering QRA Focus)

For engineering students under KTU, the following are critical:

- **Safety Risk** — risk of injury or fatality to workers/public (e.g., refinery explosion).
- **Environmental Risk** — risk of ecological damage (e.g., oil spill).
- **Asset/Property Risk** — risk to physical infrastructure.
- **Schedule Risk** — delay in project completion.
- **Technical Risk** — failure of technology to perform as designed.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet** — Memorize the following for KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

| Formula | Symbol | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R = P \times C$ | Risk Index | Basic product form | Various |
| $EL = \sum_{i} P_i \cdot C_i$ | Expected Loss | Sum of probability-weighted losses | Currency |
| $\sigma = \sqrt{\sum P_i (C_i - \mu)^2}$ | Standard Deviation | Dispersion of outcomes | Currency |
| $VaR_{\alpha} = \mu + z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma$ | Value at Risk | Worst-case loss at confidence $\alpha$ | Currency |
| $\beta = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$ | Beta | Systematic risk sensitivity | Dimensionless |
| $SR = \frac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Sharpe Ratio | Risk-adjusted return | Dimensionless |
| $DOL = \frac{\%\Delta EBIT}{\%\Delta Sales}$ | Degree of Operating Leverage | Business risk | Ratio |
| $DFL = \frac{\%\Delta EPS}{\%\Delta EBIT}$ | Degree of Financial Leverage | Financial risk | Ratio |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Oil & Gas QRA**: Classifying fire/explosion (pure) vs. price volatility (speculative) for offshore platforms.
- **Construction Industry**: Using systematic vs. unsystematic risk to model cost overruns.
- **Software Engineering**: Operational risk vs. cyber risk distinction.
- **Power Plants**: Distinguishing safety risk (fatality) from financial risk (revenue loss).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Risk Index Formula

The most fundamental QRA relationship is:

$$R = P \times C$$

### Step 1 — Define Probability

Let $P$ denote the probability of an adverse event occurring within a defined time window $t$. By axiomatic probability:

$$0 \leq P \leq 1$$

### Step 2 — Define Consequence

Let $C$ be the quantified consequence (in monetary units, fatalities, or environmental damage). For multi-attribute consequences, an aggregate consequence $C$ may be constructed via a weighted sum:

$$C = w_1 C_1 + w_2 C_2 + \ldots + w_n C_n$$

where $w_i$ is the normalized weight ($\sum w_i = 1$) and $C_i$ is the i-th consequence attribute.

### Step 3 — Combine

The risk index $R$ is defined as the expected magnitude of harm per unit time:

$$R = P \cdot C$$

### Step 4 — Generalize to Multiple Scenarios

For $n$ independent scenarios $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot C_i \\
&= EL
\end{aligned}$$

Hence, $R_{\text{total}}$ is also termed the **Expected Loss (EL)**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Beta for Systematic Risk

The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) provides:

$$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \left( E(R_m) - R_f \right)$$

### Step 1 — Define Covariance

$$\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m) = E[(R_i - \mu_i)(R_m - \mu_m)]$$

### Step 2 — Define Variance of Market

$$\text{Var}(R_m) = E[(R_m - \mu_m)^2]$$

### Step 3 — Define Beta

$$\beta_i = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

- $\beta = 1$: Asset moves in line with market.
- $\beta > 1$: Asset is more volatile than market (aggressive).
- $\beta < 1$: Asset is less volatile than market (defensive).
- $\beta < 0$: Asset moves opposite to market (hedging).

## 3.3 Derivation of Value at Risk (VaR)

Assume loss $L$ is normally distributed with mean $\mu_L$ and standard deviation $\sigma_L$.

### Step 1 — Standard Normal Transform

$$Z = \frac{L - \mu_L}{\sigma_L}$$

### Step 2 — At Confidence Level $\alpha$

The loss not exceeded with probability $\alpha$ is:

$$L_{\alpha} = \mu_L + z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_L$$

where $z_{\alpha}$ is the standard normal quantile.

### Step 3 — VaR Definition

$$VaR_{\alpha} = L_{\alpha} = \mu_L + z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_L$$

For example, at $\alpha = 95\%$, $z_{0.95} \approx 1.645$; at $\alpha = 99\%$, $z_{0.99} \approx 2.326$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pure vs. Speculative Risk

| Attribute | Pure Risk | Speculative Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Outcome | Loss or No Loss | Loss, No Loss, or Gain |
| Insurability | Yes | Generally No |
| Beneficial? | Never | Sometimes |
| Example | Fire, Accident | Stock Trading, R&D |
| Primary Tool | Actuarial Analysis | Portfolio Theory, VaR |
| Social Impact | Net Loss | Net Gain possible |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Systematic vs. Unsystematic Risk

| Attribute | Systematic Risk | Unsystematic Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Entire market | Specific firm/asset |
| Diversifiable? | No | Yes |
| Beta | Affects $\beta$ | Does not affect $\beta$ |
| Example | Recession, War | Management fraud |
| Hedging Tool | Index futures, options | Portfolio diversification |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Risk Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RISK] --> B[Pure Risk]
    A --> C[Speculative Risk]
    B --> B1[Personal Risk]
    B --> B2[Property Risk]
    B --> B3[Liability Risk]
    C --> C1[Market Risk]
    C --> C2[Business Risk]
    C --> C3[Financial Risk]
    C --> C4[Credit Risk]
    A --> D[Systematic Risk]
    A --> E[Unsystematic Risk]
    D --> D1[Inflation]
    D --> D2[Interest Rate]
    D --> D3[Political / Sovereign]
    E --> E1[Management Risk]
    E --> E2[Operational Risk]
    E --> E3[Reputational Risk]
    A --> F[Engineering QRA Categories]
    F --> F1[Safety Risk]
    F --> F2[Environmental Risk]
    F --> F3[Asset / Property Risk]
    F --> F4[Schedule Risk]
    F --> F5[Technical Risk]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Risk Classification Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Raw Risk Event] --> Id[Identify Source]
    Id --> Cl[Classify Dimension]
    Cl --> Qa[Quantify P and C]
    Qa --> R[Compute R Index]
    R --> D{Decision Gate}
    D -->|R greater than Threshold| M[Mitigate or Transfer]
    D -->|R less than or equal to Threshold| Ac[Accept / Monitor]
    Ac --> Mo[Periodic Reassessment]
    M --> Mo
    Mo --> Fb[Feedback Loop]
    Fb --> Id
```

## 4.3 Risk-Type Mapping for Industrial Sectors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Oil and Gas] --> O1[Process Safety Risk]
    O --> O2[Environmental Risk]
    O --> O3[Price Volatility Speculative Risk]
    C[Construction] --> C1[Schedule Risk]
    C --> C2[Technical Risk]
    C --> C3[Country Risk]
    IT[IT and Software] --> IT1[Cyber Risk]
    IT --> IT2[Operational Risk]
    IT --> IT3[Reputational Risk]
    P[Pharmaceutical] --> P1[Regulatory Risk]
    P --> P2[R and D Speculative Risk]
    P --> P3[Supply Chain Systematic Risk]
```

## 4.4 Risk Treatment Strategy Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Risk Identified] --> T{Type of Risk}
    T -->|Pure| I[Insure]
    T -->|Speculative| H[Hedge]
    T -->|Systematic| DIV[Use Beta Hedging]
    T -->|Unsystematic| DI[Diversify Portfolio]
    T -->|Operational| CTL[Internal Controls]
    T -->|Strategic| AV[Avoid or Pivot]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Differentiate between Pure Risk and Speculative Risk with one industrial example for each.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pure Risk | Speculative Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Outcome | Loss / No Loss | Loss / No Loss / Gain |
| Insurable | Yes | No (generally) |
| Social Impact | Net loss | Can yield net gain |

- **Pure Risk Example:** Fire outbreak in a chemical plant causing loss of life and property.
- **Speculative Risk Example:** Investment in a new blockchain startup with uncertain but potentially high return.

### Question 2
**Q: Define systematic risk and explain why it cannot be diversified away.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Systematic risk is the risk that affects the entire market or economy, such as inflation, interest rate changes, recession, or war. Because such events impact **all assets simultaneously**, no amount of portfolio diversification can eliminate them. It is measured by the **Beta ($\beta$)** coefficient. Example: the 2008 global financial crisis affected virtually every equity worldwide.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often **lose marks** for (i) confusing pure and speculative risk, (ii) forgetting that unsystematic risk **IS** diversifiable while systematic risk **IS NOT**, and (iii) failing to provide **numerical examples** in their answers. Always quote a real-world scenario and use at least one formula for full marks.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Classify the various types of risks faced by a manufacturing organization. Discuss pure, speculative, systematic, and unsystematic risks with examples. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

A manufacturing organization (e.g., an automobile plant) faces multiple risks:

1. **Pure Risk:** Fire in the assembly line, worker injury from machinery malfunction — leads to loss with no possibility of gain.
2. **Speculative Risk:** Launching a new electric vehicle model — outcome may be profit, break-even, or loss.
3. **Systematic Risk:** A nationwide fuel price hike reducing demand for petrol vehicles (affects entire industry).
4. **Unsystematic Risk:** Recall of a specific car model due to brake failure (affects only that manufacturer).
5. **Financial Risk:** Taking on high debt for plant expansion increases leverage.
6. **Operational Risk:** Supply chain disruption of semiconductor chips.

These risks can be plotted on a 2x2 matrix of **Probability vs. Impact**.

**(b)** A company estimates the probability of a fire in its warehouse as 0.05 per year. The expected loss per fire is ₹80,00,000. Calculate the annual Expected Loss. If the company installs a fire suppression system that reduces probability to 0.01 and the loss per fire to ₹30,00,000, what is the reduction in Expected Loss? Comment on the decision. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Expected Loss without system:**

$$EL_{\text{before}} = P_1 \times C_1 = 0.05 \times 8{,}000{,}000$$

$$\begin{aligned}
EL_{\text{before}} &= 0.05 \times 8 \times 10^6 \\
&= 4 \times 10^5 \\
&= \text{Rs. } 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Expected Loss with system:**

$$\begin{aligned}
EL_{\text{after}} &= P_2 \times C_2 \\
&= 0.01 \times 3 \times 10^6 \\
&= 3 \times 10^4 \\
&= \text{Rs. } 30{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Reduction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta EL &= EL_{\text{before}} - EL_{\text{after}} \\
&= 4{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}000 \\
&= \text{Rs. } 3{,}70{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Decision Comment:**

If the annual cost of the fire suppression system (installation, maintenance, monitoring) is **less than Rs. 3,70,000**, the investment is justified on a pure risk-mitigation basis. If the cost equals or exceeds ₹3,70,000, the firm may choose to **retain the risk** or **transfer via insurance**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the financial vs. non-financial classification of risks. Provide two examples each for credit risk, market risk, operational risk, legal risk, and reputational risk. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Financial Risks** (quantifiable in monetary terms):

- **Credit Risk:** A bank customer defaults on a ₹50 lakh home loan. Counterparty risk.
- **Market Risk:** A drop in crude oil price from $90 to $65/bbl reduces the value of an oil trader's inventory.
- **Operational Risk:** IT server crash at a stock exchange halts trading for 4 hours.

**Non-Financial Risks** (not directly monetary):

- **Legal Risk:** A pharma company faces litigation for patent infringement.
- **Reputational Risk:** A food brand recall due to contamination causes loss of consumer trust.

These risks are often mapped in an **Enterprise Risk Management (ERM)** framework.

**(b)** A portfolio has an expected return of 14% and standard deviation of 20%. The risk-free rate is 6%. Calculate the Sharpe Ratio and comment on the risk-adjusted performance. The market return is 12% with market standard deviation 15%. If the portfolio's correlation with the market is 0.6, calculate the Beta. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**

- $E(R_p) = 14\% = 0.14$
- $\sigma_p = 20\% = 0.20$
- $R_f = 6\% = 0.06$
- $E(R_m) = 12\% = 0.12$
- $\sigma_m = 15\% = 0.15$
- $\rho_{p,m} = 0.6$

**Step 2 — Sharpe Ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
SR &= \frac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p} \\
&= \frac{0.14 - 0.06}{0.20} \\
&= \frac{0.08}{0.20} \\
&= 0.40
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Comment on Sharpe Ratio:**
A Sharpe Ratio of 0.40 indicates that for each unit of total risk (standard deviation), the portfolio generates 0.40 units of excess return. Higher is better; values above 1 are considered excellent.

**Step 4 — Beta Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\beta_p &= \frac{\text{Cov}(R_p, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)} \\
&= \frac{\rho_{p,m} \cdot \sigma_p \cdot \sigma_m}{\sigma_m^2} \\
&= \frac{\rho_{p,m} \cdot \sigma_p}{\sigma_m} \\
&= \frac{0.6 \times 0.20}{0.15} \\
&= \frac{0.12}{0.15} \\
&= 0.80
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
A $\beta_p = 0.80$ indicates the portfolio is **less volatile than the market**. For every 1% movement in the market, the portfolio moves 0.80%. This is a **defensive portfolio** suitable for risk-averse investors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Types of Risk**

- [x] **Risk** = Probability $\times$ Consequence ($R = P \times C$)
- [x] **Pure Risk** → Loss or No Loss → Insurable → e.g., fire, accident
- [x] **Speculative Risk** → Loss, No Loss, or Gain → Not insurable → e.g., stock market
- [x] **Systematic Risk** → Affects entire market → NOT diversifiable → measured by $\beta$
- [x] **Unsystematic Risk** → Firm-specific → Diversifiable → e.g., management fraud
- [x] **Static Risk** → Environmental discontinuity (natural disasters)
- [x] **Dynamic Risk** → Economic/technological change
- [x] **Business Risk** → Operating income variability (DOL)
- [x] **Financial Risk** → Due to debt financing (DFL)
- [x] **Expected Loss** $EL = \sum P_i C_i$
- [x] **Value at Risk** $VaR_{\alpha} = \mu + z_{\alpha} \sigma$
- [x] **Sharpe Ratio** $SR = (R_p - R_f) / \sigma_p$ — higher is better
- [x] **Beta** $\beta_i = \text{Cov}(R_i, R_m) / \text{Var}(R_m)$ — measures systematic risk
- [x] **Engineering QRA categories:** Safety, Environmental, Asset, Schedule, Technical
- [x] Always quantify risk before deciding on **Avoid, Reduce, Transfer, or Accept**
- [x] For KTU exam: always give **numerical examples** and cite **one industrial scenario**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceptable Risk - ALARP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT754 - QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Acceptable Risk - ALARP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Acceptable Risk & ALARP – Foundational Definition and Intuition

## 1. Formal Academic Definition

**Acceptable Risk** is defined as a level of risk below which an activity, process, or facility is considered tolerable by society, regulators, and the operating organization, such that no additional risk reduction measures are reasonably required. In Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA), it is the **predetermined numerical or graphical risk criterion** (typically expressed as fatality frequency or individual risk per year) below which the residual risk is deemed acceptable.

**ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** is the internationally recognized risk management principle (originating from UK case law *Edwards v. National Coal Board, 1949*) which mandates that all risks arising from a hazardous installation must be reduced to a level **"as low as reasonably practicable"**, balancing the **magnitude of risk** against the **sacrifice (cost, effort, time) needed to reduce it further**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For the PECHT754 course, Module 4 specifically focuses on **risk acceptance criteria and the ALARP framework**. You must understand both *qualitative* (good practice) and *quantitative* (numerical criteria) ALARP demonstrations. The HSE (UK Health and Safety Executive) framework is the global reference standard adopted by KTU.

## 2. Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are crossing a busy highway. The **zero-risk** option (acceptable risk = 0) would be to never cross — but that is impractical if you need to reach work. The **maximum-risk** option would be to sprint blindly across 10 lanes. The **ALARP option** is to:

1. Use the pedestrian overpass (best practicable solution),
2. Wait for a safe gap,
3. Look both ways.

You have not eliminated the risk of crossing (it can never be zero), but you have reduced it **as low as reasonably practicable** — meaning you did not spend your entire life's savings building a personal underground tunnel just to cross the road. The cost of that tunnel would be **grossly disproportionate** to the marginal safety benefit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** ALARP is **NOT** "As Low As Reasonably Possible" — it is "Reasonably **Practicable**". This distinction is a favourite exam question. Practicable means *feasible and proportionate*, not theoretically achievable.

## 3. Risk Terminology Hierarchy (KTU Standard)

Risk is classified into three hierarchical zones in QRA. Mastering these three regions is mandatory for board-level answers:

| Zone | Definition | Action Required |
|---|---|---|
| **Unacceptable / Intolerable Region** | Risk so high it cannot be justified except in extraordinary circumstances. | Risk MUST be reduced; operation prohibited unless ALARP demonstrated. |
| **ALARP / Tolerable Region** | Risk is tolerated only if reduction is impracticable or its cost is grossly disproportionate. | Apply ALARP demonstration continuously. |
| **Acceptable / Broadly Acceptable Region** | Risk is negligible compared to everyday hazards (e.g., being struck by lightning). | No further risk reduction required. |

> [!TIP]
> The numerical boundary between *Intolerable* and *Tolerable* is called the **Upper Tolerable Limit (UTL)**, and between *Tolerable* and *Broadly Acceptable* is called the **Lower Tolerable Limit (LTL)**. Memorize these abbreviations — KTU frequently tests them.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. The ALARP Decision Logic – Step-by-Step Reasoning

The application of ALARP follows a rigorous decision-tree logic in process plant design and operation. Below is the universally accepted HSE framework taught under KTU 2024 PECHT754:

### Step 1 – Hazard Identification
- Use HAZOP, FMEA, What-If, or Checklists to identify all credible hazards in the process plant.

### Step 2 – Risk Estimation
- Quantify frequency of each hazardous event using Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), Event Tree Analysis (ETA), or historical failure databases (e.g., OREDA, IEEE Std 500).

### Step 3 – Compare Against Risk Criteria
- Plot the estimated risk on an **F-N curve** (Farmer's curve) and compare with the company's tolerability criteria.
- If risk lies in the **Intolerable Region** → STOP. Risk reduction is mandatory.

### Step 4 – Apply the "Reasonably Practicable" Test
If the risk lies in the Tolerable region, apply the following three ALARP tests:

1. **Inherent Safety Test** — Can the hazard be eliminated by design (substitution, attenuation, simplification)?
2. **Good Practice Test** — Does the design comply with industry codes, standards, and best practices (e.g., API, ASME, NFPA)?
3. **Cost-Benefit / Gross Disproportion Test** — Is the cost of further risk reduction grossly disproportionate to the safety benefit gained?

### Step 5 – Documentation & Residual Risk Acceptance
- Document all ALARP decisions, residual risks, and the basis for acceptance. This forms the **Safety Case** required under KTU's industrial safety regulations.

## 2. KTU Formula Sheet – Risk Acceptance & ALARP Metrics

The following equations are mandatory for the End Semester Examination (ESE):

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Individual Risk (IR)** | $IR = \sum_i f_i \times p_i$ | $f_i$ = frequency of initiating event (per year); $p_i$ = probability of fatality given event | Risk to a person at a specific location 24/7 |
| **Societal Risk (F-N)** | Plot of cumulative frequency $F$ vs number of fatalities $N$ | Frequency per year; Number of fatalities | Measures risk to a population group |
| **Implied Cost of Averting a Fatality (ICAF)** | $ICAF = \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta R}$ | $\Delta C$ = incremental cost of risk reduction; $\Delta R$ = reduction in fatality risk | Cost-benefit test for ALARP |
| **Value of a Statistical Life (VSL)** | Empirical monetary value, ≈ **$10 million** or **₹85 lakh** (India) | Monetary unit per statistical life prevented | Benchmark for societal willingness to pay |
| **Risk Reduction Cost (RRC)** | $RRC = \dfrac{C_{measure}}{N_{lives\_saved}}$ | $C_{measure}$ = annualized cost; $N_{lives\_saved}$ = statistical lives saved per year | Direct ALARP economic test |
| **F-N Criterion (HSE)** | $F = k \times N^{-n}$ | $k$, $n$ = criterion constants; typically $n = 1$ | Defines tolerable societal risk line |
| **Probability of Fatality (PoF)** | $PoF = f_{event} \times P_{f \vert event}$ | Dimensionless (per year) | Used to derive IR |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The ICAF is **NOT** the same as VSL. ICAF is the marginal cost the company actually spends; VSL is society's willingness to pay. ALARP is reached when ICAF ≫ VSL (typically when ICAF > 10 × VSL — the "Gross Disproportion" factor of **10** is a UK HSE standard).

## 3. Numerical Criteria Used in KTU Reference Problems

The following UK HSE criteria are commonly cited in KTU question papers as the global benchmark:

**Individual Risk (IR) per year for workers:**
- Upper Tolerable Limit: $10^{-3}$ per year
- Lower Tolerable Limit (Broadly Acceptable): $10^{-6}$ per year

**Individual Risk (IR) per year for public:**
- Upper Tolerable Limit: $10^{-4}$ per year
- Lower Tolerable Limit: $10^{-6}$ per year

**Societal Risk (F-N Curve, HSE, UK):**
- Intolerable: $F = 10^{-1} \times N^{-1}$ (upper boundary line)
- Negligible / ALARP lower boundary: $F = 10^{-3} \times N^{-1}$ (lower boundary line)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For Indian context (e.g., ONGC, IOCL refineries studied in PECHT754), the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)** and **Oil Industry Safety Directorate (OISD)** have adapted similar but slightly more lenient numerical limits. Always mention both Indian and UK standards in long-answer questions to score full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. Derivation of the ALARP Cost-Benefit Inequality

ALARP is mathematically formalized through the **Gross Disproportion Test**. We derive the condition under which further risk reduction is required.

Let the following variables be defined:

- $R_0$ = Initial level of risk (probability of fatality per year)
- $R_1$ = Residual risk after applying additional safety measure
- $C$ = Annualized cost of the additional safety measure (₹/year)
- $V$ = Value of preventing a statistical fatality (₹ per life saved)
- $k$ = Disproportion factor (typically $k = 10$ for HSE framework)

The expected number of statistical lives saved per year by the additional measure is:

$$\Delta L = R_0 - R_1$$

The monetary benefit (in terms of statistical life valuation) gained per year is:

$$\Delta B = V \times \Delta L$$

The **ALARP inequality** states that a measure is *reasonably practicable* and therefore must be implemented if the benefit is not grossly disproportionate to the cost:

$$\Delta B > k \times C$$

Substituting the expressions for $\Delta B$ and $C$:

$$V \times (R_0 - R_1) > k \times C$$

Rearranging to solve for the maximum permissible cost $C_{max}$ for any proposed safety measure:

$$C_{max} = \dfrac{V \times (R_0 - R_1)}{k}$$

If the actual cost $C_{actual} \le C_{max}$, the measure must be implemented (residual risk is NOT ALARP). If $C_{actual} > C_{max}$, the measure is considered **grossly disproportionate** and may be omitted, provided documentation is maintained.

## 2. Worked Numerical Derivation – KTU Style 14-Mark Question Pattern

**Problem Statement:** A chemical reactor has an inherent individual fatality risk of $R_0 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per year. The company proposes two independent safety upgrades:

- **Upgrade A:** Cost = ₹12 lakh/year, reduces risk to $R_1 = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ per year
- **Upgrade B:** Cost = ₹45 lakh/year, reduces risk to $R_2 = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ per year

Given $V = ₹85$ lakh (VSL India), $k = 10$, determine which upgrades are ALARP-mandatory.

### Step 1 — Compute Risk Reduction for Upgrade A

$$\Delta L_A = R_0 - R_1 = 5 \times 10^{-4} - 1 \times 10^{-4}$$

Evaluating the subtraction:

$$\Delta L_A = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ fatalities per year}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Maximum Justifiable Cost for Upgrade A

$$C_{max,A} = \dfrac{V \times \Delta L_A}{k}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$C_{max,A} = \dfrac{85 \times 10^{5} \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{10}$$

Performing the multiplication in the numerator:

$$85 \times 10^{5} \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 340 \times 10^{1} = 3400$$

Dividing by $k = 10$:

$$C_{max,A} = \dfrac{3400}{10} = 340 \text{ ₹ lakh/year (converted correctly: ₹34,00,000/year)}$$

### Step 3 — Compare with Actual Cost

$$C_{actual,A} = 12 \text{ lakh/year}$$

$$C_{actual,A} = 12 < 340 = C_{max,A}$$

Since $C_{actual} < C_{max}$, Upgrade A **MUST be implemented** — it is reasonably practicable.

### Step 4 — Compute Risk Reduction for Upgrade B

$$\Delta L_B = R_1 - R_2 = 1 \times 10^{-4} - 2 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\Delta L_B = 10 \times 10^{-5} - 2 \times 10^{-5} = 8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ fatalities/year}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Maximum Justifiable Cost for Upgrade B

$$C_{max,B} = \dfrac{85 \times 10^{5} \times 8 \times 10^{-5}}{10}$$

Numerator calculation:

$$85 \times 8 = 680$$

$$680 \times 10^{5} \times 10^{-5} = 680 \times 10^{0} = 680$$

Dividing by $k = 10$:

$$C_{max,B} = 68 \text{ lakh/year}$$

### Step 6 — Compare with Actual Cost for Upgrade B

$$C_{actual,B} = 45 \text{ lakh/year}$$

$$C_{actual,B} = 45 < 68 = C_{max,B}$$

Since $C_{actual} < C_{max}$, Upgrade B is also reasonably practicable and **MUST be implemented** to demonstrate ALARP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of the worked derivation:** Both upgrades are within the ALARP economic envelope. The risk must be reduced to $R_2 = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ per year, which falls in the **ALARP / Tolerable Region** of the IR criteria. If a third upgrade C had $C_{actual} > C_{max}$, it would be considered *grossly disproportionate* and could be omitted.

## 3. Derivation of ICAF from First Principles

The **Implied Cost of Averting a Fatality (ICAF)** is the unit economic metric used by safety regulators. We derive it from the basic economic principle of marginal substitution.

Consider a series of optional safety measures $M_1, M_2, \dots, M_n$ applied sequentially. For the $i$-th measure:

- $\Delta C_i$ = Annualized incremental cost over the previous option
- $\Delta R_i$ = Reduction in fatality risk achieved

The ICAF for the $i$-th measure is defined as the marginal cost per statistical life saved:

$$ICAF_i = \dfrac{\Delta C_i}{\Delta R_i}$$

ALARP is achieved when:

$$ICAF_i \gg VSL$$

The HSE uses a factor of **10** as the threshold of gross disproportion:

$$ICAF_i > 10 \times VSL \implies \text{Further reduction is NOT reasonably practicable}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 1. ALARP Risk Reduction Triangle (Mermaid Flow)

The following block diagram illustrates the ALARP framework as applied in process plant design under QRA Module 4:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Identified in Process Plant] --> B{Estimate Individual and Societal Risk}
    B --> C[Plot on F-N Curve and IR Map]
    C --> D{Risk above Upper Tolerable Limit}
    D -- YES --> E[Risk is UNACCEPTABLE - Mandatory Reduction]
    D -- NO --> F{Risk between Upper and Lower Tolerable Limits}
    F -- YES --> G[Apply ALARP Demonstration]
    F -- NO --> H[Risk is BROADLY ACCEPTABLE - No Further Action]
    G --> G1[Inherent Safety Test]
    G --> G2[Good Practice Test]
    G --> G3[Cost Benefit Gross Disproportion Test]
    G1 --> I{Residual Risk Practicable?}
    G2 --> I
    G3 --> I
    I -- YES --> J[Implement Measure - Repeat from G]
    I -- NO --> K[Document ALARP Decision and Accept Residual Risk]
    E --> G
    H --> Z[Operation Permitted with Standard Controls]
    K --> Z
```

## 2. Risk Zone Hierarchy Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[IR greater than 10 power minus 3 per year]:::unacc --> P2[Intolerable Zone]
    P2 --> P3[ALARP or Tolerable Zone: IR between 10 power minus 6 and 10 power minus 3 per year]
    P3 --> P4[Broadly Acceptable Zone: IR less than 10 power minus 6 per year]:::safe
    classDef unacc fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,color:#fff
    classDef safe fill:#51cf66,stroke:#060,color:#000
```

## 3. Cost-Benefit Economic Trade-off Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Safety Measure Proposed] --> S2[Compute Risk Reduction delta L]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Annualized Cost C]
    S3 --> S4{Is C less than V times delta L divided by k?}
    S4 -- YES --> S5[Measure is REASONABLY PRACTICABLE]
    S4 -- NO --> S6[Cost is GROSSLY DISPROPORTIONATE]
    S5 --> S7[Implement Measure]
    S6 --> S8[Document and Omit with Justification]
```

## 4. Block-Level Functional Architecture of ALARP Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Step 1: Hazard Identification HAZOP and FMEA] --> Q2[Step 2: Frequency Estimation using FTA and ETA]
    Q2 --> Q3[Step 3: Consequence Modelling using ALOHA and PHAST]
    Q3 --> Q4[Step 4: Risk Calculation Individual and Societal]
    Q4 --> Q5[Step 5: Comparison with Risk Criteria F-N and IR]
    Q5 --> Q6{Acceptable without further action?}
    Q6 -- YES --> Q7[Operation Permitted]
    Q6 -- NO --> Q8[Initiate ALARP Demonstration Loop]
    Q8 --> Q9[Good Practice Compliance Check]
    Q8 --> Q10[Inherent Safety Modification Check]
    Q8 --> Q11[Cost Benefit Gross Disproportion Analysis]
    Q9 --> Q12[Re evaluate Residual Risk]
    Q10 --> Q12
    Q11 --> Q12
    Q12 --> Q5
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Rendering Note:** When pasting these diagrams into the KTU digital notes portal or PDF, ensure the file is rendered with the mermaid CLI plugin or use mermaid.live. Use **node identifiers prefixed with letters** (e.g., `S1`, `Q1`) to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts in the renderer.
